title: Graduate Microeconomic Theory
text: 1 Microeconomics Program Overview

title: Graduate Microeconomic Theory
text:
1.1 Introduction
This program is a systematic, graduate-level treatment of microeconomic theory designed to bridge the gap between the formal apparatus of MWG and the practical demands of economic model-building for empirical research. The goal is not merely to reproduce proofs and definitions, but to develop the theoretical intuition and technical fluency needed to construct original models, derive testable predictions, and connect those predictions to identification strategies in applied work. The program is organized so that each part builds on its predecessors: mathematical foundations underpin consumer and producer theory, which together support general equilibrium, which in turn provides the foundation for welfare analysis, game theory, and mechanism design. Every note is written at the level of MWG and uses the same notational conventions, allowing these notes to serve as a genuine companion to the textbook.

Social welfare functions, Arrow’s impossibility theorem, second-best theory, compensation criteria

Adverse selection, screening, signaling, moral hazard, mechanism design (revelation principle, implementation)


IX
Markets and Mechanism Design

Monopoly, oligopoly, public goods, externalities, auction theory, VCG mechanisms

title: Graduate Microeconomic Theory
text:
1.3 How to Use This Program
Sequential reading. The parts are ordered by logical dependency. Parts I–III form the core; Part IV can be read after Part II; Parts V–VI require Parts II–III; Parts VII–IX can be read in parallel after Part V. The recommended reading map below makes dependencies explicit.
Note-taking with templates. The Templates/ folder contains two master templates: - Theorem Note Template — use this whenever adding a new definition or theorem note. - Model Building Template — use this whenever building a new theoretical model from scratch.
When creating a new note, copy the appropriate template, fill in all sections, and do not leave any section blank. The placeholder text in each template explains what belongs in each section.
Problem sets. Each Part Index links to problem sets drawn from MWG end-of-chapter exercises and from standard PhD qualifying exams. Problem sets do not include solutions. Working through them independently is essential for genuine mastery; the notes provide the theory, but fluency comes only from solving problems.
Cross-referencing. Every note includes a Cross-References section with wikilinks to related notes. Follow these links actively — microeconomic theory is a highly interconnected body of knowledge and the connections between results (e.g., between the Envelope Theorem and Shephard’s Lemma, or between Kakutani and Nash existence) are often as important as the results themselves.

Minimum path to Mechanism Design: Parts I → II → III → V → VII → VIII → IX.
Minimum path to Applied Theory: Parts I → II → III → IV (these four parts are sufficient for most applied theory papers in IO, labor, and public economics).

title: Graduate Microeconomic Theory
text:
1.6 Model-Building Workflow
The following 10-step checklist guides the construction of any new theoretical model. Each step corresponds to a section in the Model Building Template. Use this checklist as a quality-control device: a model is not complete until every step has been addressed.

Specify the economic environment. Define the commodity space \(\mathcal{L}\), the time horizon (static, finite-horizon, infinite-horizon), and the state space \(\Omega\) for uncertainty. Be explicit about what is exogenous and what is endogenous.
Define agents and their primitives. List each agent \(i \in I\). For each agent, specify: (a) the type space \(\Theta_i\) (private information); (b) the action space \(A_i\); (c) the payoff/utility function \(u_i: A \times \Theta \to \mathbb{R}\).
Write down the timing and information structure. Draw a game tree or timeline. Specify what each agent knows at each decision node. Identify the information partition.
Write each agent’s optimization problem. State the objective function and all constraints formally. For consumer problems: utility maximization or expenditure minimization. For firm problems: profit maximization or cost minimization. For mechanism design: planner’s objective subject to IC and IR constraints.
Define the equilibrium concept. Choose the appropriate solution concept (Walrasian, Nash, SPNE, PBE, BNE, dominant strategy, etc.) and state its definition formally. Justify why this concept is appropriate for the model.
Prove existence. State sufficient conditions for equilibrium existence and cite the relevant fixed-point theorem (Brouwer, Kakutani) or existence result from Part I or V. If conditions are not satisfied, explain why and what assumptions must be added.
Prove or discuss uniqueness. State conditions under which the equilibrium is unique. If multiplicity cannot be ruled out, discuss selection criteria or interpret the multiplicity economically.
Derive comparative statics. Identify the key parameters and predict how equilibrium outcomes change as each parameter changes. Use the Envelope Theorem (Part I), the implicit function theorem, or monotone comparative statics (Topkis/Milgrom-Shannon).
Derive testable predictions. Translate the comparative statics into statements that are in principle falsifiable with data. Be specific: what is the dependent variable, what is the treatment variable, and what is the predicted sign or magnitude?
Specify an identification strategy. For each testable prediction, describe how it could be identified empirically: natural experiment, instrumental variable (with exclusion restriction argument), regression discontinuity, difference-in-differences, or structural estimation. Discuss the key threats to identification.

title: Graduate Microeconomic Theory
text:
1.7 Notation Conventions
Throughout this vault, the following notation is standard (following MWG):

\(L\) — number of commodities; \(\ell = 1, \ldots, L\) indexes commodities
\(\mathbb{R}^L_+\) — nonnegative orthant (consumption sets)
\(p \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\) — strictly positive price vector
\(w > 0\) — wealth (budget)
\(x(p,w)\) — Walrasian (Marshallian) demand
\(h(p,u)\) — Hicksian (compensated) demand
\(v(p,w)\) — indirect utility function
\(e(p,u)\) — expenditure function
\(\pi(p)\) — profit function; \(y(p)\) — supply correspondence
\(c(w,q)\) — cost function; \(z(w,q)\) — factor demand
\(\succsim\) — weak preference relation; \(\succ\) strict preference; \(\sim\) indifference
\(\phi: X \rightrightarrows Y\) — correspondence (set-valued map)
\(\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda)\) — Lagrangian function

title: 2  Part I: Mathematical Foundations — Index
text:
3.1 Overview
Mathematical foundations come first because every existence proof, every comparative statics argument, and every equilibrium characterization in subsequent parts rests on a small set of deep mathematical results. The most important are:

Compactness and continuity (covered in Note 1.1) — needed to guarantee that optimization problems have solutions (via the Weierstrass Maximum Theorem). Without compactness of the budget set or production set, a consumer or firm might have no optimal choice.
Correspondences and hemicontinuity (Note 1.2) — because demand and supply are in general set-valued (correspondences), not functions; Berge’s Maximum Theorem gives conditions under which the value function and the argmax correspondence inherit the continuity properties of the objective and constraint correspondence.
Optimization theory (Note 1.3) — the Lagrangian method, KKT conditions, and the Envelope Theorem are the workhorses of comparative statics. The Envelope Theorem alone underlies Hotelling’s Lemma, Shephard’s Lemma, Roy’s Identity, and numerous other fundamental results.
Fixed-point theorems (Note 1.4) — Brouwer’s theorem and its generalization, Kakutani’s theorem, are the load-bearing mathematical structure behind every equilibrium existence proof: Nash equilibrium, Walrasian equilibrium, and the existence of optimal mechanisms all reduce to an application of Kakutani.

These four notes are prerequisites for the entire program. They should be mastered — not just read — before proceeding to Part II.

title: 2  Part I: Mathematical Foundations — Index
text:
3.4 What Part I Enables
After completing Part I, you will have the tools needed for: - Part II (Consumer Theory): Weierstrass for existence of \(x(p,w)\); Berge for UHC of demand; Envelope Theorem for Roy’s Identity and Shephard’s Lemma. - Part III (Producer Theory): Same tools applied to the firm’s profit and cost problems. - Part V (General Equilibrium): Kakutani for existence of Walrasian equilibrium. - Part VII (Game Theory): Kakutani for existence of Nash equilibrium; Berge for payoff continuity in mixed strategies.

title: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology
text: 4 1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology
crumbs: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology

title: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology
text:
4.1 Context
The purpose of this note is to establish the topological foundations on which all of microeconomic theory rests. The central concern is: under what conditions does an optimization problem have a solution? The answer, given by the Weierstrass Maximum Theorem, requires the domain to be compact and the objective function to be continuous. Understanding what these conditions mean, why they are needed, and how to verify them in economic applications is the primary goal of this note. The note proceeds from metric spaces through open and closed sets, convergence, compactness (with the Heine-Borel characterization), and continuity, culminating in the Weierstrass theorem and its economic applications. Convex sets are introduced at the end because convexity of budget sets and production sets is a standing assumption throughout the program; the relationship between compactness and convexity also matters for the fixed-point theorems in Note 1.4.
crumbs: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology

title: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology
text:
4.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 1.1 — Metric Space



A metric space is a pair \((X, d)\) where \(X\) is a nonempty set and \(d: X \times X \to \mathbb{R}_+\) is a metric (distance function) satisfying, for all \(x, y, z \in X\): 1. (Nonnegativity) \(d(x, y) \geq 0\), with \(d(x, y) = 0\) if and only if \(x = y\). 2. (Symmetry) \(d(x, y) = d(y, x)\). 3. (Triangle inequality) \(d(x, z) \leq d(x, y) + d(y, z)\).








CautionRemark



The canonical example throughout this program is \((\mathbb{R}^L, d_E)\) with the Euclidean metric \(d_E(x, y) = \|x - y\| = \left(\sum_{\ell=1}^L (x_\ell - y_\ell)^2\right)^{1/2}\). This metric generates the standard topology on \(\mathbb{R}^L\) that underlies all of consumer and producer theory. Other metrics on \(\mathbb{R}^L\) (e.g., the sup-norm \(d_\infty(x,y) = \max_\ell |x_\ell - y_\ell|\)) generate the same topology (they are equivalent), so topological properties such as compactness and continuity are unaffected by the choice among them.








NoteDefinition 1.2 — Open and Closed Balls



Let \((X, d)\) be a metric space, \(x \in X\), and \(\varepsilon > 0\). The open ball of radius \(\varepsilon\) centered at \(x\) is: \[B_\varepsilon(x) = \{ y \in X : d(x, y) < \varepsilon \}.\] The closed ball is \(\bar{B}_\varepsilon(x) = \{ y \in X : d(x, y) \leq \varepsilon \}\).








NoteDefinition 1.3 — Open Sets and Closed Sets



Let \((X, d)\) be a metric space. - A set \(U \subseteq X\) is open if for every \(x \in U\) there exists \(\varepsilon > 0\) such that \(B_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq U\). - A set \(F \subseteq X\) is closed if its complement \(X \setminus F\) is open; equivalently, \(F\) contains all its limit points (see Definition 1.4). - The interior of \(A \subseteq X\) is \(\text{int}(A) = \bigcup \{U \subseteq A : U \text{ open}\}\). - The closure of \(A\) is \(\bar{A} = \bigcap \{F \supseteq A : F \text{ closed}\}\). - The boundary of \(A\) is \(\partial A = \bar{A} \setminus \text{int}(A)\).








CautionRemark



In \(\mathbb{R}^L\): the open orthant \(\mathbb{R}^L_{++} = \{x : x_\ell > 0 \; \forall \ell\}\) is open; the closed orthant \(\mathbb{R}^L_+ = \{x : x_\ell \geq 0 \; \forall \ell\}\) is closed. The budget set \(\{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : p \cdot x \leq w\}\) is closed (and, as we shall see, bounded, hence compact). The strict budget set \(\{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : p \cdot x < w\}\) is not closed.








NoteDefinition 1.4 — Convergence and Limit Points



Let \((X, d)\) be a metric space. A sequence \((x_n)_{n=1}^\infty\) in \(X\) converges to \(x \in X\), written \(x_n \to x\), if: \[\forall \varepsilon > 0, \; \exists N \in \mathbb{N} \text{ such that } n \geq N \implies d(x_n, x) < \varepsilon.\] The point \(x\) is called the limit of the sequence. A point \(x \in X\) is a limit point (or accumulation point) of \(A \subseteq X\) if every open ball \(B_\varepsilon(x)\) contains a point of \(A\) different from \(x\).








NoteDefinition 1.5 — Cauchy Sequence and Completeness



A sequence \((x_n)\) in \((X, d)\) is a Cauchy sequence if: \[\forall \varepsilon > 0, \; \exists N \in \mathbb{N} \text{ such that } m, n \geq N \implies d(x_m, x_n) < \varepsilon.\] A metric space \((X, d)\) is complete if every Cauchy sequence in \(X\) converges to a point in \(X\).








CautionRemark



\((\mathbb{R}^L, d_E)\) is complete (this follows from the completeness of \(\mathbb{R}\), which in turn follows from the least upper bound property). Closed subsets of complete metric spaces are complete. This fact is used implicitly whenever we work with closed constraint sets.








NoteDefinition 1.6 — Compactness



A subset \(K \subseteq X\) is compact if every open cover of \(K\) has a finite subcover. That is, \(K\) is compact if for every collection of open sets \(\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in \mathcal{A}}\) with \(K \subseteq \bigcup_\alpha U_\alpha\), there exist finitely many indices \(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n\) such that \(K \subseteq U_{\alpha_1} \cup \cdots \cup U_{\alpha_n}\).








NoteDefinition 1.7 — Sequential Compactness



A subset \(K \subseteq X\) is sequentially compact if every sequence in \(K\) has a subsequence that converges to a point in \(K\).








NoteDefinition 1.8 — Continuity



Let \((X, d_X)\) and \((Y, d_Y)\) be metric spaces. A function \(f: X \to Y\) is continuous at \(x \in X\) if: \[\forall \varepsilon > 0, \; \exists \delta > 0 \text{ such that } d_X(x, x') < \delta \implies d_Y(f(x), f(x')) < \varepsilon.\] The function \(f\) is continuous (on \(X\)) if it is continuous at every \(x \in X\).








NoteDefinition 1.9 — Convex Set



A set \(C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) is convex if for all \(x, y \in C\) and all \(\lambda \in [0, 1]\): \[\lambda x + (1 - \lambda) y \in C.\] A convex combination of points \(x^1, \ldots, x^K \in \mathbb{R}^L\) is any point \(\sum_{k=1}^K \lambda_k x^k\) with \(\lambda_k \geq 0\) and \(\sum_k \lambda_k = 1\). The convex hull of a set \(A\) is the smallest convex set containing \(A\): \(\text{co}(A) = \bigcap \{C \supseteq A : C \text{ convex}\}\).
crumbs: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology

title: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology
text:
4.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 1.1 — Heine-Borel Theorem



A subset \(K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) (with the Euclidean metric) is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.








TipProof





We prove both directions.
(\(\Rightarrow\)) Compact implies closed and bounded.
Closed: Let \((x_n)\) be any sequence in \(K\) converging to some \(x \in \mathbb{R}^L\). We must show \(x \in K\). Since \(x_n \to x\), for every \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(N\) such that \(n \geq N\) implies \(x_n \in B_\varepsilon(x)\). In particular, \(B_\varepsilon(x)\) intersects \(K\) for every \(\varepsilon > 0\), so \(x\) is a limit point of \(K\). In a compact Hausdorff space (and metric spaces are Hausdorff), compact sets are closed. More directly: suppose \(x \notin K\). Then for each \(y \in K\), let \(\varepsilon_y = d(x, y)/2 > 0\). The balls \(\{B_{\varepsilon_y}(y)\}_{y \in K}\) cover \(K\), so by compactness there is a finite subcover: \(K \subseteq B_{\varepsilon_{y_1}}(y_1) \cup \cdots \cup B_{\varepsilon_{y_n}}(y_n)\). Let \(\delta = \min_i \varepsilon_{y_i} > 0\). Then \(B_\delta(x)\) is disjoint from \(K\) (by the triangle inequality), contradicting that \(x\) is a limit point. So \(x \in K\).
Bounded: Cover \(K\) by the open balls \(\{B_1(y)\}_{y \in K}\). By compactness, a finite subcollection \(B_1(y_1), \ldots, B_1(y_n)\) covers \(K\). Let \(M = \max_i \|y_i\| + 1\). Then every \(x \in K\) satisfies \(\|x\| \leq \max_i \|x - y_i\| + \|y_i\| < 1 + \max_i \|y_i\| = M\). So \(K\) is bounded.
(\(\Leftarrow\)) Closed and bounded implies compact.
Since \(K\) is bounded, \(K \subseteq [-M, M]^L\) for some \(M > 0\). It suffices to show \([-M,M]^L\) is compact (then \(K\), being a closed subset of a compact set, is compact). We use the sequential characterization: in a metric space, compactness and sequential compactness are equivalent (this is a standard result in analysis; proof omitted here but available in Rudin Ch. 3).
Let \((x_n)\) be any sequence in \([-M, M]^L\). Since each coordinate sequence \((x_{n,\ell})\) is bounded in \(\mathbb{R}\), by the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem (every bounded sequence in \(\mathbb{R}\) has a convergent subsequence), we can extract a convergent subsequence for coordinate 1. From that subsequence, extract a further subsequence converging in coordinate 2. Iterate for all \(L\) coordinates. The resulting subsequence \((x_{n_k})\) converges in all \(L\) coordinates, hence converges in \(\mathbb{R}^L\). Its limit lies in \([-M, M]^L\) (since each coordinate limit lies in \([-M, M]\)). Since \(K\) is closed and \((x_{n_k}) \subseteq K\) converges to a point in \([-M,M]^L\), that limit point also lies in \(K\). Thus every sequence in \(K\) has a subsequence converging in \(K\), so \(K\) is sequentially compact, hence compact. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



The Heine-Borel theorem is specific to \(\mathbb{R}^L\) (finite-dimensional Euclidean space). In infinite-dimensional spaces (e.g., the space of continuous functions \(C([0,1])\) with the sup-norm), closed and bounded sets are not in general compact. This distinction matters for dynamic models or models with infinite commodity spaces.








ImportantTheorem 1.2 — Equivalence of Compactness and Sequential Compactness (Metric Spaces)



In any metric space \((X, d)\), a set \(K \subseteq X\) is compact if and only if it is sequentially compact.








TipProof





This is a standard result. The proof that compact implies sequentially compact proceeds by contradiction: if a sequence had no convergent subsequence, one constructs an open cover with no finite subcover. The converse uses the totality of metric spaces (existence of \(\varepsilon\)-nets). We refer to Rudin (1976, Theorem 3.16) for the complete proof and use the result freely throughout this program. \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 1.3 — Continuity Preserves Compactness



Let \(f: X \to Y\) be continuous and \(K \subseteq X\) compact. Then \(f(K) = \{f(x) : x \in K\}\) is compact in \(Y\).








TipProof





Let \(\{V_\alpha\}\) be an open cover of \(f(K)\). Since \(f\) is continuous, \(f^{-1}(V_\alpha)\) is open in \(X\) for each \(\alpha\), and \(\{f^{-1}(V_\alpha)\}\) covers \(K\). By compactness of \(K\), a finite subcollection \(f^{-1}(V_{\alpha_1}), \ldots, f^{-1}(V_{\alpha_n})\) covers \(K\). Then \(V_{\alpha_1}, \ldots, V_{\alpha_n}\) covers \(f(K)\). \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 1.4 — Sequential Characterization of Continuity



A function \(f: X \to Y\) between metric spaces is continuous at \(x \in X\) if and only if for every sequence \(x_n \to x\) in \(X\), \(f(x_n) \to f(x)\) in \(Y\).








TipProof





(\(\Rightarrow\)) Suppose \(f\) is continuous at \(x\) and \(x_n \to x\). Given \(\varepsilon > 0\), continuity gives \(\delta > 0\) with \(d_X(x', x) < \delta \implies d_Y(f(x'), f(x)) < \varepsilon\). Since \(x_n \to x\), there exists \(N\) with \(n \geq N \implies d_X(x_n, x) < \delta\), hence \(d_Y(f(x_n), f(x)) < \varepsilon\).
(\(\Leftarrow\)) Suppose \(f\) is not continuous at \(x\). Then \(\exists \varepsilon > 0\) such that for every \(\delta > 0\), \(\exists x' \in X\) with \(d_X(x', x) < \delta\) but \(d_Y(f(x'), f(x)) \geq \varepsilon\). Taking \(\delta = 1/n\) gives a sequence \(x_n\) with \(d_X(x_n, x) < 1/n\) (so \(x_n \to x\)) but \(d_Y(f(x_n), f(x)) \geq \varepsilon\) for all \(n\). Thus \(f(x_n) \not\to f(x)\). \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 1.5 — Weierstrass Maximum Theorem (Proposition M.F.2)



Let \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) be nonempty and compact, and let \(f: X \to \mathbb{R}\) be continuous. Then: 1. \(f\) is bounded above and below on \(X\). 2. \(f\) attains its maximum: \(\exists x^* \in X\) such that \(f(x^*) = \sup_{x \in X} f(x)\). 3. \(f\) attains its minimum: \(\exists x_* \in X\) such that \(f(x_*) = \inf_{x \in X} f(x)\).








TipProof





Step 1: \(f(X)\) is compact. By Theorem 1.3 (continuity preserves compactness), since \(X\) is compact and \(f\) is continuous, \(f(X) \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) is compact. By Heine-Borel (Theorem 1.1), \(f(X)\) is closed and bounded.
Step 2: \(\sup f(X)\) is finite and belongs to \(f(X)\). Since \(f(X)\) is bounded above, \(M = \sup f(X) \in \mathbb{R}\) (the supremum is finite). We claim \(M \in f(X)\). By definition of supremum, for each \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) there exists \(y_n \in f(X)\) with \(M - 1/n < y_n \leq M\), so \(y_n \to M\). Since \(f(X)\) is closed and \((y_n)\) is a sequence in \(f(X)\) converging to \(M\), we have \(M \in f(X)\).
Step 3: Maximum is attained. Since \(M \in f(X)\), there exists \(x^* \in X\) with \(f(x^*) = M = \sup_{x \in X} f(x)\). So \(f(x^*) \geq f(x)\) for all \(x \in X\): \(x^*\) is a maximizer.
Step 4: Minimum. Apply the same argument to \(-f\), which is also continuous. Its maximum on \(X\) is \(-\inf_{x \in X} f(x)\), which is attained at some \(x_* \in X\). Then \(f(x_*) = \inf_{x \in X} f(x)\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



All three hypotheses are necessary: - Nonemptiness: Obvious. - Compactness (closed): \(f(x) = x\) on \((0, 1)\) (open): supremum is \(1\), not attained. More economically, a consumer facing an open budget set \(\{x : p \cdot x < w\}\) may not have an optimal choice. - Compactness (bounded): \(f(x) = x\) on \([0, \infty)\): no maximum. - Continuity: \(f(x) = 0\) for \(x < 1\), \(f(1) = -1\) on \([0,1]\): supremum is \(0\), not attained.








ImportantTheorem 1.6 — Properties of Convex Sets



Let \(C, D \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) be convex. Then: 1. \(C \cap D\) is convex. 2. \(C + D = \{x + y : x \in C, y \in D\}\) (Minkowski sum) is convex. 3. \(\lambda C = \{\lambda x : x \in C\}\) is convex for any \(\lambda \in \mathbb{R}\). 4. The closure \(\bar{C}\) of a convex set is convex. 5. If \(C\) is convex and \(f: \mathbb{R}^L \to \mathbb{R}\) is a linear function, then \(f(C)\) is a convex subset of \(\mathbb{R}\).








TipProof





Each part follows directly from the definition of convexity. We prove (1) as representative. Let \(x, y \in C \cap D\) and \(\lambda \in [0,1]\). Since \(C\) is convex and \(x, y \in C\): \(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y \in C\). Since \(D\) is convex and \(x, y \in D\): \(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y \in D\). Hence \(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y \in C \cap D\). \(\blacksquare\)
crumbs: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology

title: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology
text:
4.4 Economic Intuition
Why compactness is needed in consumer theory. The consumer’s problem is to maximize a continuous utility function \(u: \mathbb{R}^L_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) subject to the budget constraint \(p \cdot x \leq w\), \(x \geq 0\). The budget set \(B(p, w) = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : p \cdot x \leq w\}\) is closed (as the intersection of closed half-spaces) and bounded (since \(x_\ell \leq w/p_\ell\) for each \(\ell\) when \(p \gg 0\)). By Heine-Borel, \(B(p,w)\) is compact. By Weierstrass, the maximum of \(u\) over \(B(p,w)\) exists. Without boundedness (e.g., if \(p_\ell = 0\) for some \(\ell\), so the consumer can consume infinite amounts of good \(\ell\) at zero cost), the budget set is unbounded and a solution may not exist. This is why the assumption \(p \gg 0\) (all prices strictly positive) is maintained in basic consumer theory.
Why convexity is needed for uniqueness. Compactness guarantees existence of a maximum, but not uniqueness. If the utility function is strictly quasi-concave (a convexity condition on preferences), then the set of maximizers is a singleton: the demand function \(x(p,w)\) is single-valued. Without quasi-concavity, demand may be multi-valued (a correspondence), and comparative statics arguments become more delicate.
Why these conditions matter for model-building. When building a new model, the first question to ask is: does each agent’s optimization problem have a solution? The Weierstrass theorem provides a two-part checklist: (1) Is the constraint set compact? If not, add an assumption that makes it compact (e.g., bounded endowments, finite action space). (2) Is the objective function continuous? If not, identify the source of discontinuity (e.g., a jump in a pricing rule) and either smooth it or use a fixed-point argument directly. Models where existence of optimal choices is not guaranteed are not well-posed.






NoteExample



Budget set compactness. Let \(L = 2\), \(p = (1, 2)\), \(w = 10\). Then \(B(p,w) = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbb{R}^2_+ : x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 10\}\). This is the triangle with vertices \((0,0)\), \((10,0)\), \((0,5)\). It is closed (it includes its boundary) and bounded (all points satisfy \(x_1 \leq 10\), \(x_2 \leq 5\)). By Heine-Borel, it is compact. The utility function \(u(x_1, x_2) = x_1^{1/2} x_2^{1/2}\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}^2_+\). By Weierstrass, a utility-maximizing bundle exists. (It is \(x^* = (5, 2.5)\) by the standard Cobb-Douglas formula.)
crumbs: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology

title: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology
text:
4.5 Cross-References

1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity — Berge’s Maximum Theorem extends Weierstrass to optimization over a correspondence; requires compactness and continuity developed here.
1.3 Optimization Theory — Lagrangian and envelope theorem methods assume interior solutions or use the topology of the constraint set developed here.
1.4 Fixed Point Theorems — Brouwer and Kakutani require the domain to be compact and convex; those properties are defined and studied here.
Index — Budget set compactness and utility continuity are the foundation of demand existence throughout Part II.
crumbs: 3  1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology

title: 4  1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity
text: 5 1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity

title: 4  1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity
text:
5.1 Context
Many fundamental objects in microeconomics are correspondences (set-valued maps) rather than single-valued functions: the Walrasian demand \(x(p,w)\) may be multi-valued when indifference curves have flat segments; supply correspondences are multi-valued whenever marginal cost is flat; the budget correspondence \(B(p,w)\) is always set-valued; and best-response correspondences in game theory are generally set-valued. To study how these objects change as parameters vary, we need a notion of continuity for correspondences. This note develops two one-sided notions — upper hemicontinuity (UHC) and lower hemicontinuity (LHC) — and then proves Berge’s Maximum Theorem, which is the workhorse result connecting continuity of correspondences to continuity of value functions. The note applies these results to the budget correspondence and the Walrasian demand correspondence. Berge’s theorem will be used repeatedly in Parts II, III, V, and VII.

title: 4  1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity
text:
5.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 1.10 — Correspondence



Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be nonempty sets. A correspondence \(\phi: X \rightrightarrows Y\) assigns to each \(x \in X\) a nonempty subset \(\phi(x) \subseteq Y\). The graph of \(\phi\) is the set: \[\text{Gr}(\phi) = \{ (x, y) \in X \times Y : y \in \phi(x) \}.\] The image of a set \(A \subseteq X\) under \(\phi\) is \(\phi(A) = \bigcup_{x \in A} \phi(x)\).








CautionRemark



Every function \(f: X \to Y\) is a correspondence with \(\phi(x) = \{f(x)\}\) (a singleton) for all \(x\). The theory of correspondences is strictly more general.








NoteDefinition 1.11 — Upper Hemicontinuity (Sequential Definition)



Let \((X, d_X)\) and \((Y, d_Y)\) be metric spaces. A correspondence \(\phi: X \rightrightarrows Y\) is upper hemicontinuous (UHC) at \(x \in X\) if: whenever \(x_n \to x\) in \(X\) and \(y_n \in \phi(x_n)\) for all \(n\), the sequence \((y_n)\) has a subsequence converging to some \(y \in \phi(x)\).
\(\phi\) is UHC (on \(X\)) if it is UHC at every \(x \in X\).








NoteDefinition 1.12 — Upper Hemicontinuity (Open-Sets Definition)



A correspondence \(\phi: X \rightrightarrows Y\) is UHC at \(x \in X\) if for every open set \(V \supseteq \phi(x)\) in \(Y\), there exists an open set \(U \ni x\) in \(X\) such that \(\phi(U) = \bigcup_{x' \in U} \phi(x') \subseteq V\).








CautionRemark



Definitions 1.11 and 1.12 are equivalent when \(Y\) is compact (see Theorem 1.7 below). The open-sets definition is cleaner for abstract proofs, while the sequential definition is easier to verify in applications. When \(\phi\) is single-valued (a function), UHC reduces to ordinary continuity.








NoteDefinition 1.13 — Closed Graph



A correspondence \(\phi: X \rightrightarrows Y\) has a closed graph if \(\text{Gr}(\phi)\) is a closed subset of \(X \times Y\) (under the product metric). Equivalently: if \(x_n \to x\), \(y_n \to y\), and \(y_n \in \phi(x_n)\) for all \(n\), then \(y \in \phi(x)\).








ImportantTheorem 1.7 — UHC and Closed Graph



Let \(\phi: X \rightrightarrows Y\) where \(Y\) is compact. Then \(\phi\) is UHC at \(x\) if and only if \(\phi\) has a closed graph at \(x\) and \(\phi(x)\) is closed.
More precisely: if \(\phi\) is UHC and has compact values, then \(\phi\) has a closed graph. Conversely, if \(Y\) is compact and \(\phi\) has a closed graph, then \(\phi\) is UHC.








TipProof





UHC + compact values \(\Rightarrow\) closed graph. Suppose \(x_n \to x\) and \(y_n \in \phi(x_n)\) with \(y_n \to y\). Since \(\phi\) is UHC, \((y_n)\) has a subsequence \(y_{n_k} \to z \in \phi(x)\). But \(y_n \to y\), so every subsequence converges to \(y\), hence \(z = y \in \phi(x)\).
\(Y\) compact, closed graph \(\Rightarrow\) UHC. Let \(x_n \to x\) and \(y_n \in \phi(x_n)\). Since \(Y\) is compact, \((y_n)\) has a convergent subsequence \(y_{n_k} \to y\). Since \(\phi\) has a closed graph and \((x_{n_k}, y_{n_k}) \to (x, y)\) with \(y_{n_k} \in \phi(x_{n_k})\), we have \(y \in \phi(x)\). So \(\phi\) is UHC. \(\blacksquare\)









NoteDefinition 1.14 — Lower Hemicontinuity (Sequential Definition)



A correspondence \(\phi: X \rightrightarrows Y\) is lower hemicontinuous (LHC) at \(x \in X\) if: whenever \(x_n \to x\) and \(y \in \phi(x)\), there exist \(y_n \in \phi(x_n)\) such that \(y_n \to y\).
\(\phi\) is LHC (on \(X\)) if it is LHC at every \(x \in X\).








NoteDefinition 1.15 — Continuous Correspondence



A correspondence \(\phi: X \rightrightarrows Y\) is continuous at \(x\) if it is both UHC and LHC at \(x\). It is continuous if it is continuous at every \(x \in X\).








CautionRemark



UHC and LHC capture different aspects of “continuity.” Intuitively: - UHC says the correspondence does not “explode” (the image does not suddenly become much larger as we perturb \(x\)). Equivalently, the graph does not have points “above” the limit that escaped from the image. - LHC says the correspondence does not “implode” (the image does not suddenly become much smaller; every selection near the limit can be tracked by nearby selections). Equivalently, no part of \(\phi(x)\) is “isolated” from nearby images.
The distinction matters: Walrasian demand is always UHC, but is LHC only if the optimum is unique (otherwise, a point removed from the argmax set by a perturbation violates LHC).

title: 4  1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity
text:
5.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 1.8 — Berge’s Maximum Theorem (Theorem M.K.6)



Let \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}^K\) and \(Y \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) be metric spaces. Let \(f: X \times Y \to \mathbb{R}\) be a continuous function, and let \(\phi: X \rightrightarrows Y\) be a continuous (UHC and LHC) correspondence with nonempty compact values \(\phi(x)\) for all \(x \in X\).
Define the value function: \[V(x) = \max_{y \in \phi(x)} f(x, y)\] and the argmax correspondence: \[\phi^*(x) = \arg\max_{y \in \phi(x)} f(x, y) = \{ y \in \phi(x) : f(x, y) = V(x) \}.\]
Then: 1. \(V: X \to \mathbb{R}\) is continuous. 2. \(\phi^*: X \rightrightarrows Y\) is UHC and has nonempty compact values.








TipProof





Preliminary: Since \(\phi(x)\) is compact and \(f(x, \cdot)\) is continuous (as a restriction of the continuous function \(f\)), Weierstrass (Theorem 1.5) guarantees \(V(x)\) is well-defined and \(\phi^*(x)\) is nonempty for all \(x\). Compactness of \(\phi^*(x)\): \(\phi^*(x) = \{y \in \phi(x) : f(x,y) = V(x)\}\) is the preimage of the closed set \(\{V(x)\}\) under the continuous map \(f(x, \cdot)\), intersected with the compact set \(\phi(x)\), hence compact.
Step 1: \(V\) is upper semicontinuous. Let \(x_n \to x\). We want \(\limsup_{n} V(x_n) \leq V(x)\).
For each \(n\), let \(y_n \in \phi^*(x_n)\), so \(V(x_n) = f(x_n, y_n)\). Since \(\phi\) is UHC and \(y_n \in \phi(x_n)\), the sequence \((y_n)\) has a subsequence \(y_{n_k} \to y \in \phi(x)\).
Along this subsequence: \(V(x_{n_k}) = f(x_{n_k}, y_{n_k}) \to f(x, y)\) (continuity of \(f\)). Since \(y \in \phi(x)\), we have \(f(x, y) \leq V(x)\). Thus: \[\limsup_k V(x_{n_k}) = f(x, y) \leq V(x).\] Since this holds for any subsequence, \(\limsup_n V(x_n) \leq V(x)\).
Step 2: \(V\) is lower semicontinuous. Let \(x_n \to x\). We want \(\liminf_n V(x_n) \geq V(x)\).
Let \(y^* \in \phi^*(x)\), so \(V(x) = f(x, y^*)\). Since \(\phi\) is LHC at \(x\) and \(y^* \in \phi(x)\), there exist \(y_n \in \phi(x_n)\) with \(y_n \to y^*\).
Then: \(V(x_n) \geq f(x_n, y_n) \to f(x, y^*) = V(x)\) (continuity of \(f\)). So \(\liminf_n V(x_n) \geq V(x)\).
Step 3: \(V\) is continuous. From Steps 1 and 2: \(\limsup_n V(x_n) \leq V(x) \leq \liminf_n V(x_n)\). Since \(\liminf \leq \limsup\) always holds, we have equality throughout: \(\lim_n V(x_n) = V(x)\). So \(V\) is continuous (by the sequential characterization, Theorem 1.4).
Step 4: \(\phi^*\) is UHC. Let \(x_n \to x\) and \(y_n \in \phi^*(x_n)\). We want a subsequence \(y_{n_k} \to y \in \phi^*(x)\).
Since \(y_n \in \phi^*(x_n) \subseteq \phi(x_n)\) and \(\phi\) is UHC, \((y_n)\) has a subsequence \(y_{n_k} \to y \in \phi(x)\).
We check \(y \in \phi^*(x)\): \(f(x_{n_k}, y_{n_k}) = V(x_{n_k})\). Taking limits (using continuity of \(f\) and \(V\)): \(f(x, y) = V(x)\). So \(y \in \phi^*(x)\).
Thus \(\phi^*\) is UHC. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Note carefully where each hypothesis is used: - UHC of \(\phi\) is used in Steps 1 and 4 (to extract convergent subsequences from \(\phi(x_n)\)). - LHC of \(\phi\) is used in Step 2 (to track the maximizer \(y^*\) at the limit with nearby feasible points). - Compactness of \(\phi(x)\) is used to ensure existence via Weierstrass and to extract convergent subsequences. - Continuity of \(f\) is used in all steps to pass limits through \(f\).
If \(\phi\) is only UHC (not LHC), then \(V\) is only upper semicontinuous. If \(\phi\) is only LHC, then \(V\) is only lower semicontinuous. Full continuity of \(V\) requires full continuity of \(\phi\).

title: 4  1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity
text:
5.4 Economic Applications






ImportantTheorem 1.9 — Budget Correspondence is Continuous



Let \(L \geq 1\). Define the budget correspondence \(B: \mathbb{R}^L_{++} \times \mathbb{R}_{++} \rightrightarrows \mathbb{R}^L_+\) by: \[B(p, w) = \{ x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : p \cdot x \leq w \}.\] Then \(B\) is continuous (UHC and LHC) and has nonempty compact values for all \((p, w) \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++} \times \mathbb{R}_{++}\).








TipProof





Nonemptiness: \(0 \in B(p,w)\) always (since \(p \cdot 0 = 0 \leq w\)).
Compactness of \(B(p,w)\): \(B(p,w)\) is closed (intersection of the closed half-space \(\{p \cdot x \leq w\}\) and the closed set \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\)). It is bounded: for each \(\ell\), \(x_\ell \leq (p \cdot x)/p_\ell \leq w/p_\ell < \infty\) since \(p_\ell > 0\). By Heine-Borel, \(B(p,w)\) is compact.
UHC: Suppose \((p_n, w_n) \to (p, w)\) and \(x_n \in B(p_n, w_n)\). We need a subsequence of \((x_n)\) converging to a point in \(B(p,w)\).
Since \(p_n \to p \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\) and \(w_n \to w > 0\), for large enough \(n\), \(p_n \geq \frac{p}{2} \gg 0\) (componentwise) and \(w_n \leq 2w\). So \(x_{n,\ell} \leq w_n / p_{n,\ell} \leq 2w / (p_\ell/2) = 4w/p_\ell\) for all \(n\) large. Thus \((x_n)\) is eventually bounded. By Bolzano-Weierstrass, it has a convergent subsequence \(x_{n_k} \to x \geq 0\).
Check \(x \in B(p,w)\): \(p_{n_k} \cdot x_{n_k} \leq w_{n_k}\) for all \(k\); taking limits: \(p \cdot x \leq w\). Also \(x \geq 0\). So \(x \in B(p,w)\). UHC verified.
LHC: Suppose \((p_n, w_n) \to (p,w)\) and \(x \in B(p,w)\). We need \(x_n \in B(p_n, w_n)\) with \(x_n \to x\).
Case 1: \(p \cdot x < w\) (strict interior of budget set). Since \(p_n \cdot x \to p \cdot x < w\) and \(w_n \to w\), for large \(n\): \(p_n \cdot x < w_n\). Take \(x_n = x\) for all large \(n\). Then \(x_n \in B(p_n, w_n)\) and \(x_n \to x\).
Case 2: \(p \cdot x = w\) (on the budget hyperplane). Let \(e = (1, 1, \ldots, 1)\). For small \(\varepsilon > 0\), define \(x(\varepsilon) = (1-\varepsilon)x\). Then \(p \cdot x(\varepsilon) = (1-\varepsilon)w < w\), so \(x(\varepsilon)\) is in the interior of \(B(p,w)\). For fixed \(\varepsilon\), Case 1 gives \(x_n(\varepsilon) = x(\varepsilon)\) for large \(n\) with \(x_n(\varepsilon) \in B(p_n, w_n)\).
More carefully: define \(\lambda_n = \min(1, w_n / (p_n \cdot x))\) if \(p_n \cdot x > 0\) and \(\lambda_n = 1\) if \(p_n \cdot x = 0\). Set \(x_n = \lambda_n x\). Then \(p_n \cdot x_n = \lambda_n (p_n \cdot x) \leq w_n\), so \(x_n \in B(p_n, w_n)\). Moreover, \(\lambda_n = w_n / (p_n \cdot x) \to w / (p \cdot x) = 1\) (since \(p_n \to p\), \(w_n \to w\), and \(p \cdot x = w > 0\)). Thus \(x_n \to x\).
If \(x = 0\): take \(x_n = 0\) always. \(0 \in B(p_n, w_n)\) and \(0 \to 0 = x\).
In all cases, LHC is verified. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Walrasian demand correspondence. By Berge’s Maximum Theorem (Theorem 1.8), since \(u: \mathbb{R}^L_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) is continuous, \(B: \mathbb{R}^L_{++} \times \mathbb{R}_{++} \rightrightarrows \mathbb{R}^L_+\) is continuous with compact values, the Walrasian demand correspondence: \[x(p,w) = \arg\max_{x \in B(p,w)} u(x)\] is UHC with compact values, and the indirect utility function: \[v(p,w) = \max_{x \in B(p,w)} u(x)\] is continuous in \((p,w)\). This is MWG Proposition 3.D.3.

title: 4  1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity
text:
5.5 Economic Intuition
Upper hemicontinuity of demand says: if I slightly perturb prices and wealth, optimal choices cannot “jump to infinity” — any sequence of optimal choices under slightly perturbed parameters must have a subsequence that converges to an optimal choice at the limit parameters. This is the formalization of the intuition that demand is “well-behaved” as prices vary. The budget correspondence is the key link: it is UHC because the budget set cannot suddenly expand without bound (it stays bounded due to positive prices), and it is LHC because the budget set cannot suddenly collapse (a strictly affordable bundle remains strictly affordable after a small perturbation, and a boundary bundle can be approximated by interior ones).
Lower hemicontinuity is the harder condition to verify and the more restrictive one economically. It fails whenever the feasible set has a “kink” or “notch” that disappears under perturbation — for example, a quantity constraint or a non-convex budget set. In such cases, Berge’s theorem gives only upper semicontinuity of the value function. Many applied models are built on value functions that are assumed to be continuous, and verifying the LHC condition on the constraint correspondence is the key step in justifying this assumption.






NoteExample



Failure of LHC. Consider a non-linear budget constraint: \(\phi(p) = \{x \in \mathbb{R}_+ : x \leq 1/p\}\). For \(p > 0\): \(\phi(p) = [0, 1/p]\), which is nonempty and compact. At \(p \to 0^+\): \(1/p \to \infty\), so \(\phi(p)\) “explodes.” This correspondence is LHC (for any \(x \in \phi(0^+)\), we can find \(x_n \in \phi(p_n)\) with \(x_n \to x\)), but is not well-defined at \(p = 0\) since \(\phi(0)\) would be unbounded. This is why we require \(p \gg 0\) in the budget correspondence.

title: 4  1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity
text:
5.6 Cross-References

1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology — Compactness (used in Berge’s theorem for compact values and Weierstrass for existence); continuity of functions (special case of UHC).
1.3 Optimization Theory — Envelope Theorem complements Berge: Berge says \(V\) is continuous; Envelope says \(V\) is differentiable under stronger conditions.
1.4 Fixed Point Theorems — Kakutani’s theorem applies to UHC correspondences; Berge provides the UHC property needed for best-response correspondences.
Index — Budget correspondence continuity and UHC of demand are used throughout; Berge underlies Proposition 3.D.3 on continuity of demand.

title: 5  1.3 Optimization Theory
text: 6 1.3 Optimization Theory

title: 5  1.3 Optimization Theory
text:
6.1 Context
Optimization theory is the engine of microeconomics: virtually every positive result (a demand function, a supply function, a contract) is the solution to some agent’s optimization problem, and virtually every comparative statics result (how demand changes with income, how cost changes with output) follows from differentiating the optimized value with respect to a parameter. This note develops the key tools in sequence: unconstrained FOCs and SOCs, the Lagrangian method for equality constraints, the Kuhn-Tucker conditions for inequality constraints, and — most importantly for applications — the Envelope Theorem in both unconstrained and constrained versions. The Envelope Theorem is a single mathematical result with at least a dozen named corollaries in economic theory: Hotelling’s Lemma, Shephard’s Lemma, Roy’s Identity, the Slutsky equation, and the characterization of shadow prices all follow directly. The note closes with quasi-concavity, which is the minimal convexity condition needed for the consumer’s and firm’s optimization programs to be well-behaved.

title: 5  1.3 Optimization Theory
text:
6.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 1.16 — Local and Global Optima



Let \(f: U \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L \to \mathbb{R}\). - \(x^* \in U\) is a local maximum of \(f\) on \(U\) if there exists \(\varepsilon > 0\) such that \(f(x^*) \geq f(x)\) for all \(x \in B_\varepsilon(x^*) \cap U\). - \(x^*\) is a global maximum if \(f(x^*) \geq f(x)\) for all \(x \in U\). - Replace \(\geq\) with \(\leq\) for minima; replace \(\geq\) with \(>\) for strict local/global maxima.








NoteDefinition 1.17 — Concavity and Quasi-Concavity



A function \(f: C \to \mathbb{R}\) on a convex set \(C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) is: - Concave if for all \(x, y \in C\) and \(\lambda \in [0,1]\): \(f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y) \geq \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y)\). - Strictly concave if the inequality is strict for \(x \neq y\) and \(\lambda \in (0,1)\). - Quasi-concave if for all \(x, y \in C\) and \(\lambda \in [0,1]\): \(f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda)y) \geq \min\{f(x), f(y)\}\). - Strictly quasi-concave if the inequality is strict for \(x \neq y\) and \(\lambda \in (0,1)\).
Equivalently, \(f\) is quasi-concave if and only if every upper contour set \(\{x \in C : f(x) \geq t\}\) is convex for all \(t \in \mathbb{R}\).








CautionRemark



Concavity implies quasi-concavity (but not conversely). Quasi-concavity is the right condition for preferences: it says “averages are weakly preferred to extremes,” i.e., indifference curves are convex to the origin. Strictly quasi-concave utility functions yield unique demand (single-valued \(x(p,w)\)). Concavity is a stronger condition used for profit functions and cost functions (as functions of output).








NoteDefinition 1.18 — Hessian Matrix



For \(f: U \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L \to \mathbb{R}\) twice continuously differentiable, the Hessian is the \(L \times L\) symmetric matrix: \[H_f(x) = D^2 f(x) = \left( \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}(x) \right)_{i,j=1}^L.\] \(f\) is concave on \(U\) (convex \(U\)) if and only if \(H_f(x)\) is negative semidefinite for all \(x \in U\); strictly concave if \(H_f\) is negative definite everywhere.








NoteDefinition 1.19 — Lagrangian



For the constrained problem: \[\max_{x \in \mathbb{R}^L} \; f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_k(x) = 0, \; k = 1, \ldots, K,\] the Lagrangian is: \[\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = f(x) - \sum_{k=1}^K \lambda_k g_k(x),\] where \(\lambda = (\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_K) \in \mathbb{R}^K\) are Lagrange multipliers.








NoteDefinition 1.20 — Kuhn-Tucker Lagrangian



For the problem with inequality constraints: \[\max_{x \in \mathbb{R}^L} \; f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_k(x) \leq 0, \; k = 1, \ldots, K,\] the Kuhn-Tucker Lagrangian is: \[\mathcal{L}(x, \mu) = f(x) - \sum_{k=1}^K \mu_k g_k(x),\] where \(\mu_k \geq 0\) for all \(k\).

title: 5  1.3 Optimization Theory
text:
6.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 1.10 — Unconstrained First and Second-Order Conditions



Let \(f: U \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L \to \mathbb{R}\) be \(C^2\) on the open set \(U\).
(Necessity) If \(x^*\) is a local maximum, then: \[\text{(FOC)} \quad Df(x^*) = 0 \quad \text{(i.e., } \nabla f(x^*) = 0\text{)},\] \[\text{(SOC)} \quad D^2 f(x^*) \text{ is negative semidefinite.}\]
(Sufficiency) If \(Df(x^*) = 0\) and \(D^2 f(x^*)\) is negative definite, then \(x^*\) is a strict local maximum.
(Global) If \(f\) is concave on \(U\) (convex), then any local maximum is a global maximum, and any point with \(Df(x^*) = 0\) is a global maximum.








TipProof





FOC Necessity. Suppose \(x^*\) is a local maximum. For any direction \(v \in \mathbb{R}^L\), the function \(\varphi(t) = f(x^* + tv)\) achieves a local maximum at \(t=0\), so \(\varphi'(0) = Df(x^*) \cdot v = 0\). Since \(v\) is arbitrary, \(Df(x^*) = 0\).
SOC Necessity. Similarly, \(\varphi''(0) = v^T D^2 f(x^*) v \leq 0\) for all \(v\), since \(t=0\) is a local max of \(\varphi\). This is the definition of negative semidefiniteness.
Sufficiency. By Taylor’s theorem, \(f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + Df(x^*) \cdot h + \frac{1}{2} h^T D^2 f(x^*) h + o(\|h\|^2)\). If \(Df(x^*) = 0\) and \(D^2 f(x^*)\) is negative definite, then \(h^T D^2 f(x^*) h \leq -c\|h\|^2\) for some \(c > 0\), so \(f(x^*+h) \leq f(x^*) - (c/2)\|h\|^2 < f(x^*)\) for small \(\|h\| > 0\).
Global (concave case). If \(f\) is concave and \(x^*\) is a local maximum, suppose \(f(y) > f(x^*)\) for some \(y\). For \(\lambda \in (0,1)\) small, \(f(\lambda y + (1-\lambda) x^*) \geq \lambda f(y) + (1-\lambda)f(x^*) > f(x^*)\), and \(\lambda y + (1-\lambda)x^*\) is arbitrarily close to \(x^*\) for small \(\lambda\), contradicting local maximality. \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 1.11 — Lagrange’s Theorem (Equality-Constrained FOCs)



Let \(f: \mathbb{R}^L \to \mathbb{R}\) and \(g: \mathbb{R}^L \to \mathbb{R}^K\) be \(C^1\). Suppose \(x^*\) is a local maximum of \(f\) subject to \(g(x) = 0\), and suppose the constraint qualification holds at \(x^*\): the Jacobian \(Dg(x^*) \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times L}\) has full row rank (rank \(K\)). Then there exists a unique \(\lambda^* \in \mathbb{R}^K\) such that: \[D_x \mathcal{L}(x^*, \lambda^*) = Df(x^*) - \lambda^{*T} Dg(x^*) = 0.\] Equivalently: \[\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_\ell}(x^*) = \sum_{k=1}^K \lambda^*_k \frac{\partial g_k}{\partial x_\ell}(x^*), \quad \ell = 1, \ldots, L.\]








TipProof





Step 1: Characterize the tangent space of the constraint set. The constraint set is \(S = \{x : g(x) = 0\}\). The tangent space of \(S\) at \(x^*\) is \(T_{x^*} S = \ker(Dg(x^*)) = \{v \in \mathbb{R}^L : Dg(x^*) v = 0\}\).
Step 2: \(Df(x^*)\) must annihilate \(T_{x^*} S\). If \(v \in T_{x^*} S\), let \(\gamma: (-\varepsilon, \varepsilon) \to S\) be a \(C^1\) curve with \(\gamma(0) = x^*\) and \(\gamma'(0) = v\). Since \(x^*\) is a local max of \(f\) on \(S\), \(t = 0\) is a local max of \(f \circ \gamma\), so \((f \circ \gamma)'(0) = Df(x^*) \cdot v = 0\). (Such a curve exists by the implicit function theorem, which applies because \(Dg(x^*)\) has full rank.)
Step 3: Invoke the fundamental theorem of linear algebra. We have \(Df(x^*) \in (\ker Dg(x^*))^\perp = \text{rowspace}(Dg(x^*))\).
Therefore \(Df(x^*)\) is a linear combination of the rows of \(Dg(x^*)\): \(\exists \lambda^* \in \mathbb{R}^K\) such that \(Df(x^*) = \lambda^{*T} Dg(x^*)\).
Step 4: Uniqueness of \(\lambda^*\). Since \(Dg(x^*)\) has full row rank \(K\), the rows are linearly independent, so \(\lambda^*\) is unique. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



The Lagrange multiplier \(\lambda_k^*\) has the interpretation of a shadow price: it measures how much the optimal value \(f(x^*)\) would increase if constraint \(k\) were relaxed from \(g_k(x) = 0\) to \(g_k(x) = \varepsilon\) (by \(\varepsilon \lambda_k^*\) to first order). This interpretation is made precise by the Envelope Theorem (Theorem 1.13 below).








ImportantTheorem 1.12 — Kuhn-Tucker Necessary Conditions



Let \(f: \mathbb{R}^L \to \mathbb{R}\) and \(g_k: \mathbb{R}^L \to \mathbb{R}\) for \(k = 1, \ldots, K\) be \(C^1\). Consider: \[\max_{x \in \mathbb{R}^L} f(x) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_k(x) \leq 0, \; k = 1, \ldots, K.\] Let \(x^*\) be a local maximum and suppose a constraint qualification holds at \(x^*\) (e.g., LICQ: the gradients \(\nabla g_k(x^*)\) for active constraints \(k \in A(x^*) = \{k : g_k(x^*) = 0\}\) are linearly independent). Then there exist multipliers \(\mu^* = (\mu^*_1, \ldots, \mu^*_K) \geq 0\) such that:
(Stationarity) \(\displaystyle \nabla f(x^*) = \sum_{k=1}^K \mu_k^* \nabla g_k(x^*)\)
(Primal Feasibility) \(g_k(x^*) \leq 0\) for all \(k\)
(Dual Feasibility) \(\mu_k^* \geq 0\) for all \(k\)
(Complementary Slackness) \(\mu_k^* g_k(x^*) = 0\) for all \(k\).








TipProof





The active constraints \(g_k(x^*) = 0\) for \(k \in A(x^*)\) are equality constraints at \(x^*\); the inactive constraints \(g_k(x^*) < 0\) for \(k \notin A(x^*)\) are locally nonbinding and can be ignored. Apply Lagrange’s Theorem (Theorem 1.11) to \(f\) subject to the active equality constraints, obtaining multipliers \(\mu_k^*\) for \(k \in A(x^*)\). Set \(\mu_k^* = 0\) for \(k \notin A(x^*)\) (this enforces complementary slackness automatically for inactive constraints).
The sign condition \(\mu_k^* \geq 0\) follows from the observation that the active constraints enter as \(g_k(x^*) \leq 0\): relaxing a binding \(\leq\)-constraint (increasing the right-hand side from 0 to \(\varepsilon > 0\)) cannot decrease the maximum (more feasibility only helps), so the shadow price of a binding \(\leq\)-constraint must be non-negative. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Complementary slackness says: either a constraint is binding (\(g_k(x^*) = 0\)) or its multiplier is zero (\(\mu_k^* = 0\)), or both. In consumer theory: the budget constraint is almost always binding at the optimum (by local non-satiation), so \(\mu > 0\); the non-negativity constraint \(x_\ell \geq 0\) may be binding (if good \(\ell\) is not consumed) with \(\mu_\ell^*\) equal to the imputed value of the good, or non-binding with \(\mu_\ell^* = 0\).








ImportantTheorem 1.13 — Sufficiency Under Convexity



Suppose \(f\) is concave and each \(g_k\) is convex. If \((x^*, \mu^*)\) satisfies the KKT conditions, then \(x^*\) is a global maximum.








TipProof





For any feasible \(x\) (with \(g_k(x) \leq 0\)): \[f(x) - f(x^*) \leq Df(x^*) \cdot (x - x^*) \quad (\text{concavity of } f)\] \[= \sum_k \mu_k^* \nabla g_k(x^*) \cdot (x - x^*) \quad (\text{stationarity})\] \[\leq \sum_k \mu_k^* [g_k(x) - g_k(x^*)] \quad (\text{convexity of each } g_k: g_k(x) \geq g_k(x^*) + \nabla g_k(x^*) \cdot (x-x^*))\] \[= \sum_k \mu_k^* g_k(x) - \sum_k \mu_k^* g_k(x^*)\] \[\leq \sum_k \mu_k^* \cdot 0 - \sum_k \mu_k^* g_k(x^*) \quad (\text{since } g_k(x) \leq 0 \text{ and } \mu_k^* \geq 0)\] \[= -\sum_k \mu_k^* g_k(x^*) = 0 \quad (\text{complementary slackness: } \mu_k^* g_k(x^*) = 0).\] So \(f(x) \leq f(x^*)\) for all feasible \(x\). \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 1.14 — Envelope Theorem (Unconstrained Version)



Let \(f: \mathbb{R}^L \times \mathbb{R}^M \to \mathbb{R}\) be \(C^1\) in \((x, \alpha)\) where \(x\) is the choice variable and \(\alpha \in \mathbb{R}^M\) is a parameter vector. Define the value function: \[V(\alpha) = \max_{x \in \mathbb{R}^L} f(x, \alpha),\] and suppose that for each \(\alpha\), the maximizer \(x^*(\alpha)\) is unique and varies \(C^1\) with \(\alpha\). Then \(V\) is differentiable and: \[\frac{\partial V}{\partial \alpha_m}(\alpha) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \alpha_m}(x^*(\alpha), \alpha) \quad \text{for each } m = 1, \ldots, M.\]








TipProof





Differentiate \(V(\alpha) = f(x^*(\alpha), \alpha)\) with respect to \(\alpha_m\) using the chain rule: \[\frac{dV}{d\alpha_m} = \sum_{\ell=1}^L \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_\ell}(x^*, \alpha) \frac{\partial x^*_\ell}{\partial \alpha_m} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial \alpha_m}(x^*, \alpha).\] By the FOC for an interior maximum: \(\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_\ell}(x^*(\alpha), \alpha) = 0\) for all \(\ell\). Therefore the first sum vanishes and: \[\frac{dV}{d\alpha_m} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \alpha_m}(x^*(\alpha), \alpha). \qquad \blacksquare\]









CautionRemark



The key insight is that we can ignore the indirect effect of \(\alpha\) on \(V\) through the optimal choice \(x^*(\alpha)\), because at the optimum the FOC guarantees that a marginal change in \(x\) has zero first-order effect on \(f\). Only the direct effect of \(\alpha\) on \(f\) matters for the derivative of \(V\).








ImportantTheorem 1.15 — Envelope Theorem (Constrained Version, Theorem M.L.1)



Consider the parameterized constrained problem: \[V(\alpha) = \max_{x} f(x, \alpha) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_k(x, \alpha) = 0, \; k = 1, \ldots, K.\] Let \(\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda, \alpha) = f(x, \alpha) - \sum_k \lambda_k g_k(x, \alpha)\). Suppose \(x^*(\alpha)\) and \(\lambda^*(\alpha)\) are \(C^1\) and satisfy the FOCs for all \(\alpha\) near \(\alpha_0\). Then \(V\) is differentiable at \(\alpha_0\) and: \[\frac{\partial V}{\partial \alpha_m}(\alpha_0) = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \alpha_m}(x^*(\alpha_0), \lambda^*(\alpha_0), \alpha_0) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \alpha_m}(x^*, \alpha_0) - \sum_{k=1}^K \lambda_k^* \frac{\partial g_k}{\partial \alpha_m}(x^*, \alpha_0).\]








TipProof





Step 1: Express \(V\) in terms of the Lagrangian at the optimum. At the optimum, all constraints hold: \(g_k(x^*(\alpha), \alpha) = 0\). Therefore: \[V(\alpha) = f(x^*(\alpha), \alpha) = \mathcal{L}(x^*(\alpha), \lambda^*(\alpha), \alpha)\] (since the \(\lambda_k g_k\) terms vanish at the constraint).
Step 2: Differentiate with respect to \(\alpha_m\). \[\frac{dV}{d\alpha_m} = \frac{d}{d\alpha_m} \mathcal{L}(x^*(\alpha), \lambda^*(\alpha), \alpha)\] \[= \underbrace{\sum_\ell \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_\ell} \frac{\partial x^*_\ell}{\partial \alpha_m}}_{=0 \text{ by FOC}} + \underbrace{\sum_k \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda_k} \frac{\partial \lambda^*_k}{\partial \alpha_m}}_{=0 \text{ since } \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \lambda_k = -g_k = 0} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \alpha_m}.\] Explanation of zero terms: - \(\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial x_\ell = 0\) at \((x^*, \lambda^*)\) by the Lagrange FOC. - \(\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial \lambda_k = -g_k(x^*, \alpha) = 0\) because the constraints hold at the optimum.
Therefore: \(\displaystyle\frac{dV}{d\alpha_m} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \alpha_m}(x^*, \lambda^*, \alpha) = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \alpha_m}(x^*, \alpha) - \sum_k \lambda_k^* \frac{\partial g_k}{\partial \alpha_m}(x^*, \alpha).\) \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Economic applications of the Envelope Theorem:

Hotelling’s Lemma: Let \(\pi(p) = \max_y \{p \cdot y : y \in Y\}\) be the profit function. The parameter is \(p_\ell\), the objective is \(\sum_j p_j y_j\), and there is no parameter-dependent constraint. By the unconstrained Envelope Theorem: \(\frac{\partial \pi}{\partial p_\ell} = y_\ell^*(p)\) — the supply of good \(\ell\) equals the derivative of the profit function.
Shephard’s Lemma: Let \(c(w, q) = \min_z \{w \cdot z : f(z) \geq q\}\). Treating the minimization as \(-c(w,q) = \max_z \{-w \cdot z : f(z) \geq q\}\) with parameter \(w_\ell\): \(\frac{\partial c}{\partial w_\ell} = z_\ell^*(w,q)\) — the conditional factor demand equals the derivative of the cost function.
Roy’s Identity: Let \(v(p,w) = \max_{p \cdot x \leq w} u(x)\). The budget constraint can be written \(p \cdot x - w = 0\); the Lagrangian is \(\mathcal{L} = u(x) - \lambda(p \cdot x - w)\). By the constrained Envelope Theorem: \(\frac{\partial v}{\partial p_\ell} = -\lambda^* x_\ell^*(p,w)\) and \(\frac{\partial v}{\partial w} = \lambda^*\). Dividing: \(x_\ell^*(p,w) = -\frac{\partial v / \partial p_\ell}{\partial v / \partial w}\) — Roy’s Identity.

title: 5  1.3 Optimization Theory
text:
6.4 Economic Intuition
The central insight of the Envelope Theorem is the principle of “optimized-out” indirect effects. When an agent is already at an optimum, a small change in a parameter can affect welfare both directly (the new parameter changes the objective function for fixed choices) and indirectly (the agent re-optimizes). But at an optimum, the first-order effect of re-optimizing is exactly zero — the agent’s FOC says that small changes in the choice variable have zero first-order effect on the objective. This is why only the direct effect survives in the Envelope Theorem.
This has a profound practical implication for model-building: to derive how a value function responds to a parameter (e.g., how welfare responds to a price change, how cost responds to a wage change), you do not need to solve for how optimal choices respond to the parameter — you only need to differentiate the objective function directly with respect to the parameter, holding optimal choices fixed. This dramatically simplifies comparative statics.
The Kuhn-Tucker multipliers have a clean economic interpretation via the Envelope Theorem: \(\lambda_k^*\) is the marginal value (in terms of the objective function) of relaxing constraint \(k\) by one unit. In consumer theory, the multiplier on the budget constraint is the marginal utility of wealth \(\lambda^* = \partial v / \partial w\): how much utility increases per unit of additional wealth. This allows the shadow price interpretation of Lagrange multipliers to be made rigorous.






NoteExample



CES expenditure function. Let \(u(x_1, x_2) = (x_1^\rho + x_2^\rho)^{1/\rho}\) with \(\rho \leq 1\), \(\rho \neq 0\) (CES utility with elasticity of substitution \(\sigma = 1/(1-\rho)\)). The expenditure function is \(e(p,u) = u \cdot (p_1^{\rho/(\rho-1)} + p_2^{\rho/(\rho-1)})^{(\rho-1)/\rho}\). By Shephard’s Lemma (Envelope Theorem applied to the EMP): \[h_1(p,u) = \frac{\partial e}{\partial p_1} = u \cdot \frac{p_1^{1/(\rho-1)}}{(p_1^{\rho/(\rho-1)} + p_2^{\rho/(\rho-1)})^{1/\rho}},\] which is the Hicksian demand for good 1. Note: this required only differentiating \(e\), not re-solving the EMP.

title: 5  1.3 Optimization Theory
text:
6.5 Cross-References

1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology — Weierstrass (Theorem 1.5) guarantees existence of optima; quasi-concavity of preferences relates to convex upper contour sets defined using the topology here.
1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity — Berge’s Theorem (Theorem 1.8) gives continuity of \(V\); the Envelope Theorem gives differentiability of \(V\) under stronger assumptions.
1.4 Fixed Point Theorems — KKT conditions characterize fixed points of best-response maps in game theory.
Index — Roy’s Identity, Slutsky equation, and duality (UMP/EMP) all follow from Envelope Theorem applications.
Index — Hotelling’s Lemma and Shephard’s Lemma are direct Envelope Theorem applications.

title: 6  1.4 Fixed-Point Theorems
text: 7 1.4 Fixed-Point Theorems

title: 6  1.4 Fixed-Point Theorems
text:
7.1 Context
A fixed point of a function or correspondence \(\phi: C \to C\) (or \(\phi: C \rightrightarrows C\)) is a point \(x^*\) such that \(x^* \in \phi(x^*)\). The importance of this concept for microeconomics cannot be overstated: virtually every notion of economic equilibrium — Walrasian equilibrium, Nash equilibrium, competitive equilibrium with production, and optimal mechanisms — can be reformulated as the fixed point of some suitably constructed map. The two central results are Brouwer’s Fixed-Point Theorem (for continuous functions) and its generalization, Kakutani’s Fixed-Point Theorem (for UHC correspondences). Both require the domain to be nonempty, compact, and convex — conditions whose economic content is discussed at length below. This note proves Brouwer’s theorem via Sperner’s lemma, derives Kakutani from Brouwer using an approximation argument, and then applies both to preview the existence proofs for Nash equilibrium and Walrasian equilibrium that will be completed in Parts V and VII.

title: 6  1.4 Fixed-Point Theorems
text:
7.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 1.21 — Fixed Point



Let \(C\) be a nonempty set and \(\phi: C \rightrightarrows C\) a correspondence (or \(f: C \to C\) a function). A point \(x^* \in C\) is a fixed point of \(\phi\) (resp. \(f\)) if \(x^* \in \phi(x^*)\) (resp. \(x^* = f(x^*)\)).








NoteDefinition 1.22 — Simplex



The standard \(n\)-simplex is: \[\Delta^n = \left\{ x \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1}_+ : \sum_{i=0}^n x_i = 1 \right\}.\] More generally, given \(n+1\) affinely independent points (vertices) \(v^0, v^1, \ldots, v^n \in \mathbb{R}^L\), the simplex with these vertices is \(\text{co}(\{v^0, \ldots, v^n\})\) (their convex hull). A simplicial subdivision (triangulation) of a simplex \(\Delta\) is a decomposition of \(\Delta\) into smaller simplices that fit together face-to-face.








NoteDefinition 1.23 — Sperner Labeling



Let \(\Delta^n\) be subdivided into smaller simplices, and let the vertices of the subdivision be labeled with integers in \(\{0, 1, \ldots, n\}\). The labeling is a Sperner labeling if: 1. Vertex \(v^i\) of the original simplex (for \(i = 0, 1, \ldots, n\)) receives label \(i\). 2. Any vertex lying on the face \(F_I = \text{co}(\{v^i : i \in I\})\) (for \(I \subseteq \{0, \ldots, n\}\)) receives a label in \(I\). A small simplex in the subdivision is completely labeled if its \(n+1\) vertices carry all \(n+1\) labels \(0, 1, \ldots, n\).

title: 6  1.4 Fixed-Point Theorems
text:
7.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 1.16 — Sperner’s Lemma



For any Sperner labeling of any simplicial subdivision of \(\Delta^n\), the number of completely labeled simplices is odd (in particular, at least one completely labeled simplex exists).








TipProof





(For \(n = 2\); the general case is analogous.) Consider a triangulation of \(\Delta^2\) with a Sperner labeling. Count ordered pairs \((T, e)\) where \(T\) is a small triangle and \(e\) is an edge of \(T\) carrying labels \(\{0, 1\}\). Count this in two ways:

From the inside (triangles): A completely labeled triangle (labels \(\{0,1,2\}\)) has exactly one \(\{0,1\}\)-edge. A triangle with labels \(\{0,1,1\}\) or \(\{0,0,1\}\) has exactly two \(\{0,1\}\)-edges. All other triangles have zero. So the count equals \((\text{number of completely labeled triangles}) + 2(\text{number of triangles with exactly two }\{0,1\}\text{-edges})\).
From the boundary: An edge on the boundary of \(\Delta^2\) can be a \(\{0,1\}\)-edge only if it lies on the face connecting \(v^0\) and \(v^1\) (since by Sperner’s condition, boundary vertices have restricted labels). Count the \(\{0,1\}\)-edges on the \(v^0\)-to-\(v^1\) face of \(\Delta^2\): these are the boundary edges with one endpoint labeled 0 and one labeled 1. Starting at \(v^0\) (label 0) and walking to \(v^1\) (label 1) along the subdivision, the label must change from 0 to 1 an odd number of times net. So the number of boundary \(\{0,1\}\)-edges is odd.

An interior \(\{0,1\}\)-edge is shared by exactly two triangles (counted twice); a boundary \(\{0,1\}\)-edge is counted once. So the total count is: \[(\text{boundary count, odd}) + 2(\text{interior } \{0,1\}\text{-edges}).\] This equals the sum from triangles: \((\text{completely labeled}) + 2(\text{two-edge triangles})\). Reducing modulo 2: the number of completely labeled triangles is congruent to the boundary count (odd), hence odd. \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 1.17 — Brouwer’s Fixed-Point Theorem



Let \(C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) be nonempty, compact, and convex, and let \(f: C \to C\) be continuous. Then \(f\) has a fixed point: there exists \(x^* \in C\) with \(f(x^*) = x^*\).








TipProof





It suffices to prove the theorem for \(C = \Delta^n\) (the standard \(n\)-simplex), since any nonempty compact convex set in \(\mathbb{R}^L\) is homeomorphic to a simplex of appropriate dimension, and homeomorphisms preserve fixed-point properties.
Step 1: Construct Sperner labelings on finer and finer subdivisions. For each \(m \in \mathbb{N}\), consider the \(m\)-th barycentric subdivision \(\mathcal{T}_m\) of \(\Delta^n\) (mesh size \(\sim 1/m\)). For each vertex \(v\) of \(\mathcal{T}_m\), assign a label \(\ell(v) \in \{0, 1, \ldots, n\}\) as follows: write \(v = (v_0, \ldots, v_n) \in \Delta^n\) (barycentric coordinates). Define: \[\ell(v) = \min \{ i \in \{0, \ldots, n\} : f(v)_i \leq v_i \}.\] (Here \(f(v)_i\) is the \(i\)-th component of \(f(v)\).) This is well-defined because \(f: \Delta^n \to \Delta^n\) satisfies \(\sum_i f(v)_i = 1 = \sum_i v_i\), so at least one \(i\) must have \(f(v)_i \leq v_i\).
Sperner condition: One verifies (exercise) that this labeling satisfies the Sperner condition. In particular, \(v^i\) has \(v^i_i = 1\) and \(v^i_j = 0\) for \(j \neq i\); since \(f(v^i)_i \leq 1 = v^i_i\), label \(i\) is assigned. On a face where some coordinates are zero, the label is always an index in that face.
Step 2: By Sperner’s Lemma, each \(\mathcal{T}_m\) has a completely labeled simplex \(S_m\). Choose one such completely labeled simplex \(S_m\) for each \(m\). Let \(x^{(m)}\) be a vertex of \(S_m\).
Step 3: Extract a convergent subsequence. Since all \(x^{(m)} \in \Delta^n\) (compact), by Bolzano-Weierstrass there is a convergent subsequence \(x^{(m_k)} \to x^* \in \Delta^n\).
Step 4: Show \(x^*\) is a fixed point. The mesh of \(\mathcal{T}_m \to 0\), so all vertices of \(S_{m_k}\) converge to \(x^*\). For each \(i\), the \(i\)-labeled vertex \(v^i_{m_k}\) of \(S_{m_k}\) also converges to \(x^*\) (since the simplex diameter \(\to 0\)). By definition of the labeling: \(f(v^i_{m_k})_i \leq (v^i_{m_k})_i\).
Taking \(k \to \infty\) (using continuity of \(f\)): \(f(x^*)_i \leq x^*_i\) for all \(i = 0, \ldots, n\).
But \(\sum_i f(x^*)_i = 1 = \sum_i x^*_i\), so we cannot have \(f(x^*)_i < x^*_i\) for any \(i\) (as that would make the sum strictly less than \(\sum_i x^*_i = 1\)). Therefore \(f(x^*)_i = x^*_i\) for all \(i\), i.e., \(f(x^*) = x^*\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Why each hypothesis is necessary: - Compactness (boundedness): \(f(x) = x + 1\) on \(\mathbb{R}\): no fixed point. - Compactness (closedness): \(f(x) = x/2 + 1/2\) on \((0,1)\): fixed point at \(x=1\) is not in the domain. - Convexity: The map \(f: S^1 \to S^1\) (rotation by \(\pi\) on the unit circle) has no fixed point. The circle is compact but not convex. - Continuity: \(f: [0,1] \to [0,1]\) with \(f(x) = 1\) for \(x \in [0, 1/2)\) and \(f(x) = 0\) for \(x \in [1/2, 1]\): no fixed point.








ImportantTheorem 1.18 — Kakutani’s Fixed-Point Theorem



Let \(C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) be nonempty, compact, and convex, and let \(\phi: C \rightrightarrows C\) be a correspondence satisfying: 1. \(\phi(x)\) is nonempty and convex for all \(x \in C\). 2. \(\phi\) is upper hemicontinuous.
Then \(\phi\) has a fixed point: there exists \(x^* \in C\) with \(x^* \in \phi(x^*)\).








TipProof





Strategy: Approximate \(\phi\) by a sequence of continuous functions, apply Brouwer to each, and pass to the limit.
Step 1: Construct continuous approximations. For each \(m \in \mathbb{N}\), consider the \(m\)-th barycentric subdivision \(\mathcal{T}_m\) of a simplex \(\Delta\) containing \(C\) (we may take \(C = \Delta\) by convexity). For each vertex \(v\) of \(\mathcal{T}_m\), pick any \(y_v \in \phi(v)\) (nonemptiness). Define the piecewise-linear approximation \(f_m: \Delta \to \Delta\) by barycentric interpolation: for a point \(x\) in a simplex \([v_0, \ldots, v_n]\) of \(\mathcal{T}_m\) with barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0, \ldots, \lambda_n\): \[f_m(x) = \sum_{i=0}^n \lambda_i y_{v_i}.\] Since \(y_{v_i} \in \phi(v_i) \subseteq C\) and \(C\) is convex, \(f_m(x) \in C\). Also \(f_m\) is continuous (piecewise linear). So \(f_m: C \to C\) is a continuous function from a convex compact set to itself.
Step 2: Apply Brouwer. By Brouwer’s theorem (Theorem 1.17), each \(f_m\) has a fixed point \(x_m \in C\): \(f_m(x_m) = x_m\).
Step 3: Extract convergent subsequence. Since \(C\) is compact, \((x_m)\) has a convergent subsequence \(x_{m_k} \to x^* \in C\).
Step 4: Show \(x^* \in \phi(x^*)\). By construction, \(x_{m_k} = f_{m_k}(x_{m_k}) = \sum_i \lambda^{(k)}_i y_{v^{(k)}_i}\), where \(v^{(k)}_0, \ldots, v^{(k)}_n\) are the vertices of the simplex of \(\mathcal{T}_{m_k}\) containing \(x_{m_k}\), and \(y_{v^{(k)}_i} \in \phi(v^{(k)}_i)\).
As \(k \to \infty\), the mesh size \(\to 0\), so all \(v^{(k)}_i \to x^*\). Consider the sequence \(y_{v^{(k)}_i} \in \phi(v^{(k)}_i)\). Since \(C\) is compact, passing to a further subsequence, \(y_{v^{(k)}_i} \to y_i\) for some \(y_i\). By UHC of \(\phi\) and \(v^{(k)}_i \to x^*\): \(y_i \in \phi(x^*)\).
Then \(x_{m_k} = \sum_i \lambda^{(k)}_i y_{v^{(k)}_i} \to \sum_i \bar{\lambda}_i y_i\) for some convex weights \(\bar{\lambda}_i \geq 0\), \(\sum_i \bar{\lambda}_i = 1\). But \(x_{m_k} \to x^*\), so \(x^* = \sum_i \bar{\lambda}_i y_i\). Since each \(y_i \in \phi(x^*)\) and \(\phi(x^*)\) is convex, \(x^* \in \phi(x^*)\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Why convexity of \(\phi(x)\) is necessary: If the correspondence has non-convex values, the argument in Step 4 fails: a convex combination of elements of \(\phi(x^*)\) need not be in \(\phi(x^*)\). Economically: non-convex best-response correspondences (e.g., from non-quasi-concave payoffs) may fail to have fixed points — Nash equilibria in pure strategies may not exist.

title: 6  1.4 Fixed-Point Theorems
text:
7.4 Economic Applications






ImportantTheorem 1.19 — Nash Equilibrium Existence (Preview)



Informal statement. For any finite normal-form game with mixed strategy extensions, the best-response correspondence has the properties required by Kakutani’s theorem, and therefore a Nash equilibrium (in mixed strategies) exists.
Formal setup (to be completed in Part VII). Let \(I = \{1, \ldots, N\}\) be the set of players. For each \(i\), let \(S_i\) be a finite set of pure strategies, and let \(\Sigma_i = \Delta(S_i)\) be the set of mixed strategies (probability distributions over \(S_i\)). The mixed strategy space \(\Sigma = \prod_i \Sigma_i\) is a product of simplices, hence compact and convex. For player \(i\), the expected payoff function \(U_i: \Sigma \to \mathbb{R}\) is multilinear (hence continuous). The best-response correspondence is: \[\beta_i(\sigma_{-i}) = \arg\max_{\sigma_i \in \Sigma_i} U_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i}).\] The joint best-response correspondence is \(\beta: \Sigma \rightrightarrows \Sigma\) with \(\beta(\sigma) = \prod_i \beta_i(\sigma_{-i})\).
Why Kakutani applies: 1. \(\Sigma\) is compact and convex. 2. \(\beta(\sigma)\) is nonempty (by Weierstrass, since \(\Sigma_i\) is compact and \(U_i\) is continuous) and convex (since \(U_i\) is linear in \(\sigma_i\), the argmax over a linear functional on a convex set is convex). 3. \(\beta\) is UHC (by Berge’s Maximum Theorem, Theorem 1.8, applied to \(U_i\) and \(\Sigma_i\)).
By Kakutani, \(\exists \sigma^* \in \beta(\sigma^*)\), i.e., \(\sigma^*_i \in \beta_i(\sigma^*_{-i})\) for all \(i\): a Nash equilibrium.








ImportantTheorem 1.20 — Walrasian Equilibrium Existence (Preview)



Informal statement. Under standard assumptions on preferences and endowments, the excess demand correspondence has the properties required by Kakutani’s theorem (applied to a fixed-point argument on the price simplex), and therefore a Walrasian equilibrium exists.
Formal setup (to be completed in Part V). The argument (following MWG Theorem 17.C.1) proceeds as follows:

Restrict attention to prices in the unit simplex \(\Delta^{L-1} = \{p \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : \sum_\ell p_\ell = 1\}\) (compact, convex).
Define the aggregate excess demand correspondence \(z: \Delta^{L-1}_{++} \rightrightarrows \mathbb{R}^L\) by \(z(p) = \sum_i x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i) - \sum_i \omega_i\).
Define the price-adjustment correspondence (tatônnement) \(T: \Delta^{L-1} \rightrightarrows \Delta^{L-1}\) by: \[T(p) = \arg\max_{q \in \Delta^{L-1}} q \cdot \bar{z}(p),\] where \(\bar{z}(p)\) is the mean excess demand. This picks out prices that maximize the “value” of excess demand — the tatônnement response to disequilibrium.
A fixed point of \(T\) (i.e., \(p^* \in T(p^*)\)) is a Walrasian equilibrium: \(p^*\) maximizes \(q \cdot z(p^*)\) at \(q = p^*\), which (by Walras’ law and other standard steps) implies \(z(p^*) \leq 0\) (market clearing or free-disposal condition).
Kakutani applies to \(T\) because: \(\Delta^{L-1}\) is compact and convex; \(T(p)\) is nonempty and convex (argmax of a linear function over a compact convex set); \(T\) is UHC (by Berge, since \(z\) is continuous under regularity conditions on preferences).

The full proof and all regularity conditions are given in Index.

title: 6  1.4 Fixed-Point Theorems
text:
7.5 Economic Intuition
The economic content of a fixed-point theorem is the concept of self-consistency. An equilibrium is not simply a solution to an agent’s optimization problem — it is a state where every agent is optimizing given what every other agent is doing, and these decisions are mutually consistent. This mutual consistency is exactly what a fixed point captures: \(x^*\) is a fixed point of the aggregate response map if and only if, when all agents believe the outcome will be \(x^*\), their best responses collectively generate exactly \(x^*\).
Brouwer’s theorem guarantees existence of such self-consistent states whenever the aggregate response map is continuous — that is, whenever small changes in the “guessed equilibrium” lead to small changes in the “best-response outcome.” Kakutani extends this to allow for multi-valued best responses (indifferences, mixed strategies, boundary optima), requiring only that the correspondence of best responses is UHC with convex values. The convexity of values — the requirement that players can mix between optimal responses — is the key condition that rules out non-existence of equilibrium in pure strategies.
The compactness condition is about preventing equilibrium candidates from escaping to infinity. In game theory, restricting to mixed strategies (probabilities) automatically provides a compact strategy space. In general equilibrium, one normalizes prices to the simplex to achieve compactness. In mechanism design, one restricts messages to compact sets. In every case, the same principle applies: make the domain compact to ensure the fixed-point theorem applies.

title: 6  1.4 Fixed-Point Theorems
text:
7.6 Cross-References

1.1 Metric Spaces and Topology — Compactness and convexity are the domain conditions required by both Brouwer and Kakutani; defined and studied in Note 1.1.
1.2 Correspondences and Hemicontinuity — UHC (required for Kakutani) and Berge’s Maximum Theorem (which provides UHC of best-response correspondences) are from Note 1.2.
1.3 Optimization Theory — KKT conditions characterize the best-response map whose fixed points are Nash equilibria.
Index — Full proof of Walrasian equilibrium existence via Kakutani (Theorem 17.C.1 in MWG).
Index — Full proof of Nash equilibrium existence in finite games via Kakutani.

title: 7  Part II — Consumer Theory: Index
text: 8 Part II — Consumer Theory: Index
crumbs: 7  Part II — Consumer Theory: Index

title: 7  Part II — Consumer Theory: Index
text:
8.1 Overview
Consumer theory provides the micro-foundations for demand, which is the backbone of most empirical structural models in industrial organization, trade, public finance, and macroeconomics. Beginning from primitive assumptions on preferences — orderings over consumption bundles — the theory derives demand functions that are coherent (internally consistent), testable (satisfying observable restrictions such as Slutsky symmetry), and welfare-relevant (supporting exact measures of welfare change via compensating and equivalent variation). At its core, consumer theory is an exercise in constrained optimization: a rational agent maximizes a utility function subject to a budget constraint, yielding Walrasian demand; dually, a cost-minimizing agent minimizes expenditure to reach a utility target, yielding Hicksian demand. The duality between these two problems — formalized through the Slutsky equation — is perhaps the single most productive piece of mathematical structure in all of microeconomics, linking the observable Walrasian demand system to the welfare-theoretically primitive Hicksian system. Extensions to revealed preference theory show that the theory is empirically meaningful (Afriat’s theorem), while aggregation theory (Gorman) identifies the precise conditions under which heterogeneous consumers can be aggregated into a representative agent.
crumbs: 7  Part II — Consumer Theory: Index

title: 7  Part II — Consumer Theory: Index
section: 8.2 The Two Fundamental Problems and Their Duality
text:
8.2 The Two Fundamental Problems and Their Duality
Utility Maximization Problem (UMP): \[\max_{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+} u(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad p \cdot x \leq w\] Solution: Walrasian (Marshallian) demand \(x(p,w)\); value function: indirect utility \(v(p,w)\).
Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP): \[\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+} p \cdot x \quad \text{subject to} \quad u(x) \geq \bar{u}\] Solution: Hicksian (compensated) demand \(h(p,\bar{u})\); value function: expenditure function \(e(p,\bar{u})\).
Duality: The two problems are dual to each other in the sense that: \[e(p, v(p,w)) = w, \qquad v(p, e(p,\bar{u})) = \bar{u}\] \[x(p,w) = h(p,v(p,w)), \qquad h(p,\bar{u}) = x(p, e(p,\bar{u}))\]
These identities allow us to move freely between Walrasian and Hicksian objects, and underlie the Slutsky decomposition of price effects into substitution and income components.
