title: 7  Part II — Consumer Theory: Index
text:
8.4 Key Results Summary
Walras’ Law: For any locally nonsatiated preference, \(p \cdot x(p,w) = w\) for all \((p,w)\). The consumer always exhausts the budget.
Roy’s Identity: Given a differentiable indirect utility function \(v(p,w)\): \[x_l(p,w) = -\frac{\partial v(p,w)/\partial p_l}{\partial v(p,w)/\partial w}\] This allows recovery of Walrasian demand from the indirect utility function.
Shephard’s Lemma: Given a differentiable expenditure function \(e(p,\bar{u})\): \[h_l(p,\bar{u}) = \frac{\partial e(p,\bar{u})}{\partial p_l}\] This allows recovery of Hicksian demand from the expenditure function.
Slutsky Symmetry: The matrix of compensated price effects \(S_{lk} = \partial h_l / \partial p_k\) is symmetric and negative semidefinite. Symmetry is the key testable restriction on observed demand systems.
Gorman Aggregation: Aggregate demand can be written as a function of aggregate wealth (independently of its distribution) if and only if all individual indirect utility functions take the Gorman polar form \(v^i(p,w^i) = [w^i - a^i(p)]/b(p)\), implying linear Engel curves with common slopes \(b(p)\).
Sonnenschein-Mantel-Debreu (SMD) Theorem: When Gorman conditions fail, essentially any continuous function satisfying Walras’ Law and homogeneity of degree zero can arise as aggregate excess demand. Aggregation imposes almost no restrictions beyond those individual demands already satisfy.

title: 8  2.1 — Preference Relations
text: 9 2.1 — Preference Relations

title: 8  2.1 — Preference Relations
text:
9.1 Context
The primitive object of consumer theory is a preference relation — a binary relation on a consumption set that captures how an agent ranks bundles of goods. Rather than assuming utility functions directly, we derive them from preference axioms. This approach clarifies which properties of utility are structural (derivable from rationality) and which are additional restrictions (such as concavity or separability). The axiomatic approach also makes the theory testable: each axiom can, in principle, be questioned empirically or philosophically. The central axioms are rationality (completeness and transitivity), continuity (technical, ensuring a utility representation), and behavioral restrictions on how preferences respond to more or less of goods (monotonicity, convexity).

title: 8  2.1 — Preference Relations
text:
9.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 2.1.1 — Consumption Set



The consumption set is \(X \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L_+\), where \(L \geq 1\) is the number of goods. Each element \(x = (x_1, \ldots, x_L) \in X\) is a consumption bundle. We typically take \(X = \mathbb{R}^L_+\).








NoteDefinition 2.1.2 — Preference Relation



A preference relation \(\succsim\) on \(X\) is a binary relation on \(X\). We read \(x \succsim y\) as “\(x\) is at least as good as \(y\).” Two derived relations are: - Strict preference: \(x \succ y \iff x \succsim y\) and \(\neg(y \succsim x)\) - Indifference: \(x \sim y \iff x \succsim y\) and \(y \succsim x\)








NoteDefinition 2.1.3 — Completeness



\(\succsim\) is complete if for all \(x, y \in X\): \(x \succsim y\) or \(y \succsim x\) (or both).
Economic motivation: Every pair of bundles is comparable. The consumer is never “stuck” — they can always express a preference or indifference. While philosophically demanding (it rules out genuine incomparability), completeness is maintained as a tractability assumption in standard theory.








NoteDefinition 2.1.4 — Transitivity



\(\succsim\) is transitive if for all \(x, y, z \in X\): if \(x \succsim y\) and \(y \succsim z\), then \(x \succsim z\).
Economic motivation: Transitivity rules out preference cycles. Without it, a consumer could be “money-pumped” — paying to cycle indefinitely among bundles. Transitivity together with completeness constitutes rationality.








NoteDefinition 2.1.5 — Rationality



A preference relation \(\succsim\) is rational if it is both complete and transitive.








NoteDefinition 2.1.6 — Continuity



\(\succsim\) is continuous if for all \(y \in X\), the upper contour set \(\{x \in X : x \succsim y\}\) and the lower contour set \(\{x \in X : y \succsim x\}\) are both closed subsets of \(X\).
Equivalently, \(\succsim\) is continuous if for any sequences \(x^n \to x\) and \(y^n \to y\) with \(x^n \succsim y^n\) for all \(n\), we have \(x \succsim y\).
Economic motivation: Continuity is a technical regularity condition. It prevents situations in which a small change in a bundle causes a discrete jump in preference rank. Its primary role is to guarantee the existence of a utility representation (Debreu’s theorem).








NoteDefinition 2.1.7 — Monotonicity




\(\succsim\) is weakly monotone if \(x \geq y\) (componentwise) implies \(x \succsim y\).
\(\succsim\) is strongly monotone if \(x \geq y\) and \(x \neq y\) implies \(x \succ y\).

Economic motivation: Monotonicity captures the idea that goods are desirable — “more is better.” Weak monotonicity allows indifference over additional units; strong monotonicity insists that having strictly more of at least one good is strictly preferred.








NoteDefinition 2.1.8 — Local Nonsatiation (LNS)



\(\succsim\) satisfies local nonsatiation if for every \(x \in X\) and every \(\varepsilon > 0\), there exists \(y \in X\) with \(\|y - x\| < \varepsilon\) such that \(y \succ x\).
Economic motivation: LNS is weaker than monotonicity. It rules out thick indifference curves (bliss points surrounded by a region of indifference), but allows some goods to be bads. LNS is the minimal assumption needed to derive Walras’ Law (\(p \cdot x(p,w) = w\)): if the consumer were satiated, they might not exhaust their budget.








NoteDefinition 2.1.9 — Convexity




\(\succsim\) is weakly convex if for all \(y \in X\), the upper contour set \(\{x \in X : x \succsim y\}\) is convex. Equivalently: if \(x \succsim y\) and \(z \succsim y\), then \(\alpha x + (1-\alpha) z \succsim y\) for all \(\alpha \in [0,1]\).
\(\succsim\) is strictly convex if \(x \succsim y\), \(z \succsim y\), and \(x \neq z\) implies \(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z \succ y\) for all \(\alpha \in (0,1)\).

Economic motivation: Convexity captures a taste for variety or diversification. Consumers prefer mixtures of bundles to extremes. Geometrically, it corresponds to convex-to-the-origin indifference curves. Strict convexity additionally rules out linear segments of indifference curves, ensuring uniqueness of the utility-maximizing bundle (given a linear budget set).

title: 8  2.1 — Preference Relations
text:
9.3 Main Results






ImportantProposition 2.1.1 — Continuity Implies Closed Contour Sets



If \(\succsim\) is complete, transitive, and continuous on \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\), then for every \(y \in \mathbb{R}^L_+\), the sets \[UC(y) = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : x \succsim y\} \quad \text{and} \quad LC(y) = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : y \succsim x\}\] are closed in \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\).








TipProof





This follows directly from the definition of continuity (Definition 2.1.6): continuity of \(\succsim\) is defined as the requirement that \(UC(y)\) and \(LC(y)\) are closed for all \(y\). The content of the proposition is that this property is equivalent to sequential continuity.
Sequential characterization: \(\succsim\) is continuous if and only if for all sequences \(\{x^n\}\) and \(\{z^n\}\) in \(X\) with \(x^n \succsim z^n\) for all \(n\), \(x^n \to x\), and \(z^n \to z\), we have \(x \succsim z\).
We show that sequential continuity implies \(UC(y)\) is closed (the argument for \(LC(y)\) is symmetric).
Let \(\{x^n\}\) be a sequence in \(UC(y)\) with \(x^n \to x\). We need to show \(x \in UC(y)\), i.e., \(x \succsim y\).
By assumption, \(x^n \succsim y\) for all \(n\). Consider the constant sequence \(z^n = y \to y\). By sequential continuity applied to the pair \((x^n, z^n)\) where \(x^n \succsim z^n = y\), we conclude \(x \succsim y\).
Hence \(x \in UC(y)\), so \(UC(y)\) is closed.
Conversely, suppose \(UC(y)\) and \(LC(y)\) are closed for all \(y\). Let \(x^n \to x\) and \(y^n \to y\) with \(x^n \succsim y^n\). Suppose for contradiction that \(y \succ x\), i.e., \(y \succsim x\) but \(\neg(x \succsim y)\). Then \(x \in LC(y) \setminus UC(y)\). By openness of \(LC(y)^c \cup UC(y) = \{z : y \not\succsim z\} \cup \{z : z \succsim y\}\)… but more directly: since \(y \succ x\), there exist open neighborhoods \(U \ni x\) and \(V \ni y\) such that every pair \((x', y')\) with \(x' \in U, y' \in V\) satisfies \(y' \succ x'\) (this uses the equivalence with open-set continuity under completeness and transitivity). For large \(n\), \(x^n \in U\) and \(y^n \in V\), which would give \(y^n \succ x^n\), contradicting \(x^n \succsim y^n\). \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.1.1 — Rationality as a Maintained Hypothesis



The combination of completeness and transitivity — rationality — is a strong behavioral postulate. It is maintained in most of economic theory not because it is believed to be perfectly accurate, but because: 1. It provides a determinate theory of choice: the agent’s decision is fully characterized by the preference relation. 2. It is normatively defensible: violating transitivity opens an agent to money pumps. 3. It generates sharp, testable predictions via the revealed preference literature.
Behavioral economics documents systematic violations of both completeness (preference reversals, ambiguity) and transitivity (cyclic choices under framing effects). These motivate alternative models (prospect theory, regret theory), but standard micro theory retains rationality as the benchmark.








ImportantProposition 2.1.2 — Properties of Derived Relations



If \(\succsim\) is rational (complete and transitive), then: (a) \(\succ\) is irreflexive and transitive. (b) \(\sim\) is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive (an equivalence relation). (c) If \(x \succ y \succsim z\), then \(x \succ z\).








TipProof





(a) Irreflexivity of \(\succ\): By completeness, \(x \succsim x\) for all \(x\) (take \(y = x\)). Hence \(\neg(x \succ x)\).
Transitivity of \(\succ\): Suppose \(x \succ y\) and \(y \succ z\). Then \(x \succsim y\) and \(y \succsim z\), so by transitivity of \(\succsim\), \(x \succsim z\). It remains to show \(\neg(z \succsim x)\). Suppose for contradiction \(z \succsim x\). Then \(z \succsim x \succsim y\), so \(z \succsim y\) by transitivity, contradicting \(y \succ z\). Hence \(x \succ z\).
(b) Reflexivity: \(x \succsim x\) by completeness (apply with \(y = x\)), so \(x \sim x\). Symmetry: if \(x \sim y\) then \(x \succsim y\) and \(y \succsim x\), so \(y \sim x\). Transitivity: if \(x \sim y\) and \(y \sim z\), then \(x \succsim y \succsim z\) and \(z \succsim y \succsim x\), so \(x \sim z\).
(c) \(x \succ y\) and \(y \succsim z\) gives \(x \succsim z\) by transitivity. If \(z \succsim x\), then \(z \succsim x \succsim y\) gives \(z \succsim y\), contradicting \(y \succ z\)… wait, we only have \(y \succsim z\) not \(y \succ z\). Instead: if \(z \succsim x\), transitivity gives \(z \succsim x \succsim y\), so \(z \succsim y\); but also by \(x \succ y\) we have \(\neg(y \succsim x)\); and \(z \succsim x\) together with transitivity gives \(z \succsim y\). This does not immediately give a contradiction. We need: \(x \succ y\) means \(\neg(y \succsim x)\). If \(z \succsim x\), then \(y \succsim z \succsim x\) by transitivity of \(\succsim\), giving \(y \succsim x\), contradiction. \(\square\)

title: 8  2.1 — Preference Relations
section: 9.4 Lexicographic Preferences: A Failure of Continuity
text:
9.4 Lexicographic Preferences: A Failure of Continuity






NoteExample 2.1.1 — Lexicographic Preferences



On \(X = \mathbb{R}^2_+\), define \((x_1, x_2) \succsim^{lex} (y_1, y_2)\) if either \(x_1 > y_1\), or \(x_1 = y_1\) and \(x_2 \geq y_2\).
This preference is complete and transitive (verify: it is essentially the dictionary ordering). However, it is not continuous, and no utility function represents it.








TipProof





Failure of continuity: Consider \(y = (1, 0)\) and the upper contour set \(UC(y) = \{x : x \succsim^{lex} (1,0)\}\). This set equals \(\{x : x_1 > 1\} \cup \{x : x_1 = 1, x_2 \geq 0\} = \{x : x_1 \geq 1\}\). Wait — more carefully: \(UC(y)\) consists of all \(x\) with \(x_1 > 1\), plus all \(x\) with \(x_1 = 1\) and \(x_2 \geq 0\). So \(UC(y) = \{x : x_1 > 1\} \cup \{(1, x_2) : x_2 \geq 0\}\).
Consider the sequence \(x^n = (1 - 1/n, n)\). Then \(x^n_1 = 1 - 1/n < 1 = y_1\), so \(y \succ^{lex} x^n\), meaning \(x^n \notin UC(y)\). However, \(x^n \to (1, \infty)\)… actually \(x^n\) does not converge in \(\mathbb{R}^2_+\). Let us use a better sequence.
Consider \(y = (1, 1)\) and \(x^n = (1 - 1/n, M)\) for any fixed large \(M\). Then \(x^n_1 < y_1 = 1\), so \(y \succ^{lex} x^n\), i.e., \(x^n \notin UC(y)\) for any \(n\). But \(x^n \to (1, M)\), and \((1, M) \succsim^{lex} (1,1)\) iff \(M \geq 1\), so if \(M \geq 1\) then the limit \((1, M)\) is in \(UC(y)\) but each \(x^n\) is not. Hence \(UC(y)\) is not closed.
No utility representation exists: Suppose for contradiction that \(u: \mathbb{R}^2_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) represents \(\succsim^{lex}\). For each \(x_1 \in \mathbb{R}_+\), consider the “fiber” at \(x_1\): the set of bundles \(\{(x_1, t) : t \geq 0\}\). For any \(t < t'\), \((x_1, t') \succ^{lex} (x_1, t)\), so \(u(x_1, t') > u(x_1, t)\). Hence for each fixed \(x_1\), the function \(t \mapsto u(x_1, t)\) is strictly increasing. In particular, the set \(\{u(x_1, t) : t \in \mathbb{Q}_+\}\) is a countably infinite subset of \(\mathbb{R}\) with distinct values.
Now, for \(x_1 \neq x_1'\) (say \(x_1 < x_1'\)), we have \((x_1', t) \succ^{lex} (x_1, t')\) for all \(t, t' \geq 0\). Hence \(u(x_1', t) > u(x_1, t')\) for all \(t, t'\). This means the entire range of \(u\) on the fiber at \(x_1\) lies strictly below the entire range of \(u\) on the fiber at \(x_1'\), whenever \(x_1 < x_1'\).
Thus the ranges \(\{u(x_1, t) : t \geq 0\}\) for different values of \(x_1\) are pairwise disjoint intervals in \(\mathbb{R}\), each containing the set \(\{u(x_1, t) : t \in \mathbb{Q}_+\}\), which is a countably infinite set with distinct values (so not a single point). We have uncountably many such disjoint subintervals of \(\mathbb{R}\) (one for each \(x_1 \in \mathbb{R}_+\)), each containing at least two rationals. But since any collection of pairwise disjoint nondegenerate intervals is at most countable (each contains a rational), we have a contradiction. \(\square\)

title: 8  2.1 — Preference Relations
text:
9.5 Economic Intuition
The preference axioms fall naturally into two groups: substantive and technical.
Substantive axioms make real behavioral restrictions: Completeness says every comparison is possible. Transitivity rules out cycles. Monotonicity encodes the desirability of goods. Convexity encodes the preference for variety. These are the axioms that behavioral economics tests and sometimes rejects.
Technical axioms primarily serve mathematical purposes: Continuity ensures that preference can be represented by a function (and that the optimum exists and varies continuously with parameters). Without continuity, we lose the utility representation theorem, and the entire machinery of calculus-based optimization becomes unavailable. The lexicographic example shows that continuity cannot be weakened without losing the existence of a utility function.
Which axioms are load-bearing for economics? The core welfare theorems and the Slutsky decomposition require rationality and local nonsatiation. Monotonicity and convexity are used for clean demand characterizations (interior solutions, uniqueness) but many results hold without them. Continuity is almost always maintained because without it the theory becomes intractable.

title: 8  2.1 — Preference Relations
text:
9.6 Cross-References

2.2 Utility Representation — Representation theorem requires exactly rationality + continuity
2.3 Utility Maximization Problem — Convexity ensures uniqueness of demand; LNS implies Walras’ Law
2.5 Duality and Slutsky — Monotonicity and convexity are maintained throughout duality theory
1.2 Convex Analysis — Mathematical background on convex sets

title: 9  2.2 — Utility Representation
text: 10 2.2 — Utility Representation

title: 9  2.2 — Utility Representation
text:
10.1 Context
Given a rational and continuous preference relation, we wish to represent it numerically — to find a real-valued function that preserves the preference ordering. The existence of such a representation is not trivial: the lexicographic example in 2.1 Preference Relations showed that rationality alone is insufficient. Debreu’s 1954 theorem establishes that rationality plus continuity is sufficient. Once the representation exists, we must grapple with its non-uniqueness: utility is ordinal, unique only up to monotone transformations, and therefore carries no cardinal or interpersonal meaning. Beyond the general existence question, consumer theory makes use of specific functional forms — quasi-linear, homothetic, CES — each of which imposes restrictions on income effects and expenditure shares that are central to structural demand estimation.

title: 9  2.2 — Utility Representation
text:
10.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 2.2.1 — Utility Function



A function \(u: X \to \mathbb{R}\) represents the preference relation \(\succsim\) on \(X\) if for all \(x, y \in X\): \[x \succsim y \iff u(x) \geq u(y)\] Such a function is called a utility function for \(\succsim\).








CautionRemark 2.2.1 — Ordinal Utility



Utility is ordinal: if \(u\) represents \(\succsim\) and \(f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) is strictly increasing, then \(v = f \circ u\) also represents \(\succsim\). Proof: \(x \succsim y \iff u(x) \geq u(y) \iff f(u(x)) \geq f(u(y)) \iff v(x) \geq v(y)\). Hence utility is unique only up to a strictly increasing transformation. This means cardinal statements (“good \(x\) gives twice the utility of \(y\)”) are meaningless; only ordinal comparisons survive. This severely limits welfare analysis: interpersonal utility comparisons and aggregate welfare measures require additional structure beyond ordinal preferences.








NoteDefinition 2.2.2 — Quasi-Linear Utility



Preferences on \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\) are quasi-linear with respect to good 1 if they can be represented by: \[u(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_L) = x_1 + v(x_2, \ldots, x_L)\] where \(v: \mathbb{R}^{L-1}_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) is a function of goods \(2\) through \(L\) only.








NoteDefinition 2.2.3 — Homothetic Preferences



Preferences \(\succsim\) on \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\) are homothetic if \(x \sim y\) implies \(\lambda x \sim \lambda y\) for all \(\lambda > 0\). Equivalently, \(\succsim\) is homothetic if it can be represented by a utility function that is homogeneous of degree one: \(u(\lambda x) = \lambda u(x)\) for all \(\lambda > 0, x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+\).








NoteDefinition 2.2.4 — CES Utility



The Constant Elasticity of Substitution (CES) utility function is: \[u(x) = \left(\sum_{l=1}^L \alpha_l x_l^\rho\right)^{1/\rho}, \quad \alpha_l > 0, \; \rho \in (-\infty, 1) \setminus \{0\}\] The elasticity of substitution between any two goods \(l\) and \(k\) is: \[\sigma = \frac{1}{1-\rho}\] Special cases: \(\rho \to 1\) gives linear (perfect substitutes, \(\sigma \to \infty\)); \(\rho \to 0\) gives Cobb-Douglas (\(\sigma = 1\)); \(\rho \to -\infty\) gives Leontief/perfect complements (\(\sigma \to 0\)).

title: 9  2.2 — Utility Representation
text:
10.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 2.2.1 — Debreu Representation Theorem



Let \(\succsim\) be a rational (complete and transitive) and continuous preference relation on \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\). Then there exists a continuous utility function \(u: \mathbb{R}^L_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) that represents \(\succsim\).








TipProof





We sketch the Debreu (1954) construction, which builds on the idea of using a countable dense subset of \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\) to construct utility values.
Step 1: Strong Monotonicity Simplification. For clarity, we first prove the result under strong monotonicity; the general case requires more care but uses the same architecture.
Under strong monotonicity, for any \(x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+\), consider the “diagonal ray” \(\{(t,t,\ldots,t) : t \geq 0\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L_+\). Since \(\succsim\) is strongly monotone, the map \(t \mapsto (t, \ldots, t)\) is strictly increasing in preference. By continuity of \(\succsim\) and the intermediate value theorem (applied to preference):
Claim: For every \(x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+\), there exists a unique \(t(x) \geq 0\) such that \(x \sim (t(x), \ldots, t(x))\).
Proof of Claim: Consider $f(t) = $ “preference comparison of \(x\) vs. \((t,\ldots,t)\).” When \(t\) is large enough (say \(t > \max_l x_l\)), strong monotonicity gives \((t,\ldots,t) \succ x\). When \(t = 0\), we have \(x \succsim (0,\ldots,0)\) by weak monotonicity. By continuity of \(\succsim\), the function \(t \mapsto \mathbf{1}[x \succsim (t,\ldots,t)]\) is upper semicontinuous and \(t \mapsto \mathbf{1}[(t,\ldots,t) \succsim x]\) is also upper semicontinuous. By a connectedness argument, there exists \(t^*\) with \(x \sim (t^*,\ldots,t^*)\). Uniqueness follows from strict monotonicity: if \(t' > t^*\) then \((t',\ldots,t') \succ (t^*,\ldots,t^*) \sim x\), so \((t',\ldots,t') \not\sim x\).
Step 2: Define \(u(x) = t(x)\). Set \(u(x) := t(x)\), the unique \(t \geq 0\) such that \(x \sim (t,\ldots,t)\).
\(u\) represents \(\succsim\): \(x \succsim y \iff (t(x),\ldots) \sim x \succsim y \sim (t(y),\ldots) \iff (t(x),\ldots) \succsim (t(y),\ldots) \iff t(x) \geq t(y)\) (by monotonicity of the diagonal).
Step 3: Continuity of \(u\). Let \(x^n \to x\). We need \(u(x^n) = t(x^n) \to t(x) = u(x)\). Let \(t_n = t(x^n)\), so \(x^n \sim (t_n,\ldots,t_n)\). Suppose \(t_n \not\to t(x)\); then there exists a subsequence \(t_{n_k} \to t' \neq t(x)\) (or diverging). By continuity of \(\succsim\) applied to \(x^{n_k} \sim (t_{n_k},\ldots,t_{n_k})\) and taking limits: \(x \sim (t', \ldots, t')\). By uniqueness, \(t' = t(x)\), contradiction. Hence \(t_n \to t(x)\), so \(u\) is continuous.
Step 4: General Case (no monotonicity). Debreu’s original proof uses a different architecture. The key insight is:

\(\mathbb{R}^L_+\) is a second-countable topological space (it has a countable base). Every preference relation that is representable must have a countable “dense” subset in the preference order. More precisely, \(\succsim\) is continuous iff the topology it induces on \(X\) (generated by upper and lower contour sets) is second-countable, which holds for \(X = \mathbb{R}^L_+\).
Let \(D = \mathbb{Q}^L_+ \cap X\) be the countable dense subset of \(X\). For each \(x\), the set \(D_x^- = \{d \in D : x \succ d\}\) is non-empty for most \(x\) (by density and LNS). Define \(u(x)\) via the measure of “how much of \(D\) lies below \(x\)” — formally, by assigning rational numbers to elements of \(D\) consistently with \(\succsim\) on \(D\) and then extending to all of \(X\) by: \(u(x) = \sup\{r \in \mathbb{Q} : \exists d \in D, r = u(d), d \prec x\}\).

The full Debreu proof establishes that continuity of \(\succsim\) ensures this construction yields a well-defined, continuous utility function on all of \(X\). The key step is showing continuity of \(u\): upper semicontinuity follows from the fact that \(\{x : u(x) \geq c\} = UC(d)\) for appropriately chosen \(d \in D\), and these are closed. \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.2.2 — Necessity of Continuity



The lexicographic preference (2.1 Preference Relations, Example 2.1.1) is rational (complete and transitive) but not continuous, and has no utility representation. This shows continuity cannot be dropped from Theorem 2.2.1. In infinite-dimensional spaces, even stronger conditions are needed.








ImportantProposition 2.2.1 — Quasi-Concavity and Convex Preferences



Let \(u: \mathbb{R}^L_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) be a continuous utility function representing \(\succsim\). Then \(u\) is quasi-concave (i.e., \(\{x : u(x) \geq c\}\) is convex for all \(c \in \mathbb{R}\)) if and only if \(\succsim\) is weakly convex (Definition 2.1.9(a)).








TipProof





(\(\Rightarrow\)) Suppose \(u\) is quasi-concave. Let \(x \succsim y\) and \(z \succsim y\); we must show \(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z \succsim y\) for all \(\alpha \in [0,1]\).
Since \(x \succsim y\), we have \(u(x) \geq u(y)\). Since \(z \succsim y\), we have \(u(z) \geq u(y)\). Let \(c = u(y)\). Then \(x, z \in \{x' : u(x') \geq c\}\). By quasi-concavity, this set is convex, so \(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z \in \{x' : u(x') \geq c\}\), i.e., \(u(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z) \geq c = u(y)\). Hence \(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z \succsim y\).
(\(\Leftarrow\)) Suppose \(\succsim\) is weakly convex. Let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\) and let \(U_c = \{x : u(x) \geq c\}\) be the upper level set. We show \(U_c\) is convex. Take \(x, z \in U_c\), so \(u(x) \geq c\) and \(u(z) \geq c\).
Let \(y\) be any element with \(u(y) = c\) (if no such \(y\) exists because \(c\) is below the infimum of \(u\), then \(U_c = X\) which is convex; if \(c\) exceeds the supremum, \(U_c = \emptyset\), also convex). Actually, we work directly: \(u(x) \geq c\) means \(x \succsim y\) for any \(y\) with \(u(y) = c\). Similarly \(z \succsim y\). By weak convexity, \(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z \succsim y\), so \(u(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z) \geq u(y) = c\).
More carefully, without assuming a \(y\) exists with \(u(y) = c\): if \(u(x) \geq c\) and \(u(z) \geq c\), let \(m = \min(u(x), u(z)) \geq c\). Consider any \(y\) with \(u(y) = m\) (which is achievable by continuity of \(u\) on the path from \(x\) to \(z\) if \(u(x) \neq u(z)\)). This sub-case analysis can be avoided by noting: quasi-concavity means \(u(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z) \geq \min(u(x), u(z))\) for all \(\alpha\). We prove this directly.
Define \(v := \alpha x + (1-\alpha)z\) for \(\alpha \in (0,1)\). WLOG \(u(x) \leq u(z)\), so \(x \succsim\) (?) — actually \(u(x) \leq u(z)\) means \(z \succsim x\). Let \(c' = u(x) = \min(u(x), u(z))\). We need \(u(v) \geq c'\), i.e., \(v \succsim\) any bundle with utility \(c'\). Since \(u(x) = c'\), we need \(v \succsim x\). We have \(x \succsim x\) (trivially) and \(z \succsim x\) (since \(u(z) \geq u(x)\)). By weak convexity (taking \(y = x\)): \(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z \succsim x\), so \(u(v) \geq u(x) = c'\). Hence \(u(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z) \geq \min(u(x), u(z))\), which is exactly quasi-concavity. \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.2.3 — Strict Quasi-Concavity



\(u\) is strictly quasi-concave if \(u(x) \geq u(y)\), \(u(z) \geq u(y)\), \(x \neq z\) implies \(u(\alpha x + (1-\alpha)z) > u(y)\) for \(\alpha \in (0,1)\). This corresponds exactly to strict convexity of preferences and ensures uniqueness of the utility-maximizing bundle given a convex budget set.

title: 9  2.2 — Utility Representation
text:
10.4 Special Functional Forms

10.4.1 Quasi-Linear Utility
\(u(x_1, \ldots, x_L) = x_1 + v(x_2, \ldots, x_L)\)
Key property: The marginal utility of wealth is constant (equal to 1), so there is no income effect on goods \(2, \ldots, L\). Specifically, if \(w\) increases by \(\Delta w\), all additional income is spent on good 1; demand for goods \(2, \ldots, L\) is unaffected. This makes quasi-linear utility ideal for settings where good 1 is a “numeraire” (e.g., money) and one studies demand for a single market good. Engel curves for goods \(2, \ldots, L\) are vertical; consumer surplus is an exact welfare measure.


10.4.2 Homothetic Preferences
Represented by a utility function homogeneous of degree one: \(u(\lambda x) = \lambda u(x)\).
Key property: Engel curves are rays through the origin — budget shares \(p_l x_l / w\) are constant in \(w\) for all \(l\). This is verified: the UMP gives \(x(p, w) = w \cdot x(p, 1)\) (by homogeneity of degree one in \(w\) from homogeneity of \(u\)). Thus demand scales proportionally with income, and expenditure shares are independent of \(w\). CES and Cobb-Douglas are homothetic; quasi-linear is not (unless \(v\) is also homogeneous).


10.4.3 CES Utility
\[u(x) = \left(\sum_{l=1}^L \alpha_l x_l^\rho\right)^{1/\rho}, \quad \rho \neq 0, \rho < 1\]
The elasticity of substitution between goods \(l\) and \(k\) is: \[\sigma_{lk} = -\frac{d \ln(x_l/x_k)}{d \ln(p_l/p_k)}\bigg|_{u = \bar{u}} = \frac{1}{1-\rho}\]
This is the same for all pairs, hence “constant” elasticity of substitution. The CES family nests: - \(\rho \to 1\): \(u(x) = \sum_l \alpha_l x_l\) (linear/perfect substitutes, \(\sigma \to \infty\)) - \(\rho \to 0\): \(u(x) = \prod_l x_l^{\alpha_l}\) (Cobb-Douglas, \(\sigma = 1\)) — derivation via L’Hopital - \(\rho \to -\infty\): \(u(x) = \min_l \{x_l/\alpha_l\}\) (Leontief/perfect complements, \(\sigma = 0\))
Cobb-Douglas derivation from CES (\(\rho \to 0\)): Take \(\ln u = \frac{1}{\rho} \ln\left(\sum_l \alpha_l x_l^\rho\right)\). As \(\rho \to 0\), applying L’Hopital: \[\lim_{\rho \to 0} \frac{\ln(\sum_l \alpha_l x_l^\rho)}{\rho} = \frac{\sum_l \alpha_l x_l^\rho \ln x_l}{\sum_l \alpha_l x_l^\rho}\bigg|_{\rho=0} = \frac{\sum_l \alpha_l \ln x_l}{\sum_l \alpha_l} = \sum_l \bar{\alpha}_l \ln x_l\] where \(\bar{\alpha}_l = \alpha_l / \sum_k \alpha_k\). So \(u \to \exp\left(\sum_l \bar{\alpha}_l \ln x_l\right) = \prod_l x_l^{\bar{\alpha}_l}\), the Cobb-Douglas form.

title: 9  2.2 — Utility Representation
text:
10.5 Economic Intuition
The Debreu theorem tells us that the preference-based and utility-function-based approaches to consumer theory are equivalent under the maintained assumptions of rationality and continuity. This equivalence justifies the standard practice of positing a utility function directly, knowing it encodes a well-defined underlying preference ordering.
The ordinal nature of utility means that two economists who use different utility representations of the same preferences (one using \(u\), another using \(\ln u\)) will derive identical demand functions, identical indifference curves, and identical behavioral predictions. The only things that can legitimately be said about utility are ordinal: “this bundle gives higher utility than that one.” Cardinal statements (“bundle \(x\) gives twice the welfare of bundle \(y\)”) are conventions imposed by the specific utility representation chosen, not features of the underlying preferences.
Special functional forms play a crucial role in applied work because they impose tractable restrictions on the shape of demand, allowing closed-form solutions and structural estimation.

title: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)
text: 11 2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)
crumbs: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)

title: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)
text:
11.1 Context
The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP) is the central optimization problem of consumer theory. Given prices \(p\) and wealth \(w\), the consumer selects the most preferred feasible bundle. The solution — the Walrasian (Marshallian) demand — is the observable outcome of the UMP, and it is the primary object studied in demand analysis. The value function of the UMP is the indirect utility function, from which demand can be recovered via Roy’s Identity. Understanding the properties of Walrasian demand — homogeneity, Walras’ Law, and the structure imposed by the Slutsky matrix — is the foundation for all empirical demand work.
crumbs: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)

title: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)
text:
11.2 Setup and Definitions






NoteDefinition 2.3.1 — Budget Set



Given prices \(p = (p_1, \ldots, p_L) \gg 0\) (strictly positive) and wealth \(w > 0\), the Walrasian budget set is: \[B(p, w) = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : p \cdot x \leq w\}\] This is a compact (closed and bounded), convex subset of \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\). Compactness follows: closedness is clear; boundedness because \(x \in B(p,w)\) implies \(x_l \leq w/p_l < \infty\) for all \(l\) (since \(p \gg 0\)).








NoteDefinition 2.3.2 — Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)



The UMP is: \[\max_{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+} u(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad p \cdot x \leq w\]








NoteDefinition 2.3.3 — Walrasian Demand Correspondence



The Walrasian (Marshallian) demand correspondence \(x(p,w)\) is the set of solutions to the UMP: \[x(p, w) = \arg\max_{x \in B(p,w)} u(x)\] When \(u\) is strictly quasi-concave, \(x(p,w)\) is single-valued (a function).








NoteDefinition 2.3.4 — Indirect Utility Function



The indirect utility function \(v: \mathbb{R}^L_{++} \times \mathbb{R}_{++} \to \mathbb{R}\) is the value function of the UMP: \[v(p, w) = \max_{x \in B(p,w)} u(x) = u(x(p,w))\]
crumbs: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)

title: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)
text:
11.3 Existence of Solutions






ImportantProposition 2.3.1 — Existence of Utility Maximum



If \(u: \mathbb{R}^L_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(p \gg 0\), \(w > 0\), then the UMP has at least one solution.








TipProof





The budget set \(B(p,w)\) is compact (closed and bounded in \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\), as argued in Definition 2.3.1). The objective function \(u\) is continuous. By the Weierstrass Extreme Value Theorem, a continuous function on a compact set attains its maximum. Hence \(\max_{x \in B(p,w)} u(x)\) is achieved at some \(x^* \in B(p,w)\). \(\square\)
crumbs: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)

title: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)
text:
11.4 First-Order Conditions
Assume \(u\) is continuously differentiable. The Lagrangian for the UMP (relaxing the non-negativity constraints for interior solutions) is: \[\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = u(x) - \lambda(p \cdot x - w)\]






ImportantProposition 2.3.2 — KKT Conditions for Interior Solution



If \(x^* \gg 0\) is an interior solution to the UMP and \(u\) is continuously differentiable, then there exists \(\lambda^* > 0\) such that: \[\frac{\partial u(x^*)}{\partial x_l} = \lambda^* p_l \quad \text{for all } l = 1, \ldots, L\] Equivalently: \(\nabla u(x^*) = \lambda^* p\), or for any two goods \(l\) and \(k\): \[\frac{\partial u/\partial x_l}{\partial u/\partial x_k} = \frac{p_l}{p_k}\] That is, the Marginal Rate of Substitution equals the price ratio.








TipProof





At an interior solution, the non-negativity constraints \(x_l \geq 0\) are not binding. The only active constraint is the budget constraint \(p \cdot x \leq w\). Under local nonsatiation, this constraint binds at the optimum (Walras’ Law, proven below), so we have an equality constraint. By the Lagrange necessary conditions (which apply since the constraint qualification holds — the gradient of \(p \cdot x - w\) is \(p \neq 0\)), there exists \(\lambda^* \in \mathbb{R}\) such that: \[\frac{\partial u(x^*)}{\partial x_l} = \lambda^* p_l \quad \text{for all } l\] Since \(u\) is increasing (local nonsatiation) and \(p_l > 0\), we have \(\partial u / \partial x_l \geq 0\) (at the optimum), so \(\lambda^* \geq 0\). If \(\lambda^* = 0\), then \(\nabla u(x^*) = 0\), but LNS implies \(\nabla u(x^*) \neq 0\) (there is always a direction of improvement). Hence \(\lambda^* > 0\). \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.3.1 — Interpretation of \(\lambda^*\)



The Lagrange multiplier \(\lambda^*\) equals \(\partial v(p,w)/\partial w\), the marginal utility of wealth. This follows from the envelope theorem: \(\frac{\partial v}{\partial w} = \lambda^*\). Thus \(\lambda^*\) tells us how much utility increases when the budget expands by one unit. The condition \(\nabla u(x^*) = \lambda^* p\) says that at the optimum, the marginal utility per dollar spent is equalized across all goods: \(\frac{\partial u/\partial x_l}{p_l} = \lambda^*\) for all \(l\).
crumbs: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)

title: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)
text:
11.5 Properties of Walrasian Demand






ImportantProposition 2.3.3 — Homogeneity of Degree Zero



If \(u\) represents a locally nonsatiated preference, then \(x(\alpha p, \alpha w) = x(p, w)\) for all \(\alpha > 0\).








TipProof





The budget set satisfies \(B(\alpha p, \alpha w) = \{x \geq 0 : \alpha p \cdot x \leq \alpha w\} = \{x \geq 0 : p \cdot x \leq w\} = B(p, w)\). Since the feasible set is unchanged, the optimization problem is identical, so the set of maximizers is unchanged: \(x(\alpha p, \alpha w) = x(p, w)\). \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.3.2



Homogeneity of degree zero reflects the absence of money illusion: only relative prices and real wealth matter. Doubling all prices and wealth leaves the budget set — and therefore demand — unchanged.








ImportantProposition 2.3.4 — Walras’ Law



If \(\succsim\) is locally nonsatiated, then \(p \cdot x(p,w) = w\) for all \((p,w)\).








TipProof





Suppose for contradiction that \(x^* = x(p,w)\) satisfies \(p \cdot x^* < w\). Then \(x^*\) is in the interior of \(B(p,w)\). By local nonsatiation (Definition 2.1.8), for any \(\varepsilon > 0\) there exists \(y\) with \(\|y - x^*\| < \varepsilon\) and \(y \succ x^*\). For \(\varepsilon\) small enough, \(y \in B(p,w)\) (since \(x^*\) is interior to \(B(p,w)\), small perturbations remain feasible). But then \(y \in B(p,w)\) and \(y \succ x^*\), contradicting \(x^*\) being the utility-maximizing element of \(B(p,w)\). Hence \(p \cdot x(p,w) = w\). \(\square\)









ImportantProposition 2.3.5 — Uniqueness under Strict Quasi-Concavity



If \(u\) is strictly quasi-concave, then \(x(p,w)\) is single-valued for all \((p,w)\) with \(p \gg 0\), \(w > 0\).








TipProof





Suppose \(x^*\) and \(x^{**}\) are both in \(x(p,w)\) with \(x^* \neq x^{**}\). Then \(u(x^*) = u(x^{**}) = v(p,w)\). By strict quasi-concavity, \(u\left(\frac{x^* + x^{**}}{2}\right) > \min(u(x^*), u(x^{**})) = v(p,w)\). Since \(B(p,w)\) is convex, \(\frac{x^* + x^{**}}{2} \in B(p,w)\), and this midpoint achieves strictly higher utility, contradicting the optimality of \(x^*\) and \(x^{**}\). \(\square\)
crumbs: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)

title: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)
section: 11.6 Properties of the Indirect Utility Function
text:
11.6 Properties of the Indirect Utility Function






ImportantProposition 2.3.6 — Properties of \(v(p,w)\)



Suppose \(u\) is continuous and locally nonsatiated. The indirect utility function \(v(p,w)\) satisfies: 1. Homogeneous of degree zero: \(v(\alpha p, \alpha w) = v(p,w)\) for all \(\alpha > 0\) 2. Continuous in \((p,w)\) 3. Nonincreasing in \(p_l\): if \(p_l\) rises, \(v\) does not increase 4. Strictly increasing in \(w\) 5. Quasi-convex in \((p,w)\): the lower level set \(\{(p,w) : v(p,w) \leq \bar{v}\}\) is convex








TipProof





(1) Homogeneity: \(v(\alpha p, \alpha w) = \max_{x \in B(\alpha p, \alpha w)} u(x) = \max_{x \in B(p,w)} u(x) = v(p,w)\) since \(B(\alpha p, \alpha w) = B(p,w)\).
(2) Continuity: By Berge’s Maximum Theorem: the constraint correspondence \((p,w) \mapsto B(p,w)\) is continuous (in fact, the budget set varies continuously with \((p,w)\) when \(p \gg 0, w > 0\)), and \(u\) is continuous. The maximum theorem implies \(v(p,w)\) is continuous.
(3) Nonincreasing in \(p_l\): If \(p_l\) increases to \(p_l' > p_l\) (other prices fixed), then \(B(p', w) \subseteq B(p, w)\) (the feasible set shrinks). Hence \(v(p', w) = \max_{x \in B(p',w)} u(x) \leq \max_{x \in B(p,w)} u(x) = v(p,w)\).
(4) Strictly increasing in \(w\): If \(w' > w\), then \(B(p, w) \subsetneq B(p, w')\). By local nonsatiation, the optimal \(x(p,w)\) satisfies \(p \cdot x(p,w) = w < w'\), so it is in the interior of \(B(p, w')\). There exists a better bundle in \(B(p, w')\), so \(v(p, w') > v(p, w)\).
(5) Quasi-convexity: We need to show that if \(v(p,w) \leq \bar{v}\) and \(v(p',w') \leq \bar{v}\), then \(v(\alpha p + (1-\alpha)p', \alpha w + (1-\alpha)w') \leq \bar{v}\). Let \(\tilde{p} = \alpha p + (1-\alpha)p'\) and \(\tilde{w} = \alpha w + (1-\alpha)w'\). We show \(B(\tilde{p}, \tilde{w}) \subseteq B(p, w) \cup B(p', w')\)… actually this is not quite right. The standard proof proceeds as follows: suppose \(x^*\) maximizes \(u\) subject to \(\tilde{p} \cdot x \leq \tilde{w}\). Then \(\tilde{p} \cdot x^* \leq \tilde{w}\), so \(\alpha p \cdot x^* + (1-\alpha) p' \cdot x^* \leq \alpha w + (1-\alpha)w'\). This means either \(p \cdot x^* \leq w\) or \(p' \cdot x^* \leq w'\) (or both); for if \(p \cdot x^* > w\) and \(p' \cdot x^* > w'\), then \(\alpha p \cdot x^* + (1-\alpha) p' \cdot x^* > \alpha w + (1-\alpha)w'\), a contradiction. Hence \(x^*\) is feasible in \(B(p,w)\) or \(B(p',w')\). If \(x^* \in B(p,w)\), then \(v(\tilde{p}, \tilde{w}) = u(x^*) \leq v(p,w) \leq \bar{v}\). Similarly for the other case. \(\square\)
crumbs: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)

title: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)
text:
11.7 Roy’s Identity






ImportantTheorem 2.3.1 — Roy’s Identity



Suppose \(v(p,w)\) is differentiable at \((p,w)\) and \(\partial v(p,w)/\partial w \neq 0\). Then: \[x_l(p,w) = -\frac{\partial v(p,w)/\partial p_l}{\partial v(p,w)/\partial w} \quad \text{for all } l = 1, \ldots, L\]








TipProof





We use the Envelope Theorem. The UMP is: \[v(p,w) = \max_x u(x) - \lambda(p \cdot x - w)\] where \(\lambda = \lambda(p,w)\) is the optimal multiplier.
By the envelope theorem applied to the parameter \(w\): \[\frac{\partial v(p,w)}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w}\bigg|_{x = x(p,w)} = \lambda^*\] (since \(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial w = \lambda\)).
By the envelope theorem applied to the parameter \(p_l\): \[\frac{\partial v(p,w)}{\partial p_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_l}\bigg|_{x = x(p,w)} = -\lambda^* x_l(p,w)\] (since \(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial p_l = -\lambda x_l\)).
Hence: \[\frac{\partial v/\partial p_l}{\partial v/\partial w} = \frac{-\lambda^* x_l(p,w)}{\lambda^*} = -x_l(p,w)\] Rearranging: \[x_l(p,w) = -\frac{\partial v(p,w)/\partial p_l}{\partial v(p,w)/\partial w} \qquad \square\]









CautionRemark 2.3.3 — Roy’s Identity: Significance



Roy’s Identity is a powerful result: it allows full recovery of the Walrasian demand system from the indirect utility function alone, using only differentiation. This is the basis for many structural demand models: one specifies \(v(p,w)\), checks it satisfies the integrability conditions, and recovers demand via Roy’s Identity. The denominator \(\partial v/\partial w = \lambda^*\) is the marginal utility of income; the ratio \(-(\partial v/\partial p_l)/(\partial v/\partial w)\) can be interpreted as the compensated demand for \(l\) at the margin.
crumbs: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)

title: 10  2.3 — The Utility Maximization Problem (UMP)
text:
11.8 Economic Intuition
The UMP encapsulates the fundamental scarcity problem: the consumer wants more of everything but is constrained by income. The KKT conditions \(\nabla u = \lambda p\) express the optimality condition that the consumer allocates spending across goods until the marginal utility per dollar is equalized everywhere. If \(\partial u/\partial x_l / p_l > \partial u/\partial x_k / p_k\), then good \(l\) provides more utility per dollar spent, and the consumer should reallocate toward \(l\).
Walras’ Law (\(p \cdot x = w\)) follows from the assumption that more is always better (LNS): a consumer who leaves money on the table could achieve strictly higher utility, contradicting optimality. This simple observation has powerful implications: it reduces the dimensionality of the demand system by one (one demand equation is redundant), and combined with homogeneity, it places tight restrictions on how demand responds to price changes.
Roy’s Identity closes the loop: given the indirect utility function (a scalar), we can recover the entire demand system (a vector) by differentiation. This duality between the “primal” demand representation and the “dual” indirect utility representation is exploited throughout applied economics.

text: 12 2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)
crumbs: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)

title: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)
text:
12.1 Context
The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP) is the dual to the UMP. While the UMP asks “what is the best bundle I can afford?”, the EMP asks “what is the cheapest way to achieve a given utility level?” The solution is the Hicksian (compensated) demand correspondence, and the value function is the expenditure function. The Hicksian demand isolates the pure substitution effect of a price change by holding utility fixed; it is the welfare-theoretically natural object for computing the compensating and equivalent variation. The expenditure function is the key tool for welfare measurement: a price change is valued by asking how much wealth must change to keep the consumer at their original utility level.
crumbs: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)

title: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)
text:
12.2 Setup and Definitions






NoteDefinition 2.4.1 — Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)



Given prices \(p \gg 0\) and a utility target \(\bar{u} > u(0)\) (infimum of \(u\) on \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\)), the EMP is: \[\min_{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+} \; p \cdot x \quad \text{subject to} \quad u(x) \geq \bar{u}\]








NoteDefinition 2.4.2 — Hicksian Demand Correspondence



The Hicksian (compensated) demand correspondence is: \[h(p, \bar{u}) = \arg\min_{x \geq 0, \; u(x) \geq \bar{u}} p \cdot x\] Elements of \(h(p, \bar{u})\) are the utility-compensated (Hicksian) demands.








NoteDefinition 2.4.3 — Expenditure Function



The expenditure function is the value function of the EMP: \[e(p, \bar{u}) = \min_{x \geq 0, \; u(x) \geq \bar{u}} p \cdot x = p \cdot h(p, \bar{u})\]
crumbs: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)

title: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)
text:
12.3 Existence






ImportantProposition 2.4.1 — Existence of Expenditure Minimum



If \(u\) is continuous and locally nonsatiated, and \(p \gg 0\), \(\bar{u} > u(0)\), then the EMP has a solution.








TipProof





The constraint set \(A = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : u(x) \geq \bar{u}\}\) is closed (by continuity of \(u\)) and nonempty (by assumption, \(\bar{u}\) is attainable). The objective \(p \cdot x\) is continuous and, since \(p \gg 0\), goes to infinity as \(\|x\| \to \infty\). Hence any sequence in \(A\) along which \(p \cdot x\) approaches its infimum must be bounded; the infimum is achieved in the closure of \(A = A\). More formally, fix any \(\hat{x} \in A\) (which exists by assumption). Restrict to the compact set \(A \cap \{x : p \cdot x \leq p \cdot \hat{x}\}\), which is closed (intersection of two closed sets) and bounded (since \(p \gg 0\) bounds all components from above). The continuous function \(p \cdot x\) attains its minimum on this compact set, which equals its infimum over all of \(A\). \(\square\)
crumbs: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)

title: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)
text:
12.4 Properties of the Expenditure Function






ImportantTheorem 2.4.1 — Properties of \(e(p, \bar{u})\)



Suppose \(u\) is continuous and locally nonsatiated. The expenditure function \(e(p, \bar{u})\) satisfies: 1. Homogeneous of degree one in \(p\): \(e(\alpha p, \bar{u}) = \alpha e(p, \bar{u})\) for all \(\alpha > 0\) 2. Strictly increasing in \(\bar{u}\) 3. Nondecreasing in \(p_l\) for each \(l\) 4. Concave in \(p\) 5. Continuous in \((p, \bar{u})\)








TipProof





(1) Homogeneity of degree one: \(e(\alpha p, \bar{u}) = \min_{u(x) \geq \bar{u}} (\alpha p) \cdot x = \alpha \min_{u(x) \geq \bar{u}} p \cdot x = \alpha \, e(p, \bar{u})\). The second equality holds because \(\alpha > 0\) does not affect the argmin, only scales the objective.
(2) Strictly increasing in \(\bar{u}\): Let \(\bar{u}' > \bar{u}\). Then the constraint set \(\{x : u(x) \geq \bar{u}'\} \subsetneq \{x : u(x) \geq \bar{u}\}\) (strictly, since we can find an \(x\) with \(u(x) \in [\bar{u}, \bar{u}')\) by continuity and the assumption that \(\bar{u}'\) is achievable). Hence \(e(p, \bar{u}') \geq e(p, \bar{u})\). For strict inequality: let \(x^* \in h(p, \bar{u})\), so \(u(x^*) = \bar{u}\) (by LNS; if \(u(x^*) > \bar{u}\), we could reduce expenditure slightly while maintaining \(u(x) \geq \bar{u}\), contradicting minimality). So \(x^*\) is not in \(\{x: u(x) \geq \bar{u}'\}\), meaning the minimizer for \(\bar{u}'\) must lie in a strictly smaller set, which by LNS forces strictly higher expenditure.
(3) Nondecreasing in \(p_l\): If \(p_l\) increases to \(p_l'\), then the cost of any bundle \(x\) weakly increases (since \(x_l \geq 0\)). Hence \(e(p', \bar{u}) = \min_{u(x) \geq \bar{u}} p' \cdot x \geq \min_{u(x) \geq \bar{u}} p \cdot x = e(p, \bar{u})\).
(4) Concavity in \(p\): Let \(p, p'\) be two price vectors and \(\alpha \in [0,1]\). Let \(\tilde{p} = \alpha p + (1-\alpha)p'\). Let \(x^*\) be a minimizer for the EMP at prices \(\tilde{p}\), so \(e(\tilde{p}, \bar{u}) = \tilde{p} \cdot x^* = \alpha p \cdot x^* + (1-\alpha) p' \cdot x^*\).
Since \(u(x^*) \geq \bar{u}\), \(x^*\) is feasible for the EMP at prices \(p\) and prices \(p'\). Hence: \[p \cdot x^* \geq e(p, \bar{u}) \quad \text{and} \quad p' \cdot x^* \geq e(p', \bar{u})\] Therefore: \[e(\tilde{p}, \bar{u}) = \alpha p \cdot x^* + (1-\alpha) p' \cdot x^* \geq \alpha \, e(p, \bar{u}) + (1-\alpha) \, e(p', \bar{u})\] This is exactly concavity: \(e(\alpha p + (1-\alpha)p', \bar{u}) \geq \alpha \, e(p, \bar{u}) + (1-\alpha) \, e(p', \bar{u})\). \(\square\)
(5) Continuity: By Berge’s Maximum Theorem applied to the EMP, since the constraint correspondence \(p \mapsto \{x \geq 0 : u(x) \geq \bar{u}\}\) is constant (and thus continuous in \(p\)), and the objective \(p \cdot x\) is continuous in \((p,x)\), the value function \(e(p, \bar{u})\) is continuous in \(p\). Continuity in \(\bar{u}\) follows similarly. \(\square\)
crumbs: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)

title: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)
text:
12.5 Shephard’s Lemma






ImportantTheorem 2.4.2 — Shephard’s Lemma



If \(e(p, \bar{u})\) is differentiable in \(p\) at \((p^0, \bar{u})\), then: \[h_l(p^0, \bar{u}) = \frac{\partial e(p^0, \bar{u})}{\partial p_l} \quad \text{for all } l = 1, \ldots, L\]








TipProof





We apply the Envelope Theorem to the EMP. Write the EMP as: \[e(p, \bar{u}) = \min_x \; p \cdot x \quad \text{s.t.} \quad u(x) \geq \bar{u}\] The Lagrangian is \(\mathcal{L}(x, \mu) = p \cdot x - \mu(u(x) - \bar{u})\) where \(\mu \geq 0\).
By the envelope theorem, the derivative of the optimal value function with respect to a parameter equals the partial derivative of the Lagrangian with respect to that parameter, evaluated at the optimum: \[\frac{\partial e(p, \bar{u})}{\partial p_l} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_l}\bigg|_{x = h(p,\bar{u})} = x_l^* = h_l(p, \bar{u})\] since \(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial p_l = x_l\). \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.4.1 — Shephard’s Lemma and the Substitution Matrix



Shephard’s Lemma immediately implies: the substitution matrix (Hessian of \(e\) with respect to \(p\)): \[\frac{\partial h_l(p, \bar{u})}{\partial p_k} = \frac{\partial^2 e(p, \bar{u})}{\partial p_l \partial p_k}\] Since \(e\) is concave in \(p\), its Hessian is negative semidefinite. Since the Hessian of \(e\) is symmetric (Young’s theorem), the substitution matrix is symmetric: \(\partial h_l/\partial p_k = \partial h_k/\partial p_l\). These properties — NSD and symmetry — are the content of the Slutsky restrictions, derived more fully in 2.5 Duality and Slutsky.
crumbs: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)

title: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)
text:
12.6 Properties of Hicksian Demand






ImportantProposition 2.4.2 — Properties of Hicksian Demand



Under the maintained assumptions, Hicksian demand \(h(p, \bar{u})\) satisfies: 1. Homogeneous of degree zero in \(p\): \(h(\alpha p, \bar{u}) = h(p, \bar{u})\) for all \(\alpha > 0\) 2. The substitution matrix \(\partial h/\partial p\) is symmetric: \(\frac{\partial h_l}{\partial p_k} = \frac{\partial h_k}{\partial p_l}\) 3. The substitution matrix \(\partial h/\partial p\) is negative semidefinite: \(z^\top \frac{\partial h(p,\bar{u})}{\partial p} z \leq 0\) for all \(z \in \mathbb{R}^L\) 4. \(\frac{\partial h}{\partial p} \cdot p = 0\) (from homogeneity)








TipProof





(1) Homogeneity of degree zero: \(h(\alpha p, \bar{u}) = \arg\min_{u(x) \geq \bar{u}} (\alpha p) \cdot x = \arg\min_{u(x) \geq \bar{u}} p \cdot x = h(p, \bar{u})\), since scaling the objective by \(\alpha > 0\) does not change the argmin.
(2) Symmetry: By Shephard’s Lemma, \(h_l = \partial e / \partial p_l\) and \(h_k = \partial e/\partial p_k\). Therefore \(\partial h_l/\partial p_k = \partial^2 e / \partial p_k \partial p_l = \partial^2 e/\partial p_l \partial p_k = \partial h_k/\partial p_l\) by Young’s theorem (equality of mixed partials), valid when \(e\) is twice continuously differentiable.
(3) Negative semidefiniteness: Since \(e(p, \bar{u})\) is concave in \(p\) (Theorem 2.4.1(4)), its Hessian \(D^2_p e = \partial h/\partial p\) is negative semidefinite. More explicitly: by concavity, for any vector \(z\): \[e(p + tz, \bar{u}) \leq e(p, \bar{u}) + t \nabla_p e \cdot z + \frac{t^2}{2} z^\top D^2_p e \cdot z\] but also from concavity: \(e(p + tz, \bar{u}) \leq e(p, \bar{u}) + t z^\top \nabla_p e(p, \bar{u})\) is not directly useful. The correct statement: \(e\) is concave iff its Hessian is NSD wherever it exists. Hence \(D^2_p e \preceq 0\), i.e., \(z^\top D^2_p e \, z \leq 0\) for all \(z\).
(4) \((\partial h/\partial p) p = 0\): Differentiating the homogeneity condition \(h(\alpha p, \bar{u}) = h(p, \bar{u})\) with respect to \(\alpha\) at \(\alpha = 1\) yields \(\frac{\partial h(p, \bar{u})}{\partial p} p = 0\) (by Euler’s theorem for homogeneous functions of degree zero). \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.4.2 — No Income Effect



Hicksian demand holds utility constant by construction. Therefore, it has no income effect: changes in \(p\) affect \(h\) only through the substitution channel. The own-price effect satisfies \(\partial h_l/\partial p_l \leq 0\) (from NSD of the substitution matrix — set \(z = e_l\)), meaning Hicksian demand is always downward sloping. This contrasts with Marshallian demand, which can slope upward for Giffen goods (where a strong income effect reverses the substitution effect).
crumbs: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)

title: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)
text:
12.7 Economic Intuition
The EMP is the “cost side” of consumer theory. It answers a dual question to the UMP: instead of maximizing utility within a budget, it minimizes cost while meeting a utility standard. The Hicksian demand isolates the pure substitution response to a price change, holding the consumer’s welfare fixed. This makes it the correct object for welfare measurement: if good \(l\)’s price rises, the Hicksian demand shows how the consumer would substitute away from \(l\) if they were compensated enough to maintain their original utility level.
The concavity of \(e\) in \(p\) has a transparent revealed-preference interpretation: if a firm (or consumer) can substitute away from goods whose prices rise, total costs are “convex” — but if the cost function is evaluated at a fixed bundle, costs are linear in prices. The minimum cost is therefore always below or equal to the cost of the fixed bundle, making the minimum-cost function concave.
Shephard’s Lemma is the dual analog of Roy’s Identity: just as the Walrasian demand can be recovered from the indirect utility function by differentiation, the Hicksian demand can be recovered from the expenditure function by differentiation. This provides a remarkably clean connection between welfare (expenditure) and observable demand.
crumbs: 11  2.4 — The Expenditure Minimization Problem (EMP)

title: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation
text: 13 2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation
crumbs: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation

title: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation
text:
13.1 Context
The duality between the UMP and EMP is the central organizing principle of consumer theory. The two problems — maximizing utility subject to a budget constraint, and minimizing expenditure to achieve a utility target — are mathematically dual, and their value functions and solution correspondences are tightly linked. The Slutsky equation is the crown jewel of this duality: it decomposes the total price effect on Walrasian demand into a substitution effect (measured by Hicksian demand) and an income effect. This decomposition is indispensable for welfare analysis and demand estimation, since it separates the welfare-neutral reallocation effect (substitution) from the wealth effect (income). The Slutsky matrix must be symmetric and negative semidefinite — properties that serve as the key testable restrictions on any demand system.
crumbs: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation

title: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation
text:
13.2 Duality Relations






ImportantTheorem 2.5.1 — The Four Duality Identities



Let \(u\) be continuous and locally nonsatiated, \(p \gg 0\), \(w > 0\), \(\bar{u} \in \text{range}(v(p, \cdot))\). Then: 1. \(e(p, v(p,w)) = w\) 2. \(v(p, e(p,\bar{u})) = \bar{u}\) 3. \(x(p,w) = h(p, v(p,w))\) 4. \(h(p,\bar{u}) = x(p, e(p,\bar{u}))\)








TipProof





(1) \(e(p, v(p,w)) = w\): Let \(x^* = x(p,w)\), so \(v(p,w) = u(x^*)\) and \(p \cdot x^* = w\) (by Walras’ Law). We claim \(x^*\) also solves the EMP at \(\bar{u} = v(p,w)\). Feasibility: \(u(x^*) = v(p,w) = \bar{u}\). Optimality: suppose some \(y\) with \(u(y) \geq \bar{u} = u(x^*)\) had \(p \cdot y < p \cdot x^* = w\). Then \(y \in B(p,w)\) and \(u(y) \geq u(x^*)\). By local nonsatiation, we can find \(y'\) near \(y\) with \(u(y') > u(x^*)\) and \(y' \in B(p,w)\), contradicting optimality of \(x^*\) in the UMP. Hence \(x^*\) is a solution to the EMP at \(\bar{u} = v(p,w)\), and \(e(p, v(p,w)) = p \cdot x^* = w\).
(2) \(v(p, e(p,\bar{u})) = \bar{u}\): Let \(h^* = h(p, \bar{u})\), so \(e(p, \bar{u}) = p \cdot h^*\) and \(u(h^*) \geq \bar{u}\). We show \(h^*\) also solves the UMP at wealth \(w = e(p, \bar{u})\). Feasibility: \(p \cdot h^* = e(p, \bar{u}) = w\). Optimality: suppose some \(y \in B(p, w)\) had \(u(y) > u(h^*)\). Then \(u(y) > \bar{u}\) (since \(u(h^*) \geq \bar{u}\), so \(u(y) > u(h^*) \geq \bar{u}\)), making \(y\) feasible for the EMP at \(\bar{u}\). But \(p \cdot y \leq w = p \cdot h^* = e(p, \bar{u})\), contradicting the minimality of \(h^*\) in the EMP (by LNS, equality holds, so \(p \cdot y = e(p,\bar{u})\) and we can find a cheaper nearby bundle with \(u \geq \bar{u}\), contradiction). Hence \(h^*\) solves the UMP at \(e(p, \bar{u})\). Therefore \(v(p, e(p, \bar{u})) = u(h^*) = \bar{u}\) (the last equality: \(u(h^*) = \bar{u}\) by LNS — if \(u(h^*) > \bar{u}\), we could find a cheaper feasible bundle, contradicting optimality of \(h^*\)).
(3) \(x(p,w) = h(p, v(p,w))\): Let \(\bar{u} = v(p,w)\). From (1), \(e(p, \bar{u}) = w\). From (4) (proven below independently), \(h(p, \bar{u}) = x(p, e(p, \bar{u})) = x(p, w)\).
Direct proof: Let \(x^* = x(p,w)\). We showed in (1) that \(x^*\) solves the EMP at \(\bar{u} = v(p,w)\). Hence \(x^* \in h(p, v(p,w))\), i.e., \(x(p,w) = h(p, v(p,w))\).
(4) \(h(p,\bar{u}) = x(p, e(p,\bar{u}))\): Let \(h^* = h(p, \bar{u})\) and \(w = e(p, \bar{u}) = p \cdot h^*\). We showed in (2) that \(h^*\) solves the UMP at wealth \(w\). Hence \(h^* \in x(p, e(p, \bar{u}))\), i.e., \(h(p, \bar{u}) = x(p, e(p, \bar{u}))\). \(\square\)
crumbs: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation

title: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation
text:
13.3 The Slutsky Equation






ImportantTheorem 2.5.2 — The Slutsky Equation



Suppose \(x(p,w)\) and \(h(p, \bar{u})\) are differentiable. For any \((p, w)\) with \(v(p,w) = \bar{u}\): \[\frac{\partial x_l(p,w)}{\partial p_k} = \frac{\partial h_l(p,\bar{u})}{\partial p_k} - x_k(p,w) \frac{\partial x_l(p,w)}{\partial w}\] In matrix form: \(Dx_p(p,w) = S(p,w) - x(p,w) \cdot Dx_w(p,w)^\top\), where \(S_{lk} = \partial h_l/\partial p_k\) is the Slutsky matrix.








TipProof





We differentiate the duality identity (4): \(h_l(p, \bar{u}) = x_l(p, e(p, \bar{u}))\) with respect to \(p_k\).
The right-hand side is a composite function of \(p_k\) through two channels: directly through the first argument of \(x_l\), and through the second argument \(e(p, \bar{u})\) which depends on \(p_k\) via Shephard’s Lemma. Applying the chain rule: \[\frac{\partial h_l(p, \bar{u})}{\partial p_k} = \frac{\partial x_l(p, e(p,\bar{u}))}{\partial p_k}\bigg|_w + \frac{\partial x_l(p, e(p,\bar{u}))}{\partial w} \cdot \frac{\partial e(p, \bar{u})}{\partial p_k}\] The partial derivative on the right (first term) holds \(w = e(p, \bar{u})\) fixed, so it is \(\partial x_l(p,w)/\partial p_k\). By Shephard’s Lemma, \(\partial e/\partial p_k = h_k(p, \bar{u}) = x_k(p,w)\) (using identity (3)). Substituting: \[\frac{\partial h_l}{\partial p_k} = \frac{\partial x_l}{\partial p_k} + \frac{\partial x_l}{\partial w} \cdot x_k(p,w)\] Rearranging: \[\frac{\partial x_l(p,w)}{\partial p_k} = \frac{\partial h_l(p,\bar{u})}{\partial p_k} - x_k(p,w) \frac{\partial x_l(p,w)}{\partial w} \qquad \square\]









CautionRemark 2.5.1 — Interpretation of the Slutsky Equation



The Slutsky equation decomposes the total price effect \(\partial x_l/\partial p_k\) into: - Substitution effect \(\partial h_l/\partial p_k\): the change in demand for \(l\) when \(p_k\) changes and the consumer is compensated to remain at the same utility level. This is always non-positive for own-price changes (\(l = k\)). - Income effect \(-x_k \cdot \partial x_l/\partial w\): the change in demand for \(l\) due to the reduction in real wealth caused by the price increase. The consumer purchased \(x_k\) units of good \(k\), so a rise of \(dp_k\) reduces effective wealth by \(x_k \, dp_k\).
crumbs: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation

title: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation
text:
13.4 The Slutsky Matrix






NoteDefinition 2.5.1 — Slutsky Matrix



The Slutsky matrix is the \(L \times L\) matrix of substitution effects: \[S(p,w) = \left[\frac{\partial h_l(p,v(p,w))}{\partial p_k}\right]_{l,k} = \left[\frac{\partial x_l}{\partial p_k} + x_k \frac{\partial x_l}{\partial w}\right]_{l,k}\] The second expression shows that \(S\) can be constructed entirely from the observable Walrasian demand and its derivatives.








ImportantProposition 2.5.1 — Properties of the Slutsky Matrix



Under the maintained assumptions, \(S(p,w)\) is: 1. Symmetric: \(S_{lk} = S_{kl}\) 2. Negative semidefinite: \(z^\top S(p,w) z \leq 0\) for all \(z \in \mathbb{R}^L\) 3. \(S(p,w) p = 0\) (Slutsky matrix times \(p\) is zero)








TipProof





(1) Symmetry: \(S_{lk} = \partial h_l/\partial p_k = \partial^2 e/\partial p_l \partial p_k\) (by Shephard’s Lemma). By Young’s theorem, \(\partial^2 e/\partial p_l \partial p_k = \partial^2 e/\partial p_k \partial p_l = S_{kl}\).
(2) NSD: Since \(e(p, \bar{u})\) is concave in \(p\) (Theorem 2.4.1(4)), its Hessian \(D^2_p e\) is negative semidefinite. By Shephard’s Lemma, \(D^2_p e = \partial h/\partial p = S\). Hence \(z^\top S z \leq 0\) for all \(z\).
Alternatively, for own-price effects: \(S_{ll} = \partial h_l/\partial p_l \leq 0\) because \(h_l\) is the minimizer of a cost problem and the own-price effect of the compensated demand is non-positive (complementary slackness and the cost-minimization first-order conditions).
(3) \(Sp = 0\): Differentiate the homogeneity condition \(h(\alpha p, \bar{u}) = h(p, \bar{u})\) with respect to \(\alpha\) at \(\alpha = 1\): \[\sum_k \frac{\partial h_l}{\partial p_k} p_k = 0 \quad \text{for all } l\] This is \([S \cdot p]_l = 0\) for all \(l\), i.e., \(Sp = 0\). \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.5.2 — NSD and Giffen Goods



\(S_{ll} \leq 0\) means the own-price Hicksian (compensated) effect is non-positive: compensated own-price demand curves slope downward. For Walrasian (Marshallian) demand, the own-price effect is \(\partial x_l/\partial p_l = S_{ll} - x_l \cdot \partial x_l/\partial w\). If good \(l\) is inferior (\(\partial x_l/\partial w < 0\)), then the income effect \(-x_l \cdot \partial x_l / \partial w > 0\) works against the negative substitution effect. If this income effect dominates, \(\partial x_l/\partial p_l > 0\): demand rises with price — a Giffen good. This requires inferiority plus a sufficiently strong income effect.
crumbs: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation

title: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation
text:
13.5 Income and Substitution Effects






NoteDefinition 2.5.2 — Normal and Inferior Goods



Good \(l\) is normal at \((p,w)\) if \(\partial x_l(p,w)/\partial w > 0\) (demand increases with wealth). It is inferior if \(\partial x_l/\partial w < 0\).








NoteDefinition 2.5.3 — Complements and Substitutes



Goods \(l\) and \(k\) are net complements if \(S_{lk} = \partial h_l/\partial p_k < 0\) (compensated demand for \(l\) falls when \(p_k\) rises). They are net substitutes if \(S_{lk} > 0\).
Note: By symmetry of \(S\), the relationship is symmetric: \(l\) is a net substitute for \(k\) iff \(k\) is a net substitute for \(l\).
crumbs: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation

title: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation
text:
13.6 Compensating and Equivalent Variation






NoteDefinition 2.5.4 — Compensating Variation



The Compensating Variation (CV) of a price change from \(p^0\) to \(p^1\) (with wealth \(w\)) is: \[CV = e(p^0, \bar{u}^0) - e(p^1, \bar{u}^0) = w - e(p^1, v(p^0, w))\] where \(\bar{u}^0 = v(p^0, w)\). CV is the amount of wealth that must be given to the consumer after the price change to restore the original utility level.








NoteDefinition 2.5.5 — Equivalent Variation



The Equivalent Variation (EV) of a price change from \(p^0\) to \(p^1\) is: \[EV = e(p^1, \bar{u}^1) - e(p^0, \bar{u}^1) = e(p^1, v(p^1, w)) - e(p^0, v(p^1, w)) = w - e(p^0, v(p^1, w))\] where \(\bar{u}^1 = v(p^1, w)\). EV is the amount of wealth that would make the consumer indifferent between the price change and receiving the wealth transfer at old prices.








CautionRemark 2.5.3 — CV, EV, and Consumer Surplus



For a single price change in good \(l\) from \(p_l^0\) to \(p_l^1\): \[CV = -\int_{p_l^0}^{p_l^1} h_l(p_l, p_{-l}, \bar{u}^0) \, dp_l, \qquad EV = -\int_{p_l^0}^{p_l^1} h_l(p_l, p_{-l}, \bar{u}^1) \, dp_l\] The area under the ordinary (Marshallian) demand curve (consumer surplus) lies between CV and EV (for normal goods). CV and EV coincide with consumer surplus only in the quasi-linear case (no income effects).
crumbs: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation

title: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation
section: 13.7 Empirical Link: Slutsky Symmetry as a Testable Restriction
text:
13.7 Empirical Link: Slutsky Symmetry as a Testable Restriction
The Slutsky symmetry conditions \(S_{lk} = S_{kl}\) are the key testable restrictions on demand systems. In applied demand analysis, one estimates a demand system \(x(p,w)\) and tests whether the implied Slutsky matrix is symmetric and NSD. These restrictions reduce the number of free parameters in a flexible demand system (e.g., AIDS, QUAIDS) by imposing cross-equation restrictions that can be tested with standard Wald, LR, or LM tests. If symmetry is rejected, the estimated demand system is not rationalizable by any well-behaved utility function, which has implications for the validity of welfare calculations based on CV and EV.
crumbs: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation

title: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation
text:
13.8 Economic Intuition
The Slutsky decomposition gives a complete picture of how demand responds to price changes. The substitution effect always works in the expected direction: a price increase for good \(l\) leads to substitution away from \(l\) toward other goods. The income effect depends on whether the good is normal (strengthening the demand reduction) or inferior (partially or wholly counteracting it). A Giffen good requires a very strong inferiority: the income loss from the higher price of a good that constitutes a large share of spending must swamp the substitution effect. Such goods are theoretically possible but empirically rare.
The symmetry of \(S\) reflects the underlying structure of preferences: the marginal willingness to substitute \(l\) for \(k\) (at constant utility) is the same as the marginal willingness to substitute \(k\) for \(l\). This symmetry is a restriction that preferences impose on the demand system — if it is violated in data, the data cannot be rationalized by any well-behaved utility function.
crumbs: 12  2.5 — Duality and the Slutsky Equation

text:
