title: 13  2.6 — Revealed Preference
text: 14 2.6 — Revealed Preference

title: 13  2.6 — Revealed Preference
text:
14.1 Context
Revealed preference theory, initiated by Samuelson (1938) and extended by Houthakker (1950) and Afriat (1967), provides a nonparametric approach to consumer theory. Rather than assuming a specific utility function and deriving its properties, revealed preference theory asks: what does observed demand behavior tell us about the consumer’s underlying preferences? This approach is attractive because it connects theory directly to observable data. The Weak Axiom of Revealed Preference (WARP) is the minimum consistency requirement on demand behavior, implying the negative semidefiniteness of the Slutsky matrix. The Strong Axiom (SARP) adds transitivity and is equivalent to rationalizability by a utility function. Afriat’s theorem provides the definitive answer for finite datasets: a demand system is rationalizable if and only if it satisfies GARP, and provides an explicit construction of a rationalizing utility function.

title: 13  2.6 — Revealed Preference
text:
14.2 Revealed Preference Relations






NoteDefinition 2.6.1 — Directly Revealed Preferred



Given a demand function \(x(p,w)\), bundle \(x\) is directly revealed preferred to bundle \(y\) (written \(x \, R^0 \, y\), or “\(x\) is revealed preferred to \(y\)”) if there exist \((p, w)\) such that \(x = x(p, w)\) and \(p \cdot y \leq w\).
Interpretation: at the prices when \(x\) was chosen, \(y\) was also affordable, yet the consumer chose \(x\). This reveals that the consumer considers \(x\) at least as good as \(y\).








NoteDefinition 2.6.2 — Revealed Preferred (Transitive Closure)



Bundle \(x\) is revealed preferred to \(y\) (written \(x \, R \, y\)) if there exists a chain \(x = z^0, z^1, \ldots, z^k = y\) such that \(z^m \, R^0 \, z^{m+1}\) for all \(m = 0, \ldots, k-1\). This is the transitive closure of \(R^0\).

title: 13  2.6 — Revealed Preference
text:
14.3 Weak Axiom of Revealed Preference (WARP)






NoteDefinition 2.6.3 — Weak Axiom of Revealed Preference (WARP)



A demand function \(x(p,w)\) satisfies WARP if: for all \((p, w)\) and \((p', w')\), \[\text{if } p \cdot x(p',w') \leq w \text{ and } x(p',w') \neq x(p,w), \text{ then } p' \cdot x(p,w) > w'\] Equivalently: if \(x(p',w')\) was affordable at \((p,w)\) but not chosen, then \(x(p,w)\) must not be affordable at \((p', w')\).
In the revealed preference language: if \(x(p,w) \, R^0 \, x(p',w')\), then \(\neg [x(p',w') \, R^0 \, x(p,w)]\) (unless the two bundles are equal).








CautionRemark 2.6.1 — WARP and Consistency



WARP is a minimal consistency requirement: it says the consumer does not directly contradict herself. If she chose \(x\) over \(y\) when \(y\) was affordable (revealing \(x \succsim y\)), she cannot later choose \(y\) when \(x\) is affordable (which would reveal \(y \succsim x\)), unless \(x = y\). WARP is implied by utility maximization, but is weaker: it does not require transitivity.

title: 13  2.6 — Revealed Preference
section: 14.4 WARP Implies NSD of the Slutsky Matrix
text:
14.4 WARP Implies NSD of the Slutsky Matrix






ImportantTheorem 2.6.1 — WARP and Negative Semidefiniteness



Suppose demand \(x(p,w)\) satisfies Walras’ Law, homogeneity of degree zero, and WARP. Then the Slutsky matrix \(S(p,w)\) is negative semidefinite.








TipProof





We follow the MWG (Ch. 2) argument.
Setup: We want to show \(z^\top S(p,w) z \leq 0\) for all \(z \in \mathbb{R}^L\). Consider a compensated price change: let \(p' = p + \Delta p\) and \(w' = w + \Delta w\) where the wealth adjustment compensates so that \(x(p,w)\) remains affordable at \((p', w')\): \(w' = p' \cdot x(p,w) = w + \Delta p \cdot x(p,w)\). This is the Slutsky compensation: wealth is adjusted to keep the original bundle affordable.
Step 1: With Slutsky compensation, \(x(p,w)\) is affordable at \((p', w')\): \(p' \cdot x(p,w) = w'\) by construction.
Step 2: By WARP: if \(x(p',w') \neq x(p,w)\), then \(x(p,w)\) cannot be affordable at \((p', w')\) when \(x(p', w')\) is chosen at \((p, w)\)… wait, we need to be careful about the direction. With Slutsky compensation, \(p' \cdot x(p,w) = w'\), so \(x(p,w)\) is affordable at \((p', w')\). If \(x(p', w') \neq x(p,w)\), then \(x(p,w)\) is affordable at \((p', w')\) but not chosen (instead \(x(p', w')\) is chosen). By WARP, it must be that \(x(p', w')\) is NOT affordable at \((p, w)\): \(p \cdot x(p', w') > w\).
Step 3: Walras’ Law at \((p', w')\): \(p' \cdot x(p',w') = w'\). So: \[p \cdot x(p',w') > w = p \cdot x(p,w) \quad (\text{by Walras' Law at } (p,w))\] Wait, \(p \cdot x(p,w) = w\) by Walras’ Law. So: \[p \cdot x(p', w') > w = p \cdot x(p, w)\] Therefore: \(p \cdot [x(p',w') - x(p,w)] > 0\).
Step 4: From Walras’ Law at \((p', w')\): \(p' \cdot x(p',w') = w'\). And \(w' = p' \cdot x(p,w)\). So: \[p' \cdot [x(p',w') - x(p,w)] = w' - p' \cdot x(p,w) = 0\] Hence \(\Delta p \cdot [x(p',w') - x(p,w)] = (p' - p) \cdot \Delta x = -p \cdot \Delta x\)… let \(\Delta x = x(p',w') - x(p,w)\): \[p' \cdot \Delta x = 0 \implies (p + \Delta p) \cdot \Delta x = 0 \implies p \cdot \Delta x + \Delta p \cdot \Delta x = 0\] Combined with Step 3 (\(p \cdot \Delta x > 0\)): \(\Delta p \cdot \Delta x < 0\).
Step 5: Taking \(\Delta p \to 0\) (differentiating): \(\Delta p \cdot \Delta x \approx \Delta p \cdot [S(p,w) \Delta p]\) (to first order, since the Slutsky-compensated change in demand equals \(S \Delta p\) to first order). Therefore \(\Delta p^\top S(p,w) \Delta p \leq 0\) for all \(\Delta p\), with equality when \(\Delta x = 0\) (i.e., \(x(p',w') = x(p,w)\)). This establishes NSD of \(S\). \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.6.2 — WARP vs. Utility Maximization



WARP is necessary but not sufficient for full utility maximization. It implies \(S\) is NSD but does not imply symmetry of \(S\). Full rationalizability (existence of a utility function) requires additionally transitivity, which is encoded in SARP or GARP.

title: 13  2.6 — Revealed Preference
text:
14.5 Strong Axiom of Revealed Preference (SARP)






NoteDefinition 2.6.4 — Strong Axiom of Revealed Preference (SARP)



A demand function \(x(p,w)\) satisfies SARP if: for all \(x \neq y\), \(x \, R \, y\) implies \(\neg (y \, R \, x)\).
That is, there are no cycles in the revealed preference relation \(R\) (the transitive closure of \(R^0\)).








ImportantProposition 2.6.1 — SARP and Rationalizability



A demand function satisfies SARP if and only if it is consistent with maximization of a strictly monotone and strictly convex utility function.








TipProof





(Sketch.) The key step is to show that SARP implies the demand can be rationalized by a utility function. Given SARP, define \(u(x)\) as the “length” of the longest revealed preference chain strictly below \(x\); this gives a well-defined function (no cycles by SARP). One then verifies that \(u\) is a utility function that rationalizes the demand. Conversely, if \(u\) rationalizes demand, then \(x \, R^0 \, y\) means \(u(x) \geq u(y)\), and transitivity of \(\geq\) implies SARP. \(\square\)

title: 13  2.6 — Revealed Preference
text:
14.6 Afriat’s Theorem






NoteDefinition 2.6.5 — GARP (Generalized Axiom of Revealed Preference)



Given finite data \(\{(p^t, x^t)\}_{t=1}^T\) where \(p^t \cdot x^t = w^t\) (Walras’ Law imposed), the data satisfies GARP if: for all \(t, s\), \(x^t \, R \, x^s\) implies \(p^s \cdot x^t \geq p^s \cdot x^s = w^s\).
Equivalently: if the transitive closure of revealed preference ranks \(x^t\) above \(x^s\), then \(x^s\) is not strictly cheaper than \(x^t\) at prices \(p^s\). (GARP allows for weak inequality — \(p^s \cdot x^t \geq w^s\) — in contrast to SARP which requires strict inequality for different bundles.)








ImportantTheorem 2.6.2 — Afriat’s Theorem



Given finite data \(\{(p^t, x^t)\}_{t=1}^T\) with \(p^t \gg 0\) and \(x^t \geq 0\), the following statements are equivalent: 1. There exists a locally nonsatiated utility function \(u\) that rationalizes the data: \(x^t = \arg\max_{p^t \cdot x \leq w^t} u(x)\) for all \(t\). 2. The data satisfies GARP. 3. There exist numbers \(\{U^t, \lambda^t\}_{t=1}^T\) with \(\lambda^t > 0\) such that for all \(t, s\): \[U^t \leq U^s + \lambda^s p^s \cdot (x^t - x^s) \quad \text{(Afriat inequalities)}\] 4. There exists a continuous, concave, monotone utility function that rationalizes the data.








TipProof





We sketch the key directions.
(1) \(\Rightarrow\) (2): If \(u\) rationalizes the data and \(x^t \, R^0 \, x^s\) (i.e., \(p^t \cdot x^s \leq w^t\)), then since \(x^t\) was chosen over \(x^s\) at prices \(p^t\), we have \(u(x^t) \geq u(x^s)\). By transitivity, \(x^t \, R \, x^s\) implies \(u(x^t) \geq u(x^s)\), so \(x^s\) is not strictly cheaper than \(x^t\) at \(p^s\). More precisely, if \(p^s \cdot x^t < w^s = p^s \cdot x^s\), then \(x^t\) would be strictly cheaper than \(x^s\) at prices \(p^s\), meaning \(x^s\) would not minimize… wait, this direction: if \(u(x^t) \geq u(x^s)\) and \(x^s\) is optimal at \(p^s\), then \(p^s \cdot x^t \geq p^s \cdot x^s = w^s\) (otherwise \(x^t\) would be feasible and better, contradicting optimality of \(x^s\)). Hence GARP holds.
(2) \(\Rightarrow\) (3): The Afriat inequalities can be derived from GARP via a linear programming argument (Varian 1982). GARP ensures no cycles in a certain directed graph, which by a theorem of network theory (Farkas’ lemma) guarantees the existence of numbers \(\{U^t, \lambda^t\}\) satisfying the Afriat inequalities.
(3) \(\Rightarrow\) (4): Given \(\{U^t, \lambda^t\}\), define: \[u(x) = \min_t \{U^t + \lambda^t p^t \cdot (x - x^t)\}\] This is a minimum of affine functions, hence continuous and concave. We verify it rationalizes the data: \(u(x^t) = U^t\) (the minimum is achieved at \(s = t\)), and for any \(x\) with \(p^t \cdot x \leq w^t = p^t \cdot x^t\), we have \(u(x) \leq U^t + \lambda^t p^t \cdot (x - x^t) \leq U^t = u(x^t)\) (using \(\lambda^t > 0\) and \(p^t \cdot (x - x^t) \leq 0\)). Hence \(x^t\) maximizes \(u\) over the budget set.
(4) \(\Rightarrow\) (1): Trivial, since (4) gives a specific utility function rationalizing the data. \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.6.3 — Varian’s GARP Test



Varian (1982) provided a computationally efficient algorithm (based on Warshall’s algorithm for transitive closure of a directed graph) to check GARP for any finite dataset. This makes Afriat’s theorem practically useful: given observed price-quantity data, one can test rationality without assuming any parametric functional form for utility. This is the foundation of nonparametric demand analysis.








NoteExample 2.6.1 — WARP Violation



Suppose \(L = 2\) and we observe two data points: - Observation 1: \(p^1 = (1, 2)\), \(w^1 = 4\), \(x^1 = (2, 1)\) - Observation 2: \(p^2 = (2, 1)\), \(w^2 = 4\), \(x^2 = (1, 2)\)
Check: \(p^1 \cdot x^2 = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 5 > 4 = w^1\): \(x^2\) was not affordable at prices \(p^1\). Hence we cannot say \(x^1 \, R^0 \, x^2\).
\(p^2 \cdot x^1 = 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 5 > 4 = w^2\): \(x^1\) was not affordable at prices \(p^2\). Hence \(x^2 \, R^0 \, x^1\) does not hold either.
No revealed preference violation: WARP is satisfied. Now consider instead \(x^2 = (0, 2)\): \(p^1 \cdot x^2 = 0 + 4 = 4 \leq w^1\): \(x^2\) is affordable at \(p^1\), yet \(x^1\) was chosen, so \(x^1 \, R^0 \, x^2\). \(p^2 \cdot x^1 = 4 + 1 = 5 > 4\): \(x^1\) is not affordable at \(p^2\) when \(x^2\) is chosen. WARP holds.

title: 13  2.6 — Revealed Preference
text:
14.7 Empirical Link
GARP testing (Varian 1982) is the foundation of nonparametric demand analysis. Using household-level panel data, one can test whether individual consumers satisfy rationality without imposing any parametric functional form. Violations of GARP measure “how irrational” behavior is (using money-pump indices or Afriat efficiency scores). The literature finds that most households satisfy GARP most of the time, but there are systematic violations associated with inattention, limited cognition, and changing household composition.
SARP and GARP are also used in partial identification of demand systems: even without full rationalizability, the revealed preference restrictions generate testable inequalities on counterfactual demand, which can be used to bound welfare effects of policy changes.

title: 13  2.6 — Revealed Preference
text:
14.8 Cross-References

2.5 Duality and Slutsky — Slutsky symmetry and NSD are the differential counterpart of WARP/SARP
2.7 Integrability — Integrability is the continuous analog of SARP/GARP
2.3 Utility Maximization Problem — The UMP is the behavioral model whose consistency WARP tests

title: 14  2.7 — Integrability
text: 15 2.7 — Integrability

title: 14  2.7 — Integrability
text:
15.1 Context
The integrability problem asks the inverse question to the UMP: given an observed demand system \(x(p,w)\), does there exist a utility function that rationalizes it? The utility maximization problem tells us that demand derived from a well-behaved utility function must satisfy certain properties: homogeneity of degree zero, Walras’ Law, and Slutsky symmetry plus NSD. The integrability theorem (Hurwicz-Uzawa) establishes that these conditions are not only necessary but also sufficient for local (and, under appropriate conditions, global) rationalizability. This gives a clean characterization of rational demand systems. The integrability problem is also practically important: given estimated demand parameters, one can use integrability to recover the expenditure function and perform welfare calculations (CV, EV) that are consistent with an underlying preference ordering.

title: 14  2.7 — Integrability
text:
15.2 The Integrability Problem






NoteDefinition 2.7.1 — Rationalizability



A demand function \(x: \mathbb{R}^L_{++} \times \mathbb{R}_{++} \to \mathbb{R}^L_+\) is rationalizable if there exists a locally nonsatiated utility function \(u: \mathbb{R}^L_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) such that \(x(p,w)\) solves the UMP \(\max_{p \cdot y \leq w} u(y)\) for all \((p,w)\).

title: 14  2.7 — Integrability
text:
15.3 Necessary Conditions






ImportantProposition 2.7.1 — Necessary Conditions for Rationalizability



If \(x(p,w)\) is the Walrasian demand derived from a locally nonsatiated, differentiable utility function, then \(x(p,w)\) satisfies: 1. Homogeneity of degree zero: \(x(\alpha p, \alpha w) = x(p,w)\) for all \(\alpha > 0\) 2. Walras’ Law: \(p \cdot x(p,w) = w\) for all \((p,w)\) 3. Slutsky symmetry: \(S_{lk}(p,w) = S_{kl}(p,w)\) for all \(l, k\), where \(S_{lk} = \partial x_l/\partial p_k + x_k \partial x_l/\partial w\) 4. Slutsky NSD: the Slutsky matrix \(S(p,w)\) is negative semidefinite








TipProof






and (2) were proven in 2.3 Utility Maximization Problem (Propositions 2.3.3 and 2.3.4).
and (4) were proven in 2.5 Duality and Slutsky (Proposition 2.5.1): \(S = \partial h/\partial p\) and the Hessian of the concave expenditure function \(e\) is symmetric (Young’s theorem) and NSD (concavity). \(\square\)

title: 14  2.7 — Integrability
text:
15.4 The Integrability Theorem






ImportantTheorem 2.7.1 — Hurwicz-Uzawa Integrability Theorem



Let \(x: \mathbb{R}^L_{++} \times \mathbb{R}_{++} \to \mathbb{R}^L_+\) be a \(C^1\) demand function satisfying: 1. Homogeneity of degree zero in \((p,w)\) 2. Walras’ Law: \(p \cdot x(p,w) = w\) 3. The Slutsky matrix \(S(p,w)\) is symmetric and negative semidefinite
Then \(x(p,w)\) is locally rationalizable: for every \((p^0, w^0)\), there exists a neighborhood \(U\) of \((p^0, w^0)\) and a locally nonsatiated utility function \(u\) such that \(x(p,w)\) solves the UMP on \(U\).
If additionally \(S(p,w)\) is everywhere NSD (not just locally) and the system satisfies appropriate global conditions, the rationalization is global.








TipProof





Step 1: From demand to Hicksian demand and the expenditure function. The strategy is to construct \(e(p, \bar{u})\) by integrating the ODE system implied by Shephard’s Lemma: \(\partial e/\partial p_l = h_l(p, \bar{u})\).
Fix a utility level \(\bar{u}\) and initial prices \(p^0\). Define the Hicksian demand implicitly: by duality (Theorem 2.5.1), \(h(p, \bar{u}) = x(p, e(p, \bar{u}))\). Substituting into Shephard’s Lemma, we get a system of ODEs: \[\frac{\partial e(p, \bar{u})}{\partial p_l} = x_l(p, e(p, \bar{u})), \quad l = 1, \ldots, L\] with initial condition \(e(p^0, \bar{u}) = w^0\) (where \(v(p^0, w^0) = \bar{u}\)).
Step 2: Integrability condition (Frobenius theorem). The ODE system \(\partial e/\partial p_l = x_l(p, e)\) has a (locally unique) solution if and only if the system is integrable — i.e., the cross-partial conditions are satisfied: \[\frac{\partial^2 e}{\partial p_k \partial p_l} = \frac{\partial^2 e}{\partial p_l \partial p_k}\] Substituting the ODE: \(\partial^2 e/\partial p_k \partial p_l = \partial x_l/\partial p_k + x_k \partial x_l/\partial e = \partial x_l/\partial p_k + x_k \partial x_l/\partial w\) (since \(\partial e/\partial p_k = x_k\), and \(\partial x_l/\partial e = \partial x_l/\partial w\)).
The integrability condition becomes: \[\frac{\partial x_l}{\partial p_k} + x_k \frac{\partial x_l}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial x_k}{\partial p_l} + x_l \frac{\partial x_k}{\partial w}\] i.e., \(S_{lk} = S_{kl}\): Slutsky symmetry. Thus, by the Frobenius theorem on integrability of PDE systems, the existence of a solution to the ODE system is equivalent to Slutsky symmetry.
Step 3: Constructing the utility function. Once we have the expenditure function \(e(p, \bar{u})\) (constructed by solving the ODE), we define the utility function via its upper contour sets. For each wealth level \(w\) and price vector \(p\), define: \[u(x) = \max\{\bar{u} : e(p, \bar{u}) \leq p \cdot x \text{ for some } p \text{ supporting } x\}\] Under appropriate regularity (which follows from NSD of \(S\) ensuring \(e\) is concave in \(p\) and thus well-behaved), this construction yields a valid utility function. Alternatively, one can use the expenditure function to define preferences directly via indifference curves.
Step 4: Verification. One must verify that the constructed \(u\) rationalizes the original demand \(x(p,w)\). This follows from the duality identities: if \(e(p, \bar{u})\) satisfies Shephard’s Lemma with \(h_l = x_l(p, e)\), then the demand system derived from maximizing \(u\) (defined from \(e\)) recovers \(x(p,w)\). The NSD condition ensures \(e\) is concave, which is needed for \(u\) to be quasi-concave. \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.7.1 — The Frobenius Theorem



The Frobenius theorem states that a system of PDEs \(\partial f/\partial x_i = g_i(x, f)\) has a local solution if and only if the integrability conditions \(\partial g_i/\partial x_j + g_j \partial g_i/\partial f = \partial g_j/\partial x_i + g_i \partial g_j/\partial f\) hold (i.e., the vector field \((g_i)\) is integrable). In our application, \(f = e\), \(x = p\), \(g_l = x_l(p, e)\), and the integrability condition reduces exactly to Slutsky symmetry.

title: 14  2.7 — Integrability
section: 15.5 Recovering the Expenditure Function from Hicksian Demand
text:
15.5 Recovering the Expenditure Function from Hicksian Demand
Given \(h(p, \bar{u})\) satisfying Shephard’s conditions (homogeneity of degree zero in \(p\), symmetry, NSD), we recover \(e(p, \bar{u})\) by solving: \[\frac{d}{dt} e(p(t), \bar{u}) = \sum_l \frac{\partial e}{\partial p_l} \dot{p}_l(t) = \sum_l h_l(p(t), \bar{u}) \dot{p}_l(t)\] along any path \(p(t)\) from \(p^0\) to \(p\). The symmetry of \(S = \partial h/\partial p\) (Slutsky symmetry) guarantees path-independence of this line integral. Hence: \[e(p, \bar{u}) = e(p^0, \bar{u}) + \int_{p^0}^{p} h(q, \bar{u}) \cdot dq\] where the integral is along any path from \(p^0\) to \(p\).






NoteExample 2.7.1 — Recovering Utility from a Two-Good Demand System



Suppose \(L = 2\) and we observe: \[x_1(p, w) = \frac{\alpha w}{p_1}, \qquad x_2(p, w) = \frac{(1-\alpha) w}{p_2}\] for some \(\alpha \in (0,1)\). (This is the Walrasian demand for Cobb-Douglas utility \(u = x_1^\alpha x_2^{1-\alpha}\).)
Step 1: Verify necessary conditions. - Homogeneity: \(x_l(\alpha p, \alpha w) = \alpha w/(\alpha p_l) \cdot [\text{appropriate factor}]\): \(x_1(\lambda p, \lambda w) = \alpha \lambda w / (\lambda p_1) = \alpha w/p_1 = x_1(p,w)\). ✓ - Walras’ Law: \(p_1 x_1 + p_2 x_2 = \alpha w + (1-\alpha) w = w\). ✓ - Slutsky matrix: \(S_{11} = \partial x_1/\partial p_1 + x_1 \partial x_1/\partial w = -\alpha w/p_1^2 + (\alpha w/p_1) \cdot (\alpha/p_1) = -\alpha w/p_1^2 + \alpha^2 w/p_1^2 = -\alpha(1-\alpha)w/p_1^2 \leq 0\). ✓ - Cross-Slutsky: \(S_{12} = \partial x_1/\partial p_2 + x_2 \partial x_1/\partial w = 0 + \frac{(1-\alpha)w}{p_2} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{p_1} = \frac{\alpha(1-\alpha)w}{p_1 p_2}\). - \(S_{21} = \partial x_2/\partial p_1 + x_1 \partial x_2/\partial w = 0 + \frac{\alpha w}{p_1} \cdot \frac{1-\alpha}{p_2} = \frac{\alpha(1-\alpha)w}{p_1 p_2} = S_{12}\). Symmetry ✓.
Step 2: Integrate to find \(e(p, \bar{u})\). The ODE system is: \(\partial e/\partial p_l = h_l(p, \bar{u}) = x_l(p, e) = \alpha e/p_1\) (for \(l=1\)) and \((1-\alpha)e/p_2\) (for \(l=2\)).
This is a system of linear ODEs. Integrating: \(d(\ln e) = \alpha \, d(\ln p_1) + (1-\alpha) d(\ln p_2)\), which integrates to \(\ln e = \alpha \ln p_1 + (1-\alpha) \ln p_2 + C(\bar{u})\), or: \[e(p, \bar{u}) = A(\bar{u}) \cdot p_1^\alpha p_2^{1-\alpha}\] where \(A(\bar{u})\) is determined by the initial condition. Setting \(e(p, v(p,w)) = w\): \(A(v(p,w)) = w/(p_1^\alpha p_2^{1-\alpha})\). Since \(v(p,w) = u(x(p,w)) = ({\alpha w}/{p_1})^\alpha \cdot ({(1-\alpha)w}/{p_2})^{1-\alpha} = w \cdot \alpha^\alpha(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha}/p_1^\alpha p_2^{1-\alpha}\), we get \(A(\bar{u}) = \bar{u}/(\alpha^\alpha(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha})\).
Therefore: \[e(p, \bar{u}) = \frac{\bar{u}}{\alpha^\alpha(1-\alpha)^{1-\alpha}} \cdot p_1^\alpha p_2^{1-\alpha}\] This is the correct Cobb-Douglas expenditure function, confirming that the demand system is rationalized by \(u = x_1^\alpha x_2^{1-\alpha}\).

title: 14  2.7 — Integrability
section: 15.6 Practical Implications for Structural Demand Estimation
text:
15.6 Practical Implications for Structural Demand Estimation
The integrability theorem has a crucial practical consequence for structural demand estimation. In applied work, one typically:

Specify a demand system \(x(p,w; \theta)\) with parameters \(\theta\) — e.g., the Almost Ideal Demand System (AIDS) of Deaton-Muellbauer.
Estimate \(\theta\) from price and quantity data.
Impose integrability restrictions: Slutsky symmetry (\(S_{lk} = S_{kl}\)) as cross-equation restrictions; homogeneity of degree zero as within-equation restrictions; NSD as inequality restrictions.
Recover the expenditure function by integrating the estimated Hicksian demand system.
Compute welfare measures (CV, EV) from the recovered expenditure function.

Step 3 is possible precisely because of the Hurwicz-Uzawa theorem: if the estimated demand satisfies Slutsky symmetry, homogeneity, and NSD, then an underlying utility function exists, and welfare calculations are internally consistent (i.e., they correspond to actual preference-based comparisons). Without imposing integrability, welfare measures derived from the estimated demand system may be internally inconsistent.






CautionRemark 2.7.2 — Global vs. Local Integrability



The Hurwicz-Uzawa theorem guarantees local integrability. Global integrability — the existence of a single utility function rationalizing demand everywhere — requires additional conditions ensuring the ODE solutions are well-defined globally and that the resulting \(e(p, \bar{u})\) is globally well-behaved. In practice, parametric demand systems (AIDS, QUAIDS) are designed to be globally integrable by construction, with the integrability conditions imposed as parameter restrictions.

text:

title: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation
text: 16 2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation
crumbs: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation

title: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation
text:
16.1 Context
When moving from individual consumer theory to market-level analysis, one faces the aggregation problem: under what conditions can we treat the market as if it were a single representative consumer? In general, aggregate demand depends not only on aggregate wealth but also on its distribution across consumers, making the aggregate demand function poorly behaved. Gorman’s (1953) theorem provides the precise conditions under which aggregation is clean: individual indirect utility functions must have the Gorman polar form, implying parallel linear Engel curves. This is a strong restriction, satisfied by homothetic preferences and quasi-linear preferences but not by general preferences. When the Gorman conditions fail, the Sonnenschein-Mantel-Debreu (SMD) theorem shows that aggregate excess demand satisfies essentially no restrictions beyond those already imposed by individual optimization — a sobering result for the macroeconomic representative-agent hypothesis.
crumbs: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation

title: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation
text:
16.2 Setup
Consider an economy with \(I\) consumers, \(L\) goods, and prices \(p \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\). Consumer \(i\) has wealth \(w^i > 0\) and Walrasian demand \(x^i(p, w^i)\).






NoteDefinition 2.8.1 — Aggregate Demand



Aggregate demand is: \[X(p, w^1, \ldots, w^I) = \sum_{i=1}^I x^i(p, w^i)\] where \(w^i\) are individual wealth levels. Aggregate wealth is \(W = \sum_i w^i\). We say aggregate demand depends only on \((p, W)\) — is wealth-distribution-free — if \(X\) can be written as \(X(p, W)\) for some function \(X\) that does not depend on how \(W\) is distributed.
crumbs: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation

title: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation
text:
16.3 Gorman Aggregation






NoteDefinition 2.8.2 — Engel Curve



For fixed prices \(p\), the Engel curve of consumer \(i\) for good \(l\) is the function \(w^i \mapsto x^i_l(p, w^i)\). It describes how demand for good \(l\) changes as income varies. The Engel curve system is \(w^i \mapsto x^i(p, w^i) \in \mathbb{R}^L\).








ImportantTheorem 2.8.1 — Gorman’s Aggregation Theorem (Gorman 1953, 1961)



Suppose each \(x^i(p, w^i)\) satisfies Walras’ Law. Aggregate demand \(\sum_i x^i(p, w^i)\) is independent of the distribution of wealth — i.e., depends only on \((p, W)\) with \(W = \sum_i w^i\) — for all distributions \((w^1, \ldots, w^I)\) summing to \(W\) if and only if each consumer’s demand takes the form: \[x^i(p, w^i) = a^i(p) + b(p) \cdot w^i\] for some vector-valued functions \(a^i(p) \in \mathbb{R}^L\) (consumer-specific) and \(b(p) \in \mathbb{R}^L\) (common to all consumers). That is, Engel curves are linear in \(w^i\) with identical slopes \(b(p)\) across consumers.








TipProof





Necessity (\(\Rightarrow\)): Suppose aggregate demand depends only on \((p, W)\): \(\sum_i x^i(p, w^i) = X(p, W)\) for all \((w^1, \ldots, w^I)\) with \(\sum_i w^i = W\).
Fix \(p\) and consider two wealth distributions \((w^1, \ldots, w^I)\) and \((w^1 + \varepsilon, w^2 - \varepsilon, w^3, \ldots, w^I)\) — a transfer of \(\varepsilon\) from consumer 2 to consumer 1. Both sum to \(W\), so by assumption, aggregate demands are equal: \[x^1(p, w^1 + \varepsilon) + x^2(p, w^2 - \varepsilon) + \sum_{i \geq 3} x^i(p, w^i) = x^1(p, w^1) + x^2(p, w^2) + \sum_{i \geq 3} x^i(p, w^i)\] Canceling the common terms: \[x^1(p, w^1 + \varepsilon) - x^1(p, w^1) = x^2(p, w^2) - x^2(p, w^2 - \varepsilon)\] Dividing by \(\varepsilon\) and taking \(\varepsilon \to 0\): \[\frac{\partial x^1(p, w^1)}{\partial w^1} = \frac{\partial x^2(p, w^2)}{\partial w^2}\] This holds for all \(w^1, w^2\). Hence \(\partial x^i_l/\partial w^i\) must be the same function for all consumers \(i\) and all wealth levels \(w^i\): \(\partial x^i_l(p, w^i)/\partial w^i = b_l(p)\) for some function \(b_l(p)\) that does not depend on \(i\) or \(w^i\).
Integrating over \(w^i\): \(x^i_l(p, w^i) = b_l(p) w^i + a^i_l(p)\) for some integration constant \(a^i_l(p)\) (which may depend on \(i\) but not on \(w^i\)). In vector form: \(x^i(p, w^i) = a^i(p) + b(p) w^i\).
Sufficiency (\(\Leftarrow\)): If \(x^i(p, w^i) = a^i(p) + b(p) w^i\) for all \(i\), then: \[\sum_i x^i(p, w^i) = \sum_i a^i(p) + b(p) \sum_i w^i = A(p) + b(p) W\] where \(A(p) = \sum_i a^i(p)\). This depends only on \((p, W)\), not on the distribution. \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.8.1 — Interpretation of Gorman Conditions



The Gorman condition — linear Engel curves with identical slopes — has a strong economic interpretation. It says that if we transfer one dollar from any consumer to any other consumer, aggregate demand does not change (only the redistribution matters, but the slopes are the same so the effects cancel). This is a strong income distribution neutrality condition. It fails whenever different consumers have different marginal propensities to spend on different goods.
crumbs: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation

title: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation
text:
16.4 Gorman Polar Form






ImportantProposition 2.8.1 — Gorman Polar Form of Indirect Utility



The demand \(x^i(p, w^i) = a^i(p) + b(p) w^i\) is consistent with utility maximization if and only if consumer \(i\)’s indirect utility function takes the Gorman polar form: \[v^i(p, w^i) = \frac{w^i - a^i(p)}{b(p)} \cdot \phi(p)\] where, up to normalization, this simplifies to: \[v^i(p, w^i) = \frac{w^i - c^i(p)}{b(p)}\] for functions \(c^i(p)\) (individual “fixed costs”) and \(b(p)\) (common “price index”).








TipProof





Apply Roy’s Identity to \(v^i(p, w^i)\). With \(v^i(p, w^i) = [w^i - c^i(p)]/b(p)\): \[\frac{\partial v^i}{\partial w^i} = \frac{1}{b(p)}, \qquad \frac{\partial v^i}{\partial p_l} = \frac{-\partial c^i/\partial p_l \cdot b(p) - (w^i - c^i) \partial b/\partial p_l}{[b(p)]^2}\] By Roy’s Identity: \[x^i_l(p, w^i) = -\frac{\partial v^i/\partial p_l}{\partial v^i/\partial w^i} = \frac{\partial c^i/\partial p_l \cdot b(p) + (w^i - c^i) \partial b/\partial p_l}{b(p)}\] \(= \frac{\partial c^i(p)}{\partial p_l} + \frac{\partial \ln b(p)}{\partial p_l} (w^i - c^i(p))\) \(= \frac{\partial c^i}{\partial p_l} - c^i(p) \frac{\partial \ln b}{\partial p_l} + w^i \frac{\partial \ln b}{\partial p_l}\) \(= a^i_l(p) + b_l(p) w^i\) where \(a^i_l(p) = \partial c^i/\partial p_l - c^i \partial \ln b/\partial p_l\) and \(b_l(p) = \partial \ln b/\partial p_l\). This confirms the demand takes the Gorman linear form. \(\square\)
crumbs: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation

title: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation
text:
16.5 Implications of Gorman Aggregation
Homothetic preferences satisfy Gorman: If preferences are homothetic, then \(x^i(p, w^i) = x^i(p, 1) \cdot w^i\) (demand is proportional to wealth, with no constant term). This is the special case \(a^i(p) \equiv 0\) and \(b(p) = x^i(p, 1)\). The common slope requires all consumers to have identical homothetic preferences (same \(b(p)\)). Engel curves pass through the origin for all consumers.
Quasi-linear preferences satisfy Gorman: With \(u^i = x^i_1 + v^i(x^i_2, \ldots)\), the demand for goods \(2, \ldots, L\) is independent of wealth, so \(\partial x^i_l/\partial w^i = 0\) for \(l \geq 2\) (and \(= 1\) for \(l=1\)). This is also a special case with \(b(p)\) having first component 1 and remaining components 0.
PIGL and PIGLOG systems: Muellbauer (1975, 1976) identified more general demand systems compatible with aggregation: the Price-Independent Generalized Linear (PIGL) and Price-Independent Generalized Logarithmic (PIGLOG) systems. The Almost Ideal Demand System (AIDS) of Deaton-Muellbauer (1980) is a PIGLOG system, allowing linear Engel curves in \(\ln w\) — a slight generalization of Gorman that permits aggregation with a specific structure.
crumbs: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation

title: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation
section: 16.6 Failure of Aggregation: The Sonnenschein-Mantel-Debreu Theorem
text:
16.6 Failure of Aggregation: The Sonnenschein-Mantel-Debreu Theorem
When the Gorman conditions fail, aggregate demand depends on the wealth distribution, and essentially any reasonable function can arise as aggregate excess demand.






ImportantTheorem 2.8.2 — Sonnenschein-Mantel-Debreu Theorem



Let \(z: \mathbb{R}^L_{++} \to \mathbb{R}^L\) be any continuous function satisfying: 1. Homogeneity of degree zero: \(z(\alpha p) = z(p)\) for all \(\alpha > 0\) 2. Walras’ Law: \(p \cdot z(p) = 0\) for all \(p\)
Then for any compact set \(K \subset \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\), there exists an economy (with \(I \geq L\) consumers, each with a continuous, strongly monotone, strictly convex utility function) whose aggregate excess demand on \(K\) is exactly \(z(p)\).








TipProof





(Sketch of Debreu 1974 and Mantel 1974.) The proof is constructive: one builds \(I = L\) consumers, each with an appropriately chosen expenditure function, such that the sum of their Hicksian demands aggregates to give \(z(p)\) on \(K\). The key insight is that individual demands can be “mixed” arbitrarily by choosing appropriate utility functions and wealth distributions. The two conditions (homogeneity and Walras’ Law) are the only restrictions that survive aggregation from individual optimization. Since any aggregate excess demand satisfying just these two conditions is achievable, the aggregation process destroys all the Slutsky restrictions that individual demands satisfy. \(\square\)









CautionRemark 2.8.2 — Implications of SMD



The SMD theorem has profound implications: 1. For general equilibrium theory: Aggregate excess demand satisfies essentially no restrictions beyond homogeneity and Walras’ Law. In particular, it need not satisfy the gross substitutes property, which is why the uniqueness and stability of competitive equilibria cannot be established in full generality. 2. For the representative agent: The SMD theorem shows that there is generally no representative consumer whose preferences over aggregate bundles coincide with those of the heterogeneous population (in the sense of supporting the same competitive equilibria or welfare conclusions). 3. For macro models: The widespread use of representative-agent models in macroeconomics is justified only when preferences are homothetic or quasi-linear (special cases of Gorman) or as an approximation when distributional concerns are secondary.
crumbs: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation

title: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation
text:
16.7 Aggregation and the Representative Consumer






NoteDefinition 2.8.3 — Representative Consumer



A positive representative consumer exists for an economy if there is a utility function \(U: \mathbb{R}^L_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) and a wealth function \(W(p, w^1, \ldots, w^I)\) such that: \[\sum_i x^i(p, w^i) = X(p, W(p, w^1, \ldots, w^I))\] where \(X(p, W)\) solves \(\max_{p \cdot x \leq W} U(x)\).
A normative representative consumer additionally exists if the representative’s welfare can be used to rank social allocations: social welfare \(\mathcal{W} = V(p, W)\) (the indirect utility of the representative) is maximized at competitive equilibria, and changes in \(V\) correctly rank social welfare changes (i.e., the representative’s CV equals the sum of individual CVs).








ImportantProposition 2.8.2 — Gorman Implies Representative Consumer



If all consumers have Gorman polar form indirect utility \(v^i(p, w^i) = [w^i - c^i(p)]/b(p)\), then: 1. A positive representative consumer exists with indirect utility \(V(p, W) = [W - C(p)]/b(p)\), where \(C(p) = \sum_i c^i(p)\). 2. A normative representative consumer exists if individual welfare is measured by \(v^i\) (the sum of individual utilities \(\sum_i v^i\) is maximized by the competitive equilibrium, and equals \(V(p, W)\) up to a constant).








TipProof





By Gorman’s theorem, \(\sum_i x^i(p, w^i) = A(p) + b(p) W\) with \(A(p) = \sum_i a^i(p)\). This aggregate demand coincides with the Walrasian demand of a consumer with indirect utility \(V(p, W) = [W - C(p)]/b(p)\) (by Roy’s Identity applied to \(V\), which recovers \(X_l = -\partial V/\partial p_l / \partial V/\partial W\)). For the normative part: \(\sum_i v^i(p, w^i) = \sum_i [w^i - c^i(p)]/b(p) = [W - C(p)]/b(p) = V(p, W)\). Hence maximizing \(V\) is equivalent to maximizing \(\sum_i v^i\), which is Pareto-efficient under Gorman preferences. \(\square\)
crumbs: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation

title: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation
text:
16.8 Economic Intuition
The aggregation problem highlights the tension between tractability and generality in economic modeling. The Gorman condition is strong: it requires that a dollar of additional income has the same expenditure effects for rich and poor consumers (across all goods), which is empirically questionable. Yet without it, every distributional change affects aggregate demand, and the “market demand curve” becomes a poor summary statistic for market behavior.
In practice, the representative-agent assumption is often defended on one of three grounds: (1) calibration to macro data implicitly imposes homothetic preferences; (2) distributional effects are assumed second-order relative to aggregate wealth effects; (3) the model is explicitly a reduced form for a more complex heterogeneous-agent structure. The SMD theorem serves as a theoretical warning that (2) is not always innocuous, and motivates the growing literature on heterogeneous-agent models in macroeconomics and trade.
crumbs: 15  2.8 — Aggregate Demand and Gorman Aggregation

title: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory
text:
17.1 Problem 1 — Properties of CES Utility
Let \(u: \mathbb{R}^2_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) be the CES utility function with two goods: \[u(x_1, x_2) = (x_1^\rho + x_2^\rho)^{1/\rho}, \qquad \rho \in (-\infty, 1), \rho \neq 0\]
(a) Show that \(u\) is homogeneous of degree one: \(u(\lambda x) = \lambda u(x)\) for all \(\lambda > 0\). Conclude that CES preferences are homothetic.
(b) Compute \(\partial u/\partial x_l\) and form the bordered Hessian of \(u\). Use the bordered Hessian conditions to verify that \(u\) is strictly quasi-concave for \(\rho < 1\).
(c) The elasticity of substitution between goods 1 and 2 (along an indifference curve) is defined as: \[\sigma = -\frac{d \ln(x_1/x_2)}{d \ln(\partial u/\partial x_2 \cdot / \cdot \partial u/\partial x_1)}\bigg|_{u = \text{const}}\] Show that \(\sigma = 1/(1-\rho)\) for the CES utility function. Verify the limiting cases: as \(\rho \to 0\), \(\sigma \to 1\); as \(\rho \to 1\), \(\sigma \to \infty\); as \(\rho \to -\infty\), \(\sigma \to 0\).
(d) Show that as \(\rho \to 0\), \(u(x_1, x_2)\) converges (up to a monotone transformation) to the Cobb-Douglas function \(x_1^{1/2} x_2^{1/2}\). (Hint: take the logarithm of \(u\) and apply L’Hopital’s rule.)
(e) Show that as \(\rho \to -\infty\), \(u(x_1, x_2) \to \min(x_1, x_2)\). (Hint: write \(u = x_1 (1 + (x_2/x_1)^\rho)^{1/\rho}\) for \(x_1, x_2 > 0\) and analyze the limit.)
crumbs: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory

title: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory
section: 17.2 Problem 2 — Walrasian and Hicksian Demand for Cobb-Douglas
text:
17.2 Problem 2 — Walrasian and Hicksian Demand for Cobb-Douglas
Consider the Cobb-Douglas utility function \(u(x_1, x_2) = x_1^\alpha x_2^{1-\alpha}\) for \(\alpha \in (0,1)\).
(a) Walrasian demand: Solve the UMP to derive the Walrasian demand functions: \[x_1(p, w) = \frac{\alpha w}{p_1}, \qquad x_2(p, w) = \frac{(1-\alpha) w}{p_2}\] Show your work using the Lagrangian and KKT conditions. Verify that the solution satisfies (i) Walras’ Law, (ii) homogeneity of degree zero.
(b) Indirect utility: Derive the indirect utility function \(v(p,w)\). Verify that \(v\) is (i) homogeneous of degree zero, (ii) decreasing in \(p_l\), (iii) increasing in \(w\).
(c) Expenditure function: Use the result \(v(p, e(p, \bar{u})) = \bar{u}\) and your expression for \(v(p,w)\) to derive the expenditure function \(e(p, \bar{u})\).
(d) Hicksian demand: Apply Shephard’s Lemma to \(e(p, \bar{u})\) to derive \(h_l(p, \bar{u})\) for \(l = 1, 2\). Verify that \(h_l(p, v(p,w)) = x_l(p,w)\) for each \(l\) (confirming duality identity (3) from 2.5 Duality and Slutsky).
(e) Verify that the Hicksian demands satisfy: (i) homogeneity of degree zero in \(p\); (ii) \(h_l(p, \bar{u})\) is non-increasing in \(p_l\) (own-price effect); (iii) \(\partial h_1/\partial p_2 = \partial h_2/\partial p_1\) (symmetry of substitution effects).
crumbs: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory

title: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory
text:
17.3 Problem 3 — Slutsky Symmetry Verification
Consider the demand system for \(L = 3\) goods: \[x_1(p,w) = \frac{w}{3p_1}, \qquad x_2(p,w) = \frac{w}{3p_2}, \qquad x_3(p,w) = \frac{w}{3p_3}\]
(a) Verify Walras’ Law: \(\sum_l p_l x_l = w\).
(b) Compute the full \(3 \times 3\) Slutsky matrix \(S\) with entries \(S_{lk} = \partial x_l/\partial p_k + x_k \partial x_l/\partial w\).
(c) Verify that \(S\) is symmetric (\(S_{lk} = S_{kl}\) for all \(l \neq k\)).
(d) Verify that \(S\) is negative semidefinite by computing the eigenvalues of \(S\) or by checking the leading principal minors of \(-S\).
(e) Verify that \(Sp = 0\) (i.e., the Slutsky matrix times the price vector is zero). Provide the economic interpretation of this property.
(f) Identify the utility function that rationalizes this demand system. (Hint: apply the integrability procedure from 2.7 Integrability.)
crumbs: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory

(a) For each ordered pair \((t, s)\) with \(t \neq s\), compute \(p^t \cdot x^s\) and determine whether \(x^t \, R^0 \, x^s\) (i.e., whether bundle \(x^s\) was affordable at observation \(t\)’s prices and wealth). Construct the direct revealed preference table.
(b) Check whether the data satisfies WARP. For each pair where \(x^t \, R^0 \, x^s\), verify that \(\neg (x^s \, R^0 \, x^t)\).
(c) Construct the full revealed preference relation \(R\) (transitive closure of \(R^0\)). Does the data satisfy SARP?
(d) Check whether the data satisfies GARP. Recall GARP: if \(x^t \, R \, x^s\) (weakly revealed preferred), then \(p^s \cdot x^t \geq w^s\).
(e) If the data satisfies GARP, write down the Afriat inequalities \(U^t \leq U^s + \lambda^s p^s \cdot (x^t - x^s)\) for all \(t, s\) and find a feasible solution \(\{U^t, \lambda^t\}\) with \(\lambda^t > 0\).
(f) Using the feasible \(\{U^t, \lambda^t\}\) from (e), construct Afriat’s rationalizing utility function \(u(x) = \min_t \{U^t + \lambda^t p^t \cdot (x - x^t)\}\). Evaluate \(u\) at each observed bundle \(x^t\) and verify it rationalizes the data.
crumbs: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory

title: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory
section: 17.5 Problem 5 — Envelope Theorem and Roy’s Identity
text:
17.5 Problem 5 — Envelope Theorem and Roy’s Identity
Let \(u(x_1, x_2) = \min\{\alpha x_1, \beta x_2\}\) for \(\alpha, \beta > 0\) (Leontief utility).
(a) Explain why the utility function is not differentiable. Despite this, solve the UMP directly (without calculus) using the observation that at the optimum, \(\alpha x_1^* = \beta x_2^*\). Derive: \[x_1(p,w) = \frac{\beta w}{\beta p_1 + \alpha p_2}, \qquad x_2(p,w) = \frac{\alpha w}{\beta p_1 + \alpha p_2}\]
(b) Derive the indirect utility function \(v(p,w)\).
(c) Roy’s Identity is typically derived using the envelope theorem for differentiable utility functions. Verify Roy’s Identity directly for the Leontief case: compute \(-(\partial v/\partial p_l)/(\partial v/\partial w)\) and check it equals \(x_l(p,w)\) for \(l = 1, 2\).
(d) Now consider a smooth utility function \(u(x_1, x_2)\) with \(v(p,w)\) denoting its indirect utility. Provide a complete proof of Roy’s Identity using the envelope theorem applied to the Lagrangian of the UMP. Be explicit about what the envelope theorem states and how it applies.
(e) Derive the Hicksian demands \(h_l(p, \bar{u})\) for the Leontief utility directly (without calculus). Verify Shephard’s Lemma: \(\partial e(p, \bar{u})/\partial p_l = h_l(p, \bar{u})\).
crumbs: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory

title: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory
text:
17.6 Problem 6 — Gorman Aggregation Check
Consider two consumers with the following indirect utility functions: \[v^1(p, w^1) = \frac{w^1 - p_1}{p_2}, \qquad v^2(p, w^2) = \frac{w^2 - 2p_1}{p_2}\]
(a) Apply Roy’s Identity to derive the Walrasian demand functions \(x^i(p, w^i)\) for \(i = 1, 2\).
(b) Check whether these demands take the Gorman polar form: \(x^i(p, w^i) = a^i(p) + b(p) w^i\). Identify \(a^i(p)\) and \(b(p)\) explicitly.
(c) Verify that the slope \(b(p)\) is the same for both consumers, confirming that the Gorman condition holds.
(d) Derive the aggregate demand \(X(p, W) = x^1(p, w^1) + x^2(p, w^2)\) as a function of \((p, W)\) where \(W = w^1 + w^2\). Confirm that \(X\) does not depend on the distribution \((w^1, w^2)\) separately.
(e) Now suppose consumer 2’s indirect utility is instead \(v^2(p, w^2) = (w^2)^2 / p_2\). Show that the demand does not take the Gorman form. Provide an example of a wealth distribution change that alters aggregate demand while holding \(W\) fixed.
(f) Provide a verbal and graphical explanation of why linear Engel curves with identical slopes are necessary for wealth-distribution-free aggregation.
crumbs: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory

title: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory
text:
17.7 Problem 7 — Integrability
Consider the following demand system for \(L = 2\) goods: \[x_1(p, w) = \frac{w}{2p_1} + \frac{p_2}{p_1}, \qquad x_2(p, w) = \frac{w}{2p_2} - 1\]
(a) Verify Walras’ Law: \(p_1 x_1 + p_2 x_2 = w\).
(b) Verify homogeneity of degree zero: \(x_l(\lambda p, \lambda w) = x_l(p, w)\) for all \(\lambda > 0\).
(c) Compute the Slutsky matrix \(S(p,w)\) with entries \(S_{lk} = \partial x_l/\partial p_k + x_k \partial x_l/\partial w\). Determine whether \(S\) is symmetric.
(d) Is this demand system integrable? If yes, proceed to recover the expenditure function and hence a rationalizing utility function. If no, explain precisely which integrability condition fails.
(e) Suppose instead the demand system is given by: \[x_1(p,w) = \frac{w}{2p_1}, \qquad x_2(p,w) = \frac{w}{2p_2}\] Verify symmetry and NSD of the Slutsky matrix. Solve the ODE system \(\partial e/\partial p_l = h_l(p, \bar{u}) = x_l(p, e)\) with initial condition \(e(p^0, \bar{u}) = \bar{u} \sqrt{p_1^0 p_2^0}\) (for some reference \(p^0\)). Recover \(e(p, \bar{u})\) and identify the corresponding utility function.
crumbs: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory

title: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory
section: 17.8 Problem 8 — Welfare Analysis Using CV and EV
text:
17.8 Problem 8 — Welfare Analysis Using CV and EV
A consumer has Cobb-Douglas utility \(u(x_1, x_2) = x_1^{1/2} x_2^{1/2}\) and wealth \(w = 100\). Initial prices are \(p^0 = (1, 1)\). The price of good 1 increases to \(p^1 = (4, 1)\).
(a) Derive the indirect utility function \(v(p,w)\) and the expenditure function \(e(p, \bar{u})\) for this utility function (use the results of Problem 2 with \(\alpha = 1/2\) as a guide or re-derive from scratch).
(b) Compute the consumer’s initial utility \(\bar{u}^0 = v(p^0, w)\) and post-change utility \(\bar{u}^1 = v(p^1, w)\).
(c) Compensating Variation: Compute \(CV = w - e(p^1, \bar{u}^0)\). Interpret: how much wealth must be given to the consumer after the price change to restore their original utility? Is \(|CV|\) larger or smaller than the consumer surplus loss (area under the Marshallian demand curve)?
(d) Equivalent Variation: Compute \(EV = e(p^1, \bar{u}^1) - e(p^0, \bar{u}^1) = w - e(p^0, \bar{u}^1)\). Interpret: what lump-sum payment at the old prices would make the consumer indifferent to the price change?
(e) Verify the welfare ranking: for a price increase, \(|EV| \leq |\Delta CS| \leq |CV|\) (where \(\Delta CS\) is the change in consumer surplus). Compute \(\Delta CS = \int_1^4 x_1(p_1, 1, 100) \, dp_1\) and verify the inequality.
(f) Now suppose the government proposes two alternative policies: (Policy A) a lump-sum tax of \(T_A = |CV|\) on the consumer; (Policy B) maintaining the price change at \(p^1\). Show that the consumer is indifferent between facing \(p^1\) (no compensation) and facing \(p^0\) with the tax \(T_A\). Which policy raises more government revenue while leaving the consumer at utility \(\bar{u}^0\)? What does this imply about the efficiency of price distortions relative to lump-sum taxes?
(g) Using the Slutsky equation, decompose the change in Marshallian demand \(\Delta x_1 = x_1(p^1, w) - x_1(p^0, w)\) into substitution and income effects. Verify that the substitution effect is non-positive and that the income effect is also non-positive (since good 1 is normal for Cobb-Douglas). Does this demand system exhibit any Giffen behavior?
crumbs: 16  Problem Set — Part II: Consumer Theory

title: 17  Part III: Producer Theory — Overview
text: 18 Part III: Producer Theory — Overview
crumbs: 17  Part III: Producer Theory — Overview

title: 17  Part III: Producer Theory — Overview
text:
18.1 Context
Producer theory provides the supply side of general equilibrium analysis and is the microeconomic foundation for industrial organization, trade theory, and regulatory economics. The central objects of study are the production set \(Y\), which encodes all technologically feasible input-output combinations, and two derived optimization problems: the profit maximization problem (PMP) and the cost minimization problem (CMP).
The structure of producer theory closely mirrors consumer theory. The duality between the profit function \(\pi(p)\) and the production set \(Y\) parallels the duality between the expenditure function \(e(p, u)\) and the indirect utility function \(v(p, w)\) in consumer theory. Hotelling’s lemma — the derivative of the profit function yields the supply correspondence — is the producer-side analogue of Roy’s identity and Shephard’s lemma for the consumer. This duality is not merely a formal analogy: both derive from the envelope theorem applied to parametric optimization, and both reveal how observable choice data constrain the underlying technology or preferences.
Producer theory also provides the technical underpinning for welfare analysis: producer surplus equals profit under competitive pricing, and the comparative statics of the cost and profit functions determine how supply responds to price and factor-price changes. The Le Chatelier principle formalizes the intuition that long-run supply elasticities exceed short-run ones.
The four notes in this part cover the building blocks of production theory from the ground up, culminating in a unified treatment of supply and factor demands and their relationship to market structure.
crumbs: 17  Part III: Producer Theory — Overview

title: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology
text: 19 3.1 Production Sets and Technology
crumbs: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology

title: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology
text:
19.1 Context
The starting point of producer theory is a formal description of what is technologically feasible. Rather than imposing a particular functional form on technology, MWG takes a set-theoretic approach: the production set \(Y \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) is the primitive object, and all behavioral results (supply, cost minimization, comparative statics) follow from properties of \(Y\). This level of generality accommodates multiple outputs, joint production, and heterogeneous inputs within a single framework. In the special but important single-output case, the production set reduces to the familiar production function.
crumbs: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology

title: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology
text:
19.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 3.1 — Commodity Space and Sign Convention



There are \(L\) commodities. A production vector (or netput vector) is \(y = (y_1, \ldots, y_L) \in \mathbb{R}^L\), where: - \(y_l > 0\): commodity \(l\) is an output (net supply to the market) - \(y_l < 0\): commodity \(l\) is an input (net demand from the market)
This sign convention consolidates inputs and outputs into a single vector, enabling a unified treatment.








NoteDefinition 3.2 — Production Set



The production set \(Y \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) is the set of all technologically feasible production vectors. The set \(Y\) is the complete description of the firm’s technology.








NoteDefinition 3.3 — Input Requirement Set



For a given output vector \(\bar{q}\), the input requirement set is: \[V(\bar{q}) = \{z \in \mathbb{R}^K_+ : (-z, \bar{q}) \in Y\}\] This is the set of input vectors \(z\) that can produce at least output \(\bar{q}\).








NoteDefinition 3.4 — Isoquant



The isoquant at output level \(q\) is: \[Q(q) = \{z \in \mathbb{R}^K_+ : z \in V(q) \text{ and } z \notin V(q') \text{ for any } q' > q\}\] That is, it is the boundary of the input requirement set: the minimal inputs needed to produce exactly \(q\).
crumbs: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology

title: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology
text:
19.3 Properties of the Production Set
Each property below is stated formally, interpreted economically, and its implication for the profit function \(\pi(p) = \sup_{y \in Y} p \cdot y\) is noted.

19.3.1 Property P1: Non-emptiness






NoteDefinition 3.5 — Non-emptiness (Possibility of Inaction)



\(Y \neq \emptyset\), and in particular \(0 \in Y\).


Economic Interpretation: The firm can always choose to produce nothing — the “shutdown” option. This is an important outside option that bounds losses to zero (if the firm can always costlessly not produce).
Implication for \(\pi(p)\): Since \(0 \in Y\), we have \(p \cdot 0 = 0 \leq \pi(p)\) for all \(p\). Hence the profit function satisfies \(\pi(p) \geq 0\) whenever inaction is feasible.


19.3.2 Property P2: Closedness






NoteDefinition 3.6 — Closedness



\(Y\) is a closed set: if \(\{y^n\} \subset Y\) and \(y^n \to y\), then \(y \in Y\).


Economic Interpretation: The production possibilities do not have “holes” at their boundaries. If a sequence of feasible production plans converges, the limit is also feasible.
Implication for \(\pi(p)\): Closedness, together with boundedness of the feasible set for given prices, ensures the supremum defining \(\pi(p)\) is attained (by the extreme value theorem). Without closedness, optimal production plans may not exist.


19.3.3 Property P3: No Free Lunch






NoteDefinition 3.7 — No Free Lunch



\[Y \cap \mathbb{R}^L_+ \subseteq \{0\}\] It is impossible to produce positive outputs without using any inputs.


Economic Interpretation: Nothing comes from nothing. The only production vector in the positive orthant (all outputs, no inputs) that is feasible is the zero vector. This rules out technologies that create commodities from thin air.
Implication for \(\pi(p)\): Rules out unbounded profit from zero outlay in the absence of any price-taking inputs, providing a basic regularity condition.


19.3.4 Property P4: Free Disposal






NoteDefinition 3.8 — Free Disposal



\[y \in Y \text{ and } y' \leq y \Rightarrow y' \in Y\] where \(y' \leq y\) means \(y'_l \leq y_l\) for all \(l\).


Economic Interpretation: The firm can freely dispose of any outputs or use more inputs than necessary. If a production plan \(y\) is feasible, then producing less output or using more inputs (making components of \(y'\) more negative) remains feasible. There are no disposal costs.
Implication for \(\pi(p)\): Free disposal implies that the input requirement set \(V(q)\) is comprehensive upward: \(z \in V(q)\) and \(z' \geq z\) implies \(z' \in V(q)\). This makes the CMP well-behaved since wasting inputs is costless.


19.3.5 Property P5: Irreversibility






NoteDefinition 3.9 — Irreversibility



\[y \in Y, \; y \neq 0 \Rightarrow -y \notin Y\]


Economic Interpretation: Production processes cannot simply be “run backwards.” If inputs can be turned into outputs, those outputs cannot be reversed back into the original inputs at the same rate. This reflects the asymmetry of physical production processes.
Implication for \(\pi(p)\): Irreversibility, together with No Free Lunch, prevents the technology from being trivially unbounded in all price configurations.


19.3.6 Property P6: Nonincreasing Returns to Scale (NIRTS)






NoteDefinition 3.10 — Nonincreasing Returns to Scale



\[y \in Y \Rightarrow \alpha y \in Y \quad \forall \alpha \in [0, 1]\] Scaling down any feasible production plan remains feasible.


Economic Interpretation: The firm can always do less: if it can produce \(y\), it can produce \(\alpha y\) for any fraction \(\alpha \in [0,1]\). This corresponds to the ability to idle part of the plant. Equivalently, in the single-output case: \(f(\lambda z) \leq \lambda f(z)\) for \(\lambda \in [0,1]\), i.e., decreasing or constant returns to scale.
Implication for \(\pi(p)\): Under NIRTS, \(\pi(p) \leq 0\) or \(\pi(p) = 0\). Combined with Non-emptiness (\(0 \in Y \Rightarrow \pi \geq 0\)), NIRTS implies \(\pi(p) \geq 0\) for all \(p\), and the profit-maximizing scale is finite.


19.3.7 Property P7: Nondecreasing Returns to Scale (NDIRTS)






NoteDefinition 3.11 — Nondecreasing Returns to Scale



\[y \in Y \Rightarrow \alpha y \in Y \quad \forall \alpha \geq 1\] Any feasible production plan can be scaled up.


Economic Interpretation: The firm can always do at least proportionally more if it scales up all inputs. This could arise from specialization, indivisible fixed costs amortized over large production runs, or network effects. In the single-output case: \(f(\lambda z) \geq \lambda f(z)\) for \(\lambda \geq 1\).
Implication for \(\pi(p)\): Under NDIRTS (without NIRTS), the profit function may be \(+\infty\) at certain prices, since scaling up is always feasible. Positive profits tend to grow without bound as scale increases.


19.3.8 Property P8: Constant Returns to Scale (CRS)






NoteDefinition 3.12 — Constant Returns to Scale



\(Y\) is a cone: \[y \in Y \Rightarrow \alpha y \in Y \quad \forall \alpha \geq 0\] Equivalently, CRS = NIRTS \(\cap\) NDIRTS.


Economic Interpretation: Doubling all inputs exactly doubles all outputs. The firm’s technology exhibits no scale economies or diseconomies. CRS is the benchmark case for competitive general equilibrium models, since entry and exit drive profits to zero and scale is indeterminate.
Implication for \(\pi(p)\): Under CRS, the profit function satisfies \(\pi(\alpha p) = \alpha \pi(p)\) (homogeneous of degree 1), but also: since \(Y\) is a cone, either \(\pi(p) = 0\) (if prices are consistent with zero profit) or \(\pi(p) = +\infty\) (if there is a strictly profitable direction). This is formalized in Proposition 3.1 below.


19.3.9 Property P9: Convexity






NoteDefinition 3.13 — Convexity of \(Y\)



\[y, y' \in Y \Rightarrow \lambda y + (1-\lambda) y' \in Y \quad \forall \lambda \in [0,1]\]


Economic Interpretation: Mixing two feasible production plans is feasible. This captures the idea of diminishing marginal returns: as we shift resources from one activity to another, production possibilities shrink at the margin. Convexity of \(Y\) is implied by CRS plus NIRTS (any convex combination of a CRS set lies in the set), but is also consistent with DRS technologies.
Implication for \(\pi(p)\): Convexity of \(Y\) implies that the profit-maximizing problem has a convex feasible set, which (together with linearity of the objective) ensures that the set of optimal solutions \(y(p)\) is convex-valued, and that the profit function \(\pi(p)\) is convex in \(p\) (see 3.2 Profit Maximization).
crumbs: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology

title: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology
text:
19.4 Single-Output Case and Production Function






NoteDefinition 3.14 — Production Function



In the single-output case, there are \(K\) inputs with quantities \(z = (z_1, \ldots, z_K) \geq 0\) and one output \(q \geq 0\). The production function \(f: \mathbb{R}^K_+ \to \mathbb{R}_+\) gives the maximum output obtainable from input vector \(z\): \[f(z) = \max\{q \geq 0 : (-z, q) \in Y\}\] The production set is then: \[Y = \{(-z, q) \in \mathbb{R}^{K+1} : q \leq f(z), \; z \geq 0\}\]


Returns to Scale via the Production Function: For \(\lambda > 1\): - Constant Returns to Scale (CRS): \(f(\lambda z) = \lambda f(z)\) — doubling inputs doubles output - Increasing Returns to Scale (IRS): \(f(\lambda z) > \lambda f(z)\) — doubling inputs more than doubles output - Decreasing Returns to Scale (DRS): \(f(\lambda z) < \lambda f(z)\) — doubling inputs less than doubles output






CautionRemark 3.1 — Local vs. Global Returns to Scale



Returns to scale can be local (holding at a particular \(z\)) or global (holding for all \(z\)). A production function may exhibit IRS at low scales and DRS at high scales (the typical “U-shaped” average cost curve corresponds to this pattern). The properties P6–P8 are global.


Marginal Rate of Technical Substitution (MRTS): For a two-input technology, the MRTS between inputs 1 and 2 at a given output level is: \[MRTS_{12}(z) = -\frac{dz_2}{dz_1}\bigg|_{q = f(z) \text{ fixed}} = \frac{\partial f/\partial z_1}{\partial f/\partial z_2} = \frac{MP_1}{MP_2}\] This is the rate at which input 2 can be substituted for input 1 while keeping output constant.
crumbs: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology

title: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology
text:
19.5 Transformation Function






NoteDefinition 3.15 — Transformation Function



The transformation function \(F: \mathbb{R}^L \to \mathbb{R}\) is defined such that: \[Y = \{y \in \mathbb{R}^L : F(y) \leq 0\}\] The production frontier (or transformation frontier) is \(\{y : F(y) = 0\}\).


Properties: - \(F\) is well-defined when \(Y\) is convex and closed with a smooth boundary. - The gradient \(\nabla F(y)\) at a boundary point is the outward normal to the production frontier. - \(F\) is normalized so that \(F(y) < 0\) for interior points (strictly feasible) and \(F(y) > 0\) for infeasible plans.






NoteDefinition 3.16 — Marginal Rate of Transformation



For a multi-output technology at a boundary point \(y^*\) with \(F(y^*) = 0\), the marginal rate of transformation (MRT) between outputs \(k\) and \(l\) is: \[MRT_{kl}(y^*) = -\frac{dy_l}{dy_k}\bigg|_{F(y) = 0} = \frac{\partial F / \partial y_k}{\partial F / \partial y_l}\]


Economic Interpretation: The MRT measures the opportunity cost of producing more of output \(k\) in terms of output \(l\) foregone. At a profit maximum, the MRT between any two outputs must equal their price ratio: \(MRT_{kl} = p_k/p_l\). This is the producer analogue of the consumer’s equimarginal condition.
crumbs: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology

title: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology
text:
19.6 Main Results






ImportantProposition 3.1 — CRS Implies Zero Profit or Infinite Profit



Suppose \(Y\) satisfies free disposal and CRS (is a cone with \(0 \in Y\)). Then for every price vector \(p \gg 0\): \[\pi(p) = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \pi(p) = +\infty\]








TipProof





Step 1: \(\pi(p) \geq 0\). Since \(0 \in Y\) (from CRS, which requires \(Y\) to be a cone containing \(0\)), we have \(p \cdot 0 = 0 \leq \pi(p)\).
Step 2: Either \(\pi(p) > 0\) implies \(\pi(p) = +\infty\). Suppose there exists \(y^* \in Y\) with \(p \cdot y^* = \epsilon > 0\). Since \(Y\) is a cone, for any \(\alpha > 0\) we have \(\alpha y^* \in Y\). Therefore: \[\pi(p) \geq p \cdot (\alpha y^*) = \alpha (p \cdot y^*) = \alpha \epsilon\] Since this holds for all \(\alpha > 0\), taking \(\alpha \to \infty\) gives \(\pi(p) = +\infty\).
Step 3: Conclusion. The only two possibilities consistent with \(\pi(p) \geq 0\) are: - \(p \cdot y \leq 0\) for all \(y \in Y\): then \(\pi(p) = \sup_{y \in Y} p \cdot y = 0\) (achieved at \(y = 0\)). - \(\exists y^* \in Y\) with \(p \cdot y^* > 0\): then by Step 2, \(\pi(p) = +\infty\).
Hence \(\pi(p) \in \{0, +\infty\}\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.2 — Economic Significance of Proposition 3.1



This result explains why competitive general equilibrium models with CRS technologies require zero-profit conditions: if any technology exhibited CRS and generated positive profits at equilibrium prices, firms would want to scale up production without bound, which is inconsistent with market clearing. CRS is therefore the natural assumption for “small” competitive firms whose individual scale decisions do not affect prices.








ImportantProposition 3.2 — Convexity of \(Y\) and Concavity of \(f\)



In the single-output case, the production set \(Y = \{(-z, q) : q \leq f(z), z \geq 0\}\) is convex if and only if \(f\) is a concave function.








TipProof





(\(\Rightarrow\)) Suppose \(Y\) is convex. Take any \(z, z' \geq 0\) and \(\lambda \in [0,1]\). Then \((-z, f(z)) \in Y\) and \((-z', f(z')) \in Y\). By convexity of \(Y\): \[\lambda(-z, f(z)) + (1-\lambda)(-z', f(z')) = (-[\lambda z + (1-\lambda)z'], \; \lambda f(z) + (1-\lambda)f(z')) \in Y\] For this to be in \(Y\), we need \(\lambda f(z) + (1-\lambda)f(z') \leq f(\lambda z + (1-\lambda)z')\). This is precisely the concavity of \(f\).
(\(\Leftarrow\)) Suppose \(f\) is concave. Take \((-z, q)\) and \((-z', q')\) in \(Y\), so \(q \leq f(z)\) and \(q' \leq f(z')\). For \(\lambda \in [0,1]\): \[\lambda q + (1-\lambda)q' \leq \lambda f(z) + (1-\lambda)f(z') \leq f(\lambda z + (1-\lambda)z')\] where the second inequality uses concavity of \(f\). Hence \((-[\lambda z + (1-\lambda) z'], \lambda q + (1-\lambda)q') \in Y\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.3 — Implications for Returns to Scale




A concave production function exhibits non-increasing returns to scale (DRS or CRS globally).
A linear production function (\(f(z) = a \cdot z\)) is both concave and convex, corresponding to CRS.
A strictly concave production function implies DRS.
Note: concavity of \(f\) is a stronger condition than DRS. DRS only requires \(f(\lambda z) < \lambda f(z)\) for \(\lambda > 1\), while concavity requires the same inequality to hold for all convex combinations.
crumbs: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology

title: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology
text:
19.7 Examples






NoteExample 3.1 — Cobb-Douglas Production Function



Let \(f(z_1, z_2) = z_1^{\alpha} z_2^{\beta}\) with \(\alpha, \beta > 0\).
Returns to scale: For \(\lambda > 0\): \[f(\lambda z_1, \lambda z_2) = (\lambda z_1)^{\alpha} (\lambda z_2)^{\beta} = \lambda^{\alpha+\beta} z_1^{\alpha} z_2^{\beta} = \lambda^{\alpha+\beta} f(z_1, z_2)\] - If \(\alpha + \beta = 1\): CRS - If \(\alpha + \beta > 1\): IRS - If \(\alpha + \beta < 1\): DRS
Transformation function: \(F(-z_1, -z_2, q) = q - z_1^{\alpha} z_2^{\beta}\); the production set is \(\{F \leq 0\}\).
MRTS: \(MRTS_{12} = \frac{\alpha z_2}{\beta z_1}\), which is decreasing in \(z_1/z_2\) — confirming diminishing MRTS (convex isoquants).








NoteExample 3.2 — CES Production Function



Let \(f(z_1, z_2) = (z_1^{\rho} + z_2^{\rho})^{1/\rho}\) with \(\rho \leq 1\), \(\rho \neq 0\).
This is CRS for all admissible \(\rho\). Special cases: - \(\rho \to 1\): \(f(z_1, z_2) = z_1 + z_2\) (perfect substitutes; linear isoquants) - \(\rho \to 0\): \(f(z_1, z_2) = z_1^{1/2} z_2^{1/2}\) (Cobb-Douglas; limiting case) - \(\rho \to -\infty\): \(f(z_1, z_2) = \min(z_1, z_2)\) (Leontief; perfect complements; L-shaped isoquants)
The elasticity of substitution is \(\sigma = 1/(1-\rho)\), measuring curvature of the isoquant.
crumbs: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology

title: 18  3.1 Production Sets and Technology
text:
19.8 Economic Intuition
The production set is a more general primitive than the production function because it accommodates multiple outputs (joint production), by-products (waste), and technologies where the input-output distinction is itself endogenous. The sign convention — inputs negative, outputs positive — is elegant because it reduces the entire description of the firm’s technology to a single set in commodity space, and profit is simply \(p \cdot y\) for a price vector \(p\) with the same sign convention (all prices positive).
The nine properties P1–P9 are not all independent. For example, CRS (P8) implies both NIRTS (P6) and NDIRTS (P7). Convexity (P9) plus NIRTS (P6) gives a very well-behaved technology: the profit-maximizing problem will have a convex feasible set with a finite optimum for any price vector satisfying a no-profitable-direction condition.
The critical practical message is: returns to scale determine market structure. CRS supports competitive markets with indeterminate scale; DRS generates finite supply and positive profits; IRS creates natural monopoly pressures. Understanding the production set is thus essential not just for partial equilibrium supply analysis, but for the entire theory of market organization.
