title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text: 20 3.2 Profit Maximization

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text:
20.1 Context
Given a production set \(Y\) and a price vector \(p \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\) (all prices strictly positive), the firm’s profit maximization problem (PMP) selects the production plan that generates the highest revenue net of input costs. The PMP is the foundational behavioral model of the competitive firm. Its value function — the profit function \(\pi(p)\) — and its optimizer — the supply correspondence \(y(p)\) — encode all observable supply behavior of the firm.
The key analytical tools are (1) the properties of \(\pi(p)\), which follow directly from the structure of the optimization problem and do not require any specific functional form for \(Y\), and (2) Hotelling’s lemma, which states that the derivative of the profit function with respect to any price equals the profit-maximizing supply of that commodity. Hotelling’s lemma is a direct application of the envelope theorem and is the producer analogue of Roy’s identity in consumer theory.

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text:
20.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 3.1 — Profit Maximization Problem (PMP)



Given price vector \(p \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\) and production set \(Y \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\), the firm’s profit maximization problem is: \[\max_{y \in Y} \; p \cdot y\] where \(p \cdot y = \sum_{l=1}^L p_l y_l\) is revenue from outputs minus expenditure on inputs (using the sign convention \(y_l < 0\) for inputs).








NoteDefinition 3.2 — Profit Function



The profit function is the value function of the PMP: \[\pi(p) = \sup_{y \in Y} \; p \cdot y\] When the supremum is attained, \(\pi(p) = \max_{y \in Y} p \cdot y\).








NoteDefinition 3.3 — Supply Correspondence



The supply correspondence \(y: \mathbb{R}^L_{++} \rightrightarrows Y\) is: \[y(p) = \arg\max_{y \in Y} \; p \cdot y = \{y^* \in Y : p \cdot y^* = \pi(p)\}\] When \(y(p)\) is single-valued, it is called the supply function.

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text:
20.3 Properties of the Profit Function






ImportantProposition 3.1 — Homogeneity of Degree One of \(\pi(p)\)



The profit function \(\pi(p)\) is homogeneous of degree one in \(p\): for all \(\alpha > 0\), \[\pi(\alpha p) = \alpha \pi(p)\]








TipProof





For any \(\alpha > 0\): \[\pi(\alpha p) = \sup_{y \in Y} (\alpha p) \cdot y = \sup_{y \in Y} \alpha (p \cdot y) = \alpha \sup_{y \in Y} p \cdot y = \alpha \pi(p)\] The third equality uses the fact that multiplying the objective by a positive scalar \(\alpha\) does not change the set of maximizers, and scales the maximum value by \(\alpha\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.1



Homogeneity of degree one has a direct economic interpretation: a proportional increase in all prices (inputs and outputs alike) does not change the firm’s optimal production plan \(y(p)\) — only the monetary value of profit scales by the same factor. This is a no money illusion property for producers. Formally, \(y(\alpha p) = y(p)\) for all \(\alpha > 0\).








ImportantProposition 3.2 — Convexity of \(\pi(p)\)



The profit function \(\pi(p)\) is convex in \(p\).








TipProof





The profit function is the supremum of a family of linear functions of \(p\). Specifically, for each \(y \in Y\), the function \(p \mapsto p \cdot y\) is linear (hence convex) in \(p\). Since the supremum of any family of convex functions is convex: \[\pi(p) = \sup_{y \in Y} p \cdot y\] is convex in \(p\).
Alternative direct proof: Fix \(p, p' \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\) and \(\lambda \in [0,1]\). Let \(y^*\) be a profit-maximizing production plan at prices \(\lambda p + (1-\lambda)p'\). Then: \[\begin{align}
\pi(\lambda p + (1-\lambda)p') &= [\lambda p + (1-\lambda)p'] \cdot y^* \\
&= \lambda (p \cdot y^*) + (1-\lambda)(p' \cdot y^*) \\
&\leq \lambda \pi(p) + (1-\lambda)\pi(p')
\end{align}\] where the inequality uses \(p \cdot y^* \leq \pi(p)\) and \(p' \cdot y^* \leq \pi(p')\) (since \(y^*\) may not be optimal at \(p\) or \(p'\)). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.2 — Economic Meaning of Convexity



Convexity of \(\pi(p)\) means that the profit function “curves upward” in prices. A firm benefits from price variability: if output price fluctuates between \(p_1\) and \(p_2\), average profit under the fluctuating price exceeds profit at the average price. This is the producer analogue of Jensen’s inequality. Intuitively, the firm can adjust its optimal production plan in response to each price realization, so it takes advantage of high prices more than it suffers from low prices.








ImportantProposition 3.3 — Monotonicity of \(\pi(p)\)



The profit function is: (a) Nondecreasing in output prices: if \(p_l\) is an output price and \(p_l\) rises (holding all else equal), then \(\pi(p)\) does not decrease. (b) Nonincreasing in input prices: if \(p_l\) is an input price (i.e., the price of commodity \(l\) which enters \(y\) negatively) and \(p_l\) rises, then \(\pi(p)\) does not increase.








TipProof





Let \(p' \geq p\) (componentwise). For any \(y^* \in y(p)\) (profit maximizing at \(p\)): \[\pi(p') = \max_{y \in Y} p' \cdot y \geq p' \cdot y^* \geq p \cdot y^* = \pi(p)\] where the first inequality is from the definition of max, and the second uses \(p' \geq p\) and \(y^*\) may have mixed-sign components (but for an output \(y_l^* \geq 0\) and \(p'_l \geq p_l\) contributes nonnegatively; for an input \(y_l^* \leq 0\), if only that input price rises, then \(p'_l y_l^* \leq p_l y_l^*\), so \(\pi\) is nonincreasing in input prices).
More precisely: if only input price \(w_k\) rises (i.e., \(p'_k > p_k\) for the input commodity \(k\) with \(y_k \leq 0\)), then for any feasible \(y\), the objective \(p' \cdot y \leq p \cdot y\) (since \(y_k \leq 0\) means \(p'_k y_k \leq p_k y_k\)). Hence \(\pi(p') \leq \pi(p)\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text:
20.4 Supply Correspondence






ImportantProposition 3.4 — Nonemptiness of Supply



Suppose \(Y\) is closed and there exists \(M < \infty\) such that \(p \cdot y \leq M\) for all \(y \in Y\) (profit is bounded above). Then \(y(p) \neq \emptyset\): the PMP has a solution.








TipProof





Since \(Y\) is closed and \(p \cdot y\) is bounded above on \(Y\), there exists a sequence \(\{y^n\} \subset Y\) with \(p \cdot y^n \to \pi(p)\). If \(Y\) is bounded, a convergent subsequence exists by Bolzano-Weierstrass, and the limit lies in \(Y\) by closedness. In general, the existence of a maximizer requires that the superlevel sets \(\{y \in Y : p \cdot y \geq c\}\) are compact. Under free disposal and appropriate boundedness conditions on \(Y\), this holds. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.3 — When Does the PMP Have a Solution?



The PMP may fail to have a solution in two ways: 1. Unbounded profit: If \(Y\) is a cone (CRS), then by Proposition 3.1 of note 3.1 Production Sets, \(\pi(p) \in \{0, +\infty\}\). If \(\pi(p) = +\infty\), no maximizer exists. 2. Non-attainment: If \(Y\) is not closed, the supremum may not be achieved.
In practice, well-posed competitive models either (i) restrict to DRS technologies where the optimum is interior and finite, or (ii) use the zero-profit condition to pin down the equilibrium price under CRS.








ImportantProposition 3.5 — Convex-valuedness of \(y(p)\)



If \(Y\) is convex, then \(y(p)\) is a convex set for every \(p\).








TipProof





Let \(y, y' \in y(p)\), so \(p \cdot y = p \cdot y' = \pi(p)\). For any \(\lambda \in [0,1]\), by convexity of \(Y\), \(\lambda y + (1-\lambda)y' \in Y\). Moreover: \[p \cdot [\lambda y + (1-\lambda)y'] = \lambda(p \cdot y) + (1-\lambda)(p \cdot y') = \lambda\pi(p) + (1-\lambda)\pi(p) = \pi(p)\] So \(\lambda y + (1-\lambda)y' \in y(p)\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text:
20.5 Hotelling’s Lemma






ImportantTheorem 3.1 — Hotelling’s Lemma



Suppose \(\pi(p)\) is differentiable at \(p^0\). Then: \[y(p^0) = \nabla_p \pi(p^0), \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad y_l(p^0) = \frac{\partial \pi(p^0)}{\partial p_l} \quad \forall l = 1, \ldots, L\]








TipProof





Proof via the Envelope Theorem (see 1.3 Optimization Theory):
The profit function is \(\pi(p) = p \cdot y(p)\) where \(y(p)\) solves \(\max_{y \in Y} p \cdot y\). The Lagrangian (treating \(Y\) as a constraint set) is simply the objective \(p \cdot y\); there are no binding equality constraints other than \(y \in Y\).
By the envelope theorem, the derivative of the value function with respect to a parameter equals the partial derivative of the objective with respect to that parameter, evaluated at the optimum: \[\frac{\partial \pi(p)}{\partial p_l} = \frac{\partial}{\partial p_l}[p \cdot y]\bigg|_{y = y(p)} = y_l(p)\]
Direct proof (without appealing to the envelope theorem):
Since \(y^* = y(p^0)\) is optimal at \(p^0\), for any \(p\): \[\pi(p) \geq p \cdot y^* \quad \text{(since $y^*$ is feasible at price $p$)}\] Also \(\pi(p^0) = p^0 \cdot y^*\) by optimality. Define \(g(p) = p \cdot y^*\) (a linear function). Then: \[g(p) \leq \pi(p) \quad \forall p, \qquad g(p^0) = \pi(p^0)\] So \(g\) is a supporting hyperplane to \(\pi\) at \(p^0\): the linear function \(p \mapsto p \cdot y^*\) is below \(\pi\) everywhere and touches it at \(p^0\). If \(\pi\) is differentiable at \(p^0\), the supporting hyperplane is unique and equals the tangent hyperplane. The gradient of \(g(p) = p \cdot y^*\) is exactly \(y^*\). Therefore: \[\nabla_p \pi(p^0) = y^* = y(p^0) \qquad \blacksquare\]









CautionRemark 3.4 — Economic Content of Hotelling’s Lemma



Hotelling’s lemma says that the supply of each commodity equals the rate at which profit increases in the price of that commodity. For an output commodity \(l\), \(\partial \pi / \partial p_l = y_l \geq 0\): raising the output price increases profit, and the rate of increase equals the quantity supplied. For an input commodity \(k\), \(\partial \pi / \partial p_k = y_k \leq 0\): raising the input price decreases profit, and the rate of decrease equals the (negative of) quantity demanded. This is the most important tool for deriving supply and factor demand from the profit function.

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text:
20.6 Comparative Statics from Hotelling’s Lemma
From Hotelling’s Lemma and the convexity of \(\pi(p)\), we obtain the full matrix of price responses.






NoteDefinition 3.4 — Substitution Matrix in Production



The supply substitution matrix is the \(L \times L\) matrix of cross-price supply responses: \[S(p) = \frac{\partial y(p)}{\partial p} = D^2_p \pi(p)\] where \(D^2_p \pi\) is the Hessian matrix of \(\pi\) with respect to \(p\).








ImportantProposition 3.6 — Properties of the Supply Substitution Matrix



If \(\pi\) is twice differentiable, the supply substitution matrix \(S(p) = D^2_p \pi(p)\) satisfies: 1. Symmetry: \(S(p) = S(p)^T\) (i.e., \(\partial y_l / \partial p_k = \partial y_k / \partial p_l\)) 2. Positive semidefiniteness (PSD): \(v^T S(p) v \geq 0\) for all \(v \in \mathbb{R}^L\) 3. \(S(p) p = 0\) (Euler’s theorem applied to the HD1 supply) 4. In particular, \(\frac{\partial y_l}{\partial p_l} \geq 0\): own-price supply is nondecreasing








TipProof





Properties 1 and 2 follow directly from \(S(p) = D^2_p \pi(p)\) and the fact that \(\pi\) is convex: the Hessian of a convex function is symmetric (Young’s theorem) and positive semidefinite.
Property 3: Since \(y(p)\) is HD0 in \(p\) (scaling all prices by \(\alpha > 0\) does not change the optimal production plan), by Euler’s theorem for homogeneous functions: \[\sum_k \frac{\partial y_l}{\partial p_k} p_k = 0 \cdot y_l = 0 \quad \forall l\] i.e., \(S(p) p = 0\).
Property 4: Since \(S\) is PSD, all diagonal entries are nonneg: \(v^T S v \geq 0\) for \(v = e_l\) (standard basis vector) gives \(S_{ll} = \partial y_l / \partial p_l \geq 0\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text:
20.7 Law of Supply






ImportantTheorem 3.2 — Law of Supply



For any two price vectors \(p\) and \(p'\): \[(\Delta p) \cdot (\Delta y) \geq 0\] where \(\Delta p = p' - p\) and \(\Delta y = y(p') - y(p)\) (taking any selections from the supply correspondence).








TipProof





Let \(y \in y(p)\) and \(y' \in y(p')\). By optimality: - \(y\) is optimal at \(p\): \(p \cdot y \geq p \cdot y'\) - \(y'\) is optimal at \(p'\): \(p' \cdot y' \geq p' \cdot y\)
Adding these two inequalities: \[p \cdot y + p' \cdot y' \geq p \cdot y' + p' \cdot y\] Rearranging: \[(p' - p) \cdot (y' - y) \geq 0\] i.e., \(\Delta p \cdot \Delta y \geq 0\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.5 — Interpretation of the Law of Supply



The law of supply says that supply responds in the same direction as price changes. If only output price \(p_l\) rises, then the supply of output \(l\) does not fall (\(\Delta p_l \cdot \Delta y_l \geq 0\) since other price changes are zero). If only input price rises, input demand does not increase (recall \(y_k < 0\) for inputs). This is a very strong and general result — it requires no assumptions beyond the optimization hypothesis (that firms maximize profit).

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
section: 20.8 Profit Maximization with a Production Function
text:
20.8 Profit Maximization with a Production Function
In the single-output case, the PMP takes a more familiar form.






NoteDefinition 3.5 — PMP with Production Function



With output \(q\), inputs \(z = (z_1, \ldots, z_K) \geq 0\), output price \(p > 0\), and factor price vector \(w \in \mathbb{R}^K_{++}\): \[\max_{q, z \geq 0} \; pq - w \cdot z \quad \text{s.t.} \quad q \leq f(z)\] Since the objective is increasing in \(q\), the constraint binds: \(q = f(z)\), and the problem reduces to: \[\max_{z \geq 0} \; p f(z) - w \cdot z\]








ImportantProposition 3.7 — First-Order Conditions for PMP



Suppose \(f\) is differentiable and the interior optimum \(z^* \gg 0\) satisfies the FOC: \[p \frac{\partial f(z^*)}{\partial z_l} = w_l \quad \forall l = 1, \ldots, K\] Equivalently: value of marginal product equals factor price for every input.








TipProof





The unconstrained problem \(\max_z p f(z) - w \cdot z\) has FOC (differentiating with respect to \(z_l\)): \[p \frac{\partial f(z)}{\partial z_l} - w_l = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p \cdot MP_l(z) = w_l\] where \(MP_l = \partial f/\partial z_l\) is the marginal product of input \(l\). At an interior solution, this holds for all \(l\). The second-order condition is \(p D^2 f(z^*)\) being negative semidefinite, which is satisfied when \(f\) is concave. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.6 — MRTS and Price Ratio



Dividing the FOC for inputs \(k\) and \(l\): \[\frac{p \cdot MP_k}{p \cdot MP_l} = \frac{w_k}{w_l} \quad \Rightarrow \quad MRTS_{kl} = \frac{w_k}{w_l}\] The marginal rate of technical substitution between any two inputs equals their price ratio at the optimum. This is the familiar “isoquant tangent to isocost” condition.

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text:
20.9 Example: Cobb-Douglas Profit Maximization






NoteExample 3.2 — Cobb-Douglas Supply Function



Let \(f(z) = z^{\alpha}\) with \(\alpha \in (0,1)\) (single input; DRS). Output price \(p > 0\), input price \(w > 0\).
PMP: \(\max_{z \geq 0} p z^{\alpha} - wz\)
FOC: \(p \alpha z^{\alpha - 1} = w\) \[z^*(p,w) = \left(\frac{p\alpha}{w}\right)^{1/(1-\alpha)}\]
Supply function: \[q^*(p,w) = f(z^*) = \left(\frac{p\alpha}{w}\right)^{\alpha/(1-\alpha)}\]
Profit function: \[\pi(p,w) = pq^* - wz^* = p\left(\frac{p\alpha}{w}\right)^{\alpha/(1-\alpha)} - w\left(\frac{p\alpha}{w}\right)^{1/(1-\alpha)}\]
Simplifying (let \(A = \alpha^{\alpha/(1-\alpha)}\)): \[\pi(p,w) = (1-\alpha) \cdot A \cdot p^{1/(1-\alpha)} w^{-\alpha/(1-\alpha)}\]
Verification of Hotelling’s Lemma: \[\frac{\partial \pi}{\partial p} = (1-\alpha) \cdot A \cdot \frac{1}{1-\alpha} p^{\alpha/(1-\alpha)} w^{-\alpha/(1-\alpha)} = A \cdot \left(\frac{p}{w}\right)^{\alpha/(1-\alpha)} = q^*(p,w) \checkmark\] \[\frac{\partial \pi}{\partial w} = (1-\alpha) \cdot A \cdot p^{1/(1-\alpha)} \cdot \left(-\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\right) w^{-1/(1-\alpha)} = -z^*(p,w) \checkmark\] (The negative sign reflects that \(y_{\text{input}} = -z^*\) in the net-output convention.)
Homogeneity: \(\pi(\lambda p, \lambda w) = \lambda \pi(p,w)\) for \(\lambda > 0\) — verify: each power of \(p\) and \(w\) in \(\pi\) sums to \(\frac{1}{1-\alpha} - \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} = \frac{1-\alpha}{1-\alpha} = 1\). \(\checkmark\)

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text:
20.10 Economic Intuition
Profit maximization is the behavioral hypothesis that firms choose among all technologically feasible production plans the one that generates the largest net revenue. This is a powerful and restrictive hypothesis: it implies the law of supply (without any additional assumptions), Hotelling’s lemma (when \(\pi\) is differentiable), and symmetry and PSD of the supply Hessian.
The convexity of \(\pi(p)\) captures a fundamental asymmetry: firms respond to price changes by adjusting their production plans, and they always adjust in the direction that benefits from the change. This is the producer analogue of the substitution effect in consumer theory, but here it works in the “easy” direction — there is no income effect in producer theory.
Hotelling’s lemma is the single most important result in producer theory for applied work. It says that all information about supply behavior is encoded in the profit function: once \(\pi(p)\) is known (estimated), all supply functions follow by differentiation. This is the core of the duality approach to empirical production analysis.

title: 19  3.2 Profit Maximization
text:
20.11 Cross-References

3.1 Production Sets — the domain \(Y\) of the PMP; properties of \(Y\) and their effect on \(\pi\)
3.3 Cost Minimization — the CMP; cost function and Shephard’s lemma; duality
3.4 Supply and Factor Demands — combining PMP and CMP; comparative statics; Le Chatelier
1.3 Optimization Theory — envelope theorem; Euler’s theorem; convexity
PS Producer Theory — Problems 2 and 4

title: 20  3.3 Cost Minimization
text: 21 3.3 Cost Minimization

title: 20  3.3 Cost Minimization
text:
21.1 Context
The cost minimization problem (CMP) separates the firm’s decision into two stages: (1) for a given output target \(q\), find the cheapest input bundle; (2) choose the profit-maximizing output level. This decomposition is valid whenever the firm is a price-taker in input markets, regardless of its market power in the output market. The CMP therefore underlies not just competitive supply theory but also monopoly and oligopoly models.
The CMP is the producer analogue of the expenditure minimization problem (EMP) in consumer theory. The cost function \(c(w, q)\) plays the role of the expenditure function \(e(p, u)\), and Shephard’s lemma — \(\nabla_w c = z(w,q)\) — is the exact analogue of the Hicksian demand identity \(\nabla_p e = h(p,u)\). The duality theorem states that the production function can be fully recovered from the cost function, just as preferences can be recovered from the expenditure function. This duality is the foundation of empirical production analysis using cost functions.

title: 20  3.3 Cost Minimization
text:
21.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 3.1 — Cost Minimization Problem (CMP)



Given factor price vector \(w \in \mathbb{R}^K_{++}\), output level \(q \geq 0\), and production function \(f: \mathbb{R}^K_+ \to \mathbb{R}_+\), the cost minimization problem is: \[\min_{z \geq 0} \; w \cdot z \quad \text{s.t.} \quad f(z) \geq q\]








NoteDefinition 3.2 — Cost Function



The cost function \(c: \mathbb{R}^K_{++} \times \mathbb{R}_+ \to \mathbb{R}_+\) is the value function of the CMP: \[c(w, q) = \min_{z \geq 0} \; w \cdot z \quad \text{s.t.} \quad f(z) \geq q\] Equivalently: \(c(w,q) = \min\{w \cdot z : z \in V(q)\}\) where \(V(q) = \{z \geq 0 : f(z) \geq q\}\) is the input requirement set.








NoteDefinition 3.3 — Conditional Factor Demands



The conditional factor demand correspondence \(z: \mathbb{R}^K_{++} \times \mathbb{R}_+ \rightrightarrows \mathbb{R}^K_+\) is: \[z(w, q) = \arg\min_{z \geq 0, \, f(z) \geq q} \; w \cdot z\] These are “conditional” because they are conditioned on a fixed output level \(q\) (unlike unconditional factor demands, which optimize over \(q\) as well). They are the producer analogue of Hicksian compensated demands.

title: 20  3.3 Cost Minimization
text:
21.3 Properties of the Cost Function






ImportantProposition 3.1 — Homogeneity of Degree One of \(c(w,q)\) in \(w\)



For all \(\alpha > 0\): \[c(\alpha w, q) = \alpha \cdot c(w, q)\]








TipProof





\[c(\alpha w, q) = \min_{z \geq 0, \, f(z) \geq q} (\alpha w) \cdot z = \alpha \min_{z \geq 0, \, f(z) \geq q} w \cdot z = \alpha \cdot c(w, q)\] Multiplying factor prices by \(\alpha > 0\) scales the objective but does not change the feasible set \(V(q)\) or the minimizer \(z(w,q)\): \(z(\alpha w, q) = z(w,q)\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.1



HD1 of \(c\) in \(w\) is simply a no-money-illusion property for cost: doubling all factor prices doubles minimum cost, since the cost of any input bundle doubles. Equivalently, conditional factor demands \(z(w,q)\) are homogeneous of degree zero in \(w\).








ImportantProposition 3.2 — Monotonicity of \(c(w,q)\)




\(c(w,q)\) is nondecreasing in \(w\): if \(w' \geq w\) (componentwise), then \(c(w',q) \geq c(w,q)\).
\(c(w,q)\) is nondecreasing in \(q\): if \(q' \geq q\), then \(c(w, q') \geq c(w,q)\) (assuming more output requires weakly more inputs).









TipProof





(a) Let \(z^*\) minimize cost at \((w, q)\). Then \(c(w, q) = w \cdot z^*\). For prices \(w' \geq w\): \[c(w', q) = \min_{z \in V(q)} w' \cdot z \leq w' \cdot z^* \text{ is wrong direction}\] Wait — we need a lower bound. Since \(w' \geq w\): \[c(w', q) = \min_{z \in V(q)} w' \cdot z \geq \min_{z \in V(q)} w \cdot z = c(w,q)\] where the inequality uses \(w' \cdot z \geq w \cdot z\) for all \(z \geq 0\) (since \(w' - w \geq 0\) and \(z \geq 0\)). \(\blacksquare\)
(b) Since \(q' \geq q\), we have \(V(q') \subseteq V(q)\) (more output requires at least as many inputs). Minimizing over a smaller set gives a larger or equal minimum: \[c(w, q') = \min_{z \in V(q')} w \cdot z \geq \min_{z \in V(q)} w \cdot z = c(w,q) \qquad \blacksquare\]









ImportantProposition 3.3 — Concavity of \(c(w,q)\) in \(w\)



The cost function \(c(w, q)\) is concave in \(w\) for each fixed \(q\).








TipProof





The cost function is the infimum of a family of linear functions of \(w\). For each fixed \(z \in V(q)\), the function \(w \mapsto w \cdot z\) is linear (hence concave) in \(w\). Since the infimum of linear functions is concave: \[c(w,q) = \inf_{z \in V(q)} w \cdot z\] is concave in \(w\).
Direct proof: Fix \(w, w' \in \mathbb{R}^K_{++}\), \(\lambda \in [0,1]\). Let \(z^* = z(\lambda w + (1-\lambda)w', q)\) minimize cost at the convex combination of prices. Then: \[\begin{align}
c(\lambda w + (1-\lambda)w', q) &= [\lambda w + (1-\lambda)w'] \cdot z^* \\
&= \lambda (w \cdot z^*) + (1-\lambda)(w' \cdot z^*) \\
&\geq \lambda c(w, q) + (1-\lambda) c(w', q)
\end{align}\] where the inequality uses \(w \cdot z^* \geq c(w,q)\) and \(w' \cdot z^* \geq c(w',q)\) (since \(z^*\) is feasible but not necessarily optimal at \(w\) or \(w'\)). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.2 — Economic Meaning of Concavity



Concavity of \(c\) in \(w\) has a key economic interpretation: the firm benefits from factor price variability in a specific sense — when factor prices fluctuate, the firm can always substitute toward cheaper inputs, so average cost under variable prices is lower than or equal to cost at average prices. This is the producer analogue of compensated demand being downward-sloping.








ImportantProposition 3.4 — Continuity of \(c(w,q)\)



If the production function \(f\) is continuous and \(V(q)\) is nonempty for \(q \geq 0\), then \(c(w,q)\) is continuous in \((w,q)\).








TipProof





Continuity follows from the theorem of the maximum (Berge’s maximum theorem). The feasible correspondence \(V(q)\) is continuous (by continuity of \(f\)) and the objective \(w \cdot z\) is continuous jointly in \((w,z)\). Therefore the value function \(c(w,q) = \min_{z \in V(q)} w \cdot z\) is continuous in \((w,q)\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: 20  3.3 Cost Minimization
text:
21.4 Shephard’s Lemma






ImportantTheorem 3.1 — Shephard’s Lemma (for Costs)



Suppose \(c(w, q)\) is differentiable at \((w^0, q)\). Then: \[z_l(w^0, q) = \frac{\partial c(w^0, q)}{\partial w_l} \quad \forall l = 1, \ldots, K\]








TipProof





Proof via the Envelope Theorem:
At the optimum \(z^*(w,q) = z(w,q)\), define: \[\phi(w) = w \cdot z^*(w^0, q) \quad \text{(cost of the fixed bundle $z^*$ at varying prices)}\] This is a linear function of \(w\) with gradient \(z^*(w^0, q)\). Since \(z^*\) is optimal at \(w^0\) but not necessarily at other prices, \(\phi(w) \geq c(w,q)\) for all \(w\) (the fixed bundle is weakly costlier than the optimal bundle at other prices), and \(\phi(w^0) = c(w^0, q)\).
Therefore, the linear function \(\phi\) lies above the concave function \(c(\cdot, q)\) and touches it at \(w^0\): \(\phi\) is a supporting hyperplane to \(c(\cdot,q)\) from above at \(w^0\). Since \(c\) is differentiable at \(w^0\), the supporting hyperplane equals the tangent hyperplane: \[\nabla_w c(w^0, q) = \nabla_w \phi(w^0) = z^*(w^0, q) \qquad \blacksquare\]
Alternative direct proof: Let \(e_l\) be the \(l\)-th standard basis vector. For small \(h > 0\): \[c(w^0 + h e_l, q) \leq (w^0 + h e_l) \cdot z^* = c(w^0, q) + h z^*_l\] \[c(w^0 + h e_l, q) \geq c(w^0, q) + h \frac{\partial c}{\partial w_l}\bigg|_{\text{lin approx}}\] Together, as \(h \to 0\): \(\partial c(w^0,q)/\partial w_l = z^*_l\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.3 — Shephard’s Lemma and Duality



Shephard’s lemma has the same structure as Roy’s identity for the indirect utility function and Hotelling’s lemma for the profit function: differentiation of a value function yields the optimizer. In each case, the proof relies on the envelope theorem and the “supporting hyperplane” property of the value function. Shephard’s lemma is the foundation for empirical cost studies: estimate \(c(w,q)\), differentiate with respect to factor prices to obtain factor demands.

title: 20  3.3 Cost Minimization
text:
21.5 Factor Demand Substitution Matrix






NoteDefinition 3.4 — Substitution Matrix for Factor Demands



The factor demand substitution matrix is: \[S(w,q) = \frac{\partial z(w,q)}{\partial w} = D^2_w c(w,q)\] the \(K \times K\) Hessian matrix of \(c\) with respect to \(w\).








ImportantProposition 3.5 — Properties of the Substitution Matrix



If \(c(w,q)\) is twice differentiable, \(S(w,q) = D^2_w c(w,q)\) satisfies: 1. Symmetry: \(\frac{\partial z_l}{\partial w_k} = \frac{\partial z_k}{\partial w_l}\) for all \(k, l\) 2. Negative semidefiniteness (NSD): \(v^T S(w,q) v \leq 0\) for all \(v \in \mathbb{R}^K\) 3. \(S(w,q) w = 0\) (from HD0 of \(z\) in \(w\)) 4. In particular, \(\frac{\partial z_l}{\partial w_l} \leq 0\): own-price factor demand is nonincreasing








TipProof





Properties 1 and 2 follow from \(S = D^2_w c\) and concavity of \(c\) in \(w\): the Hessian of a concave function is symmetric (Young’s theorem) and negative semidefinite.
Property 3: Since \(z(w,q)\) is HD0 in \(w\), Euler’s theorem gives \(\sum_k \frac{\partial z_l}{\partial w_k} w_k = 0\) for all \(l\), i.e., \(S w = 0\).
Property 4: NSD implies all diagonal entries are nonpositive: for \(v = e_l\), \(e_l^T S e_l = S_{ll} = \partial z_l / \partial w_l \leq 0\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: 20  3.3 Cost Minimization
text:
21.6 Average and Marginal Costs






NoteDefinition 3.5 — Average and Marginal Cost



Given factor prices \(w\) (treated as fixed) and the cost function \(c(w,q)\): - Average cost: \(AC(q) = \frac{c(w,q)}{q}\) for \(q > 0\) - Marginal cost: \(MC(q) = \frac{\partial c(w,q)}{\partial q}\) - Fixed cost: \(c(w, 0) = FC\) (cost when \(q = 0\); zero under free disposal if inputs can be idled) - Variable cost: \(VC(q) = c(w,q) - c(w,0)\)


The relationship between AC, MC, and returns to scale is as follows:






ImportantProposition 3.6 — Returns to Scale and Cost




CRS: \(c(w, \lambda q) = \lambda c(w, q)\) for all \(\lambda > 0\). Hence \(AC(q) = MC(q)\) (constant, independent of \(q\)).
IRS: \(c\) is subadditive in \(q\): \(c(w, q + q') \leq c(w,q) + c(w,q')\). At the optimum: \(MC(q) < AC(q)\) (marginal cost below average cost pulls the average down).
DRS: \(c\) is superadditive in \(q\). \(MC(q) > AC(q)\) (marginal cost above average cost pulls the average up).









TipProof





(a) CRS implies linear cost in \(q\): Under CRS, \(f(\lambda z) = \lambda f(z)\). If \(z^*(w,q)\) minimizes cost for output \(q\), then \(\lambda z^*(w,q)\) is feasible for output \(\lambda q\) (since \(f(\lambda z^*) = \lambda f(z^*) = \lambda q\)). Moreover, it minimizes cost for \(\lambda q\) (by HD1 of \(c\)): \[c(w, \lambda q) \leq w \cdot (\lambda z^*) = \lambda (w \cdot z^*) = \lambda c(w,q)\] Similarly, \(c(w, q) = c(w, (1/\lambda)(\lambda q)) \leq (1/\lambda) c(w, \lambda q)\), so \(c(w, \lambda q) \geq \lambda c(w,q)\). Combined: \(c(w, \lambda q) = \lambda c(w,q)\). Dividing by \(q\): $AC = c/q = $ constant; differentiating in \(\lambda\) at \(\lambda = 1\): \(MC = \partial c / \partial q = c(w,q)/q = AC\).
(b) IRS: Under IRS, \(f(\lambda z) > \lambda f(z)\) for \(\lambda > 1\). For output \(q\), the minimizer \(z^*\) satisfies \(f(z^*) = q\). For output \(\lambda q\), we need \(f(z) \geq \lambda q\). Since \(f(\lambda z^*) > \lambda q\), we can use slightly less than \(\lambda z^*\). Hence \(c(w, \lambda q) < \lambda c(w, q)\), meaning \(c\) is sublinear (subadditive). Differentiating: \(MC = \partial c / \partial q < c(w,q)/q = AC\).
(c) DRS: Analogously, \(c\) is superlinear, so \(MC > AC\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.4 — The U-Shaped AC Curve



When returns to scale shift from IRS to DRS as \(q\) increases (common in practice), the \(MC\) curve crosses the \(AC\) curve at the \(AC\) minimum. For \(q\) below the crossing point: IRS, \(MC < AC\), \(AC\) is falling. For \(q\) above: DRS, \(MC > AC\), \(AC\) is rising. The \(AC\)-minimizing output is the minimum efficient scale. At \(MC = AC\), returns to scale are locally CRS.

title: 20  3.3 Cost Minimization
section: 21.7 Duality Between Cost Function and Production Function
text:
21.7 Duality Between Cost Function and Production Function






ImportantTheorem 3.2 — Cost-Production Duality



Under standard regularity conditions (monotonicity, continuity, quasiconcavity of \(f\)), the production function can be recovered from the cost function: \[f(z) = \max\{q \geq 0 : w \cdot z \geq c(w, q) \; \forall w \gg 0\}\] That is, \(f(z)\) equals the largest output level \(q\) such that the cost of \(z\) at any prices exceeds the minimum cost of producing \(q\).








TipProof





Sketch: Let \(\hat{f}(z) = \max\{q : w \cdot z \geq c(w,q) \; \forall w \gg 0\}\).

\(f(z) \leq \hat{f}(z)\): If \(z \in V(q)\) (i.e., \(f(z) \geq q\)), then for any \(w \gg 0\): \(w \cdot z \geq c(w,q)\) by definition of \(c\) as the minimum. Hence \(q\) satisfies the condition in \(\hat{f}\), so \(\hat{f}(z) \geq q = f(z)\).
\(f(z) \geq \hat{f}(z)\): Suppose \(f(z) < q\). We must show \(\exists w\) such that \(w \cdot z < c(w,q)\). Under convexity and monotonicity of \(f\), \(z \notin V(q)\) implies that \(z\) can be separated from the convex set \(V(q)\) by a hyperplane: there exists \(w^* \gg 0\) such that \(w^* \cdot z < \min_{z' \in V(q)} w^* \cdot z' = c(w^*, q)\). Hence \(q\) does not satisfy the condition in \(\hat{f}(z)\).

Combining: \(\hat{f}(z) = f(z)\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.5 — Duality in Practice



The duality theorem means that a cost function \(c(w,q)\) satisfying the regularity properties (HD1, concavity, monotonicity in \(w\); nondecreasing in \(q\)) is the cost function of some production function, and that production function is uniquely determined by \(c\) (up to the convex hull of its isoquants). Empirical cost estimation therefore identifies the technology.

title: 20  3.3 Cost Minimization
text:
21.8 Example: CES Production Function






NoteExample 3.3 — CES Cost Function



Consider the CES production function with two inputs: \[f(z_1, z_2) = (z_1^{\rho} + z_2^{\rho})^{1/\rho}, \quad \rho \in (-\infty, 0) \cup (0, 1]\] (This is CRS.)
CMP: \(\min_{z_1, z_2 \geq 0} w_1 z_1 + w_2 z_2\) subject to \((z_1^{\rho} + z_2^{\rho})^{1/\rho} \geq q\).
FOC (Lagrangian with multiplier \(\lambda\)): \[w_i = \lambda \cdot \frac{\partial f}{\partial z_i} = \lambda \cdot z_i^{\rho-1} (z_1^{\rho} + z_2^{\rho})^{(1/\rho)-1}\]
Dividing FOCs: \(\frac{w_1}{w_2} = \left(\frac{z_1}{z_2}\right)^{\rho-1}\), so \(\frac{z_1}{z_2} = \left(\frac{w_1}{w_2}\right)^{1/(\rho-1)} = \left(\frac{w_2}{w_1}\right)^{1/(1-\rho)}\).
Let \(\sigma = 1/(1-\rho)\) (elasticity of substitution). Then \(z_1/z_2 = (w_2/w_1)^{\sigma}\).
Substituting back into the production constraint and simplifying: \[c(w_1, w_2, q) = q \cdot (w_1^{1-\sigma} + w_2^{1-\sigma})^{1/(1-\sigma)}\] where \(1-\sigma = 1 - 1/(1-\rho) = -\rho/(1-\rho)\).
Equivalently, letting \(s = 1-\sigma\): \[c(w,q) = q \cdot \left(\sum_i w_i^{s}\right)^{1/s}, \quad s = \frac{-\rho}{1-\rho} = \frac{\rho}{\rho - 1}\]
Verification of Shephard’s Lemma: \[z_1(w,q) = \frac{\partial c}{\partial w_1} = q \cdot \frac{w_1^{s-1}}{(w_1^s + w_2^s)^{(s-1)/s}} = q \cdot \left(\frac{w_1^s + w_2^s}{w_1^s}\right)^{(1-s)/s} \cdot w_1^{-1}\] This matches the conditional factor demand derived from the FOCs. \(\checkmark\)
Special cases: - \(\rho \to 1\) (\(\sigma \to \infty\); perfect substitutes): \(c(w,q) = q \cdot \min(w_1, w_2)\) - \(\rho \to 0\) (\(\sigma \to 1\); Cobb-Douglas): \(c(w,q) = q \cdot w_1^{1/2} w_2^{1/2} \cdot 2\) (for equal shares) - \(\rho \to -\infty\) (\(\sigma \to 0\); perfect complements): \(c(w,q) = q(w_1 + w_2)\) with equal input requirements

title: 20  3.3 Cost Minimization
text:
21.9 Economic Intuition
The cost function \(c(w,q)\) is the fundamental object for understanding the supply side of the economy. It encodes everything about the firm’s technology that is relevant for competitive behavior: how costs respond to output (determining the supply curve), how costs respond to factor prices (determining factor demands via Shephard’s lemma), and whether there are economies or diseconomies of scale.
The concavity of \(c\) in \(w\) has a beautiful interpretation: it means that firms can substitute inputs in response to factor price changes. When input prices change, the firm adjusts its input mix toward cheaper inputs, so the cost increase is less than proportional to the price increase. The degree of curvature of \(c\) (how concave it is) measures the degree of factor substitutability.
Shephard’s lemma provides a tight connection between the cost function and observable factor demand: it says that the slope of the cost function in factor prices equals the conditional factor demand. This is deeply useful empirically — one can estimate a cost function and recover all factor demands from it, without separately estimating each demand equation. The symmetry of cross-price effects (\(\partial z_l / \partial w_k = \partial z_k / \partial w_l\)) is a testable restriction that follows from Shephard’s lemma and Young’s theorem.

text:

text: 22 3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
crumbs: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands

title: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
text:
22.1 Context
Having established the PMP and CMP separately, this note synthesizes them. The key insight is that the PMP and CMP are dual to each other: the profit function equals the net revenue from cost-minimizing production at the profit-maximizing output. This profit-cost duality allows us to deploy the results from both problems simultaneously.
The central economic questions addressed here are: How does supply respond to price changes in the short run versus the long run (Le Chatelier principle)? How do returns to scale determine equilibrium market structure? What is the relationship between unconditional and conditional factor demands? These questions are central to applied microeconomics, industrial organization, and general equilibrium theory.
crumbs: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands

title: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
text:
22.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 3.1 — Profit-Cost Duality



The profit function and cost function are related by: \[\pi(p, w) = \max_{q \geq 0} \left[pq - c(w, q)\right]\] Profit equals revenues \(pq\) from selling \(q\) units minus the minimized cost of producing \(q\) units.








NoteDefinition 3.2 — Unconditional Factor Demands



The unconditional factor demands \(z^*(p, w)\) are the input demands at the profit-maximizing output level: \[z^*(p, w) = z\bigl(w, q^*(p,w)\bigr)\] where \(q^*(p,w)\) is the profit-maximizing output and \(z(w,q)\) are the conditional factor demands from the CMP.
crumbs: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands

title: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
text:
22.3 Profit-Cost Duality






ImportantProposition 3.1 — First-Order Condition for Profit Maximization



Suppose \(c(w, q)\) is differentiable in \(q\) and the profit-maximizing output \(q^*(p,w) > 0\) is interior. Then: \[p = MC(w, q^*) = \frac{\partial c(w, q^*)}{\partial q}\] Price equals marginal cost at the profit optimum.








TipProof





From the PMP decomposed as \(\pi(p,w) = \max_{q \geq 0} [pq - c(w,q)]\), the first-order condition for an interior maximum with respect to \(q\) is: \[\frac{d}{dq}[pq - c(w,q)] = p - \frac{\partial c(w,q)}{\partial q} = 0\] \[\Rightarrow p = \frac{\partial c(w,q^*)}{\partial q} = MC(w, q^*)\] The second-order condition is \(-\frac{\partial^2 c}{\partial q^2} \leq 0\), i.e., \(\frac{\partial^2 c}{\partial q^2} \geq 0\): marginal cost is nondecreasing in \(q\). This holds under DRS (where \(c\) is convex in \(q\)) but not necessarily under IRS. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.1 — Supply Function from MC



When \(MC(w,q)\) is strictly increasing in \(q\) (DRS), the equation \(p = MC(w, q^*)\) uniquely determines \(q^*(p,w)\) as the inverse of the MC function: \[q^*(p,w) = MC^{-1}(p; w)\] This is the firm’s supply function. The supply curve is the marginal cost curve (above minimum average variable cost).








ImportantProposition 3.2 — Envelope Relationships: Hotelling via Shephard



Using the profit-cost decomposition \(\pi(p,w) = pq^*(p,w) - c(w, q^*(p,w))\):

\(\frac{\partial \pi}{\partial p} = q^*(p,w)\) (Hotelling’s lemma for output)
\(\frac{\partial \pi}{\partial w_l} = -z^*_l(p,w)\) (Hotelling’s lemma for inputs, with negative sign)









TipProof





(a) By the envelope theorem applied to \(\pi(p,w) = \max_q [pq - c(w,q)]\), treating \(p\) as the parameter: \[\frac{\partial \pi}{\partial p} = q^*(p,w) + \underbrace{\left(p - \frac{\partial c}{\partial q}\right)}_{\text{= 0 at optimum}} \frac{\partial q^*}{\partial p} = q^*(p,w)\]
(b) By the envelope theorem, treating \(w_l\) as the parameter (it enters \(c\) but not \(pq\) directly): \[\frac{\partial \pi}{\partial w_l} = -\frac{\partial c(w, q^*)}{\partial w_l} + \underbrace{\left(p - \frac{\partial c}{\partial q}\right)}_{\text{= 0}} \frac{\partial q^*}{\partial w_l} = -\frac{\partial c(w, q^*)}{\partial w_l} = -z_l(w, q^*) = -z^*_l(p,w)\] where Shephard’s lemma was used in the last step. \(\blacksquare\)
crumbs: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands

title: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
text:
22.4 Comparative Statics: Full Matrix
The full matrix of supply and factor demand responses can be derived by combining Hotelling’s and Shephard’s lemmas.






NoteDefinition 3.3 — Full Comparative Statics Matrix



Partition prices into \((p, w)\) where \(p\) is the output price and \(w = (w_1, \ldots, w_K)\) are input prices. The full \((1+K) \times (1+K)\) comparative statics matrix is: \[\begin{pmatrix} \partial q^*/\partial p & \partial q^*/\partial w^T \\ \partial z^*/\partial p & \partial z^*/\partial w \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \partial^2 \pi/\partial p^2 & \partial^2 \pi/\partial p \partial w^T \\ \partial^2 \pi/\partial w \partial p & \partial^2 \pi/\partial w^2 \end{pmatrix} = D^2_{(p,w)} \pi\]








ImportantProposition 3.3 — Properties of the Comparative Statics Matrix



\(D^2_{(p,w)} \pi\) is: 1. Symmetric (Young’s theorem on \(\pi\)) 2. Positive semidefinite (convexity of \(\pi\)) 3. \(D^2_{(p,w)} \pi \cdot (p,w)^T = 0\) (HD0 of supply and factor demands)
In particular: - \(\partial q^*/\partial p \geq 0\): supply is nondecreasing in output price - \(\partial z^*_l/\partial w_l \leq 0\): unconditional own-price factor demand is nonincreasing - \(\partial q^*/\partial w_l = \partial z^*_l / \partial p\): cross-effects are symmetric








CautionRemark 3.2 — Symmetric Cross-Effects



The symmetry \(\partial q^*/\partial w_l = -\partial z^*_l/\partial p\) (note the sign: Hotelling gives \(\partial \pi/\partial w_l = -z^*_l\) so \(\partial z^*_l/\partial p = -\partial^2 \pi/\partial w_l \partial p\)) is a testable restriction: the effect of a factor price change on output equals (up to sign) the effect of the output price change on that factor’s demand. This has strong empirical content.
crumbs: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands

title: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
section: 22.5 Returns to Scale and Market Structure
text:
22.5 Returns to Scale and Market Structure






ImportantProposition 3.4 — CRS and Zero Profit



Under constant returns to scale, in a competitive equilibrium with positive production, profit must be zero: \(\pi(p,w) = 0\).








TipProof





By Proposition 3.1 of 3.1 Production Sets, under CRS \(\pi(p,w) \in \{0, +\infty\}\). In a competitive equilibrium, supply must equal finite demand, so \(\pi(p,w) = +\infty\) is impossible. Hence \(\pi(p,w) = 0\). Alternatively: if \(\pi > 0\), the firm would want to scale up indefinitely (since CRS means scaling up is always feasible), driving supply to infinity. \(\blacksquare\)



Implications for market structure:








Returns to Scale
Cost structure
Equilibrium implication




CRS
\(c(w,q) = q \cdot c(w,1)\); $AC = MC = $ const
Zero profit; supply horizontal; scale indeterminate


DRS
\(c\) strictly convex in \(q\); \(AC < MC\)
Finite supply; upward-sloping supply curve; positive profit


IRS
\(c\) strictly concave in \(q\); \(AC > MC\)
Natural monopoly tendency; competitive equilibrium may fail to exist









CautionRemark 3.3 — IRS and Natural Monopoly



Under IRS, the average cost curve is everywhere declining. A single firm producing all industry output always has lower average cost than any division of output among multiple firms. This is the definition of a natural monopoly. Competitive equilibrium breaks down because at any price \(p > 0\), if a firm makes profit, it wants to scale up; if \(p = AC\), it makes zero profit but a competitor who produces slightly more makes profit (since \(AC\) is declining). The natural outcome is monopoly or regulated pricing at \(p = AC\).
crumbs: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands

title: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
text:
22.6 Producer Surplus






NoteDefinition 3.4 — Producer Surplus



The producer surplus at price \(p\) is: \[PS(p) = \pi(p, w) - \pi(0, w)\] For a competitive firm with \(0 \in Y\) (inaction possible), \(\pi(0,w) = 0\) (zero profit at zero output price), so: \[PS(p) = \pi(p, w)\]
Equivalently, producer surplus is the area above the supply curve and below the price: \[PS(p) = \int_0^{q^*(p)} [p - MC(w, q)] \, dq = p \cdot q^* - \int_0^{q^*} MC(w, q) \, dq = p q^* - VC(q^*)\]








TipEquivalence of the Two Definitions





\(PS(p) = p q^* - VC(q^*)\) where \(VC(q) = c(w,q) - FC\).
The area above supply and below \(p\): supply curve is \(p = MC(q)\) (the inverse supply). The area to the left of the supply curve between \(q = 0\) and \(q = q^*\) is: \[\int_0^{p} q^*(s) \, ds = p q^* - \int_0^{q^*} MC(q) \, dq\] where integration by parts was used. Since \(\int_0^{q^*} MC(q) dq = VC(q^*) = c(q^*) - FC\): \[PS(p) = p q^* - VC(q^*) = \pi(p) + FC\] If there are no fixed costs (\(FC = 0\)), then \(PS = \pi\). \(\blacksquare\)
crumbs: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands

title: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
text:
22.7 Factor Demand Elasticities






NoteDefinition 3.5 — Factor Demand Elasticities



The own-price elasticity of (conditional) factor demand for input \(l\): \[\epsilon_{ll} = \frac{\partial \ln z_l}{\partial \ln w_l} = \frac{\partial z_l}{\partial w_l} \cdot \frac{w_l}{z_l} \leq 0\]
The cross-price elasticity of factor demand: \[\epsilon_{lk} = \frac{\partial \ln z_l}{\partial \ln w_k} = \frac{\partial z_l}{\partial w_k} \cdot \frac{w_k}{z_l}\] Inputs \(l\) and \(k\) are substitutes if \(\epsilon_{lk} > 0\) (raise price of \(k\), demand for \(l\) increases) and complements if \(\epsilon_{lk} < 0\).
The output elasticity of factor demand: \[\epsilon_{lq} = \frac{\partial \ln z_l}{\partial \ln q} = \frac{\partial z_l}{\partial q} \cdot \frac{q}{z_l}\]








ImportantProposition 3.5 — Symmetry of Cross-Elasticities (Allen-Uzawa)



The Allen-Uzawa elasticity of substitution \(\sigma_{lk}^{AU} = \frac{c \cdot c_{lk}}{c_l c_k}\) (where \(c_l = \partial c/\partial w_l\)) is symmetric: \(\sigma_{lk}^{AU} = \sigma_{kl}^{AU}\).








TipProof





From Shephard’s lemma, \(c_l = z_l\) and \(c_k = z_k\). By Young’s theorem, \(c_{lk} = \partial^2 c/\partial w_l \partial w_k = \partial^2 c/\partial w_k \partial w_l = c_{kl}\). Hence \(\sigma_{lk}^{AU} = c \cdot c_{lk}/(c_l c_k) = c \cdot c_{kl}/(c_k c_l) = \sigma_{kl}^{AU}\). \(\blacksquare\)
crumbs: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands

title: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
text:
22.8 Le Chatelier Principle
The Le Chatelier principle states that long-run responses to price changes are at least as large (in absolute value) as short-run responses. Intuitively, in the short run some inputs are fixed; in the long run all inputs are variable, providing more flexibility to respond to price changes.






NoteDefinition 3.6 — Short-Run and Long-Run



The short-run cost function \(c^{SR}(w, q; \bar{z}_K)\) holds input \(K\) fixed at \(\bar{z}_K\): \[c^{SR}(w, q; \bar{z}_K) = \min_{z_1, \ldots, z_{K-1}} w_{-K} \cdot z_{-K} + w_K \bar{z}_K \quad \text{s.t.} \quad f(z_{-K}, \bar{z}_K) \geq q\] The long-run cost function \(c^{LR}(w, q) = \min_z w \cdot z\) s.t. \(f(z) \geq q\) optimizes over all inputs.








ImportantTheorem 3.3 — Le Chatelier Principle (Supply Elasticity)



At the long-run profit-maximizing output \(q^{LR}(p,w)\), the long-run supply response to a price change is at least as large as the short-run supply response: \[\frac{\partial q^{LR}}{\partial p} \geq \frac{\partial q^{SR}}{\partial p} \geq 0\] More generally, for any input \(l\) that is variable in both the short run and long run, the long-run price response of that factor exceeds the short-run response in absolute value: \[\left|\frac{\partial z^{LR}_l}{\partial w_l}\right| \geq \left|\frac{\partial z^{SR}_l}{\partial w_l}\right|\]








TipProof





Proof sketch for supply elasticity:
At the long-run optimum \((q^*, z^*)\), \(q^{SR}(p, w; \bar{z}_K) = q^{LR}(p,w)\) when \(\bar{z}_K = z^*_K\) (the short-run optimum coincides with the long-run optimum when the fixed input is set at its long-run optimal level).
The short-run and long-run profit functions satisfy \(\pi^{LR}(p,w) \geq \pi^{SR}(p,w; z^*_K(p,w))\) for all \((p,w)\) (the long run is more flexible), with equality at the equilibrium prices \((p^0, w^0)\): both equal \(\pi^0\).
Define \(g(p) = \pi^{LR}(p, w^0)\) and \(h(p) = \pi^{SR}(p, w^0; z^*_K(p^0, w^0))\). Then: - \(g(p) \geq h(p)\) for all \(p\) (long run dominates) - \(g(p^0) = h(p^0)\) (equal at \(p^0\))
Both \(g\) and \(h\) are convex and have the same value at \(p^0\). Since \(g \geq h\) everywhere and they are equal at \(p^0\), \(g\) is “more convex” than \(h\) at \(p^0\): \[g''(p^0) \geq h''(p^0)\] By Hotelling’s lemma, \(g'(p) = q^{LR}(p,w^0)\) and \(h'(p) = q^{SR}(p,w^0; \bar{z}_K)\). Thus \(g''(p^0) = \partial q^{LR}/\partial p\) and \(h''(p^0) = \partial q^{SR}/\partial p\): \[\frac{\partial q^{LR}}{\partial p}\bigg|_{p^0} \geq \frac{\partial q^{SR}}{\partial p}\bigg|_{p^0} \qquad \blacksquare\]









CautionRemark 3.4 — Economic Interpretation of Le Chatelier



The Le Chatelier principle captures the idea that adjustment takes time. In the short run, firms cannot change all inputs; this constrains their ability to respond to price changes. In the long run, all inputs are variable and firms can fully optimize. Therefore, long-run supply curves are flatter (more elastic) than short-run supply curves, and long-run factor demand curves are steeper (more elastic in absolute value) than short-run ones.
This has important policy implications: the short-run response of supply to a price change (e.g., from a tax or subsidy) understates the long-run response. Policymakers who rely on short-run elasticities will underestimate the long-run effects of price interventions.
crumbs: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands

title: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
section: 22.9 Connecting Conditional and Unconditional Factor Demands
text:
22.9 Connecting Conditional and Unconditional Factor Demands
The relationship between conditional factor demands \(z(w,q)\) and unconditional factor demands \(z^*(p,w)\) illuminates how output decisions interact with input decisions.






ImportantProposition 3.6 — Decomposition of Factor Demand Response



For an unconditional factor demand \(z^*_l(p,w) = z_l(w, q^*(p,w))\), differentiating with respect to \(w_k\): \[\frac{\partial z^*_l}{\partial w_k} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial z_l}{\partial w_k}\bigg|_q}_{\text{substitution effect}} + \underbrace{\frac{\partial z_l}{\partial q} \cdot \frac{\partial q^*}{\partial w_k}}_{\text{output effect}}\]








TipProof





By the chain rule applied to \(z^*_l(p,w) = z_l(w, q^*(p,w))\): \[\frac{\partial z^*_l}{\partial w_k} = \frac{\partial z_l}{\partial w_k}\bigg|_{q \text{ fixed}} + \frac{\partial z_l}{\partial q} \cdot \frac{\partial q^*}{\partial w_k}\] The first term is the conditional (substitution) effect, holding output fixed. The second term captures how the change in \(w_k\) affects optimal output \(q^*\), which in turn affects input demand for \(l\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 3.5 — Sign of the Output Effect



The output effect \(\frac{\partial z_l}{\partial q} \cdot \frac{\partial q^*}{\partial w_k}\) has an ambiguous sign in general: - Raising \(w_k\) reduces optimal output \(q^*\) (since costs increase): \(\partial q^*/\partial w_k \leq 0\). - If \(l\) is a normal input (\(\partial z_l/\partial q > 0\)), higher output means more of input \(l\). - Therefore the output effect \(\frac{\partial z_l}{\partial q} \cdot \frac{\partial q^*}{\partial w_k} \leq 0\) reinforces the substitution effect for normal inputs.
The decomposition is analogous to the Slutsky decomposition in consumer theory, with the “output effect” playing the role of the income effect. Unlike the income effect in consumer theory, the output effect here has a determinate sign for normal inputs (it reinforces the substitution effect), which is why the law of unconditional factor demand (own-price factor demand decreasing) is more robust than the law of demand in consumer theory.
crumbs: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands

title: 21  3.4 Supply and Factor Demands
text:
22.10 Economic Intuition
The synthesis of the PMP and CMP reveals that competitive firm behavior is characterized by two conditions: (1) cost minimization — use the cheapest input combination for any given output level; (2) output optimization — set output where price equals marginal cost. These two conditions together fully determine the firm’s supply and input demands.
The Le Chatelier principle is one of the deepest results in producer theory. It shows that the flexibility of the optimization problem — how many variables can adjust — determines the elasticity of the response. Long-run responses are always at least as large as short-run responses because the long run simply has more degrees of freedom. This is a general principle in comparative statics: adding constraints reduces the magnitude of optimal responses.
Returns to scale drive market structure. The welfare analysis of competitive markets (the efficiency of competitive equilibrium, the deadweight loss from monopoly, the effects of entry) all depend critically on whether firms face CRS, DRS, or IRS. CRS supports efficient competitive markets with free entry; IRS creates the natural monopoly problem that is central to regulatory economics.

title: 22  Problem Set: Producer Theory
text: 23 Problem Set: Producer Theory

title: 22  Problem Set: Producer Theory
text:
23.1 Problem 1 — Production Set Properties
Let the production function be \(f: \mathbb{R}^2_+ \to \mathbb{R}_+\) defined by: \[f(z_1, z_2) = \min\left\{z_1^{1/2}, z_2\right\}\] with associated production set \(Y = \{(-z_1, -z_2, q) \in \mathbb{R}^3 : q \leq f(z_1, z_2), \; z_1, z_2 \geq 0\}\).
(a) Verify formally whether \(Y\) satisfies each of the following properties: non-emptiness (inaction), closedness, no free lunch, free disposal, and irreversibility. For each property, either prove it holds or provide a counterexample.
(b) Determine the returns to scale of this technology. Is \(Y\) a cone? If not, does \(Y\) satisfy NIRTS, NDIRTS, both, or neither? Provide a rigorous proof.
(c) Is \(Y\) convex? Prove your answer rigorously. What does your answer imply about the curvature of \(f\)?
(d) Compute the input requirement set \(V(q) = \{(z_1, z_2) \geq 0 : f(z_1, z_2) \geq q\}\) explicitly. Characterize the isoquants and verify that they have the standard properties associated with the production technology.
(e) Write down the transformation function \(F(y)\) for this technology and characterize the production frontier.

title: 22  Problem Set: Producer Theory
section: 23.2 Problem 2 — Profit Function and Hotelling’s Lemma
text:
23.2 Problem 2 — Profit Function and Hotelling’s Lemma
Consider a firm with production function \(f(z_1, z_2) = z_1^{\alpha} z_2^{\beta}\) where \(\alpha, \beta > 0\) and \(\alpha + \beta < 1\) (decreasing returns to scale). The firm is a price-taker in both input and output markets, facing output price \(p > 0\) and input prices \(w_1, w_2 > 0\).
(a) Set up the profit maximization problem. Derive the first-order conditions and solve for the profit-maximizing input demands \(z_1^*(p, w_1, w_2)\) and \(z_2^*(p, w_1, w_2)\), and the profit-maximizing output \(q^*(p, w_1, w_2)\).
(b) Derive the profit function \(\pi(p, w_1, w_2)\) explicitly. Show that it is homogeneous of degree one in \((p, w_1, w_2)\).
(c) Verify Hotelling’s lemma: show that \(\partial \pi / \partial p = q^*\) and \(\partial \pi / \partial w_i = -z_i^*\) for \(i = 1, 2\).
(d) Compute the \(3 \times 3\) matrix of second derivatives \(D^2_{(p, w_1, w_2)} \pi\). Verify that this matrix is symmetric and positive semidefinite. What economic properties does PSD imply for the supply and input demand functions?
(e) Verify the law of supply: show that if \(p\) increases (holding \(w_1, w_2\) fixed), then \(q^*\) increases. Compute the own-price supply elasticity \(\partial \ln q^* / \partial \ln p\) and express it in terms of \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\).

title: 22  Problem Set: Producer Theory
section: 23.3 Problem 3 — CES Cost Function and Shephard’s Lemma
text:
23.3 Problem 3 — CES Cost Function and Shephard’s Lemma
Consider a firm with three inputs and CES production function: \[f(z_1, z_2, z_3) = \left(z_1^{\rho} + z_2^{\rho} + z_3^{\rho}\right)^{1/\rho}, \quad \rho \in (0,1)\] with factor prices \(w = (w_1, w_2, w_3) \gg 0\) and output target \(q > 0\).
(a) Set up and solve the cost minimization problem. Derive the conditional factor demands \(z_i(w, q)\) for \(i = 1, 2, 3\).
(b) Derive the cost function \(c(w, q)\) in closed form. Show explicitly that \(c\) is: - Homogeneous of degree one in \(w\) - Nondecreasing in each \(w_i\) - Concave in \(w\) (you may verify this by computing the Hessian \(D^2_w c\) and showing it is NSD) - Linear in \(q\) (confirming CRS)
(c) Apply Shephard’s lemma: compute \(\partial c / \partial w_i\) and verify that it equals \(z_i(w,q)\) from part (a).
(d) Compute the \(3 \times 3\) factor demand substitution matrix \(S_{ij} = \partial z_i / \partial w_j\). Verify symmetry (\(S_{ij} = S_{ji}\)), NSD, and \(S w = 0\).
(e) Compute the own-price factor demand elasticities \(\epsilon_{ii} = \partial \ln z_i / \partial \ln w_i\) and the cross-price elasticities \(\epsilon_{ij}\) for \(i \neq j\). Express them in terms of \(\rho\) and the elasticity of substitution \(\sigma = 1/(1-\rho)\). Are the inputs substitutes or complements? Does the answer depend on \(\rho\)?
(f) Derive the average cost \(AC(q)\) and marginal cost \(MC(q)\). Verify that \(AC = MC\) (consistent with CRS). What happens to the cost function as \(\rho \to -\infty\) (Leontief limit)?

title: 22  Problem Set: Producer Theory
section: 23.4 Problem 4 — CRS Implies Zero or Infinite Profit
text:
23.4 Problem 4 — CRS Implies Zero or Infinite Profit
This problem asks you to extend and refine Proposition 3.1 from 3.1 Production Sets.
(a) Let \(Y \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) be a production set satisfying free disposal and constant returns to scale (i.e., \(Y\) is a cone with \(0 \in Y\)). Prove rigorously that for every price vector \(p \gg 0\), the profit function satisfies \(\pi(p) \in \{0, +\infty\}\).
(b) Suppose \(Y\) satisfies CRS and the profit function is finite at some prices \(p^0 \gg 0\): \(\pi(p^0) = 0\). Show that the zero-profit condition can be expressed as: \[p^0 \cdot y \leq 0 \quad \text{for all } y \in Y\] and interpret this as a no-arbitrage condition.
(c) Now suppose \(Y\) satisfies nonincreasing returns to scale (NIRTS) but not necessarily CRS. Show that in this case, the profit function is always finite: \(\pi(p) < +\infty\) for all \(p\). (Hint: Use the fact that NIRTS implies the firm can always scale down to zero, bounding profit above by zero from the inaction option.)
Wait — is \(\pi < +\infty\) the right claim? Re-examine: under NIRTS, is \(\pi\) necessarily finite? Provide a careful argument or a counterexample.
(d) Consider a competitive industry where all firms have CRS technology but differ in their productivity parameter \(A\): firm \(i\) has production function \(f_i(z) = A_i \cdot g(z)\) for some common function \(g\). Characterize the zero-profit conditions across firms. Which firms produce positive output in a competitive equilibrium? What determines equilibrium output prices?
(e) Explain intuitively why the result of part (a) implies that competitive general equilibrium models with CRS production require specific price vectors (those satisfying \(\pi = 0\)) and why scale is indeterminate for individual firms under CRS.

title: 22  Problem Set: Producer Theory
text:
23.5 Problem 5 — Le Chatelier Principle
A firm has production function \(f(z_1, z_2) = z_1^{1/3} z_2^{1/3}\) with output price \(p > 0\) and factor prices \(w_1, w_2 > 0\).
(a) Solve the long-run profit maximization problem (both inputs variable). Derive the long-run supply function \(q^{LR}(p, w_1, w_2)\) and the long-run profit function \(\pi^{LR}(p, w_1, w_2)\).
(b) Suppose input 2 is fixed at level \(\bar{z}_2\) in the short run. Solve the short-run profit maximization problem. Derive \(q^{SR}(p, w_1; \bar{z}_2)\) and \(\pi^{SR}(p, w_1; \bar{z}_2)\).
(c) Show explicitly that at the long-run optimum (i.e., when \(\bar{z}_2 = z_2^*(p, w_1, w_2)\)), \(q^{SR} = q^{LR}\). Then compute: \[\frac{\partial q^{LR}}{\partial p} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial q^{SR}}{\partial p}\bigg|_{\bar{z}_2 = z_2^*}\] Verify that \(\partial q^{LR}/\partial p \geq \partial q^{SR}/\partial p\) (Le Chatelier for supply).
(d) Repeat the analysis for input demand: compute \(\partial z_1^{LR}/\partial w_1\) (long-run) and \(\partial z_1^{SR}/\partial w_1\) (short-run, with \(z_2\) fixed). Verify \(|\partial z_1^{LR}/\partial w_1| \geq |\partial z_1^{SR}/\partial w_1|\).
(e) Provide an intuitive explanation of the Le Chatelier principle in this specific example. Why does fixing \(z_2\) reduce the firm’s ability to respond to price changes? What role does the cross-partial \(\partial^2 \pi^{LR}/\partial p \partial w_2\) play?
(f) State and prove the general form of the Le Chatelier principle for cost functions: \(|\partial z_l^{LR}/\partial w_l| \geq |\partial z_l^{SR}/\partial w_l|\) for any input \(l\). Your proof should use the convexity/concavity properties of the profit/cost function and not rely on specific functional forms.

title: 22  Problem Set: Producer Theory
section: 23.6 Problem 6 — Duality: Recovering the Production Function from the Cost Function
text:
23.6 Problem 6 — Duality: Recovering the Production Function from the Cost Function
This problem develops the duality between cost functions and production functions.
(a) Suppose you are given the cost function: \[c(w_1, w_2, q) = q^2 \cdot w_1^{1/2} w_2^{1/2}\] Verify that this is a valid cost function: check HD1 in \(w\), concavity in \(w\), and monotonicity in \(q\).
(b) Use Shephard’s lemma to derive the conditional factor demands \(z_1(w, q)\) and \(z_2(w,q)\).
(c) Invert the factor demands to eliminate \(w_1\) and \(w_2\) in terms of \(z_1\), \(z_2\), and \(q\). Use the constraint that at the optimum, the cost function satisfies \(c(w,q) = w_1 z_1 + w_2 z_2\) (the minimum cost equals actual expenditure). Derive the production function \(f(z_1, z_2)\) that rationalizes this cost function.
(d) Verify your answer by checking that \(c(w, q) = \min_{z: f(z) \geq q} w \cdot z\) when \(f\) is the function you derived.
(e) More generally, state the duality theorem relating cost functions and production functions: under what conditions does a function \(c(w,q)\) that is HD1 and concave in \(w\) correspond to a well-defined production function? What regularity conditions on the production function guarantee that this function is fully recoverable from \(c\)?
(f) Explain how this duality result justifies the empirical strategy of estimating a flexible cost function (e.g., the translog or generalized Leontief form) rather than estimating the production function directly. What are the advantages of working with the cost function in applied work?

title: 23  Part IV: Choice Under Uncertainty — Overview
text: 24 Part IV: Choice Under Uncertainty — Overview
crumbs: 23  Part IV: Choice Under Uncertainty — Overview

title: 23  Part IV: Choice Under Uncertainty — Overview
text:
24.1 Context
Uncertainty pervades economic life. Agents must make decisions — how much insurance to buy, how to allocate a portfolio, whether to accept a contract — before they know the realized state of the world. The theory of choice under uncertainty provides the axiomatic foundation for modeling such decisions.
The dominant framework is the von Neumann-Morgenstern (VNM) expected utility (EU) model, which rests on a specific set of axioms about preferences over probability distributions (lotteries). The central result — the VNM representation theorem — establishes that preferences satisfying rationality, continuity, and the independence axiom can be represented by the expected value of a Bernoulli utility function over outcomes. This representation is unique up to positive affine transformation, which gives it cardinal content.
Expected utility theory is foundational for a vast range of applied fields:

Insurance and contract theory: risk-averse agents are willing to pay a premium for coverage; the Arrow-Debreu framework for insurance markets rests on EU.
Financial economics: portfolio choice, asset pricing (CAPM, consumption CAPM), option pricing models all use EU as a starting point.
Game theory under incomplete information: Bayesian games, mechanism design, and the revelation principle require agents to have EU preferences over uncertain outcomes.
Poverty and inequality analysis: stochastic dominance criteria provide welfare rankings without specifying a particular social welfare function (directly linking to empirical welfare analysis).
Behavioral economics: the systematic violations of EU documented in experiments (Allais paradox, Ellsberg paradox) have motivated extensions (prospect theory, rank-dependent utility) that retain most of the EU framework’s tractability while accommodating richer behavioral patterns.

The four notes in this part build the theory from its axiomatic foundations through its key applied concepts, culminating in stochastic dominance, which bridges the normative theory and empirical welfare measurement.
crumbs: 23  Part IV: Choice Under Uncertainty — Overview

title: 23  Part IV: Choice Under Uncertainty — Overview
text:
24.3 Logical Dependencies
\[
\text{Rationality + Continuity + Independence} \xrightarrow{\text{VNM Theorem}} EU = \sum_n p_n u(c_n)
\] \[
\text{Concavity of } u \;\Leftrightarrow\; \text{Risk Aversion} \;\Rightarrow\; r_A(c) = -u''(c)/u'(c)
\] \[
F \succsim_1 G \;\Rightarrow\; F \succsim_2 G \;\Rightarrow\; \text{All risk-averse agents prefer } F
\]
crumbs: 23  Part IV: Choice Under Uncertainty — Overview

title: 23  Part IV: Choice Under Uncertainty — Overview
text:
24.4 Key Themes
The Independence Axiom is the most important and most controversial axiom. All other standard axioms (rationality, continuity) carry over from deterministic choice theory. The Independence Axiom is specific to choice under uncertainty: it says that mixing two lotteries with a common third lottery preserves the ranking. This axiom drives the linearity of the EU representation (the expected value form), and its violation — as demonstrated by the Allais paradox — shows that real agents sometimes deviate systematically from EU.
Cardinal utility plays a role here that it does not in deterministic choice theory. In deterministic consumer theory, only ordinal properties of \(u\) matter (the ranking of consumption bundles). In EU theory, the curvature of \(u\) has direct behavioral meaning: concavity of \(u\) means risk aversion; the degree of concavity (measured by Arrow-Pratt) quantifies how much the agent dislikes uncertainty. This is because the Independence Axiom forces the utility representation to be linear in probabilities.
Stochastic dominance provides welfare rankings that are robust to the particular choice of utility function, within a class. First-order stochastic dominance is robust to all monotone preferences; second-order stochastic dominance is robust to all monotone and concave preferences (risk-averse agents). This robustness is crucial for applied welfare analysis: instead of committing to a specific functional form for the social welfare function, the analyst can test for stochastic dominance and make welfare statements that hold for all functions in the relevant class.
