title: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
text: 25 4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
crumbs: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms

title: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
text:
25.1 Context
Before writing down a utility function for choice under uncertainty, one must be precise about the objects of choice. There are two major frameworks: the von Neumann-Morgenstern (VNM) framework of objective lotteries over outcomes, and the Savage framework of acts over states of the world. The VNM framework is more tractable and is the standard in microeconomic theory; the Savage framework is foundational for decision theory and handles subjective beliefs endogenously.
The key axiom distinguishing expected utility from other decision theories is the Independence Axiom. Unlike rationality and continuity, which are standard in deterministic choice theory, the Independence Axiom is specific to the probabilistic setting. It is the axiom that drives the linear-in-probabilities form of the EU representation. The Allais paradox demonstrates that the Independence Axiom is systematically violated in experiments, providing the primary empirical motivation for non-EU theories.
crumbs: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms

title: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
text:
25.2 Framework 1: Objective Uncertainty (VNM)






NoteDefinition 4.1 — Outcomes and Simple Lotteries



Let \(C = \{c_1, \ldots, c_N\}\) be a finite set of outcomes (prizes, consumption levels, wealth levels). A simple lottery over \(C\) is a probability distribution: \[L = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_N) \quad \text{with} \quad p_n \geq 0 \; \forall n \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{n=1}^N p_n = 1\] where \(p_n\) is the probability of outcome \(c_n\).








NoteDefinition 4.2 — The Space of Lotteries



The set of all simple lotteries over \(C\) is the (N-1)-dimensional simplex: \[\mathcal{L} = \left\{(p_1, \ldots, p_N) \in \mathbb{R}^N_+ : \sum_{n=1}^N p_n = 1\right\}\] Degenerate lotteries \(\delta_n\) (placing probability 1 on outcome \(c_n\)) are the vertices of the simplex.








NoteDefinition 4.3 — Compound Lotteries



A compound lottery is a lottery over lotteries: given lotteries \(L_1, \ldots, L_K \in \mathcal{L}\) and probabilities \(\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_K \geq 0\) with \(\sum_k \alpha_k = 1\), the compound lottery \((\alpha_1 \circ L_1, \ldots, \alpha_K \circ L_K)\) first selects \(L_k\) with probability \(\alpha_k\), then resolves \(L_k\).








NoteDefinition 4.4 — Reduction of Compound Lotteries



The reduced simple lottery corresponding to a compound lottery \((\alpha_1 \circ L_1, \ldots, \alpha_K \circ L_K)\) is obtained by computing the total probability of each outcome: \[\bar{p}_n = \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k p_n^k\] where \(p_n^k\) is the probability of outcome \(c_n\) under lottery \(L_k\).
The reduced lottery is the convex combination: \[\bar{L} = \sum_{k=1}^K \alpha_k L_k = \alpha_1 L_1 + \cdots + \alpha_K L_K \in \mathcal{L}\]








CautionRemark 4.1 — Reduction Principle



The assumption that agents care only about the reduced simple lottery (the ultimate probabilities over outcomes), and not about the multi-stage resolution process, is called the reduction principle. It is implicit in the VNM framework. Psychologically, the timing and framing of uncertainty can matter (this is studied in behavioral economics under “temporal resolution of uncertainty”), but for the standard VNM model, only the final distribution over outcomes matters.
crumbs: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms

title: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
text:
25.3 Framework 2: Subjective Uncertainty (Savage)






NoteDefinition 4.5 — Savage Framework



Let \(S\) be a set of states of the world (possible realizations of uncertainty), \(C\) be a set of outcomes (consequences), and \(\Sigma\) a \(\sigma\)-algebra of events. An act is a measurable function \(f: S \to C\) mapping each state to an outcome.
The agent has preferences \(\succsim\) over acts. The Savage framework does not assume objective probabilities; instead, the agent’s subjective probability measure \(\mu\) over \(S\) is derived from preferences over acts via Savage’s seven axioms.








CautionRemark 4.2 — VNM vs. Savage



In the VNM framework, probabilities are objective (given by the lottery). The agent knows the probability of each outcome. In the Savage framework, the agent has beliefs (subjective probabilities) that must be inferred from their choices. Anscombe and Aumann (1963) provided a cleaner axiomatization by mixing the two frameworks: acts map states to objective lotteries, and both subjective probabilities (over states) and VNM utility (over outcomes) are derived jointly.
For most of microeconomic theory (insurance, portfolio choice, mechanism design), the VNM framework with objective probabilities is sufficient. The Savage framework is essential for game theory under ambiguity (Knightian uncertainty) and for decision theory with heterogeneous beliefs.
crumbs: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms

title: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
text:
25.4 Preferences Over Lotteries
The agent’s preferences are a binary relation \(\succsim\) on \(\mathcal{L}\), where \(L \succsim L'\) means “lottery \(L\) is at least as good as lottery \(L'\).”






NoteDefinition 4.6 — Rational Preferences Over Lotteries



\(\succsim\) on \(\mathcal{L}\) is rational if it is: - Complete: for all \(L, L' \in \mathcal{L}\), either \(L \succsim L'\) or \(L' \succsim L\) (or both) - Transitive: \(L \succsim L'\) and \(L' \succsim L''\) imply \(L \succsim L''\)


These are exactly the same axioms as in deterministic choice theory (MWG Ch. 1). Their interpretation carries over: rationality means the agent can rank all lotteries and their rankings are consistent.
crumbs: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms

title: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
text:
25.5 The Continuity (Archimedean) Axiom






NoteDefinition 4.7 — Continuity Axiom



\(\succsim\) satisfies continuity if for all \(L, L', L'' \in \mathcal{L}\) with \(L \succ L' \succ L''\), there exist \(\alpha, \beta \in (0,1)\) such that: \[\alpha L + (1-\alpha) L'' \succ L' \quad \text{and} \quad L' \succ \beta L + (1-\beta) L''\]








CautionRemark 4.3 — Interpretation of Continuity



The continuity axiom says that the preference ranking is not lexicographic in outcomes: if \(L \succ L' \succ L''\), then a mixture of the best and worst lotteries (\(L\) and \(L''\)) can be made strictly better or worse than the intermediate lottery \(L'\) by choosing the appropriate mixing probability. This rules out the possibility that some outcomes are “infinitely better” or “infinitely worse” than others.
Example of a violation: Suppose \(c_1\) = death, \(c_2\) = poverty, \(c_3\) = wealth. An agent who has \(\delta_3 \succ \delta_2 \succ \delta_1\) but would never accept any risk of death regardless of the probability (even \(0.0001\%\) chance of death) to gain any amount of wealth would violate continuity, since \(\alpha \delta_3 + (1-\alpha)\delta_1 \prec \delta_2\) for all \(\alpha \in (0,1)\).
Mathematically, continuity ensures that the better-than and worse-than sets are open in the simplex topology, allowing a continuous utility representation to exist.
crumbs: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms

title: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
text:
25.6 The Independence Axiom






NoteDefinition 4.8 — Independence Axiom



\(\succsim\) satisfies the Independence Axiom if for all \(L, L', L'' \in \mathcal{L}\) and all \(\alpha \in (0,1)\): \[L \succsim L' \iff \alpha L + (1-\alpha) L'' \succsim \alpha L' + (1-\alpha) L''\]








CautionRemark 4.4 — Interpretation of the Independence Axiom



The Independence Axiom says that the preference between two lotteries \(L\) and \(L'\) is not affected by mixing them with a common third lottery \(L''\). The common component \((1-\alpha)L''\) is irrelevant and can be “canceled out.”
Intuition: Think of \(\alpha L + (1-\alpha) L''\) as a two-stage lottery: with probability \(\alpha\), face lottery \(L\); with probability \(1-\alpha\), face lottery \(L''\). The preference between the two compound lotteries depends only on what happens in the event (probability \(\alpha\)) where they differ — which is the preference between \(L\) and \(L'\) themselves. The common part \(L''\) occurs in neither case and is irrelevant.
Why it is controversial: The Independence Axiom is a separability condition. It says that your preference in one branch of the decision tree is independent of what happens in other branches. Real agents often violate this, particularly when the outcomes involve extreme events or when the framing of the lottery as a two-stage process affects behavior (as documented in the Allais paradox below).








CautionRemark 4.5 — Geometric Interpretation



In the simplex \(\mathcal{L}\), the Independence Axiom implies that indifference curves are straight parallel lines (hyperplanes). To see this: if \(L \sim L'\), then by Independence, \(\alpha L + (1-\alpha)L'' \sim \alpha L' + (1-\alpha)L''\) for any \(L''\) and \(\alpha\). This means the indifference set through \(L\) maps linearly to the indifference set through \(\alpha L + (1-\alpha)L''\), tracing out a straight line. Non-EU theories (e.g., rank-dependent utility) allow “fanning” of indifference curves — non-parallel lines in the simplex.
crumbs: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms

title: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
text:
25.7 The Allais Paradox
The Allais paradox (Allais, 1953) is the most famous systematic violation of the Independence Axiom.






NoteExample 4.1 — The Allais Paradox



Consider monetary outcomes \(c_1 = \$0\), \(c_2 = \$1{,}000{,}000\), \(c_3 = \$5{,}000{,}000\).
Pair 1: \[L_1 = (0, 1, 0) \quad \text{(win \$1M for certain)}\] \[L_2 = (0.01, 0.89, 0.10) \quad \text{(1\% chance of \$0; 89\% chance of \$1M; 10\% chance of \$5M)}\]
Pair 2: \[L_3 = (0.89, 0.11, 0) \quad \text{(89\% chance of \$0; 11\% chance of \$1M; 0\% chance of \$5M)}\] \[L_4 = (0.90, 0, 0.10) \quad \text{(90\% chance of \$0; 0\% chance of \$1M; 10\% chance of \$5M)}\]
Modal experimental preference: \(L_1 \succ L_2\) and \(L_4 \succ L_3\).
Why this violates Independence:
Note that: \[L_1 = 0.11 \cdot \delta_{c_2} + 0.89 \cdot \delta_{c_2}\] \[L_2 = 0.11 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{11}\delta_{c_1} + \frac{10}{11}\delta_{c_3}\right) + 0.89 \cdot \delta_{c_2}\]
So \(L_1\) and \(L_2\) differ only in the \(0.11\)-probability event: in that event, \(L_1\) gives \(\delta_{c_2}\) and \(L_2\) gives \(\frac{1}{11}\delta_{c_1} + \frac{10}{11}\delta_{c_3}\). Denoting \(M_1 = \delta_{c_2}\) and \(M_2 = \frac{1}{11}\delta_{c_1} + \frac{10}{11}\delta_{c_3}\): \[L_1 = 0.11 M_1 + 0.89 \delta_{c_2}, \quad L_2 = 0.11 M_2 + 0.89 \delta_{c_2}\]
By the Independence Axiom, \(L_1 \succ L_2\) iff \(M_1 \succ M_2\).
Similarly: \[L_3 = 0.11 M_1 + 0.89 \delta_{c_1}, \quad L_4 = 0.11 M_2 + 0.89 \delta_{c_1}\]
Again by Independence, \(L_3 \succsim L_4\) iff \(M_1 \succsim M_2\).
Contradiction: \(L_1 \succ L_2\) implies \(M_1 \succ M_2\), which implies \(L_3 \succ L_4\). But the experimental finding is \(L_4 \succ L_3\). This is a violation of the Independence Axiom.
EU representation check: Under EU, \(L_1 \succ L_2\) means: \[u(c_2) > 0.01 u(c_1) + 0.89 u(c_2) + 0.10 u(c_3)\] \[0.11 u(c_2) > 0.01 u(c_1) + 0.10 u(c_3) \quad \cdots (*)\]
And \(L_4 \succ L_3\) means: \[0.90 u(c_1) + 0.10 u(c_3) > 0.89 u(c_1) + 0.11 u(c_2)\] \[0.10 u(c_3) - 0.11 u(c_2) > 0.89 u(c_1) - 0.90 u(c_1) = -0.01 u(c_1)\] \[0.10 u(c_3) + 0.01 u(c_1) > 0.11 u(c_2) \quad \cdots (**)\]
But \((*)\) and \((**)\) directly contradict each other (\((*)\) says \(0.11 u(c_2) > 0.01 u(c_1) + 0.10 u(c_3)\) while \((**)\) says the opposite). Hence no EU representation can accommodate both preferences simultaneously.








CautionRemark 4.6 — What the Allais Paradox Tells Us



The Allais paradox reveals that agents often violate Independence when one option includes certainty (the \(L_1\) certainty effect). Agents appear to overweight the certain outcome relative to what EU would predict. This suggests:

Psychologically: People treat “certainty” as qualitatively different from “very high probability,” not just quantitatively. The move from 100% to 99% feels worse than the move from 11% to 10% (both are a 1 percentage point reduction).
Modeling response: Non-EU theories such as rank-dependent utility (Quiggin, 1982) and prospect theory (Kahneman and Tversky, 1979) accommodate such behavior by allowing the probability weighting function to be nonlinear, with overweighting of extreme probabilities (near 0 and 1).
Practical response: Despite the Allais violation, EU remains the dominant model in most applied economics because (i) violations are less prevalent in high-stakes repeated environments; (ii) EU is tractable and has well-understood implications; (iii) for aggregate outcomes and policy analysis, violations may average out.
crumbs: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms

title: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
text:
25.8 Summary of Axioms






ImportantProposition 4.1 — The Three Key Axioms



The three axioms for VNM expected utility are: 1. Rationality: \(\succsim\) is complete and transitive on \(\mathcal{L}\) 2. Continuity: For \(L \succ L' \succ L''\), \(\exists \alpha, \beta \in (0,1)\) such that \(\alpha L + (1-\alpha)L'' \succ L' \succ \beta L + (1-\beta)L''\) 3. Independence: \(L \succsim L' \iff \alpha L + (1-\alpha)L'' \succsim \alpha L' + (1-\alpha)L''\) for all \(\alpha \in (0,1)\), \(L'' \in \mathcal{L}\)
The VNM theorem states these three axioms are necessary and sufficient for an EU representation (see 4.2 Expected Utility Theory).








CautionRemark 4.7 — Which Axiom is “Doing the Work”?




Rationality alone (without continuity) gives an ordinal representation on the simplex (a utility function on lotteries exists).
Rationality + Continuity gives a continuous utility function \(U: \mathcal{L} \to \mathbb{R}\).
+ Independence forces this utility function to be linear in probabilities: \(U(L) = \sum_n p_n u(c_n)\).

The Independence Axiom is thus precisely what converts a general preference ordering on lotteries into an expected utility representation. This is the hardest axiom to justify and the most frequently violated.
crumbs: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms

title: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms
text:
25.9 Economic Intuition
The von Neumann-Morgenstern framework provides a clean, tractable model of decision-making under risk. The genius of the VNM approach is to work directly with preferences over lotteries (probability distributions), rather than trying to model the psychological experience of uncertainty. The axioms encode plausible consistency requirements: completeness (you can always make a choice), transitivity (your choices don’t cycle), continuity (no outcome is infinitely good or bad), and independence (the presence of a common background risk doesn’t affect relative preferences).
The Independence Axiom is particularly elegant: it says that in evaluating two lotteries, you should focus only on the events where they differ. The common component is irrelevant — you’ll experience it in either case. This “consequentialist” reasoning is compelling in many settings, but breaks down when the psychological experience of certainty, or the framing of the lottery, affects the evaluation.
Understanding both the power and the limits of the Independence Axiom is essential for graduate-level economic theory. It is the cornerstone of the most important representation theorem in decision theory, and its systematic violation has generated decades of research in behavioral economics and decision theory.
crumbs: 24  4.1 The State Space Framework and Axioms

title: 25  4.2 Expected Utility Theory
text: 26 4.2 Expected Utility Theory

title: 25  4.2 Expected Utility Theory
text:
26.1 Context
The von Neumann-Morgenstern (VNM) Expected Utility Theorem is the central representation theorem of decision theory under risk. It establishes that preferences over lotteries satisfying rationality, continuity, and the independence axiom can be represented as the expected value of a Bernoulli utility function over outcomes. The theorem has two directions: (1) necessity — if preferences have an EU representation, then the three axioms hold; and (2) sufficiency — the three axioms imply the EU form. The sufficiency direction is the hard and economically important result.
The EU representation has cardinal content: unlike the ordinal utility of consumer theory, the Bernoulli function \(u\) is unique only up to positive affine transformations. This means differences and curvature of \(u\) have behavioral meaning — they encode attitudes toward risk. This cardinality is a direct consequence of the Independence Axiom, which forces the utility to be linear in probabilities.

title: 25  4.2 Expected Utility Theory
text:
26.2 The VNM Representation Theorem






ImportantTheorem 4.1 — Von Neumann-Morgenstern Expected Utility Theorem



Let \(\mathcal{L}\) be the set of lotteries over a finite outcome set \(C = \{c_1, \ldots, c_N\}\). A preference relation \(\succsim\) on \(\mathcal{L}\) satisfies rationality, continuity, and independence if and only if there exists a function \(u: C \to \mathbb{R}\) (a Bernoulli utility function) such that for all \(L = (p_1, \ldots, p_N)\) and \(L' = (p'_1, \ldots, p'_N)\): \[L \succsim L' \iff \sum_{n=1}^N p_n u(c_n) \geq \sum_{n=1}^N p'_n u(c_n)\] The function \(U: \mathcal{L} \to \mathbb{R}\) defined by \(U(L) = \sum_n p_n u(c_n) = \mathbb{E}_L[u(c)]\) is the VNM utility function (or expected utility function).








TipProof





We prove both directions.

Necessity (\(\Leftarrow\)): EU representation implies the three axioms.
Suppose \(L \succsim L'\) iff \(U(L) \geq U(L')\) where \(U(L) = \sum_n p_n u(c_n)\).
Rationality: \(U\) is a real-valued function, so \(\geq\) is a complete, transitive ordering on \(\mathbb{R}\). Hence \(\succsim\) is complete and transitive. \(\checkmark\)
Continuity: Suppose \(L \succ L' \succ L''\), i.e., \(U(L) > U(L') > U(L'')\). The function \(\alpha \mapsto U(\alpha L + (1-\alpha)L'')\) is continuous and strictly increasing in \(\alpha\) on \([0,1]\), with: - At \(\alpha = 1\): \(U(L) > U(L')\) - At \(\alpha = 0\): \(U(L'') < U(L')\)
By the intermediate value theorem, \(\exists \alpha \in (0,1)\) such that \(U(\alpha L + (1-\alpha)L'') = U(L')\), and in fact for small perturbations there exist strict inequalities. So continuity holds. \(\checkmark\)
Independence: \(U(\alpha L + (1-\alpha)L'') = \alpha U(L) + (1-\alpha)U(L'')\) (linearity of expectation). Hence: \[U(L) \geq U(L') \iff \alpha U(L) + (1-\alpha)U(L'') \geq \alpha U(L') + (1-\alpha)U(L'')\] i.e., \(L \succsim L'\) iff \(\alpha L + (1-\alpha)L'' \succsim \alpha L' + (1-\alpha)L''\). \(\checkmark\)

Sufficiency (\(\Rightarrow\)): Three axioms imply EU representation.
Assume \(\succsim\) satisfies rationality, continuity, and independence on \(\mathcal{L}\).
Step 1: Ordering outcomes and establishing best/worst.
Since outcomes \(c_1, \ldots, c_N\) can be identified with degenerate lotteries \(\delta_n\), the rationality of \(\succsim\) gives a complete ordering over outcomes. Without loss of generality, re-label so that: \[\delta_1 \succsim \delta_2 \succsim \cdots \succsim \delta_N\] Let \(\bar{L} = \delta_1\) (best outcome) and \(\underline{L} = \delta_N\) (worst outcome). If \(\bar{L} \sim \underline{L}\), all outcomes are indifferent and \(U \equiv 0\) works trivially. Assume \(\bar{L} \succ \underline{L}\) (nontrivial case).
Step 2: Defining the utility function via certainty equivalents.
For each outcome \(c_n\), by continuity: since \(\bar{L} \succsim \delta_n \succsim \underline{L}\), there exists a unique \(u_n \in [0,1]\) such that: \[\delta_n \sim u_n \bar{L} + (1-u_n)\underline{L}\] Define \(u(c_n) = u_n\). Note: \(u(c_1) = 1\) (best outcome) and \(u(c_N) = 0\) (worst outcome).
(Existence of \(u_n\): by continuity, \(U_c(\alpha) := \succsim\)-rank of \(\alpha \bar{L} + (1-\alpha)\underline{L}\) is continuous in \(\alpha\), equals rank of \(\bar{L}\) at \(\alpha=1\) and rank of \(\underline{L}\) at \(\alpha=0\). IVT gives existence. Uniqueness follows from the fact that \(\alpha \bar{L} + (1-\alpha)\underline{L}\) is strictly improving in \(\alpha\) by Independence applied with \(\bar{L} \succ \underline{L}\).)
Step 3: Showing that \(U(L) = \sum_n p_n u(c_n)\) represents \(\succsim\).
For any lottery \(L = (p_1, \ldots, p_N)\), write: \[L = \sum_{n=1}^N p_n \delta_n\]
Using the indifference \(\delta_n \sim u_n \bar{L} + (1-u_n)\underline{L}\) from Step 2 and applying Independence repeatedly:
First, replace \(\delta_n\) by its indifference equivalent. By Independence (with any \(\alpha\) and mixing in the \(n\)-th component): \[\sum_{n=1}^N p_n \delta_n \sim \sum_{n=1}^N p_n [u_n \bar{L} + (1-u_n)\underline{L}]\]
Since \(\bar{L}\) and \(\underline{L}\) are fixed lotteries, the right-hand side reduces to: \[\left(\sum_{n=1}^N p_n u_n\right) \bar{L} + \left(\sum_{n=1}^N p_n (1-u_n)\right) \underline{L} = U \cdot \bar{L} + (1-U) \cdot \underline{L}\] where \(U = \sum_n p_n u_n = \sum_n p_n u(c_n)\).
So: \(L \sim U(L) \bar{L} + (1-U(L))\underline{L}\).
Similarly for \(L' = (p'_1, \ldots, p'_N)\): \(L' \sim U(L') \bar{L} + (1-U(L'))\underline{L}\).
Now: \(L \succsim L'\) iff \(U(L)\bar{L} + (1-U(L))\underline{L} \succsim U(L')\bar{L} + (1-U(L'))\underline{L}\).
Since \(\bar{L} \succ \underline{L}\), the mixture \(\alpha \bar{L} + (1-\alpha)\underline{L}\) is strictly increasing in \(\alpha\) (by Independence: higher \(\alpha\) puts more weight on the strictly better lottery). Hence: \[L \succsim L' \iff U(L) \geq U(L') \iff \sum_n p_n u(c_n) \geq \sum_n p'_n u(c_n) \qquad \blacksquare\]
Formal justification for Step 3 using Independence:
The key step — that \(\sum_n p_n \delta_n \sim \sum_n p_n [u_n \bar{L} + (1-u_n)\underline{L}]\) — follows by repeated application of Independence. Here is the inductive argument for \(N = 3\):
Let \(\delta_1 \sim u_1 \bar{L} + (1-u_1)\underline{L}\) and \(\delta_2 \sim u_2 \bar{L} + (1-u_2)\underline{L}\).
Consider \(L = p_1 \delta_1 + p_2 \delta_2 + p_3 \delta_3\).
By Independence (mixing \(\delta_1\) with \(\delta_1 \sim u_1 \bar{L} + (1-u_1)\underline{L}\), in the role of \(L \sim L'\), with \(L'' = p_2 \delta_2 + p_3\delta_3\), \(\alpha = p_1\)): \[p_1 \delta_1 + (1-p_1)(p_2\delta_2 + p_3\delta_3) \sim p_1[u_1\bar{L}+(1-u_1)\underline{L}] + (1-p_1)(p_2\delta_2+p_3\delta_3)\] The left side is \(L\) (after renormalizing \(p_2/(1-p_1)\) and \(p_3/(1-p_1)\)). Continuing recursively yields the formula. \(\blacksquare\)

title: 25  4.2 Expected Utility Theory
section: 26.3 Uniqueness of the Bernoulli Utility Function
text:
26.3 Uniqueness of the Bernoulli Utility Function






ImportantTheorem 4.2 — Uniqueness Up to Positive Affine Transformation



Suppose \(\succsim\) on \(\mathcal{L}\) has an EU representation with Bernoulli utility \(u: C \to \mathbb{R}\). Then \(v: C \to \mathbb{R}\) also represents \(\succsim\) as an EU utility if and only if: \[v(c_n) = a \cdot u(c_n) + b \quad \forall n, \quad \text{for some } a > 0, b \in \mathbb{R}\] That is, \(u\) is unique up to a positive affine transformation.








TipProof





(\(\Leftarrow\)) If \(v = au + b\) with \(a > 0\): \[\sum_n p_n v(c_n) = a \sum_n p_n u(c_n) + b\] Since \(\sum_n p_n = 1\), this equals \(a U(L) + b\), which is an increasing linear function of \(U(L)\). Hence \(v\) represents the same ordering: \(\sum_n p_n v_n \geq \sum_n p'_n v_n\) iff \(\sum_n p_n u_n \geq \sum_n p'_n u_n\). \(\checkmark\)
(\(\Rightarrow\)) Suppose both \(u\) and \(v\) represent \(\succsim\) via expected utility. From the construction in Theorem 4.1, the Bernoulli utilities are determined by the indifferences \(\delta_n \sim u_n \bar{L} + (1-u_n)\underline{L}\). The value \(u_n\) is uniquely determined (as proved in Step 2 of Theorem 4.1). If we normalize differently (setting \(u(\bar{c}) = a + b\) and \(u(\underline{c}) = b\) instead of 1 and 0), we get \(u_n' = a \cdot u_n + b\) for the rescaled version. Conversely, any two representations must have the same indifference-probability correspondences, forcing them to be related by an affine transformation. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 4.1 — Cardinality of EU



The uniqueness theorem implies that the EU utility function has cardinal content: the differences \(u(c_n) - u(c_m)\) and the ratios of differences matter (they are preserved under positive affine transformations). This is in contrast to ordinal utility in consumer theory, where only the ranking matters. The cardinality here is behavioral: it reflects attitudes toward risk.
Specifically, if \(u(c_3) - u(c_2) > u(c_2) - u(c_1)\), this says that the agent values the improvement from \(c_2\) to \(c_3\) more than from \(c_1\) to \(c_2\) — and this is a refutable behavioral claim (it implies specific choices between lotteries).

title: 25  4.2 Expected Utility Theory
text:
26.4 The St. Petersburg Paradox






NoteExample 4.2 — The St. Petersburg Paradox (Bernoulli, 1738)



The game: A fair coin is flipped repeatedly until the first head appears. If the first head appears on toss \(k\), the payoff is \(2^k\) dollars.
Expected monetary value: \[\mathbb{E}[\text{payoff}] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^k} \cdot 2^k = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} 1 = +\infty\] The expected monetary value is infinite. Yet no rational person would pay an arbitrarily large sum to play this game.
Bernoulli’s resolution (1738): Agents maximize expected utility, not expected monetary value. With \(u(c) = \ln c\): \[EU = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^k} \ln(2^k) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{k \ln 2}{2^k} = \ln 2 \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{k}{2^k} = \ln 2 \cdot 2 = 2\ln 2 < \infty\] where \(\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k/2^k = 2\) (sum of geometric-type series). So with log utility, the game has finite certainty equivalent \(CE = e^{2\ln 2} = 4\) dollars.
Menger’s objection (1934): Bernoulli’s resolution using log utility is insufficient. Consider a modified game where the payoff is \(e^{2^k}\) instead of \(2^k\). Then with \(u(c) = \ln c\): \[EU = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^k} \ln(e^{2^k}) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \frac{2^k}{2^k} = +\infty\] The paradox reappears for super-exponentially growing payoffs. The general resolution requires \(u\) to be bounded above: if \(\sup_c u(c) < \infty\), then EU is always finite for any lottery over outcomes in \(\mathbb{R}_+\).
Implication: Boundedness of \(u\) is a sufficient condition for EU to be finite for all lotteries. However, bounded utility has implications for behavior at high wealth levels (e.g., satiation) that may be empirically implausible. The standard applied compromise is to use unbounded but concave utility functions (like \(u(c) = c^{1-\rho}/(1-\rho)\)) and restrict attention to distributions with finite moments.

title: 25  4.2 Expected Utility Theory
text:
26.5 Extensions to Continuous Outcomes






NoteDefinition 4.2 — Expected Utility with a Continuous Distribution



Let the outcome space be \(C = \mathbb{R}\) (or an interval) and let \(F: \mathbb{R} \to [0,1]\) be a cumulative distribution function representing the lottery. The expected utility is: \[U(F) = \int u(c) \, dF(c) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u(c) f(c) \, dc\] if \(F\) has density \(f\), or more generally \(\int u(c) \, dF(c)\) as a Lebesgue-Stieltjes integral.








CautionRemark 4.2 — Integrability



For \(U(F)\) to be well-defined and finite, integrability conditions are needed. If \(u\) is bounded, \(\int |u(c)| dF(c) \leq \sup|u| < \infty\) always. For unbounded \(u\) (e.g., \(u(c) = \ln c\) or \(u(c) = c^{1-\rho}/(1-\rho)\)), one requires that \(F\) has finite enough moments: \[\int |u(c)| \, dF(c) < \infty\] The dominated convergence theorem ensures that if \(\mathbb{E}_F[u(c)]\) is finite, then EU is well-defined and continuous in \(F\) (in the topology of weak convergence under mild regularity).








ImportantProposition 4.2 — VNM Independence in the Continuous Case



The Independence Axiom in the continuous outcome case states: for distributions \(F, G, H\) and \(\alpha \in (0,1)\): \[F \succsim G \iff \alpha F + (1-\alpha)H \succsim \alpha G + (1-\alpha)H\] where \(\alpha F + (1-\alpha)H\) denotes the mixture distribution with CDF \(\alpha F(c) + (1-\alpha)H(c)\). The VNM theorem carries over: \(\succsim\) satisfies rationality, continuity, and independence iff \(\exists u: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) such that \(F \succsim G\) iff \(\int u \, dF \geq \int u \, dG\).

title: 25  4.2 Expected Utility Theory
text:
26.6 Non-EU Theories: A Brief Overview
The Allais paradox (see 4.1 State Space Framework) and subsequent experimental evidence have motivated several extensions of EU:
Rank-Dependent Utility (Quiggin, 1982): \[RDU(F) = \int u(c) \, d[g(F(c))]\] where \(g: [0,1] \to [0,1]\) is a probability weighting function (e.g., overweighting small probabilities near 0 and 1). This accommodates the Allais paradox and preserves first-order stochastic dominance.
Prospect Theory (Kahneman-Tversky, 1979; Tversky-Kahneman, 1992): \[V(L) = \sum_n \pi(p_n) v(c_n - r)\] where \(v\) is an S-shaped value function (concave for gains above reference point \(r\), convex for losses), \(\pi\) is a nonlinear probability weighting function, and \(r\) is a reference point (typically the status quo wealth). Key features: loss aversion (\(v'\) is steeper for losses than gains), probability distortion.
Why EU Remains the Dominant Model: Despite its empirical shortcomings, EU is used in almost all applied microeconomic models because: 1. It is tractable: closed-form solutions exist for CARA and CRRA utility with standard distributions. 2. Its comparative statics are clean: Arrow-Pratt measures fully characterize comparative risk aversion. 3. Stochastic dominance results (see 4.4 Stochastic Dominance) carry over with minimal modification. 4. In aggregate models (e.g., asset pricing, insurance markets), violations of EU at the individual level may not manifest at the market level. 5. For mechanism design and game theory, EU provides the linear structure needed for the revelation principle and incentive compatibility analysis.

title: 25  4.2 Expected Utility Theory
text:
26.7 Economic Intuition
The VNM theorem is a remarkable result: from three relatively innocuous axioms (rationality, continuity, independence), one can derive that the agent behaves as if maximizing a weighted sum of utilities across outcomes, where the weights are the probabilities. This is not just a representation trick — it has strong behavioral implications.
The key insight is that the Independence Axiom forces the utility function to be linear in probabilities. This linearity is what makes EU tractable: mixed strategies in game theory, incentive compatible mechanisms in contract theory, and portfolio choice problems in finance all become linear (or at least convex) optimization problems under EU. Remove Independence, and the mathematics becomes substantially harder.
The cardinality of the Bernoulli utility \(u\) — unique only up to positive affine transformations — means that \(u\) encodes preferences over distributions of outcomes, not just outcomes themselves. The curvature of \(u\) is the key behavioral object: it determines how much the agent dislikes variance, what premium they pay for insurance, and how they respond to mean-preserving spreads. These topics are developed in detail in 4.3 Risk Aversion.

title: 25  4.2 Expected Utility Theory
text:
26.8 Cross-References

4.1 State Space Framework — the axioms; the Allais paradox
4.3 Risk Aversion — concavity of \(u\); Arrow-Pratt measures
4.4 Stochastic Dominance — welfare rankings using EU for broad classes of \(u\)
1.1 Preference and Utility — deterministic utility representation; ordinal vs. cardinal utility
PS Uncertainty — Problems 1 and 2

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
text: 27 4.3 Risk Aversion

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
text:
27.1 Context
Risk aversion is the most important behavioral property in the theory of decision under uncertainty. A risk-averse agent prefers a sure outcome to any lottery with the same expected value. Under the VNM framework, risk aversion is equivalent to concavity of the Bernoulli utility function \(u\). This elegant equivalence follows from Jensen’s inequality.
The Arrow-Pratt measures of absolute and relative risk aversion quantify how risk-averse an agent is, and provide a cardinal measure that can be compared across agents and wealth levels. These measures are used throughout applied economics: in insurance theory (how much premium will an agent pay?), in portfolio choice (how much risky asset will an agent hold?), in macroeconomics (calibrating the equity premium puzzle), and in mechanism design (characterizing the cost of risk-bearing).

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
text:
27.2 Risk Aversion: Definition and Characterization






NoteDefinition 4.1 — Risk Aversion, Risk Neutrality, Risk Seeking



An agent with Bernoulli utility \(u\) is: - Risk-averse if \(u(\mathbb{E}[c]) \geq \mathbb{E}[u(c)]\) for all lotteries \(L\) over \(c\) - Risk-neutral if \(u(\mathbb{E}[c]) = \mathbb{E}[u(c)]\) for all \(L\) - Risk-seeking (risk-loving) if \(u(\mathbb{E}[c]) \leq \mathbb{E}[u(c)]\) for all \(L\)
where \(\mathbb{E}[c] = \sum_n p_n c_n\) is the expected value of the lottery and \(\mathbb{E}[u(c)] = \sum_n p_n u(c_n)\) is the expected utility.








ImportantTheorem 4.1 — Risk Aversion Equivalent to Concavity



An agent with Bernoulli utility \(u: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) (differentiable) is risk-averse if and only if \(u\) is concave. Risk-neutral iff \(u\) is linear. Risk-seeking iff \(u\) is convex.








TipProof





(\(\Leftarrow\)) Concavity implies risk aversion:
Suppose \(u\) is concave. For any lottery \(L\) with outcomes \(c_1, \ldots, c_N\) and probabilities \(p_1, \ldots, p_N\), by Jensen’s inequality for concave functions: \[u\left(\sum_{n=1}^N p_n c_n\right) \geq \sum_{n=1}^N p_n u(c_n)\] i.e., \(u(\mathbb{E}[c]) \geq \mathbb{E}[u(c)]\). This holds for all lotteries, so the agent is risk-averse. \(\checkmark\)
(\(\Rightarrow\)) Risk aversion implies concavity:
Suppose the agent is risk-averse: \(u(\mathbb{E}[c]) \geq \mathbb{E}[u(c)]\) for all lotteries \(L\).
To show \(u\) is concave, we must show: for any \(c, c' \in \mathbb{R}\) and \(\lambda \in [0,1]\): \[u(\lambda c + (1-\lambda)c') \geq \lambda u(c) + (1-\lambda)u(c')\]
Consider the two-outcome lottery \(L\) with outcome \(c\) with probability \(\lambda\) and outcome \(c'\) with probability \(1-\lambda\). Then: - \(\mathbb{E}[c] = \lambda c + (1-\lambda)c'\) - \(\mathbb{E}[u(c)] = \lambda u(c) + (1-\lambda)u(c')\)
Risk aversion requires: \[u(\mathbb{E}[c]) \geq \mathbb{E}[u(c)] \iff u(\lambda c + (1-\lambda)c') \geq \lambda u(c) + (1-\lambda)u(c')\] This is precisely the concavity condition. \(\blacksquare\)

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
text:
27.3 Certainty Equivalent and Risk Premium






NoteDefinition 4.2 — Certainty Equivalent



The certainty equivalent \(CE(L)\) of a lottery \(L\) for an agent with utility \(u\) is the certain outcome that the agent finds equally desirable as \(L\): \[u(CE(L)) = \mathbb{E}[u(c)] = \sum_n p_n u(c_n)\] Since \(u\) is strictly increasing, \(CE(L) = u^{-1}(\mathbb{E}[u(c)])\) is well-defined.








NoteDefinition 4.3 — Risk Premium



The risk premium \(\pi(L)\) (also denoted \(\rho\) or \(RP\)) is the maximum amount the agent is willing to pay to eliminate the risk of \(L\): \[\pi(L) = \mathbb{E}[c] - CE(L)\] For a risk-averse agent: \(CE(L) \leq \mathbb{E}[c]\), so \(\pi(L) \geq 0\) (the agent would pay to avoid the lottery). For a risk-neutral agent: \(\pi(L) = 0\). For risk-seeking: \(\pi(L) \leq 0\).








CautionRemark 4.1 — Insurance Interpretation



The risk premium is the maximum insurance premium the agent would pay to replace the random outcome \(c\) with its expected value \(\mathbb{E}[c]\). In an insurance market, an insurer offering full insurance at actuarially fair premium \(\pi^* = \pi(L)\) would just make the agent indifferent between insuring and not insuring; any premium below \(\pi^*\) would be strictly accepted.

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
section: 27.4 Arrow-Pratt Measure of Absolute Risk Aversion
text:
27.4 Arrow-Pratt Measure of Absolute Risk Aversion






NoteDefinition 4.4 — Absolute Risk Aversion (Arrow-Pratt)



The Arrow-Pratt measure of absolute risk aversion at wealth level \(c\) is: \[r_A(c) = -\frac{u''(c)}{u'(c)}\] This is well-defined when \(u\) is twice differentiable with \(u'(c) > 0\).








ImportantTheorem 4.2 — Risk Premium Approximation (Arrow-Pratt)



For a small actuarially fair lottery \(\tilde{\epsilon}\) with \(\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\epsilon}] = 0\) and \(\text{Var}[\tilde{\epsilon}] = \sigma^2\), added to a sure income \(c\), the risk premium satisfies: \[\pi \approx \frac{1}{2} r_A(c) \sigma^2\]








TipProof





Let \(c + \tilde{\epsilon}\) be the risky outcome, where \(\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\epsilon}] = 0\) and \(\text{Var}[\tilde{\epsilon}] = \sigma^2\).
The certainty equivalent \(CE = c - \pi\) satisfies \(u(c - \pi) = \mathbb{E}[u(c + \tilde{\epsilon})]\).
Left-hand side: Taylor expand \(u(c - \pi)\) around \(c\) for small \(\pi\): \[u(c - \pi) \approx u(c) - \pi u'(c)\]
Right-hand side: Taylor expand \(u(c + \tilde{\epsilon})\) around \(c\) for small \(\tilde{\epsilon}\): \[u(c + \tilde{\epsilon}) \approx u(c) + \tilde{\epsilon} u'(c) + \frac{1}{2}\tilde{\epsilon}^2 u''(c)\]
Taking expectations (using \(\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\epsilon}] = 0\) and \(\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\epsilon}^2] = \sigma^2\)): \[\mathbb{E}[u(c + \tilde{\epsilon})] \approx u(c) + 0 + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2 u''(c)\]
Setting LHS \(=\) RHS: \[u(c) - \pi u'(c) \approx u(c) + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2 u''(c)\] \[-\pi u'(c) \approx \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2 u''(c)\] \[\pi \approx -\frac{u''(c)}{u'(c)} \cdot \frac{\sigma^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} r_A(c) \sigma^2 \qquad \blacksquare\]









CautionRemark 4.2 — Interpretation of the Arrow-Pratt Formula



The risk premium \(\pi \approx \frac{1}{2} r_A(c) \sigma^2\) has a clean interpretation: - The risk premium is proportional to the variance \(\sigma^2\): more uncertain lotteries require larger compensation. - The risk premium is proportional to \(r_A(c)\): more risk-averse agents require larger compensation. - The factor \(\frac{1}{2}\) comes from the second-order Taylor expansion.
This formula is used throughout applied work as a first-order approximation. For example, in portfolio choice, the optimal portfolio weight on a risky asset is \(\approx \mu/(r_A \sigma^2)\) where \(\mu\) is the excess return — the higher the risk aversion, the lower the demand for risky assets.

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
section: 27.5 Arrow-Pratt Measure of Relative Risk Aversion
text:
27.5 Arrow-Pratt Measure of Relative Risk Aversion






NoteDefinition 4.5 — Relative Risk Aversion



The Arrow-Pratt measure of relative risk aversion at wealth \(c\) is: \[r_R(c) = -\frac{c \cdot u''(c)}{u'(c)} = c \cdot r_A(c)\] It measures risk aversion relative to the level of wealth.








CautionRemark 4.3 — Absolute vs. Relative Risk Aversion




Absolute risk aversion \(r_A(c)\): relevant when the stakes are in absolute dollar amounts (e.g., “would you accept a lottery that gains or loses $1000 with equal probability?”). A person with constant \(r_A\) has the same attitude to a $1000 gamble regardless of their wealth.
Relative risk aversion \(r_R(c)\): relevant when the stakes are proportional to wealth (e.g., “would you accept a lottery that gains or loses 10% of your wealth?”). A person with constant \(r_R\) has the same attitude to a 10% gamble regardless of their wealth.

For the risk premium of a proportional gamble \(c \cdot \tilde{\epsilon}\) (where \(\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\epsilon}] = 0\), \(\text{Var}[\tilde{\epsilon}] = \sigma^2\)): \(\pi \approx \frac{1}{2} r_R(c) \sigma^2 c\) (in dollar terms).

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
text:
27.6 CARA Utility






NoteDefinition 4.6 — CARA (Constant Absolute Risk Aversion) Utility



The Bernoulli utility function \(u: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) has constant absolute risk aversion (CARA) with parameter \(\gamma > 0\) if \(r_A(c) = \gamma\) for all \(c\). The unique (up to affine transformation) CARA utility function is: \[u(c) = -\frac{1}{\gamma} e^{-\gamma c}\] (or equivalently \(u(c) = -e^{-\gamma c}\), which differs only by a positive scaling).








TipDerivation of CARA Family





\(r_A(c) = \gamma\) means \(-u''(c)/u'(c) = \gamma\), i.e., \(u''(c) = -\gamma u'(c)\). This is a linear ODE in \(u'\): \[\frac{d(u')}{u'} = -\gamma \, dc \implies \ln u'(c) = -\gamma c + K \implies u'(c) = e^K e^{-\gamma c}\] Integrating: \(u(c) = -\frac{e^K}{\gamma} e^{-\gamma c} + C\). Up to the positive affine transformation (set \(e^K = 1\), \(C = 0\)): \(u(c) = -\frac{1}{\gamma} e^{-\gamma c}\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 4.4 — Properties of CARA




Wealth-independent risk attitudes: \(r_A(c) = \gamma\) for all \(c\). The agent takes the same absolute gamble at any wealth level (no “wealth effect” on risk attitudes).
Portfolio choice under CARA: The demand for a risky asset in a portfolio is independent of wealth (dollar amount invested in risky asset does not change with \(w\)). This is a strong and often counterfactual prediction.
CARA + Normal distribution: If \(c \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\), then \(\mathbb{E}[u(c)] = -\frac{1}{\gamma}\exp\left(-\gamma\mu + \frac{\gamma^2\sigma^2}{2}\right)\), so the agent’s preference over Normal lotteries is captured by the mean-variance criterion: \(\mu - \frac{\gamma\sigma^2}{2}\).
Certainty equivalent for Normal: \(CE = \mu - \frac{\gamma \sigma^2}{2}\), so risk premium \(= \frac{\gamma \sigma^2}{2}\), consistent with the Arrow-Pratt approximation.

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
text:
27.7 CRRA Utility






NoteDefinition 4.7 — CRRA (Constant Relative Risk Aversion) Utility



The utility function \(u\) has constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) with parameter \(\rho \geq 0\) if \(r_R(c) = \rho\) for all \(c > 0\). The CRRA family is: \[u(c) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{c^{1-\rho}}{1-\rho} & \text{if } \rho \neq 1 \\[6pt] \ln c & \text{if } \rho = 1 \end{cases}\] for \(c > 0\).








TipDerivation of CRRA Family





\(r_R(c) = \rho\) means \(-c u''(c)/u'(c) = \rho\), i.e., \(u''(c) = -\frac{\rho}{c} u'(c)\). Let \(v = u'\): \[\frac{v'}{v} = -\frac{\rho}{c} \implies \ln v = -\rho \ln c + K \implies v(c) = u'(c) = A c^{-\rho}\] Integrating (for \(\rho \neq 1\)): \(u(c) = \frac{A c^{1-\rho}}{1-\rho} + B\). Up to positive affine transformation (\(A = 1\), \(B = 0\)): \(u(c) = c^{1-\rho}/(1-\rho)\).
For \(\rho = 1\): \(u'(c) = c^{-1}\), so \(u(c) = \ln c + B\). Up to normalization: \(u(c) = \ln c\).
Verify: \(u''(c) = -\rho c^{-\rho-1}\), \(u'(c) = c^{-\rho}\), so \(r_R = -c \cdot (-\rho c^{-\rho-1})/c^{-\rho} = \rho\). \(\checkmark\) \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 4.5 — Properties of CRRA




Scale-invariant risk attitudes: \(r_R(c) = \rho\) for all \(c\). The agent takes the same proportional gamble regardless of wealth.
Portfolio share under CRRA: The fraction of wealth invested in the risky asset is independent of wealth. This is consistent with empirical evidence on portfolio composition (rich and poor households hold similar portfolio shares in risky assets, but different dollar amounts).
Special cases: \(\rho = 0\) gives \(u(c) = c\) (risk neutrality); \(\rho = 1\) gives \(u(c) = \ln c\) (log utility, Bernoulli’s original proposal); \(\rho \to \infty\) gives the maximin criterion (Rawlsian preferences: maximize the worst outcome).
Macroeconomic calibration: Empirical estimates of \(\rho\) from consumption data range from 1 to 5; for the equity premium puzzle to be resolved, \(\rho \approx 30\) is needed — implausibly high, suggesting that EU with CRRA is insufficient to explain asset prices (Mehra-Prescott, 1985).

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
text:
27.8 Comparing Risk Aversion Across Agents






NoteDefinition 4.8 — More Risk-Averse



Agent \(A\) (with utility \(u_A\)) is more risk-averse than agent \(B\) (with utility \(u_B\)) if for every lottery \(L\) and every certain outcome \(c\): \[u_B(c) \geq EU_B(L) \implies u_A(c) \geq EU_A(L)\] (Whenever \(B\) is willing to accept a certain outcome over a lottery, \(A\) is also willing.) Equivalently: \(CE_A(L) \leq CE_B(L)\) for all \(L\) (agent \(A\) has a lower certainty equivalent for every lottery).








ImportantTheorem 4.3 — Equivalent Characterizations of “More Risk-Averse”



The following are equivalent: 1. \(A\) is more risk-averse than \(B\) (as in Definition 4.8) 2. \(u_A\) is a concave transformation of \(u_B\): there exists a concave, strictly increasing function \(\phi\) such that \(u_A = \phi \circ u_B\) 3. \(r_A^A(c) \geq r_A^B(c)\) for all \(c\) (Arrow-Pratt absolute risk aversion of \(A\) is everywhere at least as large as \(B\)’s)








TipProof





(1) \(\iff\) (2):
(\(\Leftarrow\)) Suppose \(u_A = \phi(u_B)\) with \(\phi\) concave and increasing. Suppose \(u_B(c^*) \geq EU_B(L) = \mathbb{E}[u_B(\tilde{c})]\). We want to show \(u_A(c^*) \geq EU_A(L) = \mathbb{E}[u_A(\tilde{c})] = \mathbb{E}[\phi(u_B(\tilde{c}))]\).
By Jensen’s inequality applied to the concave function \(\phi\): \[\mathbb{E}[\phi(u_B(\tilde{c}))] \leq \phi(\mathbb{E}[u_B(\tilde{c})]) \leq \phi(u_B(c^*)) = u_A(c^*)\] where the last step uses \(\phi\) increasing and \(u_B(c^*) \geq \mathbb{E}[u_B(\tilde{c})]\). Hence \(u_A(c^*) \geq EU_A(L)\). \(\checkmark\)
(\(\Rightarrow\)) If \(A\) is more risk-averse than \(B\), define \(\phi = u_A \circ u_B^{-1}\) (the composition, where \(u_B^{-1}\) is the inverse of \(u_B\)). Then \(u_A = \phi(u_B)\). The function \(\phi\) must be concave: suppose for contradiction \(\phi\) is convex at some point. Then by Jensen’s inequality, \(\mathbb{E}[\phi(u_B)] \geq \phi(\mathbb{E}[u_B])\), meaning \(A\) would accept a lottery that \(B\) rejects, contradicting \(A\) being more risk-averse.
(2) \(\iff\) (3):
If \(u_A = \phi(u_B)\), then: \[u_A' = \phi'(u_B) \cdot u_B', \quad u_A'' = \phi''(u_B)(u_B')^2 + \phi'(u_B) u_B''\]
\[r_A^A = -\frac{u_A''}{u_A'} = -\frac{\phi''(u_B)(u_B')^2 + \phi'(u_B)u_B''}{\phi'(u_B)u_B'} = -\frac{\phi''(u_B) u_B'}{\phi'(u_B)} - \frac{u_B''}{u_B'} = -\frac{\phi''(u_B)u_B'}{\phi'(u_B)} + r_A^B\]
Since \(\phi\) is concave (\(\phi'' \leq 0\)) and \(\phi' > 0\) and \(u_B' > 0\): \[-\frac{\phi''(u_B)u_B'}{\phi'(u_B)} \geq 0\] Hence \(r_A^A \geq r_A^B\) everywhere. Conversely, \(r_A^A \geq r_A^B\) for all \(c\) implies \(-\phi''u_B'/\phi' \geq 0\), so \(\phi'' \leq 0\) (since \(u_B' > 0, \phi' > 0\)), so \(\phi\) is concave. \(\blacksquare\)

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
section: 27.9 Decreasing and Increasing Absolute Risk Aversion
text:
27.9 Decreasing and Increasing Absolute Risk Aversion






NoteDefinition 4.9 — DARA and IARA




Decreasing Absolute Risk Aversion (DARA): \(r_A'(c) < 0\) — wealthier agents take larger absolute risks.
Increasing Absolute Risk Aversion (IARA): \(r_A'(c) > 0\) — wealthier agents take smaller absolute risks.
Constant Absolute Risk Aversion (CARA): \(r_A'(c) = 0\).









ImportantProposition 4.1 — DARA and Risky Asset Demand



Under DARA, the demand for a risky asset (in dollar terms) is increasing in wealth. Under IARA, it is decreasing. Under CARA, it is constant.








TipProof





Consider an agent with wealth \(w\) choosing to invest dollar amount \(a\) in a risky asset with return \(\tilde{R}\) and the remainder \((w - a)\) in a safe asset with return \(r\). The portfolio problem: \[\max_a \mathbb{E}\left[u\left(w(1+r) + a(\tilde{R} - r)\right)\right]\]
FOC: \(\mathbb{E}\left[u'(c^*)(\tilde{R}-r)\right] = 0\) where \(c^* = w(1+r) + a^*(\tilde{R}-r)\).
Differentiate the FOC with respect to \(w\) (applying the implicit function theorem): \[\frac{da^*}{dw} = -\frac{\mathbb{E}[u''(c^*)(\tilde{R}-r)]}{\mathbb{E}[u''(c^*)(\tilde{R}-r)^2]} (1+r)\]
The sign of \(da^*/dw\) depends on \(\text{sign}(\mathbb{E}[u''(c^*)(\tilde{R}-r)])\).
Note that \(c^* = w(1+r) + a^*(\tilde{R}-r)\) increases in \(\tilde{R}\) when \(a^* > 0\) (risky investment). States with \(\tilde{R} > r\) have high \(c^*\) and \(u'(c^*)\) low; states with \(\tilde{R} < r\) have low \(c^*\) and \(u'(c^*)\) high. Define \(f(\tilde{R}) = u'(c^*)(\tilde{R}-r)\).
Under DARA: \(r_A(c)\) is decreasing, so \(-u''(c)/u'(c)\) decreases in \(c\), meaning \(|u''(c)|/u'(c)\) is smaller at higher wealth (higher \(\tilde{R}\) states). This means \(-u''(c^*)\) decreases faster than \(u'(c^*)\) as \(\tilde{R}\) rises. A careful calculation shows \(\mathbb{E}[u''(c^*)(\tilde{R}-r)] < 0\) under DARA, so \(da^*/dw > 0\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 4.6 — Empirical Evidence on DARA



The empirical evidence strongly supports DARA: wealthier households hold larger dollar amounts of risky assets (stocks) in their portfolios. The CARA model (with constant \(a^*\)) is counterfactual. The CRRA model (\(r_R\) constant implies DARA since \(r_A = r_R/c\) is decreasing) predicts constant portfolio shares, which is broadly consistent with the data. This is why CRRA is preferred over CARA in consumption-savings and portfolio choice models.

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
text:
27.10 Economic Intuition
Risk aversion is the central behavioral primitive in the economics of uncertainty. It explains why people buy insurance (paying a premium above the actuarially fair price to eliminate risk), why capital markets exist (allowing risk sharing across individuals), why risk is costly in principal-agent relationships (risk-averse agents require a risk premium to accept uncertain contracts), and why the equity premium puzzle exists (CRRA agents require a large equity premium to be willing to hold stocks given their risk).
The Arrow-Pratt measure \(r_A(c) = -u''/u'\) is elegant: it is the negative of the logarithmic derivative of marginal utility. Concavity of \(u\) (diminishing marginal utility) is what drives risk aversion: as consumption increases, each additional unit is worth less, so the utility loss from a downward deviation exceeds the utility gain from an equal upward deviation. The Arrow-Pratt formula \(\pi \approx \frac{1}{2}r_A \sigma^2\) quantifies this asymmetry to second order.
CARA and CRRA are the two workhorse families because they admit closed-form solutions in models with standard distributions (Normal for CARA, power-law for CRRA). The choice between them depends on the empirical question: CARA is used when the model involves additive shocks and the wealth level is not the relevant state variable; CRRA is used in life-cycle models where wealth accumulation matters.

title: 26  4.3 Risk Aversion
text:
27.11 Cross-References

4.1 State Space Framework — axioms for EU; Independence Axiom
4.2 Expected Utility Theory — VNM theorem; Bernoulli utility function
4.4 Stochastic Dominance — SSD corresponds to preferences of all risk-averse agents
PS Uncertainty — Problems 2 and 3

title: 27  4.4 Stochastic Dominance
text: 28 4.4 Stochastic Dominance

title: 27  4.4 Stochastic Dominance
text:
28.1 Context
Stochastic dominance criteria provide welfare rankings of probability distributions that are robust to the specific utility function, within a broad class. Rather than asking “does agent with utility \(u\) prefer \(F\) to \(G\)?”, stochastic dominance asks “do all agents in some class prefer \(F\) to \(G\)?”. This robustness makes stochastic dominance criteria especially valuable for policy analysis and empirical welfare measurement.
First-order stochastic dominance (FSD) applies to all agents with increasing preferences: \(F\) dominates \(G\) if every agent who prefers more to less prefers \(F\). Second-order stochastic dominance (SSD) applies to all risk-averse agents: \(F\) dominates \(G\) if every concave-utility agent prefers \(F\). SSD is equivalent to \(G\) being a “mean-preserving spread” of \(F\) in the sense of Rothschild and Stiglitz (1970), which formalizes the intuition that \(F\) is “less risky” than \(G\).
These concepts connect directly to empirical welfare analysis: stochastic dominance tests (Barrett-Donald, Linton-Maasoumi-Whang) are used in poverty and inequality measurement to rank income distributions without committing to a specific social welfare function.

title: 27  4.4 Stochastic Dominance
text:
28.2 First-Order Stochastic Dominance






NoteDefinition 4.1 — First-Order Stochastic Dominance (FSD)



Distribution \(F\) first-order stochastically dominates \(G\) (written \(F \succsim_1 G\) or \(F \text{ FSD } G\)) if: \[F(c) \leq G(c) \quad \text{for all } c \in \mathbb{R}\] with strict inequality for at least one \(c\).








CautionRemark 4.1 — Interpretation of FSD



\(F(c) \leq G(c)\) means that the probability of being below any threshold \(c\) is smaller under \(F\) than under \(G\). Equivalently, every quantile of \(F\) is at least as large as the corresponding quantile of \(G\): \(F^{-1}(\tau) \geq G^{-1}(\tau)\) for all \(\tau \in [0,1]\). FSD corresponds to the intuitive notion that \(F\) is “uniformly better” than \(G\).








ImportantTheorem 4.1 — FSD Characterization via EU



\(F \succsim_1 G\) if and only if \(\int u \, dF \geq \int u \, dG\) for all nondecreasing functions \(u: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) (for which the integrals exist).








TipProof





(\(\Rightarrow\)) FSD implies higher EU for all nondecreasing \(u\):
Suppose \(F(c) \leq G(c)\) for all \(c\). Using integration by parts (the key technical tool): \[\int u \, dF - \int u \, dG = \int u \, d(F - G)\]
Integrating by parts on \([-M, M]\) (then taking \(M \to \infty\)): \[\int_{-M}^{M} u(c) \, d(F(c) - G(c)) = \left[u(c)(F(c)-G(c))\right]_{-M}^{M} - \int_{-M}^{M} u'(c)(F(c)-G(c)) \, dc\]
Boundary terms: \((F(M)-G(M)) \to 0\) as \(M \to \infty\) (both CDFs approach 1); \((F(-M)-G(-M)) \to 0\) (both approach 0). Hence: \[\int u \, d(F-G) = -\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u'(c) [F(c) - G(c)] \, dc\]
Since \(F(c) \leq G(c)\), we have \(F(c) - G(c) \leq 0\) for all \(c\). Since \(u\) is nondecreasing, \(u'(c) \geq 0\) for all \(c\). Therefore: \[-\int u'(c)[F(c)-G(c)] \, dc = \int u'(c)[G(c)-F(c)] \, dc \geq 0\] Hence \(\int u \, dF \geq \int u \, dG\). \(\checkmark\)
(\(\Leftarrow\)) If \(\int u \, dF \geq \int u \, dG\) for all nondecreasing \(u\), then \(F \succsim_1 G\):
Suppose for contradiction that \(F(c^*) > G(c^*)\) for some \(c^*\). By right-continuity of CDFs, there exists an interval \((c^*, c^* + \delta)\) on which \(F(c) > G(c)\).
Choose \(u = \mathbf{1}_{(-\infty, c^*]}\) (indicator function, a valid nondecreasing function). Then: \[\int u \, dF = F(c^*) > G(c^*) = \int u \, dG\] — wait, we need \(\int u \, dF \geq \int u \, dG\), so this is consistent. Let me use the correct contrapositive.
Choose \(u(c) = \mathbf{1}_{[c^*, \infty)}(c)\) (indicator of being above \(c^*\)). This is nondecreasing. Then: \[\int u \, dF = 1 - F(c^*), \quad \int u \, dG = 1 - G(c^*)\] If \(F(c^*) > G(c^*)\), then \(\int u \, dF = 1 - F(c^*) < 1 - G(c^*) = \int u \, dG\). This means there exists a nondecreasing \(u\) for which \(\int u \, dF < \int u \, dG\), contradicting the assumption. Hence \(F(c) \leq G(c)\) for all \(c\). \(\blacksquare\)









NoteExample 4.1 — FSD in Income Distributions



Suppose \(F\) is the income distribution after a policy intervention (e.g., a targeted transfer) and \(G\) is the pre-intervention distribution. If the intervention raises every quantile of the income distribution (every percentile earns at least as much as before), then \(F\) FSD \(G\). All welfare functions that are increasing in income (regardless of inequality aversion) rank the post-intervention distribution as better.

title: 27  4.4 Stochastic Dominance
text:
28.3 Second-Order Stochastic Dominance






NoteDefinition 4.2 — Second-Order Stochastic Dominance (SSD)



Distribution \(F\) second-order stochastically dominates \(G\) (written \(F \succsim_2 G\) or \(F \text{ SSD } G\)) if: \[\int_{-\infty}^{c} [G(t) - F(t)] \, dt \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } c \in \mathbb{R}\]








CautionRemark 4.2 — Interpretation of SSD



The condition \(\int_{-\infty}^c [G(t)-F(t)] \, dt \geq 0\) says that the area between the CDFs (with \(G\) on top) is nonnegative up to every cutoff \(c\). Geometrically: the CDF of \(G\) lies “above and to the left” of \(F\) on average (integrated), even if they cross. SSD is weaker than FSD: if \(F\) FSD \(G\), then \(G(t) \geq F(t)\) for all \(t\), so \(G-F \geq 0\) everywhere, and the integral is certainly nonneg. The converse is false — SSD allows crossing CDFs.








ImportantTheorem 4.2 — SSD Characterization via EU



\(F \succsim_2 G\) if and only if \(\int u \, dF \geq \int u \, dG\) for all nondecreasing and concave functions \(u\) (all risk-averse agents with increasing preferences).








TipProof





(\(\Rightarrow\)) SSD implies higher EU for all nondecreasing, concave \(u\):
Using integration by parts twice. Define \(D_1(c) = G(c) - F(c)\) and \(D_2(c) = \int_{-\infty}^c D_1(t) \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^c [G(t)-F(t)] \, dt \geq 0\) (the SSD condition).
We need to show \(\int u \, dF \geq \int u \, dG\), i.e., \(\int u \, d(F - G) \geq 0\).
First integration by parts: \[\int u \, d(F-G) = -\int u \, dD_1 = -[u \cdot D_1]_{-\infty}^{\infty} + \int u' D_1 \, dc\]
Boundary terms: \(D_1(\pm\infty) = G(\pm\infty) - F(\pm\infty) = 0\). So: \[\int u \, d(F-G) = \int u'(c) D_1(c) \, dc = \int u'(c)[G(c)-F(c)] \, dc\]
Second integration by parts (integrate \(u'\), differentiate \(D_1\), where \(D_1(c) = dD_2/dc\)): \[\int u'(c) D_1(c) \, dc = \int u'(c) \, dD_2(c)\] \[= [u'(c) D_2(c)]_{-\infty}^{\infty} - \int u''(c) D_2(c) \, dc\]
Boundary terms: \(D_2(-\infty) = 0\) (integral from \(-\infty\) to \(-\infty\)). For \(D_2(\infty) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}[G(t)-F(t)]dt\): if \(F\) and \(G\) have the same mean (\(\mathbb{E}_F[c] = \mathbb{E}_G[c]\)), then \(\int(G-F)dt = 0\) by integration by parts. More generally, for the boundary term to vanish, either \(F\) and \(G\) have equal means, or \(u'(\infty) = 0\) (which holds for concave \(u\) on bounded support or if \(u'\) is bounded and \(D_2\) grows slower than \(1/u'(\infty)\)). Under standard conditions, the boundary term is 0.
Therefore: \[\int u \, d(F-G) = -\int u''(c) D_2(c) \, dc\]
Since \(u\) is concave: \(u''(c) \leq 0\). Since SSD holds: \(D_2(c) \geq 0\). Therefore: \[-\int u''(c) D_2(c) \, dc \geq 0 \qquad \checkmark\]
(\(\Leftarrow\)) If \(\int u \, dF \geq \int u \, dG\) for all nondecreasing concave \(u\), then SSD:
Suppose for contradiction that \(D_2(c^*) = \int_{-\infty}^{c^*}[G(t)-F(t)]dt < 0\) for some \(c^*\).
By continuity, \(D_2(c) < 0\) on some interval \((c^* - \delta, c^*)\).
Choose \(u(c) = \min(c, c^*) = c \cdot \mathbf{1}_{c \leq c^*} + c^* \cdot \mathbf{1}_{c > c^*}\). This is nondecreasing and concave (\(u' = \mathbf{1}_{c \leq c^*} \geq 0\), \(u'' = -\delta_{c^*} \leq 0\)).
Then: \(\int u \, dF - \int u \, dG = -\int u'' D_2 \, dc = \int \delta_{c^*}(c) D_2(c) \, dc = -D_2(c^*)\)
Wait — using the integration by parts result: \(\int u \, d(F-G) = -\int u'' D_2 \, dc\). With \(u''(c) = -\delta_0(c - c^*)\) (the Dirac mass at \(c^*\), since \(u'\) has a jump down from 1 to 0 at \(c^*\)): \[\int u \, d(F-G) = -\int (-\delta(c-c^*)) D_2(c) \, dc = D_2(c^*) < 0\]
This contradicts \(\int u \, dF \geq \int u \, dG\). Hence \(D_2(c) \geq 0\) for all \(c\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: 27  4.4 Stochastic Dominance
text:
28.4 Mean-Preserving Spreads (Rothschild-Stiglitz)






NoteDefinition 4.3 — Mean-Preserving Spread



Distribution \(G\) is a mean-preserving spread (MPS) of \(F\) if: 1. \(\int c \, dF(c) = \int c \, dG(c)\) (equal means) 2. \(G = F + \text{noise}\) in the sense that \(G\) can be obtained from \(F\) by adding noise to each realization: there exists a random variable \(\tilde{\epsilon}\) such that \(c_G = c_F + \tilde{\epsilon}\) with \(\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\epsilon} | c_F] = 0\).








ImportantTheorem 4.3 — SSD Equivalence with Mean-Preserving Spreads



\(F \succsim_2 G\) if and only if \(G\) is a mean-preserving spread of \(F\) (i.e., \(G\) has the same mean as \(F\) but is “more spread out”).








TipProof





Sketch of the equivalence:
(MPS \(\Rightarrow\) SSD): Suppose \(c_G = c_F + \tilde{\epsilon}\) with \(\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\epsilon}|c_F] = 0\). For any nondecreasing concave \(u\): \[\begin{align}
\mathbb{E}_G[u(c)] &= \mathbb{E}[u(c_F + \tilde{\epsilon})] \\
&= \mathbb{E}_{c_F}\left[\mathbb{E}[u(c_F + \tilde{\epsilon}) \mid c_F]\right] \\
&\leq \mathbb{E}_{c_F}\left[u\left(\mathbb{E}[c_F + \tilde{\epsilon} \mid c_F]\right)\right] \quad \text{(Jensen, since $u$ is concave)}\\
&= \mathbb{E}_{c_F}[u(c_F)] = \mathbb{E}_F[u(c)]
\end{align}\] So \(\mathbb{E}_F[u] \geq \mathbb{E}_G[u]\) for all concave nondecreasing \(u\), i.e., \(F \succsim_2 G\).
(SSD \(\Rightarrow\) MPS): This direction uses the theory of majorization and is more involved. Key idea: if \(F \succsim_2 G\) and they have equal means, then the “excess distribution” \(G - F\) can be decomposed as a series of pairwise spreads (elementary mean-preserving spreads), showing that \(G\) is obtained from \(F\) by adding conditional noise. A full proof uses the Hardy-Littlewood-Polya theorem on majorization.
If \(F \succsim_2 G\) but means differ (\(\mathbb{E}_G[c] < \mathbb{E}_F[c]\)): then \(G\) is worse than \(F\) both due to lower mean (FSD direction) and more spread. SSD captures both effects. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark 4.3 — Rothschild-Stiglitz Interpretation



The MPS characterization of SSD is the key result in Rothschild and Stiglitz (1970). It says: \(G\) is riskier than \(F\) (for risk-averse agents) if and only if \(G\) can be obtained from \(F\) by adding noise. This gives a precise operational definition of “more risky” that does not depend on the particular risk measure used. Applications include: - Insurance: Full insurance eliminates the MPS (replaces \(G = c_F + \tilde{\epsilon}\) with the certain \(c_F\)); this is why all risk-averse agents prefer full insurance at actuarially fair rates. - Portfolio choice: A mean-preserving spread of the return distribution makes the risky asset less attractive to all risk-averse investors. - Principal-agent theory: Performance pay introduces a MPS (adds noise) relative to a fixed wage; risk-averse agents require a premium to accept performance pay.

title: 27  4.4 Stochastic Dominance
text:
28.5 Applications






NoteExample 4.2 — FSD and Portfolio Choice



An investor has wealth \(w\) and can allocate it between two assets: a safe asset with gross return 1 and a risky asset with gross return distribution \(\tilde{R}\). A new asset has return distribution \(\tilde{R}' \text{ FSD } \tilde{R}\).
Claim: Any investor with increasing utility (prefers more to less) will weakly prefer the new asset.
Proof: By Theorem 4.1, since \(\tilde{R}'\) FSD \(\tilde{R}\), for any nondecreasing utility \(u\): \(\mathbb{E}[u(\tilde{R}')] \geq \mathbb{E}[u(\tilde{R})]\). Hence any portfolio with the new asset yields higher expected utility for any monotone preferences.








NoteExample 4.3 — SSD and Insurance



An agent has initial wealth \(w\) and faces a loss \(\tilde{L}\) with distribution \(G\) (random wealth \(w - \tilde{L}\)). Full insurance replaces \(w - \tilde{L}\) with the certain amount \(w - \mathbb{E}[\tilde{L}]\) (i.e., distribution \(F = \delta_{w-\mathbb{E}[\tilde{L}]}\)).
The certain wealth \(F\) second-order stochastically dominates the risky wealth \(G\) because: - Both have the same mean: \(\mathbb{E}_F[c] = w - \mathbb{E}[\tilde{L}] = \mathbb{E}_G[c]\). - \(G\) is a mean-preserving spread of \(F\) (adding the zero-mean noise \(-(\tilde{L} - \mathbb{E}[\tilde{L}])\) to the certain outcome).
By SSD, all risk-averse agents (all \(u\) concave) prefer full insurance at actuarially fair premium to bearing the risk.








NoteExample 4.4 — Stochastic Dominance in Welfare Analysis



In poverty analysis, let \(F\) be the income distribution after a social program and \(G\) be the pre-program distribution. If \(F\) SSD \(G\): - All social welfare functions \(W = \int u(c) dF(c)\) with \(u\) concave (inequality-averse) and increasing rank \(F\) weakly higher. - This holds for any poverty measure that can be written as \(P = \int p(c) dF(c)\) with \(p\) concave and decreasing (which includes the Foster-Greer-Thorbecke measures for appropriate parameter ranges).
This is the core result underlying nonparametric welfare dominance tests used in empirical development economics (e.g., comparing welfare pre- and post-policy without specifying the exact social welfare function).

title: 27  4.4 Stochastic Dominance
text:
28.6 Empirical Link: Stochastic Dominance Tests






CautionRemark 4.4 — Nonparametric Stochastic Dominance Testing



In empirical applications (poverty and inequality analysis, financial economics), stochastic dominance is tested nonparametrically:

Barrett-Donald (2003): Kolmogorov-Smirnov-type test for FSD and SSD between two empirical distributions.
Linton-Maasoumi-Whang (2005): Bootstrap-based tests for stochastic dominance that are valid under weak assumptions.
Application in poverty: The FSD test asks whether every poverty headcount (at any poverty line) is lower under distribution \(F\) than \(G\). The SSD test asks whether every Foster-Greer-Thorbecke \(P_1\) poverty gap measure (at any poverty line) is lower. These tests can confirm dominance without specifying a poverty line.

This connects directly to the Poverty and Inequality course: the Atkinson (1970) result that SSD is equivalent to unanimity of all increasing concave social welfare functions is the theoretical foundation for these tests.

title: 27  4.4 Stochastic Dominance
text:
28.8 Economic Intuition
Stochastic dominance is the natural framework for welfare analysis under uncertainty when the analyst does not want to commit to a specific utility function. FSD is the easy case: if one distribution gives better outcomes at every quantile, all agents with increasing preferences prefer it — this is just a universal improvement. SSD is more subtle: it applies only to risk-averse agents, but it is satisfied in many practical cases (e.g., the comparison of full insurance to a fair lottery, or a more concentrated income distribution to a more dispersed one with the same mean).
The equivalence between SSD and mean-preserving spreads is the deepest result in this note. It says that “riskier” (in the sense of SSD-dominated) is exactly the same as “a mean-preserving spread”: added noise that does not change the mean but makes the distribution more dispersed. This is an operational, non-parametric definition of riskiness that is used throughout insurance theory, financial economics, and welfare analysis.
The policy relevance of these concepts is enormous: stochastic dominance tests allow policymakers to make robust welfare statements about policy interventions without specifying the exact form of the social welfare function. If a policy generates a distribution that SSD-dominates the status quo, then all risk-averse welfare-maximizing policymakers — regardless of their precise inequality aversion — would approve the policy.

title: 28  Problem Set: Choice Under Uncertainty
text:
29.1 Problem 1 — Verifying the VNM Axioms
Let \(C = \{c_1, c_2, c_3\}\) with \(c_1 < c_2 < c_3\) (monetary outcomes: \(c_1 = 0\), \(c_2 = 10\), \(c_3 = 100\)). An agent reports the following preference ordering over a selection of lotteries:

\(\delta_{c_3} \succ \delta_{c_2} \succ \delta_{c_1}\) (strict preference over pure outcomes)
\(\delta_{c_2} \sim 0.4 \delta_{c_3} + 0.6 \delta_{c_1}\) (indifferent between certain \(c_2\) and the stated lottery)
\((0.5 \delta_{c_3} + 0.5 \delta_{c_1}) \succ \delta_{c_2}\) (strictly prefers the 50-50 gamble to certain \(c_2\))

(a) Show that the first two statements are consistent with an EU representation with some Bernoulli utility \(u\). Derive the value \(u(c_2)\) (normalizing \(u(c_1) = 0\) and \(u(c_3) = 1\)).
(b) Show that the third statement (\((0.5\delta_{c_3}+0.5\delta_{c_1}) \succ \delta_{c_2}\)) is inconsistent with the Bernoulli utility derived in part (a) and with EU theory. Identify which axiom is violated.
(c) More carefully: are the first and third statements consistent with the rationality axiom (completeness and transitivity) alone, ignoring the Independence Axiom? Is there any EU utility consistent with all three statements?
(d) Now suppose instead the agent reports \(\delta_{c_2} \sim 0.4\delta_{c_3} + 0.6\delta_{c_1}\) and \((0.5\delta_{c_3} + 0.5\delta_{c_1}) \sim \delta_{c_2}\). Is this consistent with EU? If so, what constraint does it place on \(u(c_2)\)? Explain.
(e) Suppose a fourth outcome \(c_4\) is introduced with \(c_3 < c_4\). Using continuity, argue that there must exist a \(\beta \in (0,1)\) such that \(\delta_{c_3} \sim \beta \delta_{c_4} + (1-\beta)\delta_{c_1}\). What is the interpretation of \(\beta\) in terms of the EU representation? Compute \(u(c_3)\) (normalized so that \(u(c_1) = 0\), \(u(c_4) = 1\)) in terms of \(\beta\).
crumbs: 28  Problem Set: Choice Under Uncertainty

title: 28  Problem Set: Choice Under Uncertainty
section: 29.2 Problem 2 — Certainty Equivalent and Risk Premium under CARA
text:
29.2 Problem 2 — Certainty Equivalent and Risk Premium under CARA
An agent has CARA utility \(u(c) = -e^{-\gamma c}\) with \(\gamma > 0\). The agent faces a lottery \(\tilde{c}\) with distribution \(\tilde{c} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\) (normally distributed with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\)).
(a) Compute the expected utility \(EU = \mathbb{E}[u(\tilde{c})] = \mathbb{E}[-e^{-\gamma \tilde{c}}]\) in closed form. (Hint: use the moment generating function of the normal distribution: \(\mathbb{E}[e^{t\tilde{c}}] = e^{\mu t + \sigma^2 t^2/2}\).)
(b) Derive the certainty equivalent \(CE(\tilde{c})\) defined by \(u(CE) = EU\). Show that: \[CE = \mu - \frac{\gamma \sigma^2}{2}\] Interpret this formula: how does the certainty equivalent depend on the mean, variance, and risk aversion parameter?
(c) Compute the risk premium \(\pi = \mu - CE\) and verify that it equals \(\frac{\gamma\sigma^2}{2}\). Confirm that this is consistent with the Arrow-Pratt approximation \(\pi \approx \frac{1}{2}r_A(c)\sigma^2\) (where \(r_A = \gamma\) for CARA). In this case, the approximation is exact — explain why.
(d) Now consider two agents with CARA parameters \(\gamma_A > \gamma_B > 0\). Both face the lottery \(\tilde{c} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\). Show that agent \(A\) has a lower certainty equivalent than agent \(B\): \(CE_A < CE_B\). Is this consistent with the ordering of Arrow-Pratt measures?
(e) Suppose the agent can purchase insurance that replaces the risky lottery \(\tilde{c}\) with the certain outcome \(\mu\) (full insurance at actuarially fair price). What is the maximum premium \(\pi^*\) the agent would pay for this insurance, as a function of \(\gamma\), \(\mu\), and \(\sigma^2\)? Compare the maximum premium for agents \(A\) and \(B\).
(f) Now suppose \(\tilde{c}\) follows an exponential distribution with mean \(1/\lambda\) (so \(f(c) = \lambda e^{-\lambda c}\) for \(c \geq 0\)). Compute \(CE\) and \(\pi\) for the CARA agent. Is the Arrow-Pratt approximation exact in this case? Under what conditions is the approximation exact?
crumbs: 28  Problem Set: Choice Under Uncertainty

title: 28  Problem Set: Choice Under Uncertainty
section: 29.3 Problem 3 — DARA and Risky Asset Demand
text:
29.3 Problem 3 — DARA and Risky Asset Demand
An agent has wealth \(w > 0\) and can invest \(a \geq 0\) dollars in a risky asset with net return \(\tilde{R}\) (mean \(\mu_R > r\), variance \(\sigma_R^2\)) and the remainder at safe rate \(r \geq 0\). Final wealth is: \[\tilde{W}(a) = (w - a)(1 + r) + a(1 + \tilde{R}) = w(1+r) + a(\tilde{R} - r)\]
(a) Write down the portfolio optimization problem. Derive the first-order condition for an interior optimum \(a^* > 0\): \[\mathbb{E}\left[u'(\tilde{W}(a^*)) \cdot (\tilde{R} - r)\right] = 0\] Verify the second-order condition under risk aversion.
(b) Use the implicit function theorem to show that the optimal risky investment \(a^*(w)\) satisfies: \[\frac{da^*}{dw} = -\frac{\mathbb{E}\left[u''(\tilde{W}^*)(\tilde{R}-r)\right](1+r)}{\mathbb{E}\left[u''(\tilde{W}^*)(\tilde{R}-r)^2\right]}\]
(c) Show that the denominator satisfies \(\mathbb{E}[u''(\tilde{W}^*)(\tilde{R}-r)^2] < 0\) (under strict concavity of \(u\)). Hence the sign of \(da^*/dw\) is determined by the sign of \(\mathbb{E}[u''(\tilde{W}^*)(\tilde{R}-r)]\).
(d) Prove that DARA (\(r_A'(c) < 0\), i.e., \(r_A(c)\) decreasing) implies \(\mathbb{E}[u''(\tilde{W}^*)(\tilde{R}-r)] < 0\), and therefore \(da^*/dw > 0\). (Hint: use the FOC to show \(\mathbb{E}[u'(\tilde{W}^*)(\tilde{R}-r)] = 0\), then write \(u''(\tilde{W}^*)(\tilde{R}-r) = -r_A(\tilde{W}^*) u'(\tilde{W}^*) (\tilde{R}-r)\), and use DARA and the sign of \((\tilde{R}-r)\) in different states.)
(e) What does the result imply for CARA utility? Verify directly by showing that under CARA, the FOC pins down \(a^*\) independently of \(w\).
(f) Show that under CRRA utility \(u(c) = c^{1-\rho}/(1-\rho)\), the optimal portfolio share \(\theta^* = a^*/w\) (fraction invested in the risky asset) is independent of \(w\) (scale-invariance). Interpret this result in terms of decreasing absolute risk aversion and constant relative risk aversion.
crumbs: 28  Problem Set: Choice Under Uncertainty

title: 28  Problem Set: Choice Under Uncertainty
section: 29.4 Problem 4 — Mean-Preserving Spreads and EU Rankings
text:
29.4 Problem 4 — Mean-Preserving Spreads and EU Rankings
Consider two distributions over income \(c \geq 0\): - \(F\): uniform distribution on \([2, 4]\) (density \(f(c) = 1/2\) on \([2,4]\)) - \(G\): uniform distribution on \([1, 5]\) (density \(g(c) = 1/4\) on \([1,5]\))
(a) Compute the means \(\mathbb{E}_F[c]\) and \(\mathbb{E}_G[c]\). Compute the variances \(\text{Var}_F(c)\) and \(\text{Var}_G(c)\).
(b) Compute the CDFs \(F(c)\) and \(G(c)\) explicitly. Plot them (or describe their shapes). Do they satisfy the FSD condition \(F(c) \leq G(c)\) for all \(c\)? Explain.
(c) Show that \(G\) is a mean-preserving spread of \(F\) by verifying: (i) Equal means (ii) The SSD integral condition: \(\int_{-\infty}^c [G(t) - F(t)] \, dt \geq 0\) for all \(c\)
Compute \(\int_{-\infty}^c [G(t)-F(t)]\,dt\) explicitly for all relevant ranges of \(c\).
(d) By the equivalence between SSD and MPS, conclude that \(F \succsim_2 G\). Verify this directly for the following specific utility functions: (i) \(u(c) = \ln c\) (log utility, risk-averse) (ii) \(u(c) = c^{1/2}\) (square root utility, risk-averse) (iii) \(u(c) = c^2\) (convex utility, risk-seeking)
For (i) and (ii), confirm \(\mathbb{E}_F[u] \geq \mathbb{E}_G[u]\). For (iii), confirm \(\mathbb{E}_F[u] \leq \mathbb{E}_G[u]\). Compute the expected utilities explicitly.
(e) Construct a new distribution \(H\) that is a mean-preserving spread of \(G\) (and hence also SSD-dominated by \(F\)). Verify your construction by checking the SSD condition.
(f) Suppose a government policy changes the income distribution from \(G\) to \(F\). Characterize the class of social welfare functions \(W = \int u(c) dF(c)\) that rank the new distribution \(F\) as weakly better than \(G\). Can you make a unanimous welfare statement (i.e., a statement valid for all social welfare functions in some large class) about this policy?
crumbs: 28  Problem Set: Choice Under Uncertainty

title: 28  Problem Set: Choice Under Uncertainty
section: 29.5 Problem 5 — The Allais Paradox and Violations of Independence
text:
29.5 Problem 5 — The Allais Paradox and Violations of Independence
(a) Formally present the Allais paradox as in Example 4.1 of 4.1 State Space Framework. Let \(C = \{0, 1, 5\}\) (millions of dollars). Consider the four lotteries: \[L_1 = (0, 1, 0), \quad L_2 = (0.01, 0.89, 0.10), \quad L_3 = (0.89, 0.11, 0), \quad L_4 = (0.90, 0, 0.10)\] Show algebraically that the preference \(L_1 \succ L_2\) and \(L_4 \succ L_3\) cannot be represented by any EU utility function. (Derive the conditions on \(u(0), u(1), u(5)\) implied by each preference and show they are contradictory.)
(b) Represent the four lotteries as points in the two-dimensional simplex (since \(p_1 + p_2 + p_3 = 1\), use coordinates \((p_1, p_3)\) with \(p_2 = 1-p_1-p_3\)). Draw the lotteries \(L_1, L_2, L_3, L_4\) in this coordinate system. Show geometrically that the Allais preference \(L_1 \succ L_2, L_4 \succ L_3\) corresponds to “fanning out” of indifference curves, which violates the EU requirement of parallel linear indifference curves.
(c) Now consider a rank-dependent utility (RDU) agent with utility \(u(c) = c\) (risk-neutral) and probability weighting function \(w(p) = p^{0.6}\) (a concave probability weighting function that overweights small probabilities). Compute the RDU value: \[RDU(L) = \sum_n [w(F_L(c_n)) - w(F_L(c_{n-1}))] u(c_n)\] where \(F_L(c_n) = \sum_{k \leq n} p_k\) is the CDF of \(L\) evaluated at \(c_n\) (ranked from worst to best). Compute \(RDU(L_1), RDU(L_2), RDU(L_3), RDU(L_4)\). Can RDU with this \(w\) accommodate the Allais preference?
(d) Construct a new pair of lotteries \(M_1, M_2\) and \(M_3, M_4\) with the same underlying structure as the Allais paradox (i.e., \(M_3 = M_1\) mixed with \(\delta_0\) in the same way \(L_3\) is constructed from \(L_1\)) but with different probability values, such that the resulting preferences violate the Independence Axiom. Be explicit about the construction and prove that EU cannot accommodate the stated preferences.
(e) Discuss the policy implications of the Allais paradox. If EU is systematically violated, what does this imply for: (i) The use of expected utility in welfare analysis (cost-benefit analysis of risky policies)? (ii) The design of insurance contracts (if agents have non-EU preferences, does optimal insurance still involve full coverage at actuarially fair prices)? (iii) The use of EU in game-theoretic models of strategic interaction under uncertainty (Bayesian games)?
Provide a balanced assessment: when is the EU assumption likely to be a reasonable approximation, and when is it likely to lead to seriously wrong predictions?
