title: 29  Part V — Game Theory
text: 30 Part V — Game Theory

title: 29  Part V — Game Theory
text:
30.1 Context
Game theory extends individual optimization to settings of strategic interaction: each agent’s payoff depends not only on her own choice but also on the choices of others. The central challenge is to predict behavior when no agent can optimize in isolation. The equilibrium concepts developed in this part — Nash equilibrium, subgame perfect equilibrium, and Bayesian Nash equilibrium — constitute the analytical backbone of virtually every field of applied economic theory: industrial organization, political economy, contract theory, auction theory, mechanism design, and international economics.
The MWG treatment (Chapters 7–9) covers the three main game forms: normal form (static, complete information), extensive form (dynamic, complete information), and Bayesian games (static, incomplete information). The progression mirrors the increasing richness of strategic environments encountered in applications.

title: 29  Part V — Game Theory
text:
30.2 Why Equilibrium?
The equilibrium concept resolves the problem that each player’s best response depends on others’ strategies, which in turn depend on the first player’s strategy. A Nash equilibrium (NE) is a fixed point of the joint best-response correspondence: every player is simultaneously best-responding to the others. This mutual consistency is the minimum requirement for a “stable” prediction. Stronger refinements — subgame perfection, sequential equilibrium — impose additional credibility requirements on behavior off the equilibrium path.

title: 29  Part V — Game Theory
text:
30.4 Key Relationships

Every strictly dominant strategy equilibrium is the unique NE and the unique outcome of IESDS.
Every NE strategy profile is rationalizable, but not conversely.
Every SPE is a NE, but not conversely; SPE eliminates non-credible threats.
The Folk Theorem shows that repeated interaction radically expands the set of equilibrium outcomes relative to the stage game.
BNE reduces to NE when types are degenerate (complete information); it generalizes NE to environments with private information.

title: 30  5.1 Normal Form Games
text: 31 5.1 Normal Form Games

title: 30  5.1 Normal Form Games
text:
31.1 Context
The normal form (or strategic form) is the most compact representation of a game. It abstracts away from the temporal structure of play and records only: who the players are, what strategies they have available, and what payoffs result from each combination of strategies. Despite this compression, the normal form is rich enough to define dominance, rationalizability, and Nash equilibrium. Dynamic games are typically analyzed in extensive form first, then reduced to normal form when applying equilibrium refinements. This note covers the structure of normal form games and the solution concepts based on iterated reasoning: dominance, IESDS, and rationalizability.

title: 30  5.1 Normal Form Games
text:
31.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 5.1.1 — Normal Form Game



A normal form game (or strategic form game) is a triple \[\Gamma = (I,\, (S_i)_{i \in I},\, (u_i)_{i \in I})\] where: - \(I = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}\) is the finite set of players; - \(S_i\) is player \(i\)’s strategy set (which may be finite or infinite); - \(S = S_1 \times S_2 \times \cdots \times S_n\) is the set of strategy profiles; - \(u_i: S \to \mathbb{R}\) is player \(i\)’s payoff function (von Neumann–Morgenstern utility over outcomes).
The notation \(s_{-i} \in S_{-i} = \prod_{j \neq i} S_j\) denotes the strategies of all players except \(i\).








NoteDefinition 5.1.2 — Strict Dominance



Strategy \(s_i \in S_i\) is strictly dominated by \(s_i' \in S_i\) if \[u_i(s_i', s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) \quad \text{for all } s_{-i} \in S_{-i}.\] A strategy \(s_i\) is a strictly dominant strategy for player \(i\) if it strictly dominates every other \(s_i'' \in S_i \setminus \{s_i\}\).








NoteDefinition 5.1.3 — Weak Dominance



Strategy \(s_i \in S_i\) is weakly dominated by \(s_i' \in S_i\) if \[u_i(s_i', s_{-i}) \geq u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) \quad \text{for all } s_{-i} \in S_{-i},\] with strict inequality for at least one \(s_{-i}\).








CautionRemark



Strict domination is a stronger requirement. A rational player should never play a strictly dominated strategy regardless of beliefs about opponents. Weakly dominated strategies may be best responses under some beliefs, which creates subtlety in iterated elimination.








NoteDefinition 5.1.4 — IESDS (Iterated Elimination of Strictly Dominated Strategies)



Define a sequence of reduced games as follows. Let \(S^0 = S\).
At stage \(k \geq 1\): for each player \(i\), eliminate from \(S_i^{k-1}\) every strategy \(s_i\) that is strictly dominated within the reduced game \(S^{k-1}\). That is: \[S_i^k = \{s_i \in S_i^{k-1} : \nexists\, s_i' \in S_i^{k-1} \text{ such that } u_i(s_i', s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i, s_{-i})\, \forall s_{-i} \in S_{-i}^{k-1}\}.\] The process continues until no further eliminations are possible. The resulting set \(S^\infty = \bigcap_{k=0}^\infty S^k\) is the IESDS outcome.








NoteDefinition 5.1.5 — Rationalizability



A strategy \(s_i \in S_i\) is rationalizable if it survives IESDS, i.e., \(s_i \in S_i^\infty\).
Equivalently, \(s_i\) is rationalizable if there exists a sequence of belief-closed sets \(B_i \subseteq S_i\) — called a best-response set — such that \(s_i \in B_i\) and every \(s_i \in B_i\) is a best response to some probability distribution over \(B_{-i} = \prod_{j \neq i} B_j\).

title: 30  5.1 Normal Form Games
text:
31.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 5.1.1 — Order Independence of IESDS



The outcome \(S^\infty\) of iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies is independent of the order in which strategies are eliminated.








TipProof





Let \(S^\infty\) denote the outcome of any particular elimination order. We show that any strategy surviving one elimination order survives all others.
Step 1: IESDS terminates. Since \(S\) is finite (or satisfies a finiteness condition), the sequence \(S^0 \supseteq S^1 \supseteq \cdots\) is a weakly decreasing sequence of finite sets, hence must stabilize.
Step 2: Monotonicity. Let \(T \subseteq S\) be any set with \(S^\infty \subseteq T \subseteq S\). We claim: if \(s_i\) is strictly dominated in \(S\), it remains strictly dominated in \(T\) (since the domain of \(s_{-i}\) only shrinks, strict inequality is preserved). Therefore, any strategy eliminated from \(S\) is also eliminated from \(T\).
Step 3: Order independence. Let \(A^\infty\) and \(B^\infty\) be outcomes of two different elimination orders. We show \(A^\infty \subseteq B^\infty\) (and by symmetry, \(B^\infty \subseteq A^\infty\), giving equality).
Suppose \(s_i \notin B^\infty\). Then \(s_i\) was eliminated at some stage \(k\) of the \(B\)-procedure, meaning there exists \(s_i'\) such that \(u_i(s_i', s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i, s_{-i})\) for all \(s_{-i} \in B_{-i}^{k-1}\).
Since \(A^\infty \subseteq B^{k-1}\) (the \(A\)-procedure only eliminates strictly dominated strategies, and anything eliminated in \(A\) was strictly dominated when \(S_{-i}\) was still at least as large as \(B_{-i}^{k-1}\), so it was also eliminated in \(B\) by stage \(k-1\)), we have \(s_i'\) strictly dominates \(s_i\) on \(A_{-i}^\infty\) as well. Hence \(s_i \notin A^\infty\).
This shows \(s_i \notin B^\infty \Rightarrow s_i \notin A^\infty\), i.e., \(A^\infty \subseteq B^\infty\). By symmetry \(B^\infty \subseteq A^\infty\), so \(A^\infty = B^\infty\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Order independence fails for iterated elimination of weakly dominated strategies. Different elimination orders can produce different outcomes, which is a key reason IESDS (strict) is theoretically cleaner than its weak counterpart.








ImportantTheorem 5.1.2 — Rationalizability and Nash Equilibrium



Let \(\Gamma\) be a finite normal form game. 1. Every strategy \(s_i^*\) played in a Nash equilibrium is rationalizable. 2. The converse fails in general: there exist rationalizable strategy profiles that are not Nash equilibria.








TipProof





Part 1. Let \(s^* = (s_1^*, \ldots, s_n^*)\) be a Nash equilibrium. By definition, \(s_i^*\) is a best response to \(s_{-i}^*\) for each \(i\). The set \(\{s_i^*\}\) for each \(i\) constitutes a best-response set: \(s_i^*\) is a best response to the degenerate belief that puts probability one on \(s_{-i}^*\). Therefore \(s_i^*\) is rationalizable.
Part 2. Consider the game where both players choose from \(\{H, T\}\) with payoff matrix (Matching Pennies):




H
T




H
1,−1
−1,1


T
−1,1
1,−1



No strategy is strictly dominated (each is a best response to some belief). Therefore \(S^\infty = S\), so all four strategies are rationalizable. Yet the only Nash equilibrium is the mixed strategy \((1/2, 1/2)\). Pure strategies \(H\) and \(T\) are rationalizable but not part of any pure NE. \(\blacksquare\)

title: 30  5.1 Normal Form Games
text:
31.4 Examples






NoteExample 5.1.1 — Prisoner’s Dilemma



Two players, each choosing \(\{C, D\}\) (Cooperate, Defect):
\[\begin{array}{c|cc} & C & D \\ \hline C & 2,2 & 0,3 \\ D & 3,0 & 1,1 \end{array}\]
IESDS: \(D\) strictly dominates \(C\) for both players: \[u_i(D, C) = 3 > 2 = u_i(C, C), \quad u_i(D, D) = 1 > 0 = u_i(C, D).\] After one round of elimination, \(S^\infty = \{(D,D)\}\).
Economic intuition: The unique rationalizable outcome is \((D,D)\) with payoff \((1,1)\), even though \((C,C)\) with payoff \((2,2)\) Pareto dominates it. Individual rationality conflicts with collective optimality — the fundamental tension in collective action problems, cartel stability, public goods provision, and arms races.








NoteExample 5.1.2 — Coordination Game



\[\begin{array}{c|cc} & A & B \\ \hline A & 2,2 & 0,0 \\ B & 0,0 & 1,1 \end{array}\]
IESDS: Neither \(A\) nor \(B\) is strictly dominated (each is a best response to the matching strategy). So \(S^\infty = S\); both strategies are rationalizable. Rationalizability does not select a unique outcome here — it only eliminates strategies inconsistent with any rational belief, but both \((A,A)\) and \((B,B)\) are consistent with rational play. The two pure Nash equilibria are \((A,A)\) and \((B,B)\).








NoteExample 5.1.3 — Battle of the Sexes



Two players (Opera \(O\), Football \(F\)), player 1 prefers Opera, player 2 prefers Football:
\[\begin{array}{c|cc} & O & F \\ \hline O & 2,1 & 0,0 \\ F & 0,0 & 1,2 \end{array}\]
IESDS: Neither strategy is dominated for either player. \(S^\infty = S\); both strategies are rationalizable. The game has two pure NE — \((O,O)\) and \((F,F)\) — and one mixed NE. Rationalizability predicts any of the four pure strategy combinations could be played.

title: 30  5.1 Normal Form Games
text:
31.5 Finite vs. Infinite Strategy Spaces
When \(S_i\) is infinite (e.g., \(S_i = [0,\infty)\) as in Cournot quantity competition), additional regularity conditions are needed:

Continuity: \(u_i(s_i, s_{-i})\) must be continuous in \(s_i\) for best responses to exist (Weierstrass theorem requires compactness and continuity).
Compactness: \(S_i\) is typically restricted to a compact set (e.g., \(S_i = [0,\bar{q}]\)) to guarantee best responses exist.
IESDS with infinite spaces: Elimination must be done transfinitely; order independence still holds for strict dominance but the process may require uncountably many rounds.

In Cournot duopoly with linear demand \(P = a - bQ\) and constant marginal cost \(c\): \[u_i(q_i, q_j) = (a - b(q_i + q_j) - c)q_i.\] IESDS converges to the unique Cournot-Nash equilibrium \(q_i^* = (a-c)/(3b)\) after countably infinite rounds (starting from the upper bound on rational quantities).

title: 30  5.1 Normal Form Games
text:
31.6 Economic Intuition
Rationalizability as epistemic foundation. A strategy is rationalizable if it is consistent with common knowledge of rationality — every player is rational, knows every player is rational, knows every player knows this, and so on ad infinitum. This is a purely epistemic concept: it does not require players to coordinate on an equilibrium, only that they are reasonable.
IESDS implements this reasoning iteratively: in round 1, eliminate strategies no rational player would ever use; in round 2, eliminate strategies that are only best responses to now-eliminated strategies; and so on. The surviving strategies are exactly those consistent with any depth of mutual reasoning about rationality.
The gap between rationalizability and Nash equilibrium is precisely the gap between individual rational reasoning and mutual coordination: Nash equilibrium additionally requires that beliefs are correct, i.e., that each player’s belief about others’ strategies exactly matches their actual equilibrium strategy. Rationalizability does not require this consistency of beliefs across players.

title: 30  5.1 Normal Form Games
text:
31.7 Cross-References

title: 31  5.2 Nash Equilibrium
text: 32 5.2 Nash Equilibrium

title: 31  5.2 Nash Equilibrium
text:
32.1 Context
Nash equilibrium (Nash 1950) is the central solution concept of non-cooperative game theory. It requires that each player’s strategy is a best response to the strategies of all other players — a condition of mutual best response. This is the natural stability condition: in a Nash equilibrium, no player has a unilateral incentive to deviate. The deep mathematical question is whether such a fixed point of the best-response correspondence always exists. Nash’s answer — yes, in finite games if we allow mixed strategies — follows from Kakutani’s fixed point theorem and is the primary existence result in all of game theory.

title: 31  5.2 Nash Equilibrium
text:
32.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 5.2.1 — Best Response Correspondence



Given a normal form game \(\Gamma = (I, (S_i), (u_i))\), the best response correspondence of player \(i\) is \[B_i(s_{-i}) = \arg\max_{s_i \in S_i} u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) = \{s_i \in S_i : u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) \geq u_i(s_i', s_{-i})\; \forall s_i' \in S_i\}.\] This is a correspondence \(B_i: S_{-i} \rightrightarrows S_i\); it may be multi-valued.








NoteDefinition 5.2.2 — Nash Equilibrium (Nash 1950)



A strategy profile \(s^* = (s_1^*, \ldots, s_n^*) \in S\) is a Nash equilibrium (NE) if, for every player \(i \in I\): \[s_i^* \in B_i(s_{-i}^*),\] or equivalently, \[u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*) \quad \text{for all } s_i \in S_i \text{ and all } i \in I.\] Each player is best-responding to the equilibrium strategies of all other players.








CautionRemark



A Nash equilibrium is a fixed point of the joint best-response correspondence \(B: S \rightrightarrows S\) defined by \(B(s) = \prod_{i \in I} B_i(s_{-i})\). The existence problem is precisely: does \(B\) have a fixed point?








NoteDefinition 5.2.3 — Mixed Extension



The mixed extension of \(\Gamma\) is the game \(\hat{\Gamma} = (I, (\Delta(S_i)), (\hat{u}_i))\) where \(\Delta(S_i)\) is the set of probability distributions over \(S_i\) and \[\hat{u}_i(\sigma) = \sum_{s \in S} \left(\prod_{j \in I} \sigma_j(s_j)\right) u_i(s)\] is the expected payoff under mixed strategy profile \(\sigma = (\sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_n)\).

title: 31  5.2 Nash Equilibrium
text:
32.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 5.2.1 — Nash Existence Theorem (Theorem 8.D.3 in MWG)



Every finite normal form game \(\Gamma = (I, (S_i)_{i \in I}, (u_i)_{i \in I})\) has at least one Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies.








TipProof





We apply Kakutani’s fixed point theorem (see 1.4 Fixed Point Theorems) to the joint best-response correspondence of the mixed extension.
Step 1: Define the domain. Let \(\Delta_i = \Delta(S_i)\) be the set of mixed strategies for player \(i\). Since \(S_i\) is finite with \(|S_i| = m_i\), \(\Delta_i\) is the standard \((m_i - 1)\)-simplex in \(\mathbb{R}^{m_i}\). The joint strategy space is \[\Delta = \prod_{i \in I} \Delta_i \subset \mathbb{R}^{\sum_i m_i}.\] \(\Delta\) is nonempty (contains the uniform distribution), compact (closed and bounded in finite-dimensional space), and convex (product of convex sets).
Step 2: Define the best-response correspondence. For each player \(i\), define \[\beta_i(\sigma_{-i}) = \arg\max_{\sigma_i \in \Delta_i} \hat{u}_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i}) \subseteq \Delta_i.\] Define the joint correspondence \(\beta: \Delta \rightrightarrows \Delta\) by \(\beta(\sigma) = \prod_{i \in I} \beta_i(\sigma_{-i})\).
Step 3: \(\beta\) has nonempty, convex values. Since \(S_i\) is finite, \(\hat{u}_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i})\) is linear (hence continuous) in \(\sigma_i\), and \(\Delta_i\) is compact. By the Weierstrass theorem, \(\beta_i(\sigma_{-i})\) is nonempty for every \(\sigma_{-i}\). Furthermore, \(\hat{u}_i\) is linear in \(\sigma_i\), so the set of maximizers is a convex subset of \(\Delta_i\) (it is an intersection of a linear function’s preimage with a simplex). Hence \(\beta_i(\sigma_{-i})\) is convex, and so is \(\beta(\sigma) = \prod_i \beta_i(\sigma_{-i})\).
Step 4: \(\beta\) is upper hemicontinuous (UHC). We use the closed graph characterization. Let \(\sigma^n \to \sigma\) in \(\Delta\) and \(\tau^n \to \tau\) with \(\tau^n \in \beta(\sigma^n)\) for all \(n\). We must show \(\tau \in \beta(\sigma)\).
Since \(\tau_i^n \in \beta_i(\sigma_{-i}^n)\), we have \(\hat{u}_i(\tau_i^n, \sigma_{-i}^n) \geq \hat{u}_i(\rho_i, \sigma_{-i}^n)\) for all \(\rho_i \in \Delta_i\).
Taking limits (using continuity of \(\hat{u}_i\), which is a multilinear polynomial, hence continuous): \[\hat{u}_i(\tau_i, \sigma_{-i}) \geq \hat{u}_i(\rho_i, \sigma_{-i}) \quad \forall \rho_i \in \Delta_i.\] Therefore \(\tau_i \in \beta_i(\sigma_{-i})\), and \(\tau \in \beta(\sigma)\). Thus \(\beta\) has a closed graph, which (since \(\Delta\) is compact) is equivalent to upper hemicontinuity.
Step 5: Apply Kakutani. The correspondence \(\beta: \Delta \rightrightarrows \Delta\) satisfies all conditions of Kakutani’s fixed point theorem: - \(\Delta\) is compact, convex, and nonempty - \(\beta\) has nonempty, convex values - \(\beta\) is UHC
Therefore there exists \(\sigma^* \in \Delta\) such that \(\sigma^* \in \beta(\sigma^*)\), i.e., \(\sigma_i^* \in \beta_i(\sigma_{-i}^*)\) for all \(i\). This is precisely a Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies. \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 5.2.2 — Dominant Strategy Equilibria and NE



If every player \(i\) has a strictly dominant strategy \(s_i^d\), then \(s^d = (s_1^d, \ldots, s_n^d)\) is the unique Nash equilibrium.








TipProof





First, \(s^d\) is a NE: \(s_i^d\) strictly dominates all \(s_i \neq s_i^d\), so in particular \(u_i(s_i^d, s_{-i}^d) > u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^d)\) for all \(s_i \neq s_i^d\). Therefore \(s_i^d \in B_i(s_{-i}^d)\) for all \(i\), making \(s^d\) a NE.
Second, uniqueness: suppose \(s^*\) is any NE. Then \(s_i^* \in B_i(s_{-i}^*)\), which requires \(u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*) \geq u_i(s_i, s_{-i}^*)\) for all \(s_i\). But \(s_i^d\) strictly dominates \(s_i^*\) (if \(s_i^* \neq s_i^d\)), yielding \(u_i(s_i^d, s_{-i}^*) > u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*)\), a contradiction. Therefore \(s_i^* = s_i^d\) for all \(i\). \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 5.2.3 — NE Survives IESDS



If \(s^*\) is a Nash equilibrium and \(S^\infty\) is the IESDS outcome, then \(s^* \in S^\infty\).








TipProof





We show by induction on the elimination round \(k\) that \(s_i^* \in S_i^k\) for all \(i\) and all \(k\).
Base case (\(k=0\)): \(S^0 = S\), so trivially \(s_i^* \in S_i^0\).
Inductive step: Suppose \(s_i^* \in S_i^{k-1}\) for all \(i\). Suppose for contradiction that \(s_i^*\) is eliminated at round \(k\), i.e., there exists \(s_i' \in S_i^{k-1}\) such that \(u_i(s_i', s_{-i}) > u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i})\) for all \(s_{-i} \in S_{-i}^{k-1}\).
By the inductive hypothesis, \(s_{-i}^* \in S_{-i}^{k-1}\). Therefore \(u_i(s_i', s_{-i}^*) > u_i(s_i^*, s_{-i}^*)\), contradicting \(s_i^* \in B_i(s_{-i}^*)\).
Therefore \(s_i^* \in S_i^k\) for all \(k\), so \(s^* \in S^\infty\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: 31  5.2 Nash Equilibrium
text:
32.4 Properties of Nash Equilibrium
Failure of uniqueness. NE need not be unique. Coordination games have two pure NE; Battle of the Sexes has two pure NE plus one mixed NE. Non-uniqueness is the rule, not the exception, in economics applications.
Failure of Pareto optimality. The Prisoner’s Dilemma has a unique NE that is Pareto dominated. This reflects the fundamental insight that individual rationality does not imply collective efficiency. Market failures (externalities, public goods) are essentially NE phenomena where the NE outcome fails the Pareto criterion.
NE as self-enforcing agreement. If players could meet before the game to agree on strategies, the only agreements that would actually be honored (without enforcement) are Nash equilibria: if \(s^*\) is not a NE, some player would prefer to deviate unilaterally.
NE as steady state of learning. If players repeatedly play the same game and adjust strategies toward best responses, any rest point of such adjustment dynamics must be a NE. This provides a dynamic justification for the static concept.
NE as consistent beliefs. A NE can be interpreted as a situation where each player’s belief about others’ play exactly matches actual play. The epistemic conditions for NE are: rationality plus mutual correct beliefs. This is strictly stronger than common knowledge of rationality (which gives only rationalizability).

title: 31  5.2 Nash Equilibrium
text:
32.5 Example: Computing All Nash Equilibria






NoteExample 5.2.1 — Complete NE Analysis of a 2×2 Game



Consider the Battle of the Sexes: \[\begin{array}{c|cc} & O & F \\ \hline O & 2,1 & 0,0 \\ F & 0,0 & 1,2 \end{array}\]
Pure strategy NE: - \((O,O)\): Player 1 gets 2, best response to \(O\) is \(O\) (since \(2>0\)). Player 2 gets 1, best response to \(O\) is \(O\) (since \(1>0\)). Both best-responding. NE. - \((F,F)\): Player 1 gets 1, best response to \(F\) is \(F\) (since \(1>0\)). Player 2 gets 2, best response to \(F\) is \(F\) (since \(2>0\)). NE. - \((O,F)\) and \((F,O)\): payoff 0 for both; either player can deviate to get positive payoff. Not NE.
Mixed strategy NE: Let \(\sigma_1 = (p, 1-p)\) and \(\sigma_2 = (q, 1-q)\) where \(p = \Pr_1(O)\) and \(q = \Pr_2(O)\).
For player 1 to mix, they must be indifferent: \[\hat{u}_1(O, \sigma_2) = 2q + 0(1-q) = 2q\] \[\hat{u}_1(F, \sigma_2) = 0 \cdot q + 1(1-q) = 1-q\] Indifference: \(2q = 1-q \Rightarrow q = 1/3\).
For player 2 to mix, they must be indifferent: \[\hat{u}_2(\sigma_1, O) = 1 \cdot p + 0 \cdot (1-p) = p\] \[\hat{u}_2(\sigma_1, F) = 0 \cdot p + 2(1-p) = 2-2p\] Indifference: \(p = 2-2p \Rightarrow p = 2/3\).
Mixed NE: \(\sigma_1^* = (2/3, 1/3)\), \(\sigma_2^* = (1/3, 2/3)\).
Expected payoffs at mixed NE: \(\hat{u}_1 = 2/3\), \(\hat{u}_2 = 2/3\). Both players are worse off than at either pure NE — a characteristic feature of mixed NE in coordination-type games.

title: 31  5.2 Nash Equilibrium
text:
32.6 Cross-References

5.1 Normal Form Games — Rationalizability, IESDS, dominance; every NE is rationalizable
5.3 Mixed Strategies — Mixed strategy NE; indifference condition; purification
5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium — SPE refines NE for extensive form games
5.7 Bayesian Games — BNE generalizes NE to incomplete information
1.4 Fixed Point Theorems — Kakutani’s theorem is the mathematical core of Nash existence

title: 32  5.3 Mixed Strategies
text: 33 5.3 Mixed Strategies

title: 32  5.3 Mixed Strategies
text:
33.1 Context
A mixed strategy is a probability distribution over pure strategies. While players in most economic applications are modeled as choosing pure strategies, mixed strategies play three important roles: (1) they guarantee existence of Nash equilibrium in finite games (Nash’s theorem); (2) they arise naturally in zero-sum games where randomization prevents exploitation; and (3) via the purification theorem (Harsanyi 1973), they can be reinterpreted as pure strategy behavior in a world with slight private information. The correlated equilibrium (Aumann 1974) is a further generalization that allows players to correlate their randomization via a public signal and can achieve payoffs outside the convex hull of Nash payoffs.

title: 32  5.3 Mixed Strategies
text:
33.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 5.3.1 — Mixed Strategy



A mixed strategy for player \(i\) is a probability distribution \(\sigma_i \in \Delta(S_i)\) over the finite set \(S_i\). Formally, \(\sigma_i: S_i \to [0,1]\) with \(\sum_{s_i \in S_i} \sigma_i(s_i) = 1\). A pure strategy is a degenerate mixed strategy placing probability one on a single action.
The set of mixed strategies \(\Delta(S_i)\) is the \((|S_i|-1)\)-dimensional simplex.








NoteDefinition 5.3.2 — Expected Payoff



Given a mixed strategy profile \(\sigma = (\sigma_1, \ldots, \sigma_n)\), the expected payoff of player \(i\) is \[\hat{u}_i(\sigma) = \sum_{s \in S} \left(\prod_{j=1}^n \sigma_j(s_j)\right) u_i(s),\] where the product form assumes players randomize independently.








NoteDefinition 5.3.3 — Support of a Mixed Strategy



The support of mixed strategy \(\sigma_i\) is \[\text{supp}(\sigma_i) = \{s_i \in S_i : \sigma_i(s_i) > 0\}.\] A pure strategy \(s_i\) is in the support of \(\sigma_i\) if and only if it is played with strictly positive probability.








NoteDefinition 5.3.4 — Mixed Strategy Nash Equilibrium



A mixed strategy profile \(\sigma^* = (\sigma_1^*, \ldots, \sigma_n^*) \in \prod_i \Delta(S_i)\) is a mixed strategy Nash equilibrium if for every player \(i\) and every \(\sigma_i \in \Delta(S_i)\): \[\hat{u}_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*) \geq \hat{u}_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i}^*).\]








NoteDefinition 5.3.5 — Correlated Equilibrium (Aumann 1974)



A correlated equilibrium is a probability distribution \(\mu \in \Delta(S)\) over strategy profiles such that, for every player \(i\) and every \(s_i, s_i' \in S_i\): \[\sum_{s_{-i} \in S_{-i}} \mu(s_i, s_{-i}) [u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) - u_i(s_i', s_{-i})] \geq 0.\] Equivalently: if a mediator draws \(s \sim \mu\) and tells player \(i\) only their component \(s_i\), no player wishes to deviate unilaterally after receiving their recommendation.

title: 32  5.3 Mixed Strategies
text:
33.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 5.3.1 — Indifference Condition (Support Characterization)



In any mixed strategy Nash equilibrium \(\sigma^*\), every pure strategy \(s_i\) in the support of \(\sigma_i^*\) yields the same expected payoff: \[s_i \in \text{supp}(\sigma_i^*) \implies \hat{u}_i(s_i, \sigma_{-i}^*) = \hat{u}_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*).\] Moreover, every pure strategy \(s_i \notin \text{supp}(\sigma_i^*)\) satisfies \(\hat{u}_i(s_i, \sigma_{-i}^*) \leq \hat{u}_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*)\).








TipProof





Since \(\hat{u}_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i}^*)\) is linear in \(\sigma_i\), the expected payoff of playing \(\sigma_i^*\) is a weighted average: \[\hat{u}_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*) = \sum_{s_i \in S_i} \sigma_i^*(s_i) \hat{u}_i(s_i, \sigma_{-i}^*).\]
Claim: For any \(s_i \in \text{supp}(\sigma_i^*)\), we have \(\hat{u}_i(s_i, \sigma_{-i}^*) = \hat{u}_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*)\).
Suppose for contradiction that some \(s_i^0 \in \text{supp}(\sigma_i^*)\) with \(\sigma_i^*(s_i^0) > 0\) satisfies \[\hat{u}_i(s_i^0, \sigma_{-i}^*) < \hat{u}_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*).\]
Since \(\sigma_i^*\) is optimal for player \(i\), and \(\hat{u}_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*)\) is an average over strategies in the support, there must exist some \(s_i^1 \in \text{supp}(\sigma_i^*)\) with \(\hat{u}_i(s_i^1, \sigma_{-i}^*) > \hat{u}_i(s_i^0, \sigma_{-i}^*)\). But then player \(i\) could shift all probability from \(s_i^0\) to \(s_i^1\), achieving expected payoff strictly greater than \(\hat{u}_i(\sigma_i^*, \sigma_{-i}^*)\), contradicting \(\sigma_i^*\) being a best response. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



The indifference condition is the key computational tool for finding mixed NE. To find \(\sigma_{-i}^*\) that makes player \(i\) willing to mix over \(\text{supp}(\sigma_i^*)\), one sets up the system of equations: \[\hat{u}_i(s_i, \sigma_{-i}^*) = \hat{u}_i(s_i', \sigma_{-i}^*) \quad \forall s_i, s_i' \in \text{supp}(\sigma_i^*).\] The mixing probabilities of player \(j \neq i\) are chosen to make player \(i\) indifferent — a counterintuitive feature: each player’s mixing probabilities are determined by others’ indifference conditions, not by their own indifference.








ImportantTheorem 5.3.2 — Purification Theorem (Harsanyi 1973)



Let \(\Gamma\) be a finite normal form game and \(\sigma^*\) a mixed Nash equilibrium. For any \(\epsilon > 0\), there exists a perturbation \(\Gamma^\epsilon\) of \(\Gamma\) in which each player \(i\) has private payoff information (a “type” that perturbs \(u_i\) by at most \(\epsilon\)) and a pure strategy Bayesian Nash equilibrium \(s^{**}\) of \(\Gamma^\epsilon\) whose induced distribution over strategy profiles is within \(\epsilon\) of \(\sigma^*\).








TipProof





Proof sketch. Augment each player’s payoff with an independent private shock: \(\tilde{u}_i(s, t_i) = u_i(s) + \epsilon \cdot t_i(s_i)\), where \(t_i(s_i)\) are i.i.d. draws from a continuous distribution (e.g., Uniform \([0,1]\)). Each player’s type is the realization of their shocks across all pure strategies.
In the perturbed game, a player with type \(t_i\) plays pure strategy \(s_i\) if \(\hat{u}_i(s_i, \sigma_{-i}) + \epsilon t_i(s_i) > \hat{u}_i(s_i', \sigma_{-i}) + \epsilon t_i(s_i')\) for all \(s_i' \neq s_i\).
As \(\epsilon \to 0\), the threshold for each player approaches the indifference conditions of the original mixed NE. By continuity of the induced distributions, the marginal probability that player \(i\) plays \(s_i\) in the pure strategy BNE of \(\Gamma^\epsilon\) converges to \(\sigma_i^*(s_i)\).
The formal argument uses the intermediate value theorem applied to the best-response probability as a function of \(\epsilon\); see Harsanyi (1973) for the complete proof. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Economic interpretation of purification. The purification theorem justifies mixed NE as an approximation of pure behavior under private information. When players know their own payoffs perfectly but observe others’ types only through their actions, the induced behavior mimics a mixed NE. This is the Bayesian foundation for mixing: players are not actually randomizing — they are making deterministic decisions based on their private information, but from the perspective of opponents, the behavior looks random.








ImportantTheorem 5.3.3 — Properties of Correlated Equilibrium




Every Nash equilibrium (pure or mixed) is a correlated equilibrium.
The set of correlated equilibria is convex and compact (as a subset of \(\Delta(S)\)).
A correlated equilibrium can strictly Pareto dominate all Nash equilibria.









TipProof





Part 1. Given a mixed NE \(\sigma^* = (\sigma_1^*, \ldots, \sigma_n^*)\), define \(\mu = \prod_i \sigma_i^*\) (the product distribution). For any \(s_i, s_i'\): \[\sum_{s_{-i}} \mu(s_i, s_{-i}) [u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) - u_i(s_i', s_{-i})] = \sigma_i^*(s_i) \sum_{s_{-i}} \prod_{j \neq i}\sigma_j^*(s_j) [u_i(s_i, s_{-i}) - u_i(s_i', s_{-i})].\] Since \(s_i^*\) is a best response to \(\sigma_{-i}^*\), this sum is \(\geq 0\). Hence \(\mu\) is a CE.
Part 2. The CE conditions are a finite system of linear inequalities in \(\mu \in \Delta(S)\). Therefore the CE set is the intersection of a simplex with half-spaces — a convex polytope.
Part 3. Consider the Battle of the Sexes. The two pure NE yield payoffs \((2,1)\) and \((1,2)\); the mixed NE yields \((2/3, 2/3)\). Consider the correlated strategy \(\mu(O,O) = \mu(F,F) = 1/2\). This is a CE: given recommendation \(O\), player 1 gets expected payoff \(2 > 0\) from following vs. deviating; given recommendation \(F\), player 1 gets \(1 > 0\) from following vs. deviating. Expected payoffs are \((3/2, 3/2)\), which strictly Pareto dominates the mixed NE payoff \((2/3, 2/3)\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: 32  5.3 Mixed Strategies
text:
33.4 Computing Mixed Nash Equilibria






NoteExample 5.3.1 — Mixed NE in a Zero-Sum Game (Matching Pennies)



\[\begin{array}{c|cc} & H & T \\ \hline H & 1,-1 & -1,1 \\ T & -1,1 & 1,-1 \end{array}\]
Let \(\sigma_1 = (p, 1-p)\) and \(\sigma_2 = (q, 1-q)\).
Player 1’s indifference (player 2 must make player 1 indifferent): \[\hat{u}_1(H, \sigma_2) = q - (1-q) = 2q - 1\] \[\hat{u}_1(T, \sigma_2) = -q + (1-q) = 1 - 2q\] Setting equal: \(2q - 1 = 1 - 2q \Rightarrow q = 1/2\).
Player 2’s indifference (player 1 must make player 2 indifferent): \[\hat{u}_2(\sigma_1, H) = -p + (1-p) = 1 - 2p\] \[\hat{u}_2(\sigma_1, T) = p - (1-p) = 2p - 1\] Setting equal: \(1 - 2p = 2p - 1 \Rightarrow p = 1/2\).
Unique NE: \(\sigma^* = ((1/2, 1/2), (1/2, 1/2))\). Expected payoffs: \((0,0)\). This is the value of the zero-sum game by the minimax theorem.








NoteExample 5.3.2 — Correlated Equilibrium Pareto Dominating Mixed NE



In the Battle of the Sexes (payoff matrix as before), define the correlated distribution: \[\mu(O,O) = \frac{1}{2}, \quad \mu(F,F) = \frac{1}{2}, \quad \mu(O,F) = \mu(F,O) = 0.\]
Verification that \(\mu\) is a CE: - If player 1 is told \(O\): deviation to \(F\) gives expected payoff $0 = 0 < 2 = $ payoff from following. No incentive to deviate. - If player 1 is told \(F\): deviation to \(O\) gives expected payoff \(0 < 1\). No incentive to deviate. - Analogous conditions hold for player 2.
Payoffs: \(E[\hat{u}_1] = \frac{1}{2}(2) + \frac{1}{2}(1) = \frac{3}{2}\); \(E[\hat{u}_2] = \frac{1}{2}(1) + \frac{1}{2}(2) = \frac{3}{2}\).
Compare to the mixed NE payoff of \((2/3, 2/3)\) — the correlated equilibrium achieves \((3/2, 3/2)\), strictly Pareto superior.

title: 32  5.3 Mixed Strategies
text:
33.5 Economic Intuition
Why players mix in equilibrium. A player has an incentive to mix if and only if they are indifferent between the strategies in their support. This means that in a mixed NE, each player’s mixing probabilities are set to make other players indifferent, not themselves. This is a deep and somewhat paradoxical feature: player 2’s mixing in Matching Pennies prevents player 1 from exploiting predictability, but player 2 is mixing because player 1’s mix makes them indifferent.
Mixed NE and unpredictability. In competitive or adversarial settings (zero-sum games, inspection problems, penalty kicks in soccer, tax auditing), mixed strategies represent optimal randomization to prevent exploitation. The value of the zero-sum game is determined precisely by the mixed NE, and represents what each player can guarantee regardless of opponent behavior.
Correlated equilibrium and mediation. The CE concept shows that a trustworthy mediator who makes private recommendations can improve on Nash equilibrium outcomes. The mediator does not need to enforce actions — only to make recommendations that are self-enforcing (each player prefers to follow given the recommendation). This is the basis for mechanism design applications where a principal structures information revelation.

title: 33  5.4 Extensive Form Games
text: 34 5.4 Extensive Form Games

title: 33  5.4 Extensive Form Games
text:
34.1 Context
The extensive form is the natural representation of games where players move sequentially, may have incomplete information about past actions, or must make decisions at multiple stages. Unlike the normal form, it makes explicit the temporal structure of play, the information available to each player at each decision point, and the sequentiality of choices. The distinction between strategies and actions — central to extensive form analysis — is conceptually subtle but critical: a strategy must specify behavior at every possible information set, including those that may never be reached on the equilibrium path. This apparently minor point drives the entire theory of equilibrium refinements, from subgame perfection to sequential equilibrium.

title: 33  5.4 Extensive Form Games
text:
34.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 5.4.1 — Extensive Form Game



An extensive form game \(\Gamma^E\) consists of: - A finite game tree \(T\): a directed tree with a distinguished root node \(\emptyset\), where each non-terminal node has one or more successors - A partition of non-terminal nodes into player sets \(\{H_i\}_{i \in I \cup \{0\}}\), where \(H_0\) are nodes controlled by Nature (chance moves) - At each node \(x \in H_i\), a action set \(A(x)\), with each branch from \(x\) labeled by an element of \(A(x)\) - A collection of information sets \(\mathcal{H}_i = \{h_{i,k}\}\) for each player \(i\), forming a partition of \(H_i\), with the requirement that \(A(x) = A(x')\) for all \(x, x' \in h_{i,k}\) (same actions available at indistinguishable nodes) - A payoff function \(u_i: Z \to \mathbb{R}\) for each \(i\), where \(Z\) is the set of terminal nodes - For each Nature node, a probability distribution over branches








NoteDefinition 5.4.2 — Information Sets and Perfect Information



An information set \(h\) for player \(i\) is a set of nodes in \(H_i\) such that player \(i\) cannot distinguish between nodes within \(h\). The collection \(\mathcal{H}_i\) partitions \(H_i\).
A game has perfect information if every information set is a singleton: \(|h| = 1\) for all \(h \in \bigcup_i \mathcal{H}_i\). In a game of perfect information, every player knows the complete history of play at every decision point.
A game has imperfect information if some information set contains more than one node — the player cannot distinguish between the histories in that information set when making their decision.








NoteDefinition 5.4.3 — Strategy in Extensive Form



A pure strategy for player \(i\) is a function \(s_i: \mathcal{H}_i \to \bigcup_h A(h)\) such that \(s_i(h) \in A(h)\) for every information set \(h \in \mathcal{H}_i\).
A strategy specifies an action at every information set of player \(i\), including information sets that may not be reached given \(s_i\)’s own choices at earlier information sets.








CautionRemark



The distinction between strategies and actions is fundamental. An action is a choice at a particular point in the game. A strategy is a complete contingent plan specifying what to do at every information set that could possibly be reached, whether or not the player’s own prior choices would lead there. This completeness is essential for defining equilibria where behavior off the equilibrium path matters (e.g., SPE requires credible threats at unreached subgames).
In a game where player 1 moves first (choice \(\{L, R\}\)) and player 2 observes and responds at two separate nodes (one after \(L\), one after \(R\)), player 2 has \(2 \times 2 = 4\) strategies despite only one action being taken on any given play.








NoteDefinition 5.4.4 — Behavioral Strategy



A behavioral strategy for player \(i\) is a function \(b_i: \mathcal{H}_i \to \Delta(A(h))\) that assigns an independent probability distribution over actions to each information set \(h \in \mathcal{H}_i\).
This contrasts with a mixed strategy \(\sigma_i \in \Delta(S_i)\), which randomizes over the entire pure strategy space. Under a mixed strategy, the correlations across different information sets are determined by the mixing over pure strategies.








NoteDefinition 5.4.5 — Perfect Recall



Player \(i\) has perfect recall if they never forget information they previously knew and never forget their own past actions. Formally, for any two nodes \(x, x' \in h\) in the same information set of player \(i\), the sequence of information sets and actions of player \(i\) leading to \(x\) must equal the sequence leading to \(x'\).

title: 33  5.4 Extensive Form Games
text:
34.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 5.4.1 — Kuhn’s Theorem (1953)



In a finite extensive form game with perfect recall, every mixed strategy \(\sigma_i\) is outcome-equivalent to a behavioral strategy \(b_i\): for every strategy profile of other players \(\sigma_{-i}\), the probability distribution over terminal nodes induced by \((\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i})\) equals that induced by \((b_i, \sigma_{-i})\).








TipProof





Proof sketch. We construct \(b_i\) from \(\sigma_i\) explicitly.
For each information set \(h \in \mathcal{H}_i\) and action \(a \in A(h)\), define \[b_i(a|h) = \frac{\Pr[a \text{ is taken at } h \text{ and } h \text{ is reached} \mid \sigma_i]}{\Pr[h \text{ is reached} \mid \sigma_i]},\] whenever \(\Pr[h \text{ is reached} \mid \sigma_i] > 0\).
Perfect recall is used here: Since player \(i\) has perfect recall, reaching information set \(h\) is consistent with a unique path of player \(i\)’s own past actions. Therefore, conditioning on reaching \(h\) does not create spurious correlations between actions at different information sets — the event “reach \(h\) and take \(a\)” factors cleanly.
Without perfect recall, conditioning on reaching \(h\) may depend on choices at earlier information sets, making the factorization impossible (Isbell’s counterexample).
To show outcome equivalence: the probability of any terminal node \(z\) can be computed by multiplying the probabilities along the path from the root to \(z\). Under \(\sigma_i\), the contribution from player \(i\)’s moves is \(\Pr_{\sigma_i}[\text{path to } z]\). Under the constructed \(b_i\), the same probability is obtained by multiplying the behavioral probabilities at each information set along the path — which by construction equals the conditional probability under \(\sigma_i\). By the chain rule of probability, the overall probability of \(z\) is the same. \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 5.4.2 — Zermelo’s Theorem (1913)



In any finite, two-player, zero-sum game of perfect information, one of the following holds: 1. Player 1 has a winning strategy (guarantees a win regardless of Player 2’s play), or 2. Player 2 has a winning strategy, or 3. Both players have a drawing strategy (each can guarantee at least a draw).








TipProof





We prove by backward induction on the game tree.
Setup. Since the game is finite, the tree has finitely many nodes. Assign “values” to terminal nodes: \(+1\) if Player 1 wins, \(-1\) if Player 2 wins, \(0\) for a draw.
Backward induction procedure. For each non-terminal node \(x\) at depth \(d\) (counting from the leaves):

Base case (\(d = 0\)): Terminal nodes \(z \in Z\) have assigned values \(v(z) \in \{-1, 0, +1\}\).
Inductive step. Suppose all nodes at depth \(< d\) have been assigned values. Consider node \(x\) at depth \(d\):

If \(x\) belongs to Player 1: \(v(x) = \max_{a \in A(x)} v(x_a)\), where \(x_a\) is the successor after action \(a\). Player 1 chooses the action maximizing value.
If \(x\) belongs to Player 2: \(v(x) = \min_{a \in A(x)} v(x_a)\). Player 2 minimizes.


Since the action sets are finite and all successor values are defined by hypothesis, \(v(x)\) is well-defined.
The strategies. Define each player’s strategy: at each node \(x\) they control, play the action achieving \(v(x)\). The resulting strategy profile is subgame perfect.
The outcome. The value \(v(\emptyset)\) of the root node is \(+1\), \(-1\), or \(0\): - \(v(\emptyset) = +1\): Player 1’s backward induction strategy guarantees a win. - \(v(\emptyset) = -1\): Player 2’s backward induction strategy guarantees a win. - \(v(\emptyset) = 0\): Each player’s backward induction strategy guarantees at least a draw.
The strategies are well-defined because perfect information ensures every information set is a singleton — there is no ambiguity about which node is reached, so strategy selection at every node is unambiguous.
This establishes the existence of the three-way trichotomy claimed by the theorem. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Zermelo’s theorem applies to chess, checkers, and Go: each game has a determined outcome under optimal play. However, the theorem is non-constructive — it does not tell us which player wins chess, only that the game is determined. The practical computational challenge of finding the optimal strategy is entirely separate from the existence proof.

title: 33  5.4 Extensive Form Games
text:
34.4 Examples






NoteExample 5.4.1 — The Centipede Game



Players 1 and 2 alternate moving for \(T\) rounds. At each round \(t\), the current player can either: - Stop (\(S\)): game ends; payoffs determined by the current “pot” - Continue (\(C\)): pot grows; other player moves next
Payoffs at round \(t\) (if stopped): mover gets \(2^t\), other gets \(2^{t-1}\). At round \(T\), both get \(2^T\) if player 2 continues.
Backward induction: At round \(T\), Player 2 stops (gets \(2^T > 2^{T-1}\)). Anticipating this, at round \(T-1\), Player 1 stops (gets $2^{T-1} > $ payoff from continuing given Player 2 will stop). Iterating back to round 1, Player 1 stops immediately.
Paradox: The backward induction equilibrium gives Player 1 payoff \(2\) and Player 2 payoff \(1\), whereas continuing to round \(T\) gives both payoffs of order \(2^T\). This extreme inefficiency challenges the rationality assumptions underlying backward induction — it requires Player 1 to believe Player 2 is rational, to believe Player 2 believes Player 1 is rational, and so on, even as player 1 contemplates deviating in round 1 from what appears to be the rational plan.








NoteExample 5.4.2 — Stackelberg Game as Extensive Form



Player 1 (leader) chooses output \(q_1 \geq 0\); Player 2 (follower) observes \(q_1\) and chooses \(q_2 \geq 0\).
Information sets: Player 2 has one information set for each value of \(q_1\) (perfect information — a continuum of information sets). Player 2’s strategy is a function \(s_2: \mathbb{R}_+ \to \mathbb{R}_+\) mapping observed \(q_1\) to response \(q_2\).
Normal form reduction: Player 1 has strategies \(q_1 \in \mathbb{R}_+\); Player 2 has strategies that are functions \(s_2(\cdot)\). The Nash equilibrium concept applies to the normal form but does not eliminate non-credible threats — SPE is needed.
The SPE outcome (backward induction): Player 2 best-responds \(q_2^*(q_1)\) for each \(q_1\); Player 1 anticipates this and chooses \(q_1^*\) to maximize profit given the function \(q_2^*(q_1)\). The SPE involves a first-mover advantage, yielding higher profit to Player 1 than the Cournot (simultaneous) NE.

title: 33  5.4 Extensive Form Games
text:
34.5 Economic Intuition
Why strategies, not just actions? In applying equilibrium concepts to extensive form games, we must specify what players would do at every contingency, even those not reached in equilibrium. This is because the credibility of threats and promises depends on what a player would actually do off the equilibrium path. A monopolist threatening to fight entry is a threat about a contingency (entry occurs) that may not happen in equilibrium — but the would-be entrant must assess whether the threat is credible. This requires knowing the monopolist’s strategy (complete contingent plan), not just their equilibrium action (no fight because entry doesn’t happen).
Information sets and strategic complexity. The size of the strategy space grows exponentially in the number of information sets: if player \(i\) has \(K\) information sets and \(m\) actions at each, there are \(m^K\) pure strategies. This is why extensive form games are typically analyzed using behavioral strategies (specification of mixing at each information set), which grow only linearly in \(K\).

title: 33  5.4 Extensive Form Games
text:

5.6 Repeated Games — Repeated games are a special extensive form with independent stage games

title: 34  5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium
text: 35 5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium

title: 34  5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium
text:
35.1 Context
Nash equilibrium in extensive form games permits equilibria sustained by non-credible threats — strategies that specify suboptimal actions at information sets never reached in equilibrium. Subgame perfect equilibrium (SPE), introduced by Selten (1965), eliminates such equilibria by requiring that the strategy profile induces a Nash equilibrium not just in the full game but in every subgame. The key result justifying SPE is the one-deviation principle: checking SPE reduces to checking whether any single unilateral deviation at any single information set is profitable. This dramatic simplification — from checking all possible deviations to checking one-shot deviations — makes backward induction tractable even in complex games.

title: 34  5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium
text:
35.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 5.5.1 — Subgame



A subgame \(\Gamma(x)\) of extensive form game \(\Gamma^E\) starting at node \(x\) consists of: - The subtree rooted at \(x\): all nodes \(y\) that are successors of \(x\) in the game tree, plus \(x\) itself - All information sets and actions restricted to this subtree - The condition that no information set of the original game straddles the boundary: if node \(y\) is in \(\Gamma(x)\) and \(y'\) is in the same information set as \(y\) in \(\Gamma^E\), then \(y'\) is also in \(\Gamma(x)\)
The full game \(\Gamma^E\) is a subgame of itself (the subgame rooted at the root node). Proper subgames exclude the full game.








CautionRemark



The requirement that information sets not straddle the boundary is crucial. It ensures that a subgame is self-contained: a player who reaches a node in the subgame knows they are in the subgame, and their information set structure within the subgame is consistent with the original game. In a game of perfect information, every node initiates a subgame. In games with imperfect information, subgames can only start at nodes that are singletons in their information set partition.








NoteDefinition 5.5.2 — Subgame Perfect Equilibrium (Selten 1965)



A strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) in an extensive form game \(\Gamma^E\) is a subgame perfect equilibrium (SPE) if it induces a Nash equilibrium in every subgame of \(\Gamma^E\).
Equivalently, for every subgame \(\Gamma(x)\), the restriction \(\sigma^*|_{\Gamma(x)}\) is a Nash equilibrium of \(\Gamma(x)\).








NoteDefinition 5.5.3 — One-Shot Deviation



A strategy \(\sigma_i'\) is a one-shot deviation from \(\sigma_i^*\) at information set \(h\) if \(\sigma_i'\) and \(\sigma_i^*\) agree at all information sets except \(h\): \[\sigma_i'(h') = \sigma_i^*(h') \quad \forall h' \in \mathcal{H}_i,\; h' \neq h.\]

title: 34  5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium
text:
35.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 5.5.1 — One-Deviation Principle



In a finite extensive form game, \(\sigma^*\) is a subgame perfect equilibrium if and only if there is no player \(i\) and no information set \(h \in \mathcal{H}_i\) such that player \(i\) can strictly increase their payoff in the subgame beginning at \(h\) by making a one-shot deviation from \(\sigma^*\) at \(h\), holding \(\sigma^*\) fixed everywhere else.








TipProof





(Only if) If \(\sigma^*\) is an SPE, then it induces a NE in every subgame. A fortiori, no one-shot deviation is profitable — a one-shot deviation is a special case of a deviation strategy.
(If) Suppose no profitable one-shot deviation exists but \(\sigma^*\) is not an SPE. Then there exists a subgame \(\Gamma(x)\) and a player \(i\) with a strategy \(\tilde{\sigma}_i\) that improves \(i\)’s payoff in \(\Gamma(x)\) over \(\sigma_i^*\).
Let \(\tilde{\sigma}_i\) differ from \(\sigma_i^*\) at a finite set of information sets \(\{h_1, h_2, \ldots, h_K\}\), ordered by depth in the game tree (deepest first): \(h_K\) is the deepest.
Step 1. Consider modifying \(\tilde{\sigma}_i\) to agree with \(\sigma_i^*\) at \(h_K\) and all deeper information sets, keeping \(\tilde{\sigma}_i\) elsewhere. Call this \(\tilde{\sigma}_i^{(K-1)}\).
Since \(h_K\) is the deepest deviation, in the subgame \(\Gamma(h_K)\), \(\tilde{\sigma}_i\) and \(\sigma_i^*\) agree everywhere except at \(h_K\) itself. Therefore switching from \(\tilde{\sigma}_i\) to \(\tilde{\sigma}_i^{(K-1)}\) constitutes a single-deviation at \(h_K\) within \(\Gamma(h_K)\).
If \(\tilde{\sigma}_i^{(K-1)}\) gives strictly lower payoff in \(\Gamma(h_K)\) than \(\tilde{\sigma}_i\), then \(\tilde{\sigma}_i\) improves over \(\sigma_i^*\) at \(h_K\) (a one-shot profitable deviation from \(\sigma^*\)), contradicting our assumption.
If \(\tilde{\sigma}_i^{(K-1)}\) gives the same payoff in \(\Gamma(h_K)\) as \(\tilde{\sigma}_i\), then \(\tilde{\sigma}_i^{(K-1)}\) also improves over \(\sigma_i^*\) in \(\Gamma(x)\).
Step 2. Repeat the argument for \(h_{K-1}, h_{K-2}, \ldots\) in sequence. At each step, either we find a profitable one-shot deviation (contradiction) or we reduce the number of deviations while preserving the improvement.
After \(K\) steps, we obtain \(\sigma_i^*\) itself (agreeing with \(\tilde{\sigma}_i\) at no information set), yet still improving over \(\sigma_i^*\) in \(\Gamma(x)\) — a contradiction.
Therefore, if no one-shot deviation is profitable, \(\sigma^*\) is an SPE. \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 5.5.2 — Backward Induction Produces SPE



In any finite game of perfect information, the backward induction strategy profile is a subgame perfect equilibrium.








TipProof





We show the backward induction profile \(\sigma^{BI}\) satisfies the one-deviation principle.
By construction of backward induction, at every node \(x\) (which is its own information set in perfect information), the action assigned by \(\sigma^{BI}\) maximizes player \(i(x)\)’s continuation payoff given \(\sigma^{BI}\) at all subsequent nodes.
Therefore, at any node \(x\): conditional on reaching \(x\), the action \(\sigma^{BI}(x)\) achieves \[v(\sigma^{BI}(x), x) = \max_{a \in A(x)} v(a, x \mid \sigma^{BI}_{-x}),\] where \(v(a, x \mid \sigma^{BI}_{-x})\) is the continuation value of action \(a\) followed by \(\sigma^{BI}\) at all subsequent nodes.
A one-shot deviation at \(x\) changes only \(\sigma^{BI}(x)\) to some \(a' \neq \sigma^{BI}(x)\), achieving \(v(a', x \mid \sigma^{BI}_{-x}) \leq v(\sigma^{BI}(x), x)\). So no profitable one-shot deviation exists.
By Theorem 5.5.1, \(\sigma^{BI}\) is an SPE. \(\blacksquare\)

title: 34  5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium
section: 35.4 SPE vs. NE: Eliminating Non-Credible Threats
text:
35.4 SPE vs. NE: Eliminating Non-Credible Threats






NoteExample 5.5.1 — Entry Deterrence Game



Players: Incumbent (I) and Entrant (E). Sequence: E first chooses In or Out. If In, I chooses Fight or Accommodate. Payoffs: - (Out): E gets \(0\), I gets \(2\) - (In, Accommodate): E gets \(1\), I gets \(1\) - (In, Fight): E gets \(-1\), I gets \(-1\)
Nash equilibria of the normal form: - NE 1: E plays Out; I’s strategy is “Fight if In.” E’s payoff from Out is \(0\), from In is \(-1\) (since I fights). Neither wants to deviate. NE, but I’s threat is non-credible. - NE 2: E plays In; I’s strategy is “Accommodate if In.” If E enters, I gets \(1 > -1\) from fighting. E gets \(1 > 0\) from entering. NE and SPE.
SPE analysis: The subgame after E plays In has I choosing between Fight (\(-1\)) and Accommodate (\(1\)). I prefers Accommodate — backward induction assigns Accommodate in this subgame.
Anticipating I will accommodate, E prefers In (\(1 > 0\)). The unique SPE is: E plays In; I plays Accommodate. The NE with “Fight” is eliminated because the threat is non-credible.

title: 34  5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium
text:
35.5 Limitations of Backward Induction
Chain-store paradox (Selten 1978). A monopolist faces 20 sequential potential entrants. In each market, if entry occurs the monopolist can fight (costly) or accommodate. With backward induction: in market 20, fight is suboptimal; entrant 20 enters. In market 19, fight is suboptimal (no future reputation benefit); entrant 19 enters. Iterating backward, the unique SPE has accommodation in every market and entry by all entrants. Yet in practice, firms may fight early entrants to deter later ones. The paradox reveals a tension between backward induction’s rationality assumptions and reputation-building behavior.
Rationality concerns in long centipede games. In a 100-round centipede game, backward induction predicts immediate stopping — but this relies on common knowledge of rationality up to 100 levels deep. If Player 1 deviates to Continue in round 1, Player 2 must wonder: is Player 1 irrational, or does Player 1 believe Player 2 is irrational? The backward induction prediction depends on rationality assumptions that become implausible in long games.
Incomplete information. Backward induction is well-defined only in perfect information games. With information sets containing multiple nodes, the concept of “which subgame am I in” becomes ambiguous, requiring sequential equilibrium or perfect Bayesian equilibrium.

title: 34  5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium
text:
35.6 Weak Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium (Preview)






NoteDefinition 5.5.4 — Weak Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium



A weak perfect Bayesian equilibrium (weak PBE) consists of a strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) and a system of beliefs \(\mu\) (probability distributions over nodes within each information set) such that: 1. Sequentially rational: At every information set \(h\), player \(i(h)\)’s strategy maximizes expected payoff given \(\mu(h)\) and \(\sigma_{-i}^*\) at subsequent nodes. 2. Consistent beliefs on path: At every information set \(h\) reached with positive probability under \(\sigma^*\), \(\mu(h)\) is derived from \(\sigma^*\) via Bayes’ rule.
Off-path information sets may have arbitrary beliefs in a weak PBE.


This previews the need for stronger concepts (sequential equilibrium, perfect Bayesian equilibrium) in games where beliefs at off-path information sets matter for the equilibrium outcome. In many signaling games and mechanism design applications, the off-path beliefs are the critical object that pins down the equilibrium.

title: 34  5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium
text:
35.7 Cross-References

5.2 Nash Equilibrium — NE is the base concept; SPE refines it
5.4 Extensive Form Games — Game tree, subgame definition, backward induction
5.6 Repeated Games — SPE is the relevant equilibrium concept for repeated games
5.7 Bayesian Games — Weak PBE extends SPE to incomplete information

title: 35  5.6 Repeated Games
text: 36 5.6 Repeated Games

title: 35  5.6 Repeated Games
text:
36.1 Context
Repeated games model ongoing relationships in which the same stage game is played over and over. The crucial insight — formalized in the Folk Theorem — is that patient players can sustain cooperative outcomes that are not equilibria of the stage game, by threatening to punish deviations in future periods. This captures cartel stability, international trade agreements, social norms, bilateral relationships, and community enforcement. The Folk Theorem comes in two versions: Friedman (1971) uses Nash threats; Fudenberg and Maskin (1986) use minmax threats and characterize the full set of equilibrium payoffs as players approach infinite patience.

title: 35  5.6 Repeated Games
text:
36.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 5.6.1 — Repeated Game (Supergame)



Let \(G = (I, (A_i)_{i \in I}, (u_i)_{i \in I})\) be a finite stage game. The repeated game (or supergame) \(G^\infty(\delta)\) is the extensive form game in which: - \(G\) is played at periods \(t = 0, 1, 2, \ldots\) - At each \(t\), all players observe the full history of past actions \(h^t = (a^0, a^1, \ldots, a^{t-1})\) - Each player \(i\) maximizes the discounted sum of stage payoffs: \[U_i = (1 - \delta) \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \delta^t u_i(a^t), \quad \delta \in (0, 1),\] where the factor \((1-\delta)\) normalizes payoffs to be in the same units as stage payoffs.








CautionRemark



The normalization \((1-\delta) \sum_{t=0}^\infty \delta^t u_i(a^t)\) ensures that the repeated game payoff from constant action profile \(a\) equals the stage payoff \(u_i(a)\). This makes it meaningful to compare repeated game equilibrium payoffs to stage game payoffs.








NoteDefinition 5.6.2 — Grim Trigger Strategy



The grim trigger strategy \(\tau_i\) for player \(i\) in \(G^\infty(\delta)\) is: \[\tau_i(h^t) = \begin{cases} a_i^* & \text{if } h^t = (a^*, a^*, \ldots, a^*) \text{ (all players played } a^* \text{ in all prior periods)} \\ \hat{a}_i & \text{otherwise,} \end{cases}\] where \(a^* \in A\) is the target cooperative action profile and \(\hat{a} = (\hat{a}_i)_{i \in I}\) is a punishment profile (often a stage game NE).








NoteDefinition 5.6.3 — Minmax Payoff



The minmax payoff of player \(i\) is \[\underline{v}_i = \min_{\sigma_{-i} \in \prod_{j \neq i} \Delta(A_j)} \max_{\sigma_i \in \Delta(A_i)} u_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i}).\] This is the lowest payoff opponents can hold player \(i\) to, even when \(i\) plays a best response against them. A payoff \(v_i \geq \underline{v}_i\) is individually rational for player \(i\).








NoteDefinition 5.6.4 — Feasible Payoff Set



The feasible set of payoff vectors is \[V = \text{co}\{(u_i(a))_{i \in I} : a \in A\},\] the convex hull of all stage payoff vectors. A payoff vector \(v \in V\) is feasible if it can be achieved by some (possibly correlated) mixture of action profiles. The feasible and individually rational set is \(V^* = \{v \in V : v_i \geq \underline{v}_i\; \forall i\}\).

title: 35  5.6 Repeated Games
text:
36.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 5.6.1 — Folk Theorem (Friedman 1971, Nash Threats)



Let \(G\) be a finite stage game with Nash equilibrium payoff vector \(v^{NE}\) (any NE payoff). For any payoff vector \(v \in V\) that strictly Pareto dominates \(v^{NE}\) (i.e., \(v_i > v_i^{NE}\) for all \(i\)), there exists \(\bar{\delta} < 1\) such that for all \(\delta \in (\bar{\delta}, 1)\), \(v\) is achievable as the average payoff of an SPE of \(G^\infty(\delta)\).








TipProof





We prove for the two-player case. Let \(a^*\) be a (possibly correlated) action profile achieving \(v\) and let \(\hat{a}\) be any NE of \(G\) with payoffs \(v^{NE}\).
Strategy: Both players use grim trigger: - Play \(a^*\) as long as the history is consistent with both playing \(a^*\). - If any player deviates, play \(\hat{a}\) forever after.
Payoffs: - Cooperation path: \((1-\delta)\sum_{t=0}^\infty \delta^t v_i = v_i\) for each \(i\). - Deviation payoff of player \(i\): If \(i\) deviates at time \(t\) (when path has been cooperative), their maximum stage payoff from deviation is \(\tilde{u}_i = \max_{a_i} u_i(a_i, a_{-i}^*)\). After the deviation, punishment is \(v_i^{NE}\) forever.
SPE condition via one-deviation principle: Player \(i\) prefers not to deviate if and only if the payoff from continued cooperation exceeds the payoff from deviating today and facing punishment forever: \[v_i \geq (1-\delta)\tilde{u}_i + \delta v_i^{NE}.\] (The deviation happens in one period, yielding \((1-\delta)\tilde{u}_i\), followed by NE play forever yielding discounted value \(\delta v_i^{NE}\), since after deviation all future periods also play NE.)
Rearranging: \[v_i - \delta v_i^{NE} \geq (1-\delta)\tilde{u}_i\] \[v_i - \delta v_i^{NE} \geq (1-\delta)\tilde{u}_i\] \[v_i(1 - \delta) + \delta(v_i - v_i^{NE}) \geq (1-\delta)\tilde{u}_i\]
Alternatively, solve for \(\delta\): \[(1-\delta)(v_i - \tilde{u}_i) \geq -\delta(v_i - v_i^{NE})\] \[\delta(v_i - v_i^{NE}) \geq (1-\delta)(\tilde{u}_i - v_i).\]
Since \(v_i > v_i^{NE}\) and \(\tilde{u}_i \geq v_i\) (one-shot deviation can only be better or equal to the cooperative action in the stage game), the right side is non-negative. Solving: \[\delta \geq \frac{\tilde{u}_i - v_i}{\tilde{u}_i - v_i^{NE}} \equiv \bar{\delta}_i.\]
Since \(\tilde{u}_i \geq v_i > v_i^{NE}\), we have \(0 \leq \bar{\delta}_i < 1\).
Set \(\bar{\delta} = \max_i \bar{\delta}_i < 1\). For all \(\delta > \bar{\delta}\), no player benefits from a one-shot deviation. By the one-deviation principle (Theorem 5.5.1), the grim trigger strategy profile is an SPE.
The SPE payoff is \(v_i\) for each \(i\) (evaluated under the cooperative path, which is the equilibrium path). \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 5.6.2 — Folk Theorem (Fudenberg-Maskin 1986, Minmax Threats)



Let \(G\) be a finite stage game satisfying the full dimensionality condition: the set of feasible payoff vectors \(V\) has dimension \(|I|\) (its affine hull spans \(\mathbb{R}^{|I|}\)). Then for any \(v \in V^*\) (feasible and strictly individually rational: \(v_i > \underline{v}_i\) for all \(i\)), there exists \(\bar{\delta} < 1\) such that for all \(\delta > \bar{\delta}\), \(v\) is achievable as an SPE payoff of \(G^\infty(\delta)\).








TipProof





Proof sketch. The key complication over Friedman’s version is that minmax punishments may not themselves be NE, so enforcing them requires additional coordination.
Phase structure. The strategy profile uses three phases: 1. Cooperation phase: Play the target action profile \(a^*\) achieving \(v\). If player \(j\) deviates unilaterally, enter punishment phase for \(j\). 2. Punishment phase for \(j\): All players \(k \neq j\) play their minmax strategy against \(j\) for \(T\) periods; player \(j\) plays a best response. This holds \(j\)’s payoff to approximately \(\underline{v}_j\). 3. Reward phase: After \(T\) periods of punishment, all players (including \(j\)) revert to cooperation. Players \(k \neq j\) are rewarded with a payoff above \(v_k\) for the next \(T'\) periods (using the full dimensionality condition to construct such a payoff vector) to compensate them for the cost of administering the punishment.
Full dimensionality. The full dimensionality condition ensures that for each player \(k\), there exists a feasible payoff vector that gives \(k\) strictly more than \(v_k\) while keeping other players above their continuation values — needed to construct the reward phase.
SPE verification. Using the one-deviation principle: - In the cooperation phase: as in Friedman’s proof, no deviation for \(\delta\) large enough. - In the punishment phase for \(j\): players \(k \neq j\) prefer to administer punishment (incur short-run cost) because they are rewarded afterwards; the reward must exceed the cost, which requires \(\delta\) large enough (specifically, \(T\) and \(T'\) can be tuned, and for large \(\delta\) the punishment and reward phases become negligible relative to the long-run cooperation payoff). - Player \(j\) during punishment: \(j\) cannot improve by deviating further because the punishment would restart.
As \(\delta \to 1\), any \(v \in V^*\) is achievable, giving the full Folk Theorem. \(\blacksquare\)

title: 35  5.6 Repeated Games
text:
36.4 Critical Discount Factor






NoteExample 5.6.1 — Prisoner’s Dilemma Cooperation



Stage game: \[\begin{array}{c|cc} & C & D \\ \hline C & 2,2 & 0,3 \\ D & 3,0 & 1,1 \end{array}\]
Target outcome: \((C,C)\) with payoffs \((2,2)\). Punishment: NE \((D,D)\) with payoffs \((1,1)\).
Best deviation from \(C\): play \(D\) when opponent plays \(C\), getting \(3\).
No-deviation condition for player \(i\) (by symmetry): \[2 \geq (1-\delta) \cdot 3 + \delta \cdot 1\] \[2 \geq 3 - 3\delta + \delta = 3 - 2\delta\] \[2\delta \geq 1\] \[\delta \geq \frac{1}{2}.\]
The critical discount factor is \(\delta^* = 1/2\). Cooperation is sustained as an SPE payoff if and only if \(\delta \geq 1/2\). For \(\delta < 1/2\), the unique SPE has both players defect in every period.

title: 35  5.6 Repeated Games
text:
36.5 Finite Repetition






ImportantTheorem 5.6.3 — Unraveling with Unique Stage NE



If the stage game \(G\) has a unique Nash equilibrium \(a^{NE}\), then the unique SPE of any finitely repeated game \(G^T\) (\(T\) finite) is to play \(a^{NE}\) at every period.








TipProof





By backward induction on \(T\).
Period \(T\) (last period): There is no future, so no threat can be conditioned on actions in period \(T\). The subgame in period \(T\) is exactly \(G\) with no continuation, so each player plays their stage NE strategy \(a_i^{NE}\).
Period \(T-1\): Given that period \(T\) play is \(a^{NE}\) regardless of period \(T-1\) actions (by the period \(T\) argument), the period \(T-1\) subgame is effectively a one-shot game \(G\). Therefore each player plays \(a_i^{NE}\) in period \(T-1\).
Induction: Continuing backward, in every period \(t\), given that all future periods will play \(a^{NE}\) regardless of history, the current subgame is equivalent to one-shot \(G\), and each player plays \(a_i^{NE}\).
Hence the unique SPE is \(a^{NE}\) in every period. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



The uniqueness of the stage NE is crucial. If the stage game has multiple NE, cooperation in early periods can sometimes be sustained by threatening to switch from a good NE to a bad NE in future periods (rather than threatening to play a dominated strategy forever). For example, if the stage game has NE \(\hat{a}^{good}\) (payoff 3 each) and \(\hat{a}^{bad}\) (payoff 0 each), one can sustain cooperation by rewarding cooperation with \(\hat{a}^{good}\) and punishing defection with \(\hat{a}^{bad}\) — even in finitely repeated games.

title: 35  5.6 Repeated Games
text:
36.6 Applications
Cartels and collusion. Oligopolists with \(\delta > \delta^*\) can sustain prices above the competitive level. The critical discount factor depends on the number of firms, observability of prices, and the stage game payoffs. Antitrust enforcement reduces \(\delta\) by increasing detection probability (effectively making the discount factor applicable to cartel profits lower).
International agreements. Self-enforcing trade agreements require sufficient mutual patience. The WTO’s dispute settlement mechanism works precisely because it enables punishment trigger strategies: reciprocal tariff sanctions are the credible punishment that sustains cooperation.
Social norms. Community enforcement (Kandori 1992): even without direct bilateral relationships, community-wide punishment strategies can sustain cooperation in large populations, provided information about defections spreads through the community.

title: 35  5.6 Repeated Games
text:
36.7 Cross-References

5.5 Subgame Perfect Equilibrium — SPE is the equilibrium concept for repeated games; one-deviation principle is the key tool
5.4 Extensive Form Games — Repeated game is a special extensive form
5.2 Nash Equilibrium — Stage game NE provides the punishment benchmark in Friedman’s Folk Theorem

title: 36  5.7 Bayesian Games
text: 37 5.7 Bayesian Games

title: 36  5.7 Bayesian Games
text:
37.1 Context
Bayesian games (Harsanyi 1967–68) extend normal form games to environments of incomplete information: each player may have private information about payoff-relevant characteristics — their cost, valuation, quality, type — that other players do not observe. Harsanyi’s key insight is to model private information as a type, drawn from a common prior distribution. A Bayesian Nash equilibrium (BNE) is a strategy profile in which each player, for every possible realization of their type, maximizes expected payoff given their beliefs about others’ types and others’ equilibrium strategies. The BNE framework is the foundation of auction theory, mechanism design, signaling, and the vast literature on strategic behavior under asymmetric information.

title: 36  5.7 Bayesian Games
text:
37.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 5.7.1 — Bayesian Game (Harsanyi)



A Bayesian game is a tuple \(\Gamma^B = (I, (S_i)_{i \in I}, (T_i)_{i \in I}, (\phi_i)_{i \in I}, (u_i)_{i \in I})\) where: - \(I = \{1, \ldots, n\}\) is the set of players - \(S_i\) is the action set (or strategy set, when types are ignored) - \(T_i\) is the type space of player \(i\); a type \(t_i \in T_i\) is a summary of \(i\)’s private information - \(\phi_i(t_{-i} \mid t_i) \in \Delta(T_{-i})\) is player \(i\)’s belief: the conditional distribution over others’ types given own type \(t_i\) - \(u_i: S \times T \to \mathbb{R}\) is the payoff function, where \(S = \prod_i S_i\) and \(T = \prod_i T_i\)








CautionRemark



In most applications, types are drawn from a common prior \(p \in \Delta(T)\), and beliefs are derived by Bayes’ rule: \(\phi_i(t_{-i} \mid t_i) = p(t_{-i}, t_i) / p(t_i)\). The common prior assumption — that players disagree only about private information, not about priors — is standard in Bayesian analysis.








NoteDefinition 5.7.2 — Strategy in a Bayesian Game



A pure strategy for player \(i\) is a function \(s_i: T_i \to S_i\) mapping each type to an action. A mixed strategy is a function \(\sigma_i: T_i \to \Delta(S_i)\) mapping each type to a probability distribution over actions.








NoteDefinition 5.7.3 — Bayesian Nash Equilibrium



A strategy profile \(\sigma^* = (\sigma_i^*: T_i \to \Delta(S_i))_{i \in I}\) is a Bayesian Nash Equilibrium (BNE) if, for every player \(i\) and every type \(t_i \in T_i\): \[\sigma_i^*(t_i) \in \arg\max_{\sigma_i \in \Delta(S_i)} \mathbb{E}_{t_{-i} \sim \phi_i(\cdot|t_i)}\left[u_i(\sigma_i, \sigma_{-i}^*(t_{-i}), t_i, t_{-i})\right].\] Each type \(t_i\) plays a best response to the conditional expectation of others’ strategies, given own type.








NoteDefinition 5.7.4 — Interim, Ex-Ante, and Ex-Post Payoffs



Three notions of expected payoff: - Ex-ante: \(\mathbb{E}_{t \sim p}[u_i(\sigma^*(t), t)]\) — expectation before any type is realized - Interim: \(\mathbb{E}_{t_{-i} \sim \phi_i(\cdot|t_i)}[u_i(\sigma_i^*(t_i), \sigma_{-i}^*(t_{-i}), t_i, t_{-i})]\) — expectation after \(i\) learns \(t_i\) but before others’ types are realized - Ex-post: \(u_i(\sigma^*(t), t)\) — payoff realized after all types are known
BNE is an interim concept: each type of each player best-responds given their information.

title: 36  5.7 Bayesian Games
text:
37.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 5.7.1 — Existence of BNE



In any finite Bayesian game (finite type spaces and action spaces), a Bayesian Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies exists.








TipProof





A Bayesian game with finite type spaces can be converted to an equivalent normal form game (the induced normal form) by expanding the player set: treat each \((i, t_i)\) pair as a distinct player with action set \(S_i\) and payoffs \(\mathbb{E}_{t_{-i}}[u_i(s_i, s_{-i}(t_{-i}), t_i, t_{-i}) \mid t_i]\). This “Bayesian normal form” has \(\sum_i |T_i|\) players, each with finite action set \(S_i\).
By Nash’s existence theorem (Theorem 5.2.1), the Bayesian normal form has a Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies. A Nash equilibrium of the Bayesian normal form corresponds exactly to a BNE of the original game, since each \((i, t_i)\)-player best-responding in the normal form is equivalent to type \(t_i\) of player \(i\) best-responding in the Bayesian game.
Therefore a BNE exists. \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 5.7.2 — Linear BNE in Cournot with Private Costs



Consider a two-player Cournot duopoly with inverse demand \(P(Q) = a - Q\) (where \(Q = q_1 + q_2\)). Firm \(i\)’s marginal cost \(c_i\) is private information, drawn independently from a uniform distribution on \([0, \bar{c}]\) with \(\bar{c} < a\). Then there exists a linear symmetric BNE of the form \(q_i^*(c_i) = \alpha - \beta c_i\) for constants \(\alpha, \beta\).








TipProof





Conjecture: Suppose firm \(j\) uses strategy \(q_j(c_j) = \alpha - \beta c_j\). Firm \(i\) of type \(c_i\) chooses \(q_i\) to maximize: \[\mathbb{E}_{c_j}\left[(a - q_i - q_j(c_j) - c_i) q_i\right].\]
Computing \(\mathbb{E}[q_j(c_j)] = \alpha - \beta \mathbb{E}[c_j] = \alpha - \beta \bar{c}/2\).
Firm \(i\)’s problem: \[\max_{q_i} \left(a - q_i - (\alpha - \beta\bar{c}/2) - c_i\right) q_i.\] FOC: \[a - 2q_i - (\alpha - \beta\bar{c}/2) - c_i = 0\] \[q_i^*(c_i) = \frac{a - \alpha + \beta\bar{c}/2 - c_i}{2}.\]
Consistency: For the conjectured linear form \(q_i^* = \alpha - \beta c_i\), we need: \[\alpha - \beta c_i = \frac{a - \alpha + \beta\bar{c}/2 - c_i}{2}.\] Matching coefficients: - Coefficient of \(c_i\): \(-\beta = -1/2 \Rightarrow \beta = 1/2\). - Constant: \(\alpha = (a - \alpha + \bar{c}/4)/2 \Rightarrow 2\alpha = a - \alpha + \bar{c}/4 \Rightarrow 3\alpha = a + \bar{c}/4 \Rightarrow \alpha = (a + \bar{c}/4)/3\).
BNE: \(q_i^*(c_i) = \frac{a + \bar{c}/4}{3} - \frac{c_i}{2}\).
As \(\bar{c} \to 0\), the BNE collapses to the complete information Cournot equilibrium \(q^* = a/3\). \(\blacksquare\)









NoteExample 5.7.1 — First-Price Auction with Two Bidders



Two bidders have private values \(v_1, v_2 \sim U[0,1]\), independently drawn. Each submits a bid \(b_i\); the highest bidder wins and pays their bid. Loser pays nothing.
Symmetric BNE: Conjecture \(b_i(v_i) = \beta v_i\) for some \(\beta \in (0,1)\).
Bidder \(i\) with value \(v_i\) choosing bid \(b\) wins if \(b > b_j(v_j) = \beta v_j\), i.e., \(v_j < b/\beta\). Since \(v_j \sim U[0,1]\), \(\Pr[i \text{ wins}] = b/\beta\) (assuming \(b \leq \beta\)).
Expected payoff: \[\pi_i(b, v_i) = \frac{b}{\beta}(v_i - b).\] FOC in \(b\): \[\frac{\partial \pi_i}{\partial b} = \frac{v_i - b}{\beta} - \frac{b}{\beta} = \frac{v_i - 2b}{\beta} = 0 \Rightarrow b^* = v_i/2.\]
Consistency: \(\beta = 1/2\). The symmetric BNE is \(b_i^*(v_i) = v_i/2\).
Revenue equivalence preview: In the second-price auction, the dominant strategy is to bid \(v_i\); the winner pays the second-highest bid, which equals \(\mathbb{E}[\max(v_j) \mid v_j < v_i] = v_i/2\) in expectation. Therefore both auctions yield the same expected revenue — the Revenue Equivalence Theorem.

title: 36  5.7 Bayesian Games
text:
37.4 From BNE to Mechanism Design






CautionRemark



The BNE framework formalizes a key observation: in any mechanism where players report information (a “direct mechanism”), the equilibrium behavior is a function \(\sigma_i^*: T_i \to S_i\) mapping types to reports/actions. The Revelation Principle (see Part VII) states that any BNE of any mechanism can be replicated by a direct mechanism in which truthful reporting (\(\sigma_i^*(t_i) = t_i\)) is a BNE. This reduces the mechanism design problem from searching over all possible mechanisms to characterizing incentive-compatible direct mechanisms — a foundational simplification. The BNE concept, applied to the message game, is what makes the Revelation Principle work.


Interim, ex-ante, and ex-post efficiency. Efficiency concepts in Bayesian environments depend on the information available: - Ex-post efficiency: allocation is optimal for every realization of types \(t\). Requires the mechanism to always allocate to the highest-value agent, regardless of types. - Interim efficiency: allocation maximizes expected social surplus conditional on each player’s own type. - Ex-ante efficiency: allocation maximizes expected social surplus before any type is realized.
The Myerson-Satterthwaite theorem shows that in bilateral trade, no BNE mechanism can achieve ex-post efficiency while also satisfying individual rationality and budget balance — a fundamental impossibility result in the economics of information.
