title: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory
section: 38.1 Problem 1 — Nash Equilibria of a 3×3 Game
text:
38.1 Problem 1 — Nash Equilibria of a 3×3 Game
Consider the following normal form game where Player 1 chooses rows (\(T, M, B\)) and Player 2 chooses columns (\(L, C, R\)):
\[\begin{array}{c|ccc} & L & C & R \\ \hline T & 4,3 & 0,0 & 3,4 \\ M & 2,2 & 5,5 & 1,1 \\ B & 3,4 & 0,0 & 4,3 \end{array}\]
(a) Apply IESDS. State which strategies are eliminated at each round and justify each elimination.
(b) Find all pure strategy Nash equilibria.
(c) Find all mixed strategy Nash equilibria. (Hint: by part (a), you may work with the reduced game.)
(d) Verify that every pure strategy NE you found in (b) is rationalizable. Show that the IESDS outcome from (a) coincides with the set of rationalizable strategies.
(e) Compute the payoffs of each player at every NE you found. Assess whether any NE Pareto dominates another.
crumbs: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory

title: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory
text:
38.2 Problem 2 — IESDS vs. Nash Equilibrium
Consider the three-player normal form game in which each player chooses from \(\{A, B\}\). Player 1 chooses rows, Player 2 chooses columns, Player 3 chooses the matrix. Payoffs \((u_1, u_2, u_3)\):
Player 3 chooses \(X\): \[\begin{array}{c|cc} & A & B \\ \hline A & 1,1,2 & 3,0,1 \\ B & 0,3,1 & 2,2,3 \end{array}\]
Player 3 chooses \(Y\): \[\begin{array}{c|cc} & A & B \\ \hline A & 2,2,1 & 0,1,0 \\ B & 1,0,0 & 3,3,2 \end{array}\]
(a) Perform IESDS and find \(S^\infty\).
(b) Find all Nash equilibria (pure and mixed) of this game.
(c) Explicitly exhibit a rationalizable strategy profile that is not a Nash equilibrium. For the strategy you exhibit, construct the chain of beliefs justifying each player’s choice.
(d) Prove formally that the order of elimination in (a) does not affect the outcome, using Theorem 5.1.1. Exhibit two different elimination orders that produce the same \(S^\infty\).
crumbs: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory

title: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory
section: 38.3 Problem 3 — SPE via Backward Induction and Non-Credible Threats
text:
38.3 Problem 3 — SPE via Backward Induction and Non-Credible Threats
Consider the following 3-stage sequential game:

Stage 1: Player 1 chooses \(U\) (up) or \(D\) (down).
Stage 2: Player 2, observing Player 1’s choice, chooses \(L\) or \(R\).
Stage 3: Player 1, observing both prior choices, chooses \(A\) or \(B\).

Terminal payoffs \((u_1, u_2)\): - \((U, L, A) \to (4, 2)\) - \((U, L, B) \to (1, 5)\) - \((U, R, A) \to (3, 3)\) - \((U, R, B) \to (2, 1)\) - \((D, L, A) \to (5, 1)\) - \((D, L, B) \to (0, 4)\) - \((D, R, A) \to (2, 2)\) - \((D, R, B) \to (3, 3)\)
(a) Draw the extensive form game tree, labeling all nodes and payoffs.
(b) Find the backward induction solution. State the optimal action of Player 1 at Stage 3 for each history, then the optimal action of Player 2 at Stage 2, then the optimal action of Player 1 at Stage 1.
(c) Write down the SPE strategy profile explicitly as a complete contingent plan for each player.
(d) Find all Nash equilibria of the normal form game. Identify which NE are SPE and which rely on non-credible threats. For each non-SPE Nash equilibrium, identify the subgame in which the induced strategies are not a Nash equilibrium, and specify the off-path threat that is non-credible.
(e) Apply the one-deviation principle to verify that your SPE is indeed subgame perfect. Show that no single-information-set deviation is profitable.
crumbs: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory

title: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory
section: 38.4 Problem 4 — Critical Discount Factor in Prisoner’s Dilemma
text:
38.4 Problem 4 — Critical Discount Factor in Prisoner’s Dilemma
Consider the Prisoner’s Dilemma stage game:
\[\begin{array}{c|cc} & C & D \\ \hline C & 3,3 & 0,5 \\ D & 5,0 & 1,1 \end{array}\]
Players interact in the infinitely repeated game \(G^\infty(\delta)\) with discount factor \(\delta \in (0,1)\). Payoffs are normalized as \((1-\delta)\sum_{t=0}^\infty \delta^t u_i(a^t)\).
(a) Verify that \((D, D)\) is the unique Nash equilibrium of the stage game.
(b) State the grim trigger strategy profile \(\tau\) that attempts to sustain \((C, C)\) in every period.
(c) Compute the critical discount factor \(\delta^*\) such that \(\tau\) is an SPE for all \(\delta \geq \delta^*\). Show all steps of the no-deviation calculation.
(d) Now suppose the punishment is not permanent NE play but rather \(T\) periods of \((D,D)\) followed by a return to cooperation. Derive the critical discount factor \(\delta^*(T)\) as a function of \(T\). Show that \(\delta^*(T) \to \delta^*\) (from part c) as \(T \to \infty\) and \(\delta^*(T) \to 1\) as \(T \to 1\).
(e) Suppose cooperation is \((C,C)\) but deviation is detected only with probability \(p \in (0,1)\) each period (imperfect monitoring). Rederive \(\delta^*(p)\). How does \(\delta^*\) depend on \(p\)? Interpret economically in terms of antitrust enforcement.
crumbs: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory

title: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory
section: 38.5 Problem 5 — BNE in Cournot Duopoly with Private Costs
text:
38.5 Problem 5 — BNE in Cournot Duopoly with Private Costs
Consider a Cournot duopoly with inverse demand \(P(Q) = 10 - Q\) where \(Q = q_1 + q_2\). Marginal costs are private information: firm \(i\)’s cost \(c_i\) is drawn independently from the uniform distribution on \(\{c_L, c_H\} = \{1, 3\}\) with equal probability \(1/2\).
(a) List all possible type profiles \((c_1, c_2)\) and the complete information Nash equilibrium quantity pairs for each type profile.
(b) A strategy for firm \(i\) in the Bayesian game is a pair \((q_i^L, q_i^H) \in \mathbb{R}_+^2\) where \(q_i^L\) is the quantity if \(c_i = c_L\) and \(q_i^H\) if \(c_i = c_H\). Write out firm \(i\)’s interim expected profit as a function of \((q_i^L, q_i^H)\) given that firm \(j\) plays \((q_j^L, q_j^H)\).
(c) Derive the four first-order conditions for a symmetric BNE. Solve for the symmetric BNE \((q^{L*}, q^{H*})\).
(d) Compare the BNE quantities to the complete information Cournot equilibrium quantities from (a). For which cost types does a firm produce more under incomplete information? Provide the economic intuition.
(e) Compute the ex-ante expected total quantity and expected price under the BNE. Compare to the expected total quantity and price in the complete information equilibria (averaged over type profiles).
crumbs: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory

title: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory
section: 38.6 Problem 6 — Correlated Equilibrium in Battle of the Sexes
text:
38.6 Problem 6 — Correlated Equilibrium in Battle of the Sexes
Consider the Battle of the Sexes:
\[\begin{array}{c|cc} & O & F \\ \hline O & 3,1 & 0,0 \\ F & 0,0 & 1,3 \end{array}\]
(a) Find all pure strategy Nash equilibria. Find the mixed strategy Nash equilibrium and compute expected payoffs.
(b) Consider the correlated distribution \(\mu\) defined by: \[\mu(O,O) = \frac{1}{2}, \quad \mu(F,F) = \frac{1}{2}, \quad \mu(O,F) = \mu(F,O) = 0.\] Verify all the correlated equilibrium conditions. What are the expected payoffs?
(c) Consider the more general correlated distribution: \[\mu(O,O) = p, \quad \mu(F,F) = q, \quad \mu(O,F) = r, \quad \mu(F,O) = s,\] where \(p + q + r + s = 1\) and \(p,q,r,s \geq 0\). Derive the four obedience constraints (one for each player and each possible recommendation). Characterize the set of all correlated equilibria as a convex polytope.
(d) Find the correlated equilibrium \(\mu^*\) that maximizes the sum of expected payoffs \(E_\mu[u_1 + u_2]\). Show that \(\mu^*\) strictly Pareto dominates the mixed NE. Explain why a mediator who can recommend (but not enforce) actions can improve on the Nash outcome.
(e) Show that no Nash equilibrium (pure or mixed) achieves the welfare-maximizing CE payoffs from (d). This illustrates that CE strictly expands the set of implementable payoff vectors beyond NE.
crumbs: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory

title: 37  Problem Set — Part V: Game Theory
text:
38.7 Problem 7 — Folk Theorem: Explicit Construction
Consider the following two-player stage game:
\[\begin{array}{c|cc} & C & D \\ \hline C & 4,4 & 0,6 \\ D & 6,0 & 1,1 \end{array}\]
(a) Find all Nash equilibria of the stage game. Compute the minmax payoff \(\underline{v}_i\) for each player \(i\).
(b) Characterize the feasible set \(V = \text{co}\{v \in \mathbb{R}^2 : v = u(a), a \in A\}\). Sketch \(V\) in payoff space. Identify the feasible and strictly individually rational set \(V^*\).
(c) Consider the target payoff vector \(v^* = (3, 3)\). This payoff requires correlation (verify this). Assume a mediator can publicly correlate players’ actions. Propose a correlated action profile achieving \(v^* = (3,3)\).
(d) Explicitly construct the grim trigger strategy profile \((\tau_1, \tau_2)\) sustaining \(v^* = (3,3)\) as an SPE payoff. Compute the critical discount factor \(\delta^*\) such that no deviation is profitable for \(\delta \geq \delta^*\).
(e) Now consider the target payoff \(v^{**} = (2, 5)\), which is feasible and strictly individually rational. Explain why the simple grim trigger strategy is not sufficient to sustain \(v^{**}\) (asymmetric payoffs require asymmetric strategies). Propose an explicit strategy profile sustaining \(v^{**}\) as an SPE payoff. Compute the critical discount factor.
(f) Verify the full dimensionality condition for this game. Explain intuitively why this condition is necessary for the Fudenberg-Maskin version of the Folk Theorem but not for Friedman’s version.

title: 38  Part VI — General Equilibrium
text: 39 Part VI — General Equilibrium

title: 38  Part VI — General Equilibrium
text:
39.1 Context
General equilibrium (GE) theory closes the model: rather than taking prices as parameters (partial equilibrium), GE determines all prices simultaneously as the outcome of market clearing across all markets. The framework, developed in its modern form by Arrow and Debreu (1954) and McKenzie (1954), provides both the positive foundation of competitive price theory and the normative foundation of welfare economics.
The mathematical apex of the MWG program is the GE existence proof, which applies Kakutani’s fixed point theorem to the aggregate excess demand correspondence — an elegant fusion of the price theory from Parts II–III, the mathematical tools from Part I, and the equilibrium methodology of Part V. The Welfare Theorems — which formalize Adam Smith’s invisible hand — are the deepest results in the normative theory of markets, and their proofs (using only local nonsatiation for the First, and separating hyperplanes for the Second) reveal precisely what assumptions bear the normative weight.

title: 38  Part VI — General Equilibrium
text:
39.2 Why General Equilibrium?
Partial equilibrium (supply-and-demand analysis of one market) takes all other prices as fixed. This is appropriate when the market in question is small relative to the economy. But most policy questions involve interactions between markets: a tax on labor affects goods prices; a tariff affects wages; a subsidy to education affects the returns to all skills. GE is the only framework that correctly accounts for these cross-market interactions.
The completeness of GE: Arrow-Debreu’s insight — that contingent commodities indexed by states of the world extend the framework to uncertainty — means that a single GE model subsumes not only multi-market trade but also financial markets, insurance, and intertemporal choice. The economy has one good for each commodity-state-date triple, and a Walrasian equilibrium in this extended economy is Arrow-Debreu equilibrium: the complete-market benchmark.

title: 38  Part VI — General Equilibrium
text:
39.4 Key Results

Walras’ Law: The value of aggregate excess demand is zero at all prices — a budget identity, not an equilibrium condition. It implies one market clearing equation is redundant.
First Welfare Theorem: Every Walrasian equilibrium is Pareto optimal. Proof requires only local nonsatiation, not convexity. This is the most general and robust result in welfare economics.
Second Welfare Theorem: Any Pareto optimum can be decentralized as a Walrasian equilibrium with lump-sum transfers. Proof requires convexity. This separates equity (achieved by redistribution) from efficiency (achieved by markets).
Existence: Under standard regularity conditions, at least one Walrasian equilibrium exists. Follows from Kakutani applied to the excess demand correspondence.
Non-uniqueness: The Sonnenschein-Mantel-Debreu (SMD) theorem shows that aggregate excess demand can be essentially arbitrary — uniqueness fails generically.

title: 38  Part VI — General Equilibrium
text:
39.5 Cross-References

title: 39  6.1 Exchange Economies
text: 40 6.1 Exchange Economies

title: 39  6.1 Exchange Economies
text:
40.1 Context
The pure exchange economy is the simplest general equilibrium model: there is no production, only redistribution of a fixed aggregate endowment through trade. Despite its simplicity, the exchange economy contains all the essential conceptual apparatus: competitive equilibrium, excess demand, Walras’ Law, price normalization, and the geometric intuition of the Edgeworth box. These are the foundations on which the Arrow-Debreu production economy and the welfare theorems are built.

title: 39  6.1 Exchange Economies
text:
40.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 6.1.1 — Pure Exchange Economy



A pure exchange economy \(\mathcal{E}\) consists of: - \(I = \{1, \ldots, n\}\) consumers and \(L\) goods - Consumer \(i\)’s endowment \(\omega_i \in \mathbb{R}^L_+\): the bundle they bring to the market - Consumer \(i\)’s preference relation \(\succeq_i\) (or utility function \(u_i: \mathbb{R}^L_+ \to \mathbb{R}\)) over consumption bundles - Total (aggregate) endowment: \(\bar{\omega} = \sum_{i=1}^n \omega_i \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\)








NoteDefinition 6.1.2 — Walrasian (Competitive) Equilibrium



A Walrasian equilibrium of \(\mathcal{E}\) is a pair \((x^*, p^*)\) where \(x^* = (x_1^*, \ldots, x_n^*)\) and \(p^* \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\) such that: 1. Individual optimality: For each \(i\), \(x_i^* \in x_i(p^*, p^* \cdot \omega_i)\), where \(x_i(p, w)\) is consumer \(i\)’s Walrasian demand correspondence at price \(p\) and wealth \(w\) 2. Market clearing: \(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i^* = \bar{\omega}\) (the allocation is feasible and exhausts the endowment)








NoteDefinition 6.1.3 — Excess Demand



The excess demand function (or correspondence) is \[z(p) = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i) - \bar{\omega}.\] Equilibrium requires \(z(p^*) = 0_{\mathbb{R}^L}\) (every market clears).








NoteDefinition 6.1.4 — Edgeworth Box



In a two-consumer, two-good economy with total endowment \(\bar{\omega} = (\bar{\omega}_1, \bar{\omega}_2)\), the Edgeworth box is the rectangle of feasible allocations: \[\mathcal{F} = \{(x_1, x_2) \in \mathbb{R}^4_+ : x_1 + x_2 = \bar{\omega}\}.\] Consumer 1’s origin is the southwest corner; consumer 2’s origin is the northeast corner. Any point \((x_1, x_2) \in \mathcal{F}\) represents a feasible allocation.








NoteDefinition 6.1.5 — Pareto Set and Contract Curve



An allocation \((x_1, x_2) \in \mathcal{F}\) is Pareto optimal if there is no other feasible \((x_1', x_2')\) such that \(u_i(x_i') \geq u_i(x_i)\) for all \(i\) and \(u_j(x_j') > u_j(x_j)\) for some \(j\).
The contract curve (or Pareto set) is the set of all Pareto optimal allocations in the Edgeworth box. In the interior, it consists of points where the consumers’ indifference curves are tangent, i.e., \(MRS_1 = MRS_2\).

title: 39  6.1 Exchange Economies
text:
40.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 6.1.1 — Walras’ Law



If each consumer’s preference relation \(\succeq_i\) satisfies local nonsatiation, then the excess demand function satisfies: \[p \cdot z(p) = 0 \quad \text{for all } p \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}.\]








TipProof





By local nonsatiation, each consumer exhausts their budget at any optimal choice: \[p \cdot x_i^*(p, p \cdot \omega_i) = p \cdot \omega_i \quad \forall i.\] (If the budget constraint were not binding, there would exist a preferred bundle arbitrarily close to \(x_i^*\), contradicting optimality.)
Summing over all consumers: \[\sum_{i=1}^n p \cdot x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i) = \sum_{i=1}^n p \cdot \omega_i.\] Rearranging: \[p \cdot \left(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i) - \sum_{i=1}^n \omega_i\right) = 0.\] By Definition 6.1.3, this is \(p \cdot z(p) = 0\). \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Walras’ Law has a crucial implication for market clearing. If there are \(L\) goods and \(L-1\) markets clear (i.e., \(z_\ell(p^*) = 0\) for \(\ell = 1, \ldots, L-1\)), then: \[p^* \cdot z(p^*) = 0 \Rightarrow \sum_{\ell=1}^L p_\ell^* z_\ell(p^*) = 0 \Rightarrow p_L^* z_L(p^*) = 0.\] Since \(p_L^* > 0\) in equilibrium, it follows that \(z_L(p^*) = 0\). The \(L\)-th market clears automatically. This reduces the number of independent equilibrium conditions to \(L-1\).








ImportantTheorem 6.1.2 — Properties of Excess Demand



Under standard assumptions (continuity and local nonsatiation of preferences, positive endowments), the excess demand function \(z: \mathbb{R}^L_{++} \to \mathbb{R}^L\) satisfies: 1. Walras’ Law: \(p \cdot z(p) = 0\) for all \(p \gg 0\) 2. Homogeneity of degree zero: \(z(\lambda p) = z(p)\) for all \(\lambda > 0\) 3. Continuity: \(z\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}^L_{++}\) 4. Boundary condition: As \(p_\ell \to 0\), \(z_\ell(p) \to +\infty\) (demand for a free good is unbounded)








TipProof





Homogeneity: Each individual demand \(x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i)\) is homogeneous of degree zero in \((p, w)\) since \(w_i = p \cdot \omega_i\) scales proportionally with \(p\). Therefore \(x_i(\lambda p, \lambda p \cdot \omega_i) = x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i)\), and so \(z(\lambda p) = z(p)\).
Continuity: Under standard preference assumptions (continuous, strictly convex), Walrasian demand \(x_i(p, w)\) is continuous. Continuity of \(z\) follows from continuity of the sum.
Boundary condition: As \(p_\ell \to 0\), good \(\ell\) becomes free. By local nonsatiation, each consumer wants more of every good (or at worst, is indifferent). With free good \(\ell\), the budget set becomes effectively unconstrained in dimension \(\ell\), and demand for \(\ell\) grows without bound. Formally, the Walrasian demand \(x_{i\ell}(p, w) \to +\infty\) as \(p_\ell \to 0^+\) for any fixed \(w\) (since the wealth buys infinite units of good \(\ell\)). Therefore \(z_\ell(p) \to +\infty\). \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 6.1.3 — Price Normalization



Due to homogeneity of degree zero, it is without loss of generality to restrict attention to prices in the unit simplex: \[\Delta^{L-1} = \left\{p \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : \sum_{\ell=1}^L p_\ell = 1\right\}.\] Any equilibrium price vector \(p^* \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\) corresponds to a normalized price \(p^*/\|p^*\|_1 \in \Delta^{L-1}\) that is also an equilibrium (since excess demand is unchanged).

title: 39  6.1 Exchange Economies
text:
40.4 The Edgeworth Box: Geometric Intuition






NoteExample 6.1.1 — Walrasian Equilibrium in the Edgeworth Box



Consider 2 consumers, 2 goods: \(u_1(x_{11}, x_{12}) = x_{11}^\alpha x_{12}^{1-\alpha}\), \(u_2(x_{21}, x_{22}) = x_{21}^\beta x_{22}^{1-\beta}\). Endowments: \(\omega_1 = (1, 0)\), \(\omega_2 = (0, 1)\).
Walrasian demand: With Cobb-Douglas preferences, consumer \(i\)’s demand for good \(\ell\) is a fixed fraction of wealth: \[x_{11}(p, w_1) = \frac{\alpha w_1}{p_1} = \frac{\alpha p_1}{p_1} = \alpha, \quad x_{12}(p, w_1) = \frac{(1-\alpha)p_1}{p_2}.\] \[x_{21}(p, w_2) = \frac{\beta p_2}{p_1}, \quad x_{22}(p, w_2) = \frac{(1-\beta)p_2}{p_2} = 1-\beta.\]
Market clearing for good 1: \(x_{11} + x_{21} = 1\): \[\alpha + \frac{\beta p_2}{p_1} = 1 \Rightarrow \frac{p_2}{p_1} = \frac{1-\alpha}{\beta}.\]
Equilibrium price ratio: \(p_1/p_2 = \beta/(1-\alpha)\).
Equilibrium allocation: \(x_1^* = (\alpha, (1-\alpha)\beta/\beta) = (\alpha, 1-\alpha)\) and \(x_2^* = (1-\alpha, \beta)\)… adjusting: \(x_{12}^* = (1-\alpha)p_1/p_2 = (1-\alpha) \cdot \beta/(1-\alpha) = \beta\), so \(x_1^* = (\alpha, \beta)\) and… Let us recompute. With \(p_2/p_1 = (1-\alpha)/\beta\): - \(w_1 = p_1 \cdot 1 = p_1\); \(x_{11}^* = \alpha\), \(x_{12}^* = (1-\alpha)p_1/p_2 = (1-\alpha)\beta/(1-\alpha) = \beta\). - \(w_2 = p_2 \cdot 1 = p_2\); \(x_{21}^* = \beta p_2/p_1 = \beta(1-\alpha)/\beta = 1-\alpha\), \(x_{22}^* = 1-\beta\).
Equilibrium allocation: \(x_1^* = (\alpha, \beta)\), \(x_2^* = (1-\alpha, 1-\beta)\). Market clearing: \(\alpha + (1-\alpha) = 1\) and \(\beta + (1-\beta) = 1\). Confirmed.

title: 39  6.1 Exchange Economies
text:
40.5 Core and Walrasian Equilibrium
The core of the exchange economy is the set of feasible allocations that cannot be blocked by any coalition: allocation \(x\) is in the core if no subset \(S \subseteq I\) can redistribute their endowments \(\sum_{i \in S}\omega_i\) to make all members strictly better off.
Every Walrasian equilibrium allocation is in the core (this follows from the First Welfare Theorem plus the ability of blocking coalitions to use only their own endowments). The core convergence theorem (Edgeworth, formalized by Debreu-Scarf 1963) states that as the economy is replicated infinitely many times, the core shrinks to the set of Walrasian allocations.

title: 39  6.1 Exchange Economies
text:
40.6 Cross-References

6.2 Arrow Debreu Economy — Extends to production; same excess demand framework
6.3 Existence of Equilibrium — Existence proof uses excess demand properties
6.4 Welfare Theorems — First WT uses Walrasian equilibrium definition; Second WT shows converse
Index — Walrasian demand \(x_i(p, w)\) is the consumer theory object

title: 40  6.2 The Arrow-Debreu Economy
text: 41 6.2 The Arrow-Debreu Economy

title: 40  6.2 The Arrow-Debreu Economy
text:
41.1 Context
The Arrow-Debreu economy (Arrow-Debreu 1954, McKenzie 1954) extends the pure exchange model to include production. Firms possess production sets and profit-maximize; their profits are distributed to consumers as dividend income. This model is not only more general than exchange economies — it provides the complete competitive equilibrium framework that is the benchmark for all of modern microeconomics and macroeconomics. Arrow and Debreu’s further insight — that goods indexed by state of the world are formally identical to ordinary goods — gives the model its extraordinary scope: a single Arrow-Debreu equilibrium in the extended commodity space captures not only multi-market trade but also complete-market financial allocation.

title: 40  6.2 The Arrow-Debreu Economy
text:
41.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 6.2.1 — Arrow-Debreu Economy



An Arrow-Debreu economy is \(\mathcal{E} = \big((u_i, \omega_i, (\theta_{ij})_{j \in J})_{i \in I},\, (Y_j)_{j \in J}\big)\) where: - \(I = \{1, \ldots, n\}\) consumers, \(J = \{1, \ldots, m\}\) firms, \(L\) goods - \(u_i: \mathbb{R}^L_+ \to \mathbb{R}\) is consumer \(i\)’s utility function; \(\omega_i \in \mathbb{R}^L_+\) is their endowment - \(\theta_{ij} \geq 0\) with \(\sum_{i \in I} \theta_{ij} = 1\) is consumer \(i\)’s ownership share of firm \(j\) - \(Y_j \subseteq \mathbb{R}^L\) is firm \(j\)’s production set: the set of technologically feasible input-output vectors \(y_j\) (by convention, inputs are negative, outputs positive)








NoteDefinition 6.2.2 — Profit-Maximizing Firm



At price vector \(p \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\), firm \(j\)’s profit-maximization problem is: \[\pi_j(p) = \max_{y_j \in Y_j} p \cdot y_j,\] with supply correspondence \(y_j^*(p) = \arg\max_{y_j \in Y_j} p \cdot y_j\).








NoteDefinition 6.2.3 — Consumer Wealth in Arrow-Debreu



Consumer \(i\)’s wealth at prices \(p\) is: \[w_i(p) = p \cdot \omega_i + \sum_{j \in J} \theta_{ij} \pi_j(p).\] Wealth comprises both the market value of the endowment and the dividend income from profit shares.








NoteDefinition 6.2.4 — Walrasian Equilibrium (Arrow-Debreu Economy)



A Walrasian equilibrium is a triple \((x^*, y^*, p^*)\) where: 1. \(x_i^* \in x_i(p^*, w_i(p^*))\) for each consumer \(i\) (utility maximization) 2. \(y_j^* \in y_j^*(p^*)\) for each firm \(j\) (profit maximization) 3. \(\sum_{i \in I} x_i^* = \sum_{i \in I} \omega_i + \sum_{j \in J} y_j^*\) (market clearing)








NoteDefinition 6.2.5 — Aggregate Excess Demand (with Production)



\[z(p) = \sum_{i \in I} x_i(p, w_i(p)) - \sum_{j \in J} y_j^*(p) - \bar{\omega},\] where \(\bar{\omega} = \sum_i \omega_i\). Equilibrium requires \(z(p^*) = 0\).

title: 40  6.2 The Arrow-Debreu Economy
text:
41.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 6.2.1 — Walras’ Law in the Arrow-Debreu Economy



Under local nonsatiation of consumer preferences, \(p \cdot z(p) = 0\) for all \(p \gg 0\).








TipProof





By local nonsatiation, each consumer exhausts their budget: \[p \cdot x_i(p, w_i(p)) = w_i(p) = p \cdot \omega_i + \sum_j \theta_{ij}\pi_j(p) \quad \forall i.\] Summing over consumers: \[\sum_i p \cdot x_i = \sum_i p \cdot \omega_i + \sum_i \sum_j \theta_{ij}\pi_j(p) = p \cdot \bar{\omega} + \sum_j \pi_j(p) \sum_i \theta_{ij}.\] Since \(\sum_i \theta_{ij} = 1\) for each \(j\): \[\sum_i p \cdot x_i = p \cdot \bar{\omega} + \sum_j \pi_j(p) = p \cdot \bar{\omega} + \sum_j p \cdot y_j^*(p).\] Therefore: \[p \cdot z(p) = p \cdot \left(\sum_i x_i - \sum_j y_j^* - \bar{\omega}\right) = \left(p \cdot \bar{\omega} + \sum_j p \cdot y_j^*\right) - \sum_j p \cdot y_j^* - p \cdot \bar{\omega} = 0. \quad \blacksquare\]









ImportantTheorem 6.2.2 — Homogeneity of Aggregate Excess Demand



The aggregate excess demand \(z(p)\) is homogeneous of degree zero in \(p\).








TipProof





Both \(x_i(p, w_i(p))\) and \(y_j^*(p)\) are homogeneous of degree zero in \(p\): - Consumer demand: Walrasian demand is homogeneous of degree zero in \((p, w)\). Since \(w_i(\lambda p) = \lambda p \cdot \omega_i + \sum_j \theta_{ij} \pi_j(\lambda p)\). Profit \(\pi_j(\lambda p) = \max_{y \in Y_j} \lambda p \cdot y = \lambda \pi_j(p)\) (homogeneous of degree one in \(p\)). Therefore \(w_i(\lambda p) = \lambda w_i(p)\). Hence \(x_i(\lambda p, \lambda w_i(p)) = x_i(p, w_i(p))\). - Firm supply: \(y_j^*(\lambda p) = \arg\max_{y \in Y_j} \lambda p \cdot y = \arg\max_{y \in Y_j} p \cdot y = y_j^*(p)\).
Therefore \(z(\lambda p) = z(p)\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: 40  6.2 The Arrow-Debreu Economy
text:
41.4 Arrow-Debreu Contingent Commodities
The most profound generalization of the Arrow-Debreu framework is its extension to uncertainty.






NoteDefinition 6.2.6 — Arrow-Debreu Contingent Commodity



Let \(\Omega = \{1, \ldots, S\}\) be a finite set of states of the world. An Arrow-Debreu contingent commodity \(\ell_s\) is a unit of good \(\ell\) delivered if and only if state \(s\) occurs. The extended commodity space is \(\mathbb{R}^{L \times S}\), with \(LS\) commodities.
A complete markets equilibrium (Arrow-Debreu equilibrium under uncertainty) is a Walrasian equilibrium in this \(LS\)-commodity space.








CautionRemark



Defining goods by \((commodity, state)\) pairs formally reduces GE under uncertainty to the standard GE framework. Consumers maximize expected utility over state-contingent consumption bundles; firms maximize expected profits. The existence theorem and welfare theorems apply without modification.
A price \(p_{\ell s}\) for contingent commodity \(\ell_s\) is an Arrow-Debreu price: it is the current price of a commitment to deliver one unit of good \(\ell\) in state \(s\). Under complete markets, all risk is traded away in equilibrium.








NoteDefinition 6.2.7 — Radner Equilibrium



A Radner equilibrium (Radner 1972) is an equilibrium in a sequential market structure where: - At \(t = 0\) (before the state is realized), there are markets for assets (financial instruments) - At \(t = 1\) (after the state is realized), there are spot markets for goods
Agents trade assets at \(t = 0\) to transfer wealth across states; they trade goods at \(t = 1\) given their realized wealth.








ImportantTheorem 6.2.3 — Equivalence of Radner and Arrow-Debreu Equilibria



If there are complete asset markets (the asset payoff matrix has rank equal to the number of states \(S\)), then the set of Radner equilibrium allocations coincides with the set of Arrow-Debreu equilibrium allocations.








TipProof





Proof sketch.
From Arrow-Debreu to Radner: Given an Arrow-Debreu equilibrium with contingent prices \(p_{\ell s}\), define asset portfolios that replicate the contingent commodity purchases. Each consumer’s Arrow-Debreu consumption plan can be decomposed into a financial portfolio (purchased at \(t = 0\)) and spot market purchases (at \(t = 1\)). With complete markets (rank \(S\) asset payoff matrix), any contingent wealth transfer is achievable.
From Radner to Arrow-Debreu: A Radner equilibrium with spot prices \(q_{\ell s}\) (in state \(s\)) and asset prices \(r\) satisfies: each agent maximizes utility subject to financial feasibility at \(t = 0\) and budget constraints at \(t = 1\) for each state. Define Arrow-Debreu prices \(p_{\ell s} = q_{\ell s} \cdot \phi_s\) where \(\phi_s\) is the state price (the \(t = 0\) price of one unit of account in state \(s\), derived from asset prices). Under complete markets, state prices are uniquely determined. The Radner optimal consumption plan satisfies the Arrow-Debreu budget constraint at prices \(\{p_{\ell s}\}\), and market clearing at each state maps to Arrow-Debreu market clearing. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



The Arrow-Debreu/Radner equivalence theorem is the foundation of financial economics. It implies that in complete markets, the specific trading arrangement (all contingent claims at date 0 vs. sequential trading with assets) is irrelevant for allocations. Market incompleteness — when the rank of the asset payoff matrix is less than \(S\) — is the fundamental source of financial market failures: agents cannot perfectly insure against all risks, and equilibrium allocations are generally constrained inefficient.

title: 40  6.2 The Arrow-Debreu Economy
text:
41.5 Cross-References

6.1 Exchange Economies — Pure exchange is Arrow-Debreu with \(Y_j = \{0\}\) (no production)
6.3 Existence of Equilibrium — Existence proof applies to the Arrow-Debreu economy
6.4 Welfare Theorems — Welfare theorems apply to Arrow-Debreu allocations
Index — Production sets and profit maximization are from producer theory

title: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium
text: 42 6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium
crumbs: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium

title: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium
text:
42.1 Context
The existence of Walrasian equilibrium — the question of whether supply equals demand simultaneously in all markets — is the fundamental mathematical question of competitive equilibrium theory. Arrow and Debreu (1954) and McKenzie (1954) gave the first rigorous proofs using Kakutani’s fixed point theorem. The proof strategy is elegant: normalize prices to the simplex, define a “price adjustment” correspondence that raises prices for goods in excess demand, and show that a fixed point of this combined correspondence satisfies market clearing (via Walras’ Law). This note presents the complete proof under standard assumptions. Uniqueness, by contrast, fails generically — the Sonnenschein-Mantel-Debreu theorem shows that aggregate excess demand can be essentially arbitrary.
crumbs: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium

title: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium
text:
42.2 Assumptions






NoteDefinition 6.3.1 — Standard Assumptions for Existence



Let \(\mathcal{E} = ((u_i, \omega_i)_{i \in I})\) be an exchange economy. The standard assumptions are: - (A1) Continuity: \(u_i\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}^L_+\) for each \(i\) - (A2) Strong monotonicity: \(u_i(x) > u_i(y)\) whenever \(x \gg y\) (or local nonsatiation suffices for Walras’ Law; strong monotonicity ensures the boundary condition) - (A3) Strict convexity: \(u_i\) is strictly quasi-concave, so demand \(x_i(p,w)\) is a continuous function - (A4) Interior endowments: \(\omega_i \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\) for each \(i\) (positive endowment of every good, or suitable substitute)
crumbs: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium

title: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium
text:
42.3 Main Result






ImportantTheorem 6.3.1 — Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium



Under assumptions (A1)–(A4), there exists a price vector \(p^* \in \Delta^{L-1}\) and allocation \(x^*\) constituting a Walrasian equilibrium of \(\mathcal{E}\).








TipProof





We proceed in four steps, applying Kakutani’s fixed point theorem (see 1.4 Fixed Point Theorems).

Step 1: Normalize prices and define the compact domain.
By homogeneity of degree zero of \(z(p)\), normalize prices to the simplex: \[\Delta^{L-1} = \left\{p \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : \sum_{\ell=1}^L p_\ell = 1\right\}.\]
\(\Delta^{L-1}\) is nonempty (e.g., the uniform price \(p = (1/L, \ldots, 1/L)\) belongs to it), compact (closed and bounded in \(\mathbb{R}^L\)), and convex.
Under (A4), for any \(p \in \Delta^{L-1}\) with \(p \geq 0\), consumer \(i\)’s wealth \(w_i(p) = p \cdot \omega_i \geq 0\). Under strong monotonicity, if \(p_\ell = 0\) for some \(\ell\), the demand for good \(\ell\) is unbounded (boundary condition). To avoid dealing with the boundary of \(\Delta^{L-1}\), we may work with the relative interior \(\text{int}(\Delta^{L-1})\) or use a compactness argument (truncate demands at a large bound, prove equilibrium of the truncated economy, take limits).
For simplicity, assume \(p \in \Delta^{L-1}_{++} = \{p \in \Delta^{L-1} : p_\ell > 0\; \forall \ell\}\) (strictly positive prices) and use the closure argument at the end.

Step 2: Define the excess demand and the price-adjustment correspondence.
Under (A1)–(A3), \(x_i(p, w_i(p))\) is a continuous function of \(p\) (since preferences are strictly convex, demand is single-valued). Therefore \(z(p) = \sum_i x_i(p, p \cdot \omega_i) - \bar{\omega}\) is continuous.
Define the price-adjustment correspondence \(\psi: \Delta^{L-1} \rightrightarrows \Delta^{L-1}\) by: \[\psi(p) = \arg\max_{q \in \Delta^{L-1}} q \cdot z(p).\] Interpretation: \(\psi\) assigns to each price vector the set of price vectors that maximize the value of aggregate excess demand. This pushes prices up for goods in excess demand (\(z_\ell > 0\)) and down for goods in excess supply (\(z_\ell < 0\)) — a competitive tatonnement logic.

Step 3: Show \(\psi\) satisfies the conditions of Kakutani’s theorem.
\(\psi: \Delta^{L-1} \rightrightarrows \Delta^{L-1}\) maps the compact convex set \(\Delta^{L-1}\) into itself.

Nonempty values: \(q \mapsto q \cdot z(p)\) is a continuous linear function on the compact set \(\Delta^{L-1}\); by the Weierstrass theorem, it has a maximum. So \(\psi(p) \neq \emptyset\).
Convex values: \(q \cdot z(p)\) is linear in \(q\), so its set of maximizers on \(\Delta^{L-1}\) is a convex face of the simplex (possibly a singleton if the maximum is at a vertex).
Closed graph (UHC): Let \(p^n \to p\), \(q^n \to q\) with \(q^n \in \psi(p^n)\). Then \(q^n \cdot z(p^n) \geq r \cdot z(p^n)\) for all \(r \in \Delta^{L-1}\). By continuity of \(z\) and taking limits: \(q \cdot z(p) \geq r \cdot z(p)\) for all \(r\). So \(q \in \psi(p)\), confirming the closed graph.

By Kakutani’s theorem, \(\psi\) has a fixed point \(p^* \in \psi(p^*)\), i.e., \(p^* \in \arg\max_{q \in \Delta^{L-1}} q \cdot z(p^*)\).

Step 4: Show the fixed point is a Walrasian equilibrium.
At the fixed point \(p^*\), since \(p^* \in \arg\max_{q \in \Delta^{L-1}} q \cdot z(p^*)\), it must be that \(p^*\) maximizes \(q \cdot z(p^*)\) over the simplex. The maximum of a linear function over a simplex is achieved when all weight is placed on the largest component. Therefore: \[p_\ell^* = 0 \text{ if } z_\ell(p^*) < \max_k z_k(p^*).\]
Claim: \(z(p^*) \leq 0\) componentwise and \(p_\ell^* z_\ell(p^*) = 0\) for all \(\ell\).
Suppose for contradiction that \(z_k(p^*) > 0\) for some good \(k\). Then \(k\) achieves the maximum of \(z_\ell(p^*)\) over \(\ell\) (or one of the maxima). By the fixed-point condition, \(p_k^* > 0\) (otherwise we could improve by shifting weight to \(k\)).
Now use Walras’ Law: \(p^* \cdot z(p^*) = 0\). Since \(p_k^* > 0\) and \(z_k(p^*) > 0\), the term \(p_k^* z_k(p^*)\) is strictly positive. For the sum to be zero, there must exist some \(\ell\) with \(p_\ell^* z_\ell(p^*) < 0\), i.e., \(z_\ell(p^*) < 0\) (excess supply of good \(\ell\)).
But at the fixed point, the price-adjustment rule puts zero weight on goods in excess supply: \(p_\ell^* = 0\) (since \(\ell\) does not achieve the maximum of excess demand). This means \(p_\ell^* = 0\) and \(z_\ell(p^*) < 0\), so \(p_\ell^* z_\ell(p^*) = 0\).
Therefore the only goods with \(p_\ell^* z_\ell(p^*) < 0\) must have \(p_\ell^* = 0\), and hence \(p_\ell^* z_\ell(p^*) = 0\). But then the sum \(\sum_\ell p_\ell^* z_\ell(p^*)\) cannot include negative terms — all goods with positive price have \(p_\ell^* z_\ell(p^*) \geq 0\), and by Walras’ Law the sum is zero. Hence \(p_\ell^* z_\ell(p^*) = 0\) for all \(\ell\).
For goods with \(p_\ell^* > 0\): \(z_\ell(p^*) = 0\) (market clearing). For goods with \(p_\ell^* = 0\): the boundary condition (excess demand \(\to +\infty\) as price \(\to 0\)) would require \(z_\ell(p^*) \leq 0\), otherwise no equilibrium at zero price. Under strong monotonicity, \(z_\ell(p^*) \leq 0\) when \(p_\ell^* = 0\) can be sustained only if \(\ell\) is in excess supply (free disposal or satiation at zero price).
Under the assumption of interior endowments (A4) and strong monotonicity, all prices are strictly positive at equilibrium (\(p^* \in \text{int}(\Delta^{L-1})\)), so \(z(p^*) = 0\). This establishes market clearing. \(\blacksquare\)
crumbs: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium

title: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium
text:
42.4 Conditions That Can Be Relaxed
Non-convex preferences with a continuum of agents (Aumann 1966). If there is a continuum of agents (measure space of consumers), individual non-convexities are “averaged out” by the law of large numbers. The aggregate excess demand correspondence is convex-valued even when individual demands are not. Kakutani applies to the aggregate correspondence.
Non-convex production sets with free entry. If firms can freely enter and exit, the production possibility frontier is convex at the aggregate level (even with increasing returns at the firm level), because the economy can replicate firms at any scale. This is the basis of “contestable markets” theory.
crumbs: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium

title: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium
text:
42.5 Uniqueness






ImportantTheorem 6.3.2 — Gross Substitutes Implies Uniqueness



If all goods are gross substitutes — \(\partial z_\ell(p)/\partial p_k > 0\) for all \(\ell \neq k\) — then the Walrasian equilibrium is unique.








TipProof





Proof sketch. Under gross substitutes, the excess demand function satisfies a diagonal dominance condition that prevents multiple zeros. Specifically, the gross substitutes condition implies the Jacobian matrix \(Dz(p)\) has all off-diagonal elements positive and (by Walras’ Law and homogeneity) is negative semi-definite. This structure implies that the price adjustment dynamics \(\dot{p}_\ell = z_\ell(p)\) are globally stable, ruling out multiple equilibria. The formal proof uses the theory of \(P\)-matrices or monotone maps. \(\blacksquare\)



Sonnenschein-Mantel-Debreu Theorem (sketch). For general preferences satisfying the standard assumptions, the aggregate excess demand function \(z: \Delta^{L-1} \to \mathbb{R}^L\) can be any continuous function satisfying Walras’ Law and homogeneity. No further restrictions are implied by individual rationality. This “anything goes” result means that uniqueness, stability, and comparative statics cannot be derived from first principles in general GE models — they require additional restrictions (gross substitutes, representative agent, etc.).
crumbs: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium

title: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium
text:
42.6 Regular Economies
A regular economy is one where \(Dz(p^*)\) has full rank at every equilibrium \(p^*\). By the implicit function theorem, in a regular economy: - The number of equilibria is finite and odd (by the index theorem) - Each equilibrium is locally unique - Equilibria vary smoothly with parameters (endowments, preferences)
Regular economies are generic (the set of economies with irregular equilibria has measure zero in the space of economies).
crumbs: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium

title: 41  6.3 Existence of Walrasian Equilibrium
text:
42.7 Cross-References

1.4 Fixed Point Theorems — Kakutani’s theorem is the mathematical engine of the proof
6.1 Exchange Economies — Excess demand properties (continuity, Walras’ Law) used in Step 2
6.4 Welfare Theorems — Existence is a precondition for the welfare theorems to be meaningful
6.2 Arrow Debreu Economy — Same proof applies to production economies

title: 42  6.4 Welfare Theorems
text: 43 6.4 Welfare Theorems

title: 42  6.4 Welfare Theorems
text:
43.1 Context
The welfare theorems are the mathematical formalization of Adam Smith’s invisible hand. The First Welfare Theorem states that competitive equilibrium is efficient — no central planner can improve on the market outcome (in the Pareto sense). The Second Welfare Theorem states the converse: any efficient allocation can be achieved by the market, provided the government redistributes wealth appropriately via lump-sum transfers. Together, they provide the normative foundation of competitive market theory: markets should be trusted to allocate resources efficiently, and distributional concerns should be addressed through redistribution, not interference with prices.
The First Welfare Theorem is a remarkably robust result — it requires only local nonsatiation, not convexity. The Second Welfare Theorem requires convexity, and its limitations (informational requirements of lump-sum transfers, non-convexities in reality) motivate the second-best theory.

title: 42  6.4 Welfare Theorems
text:
43.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 6.4.1 — Pareto Optimality



A feasible allocation \((x, y) = ((x_i)_{i \in I}, (y_j)_{j \in J})\) satisfying \(\sum_i x_i = \bar{\omega} + \sum_j y_j\) is Pareto optimal (PO) if there is no other feasible allocation \((\tilde{x}, \tilde{y})\) such that: - \(u_i(\tilde{x}_i) \geq u_i(x_i)\) for all \(i \in I\) - \(u_j(\tilde{x}_j) > u_j(x_j)\) for at least one \(j \in I\)
An allocation is PO if and only if it solves: \[\max_{(x,y) \text{ feasible}} u_1(x_1) \text{ subject to } u_i(x_i) \geq \bar{u}_i\; \forall i \geq 2,\] for some vector of utility bounds \((\bar{u}_2, \ldots, \bar{u}_n)\).

title: 42  6.4 Welfare Theorems
text:
43.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 6.4.1 — First Welfare Theorem (1WT)



If preferences \(\succeq_i\) satisfy local nonsatiation for all \(i\), then every Walrasian equilibrium allocation \((x^*, y^*, p^*)\) is Pareto optimal.








TipProof





Suppose for contradiction that \((x^*, y^*, p^*)\) is a Walrasian equilibrium but is not Pareto optimal. Then there exists a feasible allocation \((\tilde{x}, \tilde{y})\) such that \(u_i(\tilde{x}_i) \geq u_i(x_i^*)\) for all \(i\), with strict inequality for some \(k\).
Step 1: Every consumer’s preferred bundle costs at least as much as their equilibrium bundle.
Since \(x_i^* \in x_i(p^*, w_i^*)\) (utility maximization), any bundle \(\tilde{x}_i\) preferred to \(x_i^*\) cannot be affordable at \((p^*, w_i^*)\): \[u_i(\tilde{x}_i) > u_i(x_i^*) \implies p^* \cdot \tilde{x}_i > w_i^* = p^* \cdot x_i^*. \tag{1}\] (If \(\tilde{x}_i\) were affordable and preferred, it would have been chosen over \(x_i^*\).)
Step 2: Any bundle as good as \(x_i^*\) costs at least as much (for local nonsatiation).
For the consumer \(i\) with \(u_i(\tilde{x}_i) \geq u_i(x_i^*)\) but \(u_i(\tilde{x}_i) = u_i(x_i^*)\) (indifference), we need to show \(p^* \cdot \tilde{x}_i \geq p^* \cdot x_i^*\). Suppose not: \(p^* \cdot \tilde{x}_i < p^* \cdot x_i^* = w_i^*\). By local nonsatiation, there exists \(\tilde{x}_i'\) arbitrarily close to \(\tilde{x}_i\) with \(u_i(\tilde{x}_i') > u_i(\tilde{x}_i) = u_i(x_i^*)\) and \(p^* \cdot \tilde{x}_i' \leq w_i^*\) (by continuity of the budget set). But this means \(\tilde{x}_i'\) is affordable and preferred to \(x_i^*\), contradicting the optimality of \(x_i^*\). Therefore: \[u_i(\tilde{x}_i) \geq u_i(x_i^*) \implies p^* \cdot \tilde{x}_i \geq p^* \cdot x_i^*. \tag{2}\]
Step 3: Aggregate and derive a contradiction.
Summing (2) over all \(i\) with \(u_i(\tilde{x}_i) \geq u_i(x_i^*)\), and using (1) for the consumer \(k\) with strict preference: \[\sum_{i \in I} p^* \cdot \tilde{x}_i > \sum_{i \in I} p^* \cdot x_i^* = \sum_{i \in I} w_i^* = p^* \cdot \bar{\omega} + \sum_{j \in J} \pi_j(p^*).\]
Since \((\tilde{x}, \tilde{y})\) is feasible: \(\sum_i \tilde{x}_i = \bar{\omega} + \sum_j \tilde{y}_j\). Therefore: \[p^* \cdot \left(\bar{\omega} + \sum_j \tilde{y}_j\right) > p^* \cdot \bar{\omega} + \sum_j \pi_j(p^*)\] \[\sum_j p^* \cdot \tilde{y}_j > \sum_j \pi_j(p^*) = \sum_j \max_{y \in Y_j} p^* \cdot y.\]
But \(\tilde{y}_j \in Y_j\) by feasibility, so \(p^* \cdot \tilde{y}_j \leq \pi_j(p^*)\) for each \(j\). Summing: \(\sum_j p^* \cdot \tilde{y}_j \leq \sum_j \pi_j(p^*)\) — a contradiction. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



The proof uses only: - Individual optimization (demand and profit maximization) - Local nonsatiation (to establish that preferred bundles are more expensive) - Feasibility of the alternative allocation - No convexity is required.








ImportantTheorem 6.4.2 — Second Welfare Theorem (2WT)



Let \((\tilde{x}, \tilde{y})\) be a Pareto optimal allocation. Under the following assumptions: - Consumer preferences \(\succeq_i\) are convex and continuous for all \(i\) - Production sets \(Y_j\) are convex for all \(j\) - \(\tilde{x}_i \gg 0\) for all \(i\) (interior optimal consumption)
There exists a price vector \(p^* \in \mathbb{R}^L_{++}\) and wealth levels \(w_i^*\) with \(\sum_i w_i^* = p^* \cdot \bar{\omega} + \sum_j \pi_j(p^*)\) such that \((\tilde{x}, \tilde{y}, p^*)\) is a Walrasian equilibrium when consumers have wealth \(w_i^*\).








TipProof





The proof uses the separating hyperplane theorem to find the equilibrium price vector.
Step 1: Define the “better-than” sets.
For each consumer \(i\), define: \[\hat{P}_i = \{x_i \in \mathbb{R}^L_+ : u_i(x_i) \geq u_i(\tilde{x}_i)\}\] (the set of bundles at least as good as \(\tilde{x}_i\)).
By convexity of preferences, \(\hat{P}_i\) is a convex set. By continuity of \(u_i\), \(\hat{P}_i\) is closed. Since \(\tilde{x}_i \in \hat{P}_i\), it is nonempty.
Define: \[\hat{P} = \sum_{i \in I} \hat{P}_i = \left\{\sum_i x_i : x_i \in \hat{P}_i\right\}\] (the Minkowski sum of the individual better-than sets). \(\hat{P}\) is convex (sum of convex sets).
Step 2: Define the feasible set and apply the separating hyperplane theorem.
Define the aggregate feasible production set: \[Y = \bar{\omega} + \sum_{j \in J} Y_j = \left\{\bar{\omega} + \sum_j y_j : y_j \in Y_j\right\}.\] \(Y\) is convex (sum of convex sets).
Key claim: \(\hat{P} \cap \text{int}(Y) = \emptyset\).
Proof of claim: Suppose \(z \in \hat{P} \cap \text{int}(Y)\). Then \(z = \sum_i x_i'\) with \(u_i(x_i') \geq u_i(\tilde{x}_i)\) and \(z = \bar{\omega} + \sum_j y_j'\) with \(y_j' \in Y_j\), and \(z\) is in the interior of \(Y\). This means there exists \(\tilde{z} < z\) (componentwise) with \(\tilde{z} \in Y\) as well. But then the allocation with \(\sum_i x_i' = z > \tilde{z}\)… We need to be more careful.
Since \((\tilde{x}, \tilde{y})\) is Pareto optimal, there is no feasible allocation improving all consumers. If \(z \in \hat{P}\), then \(z = \sum_i x_i'\) with each \(x_i'\) weakly preferred to \(\tilde{x}_i\). If such a \(z\) were feasible (in \(Y = \bar\omega + \sum_j Y_j\)), then \((x', y')\) would be a feasible allocation weakly preferred by all, with feasibility following from \(\sum_i x_i' = \bar\omega + \sum_j y_j'\). Pareto optimality of \((\tilde{x}, \tilde{y})\) and the local nonsatiation would imply this is an improvement only if some consumer is strictly better off — but the definition of \(\hat{P}\) allows weak preference. For strict Pareto optimality to be contradicted, we need strict preference somewhere.
Using local nonsatiation more carefully: if \(z \in \hat{P}\) and \(z \in \text{int}(Y)\), there is a feasible \(z' = z - \epsilon \mathbf{1}\) for small \(\epsilon > 0\), still feasible. This allows us to redistribute slightly and still allocate \(\sum_i x_i' = z\), which is more than the feasible \(z'\), not less. The argument needs the interior condition to allow free disposal.
More precisely: if \(\sum_i \tilde{x}_i \in \text{int}(\bar{\omega} + \sum_j Y_j)\), then there exists a direction in which total resources could be reduced while remaining feasible, contradicting PO (we could reallocate the saved resources to strictly improve someone). Therefore \(\sum_i \tilde{x}_i\) is on the boundary of the feasible set, and \(\hat{P} \cap \text{int}(Y) = \emptyset\).
Step 3: Apply the separating hyperplane theorem.
Since \(\hat{P}\) and \(\text{int}(Y)\) are disjoint convex sets (with \(\hat{P}\) nonempty and \(\text{int}(Y)\) nonempty), by the separating hyperplane theorem there exists \(p^* \in \mathbb{R}^L \setminus \{0\}\) and \(\alpha \in \mathbb{R}\) such that: \[p^* \cdot z \geq \alpha \quad \forall z \in \hat{P}, \qquad p^* \cdot z \leq \alpha \quad \forall z \in Y.\]
Step 4: Show \(p^*\) is the equilibrium price vector.

Profit maximization: For each firm \(j\), since \(y_j \in Y_j \subset Y\) (adjusting the definition appropriately), \(p^* \cdot y_j \leq \alpha - p^* \cdot \bar{\omega}\), and equality is achieved at \(\tilde{y}_j\) (since \(\bar{\omega} + \tilde{y} \in Y\) and \(\sum_i \tilde{x}_i \in \hat{P}\) with \(p^* \cdot \sum_i \tilde{x}_i \geq \alpha \geq p^* \cdot (\bar{\omega} + \sum_j \tilde{y}_j)\), and by feasibility of \((\tilde{x}, \tilde{y})\) these are equal). Therefore \(\tilde{y}_j \in \arg\max_{y \in Y_j} p^* \cdot y\) for each \(j\).
Utility maximization: From the separation, \(p^* \cdot x_i \geq p^* \cdot \tilde{x}_i\) for any \(x_i\) weakly preferred to \(\tilde{x}_i\) (any \(x_i \in \hat{P}_i\) contributes at least \(p^* \cdot \tilde{x}_i\) to the sum \(p^* \cdot z\)). Therefore \(\tilde{x}_i\) minimizes expenditure over the upper contour set, which is equivalent to utility maximization (expenditure minimization and utility maximization are dual by convexity of preferences). With wealth \(w_i^* = p^* \cdot \tilde{x}_i\), consumer \(i\) optimally chooses \(\tilde{x}_i\).
Market clearing: Follows by construction: \(\sum_i \tilde{x}_i = \bar{\omega} + \sum_j \tilde{y}_j\).

Therefore \((\tilde{x}, \tilde{y}, p^*)\) is a Walrasian equilibrium with wealth redistribution \(w_i^* = p^* \cdot \tilde{x}_i\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: 42  6.4 Welfare Theorems
section: 43.4 Limitations of the Second Welfare Theorem
text:
43.4 Limitations of the Second Welfare Theorem
Convexity. The Second WT requires convex preferences and production sets. With non-convexities (increasing returns, indivisibilities), a Pareto optimum cannot generally be decentralized as a competitive equilibrium. This is the fundamental market failure of natural monopolies and economies of scale.
Lump-sum transfers. Implementing the 2WT requires lump-sum taxes and subsidies: wealth transfers that do not distort relative prices. In practice, lump-sum transfers are informationally demanding (the government must know individual endowments or productivities) and politically constrained. The second-best theory (Ramsey, Diamond-Mirrlees) studies optimal taxation when lump-sum transfers are unavailable.
Distributional concerns. Both welfare theorems are silent on distribution: PO allocations can be extremely unequal. The 2WT says any PO allocation (including one giving all resources to one person) is a WE under some redistribution. The choice among Pareto-optimal allocations requires a social welfare function (see Part VIII).

title: 42  6.4 Welfare Theorems
section: 43.5 Welfare Theorems with Externalities and Public Goods
text:
43.5 Welfare Theorems with Externalities and Public Goods
When utilities or production functions depend directly on others’ actions, the proof of the 1WT fails. Specifically, Step 1 fails: a bundle preferred by consumer \(i\) may cost less at equilibrium prices, because \(i\) benefits from a positive externality generated by \(j\)’s purchase.
The 1WT holds in the presence of externalities only if: - Pigouvian taxes correct the wedge between private and social marginal costs (see 6.5 Externalities and Public Goods) - Property rights are well-defined and bargaining is costless (Coase theorem)
Public goods: With nonrival goods, the competitive market cannot internalize the social value. The 1WT fails because a consumer’s private willingness to pay understates the social benefit (which is the sum of all consumers’ valuations). Lindahl pricing restores efficiency conceptually but is not incentive-compatible.

title: 42  6.4 Welfare Theorems
text:
43.6 Cross-References

6.1 Exchange Economies — Walrasian equilibrium definition used in both theorems
6.3 Existence of Equilibrium — Existence is needed for the 1WT to be non-vacuous
6.5 Externalities and Public Goods — Market failures where 1WT breaks down

title: 43  6.5 Externalities and Public Goods
text: 44 6.5 Externalities and Public Goods
crumbs: 43  6.5 Externalities and Public Goods

title: 43  6.5 Externalities and Public Goods
text:
44.1 Context
Externalities and public goods are the two central examples of market failure in microeconomics — situations where competitive markets fail to achieve Pareto optimality, and the First Welfare Theorem does not apply. Externalities are present when the actions of one agent enter directly into the utility function or production set of another, outside of any market transaction. Public goods are characterized by nonrivalry and nonexcludability, meaning private provision is suboptimal because agents can free-ride. Both phenomena generate wedges between private and social incentives, and correcting these wedges is the subject of Pigouvian taxation, the Coase theorem, and mechanism design for public goods.
crumbs: 43  6.5 Externalities and Public Goods

title: 43  6.5 Externalities and Public Goods
text:
44.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 6.5.1 — Externality



A consumption externality is present when consumer \(i\)’s utility depends on the action of consumer \(j \neq i\): \[u_i = u_i(x_i, x_j) \quad \text{where } x_j \notin x_i\text{'s choice set.}\] A production externality is present when firm \(k\)’s production set \(Y_k\) depends on firm \(j\)’s production choice \(y_j\): \[Y_k = Y_k(y_j).\]
Externalities are negative if the external effect reduces the payoff (e.g., pollution), and positive if it increases the payoff (e.g., knowledge spillovers).








NoteDefinition 6.5.2 — Pigouvian Tax



A Pigouvian tax is a per-unit tax on an activity that generates a negative externality, equal to the marginal external cost evaluated at the social optimum. The tax internalizes the externality by equating private and social marginal costs.








NoteDefinition 6.5.3 — Public Good



A good \(g\) is a public good if it is: - Nonrival: Consumption by agent \(i\) does not reduce the amount available for agent \(j\). Formally, the same \(G\) enters all utility functions: \(u_i = u_i(x_i, G)\). - Nonexcludable: It is impossible (or prohibitively costly) to prevent any agent from consuming \(G\) once it is provided.








NoteDefinition 6.5.4 — Lindahl Equilibrium



A Lindahl equilibrium for a public good economy consists of: - A public good level \(G^*\) - Personalized prices \(\tau_i\) for each consumer \(i\) (the “Lindahl price”: consumer \(i\)’s share of the cost per unit of \(G\))
such that: 1. Each consumer \(i\) maximizes \(u_i(x_i, G)\) subject to \(p \cdot x_i + \tau_i G = w_i\), yielding demand \(G_i^*(\tau_i)\) 2. All consumers demand the same quantity: \(G_1^*(\tau_1) = G_2^*(\tau_2) = \cdots = G_n^*(\tau_n) = G^*\) 3. Lindahl prices cover costs: \(\sum_{i=1}^n \tau_i = c\) (the marginal cost of providing \(G\))
crumbs: 43  6.5 Externalities and Public Goods

title: 43  6.5 Externalities and Public Goods
text:
44.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 6.5.1 — Market Failure with Negative Externalities



In a competitive equilibrium with negative production externalities, the equilibrium output is above the socially optimal level.








TipProof





Consider two firms: firm 1 produces output \(y_1\) with profit \(\pi_1(y_1)\); firm 2 has profit \(\pi_2(y_2, y_1)\) with \(\partial \pi_2/\partial y_1 < 0\) (firm 1’s output harms firm 2, e.g., upstream pollution).
Private equilibrium: Firm 1 ignores the external cost. It chooses \(y_1^{CE}\) to maximize \(\pi_1(y_1)\): \[\pi_1'(y_1^{CE}) = 0. \tag{1}\]
Social optimum: A social planner maximizes total profit \(\pi_1(y_1) + \pi_2(y_2, y_1)\). The FOC for \(y_1\) is: \[\pi_1'(y_1^*) + \frac{\partial \pi_2(y_2, y_1^*)}{\partial y_1} = 0. \tag{2}\]
Since \(\partial \pi_2/\partial y_1 < 0\), equation (2) requires \(\pi_1'(y_1^*) > 0\), which (under concavity of \(\pi_1\)) implies \(y_1^* < y_1^{CE}\).
The competitive equilibrium overproduces relative to the social optimum. \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 6.5.2 — Pigouvian Tax Corrects the Externality



At the social optimum \(y^*\), a Pigouvian tax \(\tau^* = -\partial \pi_2(y_2^*, y_1^*)/\partial y_1\) (the marginal external cost) on firm 1’s output restores efficiency: the competitive equilibrium of the tax economy equals the social optimum.








TipProof





Under the Pigouvian tax \(\tau^*\), firm 1’s problem is: \[\max_{y_1} \pi_1(y_1) - \tau^* y_1.\] FOC: \[\pi_1'(y_1) - \tau^* = 0 \Rightarrow \pi_1'(y_1) = \tau^* = -\frac{\partial \pi_2}{\partial y_1}\bigg|_{y^*}.\] This coincides with the social optimum FOC (2). Therefore \(y_1 = y_1^*\) at the tax equilibrium. \(\blacksquare\)









ImportantTheorem 6.5.3 — Coase Theorem (Coase 1960)



In a two-agent economy with an externality, if: - Property rights over the externality are well-defined (assigned to either party) - Transactions costs are zero - Information is symmetric
Then bargaining between the agents leads to the Pareto-efficient outcome, regardless of the initial assignment of property rights. The assignment of rights affects the distribution of surplus but not its size.








TipProof





Let firm 1 generate a negative externality on firm 2. Total surplus is \(S(y_1) = \pi_1(y_1) + \pi_2(y_1)\), maximized at \(y_1^*\) (social optimum).
Case 1: Firm 1 has the right to pollute (no restriction on \(y_1\)). Firm 2 will offer to pay firm 1 to reduce output below \(y_1^{CE}\). The maximum payment firm 2 is willing to make for a reduction to \(y_1\) is \(\pi_2(y_1) - \pi_2(y_1^{CE}) > 0\) (for \(y_1 < y_1^{CE}\)). Firm 1 will accept any payment exceeding \(\pi_1(y_1^{CE}) - \pi_1(y_1) > 0\).
The gains from reduction are: \([\pi_2(y_1) - \pi_2(y_1^{CE})] - [\pi_1(y_1^{CE}) - \pi_1(y_1)] = S(y_1) - S(y_1^{CE})\).
This is positive for all \(y_1 \in (y_1^*, y_1^{CE})\), so there are mutually beneficial gains from reducing output toward \(y_1^*\). Bargaining exhausts all gains from trade, leading to \(y_1^*\).
Case 2: Firm 2 has the right to be free of pollution (firm 1 can only produce with firm 2’s consent). Now firm 1 will pay firm 2 to allow some production. The same argument applies: gains from trade are \(S(y_1) - S(0)\) for \(y_1 \in (0, y_1^*)\), and bargaining leads to \(y_1^*\).
In both cases the efficient quantity \(y_1^*\) is achieved; only the distribution of profits differs. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Limitations of the Coase theorem: - Transactions costs: With many agents (e.g., air pollution affecting millions), negotiation is prohibitively costly. - Information asymmetry: If agents have private information about costs or damages, bargaining may be inefficient (Myerson-Satterthwaite theorem applies). - Wealth effects: With non-transferable utility or income effects on preferences, the efficient allocation depends on the initial rights assignment. - Hold-up problem: In long-term relationships, the possibility of renegotiation distorts ex-ante investment incentives.








ImportantTheorem 6.5.4 — Samuelson Condition for Public Good Optimality



The Pareto-optimal level of a public good \(G^*\) satisfies the Samuelson condition: \[\sum_{i=1}^n MRS_i^{Gx} = MC(G),\] where \(MRS_i^{Gx} = \frac{\partial u_i/\partial G}{\partial u_i/\partial x_i}\) is consumer \(i\)’s marginal rate of substitution between the public good and the private good, and \(MC(G)\) is the marginal cost of providing \(G\).








TipProof





Consider a \(2\)-consumer economy with one private good \(x\) and one public good \(G\), produced from private good at marginal cost \(c\) (constant). The feasibility constraint is \(x_1 + x_2 + cG = \bar{x}\) (total endowment).
A Pareto optimum maximizes \(u_1(x_1, G)\) subject to \(u_2(x_2, G) = \bar{u}_2\) and the feasibility constraint.
Lagrangian: \[\mathcal{L} = u_1(x_1, G) + \lambda[u_2(x_2, G) - \bar{u}_2] + \mu[\bar{x} - x_1 - x_2 - cG].\]
FOCs: \[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} = \frac{\partial u_1}{\partial x_1} - \mu = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{\partial u_1}{\partial x_1} = \mu.\] \[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_2} = \lambda\frac{\partial u_2}{\partial x_2} - \mu = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{\partial u_2}{\partial x_2} = \mu/\lambda.\] \[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial G} = \frac{\partial u_1}{\partial G} + \lambda\frac{\partial u_2}{\partial G} - \mu c = 0.\]
From the third condition: \[\frac{\partial u_1/\partial G}{\mu} + \frac{\lambda \partial u_2/\partial G}{\mu} = c.\] Using \(\mu = \partial u_1/\partial x_1\) and \(\mu/\lambda = \partial u_2/\partial x_2\): \[\frac{\partial u_1/\partial G}{\partial u_1/\partial x_1} + \frac{\partial u_2/\partial G}{\partial u_2/\partial x_2} = c.\] This is \(MRS_1^{Gx} + MRS_2^{Gx} = c = MC(G)\), the Samuelson condition. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Private provision fails the Samuelson condition. Under private provision, each consumer contributes \(g_i\) to the public good (\(G = \sum_i g_i\)) and takes others’ contributions as given (Nash equilibrium of the voluntary contribution game). The private FOC for consumer \(i\) is: \[MRS_i^{Gx} = MC(G).\] This sets one consumer’s MRS equal to MC, whereas the social optimum requires the sum of all MRS to equal MC. Since all MRS are positive, private provision satisfies \(MRS_i < MC/n\) at the social optimum — the private market under-provides the public good.








ImportantTheorem 6.5.5 — Lindahl Equilibrium is Pareto Optimal



Every Lindahl equilibrium \((G^*, (\tau_i^*), (x_i^*))\) is Pareto optimal.








TipProof





In a Lindahl equilibrium, each consumer \(i\) takes their personalized price \(\tau_i^*\) as given and maximizes utility. The optimality condition for consumer \(i\) is: \[MRS_i^{Gx}(x_i^*, G^*) = \tau_i^*.\] Summing over \(i\): \[\sum_{i=1}^n MRS_i^{Gx} = \sum_{i=1}^n \tau_i^* = MC(G^*)\] (using the Lindahl pricing condition \(\sum_i \tau_i^* = MC\)).
This is precisely the Samuelson condition for Pareto optimality. Therefore the Lindahl allocation is Pareto optimal. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Free-rider problem. Although the Lindahl equilibrium is efficient, it requires consumers to reveal their true willingness to pay for the public good. In practice, consumers have an incentive to underreport their valuation: if they declare \(MRS_i = 0\), they pay \(\tau_i = 0\) and still consume \(G^*\) (once others have financed it). This incentive to free-ride makes the Lindahl prices non-dominant-strategy incentive compatible.
crumbs: 43  6.5 Externalities and Public Goods

title: 43  6.5 Externalities and Public Goods
text:
44.4 Clarke-Groves Mechanism (Preview)
The Clarke-Groves mechanism (Clarke 1971, Groves 1973) is a direct revelation mechanism for public goods that elicits truthful reporting as a dominant strategy:

Each consumer reports a valuation \(\hat{v}_i(G)\) for the public good.
The public good level \(G^*\) is chosen to maximize total reported surplus: \(\sum_i \hat{v}_i(G) - C(G)\).
Consumer \(i\)’s tax is \(C(G^*) - \sum_{j \neq i} \hat{v}_j(G^*)\) — they pay the “pivotal cost”: the difference between total cost and others’ total benefit.

Under this mechanism, each consumer’s dominant strategy is to report truthfully (\(\hat{v}_i = v_i\)), because the tax does not depend on their own report — only on others’ reports. The mechanism implements the Samuelson optimum in dominant strategies.
The budget balancing problem: Clarke-Groves may run a surplus or deficit. The Groves-Ledyard mechanism addresses budget balance but uses Nash rather than dominant strategy implementation. The fundamental tension between efficiency, incentive compatibility, and budget balance is formalized in mechanism design (Part VII).
crumbs: 43  6.5 Externalities and Public Goods

title: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium
section: 45.1 Problem 1 — Walrasian Equilibrium in a 2×2 Exchange Economy
text:
45.1 Problem 1 — Walrasian Equilibrium in a 2×2 Exchange Economy
Consider a pure exchange economy with 2 consumers and 2 goods. Consumer 1 has utility \(u_1(x_{11}, x_{12}) = \min\{x_{11}, x_{12}\}\) (Leontief preferences) and endowment \(\omega_1 = (4, 2)\). Consumer 2 has utility \(u_2(x_{21}, x_{22}) = x_{21} + 2x_{22}\) (linear preferences) and endowment \(\omega_2 = (2, 4)\).
(a) Draw the Edgeworth box. Identify the contract curve (set of Pareto optima) geometrically and algebraically.
(b) Derive each consumer’s Walrasian demand as a function of prices \((p_1, p_2)\) and wealth.
(c) Set up the market clearing conditions. Use Walras’ Law to reduce to a single equation.
(d) Solve for the equilibrium price ratio \(p_1/p_2\) and the equilibrium allocation \((x_1^*, x_2^*)\).
(e) Verify that the equilibrium allocation is Pareto optimal (by checking it lies on the contract curve). Interpret: what does it mean that a competitive equilibrium is Pareto optimal for this specific economy?
(f) Suppose the initial endowment is redistributed so that \(\omega_1 = (3,3)\) and \(\omega_2 = (3,3)\). Find the new equilibrium. Does the equilibrium price ratio change? Explain why or why not.
crumbs: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium

title: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium
section: 45.2 Problem 2 — Walras’ Law and Market Clearing Reduction
text:
45.2 Problem 2 — Walras’ Law and Market Clearing Reduction
Consider a three-good, two-consumer exchange economy. Suppose the aggregate excess demand function satisfies:
\[z_1(p) = \frac{3p_2 - 2p_1}{p_1 + p_2}, \quad z_2(p) = \frac{2p_1 - 3p_2 + p_3}{p_1 + p_2 + p_3}, \quad z_3(p) = ?\]
(a) Verify that \(z_1\) and \(z_2\) are homogeneous of degree zero in \((p_1, p_2, p_3)\).
(b) Using Walras’ Law (\(p \cdot z(p) = 0\) for all \(p \gg 0\)), derive \(z_3(p)\) explicitly. Show all algebraic steps.
(c) Solve for the Walrasian equilibrium prices \((p_1^*, p_2^*, p_3^*)\) by setting \(z_1 = z_2 = 0\) (Walras’ Law ensures \(z_3 = 0\) automatically). Normalize \(p_1 = 1\).
(d) Check whether the boundary condition holds for all three goods: as \(p_\ell \to 0\), does \(z_\ell(p) \to +\infty\)?
(e) Now suppose a tax of \(t > 0\) per unit is imposed on good 3, wedging the consumer price above the producer price: \(p_3^c = p_3^p + t\). How does the equilibrium analysis change? Write out the modified market clearing conditions. Does Walras’ Law still hold?
crumbs: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium

title: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium
section: 45.3 Problem 3 — First Welfare Theorem from Scratch
text:
45.3 Problem 3 — First Welfare Theorem from Scratch
Let \(\mathcal{E} = ((u_i, \omega_i)_{i=1}^{n})\) be an exchange economy where all consumers have locally nonsatiated preferences. Let \((x^*, p^*)\) be any Walrasian equilibrium.
(a) State formally what it means for preferences to satisfy local nonsatiation. Prove that local nonsatiation implies the budget constraint is binding at any optimum: \(p \cdot x_i^* = p \cdot \omega_i\).
(b) Prove the First Welfare Theorem: \((x^*)\) is Pareto optimal.
(c) The proof in (b) uses the fact that any preferred bundle must be unaffordable. In two steps: first show this for bundles strictly preferred to \(x_i^*\); then use local nonsatiation to extend it to bundles weakly preferred.
(d) Identify precisely where the proof would fail if local nonsatiation were dropped. Construct an explicit counterexample: an economy without local nonsatiation where a Walrasian equilibrium exists but is not Pareto optimal.
(e) Does the First Welfare Theorem hold if some consumer \(i\) has satiated preferences for some goods? Explain carefully with reference to the proof.
crumbs: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium

title: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium
section: 45.4 Problem 4 — Failure of the Second Welfare Theorem with Non-Convex Preferences
text:
45.4 Problem 4 — Failure of the Second Welfare Theorem with Non-Convex Preferences
Let there be 1 consumer and 2 goods. The consumer has utility function: \[u(x_1, x_2) = \max\{x_1, x_2\}\] and total endowment \(\bar{\omega} = (1, 1)\) (so the consumer owns everything).
(a) Show that \(u\) is not quasi-concave. Sketch the upper contour sets in \(\mathbb{R}^2_+\).
(b) Identify all Pareto-optimal allocations (there is only one consumer, so any feasible allocation is trivially Pareto optimal). In particular, identify the allocations \((1, 0)\) and \((1/2, 1/2)\).
(c) For the allocation \(\tilde{x} = (1, 0)\): show that there exists no price vector \(p^* \in \mathbb{R}^2_{++}\) such that \(\tilde{x}\) is the demand at prices \(p^*\) and wealth \(w = p^* \cdot \tilde{x}\). (Hint: compute the consumer’s demand at any price and show \(\tilde{x}\) is never optimal.)
(d) For the allocation \(\tilde{x} = (0, 1)\): repeat (c).
(e) Conclude that the Second Welfare Theorem fails for non-convex preferences. Explain geometrically why the separating hyperplane argument breaks down: which step of the proof in 6.4 Welfare Theorems fails?
(f) Discuss: in a two-consumer economy with non-convex preferences, can any Pareto-optimal allocation be decentralized as a Walrasian equilibrium with lump-sum transfers? When might it be possible even without global convexity?
crumbs: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium

title: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium
section: 45.5 Problem 5 — Pigouvian Tax in a Specific Externality Model
text:
45.5 Problem 5 — Pigouvian Tax in a Specific Externality Model
Consider two firms in an economy with one input (labor, price normalized to 1) and two outputs: - Firm 1 produces steel: \(y_1 = \sqrt{L_1}\) (tons of steel from \(L_1\) units of labor) - Firm 2 produces fish: \(y_2 = \sqrt{L_2} - \gamma y_1\) (tons of fish, reduced by firm 1’s steel output at rate \(\gamma > 0\))
Output prices are \(p_1 = 4\) (steel) and \(p_2 = 2\) (fish). Both firms are price-takers.
(a) Derive each firm’s private profit-maximizing labor demand \(L_i^{CE}\) and output \(y_i^{CE}\).
(b) Write out the social planner’s problem: maximize total profit \(\pi_1 + \pi_2\) by choosing \((L_1, L_2)\) jointly.
(c) Solve for the socially optimal \((L_1^*, L_2^*, y_1^*, y_2^*)\).
(d) Compute the Pigouvian tax $^* = $ marginal external cost of steel production at the social optimum. Verify that a competitive equilibrium where firm 1 faces per-unit tax \(\tau^*\) on output \(y_1\) achieves the social optimum.
(e) Compute the change in welfare from implementing the Pigouvian tax: compare total profits under \((y_1^{CE}, y_2^{CE})\) and \((y_1^*, y_2^*)\). If \(\gamma = 1\), what is the numerical welfare gain?
crumbs: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium

title: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium
section: 45.6 Problem 6 — Lindahl Equilibrium and Free-Rider Incentives
text:
45.6 Problem 6 — Lindahl Equilibrium and Free-Rider Incentives
Consider a two-consumer economy with one private good \(x\) (numeraire) and one public good \(G\). Consumer 1 has utility \(u_1(x_1, G) = x_1 + 2\ln G\) and consumer 2 has \(u_2(x_2, G) = x_2 + 3\ln G\). The public good costs \(c = 1\) unit of private good per unit of \(G\). Both consumers have wealth \(w_i = 10\).
(a) Compute the socially optimal public good level \(G^*\) using the Samuelson condition \(\sum_i MRS_i^{Gx} = MC(G)\).
(b) Derive the Lindahl prices \((\tau_1^*, \tau_2^*)\) and verify \(\tau_1^* + \tau_2^* = 1 = MC(G^*)\).
(c) Verify that both consumers voluntarily demand \(G^*\) at the Lindahl prices: at \(\tau_i^*\), consumer \(i\)’s optimal \(G_i^*(\tau_i^*) = G^*\).
(d) Now consider private (voluntary) provision. Each consumer \(i\) contributes \(g_i \geq 0\) to the public good; \(G = g_1 + g_2\). Consumer \(i\) takes \(g_j\) as given. Derive the Nash equilibrium level of private provision \(G^{NE}\). Compare with \(G^*\) and confirm \(G^{NE} < G^*\).
(e) Suppose consumer 1 knows both willingness-to-pay values and tries to design a “contribution scheme” to implement \(G^*\). Show that consumer 2 has an incentive to misreport (free-ride) even in the Lindahl scheme if \(\tau_i\) is determined by reported valuations. This illustrates why Lindahl equilibrium is not dominant-strategy incentive compatible.
(f) Describe qualitatively how the Clarke-Groves mechanism resolves the incentive problem. What is the “pivotal tax” consumer 2 would face under Clarke-Groves? Is the mechanism budget-balanced?
crumbs: 44  Problem Set — Part VI: General Equilibrium

title: 45  Part VII — Information and Incentives
text: 46 Part VII — Information and Incentives
crumbs: 45  Part VII — Information and Incentives

title: 45  Part VII — Information and Incentives
text:
46.1 Context
Classical competitive theory assumes that all agents share common knowledge of the economic environment, including each other’s preferences, endowments, and actions. This assumption is almost never satisfied in practice. Information economics studies what happens when agents possess private information — about their own types, their actions, or exogenous shocks — and how contracts, prices, and institutions respond to this asymmetry. The canonical organizing framework is the principal-agent problem: a less-informed principal (employer, buyer, regulator) designs a mechanism to influence the behavior of a better-informed agent (employee, seller, firm). The welfare losses relative to the full-information benchmark — second-best inefficiencies, information rents, distortions — are the central objects of study.
The discipline divides along two fundamental dimensions of information asymmetry:

Hidden action (moral hazard): The principal cannot observe the agent’s choice of action (effort, care, investment). The friction is that rewarding outcomes perfectly exposes a risk-averse agent to risk, while insuring the agent perfectly destroys effort incentives. The design problem is to trade off incentive provision against risk sharing.
Hidden type (adverse selection): The agent has private information about a fixed characteristic (productivity, valuation, cost) that the principal cannot observe. The friction is that the principal cannot tailor contracts to individual types. The design problem is to construct a menu of contracts that induces agents to self-select, accepting that higher-type agents will earn information rents and lower-type agents will face distorted allocations.

These two branches generate the sub-disciplines of screening (principal moves first, designs menu), signaling (agent moves first, chooses costly action to reveal type), and mechanism design (general analysis of what outcomes are achievable under any incentive-compatible mechanism). The Revelation Principle is the organizing theorem: it collapses the space of mechanisms to direct, truth-telling mechanisms without loss of generality. Myerson’s treatment culminates in the characterization of revenue-maximizing auctions and the impossibility of efficient bilateral trade (Myerson-Satterthwaite), showing that private information has real welfare costs that cannot be contracted away.
A sixth note extends the framework to the aggregation of dispersed information across many agents through markets, linking back to Hayek’s knowledge problem, the Grossman-Stiglitz paradox, and the Kyle model of strategic informed trading.

title: 45  Part VII — Information and Incentives
text:
46.4 Conceptual Map
\[\text{Asymmetric Information} \begin{cases} \text{Hidden Action} \to \text{Moral Hazard} \to \text{Insurance/Incentive Tradeoff} \\ \text{Hidden Type} \begin{cases} \text{Principal designs contract} \to \text{Screening/Adverse Selection} \\ \text{Agent signals type} \to \text{Signaling} \end{cases} \\ \text{General mechanism} \to \text{Mechanism Design} \to \text{Revelation Principle} \end{cases}\]
The unifying thread: second-best optimality. When information is asymmetric, the first-best allocation is generally unachievable. The optimal mechanism trades off allocative efficiency against information rents and risk, and the characterization of this tradeoff — using Lagrangian methods, envelope theorems, and monotone comparative statics — is the technical core of the field.
