title: 46  7.1 — The Principal-Agent Problem
text: 47 7.1 — The Principal-Agent Problem

title: 46  7.1 — The Principal-Agent Problem
text:
47.1 Context
The principal-agent problem is the foundational model of incentive theory. A principal (P) — an employer, investor, or buyer — hires an agent (A) to perform a task. The agent’s effort is either unobservable or non-contractible. The principal observes only an output or outcome that is imperfectly correlated with the agent’s effort. The central tension is between efficiency (the principal wants high effort) and incentives (inducing high effort requires imposing risk on the agent when output is noisy). Under full information — observable effort — the first-best solution is attained. Under hidden effort, the second-best contract must balance incentive provision against risk sharing, and the resulting allocation is strictly inefficient relative to the first-best.
This note develops the model from primitives, characterizes first-best and second-best contracts, proves Holmström’s informativeness principle, and derives the optimal linear contract in the LEN model.

title: 46  7.1 — The Principal-Agent Problem
text:
47.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 7.1.1 — Principal-Agent Environment



A principal-agent environment consists of: - Agent’s action (effort): \(e \in E \subseteq \mathbb{R}_+\) - Output: \(q = q(e, \theta)\) where \(\theta\) is an exogenous noise term with distribution \(F_\theta\); the principal observes \(q\) but not \((e, \theta)\) separately - Transfer: \(t: Q \to \mathbb{R}\), a payment from principal to agent contingent on observed output \(q\) - Agent’s utility: \(U^A(t, e) = u(t) - c(e)\) where \(u(\cdot)\) is increasing and concave (\(u'' \leq 0\)) and \(c(\cdot)\) is the cost of effort with \(c' > 0\), \(c'' \geq 0\) - Principal’s utility: \(U^P(q, t) = v(q - t)\) or, in the risk-neutral case, \(U^P = \mathbb{E}[q - t]\) - Reservation utility: \(\bar{u}\), the agent’s outside option








NoteDefinition 7.1.2 — Participation Constraint (PC)



The agent voluntarily accepts the contract if and only if: \[\mathbb{E}[u(t(q))] - c(e) \geq \bar{u}\] This is the individual rationality or participation constraint (PC). It will bind at the optimum: the principal extracts all surplus above the agent’s reservation value.








NoteDefinition 7.1.3 — Incentive Compatibility Constraint (IC)



Under hidden effort, the agent chooses effort to maximize their own utility. The incentive compatibility constraint (IC) requires that the contracted effort level \(e^*\) satisfies: \[e^* \in \arg\max_{e \in E} \left\{ \mathbb{E}[u(t(q(e,\theta)))] - c(e) \right\}\] Equivalently, the agent has no incentive to deviate to any \(e \neq e^*\) given the contract \(t(\cdot)\).








NoteDefinition 7.1.4 — First-Best vs. Second-Best




First-best allocation: the effort level \(e^{FB}\) and contract \(t^{FB}\) that maximize total surplus \(\mathbb{E}[q(e,\theta)] - c(e) - \text{(risk premium)}\) subject only to the PC, ignoring the IC. Achievable when effort is observable.
Second-best allocation: the effort level \(e^{SB}\) and contract \(t^{SB}\) that maximize total surplus subject to both PC and IC. Generically \(e^{SB} < e^{FB}\) when the agent is risk-averse.

title: 46  7.1 — The Principal-Agent Problem
text:
47.3 Main Results

47.3.1 First-Best Contract






ImportantProposition 7.1.1 — First-Best with Observable Effort



Suppose effort \(e\) is observable and contractible. The first-best contract \(t^{FB}(e)\) satisfies: 1. Efficiency: \(e^{FB}\) solves \(\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}[q(e,\theta)]}{\partial e} = c'(e^{FB})\) 2. Risk sharing: If the agent is risk-averse and the principal is risk-neutral, optimal risk sharing sets \(t = \bar{t}\) (a fixed wage). The principal absorbs all output risk. 3. PC binds: \(u(\bar{t}) - c(e^{FB}) = \bar{u}\)








TipProof





The principal maximizes \(\mathbb{E}[q(e,\theta)] - t\) subject to \(u(t) - c(e) \geq \bar{u}\) (PC), where here \(t\) is a fixed payment since \(e\) is observable. Since \(u\) is concave, risk-sharing optimality requires that the agent faces no output risk — a fixed wage \(\bar{t}\) is optimal.
The Lagrangian with respect to effort level \(e\) is: \[\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}[q(e,\theta)] - \bar{t} + \lambda(u(\bar{t}) - c(e) - \bar{u})\] FOC with respect to \(e\): \[\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}[q]}{\partial e} = \lambda c'(e)\] FOC with respect to \(\bar{t}\): \[-1 + \lambda u'(\bar{t}) = 0 \implies \lambda = \frac{1}{u'(\bar{t})}\] Substituting: \(\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}[q]}{\partial e} = \frac{c'(e)}{u'(\bar{t})}\). For a risk-neutral agent, \(u'(\bar{t}) = 1\), so \(\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}[q]}{\partial e} = c'(e^{FB})\).
PC binds at the optimum: if PC slack, the principal could reduce \(\bar{t}\), increasing their payoff while maintaining incentive compatibility (which is irrelevant here since \(e\) is observable). Therefore \(u(\bar{t}) - c(e^{FB}) = \bar{u}\).





47.3.2 Second-Best Contract: Risk-Neutral Agent






ImportantProposition 7.1.2 — Second-Best with Risk-Neutral Agent: First-Best is Achievable



If the agent is risk-neutral (\(u(t) = t\)), the principal can implement the first-best effort level even under hidden action by selling the firm to the agent.








TipProof





Consider the contract: \(t(q) = q - F\) (the agent receives all output minus a fixed fee \(F\)). Under this contract, the agent solves: \[\max_{e} \mathbb{E}[q(e,\theta)] - F - c(e)\] Since the agent is the residual claimant, their FOC is \(\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}[q]}{\partial e} = c'(e)\), which is exactly the first-best efficiency condition.
The PC pins down \(F\): the agent accepts iff \(\mathbb{E}[q(e^{FB},\theta)] - F - c(e^{FB}) \geq \bar{u}\). At the optimum, \(F = \mathbb{E}[q(e^{FB},\theta)] - c(e^{FB}) - \bar{u}\).
The principal’s payoff: \(F = \mathbb{E}[q(e^{FB},\theta)] - c(e^{FB}) - \bar{u}\), which is exactly the first-best total surplus minus the agent’s reservation value. Therefore the first-best is achieved.
The key is that with a risk-neutral agent, making the agent the residual claimant (the “sell the firm” or franchise contract) aligns incentives without imposing any risk premium cost.





47.3.3 Second-Best Contract: Risk-Averse Agent
With a risk-averse agent (\(u'' < 0\)), the “sell the firm” contract imposes undesirable risk on the agent. The optimal contract must trade off insurance (reducing the agent’s risk exposure) against incentives (making pay sensitive to output to preserve effort incentives).






ImportantProposition 7.1.3 — Optimal Second-Best Contract (Holmström 1979)



Suppose the agent is strictly risk-averse (\(u'' < 0\)), effort is binary \(e \in \{e_L, e_H\}\), the principal wants to implement \(e_H\), and output \(q\) has density \(f(q|e)\) with support \(Q\). The optimal contract \(t^*(q)\) solving the principal’s program \[\max_{t(\cdot)} \int_Q [q - t(q)] f(q|e_H) dq\] subject to \[\text{(PC):} \int_Q u(t(q)) f(q|e_H) dq - c(e_H) \geq \bar{u}\] \[\text{(IC):} \int_Q u(t(q)) f(q|e_H) dq - c(e_H) \geq \int_Q u(t(q)) f(q|e_L) dq - c(e_L)\] satisfies, for each \(q \in Q\): \[\frac{1}{u'(t^*(q))} = \lambda + \mu\left(1 - \frac{f(q|e_L)}{f(q|e_H)}\right)\] where \(\lambda \geq 0\) is the PC multiplier and \(\mu \geq 0\) is the IC multiplier.








TipProof





Form the Lagrangian, pointwise in \(q\): \[\mathcal{L} = \int_Q \left\{ [q - t(q)] f(q|e_H) + \lambda [u(t(q)) f(q|e_H) - \ldots] + \mu [u(t(q))(f(q|e_H) - f(q|e_L))] \right\} dq\]
Taking the first-order condition with respect to \(t(q)\) at each \(q\): \[-f(q|e_H) + \lambda u'(t(q)) f(q|e_H) + \mu u'(t(q))[f(q|e_H) - f(q|e_L)] = 0\] Divide by \(f(q|e_H) > 0\): \[-1 + \lambda u'(t(q)) + \mu u'(t(q))\left[1 - \frac{f(q|e_L)}{f(q|e_H)}\right] = 0\] Rearranging: \[u'(t(q)) \left[\lambda + \mu\left(1 - \frac{f(q|e_L)}{f(q|e_H)}\right)\right] = 1\] Therefore: \[\frac{1}{u'(t^*(q))} = \lambda + \mu\left(1 - \frac{f(q|e_L)}{f(q|e_H)}\right)\]
Interpretation: The left side is the inverse of marginal utility — a measure of how much the agent values an additional dollar of pay. The right side depends on the likelihood ratio \(f(q|e_L)/f(q|e_H)\). When this ratio is small (output \(q\) is much more likely under \(e_H\) than \(e_L\)), the bracketed term is large, so the agent receives high pay. When the ratio is large (\(q\) is much more likely under \(e_L\)), the agent receives low pay. This aligns the agent’s compensation with the likelihood that the agent exerted high effort.
Both \(\lambda, \mu \geq 0\) (multipliers on binding constraints). Typically both PC and IC bind at the optimum; if \(\mu = 0\) (IC not binding), then \(1/u'(t) = \lambda\) everywhere, meaning \(t\) is constant — full insurance. But constant \(t\) generally violates IC.





47.3.4 Holmström’s Informativeness Principle






ImportantTheorem 7.1.4 — Holmström’s Informativeness Principle (Holmström 1979)



Let \(q\) be the output observable in the contract, and let \(s\) be an additional signal also observable by the principal. The optimal contract should condition on \(s\) if and only if \(s\) is informative about effort given \(q\), i.e., if and only if: \[s \text{ is not redundant given } q: \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial s} \log f(q, s | e) \neq 0 \text{ for some } (q,s,e)\] Equivalently, \(s\) is informative about \(e\) given \(q\) iff \(s \not\perp e \mid q\), i.e., the joint density \(f(q,s|e)\) cannot be factored as \(g(q|e) \cdot h(s)\).








TipProof





Sufficiency (adding \(s\) helps): Suppose \(s\) is informative about \(e\) given \(q\). The contract conditioning on \((q, s)\) permits a Pareto improvement relative to the contract that conditions only on \(q\). Formally, the first-order condition for the optimal contract on \((q,s)\) becomes: \[\frac{1}{u'(t^*(q,s))} = \lambda + \mu\left(1 - \frac{f(q,s|e_L)}{f(q,s|e_H)}\right)\] If \(s\) is informative, the likelihood ratio \(f(q,s|e_L)/f(q,s|e_H)\) depends on \(s\) and is not constant in \(s\) for given \(q\). Therefore the optimal \(t^*(q,s)\) is not constant in \(s\) for fixed \(q\) — the additional signal genuinely affects compensation.
Including \(s\) achieves the same effort provision at lower cost to the principal (less risk imposed on the agent for the same IC satisfaction), constituting a Pareto improvement.
Necessity (dropping \(s\) is costless if it is uninformative): Suppose \(s\) is a sufficient statistic for \(e\) given \(q\): \(f(q,s|e) = g(q|e) \cdot h(s|q)\). Then the likelihood ratio: \[\frac{f(q,s|e_L)}{f(q,s|e_H)} = \frac{g(q|e_L)}{g(q|e_H)} \cdot \frac{h(s|q)}{h(s|q)} = \frac{g(q|e_L)}{g(q|e_H)}\] does not depend on \(s\). Therefore the optimal contract \(t^*(q,s)\) derived from the first-order condition does not vary with \(s\). Conditioning on \(s\) provides no additional benefit; the contract on \(q\) alone is equally optimal.









CautionRemark 7.1.1 — Applications of the Informativeness Principle



The informativeness principle has direct practical implications: - Relative performance evaluation: If a worker’s output depends on a common shock (industry conditions, weather), then observing a peer’s output provides information about the common shock, and hence about the worker’s effort net of the shock. Therefore relative performance evaluation is optimal. This is why CEO pay should be benchmarked against industry performance. - Noisy performance measures: Including a very noisy signal imposes risk on the agent with little incentive benefit; the informativeness criterion tells us the minimum benefit required to justify inclusion. - Multi-dimensional output: If output has several dimensions, the contract should include all dimensions that are informative about effort.




47.3.5 The LEN Model: Optimal Linear Contracts
The LEN (Linear contract, Exponential utility, Normal output) model provides a tractable closed-form characterization of optimal contracts when we restrict attention to linear transfer schemes.






NoteDefinition 7.1.5 — LEN Model Setup




Output: \(q = e + \varepsilon\) where \(\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\)
Agent’s utility: \(u(t, e) = -\exp(-r(t - c(e)))\) where \(r > 0\) is the coefficient of absolute risk aversion and \(c(e) = \frac{1}{2} c e^2\) is the cost of effort (\(c > 0\))
Linear contract: \(t = \alpha + \beta q\) where \(\alpha\) is the base salary and \(\beta \in [0,1]\) is the commission/incentive rate
Principal is risk-neutral: \(U^P = \mathbb{E}[q - t] = e(1 - \beta) - \alpha\)









ImportantProposition 7.1.5 — Optimal Linear Contract in the LEN Model



In the LEN model, the optimal incentive rate is: \[\beta^* = \frac{1}{1 + r \sigma^2 c}\] and the optimal base salary \(\alpha^*\) is determined by the binding PC: \(\alpha^* = \bar{u}/(-r)^{-1} - \beta^* e^* + \frac{1}{2}c(e^*)^2 + \frac{r\sigma^2(\beta^*)^2}{2}\), where \(e^* = \beta^*/c\).








TipProof





Step 1: Agent’s certainty equivalent. Under a linear contract \(t = \alpha + \beta q = \alpha + \beta(e + \varepsilon)\), the agent’s income is: \[t - c(e) = \alpha + \beta e - \frac{c e^2}{2} + \beta \varepsilon\] Since the agent has CARA utility with coefficient \(r\) and income is normally distributed with mean \(\alpha + \beta e - \frac{c e^2}{2}\) and variance \(\beta^2 \sigma^2\), the certainty equivalent (CE) of the lottery is: \[CE = \alpha + \beta e - \frac{c e^2}{2} - \frac{r \beta^2 \sigma^2}{2}\] (For CARA-normal, \(CE = \mu - \frac{r}{2}\text{Var}\).)
Step 2: Agent’s effort choice. The agent maximizes CE over \(e\): \[\max_e \left\{ \alpha + \beta e - \frac{c e^2}{2} - \frac{r \beta^2 \sigma^2}{2} \right\}\] FOC: \(\beta - c e = 0 \implies e^*(\beta) = \frac{\beta}{c}\) The agent exerts effort proportional to the incentive rate.
Step 3: Principal’s problem. Anticipating \(e^*(\beta) = \beta/c\), the principal maximizes: \[\max_{\alpha, \beta} \mathbb{E}[q - t] = \mathbb{E}[e^*(\beta) + \varepsilon - \alpha - \beta(e^*(\beta) + \varepsilon)]\] \[= e^*(\beta) - \alpha - \beta e^*(\beta) = \frac{\beta}{c}(1-\beta) - \alpha\] Subject to the PC (binding at optimum): \[CE = \alpha + \beta \cdot \frac{\beta}{c} - \frac{c}{2}\left(\frac{\beta}{c}\right)^2 - \frac{r\beta^2\sigma^2}{2} = \bar{u}\] \[\alpha = \bar{u} - \frac{\beta^2}{2c} + \frac{r\beta^2\sigma^2}{2}\] Wait, let me redo: \(CE = \alpha + \frac{\beta^2}{c} - \frac{c}{2}\cdot\frac{\beta^2}{c^2} - \frac{r\beta^2\sigma^2}{2} = \alpha + \frac{\beta^2}{2c} - \frac{r\beta^2\sigma^2}{2} = \bar{u}\), so \(\alpha = \bar{u} - \frac{\beta^2}{2c} + \frac{r\beta^2\sigma^2}{2}\).
Substituting into the principal’s objective: \[\Pi(\beta) = \frac{\beta}{c}(1-\beta) - \bar{u} + \frac{\beta^2}{2c} - \frac{r\beta^2\sigma^2}{2}\] \[= \frac{\beta}{c} - \frac{\beta^2}{c} + \frac{\beta^2}{2c} - \frac{r\beta^2\sigma^2}{2} - \bar{u}\] \[= \frac{\beta}{c} - \frac{\beta^2}{2c} - \frac{r\beta^2\sigma^2}{2} - \bar{u}\]
Step 4: Optimal \(\beta\). Taking FOC with respect to \(\beta\): \[\frac{d\Pi}{d\beta} = \frac{1}{c} - \frac{\beta}{c} - r\sigma^2\beta = 0\] \[\frac{1}{c} = \beta\left(\frac{1}{c} + r\sigma^2\right) = \beta \cdot \frac{1 + r\sigma^2 c}{c}\] \[\boxed{\beta^* = \frac{1}{1 + r\sigma^2 c}}\]
Comparative statics: - \(\frac{\partial \beta^*}{\partial r} = -\frac{\sigma^2 c}{(1+r\sigma^2 c)^2} < 0\): More risk-averse agents receive lower-powered incentives. - \(\frac{\partial \beta^*}{\partial \sigma^2} = -\frac{rc}{(1+r\sigma^2 c)^2} < 0\): Noisier environments call for weaker incentives. - \(\frac{\partial \beta^*}{\partial c} = -\frac{r\sigma^2}{(1+r\sigma^2 c)^2} < 0\): Higher effort cost \(\Rightarrow\) lower incentives. - As \(r \to 0\) or \(\sigma^2 \to 0\): \(\beta^* \to 1\) (full incentives, first-best).









CautionRemark 7.1.2 — Testable Predictions of the LEN Model



The formula \(\beta^* = 1/(1+r\sigma^2 c)\) generates clean empirical predictions: 1. Risk-incentive tradeoff: Incentive pay intensity should be decreasing in output uncertainty (\(\sigma^2\)). Tested in sharecropping (Allen 1985; Prendergast 1999), executive compensation (Core-Guay-Larcker 2003). 2. Risk aversion: More risk-averse agents (e.g., those with less wealth) should receive flatter contracts. Evidence from microfinance and agricultural contracts. 3. Agency identification problem: Prendergast (1999) notes that empirical studies find a positive correlation between risk and incentive intensity in many datasets — a “puzzle” for the simple model. Resolution: risk may proxy for difficult environments that require more delegation and hence more incentives. This debate illustrates the challenge of mapping structural parameters to observables.




47.3.6 Limited Liability






NoteDefinition 7.1.6 — Limited Liability Constraint



If the agent has limited liability (cannot pay the principal), the transfer must satisfy \(t(q) \geq \underline{t}\) for some minimum \(\underline{t}\) (often normalized to 0). This modifies the PC: \[\mathbb{E}[u(t(q))] - c(e) \geq \bar{u}\] but now the agent may earn rent even with the PC binding when the limited liability constraint is binding at low output levels.








CautionRemark 7.1.3 — Limited Liability and Agency Rents



With limited liability: - The principal cannot extract all surplus via a franchise fee (the “sell the firm” solution breaks down even for a risk-neutral agent) - The agent earns an expected rent above the reservation utility - The optimal contract bunches payments at the minimum \(\underline{t}\) for bad outcomes (limited liability binds) and provides incentive bonuses for good outcomes - This mechanism generates a U-shaped or increasing bonus structure and is central to models of executive compensation, debt contracts, and venture capital financing

title: 46  7.1 — The Principal-Agent Problem
text:
47.4 Economic Intuition
The principal-agent problem captures the fundamental tension between ownership and control. When the person who bears financial consequences (the principal) is not the same as the person who makes decisions (the agent), incentive problems arise. The owner of a firm wants profits maximized; a manager they hire may prefer a quieter life, empire-building, or excessive risk-taking.
The first-best solution is simple: make the agent the residual claimant. If the agent gets all the upside and downside, they will internalize all consequences of their actions. This works perfectly for a risk-neutral agent. The problem with risk-averse agents is that making them the residual claimant exposes them to risk they could not face under full information — the optimal first-best contract would have insured them against output fluctuations.
The second-best contract balances two forces: - Insurance motive: To reduce the risk-averse agent’s risk exposure, the principal would prefer a flat wage. - Incentive motive: To induce effort, the principal must make the agent’s pay sensitive to output.
The optimal contract lies between these extremes: \(\beta^* \in (0,1)\). The agent bears some output risk but not all of it. The efficiency loss relative to first-best is the cost of moral hazard — a welfare loss that is a genuine feature of environments with non-contractible effort, not a market failure that can be corrected by policy.

title: 46  7.1 — The Principal-Agent Problem
text:
47.5 Cross-References

7.2 Moral Hazard — extends to discrete effort, MLRC, multitasking
7.3 Adverse Selection and Screening — hidden type analog
7.5 Mechanism Design — general mechanism design; revelation principle applied to this setting

9.5 Structural vs Reduced Form — structural estimation of agency models

title: 47  7.2 — Moral Hazard
text: 48 7.2 — Moral Hazard

title: 47  7.2 — Moral Hazard
text:
48.1 Context
Moral hazard refers to the distortion of behavior that arises when an agent takes hidden actions — actions that affect outcomes but that the principal cannot observe or verify. The term originates in insurance markets (insured individuals take less care), but the structure appears throughout economics: employers and employees, shareholders and managers, lenders and borrowers, regulators and regulated firms. This note develops the formal theory of moral hazard beginning with the discrete two-effort model, characterizes the optimal contract using the likelihood ratio, derives monotonicity conditions, and extends to the multitasking problem and team production.

title: 47  7.2 — Moral Hazard
text:
48.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 7.2.1 — Binary Effort Model



The agent chooses effort \(e \in \{e_L, e_H\}\) (low or high). Output \(q \in \{q_1, \ldots, q_n\}\) is distributed according to: - \(f_k^H = \Pr(q = q_k | e_H)\) under high effort - \(f_k^L = \Pr(q = q_k | e_L)\) under low effort The effort cost satisfies \(c(e_H) > c(e_L) \geq 0\) (normalize \(c(e_L) = 0\); write \(\Delta c = c(e_H) - c(e_L) > 0\)).








NoteDefinition 7.2.2 — Monotone Likelihood Ratio Condition (MLRC)



The distribution \(\{f_k^H\}\) satisfies the Monotone Likelihood Ratio Condition (MLRC) if the likelihood ratio \(\ell(q_k) = f_k^L / f_k^H\) is non-increasing in \(q_k\): \[q_k > q_j \implies \frac{f_k^L}{f_k^H} \leq \frac{f_j^L}{f_j^H}\] Equivalently, \(f^H(q)/f^L(q)\) is non-decreasing in \(q\): higher output is relatively more likely under high effort.








NoteDefinition 7.2.3 — Convexity of Distribution Function Condition (CDFC)



The cumulative distribution function satisfies the CDFC if \(F(q|e)\) is convex in \(e\): for any \(e'', e' > 0\) and \(\lambda \in (0,1)\): \[F(q|\lambda e'' + (1-\lambda)e') \leq \lambda F(q|e'') + (1-\lambda) F(q|e')\] i.e., increasing effort shifts the distribution of output upward in the sense of first-order stochastic dominance, and this shift is convex in effort (diminishing returns to effort in shifting the distribution).

title: 47  7.2 — Moral Hazard
text:
48.3 Main Results

48.3.1 The Principal’s Program Under Moral Hazard
The principal wishes to implement high effort \(e_H\). The program is:
\[\max_{t_1, \ldots, t_n} \sum_k (q_k - t_k) f_k^H\]
subject to:
\[\text{(PC):} \quad \sum_k u(t_k) f_k^H - \Delta c \geq \bar{u}\]
\[\text{(IC):} \quad \sum_k u(t_k) f_k^H - \Delta c \geq \sum_k u(t_k) f_k^L\]
The IC can be rewritten as: \(\sum_k u(t_k)(f_k^H - f_k^L) \geq \Delta c\).






ImportantProposition 7.2.1 — Optimal Contract Under Moral Hazard



Suppose the principal wishes to implement \(e_H\). The optimal contract \(\{t_k^*\}\) satisfies the following first-order conditions for each output level \(q_k\): \[\frac{1}{u'(t_k^*)} = \lambda + \mu\left(1 - \frac{f_k^L}{f_k^H}\right)\] where \(\lambda \geq 0\) (PC multiplier) and \(\mu \geq 0\) (IC multiplier). Both constraints bind at the optimum.








TipProof





Form the Lagrangian: \[\mathcal{L} = \sum_k (q_k - t_k) f_k^H + \lambda\left(\sum_k u(t_k) f_k^H - \Delta c - \bar{u}\right) + \mu\left(\sum_k u(t_k)(f_k^H - f_k^L) - \Delta c\right)\] Taking the FOC with respect to \(t_k\): \[-f_k^H + \lambda u'(t_k) f_k^H + \mu u'(t_k)(f_k^H - f_k^L) = 0\] Dividing by \(f_k^H > 0\): \[-1 + u'(t_k)\left[\lambda + \mu\left(1 - \frac{f_k^L}{f_k^H}\right)\right] = 0\] Therefore: \[\frac{1}{u'(t_k^*)} = \lambda + \mu\left(1 - \frac{f_k^L}{f_k^H}\right)\]
Both constraints bind: Suppose PC does not bind. Then \(\lambda = 0\), and the contract satisfies \(1/u'(t_k) = \mu(1 - f_k^L/f_k^H)\). Since \(f_k^L/f_k^H < 1\) for some \(k\), \(\mu > 0\) (IC must bind; otherwise \(t_k\) is constant which generically violates IC). Now if PC slack, the principal could reduce all \(t_k\) uniformly, increasing profit while maintaining IC, until PC binds — contradiction.
IC binds: If IC slack, then the optimal contract has only PC binding, giving a flat contract \(t_k = \bar{t}\) for all \(k\). But a flat contract violates IC (with flat pay, the agent strictly prefers \(e_L\)). Contradiction.









CautionRemark 7.2.1 — Interpretation of the Optimal Payment



The optimal transfer \(t_k^*\) is increasing in \(q_k\) iff \(u'(t_k^*)\) is decreasing in \(q_k\) iff \(t_k^*\) is increasing in \(q_k\) (since \(u'' < 0\)). From the FOC, \(1/u'(t_k^*)\) is increasing in \(q_k\) iff \(\mu(1 - f_k^L/f_k^H)\) is increasing in \(q_k\) iff the likelihood ratio \(f_k^L/f_k^H\) is decreasing in \(q_k\) — exactly the MLRC. Therefore:
Under MLRC, the optimal payment \(t_k^*\) is non-decreasing in output \(q_k\).




48.3.2 Monotone Contracts






ImportantProposition 7.2.2 — Monotonicity of the Optimal Contract



Under MLRC and CDFC, the optimal contract \(t^*(q)\) solving the principal’s program is non-decreasing in \(q\).








TipProof





From Proposition 7.2.1, the optimal payment satisfies: \[\frac{1}{u'(t^*(q))} = \lambda + \mu\left(1 - \frac{f(q|e_L)}{f(q|e_H)}\right)\] The right side is non-decreasing in \(q\) iff \(f(q|e_L)/f(q|e_H)\) is non-increasing in \(q\), which is exactly MLRC. Since \(u'' < 0\), \(1/u'(\cdot)\) is increasing. Therefore \(t^*(q)\) is non-decreasing in \(q\).
The CDFC is used to justify the first-order approach (replacing the global IC constraint with the local FOC), validating the Lagrangian approach in Proposition 7.2.1. Specifically, CDFC guarantees that the agent’s expected utility, as a function of effort, is concave, so the first-order condition is sufficient for the agent’s maximum.





48.3.3 Multitasking (Holmström-Milgrom 1991)






NoteDefinition 7.2.4 — Multitask Principal-Agent Environment



The agent performs \(n\) tasks. Let \(\mathbf{e} = (e_1, \ldots, e_n) \in \mathbb{R}_+^n\) denote the effort vector. The output of task \(i\) is \(q_i = e_i + \varepsilon_i\) where \(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \Sigma)\). The agent’s cost function is \(C(\mathbf{e}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{e}^T \Psi \mathbf{e}\) where \(\Psi\) is positive definite.
Only a subset of tasks have measurable outputs (say tasks \(1, \ldots, m\), \(m < n\)); the remaining tasks \(m+1, \ldots, n\) produce non-measurable outputs (e.g., relationship building, quality). The contract can condition only on measurable outputs: \(t = \alpha + \boldsymbol{\beta}^T \mathbf{q}_m\) where \(\mathbf{q}_m = (q_1, \ldots, q_m)\).








ImportantProposition 7.2.3 — Holmström-Milgrom Multitasking Result



In the multitask LEN model: 1. Effort substitution: Higher incentives on measurable tasks divert effort away from non-measurable tasks. If the principal values non-measurable task output, this creates a welfare cost. 2. Optimal incentives: When tasks are substitutes in the effort cost function, the optimal incentive intensity on measurable tasks is lower than in the single-task LEN model. In some cases, the optimal contract has \(\beta_i^* = 0\) for all measurable tasks. 3. Complementarity resolution: If the principal can restrict the agent to measurable tasks only, providing high-powered incentives is optimal. The problem arises when tasks cannot be separated.








TipProof





Setup: For simplicity, take \(n = 2\) tasks, both with \(\sigma^2\) variance and zero covariance. Task 1 is measurable (\(q_1 = e_1 + \varepsilon_1\)), task 2 is not. The principal values both: \(V = \gamma_1 e_1 + \gamma_2 e_2\). The cost function satisfies \(C(e_1, e_2) = \frac{c}{2}(e_1^2 + e_2^2) + \rho e_1 e_2\) where \(\rho > 0\) (effort substitutes).
The contract is \(t = \alpha + \beta q_1\). Agent’s CE: \[CE = \alpha + \beta e_1 - C(e_1, e_2) - \frac{r\beta^2\sigma^2}{2}\] Agent’s FOCs: \[\frac{\partial CE}{\partial e_1} = \beta - c e_1 - \rho e_2 = 0\] \[\frac{\partial CE}{\partial e_2} = 0 - c e_2 - \rho e_1 = 0\] From the second equation: \(e_2 = -\frac{\rho}{c} e_1 < 0\) if \(\rho > 0\)… But effort is non-negative.
With \(e_2 \geq 0\), the second FOC gives a corner solution: \(e_2^*(\beta) = \max\{0, -\frac{\rho}{c}e_1\} = 0\) when \(\rho > 0\) and \(\beta > 0\) (since \(e_1 > 0\)). So when tasks are substitutes and both are desirable, stronger incentives on task 1 crowds out task 2 at the margin.
More precisely, with \(e_2 = 0\) (forced by non-negativity), we return to a single-task problem, but the principal loses value \(\gamma_2 e_2 = 0\). The optimal \(\beta\) trades off incentives for task 1 against the loss in task 2. The net social value of increasing \(\beta\) is \(\gamma_1 \frac{de_1}{d\beta} + \gamma_2 \frac{de_2}{d\beta}\). When \(\frac{de_2}{d\beta} < 0\) (substitution) and \(\gamma_2 > 0\), the marginal benefit of incentives is reduced relative to the single-task case, so \(\beta^* < \beta^*_{\text{single task}}\).
At the extreme, if the marginal cost of reduced effort on task 2 (measured by \(\gamma_2 |de_2/d\beta|\)) exceeds the marginal benefit on task 1, the optimal incentive is \(\beta^* = 0\) — no performance pay at all.









CautionRemark 7.2.2 — Institutional Implications of Multitasking



The multitasking model provides a theoretical foundation for several institutional features: - Public sector employment: Civil servants perform non-measurable tasks (justice, fairness, long-run policy quality). High-powered incentives on measurable outputs (throughput, cases resolved) would divert effort from non-measurable tasks. Optimal institutional design may call for flat wages and job security. - Teaching: Standardized testing creates incentives on measurable outputs (test scores) at the expense of non-measurable outputs (critical thinking, citizenship). This is the “teaching to the test” problem. - Sales force compensation: High-powered commissions on sales divert effort from customer service and relationship building. Salaried sales forces may outperform commissioned ones when repeat business matters.




48.3.4 Risk Neutrality with Limited Liability: Moral Hazard Survives






ImportantProposition 7.2.4 — Moral Hazard with Risk-Neutral Agent and Limited Liability



Suppose the agent is risk-neutral (\(u(t) = t\)) but has limited liability: \(t(q) \geq 0\) for all \(q\). Then: 1. The first-best is generally not achievable. 2. The agent earns a positive expected rent: \(\mathbb{E}[t(q^*)] - c(e^*) > \bar{u}\). 3. Equilibrium effort is below first-best: \(e^* < e^{FB}\).








TipProof





With a risk-neutral agent, the sell-the-firm contract \(t(q) = q - F\) implements first-best. But if \(q\) can be very low (say \(q = 0\) occurs with positive probability), then \(t(0) = -F < 0\), violating limited liability.
Under limited liability \(t(q) \geq 0\), the contract must set \(t(q) = 0\) at bad output levels. This means the agent does not internalize the full cost of bad outcomes, creating a wedge between private and social incentives to exert effort.
Formally, with binary effort and binary output \(q \in \{q_L, q_H\}\), \(q_L = 0\), the contract is \(t_L \geq 0\) and \(t_H \geq 0\). IC: \((f_H^H - f_H^L)u(t_H) - (f_L^H - f_L^L)u(t_L) \geq \Delta c\). With \(t_L = 0\) (optimal to minimize payment at low output), IC becomes \(f_H^H t_H \geq \Delta c + f_H^L t_H\), i.e., \((f_H^H - f_H^L) t_H \geq \Delta c\). This gives \(t_H^* = \frac{\Delta c}{f_H^H - f_H^L}\).
Agent’s expected rent \(= f_H^H \cdot t_H^* - \Delta c = \frac{f_H^H \Delta c}{f_H^H - f_H^L} - \Delta c = \frac{f_H^L \Delta c}{f_H^H - f_H^L} > 0\).
The principal prefers to reduce agency rent by shading down effort below first-best — setting \(e^* < e^{FB}\) costs efficiency but reduces the rent \(t_H^*\) that must be paid to induce effort.





48.3.5 Moral Hazard in Teams (Holmström 1982)






NoteDefinition 7.2.5 — Team Production Environment



\(n\) agents each choose effort \(e_i \geq 0\). Team output is \(q = Q(e_1, \ldots, e_n) + \varepsilon\). The principal (or budget breaker) observes total output \(q\) and distributes it among agents. Each agent \(i\) receives share \(s_i(q)\) with \(\sum_i s_i(q) \leq q\); the remainder \(q - \sum_i s_i(q)\) goes to the budget breaker.








ImportantProposition 7.2.5 — Free Rider Problem and Budget Breaking (Holmström 1982)



Consider \(n\) risk-neutral agents in team production. There is no symmetric sharing rule \(s_i(q) = q/n\) that simultaneously achieves: (i) Each agent chooses the first-best effort level \(e_i^{FB}\) (ii) The budget is balanced: \(\sum_i s_i(q) = q\) for all \(q\)
However, both (i) and (ii) can be achieved simultaneously if there exists a budget breaker (principal/outside investor) who can run a deficit.








TipProof





Free rider problem: With symmetric sharing \(s_i(q) = q/n\), agent \(i\) solves: \[\max_{e_i} \frac{1}{n}\mathbb{E}[Q(e_i, e_{-i})] - c(e_i)\] FOC: \(\frac{1}{n} \frac{\partial Q}{\partial e_i} = c'(e_i)\) First-best requires \(\frac{\partial Q}{\partial e_i} = c'(e_i^{FB})\), which is \(n\) times larger. Therefore each agent under-provides effort by a factor of \(n\).
Budget breaking as solution: Holmström’s mechanism: Each agent \(i\) receives \(s_i(q)\) defined by: if \(q \geq q^*\) (the first-best output target), each agent receives \(\tilde{t}_i\); if \(q < q^*\), each agent receives \(\underline{t}_i < \tilde{t}_i\), and the deficit (if any) or surplus is absorbed by the budget breaker.
Formally, construct shares \(s_i(q) = t_i\) for \(q \geq q^*\) and \(s_i(q) = 0\) otherwise, where \(t_i\) is chosen so that agent \(i\)’s expected payoff under \(e^{FB}\) exceeds that under any deviation. Since \(q^*\) is deterministic under first-best and stochastic otherwise (output is noisy), the budget breaker earns \(q - \sum_i t_i\) when \(q \geq q^*\) and \(-0\) otherwise, with strictly positive expected profit.
The key insight: budget balancing is the constraint that prevents first-best. If the budget must balance state-by-state, then sharing rules cannot simultaneously provide full incentives to all \(n\) agents, because the “reward good performance, punish bad” rule requires paying out more in good states than total output (or less in bad states, destroying incentives). An outside budget breaker absorbs this imbalance.

title: 47  7.2 — Moral Hazard
text:
48.4 Economic Intuition
Moral hazard is the problem of observational non-verifiability: what cannot be seen cannot be contracted upon. In the discrete model, the optimal contract exploits whatever information is available about the agent’s effort — the distribution of outputs given effort choices — and uses the likelihood ratio as the sufficient statistic for inferring effort.
The MLRC is the empirically relevant condition: it says that higher output is “good news” about effort. Under MLRC, the optimal contract pays more for better outcomes. This is simply performance pay, and the MLRC tells us when performance pay is incentive-theoretically justified.
Multitasking is the most important practical extension: organizations rarely have agents doing a single, fully measurable task. Whenever effort is multi-dimensional and measurement is imperfect, high-powered incentives on measured outputs create distortions in unmeasured directions. The institutional implication is that the optimal incentive system is a portfolio choice — not just how much to pay for performance, but which dimensions to measure and incentivize at all.

text:

title: 48  7.3 — Adverse Selection and Screening
text: 49 7.3 — Adverse Selection and Screening
crumbs: 48  7.3 — Adverse Selection and Screening

title: 48  7.3 — Adverse Selection and Screening
text:
49.1 Context
Adverse selection arises when one party to a contract has private information about a characteristic that the other party values. Unlike moral hazard (where the information concerns actions), adverse selection concerns types — fixed characteristics such as productivity, willingness to pay, or risk. Akerlof’s (1970) used-car market showed that adverse selection can destroy markets entirely. The screening problem (also called the mechanism design approach to adverse selection) reverses this: the uninformed principal designs a menu of contracts to induce agents to reveal their types through self-selection. The key insight is that the principal can generally implement any allocation that satisfies incentive compatibility, but at the cost of either distorting allocations below the efficient level for low types or conceding information rents to high types.
crumbs: 48  7.3 — Adverse Selection and Screening

title: 48  7.3 — Adverse Selection and Screening
text:
49.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 7.3.1 — Screening Environment



The screening environment consists of: - Types: \(\theta \in [\underline{\theta}, \bar{\theta}]\) with density \(f(\theta) > 0\) and CDF \(F(\theta)\); \(\theta\) is private information to the agent - Allocation: \(q \in \mathbb{R}_+\) (quantity of a good, quality level, effort) - Transfer: \(t \in \mathbb{R}\) (payment from agent to principal, or negative for subsidies) - Agent’s payoff: \(u(q, t, \theta) = v(q, \theta) - t\) where \(v\) is increasing in both arguments (higher type values quantity more) - Principal’s payoff: \(\pi(q, t) = t - c(q)\) where \(c(q)\) is the cost of providing \(q\) - Contract: a menu \(\{(q(\theta), t(\theta))\}_{\theta \in [\underline{\theta}, \bar{\theta}]}\); agent of type \(\theta\) can choose any item from the menu








NoteDefinition 7.3.2 — Incentive Compatibility and Individual Rationality



A menu \(\{(q(\theta), t(\theta))\}\) is: - Incentive Compatible (IC): if each type \(\theta\) prefers the item designed for them: \[v(q(\theta), \theta) - t(\theta) \geq v(q(\hat{\theta}), \theta) - t(\hat{\theta}) \quad \forall \theta, \hat{\theta} \in [\underline{\theta}, \bar{\theta}]\] - Individually Rational (IR): if each type finds participation weakly preferable to their outside option \(\bar{u}\): \[v(q(\theta), \theta) - t(\theta) \geq \bar{u} \quad \forall \theta\] The information rent of type \(\theta\) is \(U(\theta) \equiv v(q(\theta), \theta) - t(\theta) - \bar{u} \geq 0\).








NoteDefinition 7.3.3 — Spence-Mirrlees Single-Crossing Condition



The preferences satisfy the single-crossing condition if the marginal rate of substitution between \(q\) and \(t\) is strictly monotone in \(\theta\): \[\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}\left(\frac{\partial v / \partial q}{1}\right) = v_{q\theta}(q, \theta) > 0 \quad \forall q, \theta\] Equivalently: the indifference curves of different types cross exactly once in the \((q, t)\) plane, with higher types having steeper indifference curves (willing to pay more per unit of \(q\)).








CautionRemark 7.3.1 — Interpretation of Single-Crossing



Single-crossing (also called the Spence-Mirrlees condition) is the key regularity condition in screening. It implies that if a high-type agent is indifferent between two bundles, the low-type agent strictly prefers the bundle with less \(q\). This ordering of preferences is what makes screening feasible: different types can be separated by their allocation choices.
crumbs: 48  7.3 — Adverse Selection and Screening

title: 48  7.3 — Adverse Selection and Screening
text:
49.3 Main Results

49.3.1 Revelation Principle






ImportantTheorem 7.3.1 — Revelation Principle (Mechanism Design Version)



For any incentive mechanism \(\Gamma = (M, g)\) (message space \(M\), outcome function \(g: M \to \{(q, t)\}\)) and any Nash equilibrium \(\sigma^*\) of the game induced by \(\Gamma\), there exists a direct revelation mechanism \(\Gamma' = (\Theta, g')\) where: - Each agent reports a type \(\hat{\theta} \in \Theta\) - \(g'(\hat{\theta}) = g(\sigma^*(\hat{\theta}))\) - Truth-telling \(\hat{\theta} = \theta\) is a Nash equilibrium of \(\Gamma'\) and \(\Gamma'\) achieves the same outcome as \(\Gamma\) under \(\sigma^*\).








TipProof





Let \(\Gamma = (M, g)\) be any mechanism and \(\sigma^*: \Theta \to M\) be a Nash equilibrium (each agent plays \(m^* = \sigma^*(\theta)\) given type \(\theta\)).
Construction of the direct mechanism: Define \(\Gamma' = (\Theta, g')\) where \(g'(\hat{\theta}) = g(\sigma^*(\hat{\theta}))\). The direct mechanism \(\Gamma'\) simply simulates the equilibrium play of \(\Gamma\): when the agent reports \(\hat{\theta}\), the mechanism plays the equilibrium message \(\sigma^*(\hat{\theta})\) on the agent’s behalf and applies \(g\).
Truth-telling is a Nash equilibrium in \(\Gamma'\): Agent with type \(\theta\) reports \(\hat{\theta}\) to obtain outcome \(g'(\hat{\theta}) = g(\sigma^*(\hat{\theta}))\). Under \(\Gamma\), agent \(\theta\) plays \(\sigma^*(\theta)\) in equilibrium, obtaining \(g(\sigma^*(\theta))\). By definition of Nash equilibrium in \(\Gamma\): \[u(g(\sigma^*(\theta)), \theta) \geq u(g(m), \theta) \quad \forall m \in M\] In particular, for any \(\hat{\theta} \in \Theta\): \[u(g(\sigma^*(\theta)), \theta) \geq u(g(\sigma^*(\hat{\theta})), \theta)\] i.e., \(u(g'(\theta), \theta) \geq u(g'(\hat{\theta}), \theta)\) for all \(\hat{\theta}\). This is exactly the IC condition for truth-telling in \(\Gamma'\).
Same outcome: By construction, the outcome under truth-telling in $’ $ equals the outcome under \(\sigma^*\) in \(\Gamma\).









CautionRemark 7.3.2 — Significance of the Revelation Principle



The revelation principle collapses the space of mechanisms the principal needs to consider: instead of optimizing over all possible games, message spaces, and equilibria, the principal need only consider direct, truth-telling mechanisms. This dramatically simplifies the characterization of optimal mechanisms.




49.3.2 The Screening Problem: Continuous Types






NoteDefinition 7.3.4 — Screening Problem



The principal’s program is: \[\max_{q(\cdot), t(\cdot)} \int_{\underline{\theta}}^{\bar{\theta}} [t(\theta) - c(q(\theta))] f(\theta) d\theta\] subject to: \[\text{(IC):} \quad v(q(\theta), \theta) - t(\theta) \geq v(q(\hat{\theta}), \theta) - t(\hat{\theta}) \quad \forall \theta, \hat{\theta}\] \[\text{(IR):} \quad v(q(\theta), \theta) - t(\theta) \geq \bar{u} \quad \forall \theta\]








ImportantProposition 7.3.2 — Reduction to Local IC and Envelope Condition



Under single-crossing (\(v_{q\theta} > 0\)), the global IC constraints reduce to two local conditions: 1. Monotonicity: \(q'(\theta) \geq 0\) (allocation is non-decreasing in type) 2. Envelope condition: \(U'(\theta) = v_\theta(q(\theta), \theta)\) where \(U(\theta) = v(q(\theta), \theta) - t(\theta) - \bar{u}\) is the information rent








TipProof





Step 1: Derive the envelope condition from IC. IC requires that agent \(\theta\) prefers \((q(\theta), t(\theta))\) to \((q(\hat{\theta}), t(\hat{\theta}))\) for all \(\hat{\theta}\): \[\max_{\hat{\theta}} \{v(q(\hat{\theta}), \theta) - t(\hat{\theta})\}\] is attained at \(\hat{\theta} = \theta\). Define \(V(\theta, \hat{\theta}) = v(q(\hat{\theta}), \theta) - t(\hat{\theta})\). At the optimum: \[U(\theta) = \max_{\hat{\theta}} V(\theta, \hat{\theta}) = V(\theta, \theta)\] By the envelope theorem (Dini’s theorem applied to the maximum): \[U'(\theta) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta}\bigg|_{\hat{\theta}=\theta} = v_\theta(q(\theta), \theta)\] This is the envelope condition — the sensitivity of information rent to type equals the partial derivative of the agent’s value with respect to type.
Step 2: Monotonicity. Suppose \(\theta' > \theta\). IC for type \(\theta\) choosing bundle of \(\theta'\), and IC for type \(\theta'\) choosing bundle of \(\theta\): \[v(q(\theta), \theta) - t(\theta) \geq v(q(\theta'), \theta) - t(\theta')\] \[v(q(\theta'), \theta') - t(\theta') \geq v(q(\theta), \theta') - t(\theta)\] Adding these two inequalities: \[v(q(\theta), \theta) + v(q(\theta'), \theta') \geq v(q(\theta'), \theta) + v(q(\theta), \theta')\] Rearranging: \[v(q(\theta'), \theta') - v(q(\theta'), \theta) \geq v(q(\theta), \theta') - v(q(\theta), \theta)\] \[\int_{\theta}^{\theta'} v_\theta(q(\theta'), s) ds \geq \int_{\theta}^{\theta'} v_\theta(q(\theta), s) ds\] Since \(v_{q\theta} > 0\) (single-crossing), \(v_\theta\) is increasing in \(q\). Therefore this inequality holds iff \(q(\theta') \geq q(\theta)\). Hence \(q\) is non-decreasing.
Step 3: Sufficiency. The two local conditions (monotonicity + envelope condition) together imply global IC under single-crossing. We can verify: for \(\theta' > \theta\), \[U(\theta) - [v(q(\theta'), \theta) - t(\theta')] = U(\theta) - U(\theta') + [v(q(\theta'), \theta') - t(\theta')] - [v(q(\theta'), \theta) - t(\theta')]\] \[= -\int_\theta^{\theta'} U'(s) ds + \int_\theta^{\theta'} v_\theta(q(\theta'), s) ds = \int_\theta^{\theta'} [v_\theta(q(\theta'), s) - v_\theta(q(s), s)] ds\] \[\geq 0\] since \(v_{q\theta} > 0\) and \(q(\theta') \geq q(s)\) for \(s \leq \theta'\).









ImportantProposition 7.3.3 — Optimal Allocation in the Screening Problem



Under single-crossing, the optimal allocation \(q^*(\theta)\) satisfies the “virtual type” condition: \[\underbrace{v_q(q^*(\theta), \theta)}_{\text{marginal value}} = \underbrace{c'(q^*(\theta))}_{\text{marginal cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{F(\theta)}{f(\theta)} v_{q\theta}(q^*(\theta), \theta)}_{\text{information rent adjustment}}\] The third term represents the marginal cost of the information rent paid to higher types when the allocation of type \(\theta\) is raised. The optimal allocation features: 1. No distortion at the top: \(v_q(q^*(\bar{\theta}), \bar{\theta}) = c'(q^*(\bar{\theta}))\) (efficient allocation for the highest type) 2. Downward distortion: \(q^*(\theta) < q^{FB}(\theta)\) for all \(\theta < \bar{\theta}\) 3. No rent at the bottom: \(U(\underline{\theta}) = 0\) (IR binds for the lowest type)








TipProof





Using the envelope condition \(U'(\theta) = v_\theta(q(\theta), \theta)\), integrate by parts to express the principal’s objective in terms of \(q(\cdot)\) alone.
Total rent paid: \(\int U(\theta) f(\theta) d\theta\). Using \(U(\underline{\theta}) = 0\) (IR binds at bottom; if IR binds at all, it binds at the bottom since \(U'(\theta) = v_\theta \geq 0\) so \(U\) is non-decreasing — the bottom type gets the least rent) and integrating by parts: \[\int_{\underline{\theta}}^{\bar{\theta}} U(\theta) f(\theta) d\theta = \int_{\underline{\theta}}^{\bar{\theta}} U'(\theta)(1-F(\theta)) d\theta = \int_{\underline{\theta}}^{\bar{\theta}} v_\theta(q(\theta), \theta)(1-F(\theta)) d\theta\]
The principal’s payoff (revenue minus cost): \[\int [t(\theta) - c(q(\theta))] f(\theta) d\theta = \int [v(q(\theta), \theta) - U(\theta) - \bar{u} - c(q(\theta))] f(\theta) d\theta\] \[= \int [v(q(\theta), \theta) - c(q(\theta)) - \bar{u}] f(\theta) d\theta - \int U(\theta) f(\theta) d\theta\]
Substituting the integration-by-parts result: \[= \int \left[v(q(\theta), \theta) - c(q(\theta)) - \bar{u} - \frac{(1-F(\theta))}{f(\theta)} v_\theta(q(\theta), \theta)\right] f(\theta) d\theta\]
Pointwise optimization over \(q(\theta)\) gives: \[v_q(q^*(\theta), \theta) - c'(q^*(\theta)) - \frac{1-F(\theta)}{f(\theta)} v_{q\theta}(q^*(\theta), \theta) = 0\] Rearranging: \[v_q(q^*(\theta), \theta) = c'(q^*(\theta)) + \frac{1-F(\theta)}{f(\theta)} v_{q\theta}(q^*(\theta), \theta)\]
Wait — I need to reconcile the sign. Let me restate: the formula should read \(\frac{F(\theta)}{f(\theta)}\) depending on convention (whether types are high or low). With \(\theta\) the “high type” valued at the top \(\bar\theta\):
\[v_q(q^*(\theta), \theta) = c'(q^*(\theta)) + \frac{1-F(\theta)}{f(\theta)} v_{q\theta}\]
At \(\theta = \bar{\theta}\): \((1-F(\bar\theta))/f(\bar\theta) = 0\), so \(v_q = c'\) — no distortion at the top. For \(\theta < \bar\theta\): \((1-F(\theta))/f(\theta) > 0\), so \(v_q > c'\) — marginal value exceeds marginal cost, meaning \(q^*(\theta) < q^{FB}(\theta)\). The downward distortion reduces the information rents that higher types could extract by mimicking lower types.





49.3.3 The Two-Type Example






NoteExample 7.3.1 — Two-Type Screening (Mussa-Rosen Setup)



Types: \(\theta \in \{\theta_L, \theta_H\}\) with \(\theta_H > \theta_L > 0\) and \(\Pr(\theta_H) = \lambda \in (0,1)\). Agent utility: \(v(q, \theta) = \theta q - t\) (linear in \(q\) and \(\theta\)). Principal’s cost: \(c(q) = \frac{q^2}{2}\).
First-best: Each type gets \(q_i^{FB} = \theta_i\) (from \(v_q = c'\): \(\theta = q\)) and pays \(t_i^{FB} = \theta_i q_i^{FB} - \bar{u} = \theta_i^2 - \bar{u}\).
Second-best: The principal solves: \[\max_{q_L, q_H, t_L, t_H} (1-\lambda)(t_L - \frac{q_L^2}{2}) + \lambda(t_H - \frac{q_H^2}{2})\] s.t. (IC-H): \(\theta_H q_H - t_H \geq \theta_H q_L - t_L\) (IC-L): \(\theta_L q_L - t_L \geq \theta_L q_H - t_H\) (IR-H): \(\theta_H q_H - t_H \geq \bar{u}\) (IR-L): \(\theta_L q_L - t_L \geq \bar{u}\)
Standard arguments show: IR-L binds and IC-H binds (IR-H follows from IC-H + IR-L). IC-L does not bind (downward IC rarely binds under single-crossing).
From IR-L: \(t_L = \theta_L q_L - \bar{u}\). From IC-H: \(t_H = t_L + \theta_H(q_H - q_L) = \theta_L q_L - \bar{u} + \theta_H(q_H - q_L)\).
Substituting into the principal’s objective and maximizing over \(q_L, q_H\):
FOC for \(q_H\): \(\lambda\frac{\partial}{\partial q_H}[t_H - q_H^2/2] = \lambda[\theta_H - q_H] = 0 \implies q_H^* = \theta_H\) (no distortion at top).
FOC for \(q_L\): \((1-\lambda)[\theta_L - q_L] - \lambda[\theta_H - \theta_L] + 0 = 0\) \((1-\lambda)\theta_L - (1-\lambda)q_L - \lambda(\theta_H - \theta_L) = 0\) \(q_L^* = \theta_L - \frac{\lambda}{1-\lambda}(\theta_H - \theta_L)\)
So \(q_L^* = \theta_L^{FB} - \frac{\lambda}{1-\lambda}(\theta_H - \theta_L) < \theta_L = q_L^{FB}\) — distortion at the bottom.
Information rent for \(\theta_H\): \(U_H = \theta_H q_H - t_H = \bar{u} + (\theta_H - \theta_L)q_L^* > \bar{u}\) — the high type earns a positive rent above reservation utility.




49.3.4 Bunching (Pooling at the Bottom)






NoteDefinition 7.3.5 — Bunching



Bunching occurs when the optimal allocation \(q^*(\theta)\) is constant over an interval \([\underline{\theta}, \hat{\theta}]\) — all types in this interval receive the same allocation. The monotonicity constraint \(q'(\theta) \geq 0\) binds over this interval (is slack everywhere else).








ImportantProposition 7.3.4 — Condition for Bunching



Bunching occurs when the pointwise optimal allocation derived from the virtual type condition (Proposition 7.3.3) is decreasing — i.e., when the hazard rate \(h(\theta) = f(\theta)/(1-F(\theta))\) fails to be monotone increasing (non-monotone hazard rate). When the hazard rate is not increasing in \(\theta\), the unconstrained solution violates monotonicity and must be ironed.








TipProof





From the virtual type condition: \[v_q(q^*(\theta), \theta) = c'(q^*(\theta)) + \frac{1-F(\theta)}{f(\theta)} v_{q\theta}\] Differentiating the left side with respect to \(\theta\) and analyzing when \(q^*(\theta)\) is non-decreasing: \(q^*(\theta)\) is increasing iff \(\frac{d}{d\theta}\left[\frac{1-F(\theta)}{f(\theta)}\right] \leq 0\), i.e., the inverse hazard rate is decreasing, i.e., the hazard rate \(h(\theta) = f(\theta)/(1-F(\theta))\) is increasing.
When the hazard rate is non-monotone (e.g., bathtub-shaped), the unconstrained \(q^*(\theta)\) is non-monotone. The constrained optimum irons the solution: replace \(q^*(\theta)\) by a constant \(\bar{q}\) over the bunching interval \([\underline{\theta}, \hat{\theta}]\) such that the average virtual type condition holds over the interval. This is the ironing procedure of Mussa-Rosen (1978) and Myerson (1981).
crumbs: 48  7.3 — Adverse Selection and Screening

title: 48  7.3 — Adverse Selection and Screening
text:
49.4 Economic Intuition
The screening problem captures the fundamental tradeoff between efficiency and information extraction. The principal wants to allocate goods efficiently (each type gets the socially optimal quantity), but extracting the maximum possible transfer from each type requires offering contracts that only match the revealed preferences of each type. The problem is that a high-type agent can always pretend to be a low type to get a low-quantity, low-price deal that is attractive to them.
The solution distorts the low type’s allocation downward. By reducing \(q_L\), the principal reduces the attractiveness of the low-type bundle to the high type, enabling the principal to extract more from the high type without violating IC. The high type’s information rent — the rent they earn because the principal cannot identify them — is the price the principal pays for this information asymmetry.
The “no distortion at the top” result is intuitive: the top type has no one to mimic, so there is no benefit to distorting their allocation. The distortion is purely about preventing high types from mimicking low types.

title: 49  7.4 — Signaling
text: 50 7.4 — Signaling

title: 49  7.4 — Signaling
text:
50.1 Context
Signaling is the strategic analog of screening in which the informed party moves first. An agent with private information chooses a costly action — a signal — that conveys information to an uninformed observer. Michael Spence’s insight (1973) was that education can be privately valued (it serves as a signal of ability) even if it has no direct productivity effect. The key is that the signal must be differentially costly across types: higher-ability workers find education less costly, so education can serve as a credible signal of ability.
The equilibrium concept is Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium (PBE): strategies must be optimal given beliefs, and beliefs are derived from Bayes’ rule on the equilibrium path. A central challenge is that PBE is often non-unique — both separating and pooling equilibria may exist. Equilibrium refinements such as the Intuitive Criterion (Cho-Kreps 1987) and the D1 criterion narrow down the prediction.

title: 49  7.4 — Signaling
text:
50.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 7.4.1 — Spence Job Market Signaling Model



The model consists of: - Workers: type \(\theta \in \{\theta_L, \theta_H\}\) with \(\theta_H > \theta_L > 0\); prior probability \(\Pr(\theta_H) = \lambda \in (0,1)\) - Education signal: \(e \geq 0\); marginal product (if education is pure signal) is zero; type \(\theta\) pays cost \(c(e, \theta)\) to acquire education \(e\) - Single-crossing: \(c_e(e, \theta) > 0\) (education is costly), \(c_{e\theta}(e, \theta) < 0\) (higher ability → lower marginal cost of education) - Firms: competitive; observe education \(e\); wage = expected marginal product \(w(e) = \mathbb{E}[\theta | e]\) - Payoff: worker of type \(\theta\) with education \(e\) gets \(w(e) - c(e, \theta)\)
The timing is: (1) worker learns type \(\theta\); (2) worker chooses \(e\); (3) firms observe \(e\), form beliefs \(\mu(\theta_H | e)\), set wage \(w(e) = \mu(\theta_H|e) \cdot \theta_H + (1-\mu(\theta_H|e)) \cdot \theta_L\); (4) payoffs realized.








NoteDefinition 7.4.2 — Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium (PBE)



A Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium consists of: (1) a strategy for each type \(e^*(\theta)\); (2) beliefs \(\mu(\theta_H | e)\) for firms for all \(e\); (3) a wage function \(w^*(e)\); such that: - Sequential rationality: \(e^*(\theta) \in \arg\max_e \{w^*(e) - c(e,\theta)\}\) for each \(\theta\) - Bayesian updating on path: \(\mu(\theta_H | e^*(\theta)) = \Pr(\theta_H | e^*(\theta))\) via Bayes’ rule wherever \(e^*(\theta)\) is played with positive probability - Free beliefs off path: \(\mu(\theta_H | e)\) can be anything for \(e \notin \{e^*(\theta_L), e^*(\theta_H)\}\) - Competitive wage: \(w^*(e) = \mu(\theta_H|e) \theta_H + (1-\mu(\theta_H|e))\theta_L\)








NoteDefinition 7.4.3 — Separating and Pooling Equilibria




Separating equilibrium: \(e^*(\theta_L) \neq e^*(\theta_H)\); each type chooses a distinct signal; firms can perfectly identify types; wages equal true productivity: \(w(e^*(\theta_i)) = \theta_i\)
Pooling equilibrium: \(e^*(\theta_L) = e^*(\theta_H) = e^P\); both types choose the same signal; firms cannot distinguish types; wage is the average: \(w(e^P) = \lambda \theta_H + (1-\lambda)\theta_L \equiv \bar{\theta}\)

title: 49  7.4 — Signaling
text:
50.3 Main Results

50.3.1 Separating Equilibria






ImportantProposition 7.4.1 — Characterization of Separating Equilibria



In a separating PBE: 1. The low type chooses \(e^*(\theta_L) = 0\) (no signaling for the low type) 2. The high type chooses \(e^*(\theta_H) \in [\hat{e}, \bar{e}]\) where \(\hat{e}\) is the minimum education level that deters the low type from mimicking and \(\bar{e}\) is the maximum education level the high type is willing to acquire 3. The beliefs support the equilibrium: \(\mu(\theta_H | e) = 1\) for \(e \geq e^*(\theta_H)\) and \(\mu(\theta_H | e) = 0\) for \(e < e^*(\theta_H)\) (or more generally, any beliefs that make deviation unprofitable)








TipProof





Step 1: Low type chooses \(e_L = 0\). In any separating equilibrium, wages perfectly reveal types: \(w(e^*(\theta_L)) = \theta_L\) and \(w(e^*(\theta_H)) = \theta_H\). Since \(c(0, \theta_L) = 0\) (normalize) and the low type earns \(\theta_L\) regardless of signal level (at \(e^*(\theta_L)\)), they minimize signaling costs by setting \(e^*(\theta_L) = 0\). Any \(e^*(\theta_L) > 0\) is dominated by \(e^*(\theta_L) = 0\) since education is costly and does not affect wages (in equilibrium, the low type is identified regardless).
Step 2: Incentive constraints. For separation to be incentive compatible: - Low type should not mimic the high type: \[\theta_L - c(0, \theta_L) \geq \theta_H - c(e^*(\theta_H), \theta_L)\] \[c(e^*(\theta_H), \theta_L) \geq \theta_H - \theta_L \quad \text{(IC-L)}\] - High type should not mimic the low type (i.e., should not deviate to \(e = 0\)): \[\theta_H - c(e^*(\theta_H), \theta_H) \geq \theta_L - c(0, \theta_H) = \theta_L\] \[c(e^*(\theta_H), \theta_H) \leq \theta_H - \theta_L \quad \text{(IC-H)}\]
Step 3: Threshold \(\hat{e}\). The binding IC-L (with equality) defines the threshold: \[c(\hat{e}, \theta_L) = \theta_H - \theta_L\] For \(e^*(\theta_H) \geq \hat{e}\): IC-L is satisfied (the low type does not want to acquire that much education).
Step 4: Continuum of equilibria. IC-H and IC-L together require \(e^*(\theta_H) \in [\hat{e}, \bar{e}]\) where \(\bar{e}\) solves \(c(\bar{e}, \theta_H) = \theta_H - \theta_L\). Any \(e_H \in [\hat{e}, \bar{e}]\), combined with appropriate off-path beliefs, supports a separating PBE.
Verification of beliefs: If the firm believes any education \(e < e^*(\theta_H)\) signals \(\theta_L\) (i.e., \(\mu(\theta_H|e) = 0\) for \(e < e^*(\theta_H)\)), then the equilibrium wage for deviation is \(\theta_L\), and the high type will not deviate as long as IC-H holds.





50.3.2 Pooling Equilibria






ImportantProposition 7.4.2 — Characterization of Pooling Equilibria



A pooling PBE exists at education level \(e^P \geq 0\) with wage \(w(e^P) = \bar{\theta} = \lambda \theta_H + (1-\lambda)\theta_L\) if and only if: 1. Both types prefer the pooling wage at \(e^P\) to the low wage at any deviation \(e' \neq e^P\): \[\bar{\theta} - c(e^P, \theta) \geq \theta_L - c(e', \theta) \quad \forall \theta, \forall e'\] 2. Off-path beliefs assign \(\mu(\theta_H | e') \leq\) some threshold for deviations \(e' \neq e^P\)








TipProof





At pooling education \(e^P\), the wage is \(\bar\theta\). The firm’s off-path beliefs \(\mu(\theta_H | e')\) for \(e' \neq e^P\) can be set arbitrarily, which determines \(w(e')\). A worst-case belief for deviations: \(\mu(\theta_H | e') = 0\) for all \(e' \neq e^P\), so \(w(e') = \theta_L\) for all deviations.
Under these beliefs, neither type wants to deviate if: \[\bar\theta - c(e^P, \theta) \geq \theta_L\] i.e., \(c(e^P, \theta) \leq \bar\theta - \theta_L\) for both \(\theta \in \{\theta_L, \theta_H\}\).
Since \(c_{e\theta} < 0\) (single-crossing), the high type has lower education costs, so the binding constraint is the low type: \(c(e^P, \theta_L) \leq \bar\theta - \theta_L\). This is satisfied for small enough \(e^P\), including \(e^P = 0\).
Therefore a pooling equilibrium at \(e^P = 0\) (no education) always exists with worst-case off-path beliefs.





50.3.3 Equilibrium Refinements

The Intuitive Criterion (Cho-Kreps 1987)






NoteDefinition 7.4.4 — Equilibrium Dominance and the Intuitive Criterion



In a signaling game with equilibrium payoffs \(u^*(\theta)\) for each type \(\theta\): - A message \(e'\) is equilibrium dominated for type \(\theta\) if: \[\max_{w \in [\theta_L, \theta_H]} \{w - c(e', \theta)\} < u^*(\theta)\] i.e., even the best possible wage after sending \(e'\) yields less than the equilibrium payoff.
The Intuitive Criterion requires: if \(e'\) is equilibrium dominated for \(\theta_L\) but NOT for \(\theta_H\) (i.e., \(\theta_H\) could benefit from \(e'\) if firms believe \(\theta_H\) with probability 1), then off-path beliefs must assign \(\mu(\theta_H | e') = 1\).








ImportantTheorem 7.4.3 — Intuitive Criterion Eliminates All But the Least-Costly Separating Equilibrium



The unique separating PBE surviving the Intuitive Criterion has the high type choosing \(e^*(\theta_H) = \hat{e}\) — the least-costly separating equilibrium — where \(\hat{e}\) is defined by \(c(\hat{e}, \theta_L) = \theta_H - \theta_L\).
Moreover, all pooling equilibria are eliminated by the Intuitive Criterion.








TipProof





Eliminating pooling equilibria: Consider a pooling equilibrium at \(e^P\) with equilibrium payoffs \(u^*(\theta) = \bar\theta - c(e^P, \theta)\).
Consider a deviation to \(e' > e^P\) such that: \[\bar\theta - c(e^P, \theta_L) > \theta_H - c(e', \theta_L) \quad \text{(deviation dominated for } \theta_L\text{)}\] \[\theta_H - c(e', \theta_H) > \bar\theta - c(e^P, \theta_H) \quad \text{(deviation not dominated for } \theta_H\text{)}\]
Such an \(e'\) exists because \(c_{e\theta} < 0\): higher types have lower marginal costs, so there is a range \(e'\) where \(e'\) is too costly for \(\theta_L\) but profitable for \(\theta_H\).
Specifically, single-crossing implies that the set of \(e'\) satisfying the second inequality (profitable for \(\theta_H\)) extends above the set satisfying the first (dominated for \(\theta_L\)). Pick any \(e'\) in the gap: it is dominated for \(\theta_L\) but not for \(\theta_H\).
By the Intuitive Criterion, beliefs must put \(\mu(\theta_H | e') = 1\), so \(w(e') = \theta_H\). But then \(\theta_H\) strictly prefers to deviate to \(e'\): \(\theta_H - c(e', \theta_H) > \bar\theta - c(e^P, \theta_H) = u^*(\theta_H)\). Contradiction with \(e^P\) being a pooling equilibrium.
Eliminating non-least-costly separating equilibria: Consider a separating equilibrium with \(e^*(\theta_H) = \tilde{e} > \hat{e}\), so \(u^*(\theta_H) = \theta_H - c(\tilde{e}, \theta_H)\).
Consider a deviation by the high type to \(e' \in (\hat{e}, \tilde{e})\). Since \(c(\hat{e}, \theta_L) = \theta_H - \theta_L\), for \(e' > \hat{e}\): \[c(e', \theta_L) > \theta_H - \theta_L \implies \theta_H - c(e', \theta_L) < \theta_L = u^*(\theta_L)\] So \(e'\) is equilibrium dominated for \(\theta_L\) (even if the firm pays the maximum wage \(\theta_H\), \(\theta_L\) does worse than in equilibrium).
For \(\theta_H\) at \(e' \in (\hat{e}, \tilde{e})\): \[\theta_H - c(e', \theta_H) > \theta_H - c(\tilde{e}, \theta_H) = u^*(\theta_H) \quad \text{(since } c \text{ is increasing in } e\text{)}\] So \(e'\) is NOT dominated for \(\theta_H\).
By the Intuitive Criterion: \(\mu(\theta_H | e') = 1\), so \(w(e') = \theta_H\). But then \(\theta_H\) earns \(\theta_H - c(e', \theta_H) > u^*(\theta_H)\) by deviating to \(e'\). Contradiction.
The only surviving equilibrium is \(e^*(\theta_H) = \hat{e}\): the high type chooses the minimum education that credibly separates.






50.3.4 D1 Criterion






NoteDefinition 7.4.5 — D1 Criterion



The D1 criterion strengthens the Intuitive Criterion. For a message \(e'\) off the equilibrium path, define the set of best responses for the firm that would make type \(\theta\) willing to send \(e'\): \[D_\theta(e') = \{w : w - c(e', \theta) \geq u^*(\theta)\}\] The D1 criterion requires that if \(D_{\theta_L}(e') \subset D_{\theta_H}(e')\) (strictly), then \(\mu(\theta_H | e') = 1\).
D1 implies the Intuitive Criterion. Together with single-crossing, D1 also selects the least-costly separating equilibrium.




50.3.5 Over-Education as Social Waste






ImportantProposition 7.4.4 — Signaling is Socially Wasteful



If education has no direct effect on productivity (pure signaling), then any separating equilibrium is socially inefficient relative to the no-signaling pooling allocation at \(e = 0\). The education expenditures are a social deadweight loss.








TipProof





Under pure signaling, total output is \(\lambda \theta_H + (1-\lambda)\theta_L = \bar\theta\) regardless of the education choices, since education does not affect productivity.
In the separating equilibrium with \(e^*(\theta_H) = \hat{e}\), the total social cost is \(\lambda c(\hat{e}, \theta_H) > 0\).
In the pooling equilibrium at \(e^P = 0\), total social cost of education is \(0\).
Total output in both cases: \(\bar\theta\). Therefore the separating equilibrium has strictly lower total surplus by \(\lambda c(\hat{e}, \theta_H) > 0\).
Intuitively: signaling is a zero-sum redistribution (from firms to high-ability workers) that requires a positive resource cost (education). From society’s perspective, the education expenditures are burned while the allocation of workers to jobs is the same as if there were no signaling. This is a classical result showing that equilibria with private information may be Pareto-dominated by a world without the signal technology.
Caveat: This argument assumes education has zero human capital value. If education raises productivity, the first-best has positive education, and the welfare comparison between signaling equilibria and no-education requires a more careful analysis.

title: 49  7.4 — Signaling
text:
50.4 Economic Intuition
Signaling reverses the informational structure of screening: here, the informed party (the worker) moves first and chooses an action (education) that conveys information to the uninformed party (the firm). The key is that the action must be credible — it must be too costly for low types to mimic.
The single-crossing condition is the engine of all signaling results. Because high-ability workers have lower marginal costs of education, they can credibly signal their ability by acquiring education levels that would be prohibitively expensive for low-ability workers. This creates a separating equilibrium where education levels perfectly sort workers by type, even though education has no direct productivity effect.
The multiplicity of equilibria reflects the self-fulfilling nature of beliefs in signaling games. If firms believe that all workers with \(e \geq e_H\) are high ability, then high-ability workers have an incentive to acquire \(e_H\) — and firms’ beliefs are confirmed. Any \(e_H \in [\hat{e}, \bar{e}]\) can sustain these beliefs. The Intuitive Criterion rules out equilibria with excessive education by noting that firms should be able to “understand” that a deviation to \(e' \in (\hat{e}, e_H)\) must come from a high-ability worker (since the low type would never benefit from it), and therefore should update beliefs upward.
