title: 50  7.5 — Mechanism Design
text: 51 7.5 — Mechanism Design

title: 50  7.5 — Mechanism Design
text:
51.1 Context
Mechanism design — sometimes called “reverse game theory” — asks: given a desired social objective, what game should we design so that rational agents playing the game achieve the objective? The principal knows the social objective but not the agents’ types. The space of possible mechanisms is enormous: any message space, any outcome function, any equilibrium concept. The Revelation Principle collapses this space to direct, truth-telling mechanisms. Once restricted to direct mechanisms, the design problem becomes a constrained optimization: maximize the objective subject to incentive compatibility and individual rationality.
In quasi-linear environments (where transfers are possible), rich positive results follow: the VCG mechanism achieves social surplus maximization as a dominant-strategy equilibrium; Myerson’s payment formula characterizes all incentive-compatible mechanisms; and the optimal auction maximizes revenue by assigning goods to the bidder with the highest virtual value. The negative results — Gibbard-Satterthwaite, Myerson-Satterthwaite — reveal fundamental limits on what mechanisms can achieve when private information is present.

title: 50  7.5 — Mechanism Design
text:
51.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 7.5.1 — Mechanism



A mechanism \(\Gamma = (M, g)\) consists of: - \(M = \times_{i=1}^n M_i\): the message (strategy) space, where \(M_i\) is agent \(i\)’s message set - \(g: M \to X\): the outcome function mapping messages to outcomes in some outcome space \(X\)
Each agent \(i\) has type \(\theta_i \in \Theta_i\) (private information) and payoff \(u_i(x, \theta_i)\) when outcome \(x \in X\) occurs. The game induced by \(\Gamma\) is one in which agents simultaneously choose messages and receive the outcome \(g(m)\).








NoteDefinition 7.5.2 — Direct Mechanism and Incentive Compatibility



A direct mechanism sets \(M_i = \Theta_i\) for all \(i\): agents directly report their types. - Dominant Strategy Incentive Compatible (DSIC): Truth-telling is a dominant strategy: for all \(i\), all \(\theta_i\), all \(\hat\theta_i \neq \theta_i\), and all messages \(m_{-i}\) of others: \[u_i(g(\theta_i, m_{-i}), \theta_i) \geq u_i(g(\hat\theta_i, m_{-i}), \theta_i)\] - Bayesian Incentive Compatible (BIC): Truth-telling is optimal in expectation over others’ types: for all \(i\), all \(\theta_i\), all \(\hat\theta_i\): \[\mathbb{E}_{\theta_{-i}}[u_i(g(\theta_i, \theta_{-i}), \theta_i)] \geq \mathbb{E}_{\theta_{-i}}[u_i(g(\hat\theta_i, \theta_{-i}), \theta_i)]\]








NoteDefinition 7.5.3 — Individual Rationality



A mechanism is individually rational (IR) (or ex-interim IR) if each agent is willing to participate after learning their own type: \[\mathbb{E}_{\theta_{-i}}[u_i(g(\theta_i, \theta_{-i}), \theta_i)] \geq \bar{u}_i(\theta_i)\] where \(\bar{u}_i(\theta_i)\) is agent \(i\)’s outside option.

title: 50  7.5 — Mechanism Design
text:
51.3 Main Results

51.3.1 Revelation Principle (General Version)






ImportantTheorem 7.5.1 — Revelation Principle (DSIC Version)



For any mechanism \(\Gamma = (M, g)\) and any dominant strategy equilibrium \(\sigma^*\) of the game induced by \(\Gamma\), there exists a direct mechanism \(\Gamma' = (\Theta, g')\) that is DSIC and implements the same outcome: \(g'(\theta) = g(\sigma^*(\theta))\) for all \(\theta\).








TipProof





Define the direct mechanism \(\Gamma'\) by setting \(g'(\hat\theta) = g(\sigma^*(\hat\theta))\) for all \(\hat\theta\).
Truth-telling is dominant in \(\Gamma'\): Fix agent \(i\) with type \(\theta_i\). In \(\Gamma\), \(\sigma_i^*(\theta_i)\) is a dominant strategy: \[u_i(g(\sigma_i^*(\theta_i), m_{-i}), \theta_i) \geq u_i(g(m_i, m_{-i}), \theta_i) \quad \forall m_i, m_{-i}\] In \(\Gamma'\), if agent \(i\) reports \(\hat\theta_i\) and all others report \(\theta_{-i}\), the outcome is \(g'(\hat\theta_i, \theta_{-i}) = g(\sigma_i^*(\hat\theta_i), \sigma_{-i}^*(\theta_{-i}))\).
Agent \(i\)’s payoff from truthful report \(\theta_i\) in \(\Gamma'\) (given any \(\theta_{-i}\) and any strategy of others): \[u_i(g'(\theta_i, \theta_{-i}), \theta_i) = u_i(g(\sigma_i^*(\theta_i), \sigma_{-i}^*(\theta_{-i})), \theta_i)\] Agent \(i\)’s payoff from misreport \(\hat\theta_i\) in \(\Gamma'\): \[u_i(g'(\hat\theta_i, \theta_{-i}), \theta_i) = u_i(g(\sigma_i^*(\hat\theta_i), \sigma_{-i}^*(\theta_{-i})), \theta_i)\]
Since \(\sigma^*\) is a dominant strategy equilibrium in \(\Gamma\), for the message profile \(m_{-i} = \sigma_{-i}^*(\theta_{-i})\): \[u_i(g(\sigma_i^*(\theta_i), m_{-i}), \theta_i) \geq u_i(g(\sigma_i^*(\hat\theta_i), m_{-i}), \theta_i)\] Therefore truth-telling is optimal for agent \(i\) in \(\Gamma'\), for any messages of others. This is a dominant strategy.









ImportantTheorem 7.5.2 — Revelation Principle (BIC Version)



For any mechanism \(\Gamma\) and any Bayesian Nash Equilibrium \(\sigma^*\), there exists a direct BIC mechanism with the same outcome.








TipProof





Same construction as DSIC version, with the inequality replaced by the BIC inequality (expected over others’ types). The argument goes through identically, replacing the universal quantifier over \(m_{-i}\) with an expectation over \(\theta_{-i}\).





51.3.2 Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem






ImportantTheorem 7.5.3 — Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem



Suppose: (i) \(|X| \geq 3\) (at least 3 social alternatives); (ii) agents have unrestricted preferences over \(X\) (any linear order is possible); (iii) the social choice function \(f: \times_i \mathcal{R}_i \to X\) is onto (every alternative is chosen for some preference profile). Then \(f\) is DSIC if and only if \(f\) is dictatorial: there exists agent \(d\) such that \(f(\mathbf{R}) = \arg\max_x R_d\) for all \(\mathbf{R}\).








TipProof





The proof proceeds in two steps, connecting the result to Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem.
Step 1: Reduction to Arrow’s Theorem. Given a DSIC social choice function \(f\), construct a social welfare function \(W\) from \(f\) by asking: what would \(f\) choose if agents are given any two-alternative comparison? A careful construction (using the “independence of irrelevant alternatives” structure implicit in DSIC) shows that \(W\) satisfies Arrow’s conditions: Pareto efficiency and independence of irrelevant alternatives.
Step 2: Arrow implies dictatorship. Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem states that any social welfare function satisfying Pareto efficiency and IIA on a universal domain with \(\geq 3\) alternatives is a dictatorship. Therefore \(W\) is a dictatorship for the underlying pairwise comparisons, which translates to \(f\) being dictatorial.
Connection to Arrow: Both theorems are expressions of the same deep fact — that with unrestricted preferences, no non-dictatorial rule can aggregate preferences in a consistent (incentive-compatible / IIA-respecting) way. The Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem applies to ordinal social choice; the quasi-linear restriction used in VCG escapes the impossibility by restricting the domain.





51.3.3 Quasi-Linear Environments






NoteDefinition 7.5.4 — Quasi-Linear Environment



In a quasi-linear environment: - Outcome \(x = (k, t_1, \ldots, t_n)\) where \(k \in K\) is an allocation and \(t_i \in \mathbb{R}\) is a transfer to agent \(i\) - Agent \(i\)’s payoff: \(u_i(x, \theta_i) = v_i(k, \theta_i) - t_i\) - Budget balance: \(\sum_i t_i = 0\) (transfers are internal) or \(\sum_i t_i \leq 0\) (transfers may be collected by principal) - Social surplus: \(\sum_i v_i(k, \theta_i)\)




51.3.4 VCG Mechanism






ImportantTheorem 7.5.4 — Vickrey-Clarke-Groves (VCG) Mechanism



In a quasi-linear environment, the VCG mechanism: - Allocation: \(k^*(\theta) \in \arg\max_{k \in K} \sum_i v_i(k, \theta_i)\) (maximize social surplus) - Transfer to \(i\): \(t_i(\theta) = \sum_{j \neq i} v_j(k^*(\theta), \theta_j) + h_i(\theta_{-i})\) where \(h_i: \Theta_{-i} \to \mathbb{R}\) is any function of others’ reports only
is DSIC: truth-telling is a dominant strategy for every agent.








TipProof





Agent \(i\) with type \(\theta_i\) reports \(\hat\theta_i\) and receives payoff: \[u_i = v_i(k^*(\hat\theta_i, \theta_{-i}), \theta_i) - t_i(\hat\theta_i, \theta_{-i})\] Substituting the VCG transfer: \[u_i = v_i(k^*(\hat\theta_i, \theta_{-i}), \theta_i) - \sum_{j \neq i} v_j(k^*(\hat\theta_i, \theta_{-i}), \theta_j) - h_i(\theta_{-i})\] \[= \sum_j v_j(k^*(\hat\theta_i, \theta_{-i}), \theta_j) - h_i(\theta_{-i})\]
The last expression is the total social surplus achieved when the agent reports \(\hat\theta_i\), minus a term that does not depend on \(\hat\theta_i\). To maximize their payoff, agent \(i\) chooses \(\hat\theta_i\) to maximize total social surplus: \[\hat\theta_i^* \in \arg\max_{\hat\theta_i} \sum_j v_j(k^*(\hat\theta_i, \theta_{-i}), \theta_j)\]
By definition of \(k^*\), this is maximized by truthful reporting \(\hat\theta_i = \theta_i\), because: \[k^*(\theta_i, \theta_{-i}) = \arg\max_k \sum_j v_j(k, \theta_j)\] achieves the highest total surplus when \(\hat\theta_i = \theta_i\) — no manipulation can increase total social surplus, and agent \(i\)’s payoff is precisely total social surplus (minus a constant). Therefore truth-telling is a dominant strategy.









NoteExample 7.5.1 — Second-Price Auction as a VCG Mechanism



Single good; bidder \(i\) has private value \(v_i(\theta_i) = \theta_i\). VCG allocation: award to highest-value bidder \(i^* = \arg\max_i \theta_i\). VCG transfer to \(i^*\): \(t_{i^*} = \sum_{j \neq i^*} v_j(k^*, \theta_j) - \sum_{j \neq i^*} v_j(k^{-i^*}, \theta_j)\) where \(k^{-i^*}\) is the allocation without \(i^*\). Since the good goes to \(i^*\), others get 0 regardless; the term simplifies to \(-(- v_{(2)}) = -\max_{j \neq i^*} \theta_j = -v_{(2)}\), so \(i^*\) pays \(v_{(2)}\) (the second-highest value). This is the Vickrey second-price auction. Other bidders pay 0. Truth-telling is dominant.




51.3.5 Myerson-Satterthwaite Theorem






ImportantTheorem 7.5.5 — Myerson-Satterthwaite Impossibility Theorem



Consider a bilateral trade setting: a buyer with private value \(v \sim F_B\) on \([0,1]\) and a seller with private cost \(c \sim F_S\) on \([0,1]\), with \(F_B\) and \(F_S\) having overlapping supports. There is no BIC, IR mechanism that always achieves efficient trade (trade occurs iff \(v \geq c\)) and also satisfies budget balance (\(\mathbb{E}[t_B - t_S] = 0\)).








TipProof





Step 1: Myerson’s payment formula. For a BIC mechanism, the probability of trade as a function of the buyer’s type \(Q_B(v) = \mathbb{E}_c[q(v,c)]\) must be non-decreasing in \(v\) (standard monotonicity from BIC). By the envelope theorem: \[U_B(v) = U_B(0) + \int_0^v Q_B(s) ds\] where \(U_B(v) = \mathbb{E}_c[v \cdot q(v,c) - t_B(v,c)]\) is the buyer’s expected payoff.
Step 2: Expected payments. The buyer’s expected payment: \[\mathbb{E}_v[t_B(v)] = \mathbb{E}_v[v \cdot Q_B(v)] - \mathbb{E}_v[U_B(v)]\] \[= \mathbb{E}_v[v \cdot Q_B(v)] - U_B(0) - \int_0^1 \int_0^v Q_B(s) ds \, f_B(v) dv\] Integrating by parts: \[= \mathbb{E}_v\left[v \cdot Q_B(v) - \frac{1-F_B(v)}{f_B(v)} Q_B(v)\right] - U_B(0)\] \[= \mathbb{E}_v\left[\psi_B(v) Q_B(v)\right] - U_B(0)\] where \(\psi_B(v) = v - \frac{1-F_B(v)}{f_B(v)}\) is the virtual value of the buyer.
Similarly, the seller’s expected receipt: \[\mathbb{E}_c[t_S(c)] = \mathbb{E}_c\left[\phi_S(c) Q_S(c)\right] + U_S(1)\] where \(\phi_S(c) = c + \frac{F_S(c)}{f_S(c)}\) is the seller’s virtual cost and \(Q_S(c) = \mathbb{E}_v[q(v,c)]\).
Step 3: Budget balance condition. Budget balance requires: \[\mathbb{E}[t_B(v,c)] = \mathbb{E}[t_S(v,c)]\] i.e., \(\mathbb{E}_v[t_B(v)] = \mathbb{E}_c[t_S(c)]\) (by the law of iterated expectations and linearity).
Step 4: Efficient mechanism fails budget balance. Under efficient trade, \(q^*(v,c) = \mathbf{1}[v \geq c]\). Compute: \[\mathbb{E}[t_B] - \mathbb{E}[t_S] = \mathbb{E}[\psi_B(v) \mathbf{1}[v \geq c]] - \mathbb{E}[\phi_S(c) \mathbf{1}[v \geq c]] - U_B(0) - U_S(1)\] \[= \mathbb{E}[(\psi_B(v) - \phi_S(c)) \mathbf{1}[v \geq c]] - U_B(0) - U_S(1)\]
For budget balance: \(U_B(0) + U_S(1) = \mathbb{E}[(\psi_B(v) - \phi_S(c)) \mathbf{1}[v \geq c]]\).
But IR requires \(U_B(0) \geq 0\) and \(U_S(1) \geq 0\). So budget balance + IR requires: \[0 \leq U_B(0) + U_S(1) = \mathbb{E}[(\psi_B(v) - \phi_S(c))\mathbf{1}[v \geq c]]\]
Now, with overlapping supports, there is a region where \(v \geq c\) (trade is efficient) but \(\psi_B(v) < v < c < \phi_S(c)\) (virtual values cross before actual values). Specifically, \(\psi_B(v) = v - (1-F_B(v))/f_B(v) < v\) and \(\phi_S(c) = c + F_S(c)/f_S(c) > c\). Near the overlap (small \(v \geq c\)), \(\psi_B(v) - \phi_S(c) < v - c \approx 0\), so \(\psi_B(v) - \phi_S(c) < 0\).
Therefore \(\mathbb{E}[(\psi_B(v) - \phi_S(c))\mathbf{1}[v \geq c]] < 0\) in general with overlapping supports, making it impossible to satisfy \(U_B(0) + U_S(1) \geq 0\) simultaneously. Budget balance requires a deficit from the efficient mechanism.









CautionRemark 7.5.1 — Interpretation of Myerson-Satterthwaite



The theorem shows that private information has a real welfare cost that cannot be contracted away, even in the most favorable environments (quasi-linear utilities, bilateral trade). The fundamental tension: BIC and IR together imply that information rents (positive information rents for the buyer at low values, positive rents for the seller at high costs) must be paid, and these rents exceed the gains from efficient trade in the overlap region. No budget-balancing mechanism can always achieve efficiency.




51.3.6 Optimal Auctions (Myerson 1981)






ImportantTheorem 7.5.6 — Myerson’s Optimal Auction



A seller has one unit; \(n\) buyers with IID private values \(v_i \sim F\) on \([V, \bar{V}]\), \(F\) regular (virtual value \(\psi(v) = v - (1-F(v))/f(v)\) is strictly increasing). The revenue-maximizing BIC, IR auction: 1. Allocates to the bidder with the highest virtual value \(\psi(v_i)\), if this virtual value exceeds 0 (otherwise no sale) 2. Sets a reserve price \(r^*\) solving \(\psi(r^*) = 0\), i.e., \(r^* - (1-F(r^*))/f(r^*) = 0\) 3. Expected revenue = \(\mathbb{E}[\max_i \psi(v_i) \cdot \mathbf{1}[\max_i v_i \geq r^*]]\)








TipProof





By the Revelation Principle, focus on direct BIC, IR mechanisms \((q_i(v), p_i(v))\) where \(q_i(v)\) is the probability of allocation to \(i\) and \(p_i(v)\) is the payment.
Step 1: Characterize BIC. Bidder \(i\)’s utility: \(U_i(v_i) = v_i q_i(v_i) - p_i(v_i)\) where \(q_i(v_i) = \mathbb{E}_{v_{-i}}[q_i(v_i, v_{-i})]\) (interim allocation probability). BIC requires \(q_i(v_i)\) non-decreasing and the envelope condition \(U_i'(v_i) = q_i(v_i)\).
Step 2: Myerson’s payment formula. Integrating: \[p_i(v_i) = v_i q_i(v_i) - U_i(v_i) = v_i q_i(v_i) - U_i(\underline{V}) - \int_{\underline{V}}^{v_i} q_i(s) ds\] Expected revenue from bidder \(i\): \[\mathbb{E}[p_i(v_i)] = \mathbb{E}[v_i q_i(v_i)] - U_i(\underline{V}) - \mathbb{E}\left[\int_{\underline{V}}^{v_i} q_i(s) ds\right]\] Integrating by parts, total revenue: \[\sum_i \mathbb{E}[p_i(v_i)] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_i \psi(v_i) q_i(v_i)\right] - \sum_i U_i(\underline{V})\]
Step 3: Maximize revenue. IR requires \(U_i(\underline{V}) \geq 0\); optimal: \(U_i(\underline{V}) = 0\). To maximize \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_i \psi(v_i) q_i(v_i)]\) subject to \(\sum_i q_i(v_i) \leq 1\) (feasibility), set \(q_i^*(v) = 1\) if \(\psi(v_i) = \max_j \psi(v_j) > 0\) and \(q_i^*(v) = 0\) otherwise. Under monotone virtual values, this means awarding to the highest bidder if their virtual value exceeds 0.
Step 4: Reserve price. The reserve price \(r^*\) is defined by \(\psi(r^*) = 0\). If the highest bid \(v_{(1)} < r^*\), the seller withholds the good (since \(\psi(v_{(1)}) < 0\), selling would reduce expected revenue).
With IID regular types and symmetric bidders, the optimal mechanism is a second-price auction with reserve price \(r^*\).

title: 50  7.5 — Mechanism Design
text:
51.4 Economic Intuition
Mechanism design is the tool for asking: what is the best we can do? Given private information and rational agents, what outcomes are achievable? The Revelation Principle tells us we need only consider truth-telling mechanisms. The VCG mechanism shows that, in the class of quasi-linear problems, social surplus maximization is always achievable as a dominant-strategy outcome — private information does not prevent efficiency. However, VCG may run a deficit (violating budget balance), which is why it fails in bilateral trade settings.
Myerson’s optimal auction shows that when the designer cares about revenue rather than efficiency, the optimal mechanism distorts the allocation — it excludes some trades that are socially efficient (when \(v_i > 0\) but \(\psi(v_i) < 0\)) in order to extract more surplus from infra-marginal bidders. The reserve price is the analog of the downward distortion in the screening problem: exclude the lowest-value buyers to raise the price paid by high-value buyers.
The Myerson-Satterthwaite theorem is the fundamental impossibility result: private information creates inefficiency that cannot be removed by clever mechanism design. This has practical implications for regulatory economics, market design, and the study of bargaining.

title: 51  7.6 — Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation
text: 52 7.6 — Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation
crumbs: 51  7.6 — Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation

title: 51  7.6 — Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation
text:
52.1 Context
A central question in information economics is whether markets aggregate dispersed private information. Hayek’s (1945) insight was that prices serve as signals that convey information no single agent possesses in full. The formal theory of rational expectations equilibrium (REE) makes this precise: in a fully revealing REE, the equilibrium price is a sufficient statistic for all agents’ private information. But Grossman and Stiglitz (1980) showed this leads to a paradox: if prices fully reveal information, no one will pay to acquire it; if no one acquires information, prices cannot reveal it. The resolution is the noisy rational expectations equilibrium, in which uninformed noise traders prevent prices from fully revealing information, leaving informed traders with positive returns on their information acquisition.
Kyle’s (1985) model provides the strategic counterpart: a single informed trader strategically chooses order flow to maximize profits while a competitive market maker sets prices to break even given observed order flow. Together, these models characterize when and how well markets aggregate information — with direct implications for prediction market design, financial market efficiency, and the use of market prices as policy signals.
crumbs: 51  7.6 — Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation

title: 51  7.6 — Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation
text:
52.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 7.6.1 — Rational Expectations Equilibrium (REE)



Consider an economy with agents \(i \in \{1,\ldots,n\}\), each observing private signal \(s_i\) and taking prices \(p\) as given. An REE is a price function \(p^*: S \to \mathbb{R}\) (mapping signal profiles to prices) such that: 1. Market clearing: At price \(p^*(s)\), aggregate demand equals aggregate supply for every signal realization \(s = (s_1, \ldots, s_n)\) 2. Bayesian rationality: Each agent \(i\) maximizes expected utility \(\mathbb{E}[u_i | s_i, p^*(s)]\), treating the price \(p^*(s)\) as an additional signal about the unobserved aggregate state
A fully revealing REE is an REE in which \(p^*\) is a sufficient statistic for all private signals: the joint distribution of the state conditional on \(p^*(s)\) is the same as conditional on the entire signal profile \((s_1, \ldots, s_n)\).








NoteDefinition 7.6.2 — Hayek’s Knowledge Problem



Hayek (1945) argued that the key economic problem is the use of knowledge that is dispersed among individuals — each agent has local, specific knowledge that no central planner can access. Prices solve this problem by aggregating dispersed knowledge into a single number that guides resource allocation. The formal content is: in a competitive economy, the market price \(p^*\) is an informative signal about the true state \(\omega\) even when no individual agent observes \(\omega\) directly.
crumbs: 51  7.6 — Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation

title: 51  7.6 — Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation
text:
52.3 Main Results

52.3.1 Fully Revealing Rational Expectations Equilibrium






ImportantProposition 7.6.1 — Fully Revealing REE in a Linear Economy



Consider an economy with a risky asset with true value \(v \sim N(\mu_0, \sigma_v^2)\). There are \(n\) informed agents, each observing signal \(s_i = v + \varepsilon_i\) where \(\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2)\) IID and independent of \(v\). Agent \(i\) has CARA utility with risk aversion \(r\) and wealth \(w_i\). In a competitive equilibrium, there exists a fully revealing linear REE price: \[p^* = A + B \cdot \bar{s}\] where \(\bar{s} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i s_i\) is the average signal, and \(A, B\) are constants. Given \(p^*\), all agents can recover \(\bar{s}\) and hence the posterior \(\mathbb{E}[v|\bar{s}] \to v\) as \(n \to \infty\).








TipProof





Posterior beliefs: Observing signal \(s_i\) and price \(p = A + B\bar{s}\) (equivalently, \(\bar{s} = (p-A)/B\)), agent \(i\) can infer \(\bar{s}\). The posterior distribution of \(v\) given \(s_i\) and \(\bar{s}\) is: \[v | (s_i, \bar{s}) \sim N\left(\mu_i^*, (\sigma_i^*)^2\right)\] where \(\mu_i^*\) and \((\sigma_i^*)^2\) are obtained by Bayesian updating using the prior \(N(\mu_0, \sigma_v^2)\), signal \(s_i\) with variance \(\sigma_\varepsilon^2\), and the additional signal \(\bar{s}\) with variance \(\sigma_\varepsilon^2/n\) (since \(\bar{s} = v + \bar\varepsilon\) with \(\bar\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2/n)\)).
Demand: With CARA-normal utility, agent \(i\)’s demand for the risky asset: \[x_i^* = \frac{\mu_i^* - p}{r (\sigma_i^*)^2}\]
Market clearing: \(\sum_i x_i^* = X_0\) (fixed supply \(X_0\)). Using the fact that all agents have the same posterior given \(\bar{s}\) (they all observe \(\bar{s}\) via \(p\)) and symmetry: \[n \cdot \frac{\mu^*({\bar{s}}) - p}{r (\sigma^*)^2} = X_0\] Solving for \(p\): \[p = \mu^*(\bar{s}) - \frac{r (\sigma^*)^2 X_0}{n}\]
Since \(\mu^*(\bar{s})\) is a linear function of \(\bar{s}\), this gives \(p = A + B\bar{s}\) for constants \(A\) and \(B\) that can be solved for from the Bayesian updating formulas.
Full revelation: Observing \(p = A + B\bar{s}\) and knowing \(A\) and \(B\), any agent can recover \(\bar{s} = (p-A)/B\) exactly. As \(n \to \infty\), \(\bar{s} \to v\) by the law of large numbers, so \(p^* \to v\) and the market becomes fully informationally efficient.





52.3.2 Grossman-Stiglitz Paradox






ImportantTheorem 7.6.2 — Grossman-Stiglitz Impossibility of Informationally Efficient Markets



If information acquisition is costly, a fully revealing REE cannot be an equilibrium: if prices fully reveal all private information, the return to acquiring information is zero, so rational agents choose not to acquire information. But then there is no information for prices to reveal. This is a contradiction.








TipProof





Suppose, for contradiction, that in equilibrium prices fully reveal all private information (fully revealing REE).
Step 1: Zero return to information. If prices are fully informative, an uninformed trader who observes the price has exactly the same posterior distribution over the true value as an informed trader. Therefore an uninformed trader can achieve the same expected utility as an informed trader simply by conditioning on the observable price.
Step 2: Costly acquisition. If acquiring information costs \(c > 0\) and yields no incremental advantage (since prices already reveal all information), no rational agent will acquire information. Expected utility from being uninformed = Expected utility from being informed.
Step 3: Contradiction. If no agent acquires information, prices carry no information (they are based purely on liquidity/noise trades or uninformed demands). Prices therefore cannot be fully informative. Contradiction with the assumption.
Resolution: The paradox implies that in a world with costly information, prices cannot be fully informative. The equilibrium must feature partial information revelation — prices reveal some but not all information, leaving informed traders with positive expected rents that justify information acquisition.





52.3.3 Noisy Rational Expectations Equilibrium






NoteDefinition 7.6.3 — Noisy REE



A noisy REE introduces exogenous “noise traders” whose demands are random (unrelated to fundamentals). The presence of noise traders prevents prices from fully revealing private information, creating a role for informed traders. The equilibrium features partial information revelation: prices are more informative than no information but less informative than full revelation.








NoteExample 7.6.1 — Noisy REE in the Linear-Normal Model



Augment the model of Proposition 7.6.1 with noise traders who supply \(u \sim N(0, \sigma_u^2)\) units of the asset. Now \(\bar{s} = v + \bar\varepsilon\) and the total order from informed traders plus noise traders determines price.
The equilibrium price becomes \(p^* = A + B(v + \bar\varepsilon) + Cu\) for coefficients \(A, B, C\). The price aggregates information but is confounded by noise \(u\). Given \(p^*\), an agent cannot distinguish whether a high price reflects high \(v\) (good news) or a negative noise-trader demand shock. The posterior variance of \(v\) given \(p^*\) is strictly positive, so informed traders have an informational advantage over uninformed traders.
Equilibrium informativeness: Let \(\phi\) denote the fraction of agents who acquire information (endogenous). In equilibrium, informed and uninformed agents earn the same expected utility: \[V^I(\phi) = V^U(\phi)\] where \(V^I\) (informed utility) decreases in \(\phi\) (more informed traders \(\to\) price is more informative \(\to\) informed advantage shrinks) and \(V^U\) increases in \(\phi\). The equilibrium \(\phi^*\) equates the two, yielding partial information revelation.




52.3.4 Kyle Model






NoteDefinition 7.6.4 — Kyle (1985) Model Setup



The Kyle model features: - Informed trader: observes true asset value \(v \sim N(\mu_0, \sigma_v^2)\) and submits order \(x\) - Noise traders: submit order \(u \sim N(0, \sigma_u^2)\), independent of \(v\) - Market maker: observes total order flow \(y = x + u\); cannot distinguish \(x\) from \(u\); sets price \(p = \mathbb{E}[v | y]\) (Bayesian posterior, competitive market maker breaks even) - Payoff to informed trader: \(\pi = (v - p) \cdot x\)








ImportantTheorem 7.6.3 — Kyle Linear Equilibrium



In the Kyle model, there exists a unique linear equilibrium characterized by: - Informed trader’s strategy: \(x^*(v) = \beta(v - \mu_0)\) where \(\beta = \frac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_v}\) - Market maker’s pricing rule: \(p(y) = \mu_0 + \lambda y\) where \(\lambda = \frac{\sigma_v}{2\sigma_u}\) - Price impact: \(\lambda = \frac{\sigma_v}{2\sigma_u}\) (Kyle’s lambda) - Informed trader’s expected profit: \(\Pi^* = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_v \sigma_u\)








TipProof





Step 1: Conjecture linear strategies. Assume \(x = \beta(v - \mu_0)\) and \(p = \mu_0 + \lambda y\). We will verify these by requiring: (a) \(x^*\) maximizes informed trader’s profit given \(p(\cdot)\); (b) \(p = \mathbb{E}[v|y]\) (market maker breaks even/rational Bayesian).
Step 2: Market maker’s pricing rule. Total order flow: \[y = x + u = \beta(v - \mu_0) + u\] Both \(v\) and \(u\) are normally distributed. The market maker computes \(\mathbb{E}[v|y]\):
\((v - \mu_0)\) and \(y\) are jointly normal with: - \(\text{Var}(y) = \beta^2 \sigma_v^2 + \sigma_u^2\) - \(\text{Cov}(v, y) = \text{Cov}(v, \beta(v-\mu_0) + u) = \beta \sigma_v^2\)
By the projection theorem for normal random variables: \[\mathbb{E}[v|y] = \mu_0 + \frac{\text{Cov}(v,y)}{\text{Var}(y)} (y - 0) = \mu_0 + \frac{\beta \sigma_v^2}{\beta^2 \sigma_v^2 + \sigma_u^2} y\] Therefore the market maker’s price rule is \(p = \mu_0 + \lambda y\) where: \[\lambda = \frac{\beta \sigma_v^2}{\beta^2 \sigma_v^2 + \sigma_u^2} \quad \text{...(MM condition)}\]
Step 3: Informed trader’s optimization. The informed trader with value \(v\) observes \(v\) and knows \(p = \mu_0 + \lambda(x + u)\). The trader does not observe \(u\), so from their perspective: \[\mathbb{E}[(v - p) x | v] = (v - \mu_0 - \lambda x) x\] (since \(\mathbb{E}[u] = 0\) and \(u\) is independent of \(v\)).
This is a quadratic in \(x\); maximizing: \[\frac{d}{dx}[(v - \mu_0 - \lambda x) x] = (v - \mu_0) - 2\lambda x = 0\] \[x^* = \frac{v - \mu_0}{2\lambda} \quad \text{...(IT optimality condition)}\] So \(\beta = \frac{1}{2\lambda}\).
Step 4: Solve for \(\beta\) and \(\lambda\). From IT optimality: \(\beta = \frac{1}{2\lambda}\). Substituting into MM condition: \[\lambda = \frac{\frac{1}{2\lambda} \sigma_v^2}{\frac{1}{4\lambda^2}\sigma_v^2 + \sigma_u^2} = \frac{\frac{\sigma_v^2}{2\lambda}}{\frac{\sigma_v^2}{4\lambda^2} + \sigma_u^2}\] Multiply numerator and denominator by \(4\lambda^2\): \[\lambda = \frac{2\lambda\sigma_v^2}{\sigma_v^2 + 4\lambda^2\sigma_u^2}\] \[\lambda(\sigma_v^2 + 4\lambda^2\sigma_u^2) = 2\lambda\sigma_v^2\] \[\sigma_v^2 + 4\lambda^2\sigma_u^2 = 2\sigma_v^2\] \[4\lambda^2\sigma_u^2 = \sigma_v^2\] \[\lambda = \frac{\sigma_v}{2\sigma_u}\] And \(\beta = \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{\sigma_u}{\sigma_v}\).
Step 5: Expected profit. \[\Pi^* = \mathbb{E}[(v-p)x | v] = (v - \mu_0 - \lambda x^*) x^* = \lambda (x^*)^2 = \lambda \beta^2 (v-\mu_0)^2\] Taking expectations over \(v\): \[\mathbb{E}[\Pi^*] = \lambda \beta^2 \sigma_v^2 = \frac{\sigma_v}{2\sigma_u} \cdot \frac{\sigma_u^2}{\sigma_v^2} \cdot \sigma_v^2 = \frac{\sigma_v \sigma_u}{2}\]









CautionRemark 7.6.1 — Properties of the Kyle Equilibrium



The Kyle model delivers several important results:

Price impact \(\lambda = \sigma_v/(2\sigma_u)\): Price impact increases with fundamental volatility \(\sigma_v\) (more private information to hide) and decreases with noise-trader volume \(\sigma_u\) (more liquidity = less price impact per unit of order flow). This is the key empirical prediction tested in market microstructure studies.
Informed trader “hides in the noise”: The optimal order \(x^* = \beta(v-\mu_0)\) is proportional to \(\sigma_u/\sigma_v\). More noise trading gives the informed trader cover to trade larger positions without moving prices as much.
Market depth: \(1/\lambda = 2\sigma_u/\sigma_v\) measures market depth — the quantity needed to move the price by one unit. Deeper markets (higher \(\sigma_u\)) have lower price impact.
Efficiency: The equilibrium price absorbs exactly half the information: \(\text{Var}(v|p) = \sigma_v^2/2 = \text{Var}(v)/2\). The market maker reduces uncertainty by 50% by observing order flow.





52.3.5 Prediction Markets






NoteDefinition 7.6.5 — Prediction Market



A prediction market is a financial market specifically designed to aggregate information about the probability of future events. An Arrow-Debreu security for event \(E\) pays \(\$1\) if \(E\) occurs and \(\$0\) otherwise; the equilibrium price \(p\) equals the market-implied probability of \(E\).








ImportantProposition 7.6.4 — Conditions for Prediction Market Accuracy



A prediction market accurately reveals the true probability \(\Pr(E)\) of event \(E\) if: 1. Trader diversity: There are informed traders with signals about \(E\), and their signals are collectively sufficient for \(E\) 2. No manipulation: No trader has market power or incentive to manipulate prices (which requires sufficient liquidity) 3. Rational updating: Traders update beliefs using Bayes’ rule 4. No common value problems: The true probability of \(E\) is pinned down by the fundamentals, not by circular market beliefs








CautionRemark 7.6.2 — Empirical Evidence on Prediction Markets



Prediction markets (Iowa Electronic Markets, Betfair, Polymarket) have proven remarkably accurate in forecasting election outcomes, commodity prices, and economic indicators, often outperforming professional forecasters and polls. The theoretical explanation is consistent with noisy REE: enough traders have private information, and prices efficiently aggregate this information despite individual noise. Exceptions arise when markets are thin, when common value problems create manipulation incentives, or when regulatory restrictions limit participation.








NoteDefinition 7.6.6 — Logarithmic Market Scoring Rule (LMSR)



In a market scoring rule, a market maker plays the role of an automated bookmaker. The logarithmic market scoring rule (LMSR) sets the price of an Arrow-Debreu security based on the current quantity outstanding \(q\): \[p(q) = \frac{e^{q/b}}{\sum_{j} e^{q_j/b}}\] where \(b > 0\) is a liquidity parameter. The LMSR is: - Proper: truth-telling maximizes expected profit for a trader who believes \(\Pr(E) = p\) - Path-independent: the market maker’s final loss depends only on the outcome, not the order of trades - Connection to Bayesian updating: the LMSR price update is equivalent to Bayesian updating in a specific conjugate exponential family model




52.3.6 Event Studies as Information Aggregation Tests






NoteDefinition 7.6.7 — Event Study Methodology



An event study measures the market’s informational response to a discrete event at time \(\tau\). The abnormal return is: \[AR_t = R_t - \hat{R}_t\] where \(R_t\) is the realized return at time \(t\) and \(\hat{R}_t = \hat{\alpha} + \hat{\beta} R_t^M\) is the predicted normal return from a market model estimated on a pre-event window.
The cumulative abnormal return (CAR) over an event window \([\tau_1, \tau_2]\): \[CAR(\tau_1, \tau_2) = \sum_{t=\tau_1}^{\tau_2} AR_t\]
Under the EMH, all information in a publicly announced event is immediately incorporated into prices at \(t = \tau\), so \(AR_t = 0\) for \(t > \tau\) and \(AR_t \neq 0\) only at \(t = \tau\). Pre-event \(CAR(\tau - k, \tau - 1) > 0\) indicates information leakage.








CautionRemark 7.6.3 — Bridge to Empirical Research



Event studies test the speed and accuracy of information aggregation in financial markets. In the context of commodity markets and conflict research, event studies can examine:

How quickly do commodity futures prices incorporate news of resource discoveries or mine openings in the DRC? If markets are efficient, prices should jump at announcement and remain stable thereafter.
Do conflict events affect commodity prices? Abnormal returns in mineral futures following conflict episodes in mining regions would indicate that markets internalize conflict risk.
Pre-event leakage: Positive CAR before public announcement of a conflict event suggests insider trading or information leakage — markets aggregate private information before it becomes public.

The theoretical connection: the Kyle model predicts that informed trading will generate price impact prior to public revelation of information (\(\lambda > 0\)), creating the pre-event drift observed in many event studies. The magnitude of the pre-event CAR is related to Kyle’s \(\lambda\) — higher price impact markets will show stronger pre-announcement drift.
crumbs: 51  7.6 — Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation

title: 51  7.6 — Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation
text:
52.4 Economic Intuition
The information aggregation problem is a microcosm of the tension between competitive equilibrium and strategic behavior. In the frictionless Walrasian world of fully revealing REE, prices solve Hayek’s knowledge problem perfectly. But this leads to the Grossman-Stiglitz paradox — perfectly efficient markets undermine the incentive to become informed. The real world operates in the interior: markets partially reveal information, informed traders earn rents, and prices are more informative than individual signals but less informative than the social planner’s omniscient aggregation.
Kyle’s model captures the strategic dimension: the informed trader voluntarily limits their order size to prevent prices from moving against them. This creates a tradeoff between profit per unit (higher for small orders) and total volume, leading to the half-information result. The price impact coefficient \(\lambda\) is the key structural parameter linking theory to empirics — it can be estimated from order flow data and used to infer the degree of information asymmetry in a market.

title: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives
text:
53 Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives
Instructions: Provide formal proofs for all theoretical results. State assumptions clearly. Use LaTeX for all mathematics. Unless otherwise stated, assume all functions are sufficiently smooth.
crumbs: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives

title: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives
section: 53.1 Problem 1 — Optimal Linear Contract in the LEN Model
text:
53.1 Problem 1 — Optimal Linear Contract in the LEN Model
Consider the LEN (Linear-Exponential-Normal) principal-agent model with the following parameters: - Output: \(q = e + \varepsilon\), \(\varepsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)\) with \(\sigma^2 = 4\) - Agent’s CARA utility with risk aversion coefficient \(r = 0.5\) - Effort cost: \(c(e) = \frac{1}{2}e^2\) (so the cost parameter is \(c = 1\)) - Agent’s reservation utility: \(\bar{u} = 0\) (normalizing the outside option) - Contract: linear \(t = \alpha + \beta q\)
(a) Derive the agent’s optimal effort level \(e^*(\beta)\) as a function of the incentive rate \(\beta\).
(b) Using the binding participation constraint, express the base salary \(\alpha^*(\beta)\) as a function of \(\beta\). Show all steps using the certainty-equivalent formula for CARA-normal utility.
(c) Derive the principal’s profit function \(\Pi(\beta)\) after substituting \(e^*(\beta)\) and \(\alpha^*(\beta)\). Take the derivative and find the optimal incentive rate \(\beta^*\). Compute the numerical value using the given parameters.
(d) Compute the equilibrium effort level \(e^* = e^*(\beta^*)\), the base salary \(\alpha^*\), and the principal’s expected profit \(\Pi^*\).
(e) Compare the second-best outcome to the first-best (observable effort). Compute the efficiency loss as a fraction of first-best total surplus. Interpret the result economically.
(f) Now suppose the principal can observe a noisy signal \(s = e + \eta\) where \(\eta \sim N(0, \sigma_s^2 = 1)\) in addition to \(q\). Using Holmström’s Informativeness Principle, determine whether the optimal contract should condition on \(s\). Derive the optimal contract in the augmented LEN model (with both \(q\) and \(s\)), and show analytically that the inclusion of \(s\) Pareto-improves relative to the contract based on \(q\) alone.
crumbs: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives

title: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives
text:
53.2 Problem 2 — Two-Type Screening Problem
A monopolist provides quality \(q \geq 0\) to a consumer with private type \(\theta \in \{\theta_L, \theta_H\}\), where \(\theta_H = 3\), \(\theta_L = 1\), and \(\Pr(\theta_H) = \lambda = 1/3\). Consumer utility: \(u(q, t, \theta) = \theta \sqrt{q} - t\). The monopolist’s cost: \(c(q) = q\). Reservation utility \(\bar{u} = 0\).
(a) Compute the first-best allocation \((q_L^{FB}, q_H^{FB})\) and the corresponding first-best transfers \((t_L^{FB}, t_H^{FB})\). Show that the first-best allocations are \(q_i^{FB} = \theta_i^2 / 4\).
(b) Verify that the single-crossing condition holds: show that \(MRS_{q,t}(\theta_H, q) > MRS_{q,t}(\theta_L, q)\) for all \(q > 0\).
(c) Set up the monopolist’s second-best program. Write out all four constraints (IC-H, IC-L, IR-H, IR-L) explicitly. Argue informally which constraints bind at the optimum and which are slack. State the standard argument for why IC-H and IR-L bind while IC-L and IR-H are slack.
(d) Using the binding IC-H and IR-L, express \(t_H\) and \(t_L\) as functions of \(q_H\) and \(q_L\). Substitute into the monopolist’s objective function to obtain an unconstrained program in \((q_H, q_L)\).
(e) Derive the optimal \(q_H^{SB}\) and \(q_L^{SB}\) by taking FOCs. Show that: \(q_H^{SB} = q_H^{FB}\) (no distortion at the top) and \(q_L^{SB} < q_L^{FB}\) (distortion at the bottom). Compute the numerical values.
(f) Compute the information rent earned by the high type: \(U_H = \theta_H \sqrt{q_H^{SB}} - t_H^{SB}\).
(g) Discuss when the monopolist would choose to exclude the low type entirely (shut down the low market). Find the condition on parameters under which exclusion is optimal.
crumbs: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives

title: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives
section: 53.3 Problem 3 — Signaling Game: PBE and the Intuitive Criterion
text:
53.3 Problem 3 — Signaling Game: PBE and the Intuitive Criterion
Two types of workers: \(\theta_H = 4\) (high ability) and \(\theta_L = 2\) (low ability), with \(\Pr(\theta_H) = \lambda = 0.4\). Workers choose education \(e \geq 0\). The cost of education is: \[c(e, \theta) = \frac{e^2}{2\theta}\] Wages equal expected ability: \(w = \mathbb{E}[\theta | e]\). Workers maximize \(w - c(e, \theta)\).
(a) Verify the single-crossing condition: \(\frac{\partial^2 c}{\partial e \partial \theta} < 0\) for all \(e > 0\).
(b) Find all separating PBE. Specifically: - Show \(e_L^* = 0\) in any separating equilibrium. - Find the threshold \(\hat{e}\) such that IC-L is satisfied with equality at \(e_H = \hat{e}\). - Characterize all separating equilibria parametrized by \(e_H \geq \hat{e}\). - State the off-path beliefs that support each separating equilibrium.
(c) Find all pooling PBE. Specifically: - Compute the pooling wage \(\bar\theta = \lambda \theta_H + (1-\lambda) \theta_L\). - Find the set of pooling education levels \(e^P\) that can be sustained with off-path beliefs \(\mu(\theta_H | e) = 0\) for \(e \neq e^P\). - State the conditions on \(e^P\) for a pooling PBE to exist.
(d) Apply the Intuitive Criterion: - Show that every pooling equilibrium fails the Intuitive Criterion. Exhibit explicitly the out-of-equilibrium message that the Intuitive Criterion assigns to the high type. - Show that every separating equilibrium with \(e_H > \hat{e}\) fails the Intuitive Criterion. Exhibit the deviation. - Conclude that the unique surviving equilibrium is the least-costly separating equilibrium at \(e_H = \hat{e}\). State the equilibrium explicitly.
(e) Compute the total social welfare loss due to signaling in the least-costly separating equilibrium. Is any party better off compared to the first-best no-signaling allocation?
(f) Testable prediction: State one clear empirical prediction of this model. Describe an identification strategy to test whether the observed education-wage relationship reflects signaling, human capital accumulation, or both. What data variation would allow you to separate these mechanisms?
crumbs: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives

title: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives
section: 53.4 Problem 4 — VCG Mechanism in a Public Goods Problem
text:
53.4 Problem 4 — VCG Mechanism in a Public Goods Problem
Three agents \(i \in \{1,2,3\}\) must decide whether to produce a public good (\(k = 1\)) or not (\(k = 0\)). Agent \(i\) has private value \(v_i(k) = \theta_i \cdot k\) where \(\theta_i \in \{0, 1, 2\}\) is their type. The cost of the public good is \(C = 2\), paid from VCG transfers.
(a) Define the socially efficient decision rule \(k^*(\theta)\): the good is produced iff the total value exceeds the cost. Write out the rule explicitly for all \(3^3 = 27\) type profiles (or characterize it generally).
(b) Construct the VCG mechanism for this problem: - Specify the outcome function \(g(\hat\theta)\): allocation \(k^*(\hat\theta)\) plus transfers \(t_i(\hat\theta)\). - Use the Clarke pivot rule: \(t_i(\hat\theta) = C_i(\hat\theta_{-i}) - \sum_{j\neq i} v_j(k^*(\hat\theta), \hat\theta_j)\) where \(C_i(\hat\theta_{-i})\) is the cost borne by \(i\) in the Groves mechanism. Write out the transfers explicitly.
(c) Verify that truth-telling is a dominant strategy for each agent by checking that for each type profile and each possible misreport by agent 1, agent 1’s payoff is weakly higher from truth-telling.
(d) Is the VCG mechanism budget-balanced? Compute the expected net revenue \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_i t_i]\) under the uniform prior over types and show whether it is positive, negative, or zero.
(e) Does the Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem apply to this problem? Explain why or why not (referring to the quasi-linear domain restriction).
crumbs: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives

title: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives
text:
53.5 Problem 5 — Myerson-Satterthwaite: Efficiency Deficit
Buyer has value \(v \sim U[0,1]\); seller has cost \(c \sim U[0,1]\), independent. Trade is efficient iff \(v \geq c\).
(a) Compute the virtual value of the buyer: \(\psi_B(v) = v - (1-F_B(v))/f_B(v)\), and the virtual cost of the seller: \(\phi_S(c) = c + F_S(c)/f_S(c)\). Interpret each.
(b) For the efficient allocation \(q^*(v,c) = \mathbf{1}[v \geq c]\), compute: - \(\mathbb{E}[(\psi_B(v) - \phi_S(c)) \cdot \mathbf{1}[v \geq c]]\)
Show that this expectation is negative, confirming the budget imbalance at the efficient allocation.
(c) The Myerson-Satterthwaite theorem states that budget-balanced BIC-IR efficiency is impossible. Using the formula from part (b) and the IR constraints \(U_B(0) \geq 0\) and \(U_S(1) \geq 0\), show precisely where the contradiction arises.
(d) Find the Myerson-optimal (second-best) bilateral trade mechanism: the mechanism that maximizes ex-ante gains from trade subject to BIC, IR, and budget balance. Show that trade occurs iff \(\psi_B(v) \geq \phi_S(c)\), and find the threshold values of \(v\) and \(c\) where trade occurs.
(e) Compute the efficiency deficit: the probability of efficient trades foregone in the Myerson-optimal mechanism relative to first-best. Interpret this as the cost of private information in bilateral trade.
crumbs: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives

title: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives
section: 53.6 Problem 6 — Kyle Model: Informed Trader Profits and Price Discovery
text:
53.6 Problem 6 — Kyle Model: Informed Trader Profits and Price Discovery
Consider the Kyle (1985) single-period model with: - Asset value: \(v \sim N(0, \sigma_v^2)\) with \(\sigma_v^2 = 9\) - Noise traders: \(u \sim N(0, \sigma_u^2)\) with \(\sigma_u^2 = 4\) - Informed trader submits \(x\); market maker observes \(y = x + u\) and sets \(p = \mathbb{E}[v|y]\)
(a) Derive the linear equilibrium: find the informed trader’s trading intensity \(\beta\) and the market maker’s price impact \(\lambda\). Compute their numerical values.
(b) Compute the informed trader’s expected profit \(\mathbb{E}[\Pi] = \mathbb{E}[(v-p) \cdot x]\). Show that it equals \(\frac{1}{2}\sigma_v \sigma_u\).
(c) Compute the posterior variance of the asset value conditional on the equilibrium price: \(\text{Var}(v | p)\). Show that exactly half the information is revealed in equilibrium.
(d) Now suppose noise trading doubles: \(\sigma_u^2 = 8\). Recompute \(\beta\), \(\lambda\), and \(\mathbb{E}[\Pi]\). How does the informed trader respond to the change? Does their profit increase or decrease? Interpret.
(e) Consider an alternative setup where there are \(n > 1\) strategic informed traders each observing the same \(v\). The informed traders behave à la Cournot (each chooses \(x_i\) taking others’ strategies as given). Derive the symmetric linear equilibrium in this case. Show that as \(n \to \infty\), \(\lambda \to 0\) and prices become fully informative. Reconcile with the Grossman-Stiglitz paradox.
(f) Empirical application: Describe how Kyle’s \(\lambda\) can be estimated from transaction-level data. What regression would you run? What does an increase in estimated \(\lambda\) before a scheduled earnings announcement imply about information asymmetry?
crumbs: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives

title: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives
section: 53.7 Problem 7 — Grossman-Stiglitz Equilibrium and Price Informativeness
text:
53.7 Problem 7 — Grossman-Stiglitz Equilibrium and Price Informativeness
Consider a Grossman-Stiglitz economy: - Risky asset with true value \(v \sim N(\mu_0, \sigma_v^2 = 4)\) - \(n\) agents, fraction \(\phi\) of whom acquire a signal \(s_i = v + \varepsilon_i\), \(\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma_\varepsilon^2 = 1)\), at cost \(c = 0.5\) - Fraction \((1-\phi)\) are uninformed - Noise traders supply \(u \sim N(0, \sigma_u^2 = 1)\) units - All agents have CARA utility with \(r = 1\)
(a) In the noisy REE, the price \(p\) is a linear function of ${s}{} = {s}{} + $ noise. Write the price as \(p = A + B(v + \eta)\) where \(\eta\) is a composite noise term. Identify \(\eta\) and its variance as a function of \(\phi\), \(\sigma_\varepsilon^2\), and \(\sigma_u^2\).
(b) Compute the conditional variance of \(v\) given the equilibrium price \(p\): \(\text{Var}(v|p) = \text{Var}(v|\phi \bar{s} + \text{noise})\) as a function of \(\phi\). Show that this is decreasing in \(\phi\) (more informed traders \(\to\) more informative price).
(c) Compute the expected utility of an informed agent \(V^I(\phi)\) and of an uninformed agent \(V^U(\phi)\). Show that \(V^I\) is decreasing in \(\phi\) and \(V^U\) is increasing in \(\phi\) (use the CARA-normal expected utility formula).
(d) Find the equilibrium fraction of informed traders \(\phi^*\) by setting \(V^I(\phi^*) - c = V^U(\phi^*)\). Compute \(\phi^*\) for the given parameters.
(e) At \(\phi = \phi^*\), compute the equilibrium price informativeness: the \(R^2\) from a regression of \(v\) on \(p\). How does informativeness respond to changes in information cost \(c\)? Derive \(\frac{d\phi^*}{dc}\) and interpret.
(f) Policy exercise: Suppose a regulator mandates disclosure of all private signals (i.e., forces full revelation). Show that this cannot be an equilibrium if \(c > 0\). What happens to price informativeness and social welfare compared to the Grossman-Stiglitz equilibrium?
crumbs: 52  Problem Set — Part VII: Information and Incentives

title: 53  Part VIII — Social Choice Theory
text: 54 Part VIII — Social Choice Theory
crumbs: 53  Part VIII — Social Choice Theory

title: 53  Part VIII — Social Choice Theory
text:
54.1 Context
Social choice theory addresses a fundamental question: can individual preferences be coherently aggregated into a collective ranking? The question is as old as political philosophy (Condorcet 1785), but Arrow’s (1951) contribution gave it a precise mathematical form and a devastating negative answer. Under four seemingly natural conditions — unrestricted domain, the Pareto principle, independence of irrelevant alternatives, and non-dictatorship — no aggregation rule is consistent. This impossibility result is one of the deepest theorems in all of economics and political science.
The MWG treatment (Chapters 21–22) presents Arrow’s theorem, its proof and implications, alternative welfare criteria (Pareto, Kaldor-Hicks, Scitovsky), and the Bergson-Samuelson social welfare function approach that abandons Arrow’s aggregation framework in favor of a direct ordering over social states based on utility information.
crumbs: 53  Part VIII — Social Choice Theory

title: 53  Part VIII — Social Choice Theory
text:
54.2 Why Social Choice?
From positive to normative economics. The welfare theorems (Part VI) tell us that competitive equilibria are Pareto optimal, but they are silent on which Pareto optimum to implement. A social ranking is needed to compare Pareto-optimal allocations with different distributional implications. Social choice theory studies how individual utility information is (or cannot be) aggregated into such a ranking.
The limits of the Pareto criterion. Pareto dominance is the most cautious welfare criterion: it recommends a change only when everyone agrees it is an improvement. But most real policy changes involve trade-offs — some gain, some lose. Kaldor-Hicks, Rawlsian, and utilitarian criteria are attempts to extend the Pareto principle to such cases, each embodying a different value judgment about distribution.
Arrow’s theorem as a benchmark. The impossibility theorem does not say that social decisions are impossible or irrational. It says that no mechanical rule for aggregating ordinal preferences can satisfy all four axioms simultaneously. The appropriate response is not nihilism but rather careful attention to which axiom is violated: restricting to single-peaked preferences (Black’s median voter), using cardinal utility (utilitarian SWF), or accepting some degree of dictatorship (e.g., a benevolent planner using a social welfare function).
crumbs: 53  Part VIII — Social Choice Theory

title: 53  Part VIII — Social Choice Theory
text:
54.4 Key Results

Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem: No social welfare function satisfies unrestricted domain, Pareto, IIA, and non-dictatorship simultaneously. Any two of the first three conditions imply dictatorship or violation of the fourth.
Gibbard-Satterthwaite: Every non-dictatorial social choice function (mapping profiles to alternatives, not orderings) is manipulable — there exists a profile where some agent benefits from misreporting preferences.
Sen’s Impossibility of a Paretian Liberal: There is no social choice rule that is simultaneously Paretian and respects minimal individual liberty (each person has at least one pair of alternatives over which their preferences are decisive).
Single-peaked preferences and the Median Voter: On a single-dimensional issue space, if preferences are single-peaked, majority rule is transitive and picks the median voter’s ideal point (Black 1948).
crumbs: 53  Part VIII — Social Choice Theory

title: 53  Part VIII — Social Choice Theory
text:
54.5 Cross-References

6.4 Welfare Theorems — Welfare theorems establish Pareto optimality of equilibria; social choice determines which PO allocation to implement
6.5 Externalities and Public Goods — Public good provision requires social choice criteria
5.7 Bayesian Games — Mechanism design (Part VII) applies game theory to implement social choice rules

title: 54  8.1 Social Welfare Functions
text: 55 8.1 Social Welfare Functions

title: 54  8.1 Social Welfare Functions
text:
55.1 Context
The Bergson-Samuelson social welfare function (SWF) is an alternative to Arrow’s aggregation approach: rather than mapping preference profiles to social orderings, it maps utility vectors to a social welfare index. This presupposes cardinal and interpersonally comparable utilities — a significantly stronger informational requirement than Arrow’s ordinal framework. In exchange for this requirement, the SWF approach delivers a complete and transitive social ranking, avoids Arrow’s impossibility, and provides a tractable framework for cost-benefit analysis and optimal tax theory. The utilitarian and Rawlsian SWFs represent the two poles of a spectrum of distributional preferences, parameterized by the isoelastic family, which is now standard in both welfare economics and optimal policy analysis.

title: 54  8.1 Social Welfare Functions
text:
55.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 8.1.1 — Social Choice Rule



A social choice rule is a function \(f: \mathcal{R}^I \to \mathcal{R}\) or \(f: \mathcal{R}^I \to X\) that maps a profile of individual preference orderings \((R_1, \ldots, R_I)\) over alternatives \(X\) to either a social preference ordering over \(X\) or a choice from \(X\).
Arrow’s framework (see 8.2 Arrows Impossibility Theorem) works with ordinal, interpersonally non-comparable preferences. The Bergson-Samuelson approach instead works with cardinal utility levels.








NoteDefinition 8.1.2 — Bergson-Samuelson Social Welfare Function



A Bergson-Samuelson social welfare function is a function \(W: \mathbb{R}^I \to \mathbb{R}\) that assigns a social welfare level to each utility vector \((u_1, \ldots, u_I) \in \mathbb{R}^I\): \[W = W(u_1(x), u_2(x), \ldots, u_I(x)).\] The SWF is defined over utility levels, not preference orderings. It assumes: - Cardinality: Utilities are measured on a common cardinal scale - Interpersonal comparability: The numbers \(u_i\) are comparable across individuals - Pareto principle: \(W\) is increasing in each \(u_i\)








NoteDefinition 8.1.3 — Utilitarian SWF



The utilitarian social welfare function is: \[W^U(u_1, \ldots, u_I) = \sum_{i=1}^I u_i.\] Social welfare is the sum of individual utilities. This is anonymous (symmetric in all \(u_i\)), additively separable, and treats each individual’s utility gain equally regardless of their current utility level.








NoteDefinition 8.1.4 — Rawlsian (Maximin) SWF



The Rawlsian social welfare function is: \[W^R(u_1, \ldots, u_I) = \min_{i \in I} u_i.\] Social welfare equals the utility of the worst-off individual. This reflects extreme inequality aversion: society values only improvements to the bottom of the distribution.








NoteDefinition 8.1.5 — Isoelastic SWF



The isoelastic (CES) social welfare function parameterized by \(\epsilon \geq 0\) is: \[W^\epsilon(u_1, \ldots, u_I) = \begin{cases} \sum_{i=1}^I \frac{u_i^{1-\epsilon}}{1-\epsilon} & \text{if } \epsilon \neq 1 \\ \sum_{i=1}^I \ln u_i & \text{if } \epsilon = 1 \end{cases}\] where utilities are assumed positive.
This family nests the utilitarian SWF as \(\epsilon \to 0\) (in the limit, \(W^\epsilon \to \sum u_i\)) and the Rawlsian SWF as \(\epsilon \to \infty\) (in the limit, \(W^\epsilon \to \min_i u_i\)). The parameter \(\epsilon\) is the inequality aversion coefficient.








NoteDefinition 8.1.6 — Atkinson Inequality Index



For a distribution of incomes \((y_1, \ldots, y_I)\) and isoelastic SWF with parameter \(\epsilon\), the equally-distributed equivalent income \(y^{EDE}\) is defined by: \[W^\epsilon(y^{EDE}, \ldots, y^{EDE}) = W^\epsilon(y_1, \ldots, y_I),\] i.e., the level of equal income that yields the same social welfare as the actual distribution. The Atkinson inequality index is: \[A_\epsilon = 1 - \frac{y^{EDE}}{\bar{y}},\] where \(\bar{y} = \frac{1}{I}\sum_i y_i\) is mean income. \(A_\epsilon \in [0, 1)\) measures inequality: \(A_\epsilon = 0\) iff incomes are perfectly equal; \(A_\epsilon \to 1\) iff welfare is minimized by the distribution.

title: 54  8.1 Social Welfare Functions
text:
55.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 8.1.1 — Harsanyi’s Veil of Ignorance Justification for Utilitarianism



Suppose an impartial observer must rank social states without knowing which individual they will be. If the observer satisfies the von Neumann-Morgenstern axioms (expected utility maximization) and believes each state is equally likely to be their own, then the observer’s preferences over social states are represented by the utilitarian SWF \(W^U = \sum_i u_i\).








TipProof





Let \(x\) and \(y\) be two social states with utility profiles \((u_1(x), \ldots, u_I(x))\) and \((u_1(y), \ldots, u_I(y))\) respectively.
The impartial observer, behind the veil of ignorance, faces a lottery: with probability \(1/I\) they will be individual \(i\). Their expected utility from state \(x\) is: \[V(x) = \sum_{i=1}^I \frac{1}{I} u_i(x) = \frac{1}{I} W^U(u(x)).\] Similarly \(V(y) = \frac{1}{I} W^U(u(y))\). The observer prefers \(x\) to \(y\) iff \(V(x) > V(y)\) iff \(W^U(u(x)) > W^U(u(y))\).
Therefore the observer’s ranking coincides with the utilitarian SWF. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Rawlsian critique of Harsanyi. Rawls (1971) accepts the veil-of-ignorance thought experiment but rejects expected utility maximization behind it. Rawls argues that rational individuals under uncertainty about their position would adopt the maximin rule: maximize the minimum payoff, because the risk of being the worst-off individual is not an ordinary probability but an existential uncertainty. Under maximin, the observer prefers \(x\) to \(y\) iff \(\min_i u_i(x) > \min_i u_i(y)\) — the Rawlsian SWF.
The disagreement between Harsanyi and Rawls is not about the veil-of-ignorance device but about the appropriate decision rule under uncertainty: expected utility maximization (Harsanyi) vs. maximin (Rawls). This reflects different attitudes toward risk — neutrality vs. extreme aversion.








ImportantTheorem 8.1.2 — Isoelastic SWF Nests Utilitarian and Rawlsian




As \(\epsilon \to 0\): \(W^\epsilon(u) \to \sum_i u_i = W^U(u)\) (up to a positive affine transformation).
As \(\epsilon \to \infty\): For any utility profile with \(\min_i u_i = u_{\min}\), \(W^\epsilon(u) \to u_{\min} = W^R(u)\).









TipProof





Part 1. For \(\epsilon\) near 0, \(u_i^{1-\epsilon} = e^{(1-\epsilon)\ln u_i} \approx 1 + (1-\epsilon)\ln u_i\) for small deviations, but more precisely: \(\frac{u_i^{1-\epsilon} - 1}{1-\epsilon} \to \ln u_i\) as \(\epsilon \to 1\) (l’Hôpital’s rule). For \(\epsilon \to 0\): \(W^\epsilon = \frac{1}{1-\epsilon}\sum_i u_i^{1-\epsilon} \approx \frac{1}{1-\epsilon}\sum_i u_i^1 = \frac{1}{1-\epsilon}\sum_i u_i\) which is a positive multiple of \(\sum_i u_i\). More rigorously, the social ranking induced by \(W^\epsilon\) as \(\epsilon \to 0\) converges to the ranking induced by \(W^U\), since \(u_i^{1-\epsilon} \to u_i\) uniformly on compact sets.
Part 2. Write \(u_{\min} = \min_i u_i\) and let \(u_i = u_{\min} + d_i\) with \(d_i \geq 0\). Then: \[W^\epsilon = \frac{\sum_i u_i^{1-\epsilon}}{1-\epsilon} = \frac{u_{\min}^{1-\epsilon} \sum_i (1 + d_i/u_{\min})^{1-\epsilon}}{1-\epsilon}.\] For \(\epsilon \to \infty\): \((1 + d_i/u_{\min})^{1-\epsilon} \to 0\) for all \(d_i > 0\) and equals 1 for \(d_i = 0\). Therefore only the minimizing individual(s) contribute: \[W^\epsilon \sim \frac{u_{\min}^{1-\epsilon} \cdot |\arg\min u_i|}{1-\epsilon}.\] The induced ranking satisfies: \(W^\epsilon(u) > W^\epsilon(v)\) iff \(u_{\min} > v_{\min}\) (for large \(\epsilon\)), which is the Rawlsian criterion. \(\blacksquare\)

title: 54  8.1 Social Welfare Functions
text:
55.4 Sen’s Capabilities Critique






CautionRemark



Sen’s capabilities approach (Sen 1985, 1999) challenges the utility-based welfare framework at its foundations. Sen argues that welfare should be measured not by utility (satisfaction, happiness) but by capabilities: what a person can be and do — the substantive freedoms they enjoy. Key distinctions:

Functionings: actual beings and doings (being well-nourished, being educated, participating in community)
Capabilities: the real freedoms to achieve functionings (capability to be nourished, capability to be educated)

The SWF approach — utilitarian or Rawlsian — aggregates utility or income, which may not capture the actual freedom or opportunities available to individuals. A person with a disability may require more income to achieve the same capability levels; a woman in a patriarchal society may have low utility yet high resourcefulness. Utility-based welfare can be adaptive: oppressed groups may adapt their preferences to their circumstances (“adaptive preferences”), making their utility comparisons misleading guides for policy.
Sen’s critique has practical implications: human development indices (HDI, UNDP) measure capabilities (life expectancy, education, income) rather than utility or income alone.

title: 54  8.1 Social Welfare Functions
section: 55.5 Empirical Link: SWF in Policy Analysis
text:
55.5 Empirical Link: SWF in Policy Analysis
Every cost-benefit analysis implicitly uses a social welfare function, even when analysts claim to be value-neutral. The choice of: - Distributional weights (equal vs. income-adjusted) - Discount rate (reflecting intergenerational welfare comparisons) - Aggregation rule (sum vs. minimum)
are all SWF choices. The Marginal Value of Public Funds (MVPF) framework (Hendren-Sprung-Keyser 2020) makes the implicit SWF explicit by measuring welfare effects of government policies as the ratio of beneficiary gains to government costs, allowing welfare comparisons across policies without committing to a specific SWF.

title: 54  8.1 Social Welfare Functions
text:
55.6 Cross-References

8.2 Arrows Impossibility Theorem — Arrow’s framework is the alternative to the Bergson-Samuelson approach; they contrast in their use of utility information
8.3 Welfare Criteria — Pareto and compensation criteria are specific instances of social choice rules
6.4 Welfare Theorems — Welfare theorems require a SWF to rank among Pareto-optimal equilibria

title: 55  8.2 Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem
text: 56 8.2 Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem

title: 55  8.2 Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem
text:
56.1 Context
Arrow’s impossibility theorem (Arrow 1951) is one of the most profound results in social science. It establishes that no rule for aggregating individual preference orderings into a collective ordering can simultaneously satisfy four natural conditions: it must be defined for all possible preference profiles (unrestricted domain), it must respect unanimous rankings (Pareto), the social ranking of any two alternatives must depend only on individuals’ rankings of those two alternatives (independence of irrelevant alternatives), and no single individual must be a dictator. The proof constructs a sequence of decisive coalitions and shrinks them to a single individual — the dictator — whose existence contradicts the non-dictatorship axiom. Understanding this proof is essential for understanding both the depth of the impossibility and the escape routes available (restricted domains, cardinal utility, weakening IIA).

title: 55  8.2 Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem
text:
56.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 8.2.1 — Arrovian Setup



Let \(X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m\}\) with \(|X| \geq 3\) be a finite set of social alternatives. Let \(I = \{1, \ldots, n\}\) with \(n \geq 2\) be the set of individuals. A preference profile is \(R = (R_1, \ldots, R_I)\) where each \(R_i\) is a complete and transitive weak ordering (preference relation) over \(X\).
Let \(\mathcal{R}\) be the set of all complete, transitive orderings over \(X\). A social welfare function (Arrovian) is a function \(F: \mathcal{R}^I \to \mathcal{R}\) mapping each profile to a social ordering \(R = F(R_1, \ldots, R_I)\).
Notation: write \(x P_i y\) for strict preference (\(x R_i y\) and not \(y R_i x\)), \(x I_i y\) for indifference (\(x R_i y\) and \(y R_i x\)), and \(x P y\) for strict social preference, \(x I y\) for social indifference.








NoteDefinition 8.2.2 — Arrow’s Axioms



A social welfare function \(F\) satisfies:
Axiom U (Unrestricted Domain): \(F\) is defined for every profile \((R_1, \ldots, R_I) \in \mathcal{R}^I\).
Axiom P (Pareto Principle): If \(x P_i y\) for all \(i \in I\), then \(x P y\) (under \(F(R_1, \ldots, R_I)\)).
Axiom IIA (Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives): For any two alternatives \(x, y \in X\) and any two profiles \(R\) and \(R'\): if \(R_i|_{\{x,y\}} = R_i'|_{\{x,y\}}\) for all \(i\) (every individual has the same ranking of \(x\) vs. \(y\) in both profiles), then \(F(R)|_{\{x,y\}} = F(R')|_{\{x,y\}}\) (the social ranking of \(x\) vs. \(y\) is the same).
Axiom ND (Non-Dictatorship): There is no individual \(d \in I\) such that \(x P_d y\) implies \(x P y\) for all \(x, y \in X\) and all profiles \(R \in \mathcal{R}^I\).








NoteDefinition 8.2.3 — Decisive Coalition



A coalition \(S \subseteq I\) is decisive for the ordered pair \((x, y)\) if, whenever \(x P_i y\) for all \(i \in S\) (and any preferences for others), the social ranking has \(x P y\).
A coalition \(S\) is globally decisive (or simply decisive) if it is decisive for every ordered pair \((x, y)\) with \(x \neq y\).

title: 55  8.2 Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem
text:
56.3 Main Result






ImportantTheorem 8.2.1 — Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem (Arrow 1951)



If \(|X| \geq 3\) and \(|I| \geq 2\), then there is no social welfare function satisfying axioms U, P, IIA, and ND simultaneously.








TipProof





We prove that any \(F\) satisfying U, P, and IIA must be a dictatorship, violating ND.

Step 1: The decisive coalition structure.
By Axiom P, the coalition \(I\) (all individuals) is decisive for every pair. So decisive coalitions exist.
Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be the collection of all decisive coalitions. By Pareto, \(I \in \mathcal{D}\), so \(\mathcal{D} \neq \emptyset\). Since \(I\) is finite, there exists a minimal decisive coalition \(S^*\) (a decisive coalition such that no proper subset of \(S^*\) is decisive).

Step 2: Field Expansion Lemma — Decisive for one pair means globally decisive.
We show that if coalition \(S\) is decisive for some ordered pair \((x, y)\), then \(S\) is decisive for every ordered pair.
Suppose \(S\) is decisive for \((x, y)\). Fix any other pair \((a, b)\) with \(a, b, x, y\) possibly overlapping but with \(|X| \geq 3\). We show \(S\) is decisive for \((a, b)\).
Case: \(a, b, x, y\) all distinct (use a third alternative \(z\)). Since \(|X| \geq 3\), pick \(z \neq x, y\). Consider a profile where: - All \(i \in S\): \(a P_i x P_i b\) (and any ranking of \(y\)) - All \(j \notin S\): \(b P_j a\) and \(b P_j x\) and \(a P_j x\) (specifically \(x P_j a\) is optional — the key is \(b P_j a\))
Actually, let me use the standard three-alternative argument. Fix three distinct alternatives \(a, x, b \in X\).
Consider a profile: - All \(i \in S\): \(a P_i x\) and \(x P_i b\) (so \(a P_i b\)) - All \(j \notin S\): \(x P_j a\) and \(x P_j b\) (can rank \(a\) and \(b\) arbitrarily relative to each other)
Since \(S\) is decisive for \((x,b)\) (we handle the general case by applying the argument twice), and all \(i \in S\) have \(x P_i b\)…
Let me use the cleaner standard argument directly:
Suppose \(S\) is decisive for \((x,y)\). Consider any pair \((a,b)\). Choose the profile: - All \(i \in S\): \(a P_i x\), \(x P_i y\), \(y P_i b\) - All \(j \notin S\): arbitrary, but with \(a P_j b\) (or more carefully, \(b P_j a\) and placing \(x\) and \(y\) to avoid triggering \(S\)’s decisiveness for other pairs)
Actually, the standard proof proceeds through two sub-lemmas. Let me state the key facts:
Sub-lemma (L1). If \(S\) is decisive for \((x,y)\) and \(z\) is any third alternative, consider a profile where: \(i \in S\) have \(x P_i z\) and \(x P_i y\); others have \(z P_j x\). The social ranking must have \(x P z\) (because \(S\) is decisive for \((x,y)\) and \(x P_i z\) for all \(i \in S\)… but wait, \(S\) is decisive for \((x,y)\) meaning \(S\)’s unanimous preference for \(x\) over \(y\) forces \(xPy\), not \(xPz\)).
Let me use the well-known proof via the minimal decisive coalition approach, which is cleaner:

Step 2 (revised): The minimal decisive coalition is a singleton.
Let \(S^* = \{d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_k\}\) be a minimal decisive coalition with \(|S^*| = k\). We show \(k = 1\).
Suppose \(k \geq 2\). Partition \(S^* = \{d_1\} \cup T\) where \(T = \{d_2, \ldots, d_k\}\).
Consider three distinct alternatives \(x, y, z \in X\) and a profile: - \(d_1\): \(x P z P y\) - Each \(i \in T\): \(z P x P y\) - Each \(j \notin S^*\): \(y P z P x\)
Social ranking of \((x,y)\): All \(i \in S^* = \{d_1\} \cup T\) have \(x P_i y\). Since \(S^*\) is decisive, \(x P y\).
Social ranking of \((x,z)\): \(d_1\) has \(x P z\). All \(j \notin S^*\) have \(z P x\). The subset \(\{d_1\}\) is not decisive (since \(S^*\) is minimal). Therefore \(\{d_1\}\) being decisive for \((x,z)\) is excluded. Since \(\{d_1\}\) is not globally decisive (by minimality), and in this profile only \(d_1\) prefers \(x\) over \(z\) (others in \(T\) prefer \(z\) over \(x\), and outside \(S^*\) prefer \(z\) over \(x\)), the social ranking could go either way. Suppose \(z R x\) (i.e., \(z P x\) or \(z I x\)).
Social ranking of \((y,z)\): All \(i \in T\) have \(z P y\). All \(j \notin S^*\) have \(y P z\). Exactly the members of \(T\) unanimously prefer \(z\) over \(y\). If \(T\) were decisive, we’d have \(z P y\). Since \(T\) is a proper subset of \(S^*\) and \(S^*\) is minimal, \(T\) is not decisive. So the social ordering of \((y,z)\) could be either way. Suppose \(y R z\).
But now: \(y R z\) and \(z R x\) implies \(y R x\) by transitivity of \(F(R)\). Combined with \(x P y\) (from \(S^*\) being decisive) — contradiction.
The only way to avoid contradiction is to have \(x P z\) (contradicting our supposition that \(z R x\)) or \(z P y\) (contradicting \(y R z\)). If \(x P z\) holds for all such profiles, then \(\{d_1\}\) is decisive for \((x,z)\). If \(z P y\) holds for all such profiles, then \(T\) is decisive for \((z,y)\). In either case, a proper subset of \(S^*\) is decisive — contradicting minimality of \(S^*\).
Therefore \(k \geq 2\) is impossible, and \(k = 1\). The minimal decisive coalition is a singleton \(\{d\}\).

Step 3: The decisive singleton is a dictator.
Since \(\{d\}\) is globally decisive (by the field expansion — a decisive coalition for any pair becomes decisive for all pairs, which can be shown by analogous profile arguments), individual \(d\) is a dictator: \(x P_d y\) implies \(x P y\) for all \(x, y \in X\) and all profiles.
This violates Axiom ND. \(\blacksquare\)

title: 55  8.2 Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem
text:
56.4 Implications and Escape Routes






CautionRemark



Escape Route 1: Restrict the domain (violate U).
If preference profiles are restricted to single-peaked preferences on a one-dimensional issue space (e.g., all voters have a most-preferred policy and rank alternatives by distance from their ideal), then majority rule is consistent (no Condorcet cycles), is Pareto-efficient, and satisfies IIA within the restricted domain. The Median Voter Theorem (Black 1948) states that the social choice under majority rule is the median ideal point. This escape route is appropriate for political elections but not for general economic allocations (where preferences need not be single-peaked).
Escape Route 2: Use cardinal utility (violate ordinal IIA).
The Bergson-Samuelson utilitarian SWF \(W = \sum_i u_i\) satisfies Pareto and non-dictatorship but violates Arrow’s IIA: the social ranking of \(x\) vs. \(y\) depends on utility levels (which may change when a third alternative \(z\) is introduced if utility is rescaled). Cardinal utility allows comparisons of utility differences and levels, enabling the rich family of isoelastic SWFs.
Escape Route 3: Weaken IIA (Borda count).
The Borda count assigns points based on rank position: if \(|X| = m\), alternative \(x\) gets \(m - r_i(x)\) points from voter \(i\), where \(r_i(x)\) is \(x\)’s rank in \(i\)’s ordering. The social ranking is by total points. Borda satisfies U, P, and ND but violates IIA: the social ranking of \(x\) vs. \(y\) depends on how many alternatives are ranked between them.

title: 55  8.2 Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem
text:
56.5 Gibbard-Satterthwaite Theorem






ImportantTheorem 8.2.2 — Gibbard-Satterthwaite (1973)



Let \(|X| \geq 3\) and let \(f: \mathcal{R}^I \to X\) be a social choice function (mapping profiles to chosen alternatives, not orderings) that is: - Onto: Every alternative in \(X\) is chosen at some profile - Non-dictatorial: No individual’s top-ranked alternative is always chosen
Then \(f\) is manipulable: there exists a profile \(R\) and an individual \(i\) with an alternative preference \(R_i'\) such that \(f(R_i', R_{-i}) P_i f(R_i, R_{-i})\) — individual \(i\) benefits from misreporting their preferences.








TipProof





Proof sketch. The Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem follows from Arrow’s theorem via a reduction. Any strategy-proof and onto social choice function can be used to construct a social welfare function satisfying Arrow’s axioms — and Arrow’s theorem then implies it must be dictatorial. Specifically, from \(f\), construct \(F\) by: \(x F(R) y\) iff \(f(R_y) = x\) where \(R_y\) is the profile obtained by placing \(y\) at the bottom of all rankings while preserving relative rankings of other alternatives. This construction satisfies U, P, and IIA, so Arrow’s theorem implies \(F\) is dictatorial, hence \(f\) is dictatorial. Contrapositive: non-dictatorship implies manipulability. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



The Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem is the key motivation for mechanism design. If social choice functions are always manipulable (unless dictatorial), then the analysis of incentive-compatible mechanisms becomes essential. The revelation principle in mechanism design (Part VII) characterizes exactly the social choice functions that can be implemented as the dominant strategy equilibrium of some mechanism.

title: 55  8.2 Arrow’s Impossibility Theorem
text:
