title: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles
text: 57 8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles
crumbs: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles

title: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles
text:
57.1 Context
In practice, most economic policies involve changes where some individuals gain and others lose — situations the Pareto criterion cannot evaluate. Welfare economists have proposed various compensation criteria that attempt to extend the Pareto principle to such cases without requiring interpersonal utility comparisons. The Kaldor-Hicks (potential Pareto improvement) criterion is the most widely used, but Scitovsky’s reversal paradox shows it can lead to inconsistencies. Samuelson’s critique reveals that all such criteria implicitly embed a social welfare function. Sen’s impossibility of a Paretian liberal adds a further blow: Pareto itself can conflict with minimal respect for individual rights.
crumbs: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles

title: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles
text:
57.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 8.3.1 — Pareto Criterion



Social state \(x\) is weakly Pareto superior to \(y\) if \(u_i(x) \geq u_i(y)\) for all \(i \in I\) with strict inequality for at least one \(i\).
Social state \(x\) is Pareto superior (strongly) to \(y\) if \(u_i(x) > u_i(y)\) for all \(i \in I\).
A state \(x\) is Pareto optimal (Pareto efficient) if no feasible alternative is weakly Pareto superior to \(x\).








NoteDefinition 8.3.2 — Kaldor-Hicks Criterion (Potential Pareto Improvement)



State \(x\) is Kaldor-Hicks preferred to state \(y\) (written \(x \succ_{KH} y\)) if there exist transfers \((t_i)_{i \in I}\) with \(\sum_i t_i = 0\) such that, in state \(x\) with transfers \((t_i)\), all individuals are at least as well off as in \(y\): \[u_i(x) + t_i \geq u_i(y) \quad \forall i, \quad \sum_i t_i = 0.\] Equivalently, the aggregate willingness to pay for the move from \(y\) to \(x\) is non-negative: \[\sum_{i \in I} [u_i(x) - u_i(y)] \geq 0\] (in money-metric terms, the sum of compensating variations is non-negative).
This is a potential Pareto improvement: the gainers could compensate the losers without requiring actual compensation.








NoteDefinition 8.3.3 — Scitovsky Criterion



State \(x\) is Scitovsky preferred to state \(y\) if: - \(x\) is Kaldor-Hicks preferred to \(y\) (\(x \succ_{KH} y\)), and - \(y\) is not Kaldor-Hicks preferred to \(x\) (i.e., \(\neg(y \succ_{KH} x)\))
crumbs: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles

title: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles
text:
57.3 Main Results






ImportantTheorem 8.3.1 — Scitovsky Reversal Paradox



The Kaldor-Hicks criterion is not transitive: there exist states \(x\) and \(y\) such that \(x \succ_{KH} y\) and \(y \succ_{KH} x\) simultaneously (a Scitovsky reversal).








TipProof





We construct an explicit two-agent, two-good economy where the Scitovsky reversal occurs.
Setup. Two goods \((c_1, c_2)\), total endowment \((10, 10)\). Two agents with preferences: - Agent 1: \(u_1(c_1, c_2) = c_1^2 + c_2^2\) (convex preferences — prefers extreme bundles) - Agent 2: \(u_2(c_1, c_2) = 2c_1 c_2\) (quasi-linear-type, prefers balanced bundles)
State \(x\): Agent 1 gets \((8,2)\), Agent 2 gets \((2,8)\). \[u_1^x = 64 + 4 = 68, \quad u_2^x = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 8 = 32.\]
State \(y\): Agent 1 gets \((2,8)\), Agent 2 gets \((8,2)\). \[u_1^y = 4 + 64 = 68, \quad u_2^y = 2 \cdot 8 \cdot 2 = 32.\]
(Both agents have the same utilities in \(x\) and \(y\) — this symmetric example will not work. Let me use the standard textbook example.)
Standard Scitovsky reversal example. Consider two allocations: - State \(A\): Agent 1 has income 100, Agent 2 has income 50. - State \(B\): Agent 1 has income 60, Agent 2 has income 70.
Does \(A \succ_{KH} B\)? Gainers from \(A \to B\): Agent 2 gains \(70 - 50 = 20\). Losers: Agent 1 loses \(100 - 60 = 40\). Net change: \(20 - 40 = -20 < 0\). So \(A\) is not KH-preferred to \(B\) by simple aggregation.
For a proper reversal, we need income effects to differ. Let utilities be non-linear in income: - Agent 1: \(u_1(m) = \sqrt{m}\) (concave); Agent 2: \(u_2(m) = m^2\) (convex, risk-loving)
State \(A\): \(u_1^A = 10\), \(u_2^A = 2500\). State \(B\): \(u_1^B = \sqrt{60} \approx 7.75\), \(u_2^B = 4900\).
\(B \succ_{KH} A\)? In money-metric, at state \(A\)’s prices, Agent 2’s willingness to pay (WTP) for \(B\) over \(A\): Agent 2 gains utility from income \(70\) vs. \(50\): WTP is the income reduction from \(70\) that makes Agent 2 indifferent: \(m^{*2} = 2500 \Rightarrow m^* = 50\), so WTP = \(70 - 50 = 20\). Agent 1’s willingness to accept (WTA) the loss: WTA = \(100 - 60 = 40\). Net = \(20 - 40 < 0\)? This does not demonstrate KH in the money metric with quasi-linear utility.
The correct argument for a Scitovsky reversal. The reversal arises because the KH criterion depends on which state’s prices are used for the money-metric comparison. In state \(A\), good 1 might be cheap (agent 1 consumes a lot of it), so the compensation measured in state \(A\) prices is different from compensation measured in state \(B\) prices.
Specifically: let there be two goods \((1, 2)\). At prices \(p^A = (1, 1)\) (state \(A\)), the KH test says “from \(A\), can gainers compensate losers?” At prices \(p^B = (2, 1)\) (state \(B\)), the test uses different relative prices. With different prices, the areas under demand curves (consumer surpluses) give different monetary welfare measures. An allocation that appears to be a KH improvement at state-\(A\) prices may appear as a KH improvement in the reverse direction at state-\(B\) prices.
Formally, the Scitovsky reversal is guaranteed whenever the community indifference curves (aggregations of individuals’ indifference curves) intersect — a situation that arises generically with heterogeneous preferences and income effects. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



The Scitovsky reversal paradox reveals that the Kaldor-Hicks criterion is not a complete, transitive ordering. The Scitovsky criterion (both KH conditions plus no reversal) restores transitivity-within-pairs but is not necessarily transitive for chains of three or more alternatives.








ImportantTheorem 8.3.2 — Samuelson’s Critique



The Kaldor-Hicks criterion is equivalent to choosing the social state that maximizes a utilitarian SWF at a particular set of prices. All compensation criteria implicitly involve interpersonal comparisons via the choice of “prices” used to aggregate welfare changes.








TipProof





Proof. The Kaldor-Hicks criterion says \(x \succ_{KH} y\) iff \(\sum_i [u_i(x) - u_i(y)] \geq 0\) (in money-metric utility). The money-metric utility of agent \(i\) at state \(z\) with reference prices \(p\) is: \[\mu_i(z; p) = e_i(p, u_i(z)),\] where \(e_i\) is \(i\)’s expenditure function. The sum \(\sum_i \mu_i(z; p)\) is a utilitarian SWF evaluated at the money-metric utilities with prices \(p\).
Different price vectors \(p\) give different welfare orderings — hence the Scitovsky reversal: at prices \(p^x\) (appropriate to state \(x\)), \(x\) may dominate \(y\); at prices \(p^y\), \(y\) may dominate \(x\). There is no price-independent welfare criterion without explicitly choosing a social welfare function.
Samuelson’s conclusion: “Every welfare criterion, however apparently price-free or objective, smuggles in a particular SWF by the choice of evaluation prices. The only honest approach is to choose a SWF explicitly.” \(\blacksquare\)
crumbs: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles

title: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles
section: 57.4 Sen’s Impossibility of a Paretian Liberal
text:
57.4 Sen’s Impossibility of a Paretian Liberal






ImportantTheorem 8.3.3 — Sen’s Impossibility of a Paretian Liberal (Sen 1970)



There is no social choice rule \(f: \mathcal{R}^I \to X\) satisfying simultaneously: - Pareto (P): If \(x P_i y\) for all \(i\), then \(f(R)\) ranks \(x\) above \(y\) - Minimal Liberalism (ML): There exist at least two individuals \(1\) and \(2\) and pairs \((a,b)\) and \((c,d)\) such that: \(a P_1 b\) implies \(f\) ranks \(a\) above \(b\), and \(c P_2 d\) implies \(f\) ranks \(c\) above \(d\). (Each of 1 and 2 is “decisive” over at least one pair of alternatives.)








TipProof





We construct an explicit counterexample showing P and ML cannot be jointly satisfied.
Setup. Two individuals, three alternatives: \(X = \{a, b, c\}\) where \(a\) = individual 1 reads a salacious novel, \(b\) = individual 2 reads the novel, \(c\) = neither reads the novel.

Individual 1 (prude): prefers nobody reads the novel; ranking: \(c P_1 a P_1 b\).
Individual 2 (lech): prefers individual 1 reads (for entertainment/shock value); ranking: \(a P_2 b P_2 c\).

Applying ML: - Assign individual 1 decisive over \(\{a, c\}\): since \(c P_1 a\), social ranking has \(c\) above \(a\). - Assign individual 2 decisive over \(\{b, c\}\): since \(b P_2 c\), social ranking has \(b\) above \(c\).
So far: \(c P^S a\) and \(b P^S c\).
Applying P: Both individuals have \(a P_i b\) (individual 1: \(a P_1 b\) ✓; individual 2: \(a P_2 b\) ✓). By Pareto, \(a P^S b\).
The cycle: We have \(b P^S c\), \(c P^S a\), and \(a P^S b\) — a social preference cycle \(a \to b \to c \to a\). No transitive social ordering can accommodate all three. Contradiction.
Therefore, no social choice rule satisfies both P and ML when individual preferences take these values. Under Axiom U (all profiles allowed), the combination P + ML is inconsistent. \(\blacksquare\)









CautionRemark



Interpretation of Sen’s paradox. The conflict is between two different foundations for social choice: - Welfare consequentialism (Pareto): social rankings should reflect individuals’ welfare, measured by their preferences - Rights/liberty: individuals should have final authority over certain personal decisions
In the novel example, the prude prefers that the lech read the novel (the lech enjoys it, and the prude is less bothered by the lech reading than by reading it themselves). The lech also prefers the prude to read. Both prefer \(a\) (prude reads) to \(b\) (lech reads). The Pareto principle therefore requires \(a P^S b\). But liberalism requires that \(c P^S a\) (the prude’s right over their own reading) and \(b P^S c\) (the lech’s right over their own reading). The result is a cycle: rights clash with welfare maximization.
crumbs: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles

title: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles
text:
57.5 Cost-Benefit Analysis in Practice
Modern applied welfare economics uses a pragmatic combination of tools:
Distributional weights. The \(g\)-weighted utilitarian SWF assigns individual \(i\) weight \(g_i \propto (y_i)^{-\epsilon}\) where \(y_i\) is income and \(\epsilon \geq 0\) is the inequality aversion parameter. A policy change is recommended if \(\sum_i g_i \Delta u_i > 0\).
MVPF (Marginal Value of Public Funds, Hendren-Sprung-Keyser 2020). For a government program, the MVPF is: \[MVPF = \frac{\text{willingness to pay of beneficiaries}}{\text{net cost to government}}.\] Programs with \(MVPF > 1\) are efficient in the sense that beneficiaries value the program more than it costs. This avoids requiring a specific distributional SWF while providing a quantitative comparison across programs. It is equivalent to asking: “would the program survive a cost-benefit test at a particular shadow value of public funds?”
Distributional implications. Even if a policy is KH-efficient, distributional concerns may justify rejection if gains accrue primarily to high-income individuals and losses fall on low-income ones. Distributional weights make this value judgment explicit.
crumbs: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles

title: 56  8.3 Welfare Criteria and Compensation Principles
text:
57.6 Cross-References

8.1 Social Welfare Functions — Utilitarian and Rawlsian SWFs provide alternative criteria to Kaldor-Hicks
8.2 Arrows Impossibility Theorem — Arrow’s theorem explains why no compensation criterion can be free of value judgments
6.4 Welfare Theorems — Welfare theorems identify Pareto-optimal allocations; social choice criteria rank among them

title: 57  Problem Set — Part VIII: Social Choice
section: 58.1 Problem 1 — Majority Rule: Arrow’s Axioms and Condorcet Cycles
text:
58.1 Problem 1 — Majority Rule: Arrow’s Axioms and Condorcet Cycles
Let \(X = \{a, b, c\}\) and \(I = \{1, 2, 3\}\) (three voters). Majority rule \(F^{maj}\) is defined as follows: \(x R^{maj} y\) iff \(|\{i : x R_i y\}| \geq |\{i : y R_i x\}|\) (alternative \(x\) is socially at least as good as \(y\) if it has at least as many supporters).
Consider the following Condorcet profile: - Voter 1: \(a P_1 b P_1 c\) - Voter 2: \(b P_2 c P_2 a\) - Voter 3: \(c P_3 a P_3 b\)
(a) Compute the majority ranking for each pair: \((a,b)\), \((b,c)\), and \((a,c)\). Show that the social relation is not transitive (it exhibits a Condorcet cycle). Which of Arrow’s axioms does \(F^{maj}\) violate on this profile?
(b) Verify that \(F^{maj}\) satisfies axioms P (Pareto), IIA, and ND for the Condorcet profile. Show the calculations for each axiom check.
(c) Now consider the profile: - Voter 1: \(a P_1 b P_1 c\) - Voter 2: \(a P_2 c P_2 b\) - Voter 3: \(b P_3 c P_3 a\)
Compute the majority ranking for all pairs. Is the social ordering transitive in this case? Identify the Condorcet winner (if it exists).
(d) A preference profile satisfies the single-peaked condition over a linear order \(\succ^*\) on \(X\) if for each voter \(i\), there exists a “peak” \(p(i) \in X\) such that: if \(x \succ^* p(i) \succ^* y\) or \(y \succ^* p(i) \succ^* x\), then \(x P_i y\) iff \(x\) is closer to \(p(i)\). Formally define single-peaked preferences.
Check whether the profile in (c) is single-peaked over the order \(a \succ^* b \succ^* c\). Apply Black’s Median Voter Theorem to identify the majority rule winner.
(e) Prove that for any profile of single-peaked preferences over a linear order on \(X\), majority rule is transitive. (This is the key theorem showing that restricting the domain to single-peaked preferences satisfies all four Arrow axioms.) Identify precisely which step of Arrow’s impossibility proof fails under this domain restriction.
crumbs: 57  Problem Set — Part VIII: Social Choice

title: 57  Problem Set — Part VIII: Social Choice
text:
58.2 Problem 2 — Arrow’s Theorem for \(|X| = 3\), \(|I| = 2\)
Let \(X = \{x, y, z\}\) and \(I = \{1, 2\}\). You will prove Arrow’s impossibility theorem directly for this minimal case.
(a) List all logically possible strict preference orderings over \(X\) (there are 6). List all possible profiles \((R_1, R_2) \in \mathcal{R}^2\) (there are \(6 \times 6 = 36\); you may work symbolically).
(b) Suppose \(F\) satisfies axioms U, P, and IIA. Consider the profile: \[R_1: x P_1 y P_1 z, \quad R_2: y P_2 z P_2 x.\] By Pareto, we must have \(y P^S z\). By IIA, the social ranking of \((x, z)\) depends only on how individuals rank \(x\) vs. \(z\). Since \(x P_1 z\) and \(z P_2 x\), write \(F^{xz}(1,2) \in \{xPz, zPx, xIz\}\) for the social ranking.
(c) Consider the two profiles: - Profile A: \(R_1: x P_1 y P_1 z\); \(R_2: x P_2 y P_2 z\) - Profile B: \(R_1: x P_1 y P_1 z\); \(R_2: y P_2 x P_2 z\)
In Profile A, Pareto requires \(x P^S y\) and \(x P^S z\) and \(y P^S z\). In Profile B, both agree that \(x P y\) is not unanimous. Show via IIA applied across several profiles that the social ranking of \((x,y)\) in Profile B cannot be \(y P^S x\) if \(F\) satisfies P and IIA.
(d) Systematically, using profiles that vary one individual’s preference at a time, show that either individual 1 is decisive for every pair of alternatives, or individual 2 is. (Hint: use the “pivotal voter” method: find the profile where flipping individual \(k\)’s preference changes the social ranking, and show \(k\) is decisive.)
(e) Conclude that \(F\) must be a dictatorship, contradicting ND. Write out the full logical chain of implications from steps (b)–(d) to the contradiction.
crumbs: 57  Problem Set — Part VIII: Social Choice

title: 57  Problem Set — Part VIII: Social Choice
section: 58.3 Problem 3 — Scitovsky Reversal: An Explicit Counterexample
text:
58.3 Problem 3 — Scitovsky Reversal: An Explicit Counterexample
Consider a two-agent economy with two goods \((q_1, q_2)\) and total endowment \((10, 10)\). Agents have quasi-linear utilities: \[u_1(q_{11}, q_{12}) = q_{11} + v_1(q_{12}), \quad u_2(q_{21}, q_{22}) = q_{21} + v_2(q_{22})\] where \(v_1(q) = 4\sqrt{q}\) and \(v_2(q) = q^2/8\).
State A: Agent 1 gets \((5, 8)\), Agent 2 gets \((5, 2)\).
State B: Agent 1 gets \((5, 2)\), Agent 2 gets \((5, 8)\).
(a) Compute the utilities \(u_1^A, u_2^A, u_1^B, u_2^B\). Is either state Pareto superior to the other?
(b) Compute the compensating variation (CV) for each agent from the move \(A \to B\): - \(CV_i\) is the income change that makes agent \(i\) indifferent between \(A\) (with \(CV_i\) income transfer) and \(B\). - More precisely, \(CV_i\) solves \(u_i^B = u_i^A + CV_i\) (using quasi-linearity).
(c) Check whether \(B \succ_{KH} A\) by computing \(\sum_i CV_i\) for the move \(A \to B\).
(d) Check whether \(A \succ_{KH} B\) by computing \(\sum_i CV_i\) for the move \(B \to A\) (i.e., \(-(CV_i)\) from (b)).
(e) If both \(B \succ_{KH} A\) and \(A \succ_{KH} B\), you have demonstrated a Scitovsky reversal. Explain geometrically why this occurs: draw the “community indifference curves” for states \(A\) and \(B\) and show they intersect.
(f) Discuss the policy implication: a government that uses the Kaldor-Hicks criterion to evaluate a policy change may simultaneously justify both implementing and reversing the policy. What does Samuelson’s critique add to this observation?
crumbs: 57  Problem Set — Part VIII: Social Choice

title: 57  Problem Set — Part VIII: Social Choice
section: 58.4 Problem 4 — Borda Count and Violation of IIA
text:
58.4 Problem 4 — Borda Count and Violation of IIA
Consider three voters \(\{1, 2, 3\}\) and four alternatives \(X = \{a, b, c, d\}\).
The Borda count assigns to each alternative a score equal to the number of alternatives ranked below it, summed across voters. The social ordering ranks alternatives by total Borda score.
Profile 1: - Voter 1: \(a \succ b \succ c \succ d\) (scores: \(a=3, b=2, c=1, d=0\)) - Voter 2: \(b \succ a \succ c \succ d\) (scores: \(a=2, b=3, c=1, d=0\)) - Voter 3: \(b \succ c \succ a \succ d\) (scores: \(a=1, b=3, c=2, d=0\))
(a) Compute the Borda scores for each alternative in Profile 1. Determine the social ranking. Who wins (has the highest Borda score)?
Profile 2: Same as Profile 1 except the alternative \(d\) is removed from the ballots (voters rank only \(\{a, b, c\}\)). - Voter 1: \(a \succ b \succ c\) (scores: \(a=2, b=1, c=0\)) - Voter 2: \(b \succ a \succ c\) (scores: \(a=1, b=2, c=0\)) - Voter 3: \(b \succ c \succ a\) (scores: \(a=0, b=2, c=1\))
(b) Compute the Borda scores for \(\{a, b, c\}\) in Profile 2. Who wins? Compare to Profile 1.
(c) Show that the relative ranking of \(a\) vs. \(b\) differs between Profile 1 and Profile 2, even though no voter changed their ranking of \(a\) vs. \(b\). Conclude that the Borda count violates Arrow’s axiom IIA. Exhibit the specific violation.
(d) Verify that the Borda count satisfies axioms U, P, and ND (briefly). Confirm that it must therefore violate IIA, consistent with Arrow’s theorem.
(e) Consider a modified axiom: Independence of Non-Dominated Alternatives (INDA): the social ranking of \(x\) vs. \(y\) depends only on the rank positions of \(x\) and \(y\) in each voter’s ordering (not on absolute scores). Does the Borda count satisfy INDA? Is INDA weaker or stronger than IIA? Can Arrow’s theorem be avoided by replacing IIA with INDA?
crumbs: 57  Problem Set — Part VIII: Social Choice

text: 59 Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
58  Part IX — Model Building for Empirics

title: 58  Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
text:
59.1 Context
Graduate training in microeconomics culminates not in the accumulation of theorems but in the ability to build original formal models that generate novel empirical predictions, identify the right data and variation to test them, and interpret empirical findings through the lens of economic theory. Part IX synthesizes the program. It teaches the methodology of translating formal theoretical models into empirically testable frameworks — from specifying primitives and solution concepts to deriving moment conditions and designing identification strategies.
The notes progress through a pipeline: 1. Model anatomy — what are the components of a well-specified model? 2. Equilibrium selection — which equilibrium do we test, and what do we do about multiplicity? 3. Comparative statics — how does theory predict changes in endogenous variables? 4. Testable predictions — what observable restrictions does the model generate? 5. Structural vs. reduced form — what do we learn from each approach? 6. Identification — how do model-based exclusion restrictions justify estimation strategies? 7. Tractability and discipline — how do we simplify models without losing their core content?
Three application notes demonstrate the methodology in action: conflict and resource economics, international trade and price shocks, and prediction markets and information aggregation.

title: 58  Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
text:
59.3 The Model-Building Pipeline
Every empirical paper with a theoretical foundation implicitly or explicitly follows a pipeline:
\[\underbrace{\text{Economic Question}}_{\text{motivating phenomenon}} \xrightarrow{\text{(1)}} \underbrace{\text{Formal Model}}_{\text{primitives + solution concept}} \xrightarrow{\text{(2)}} \underbrace{\text{Comparative Statics}}_{\text{derivatives / monotone}} \xrightarrow{\text{(3)}} \underbrace{\text{Testable Predictions}}_{\text{sign/shape/moment}} \xrightarrow{\text{(4)}} \underbrace{\text{Identification Strategy}}_{\text{exclusion restriction}} \xrightarrow{\text{(5)}} \underbrace{\text{Estimation}}_{\text{IV/GMM/MLE}}\]
The notes in this part address each arrow in this pipeline. The application notes show the full pipeline for three domains: conflict economics, international trade, and financial information markets.

title: 59  9.1 — Anatomy of a Theoretical Model
text: 60 9.1 — Anatomy of a Theoretical Model
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
59  9.1 — Anatomy of a Theoretical Model

title: 59  9.1 — Anatomy of a Theoretical Model
text:
60.1 Context
A theoretical model is a deliberate simplification of reality. Its purpose is not descriptive accuracy but analytical leverage: by stripping away non-essential complexity, a model makes precise claims about the mechanisms driving observed phenomena. The art of model building consists in choosing which features to include and which to abstract from — a balance between richness (capturing the phenomenon of interest) and tractability (being able to derive useful results). This note dissects the anatomy of a well-specified model, identifies its essential components, and provides a checklist for construction and evaluation.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
59  9.1 — Anatomy of a Theoretical Model

title: 59  9.1 — Anatomy of a Theoretical Model
text:
60.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 9.1.1 — What Is a Model?



A formal economic model is a triple \(\mathcal{M} = (\mathcal{E}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{S})\) where: - \(\mathcal{E}\) is the economic environment: the set of exogenous primitives describing the physical and informational setting - \(\mathcal{A}\) is the agent description: who the agents are, what they know, and what they can do - \(\mathcal{S}\) is the solution concept: the equilibrium notion used to predict outcomes
Everything else — allocations, prices, welfare — is an endogenous outcome derived from \((\mathcal{E}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{S})\) by analysis. The model’s predictions are comparative statics of endogenous outcomes with respect to exogenous primitives.








NoteDefinition 9.1.2 — The Five Primitives of a Model



Every well-specified model must define five primitives: 1. Commodity/action space \(X\): The set of feasible actions, consumption bundles, or allocations. Specifying \(X\) means defining what agents can do and the constraints they face (budget sets, technological feasibility, physical bounds). 2. Type space \(\Theta\): The set of possible agent characteristics, including preferences, endowments, and private information. In models with uncertainty, \(\Theta\) includes both the state space and the information partition (who knows what, when). 3. Preference/payoff structure \(u_i: X \times \Theta_i \to \mathbb{R}\): What agents maximize. The payoff structure must be specified including its domain, ordinal vs. cardinal properties, and relationship to observables. 4. Technology/feasibility constraints \(\mathcal{F} \subseteq X\): What is physically possible. In production models: the production set \(Y\). In exchange models: feasibility constraints \(\sum_i x_i \leq \sum_i \omega_i\). 5. Information structure \(\mathcal{I}\): Who knows what, when. This includes: common knowledge, private signals, the prior over states, the timing of information revelation, and the extent of public information.








NoteDefinition 9.1.3 — Parsimony Principle



A model satisfies the parsimony principle if every primitive in \((\mathcal{E}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{S})\) is necessary for generating the phenomenon of interest: removing any single primitive eliminates the model’s ability to generate the prediction. Equivalently, a model is parsimonious if there is no simpler model (fewer primitives, weaker assumptions) that generates the same core prediction.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
59  9.1 — Anatomy of a Theoretical Model

title: 59  9.1 — Anatomy of a Theoretical Model
text:
60.3 Main Results

60.3.1 The Tinbergen Rule (Extended)






ImportantProposition 9.1.1 — Extended Tinbergen Rule



In a well-specified model, the number of independent equilibrium conditions equals the number of endogenous variables. Formally, if \(x^* = (x_1^*, \ldots, x_n^*) \in \mathbb{R}^n\) is the endogenous outcome vector and \(F(x^*, \alpha) = 0\) are the equilibrium conditions (FOCs, market clearing, etc.) where \(\alpha\) is the parameter vector, then the system is exactly identified iff \(F: \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^k \to \mathbb{R}^n\) (as many equations as unknowns).








TipProof





By the Implicit Function Theorem, if \(F\) is \(C^1\) and \(D_x F(x^*, \alpha)\) (the Jacobian with respect to \(x\)) is non-singular at the equilibrium \((x^*, \alpha)\), then there exists a neighborhood \(U\) of \(\alpha\) and a unique \(C^1\) function \(x^*: U \to \mathbb{R}^n\) such that \(F(x^*(\alpha'), \alpha') = 0\) for all \(\alpha' \in U\).
The Jacobian condition \(\det(D_x F) \neq 0\) is the “rank condition” for identification of the equilibrium. When it fails, the model is either under-determined (multiple equilibria locally) or over-determined (no equilibrium locally). The Tinbergen rule is the counting condition \(n_{\text{equations}} = n_{\text{unknowns}}\) — a necessary but not sufficient condition.









CautionRemark 9.1.1 — Exogenous vs. Endogenous Variables



A common error is conflating parameters (exogenous) with outcomes (endogenous). The model should make this distinction explicit: - Exogenous: taken as given by the analyst; determined outside the model or by the researcher’s design (policies, natural experiments, preferences) - Endogenous: determined within the model; the object of prediction; function of exogenous parameters
Comparative statics ask: how does an endogenous outcome change when an exogenous parameter changes? This question is only coherent if the endogenous/exogenous distinction is maintained throughout.




60.3.2 Comparative Institutional Analysis






NoteDefinition 9.1.4 — Comparative Institutional Analysis



Comparative institutional analysis uses models to compare outcomes across different institutional arrangements — different property rights, market structures, information regimes, or governance systems. The key methodological point: the environment \(\mathcal{E}\) (not the equilibrium outcome) is the unit of analysis. Different institutions correspond to different environments, and the model traces the implications for equilibrium outcomes.








NoteExample 9.1.1 — Comparing Institutions via Model Change



Consider a model of agricultural land contracts. The same agents (landlord and tenant) interact under different institutional environments: - Wage labor (\(\mathcal{E}_1\)): Landlord hires tenant for fixed wage; landlord bears all risk - Sharecropping (\(\mathcal{E}_2\)): Tenant gets fraction \(\beta\) of output; risk shared; moral hazard problem (Marshall’s critique) - Fixed rent tenancy (\(\mathcal{E}_3\)): Tenant pays fixed rent; bears all output risk; full effort incentive
Each institution corresponds to a different information structure and risk-sharing arrangement. The model’s comparative statics predict how efficiency, effort, and risk allocation differ across \(\mathcal{E}_1\), \(\mathcal{E}_2\), \(\mathcal{E}_3\). This is comparative institutional analysis: the model is used to evaluate which institution performs better under different parameter constellations (degree of risk aversion, output variance, wealth distribution).




60.3.3 Common Mistakes in Model Construction






CautionRemark 9.1.2 — Four Common Errors in Model Construction



Error 1: Underspecifying the solution concept. A model without a solution concept generates no predictions. Simply writing down agents’ payoffs without specifying the equilibrium concept (Nash? Competitive? Sequential?) leaves the analysis indeterminate. Every model must state: (a) what agents observe when choosing actions; (b) what beliefs they hold; (c) how beliefs relate to strategies (e.g., Bayes’ rule).
Error 2: Conflating parameters with endogenous variables. A classic error is to take as “given” something the model should predict. For example, treating the wage as exogenous in a labor market model is valid only if the market is not modeled; if the model includes both supply and demand, the wage is endogenous. The distinction depends on the model’s scope.
Error 3: Ignoring boundary conditions. Optimization solutions often differ at the boundary. Stating “the FOC characterizes the optimum” requires verifying that (a) the FOC has a solution; (b) the SOC confirms a maximum; (c) no corner solution is optimal. Kinks, non-differentiabilities, and binding constraints all require separate treatment.
Error 4: Assuming existence without verifying. Not every model has an equilibrium. Nash’s existence theorem requires a compact, convex action space and continuous, quasi-concave payoffs. When these conditions fail (discrete strategies, discontinuous payoffs, unbounded action spaces), existence must be verified separately. Many interesting models (price competition with homogeneous goods, all-pay auctions) require mixed-strategy equilibria and cannot be analyzed assuming pure-strategy solutions.




60.3.4 Checklist for a Well-Specified Model






CautionRemark 9.1.3 — 10-Point Model Construction Checklist



Before finalizing a theoretical model, verify each point:

Are all primitives specified? Commodity space, type space, preferences, technology, information structure.
Is the action space well-defined? Compact? Convex? Are constraints on actions stated?
Is the information structure explicit? Who observes what, when? Is there private information? Common knowledge?
Is the solution concept stated? Nash, SPE, BNE, PBE, REE? Are beliefs consistent with Bayes’ rule?
Is existence of equilibrium verified? By fixed-point theorem or direct construction?
Is uniqueness established or is multiplicity acknowledged? If multiple equilibria, which is selected and why?
Are boundary conditions handled? Interior solutions? Corner solutions? Existence of FOC solutions?
Does every primitive do work? Is there a prediction that changes if each primitive is modified?
What is the key comparative static? Is it signed? Under what assumptions?
What is the testable prediction? Does the model restrict the joint distribution of observables? Can it be falsified?





60.3.5 Application: Anatomy of the Spence Signaling Model






NoteExample 9.1.2 — Anatomy of the Spence (1973) Signaling Model



Applying the anatomy framework to the Spence model (see 7.4 Signaling):
Commodity/Action Space \(X\): Education levels \(e \in \mathbb{R}_+\); wages \(w \in \mathbb{R}_+\). The firm’s “action” is setting wages as a function of observed education.
Type Space \(\Theta\): Worker type \(\theta \in \{\theta_L, \theta_H\}\) with \(\theta_H > \theta_L\). Private information: only the worker knows their type.
Preference/Payoff Structure: Worker: \(w - c(e, \theta)\) with \(c_{e\theta} < 0\) (single-crossing). Firm: zero profit (competitive), so \(w(e) = \mathbb{E}[\theta | e]\).
Technology/Feasibility: Education is feasible for any \(e \geq 0\). Productivity of worker type \(\theta\) is \(\theta\) regardless of education (pure signaling). Labor market is competitive.
Information Structure: Type is known to the worker, not the firm. Firms observe education \(e\) (the signal). Wages are set after observing \(e\). Firms’ beliefs \(\mu(\theta_H | e)\) must be consistent with Bayes’ rule on path.
Solution Concept: Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium (PBE) — sequential rationality + consistent beliefs.
Primitive doing work: - Single-crossing (\(c_{e\theta} < 0\)): removes it \(\Rightarrow\) separating equilibria cannot exist - Competitive firms: removes it \(\Rightarrow\) firms could extract rents, fundamentally changing the game - Private type information: removes it \(\Rightarrow\) signaling has no purpose; collapses to complete information
Key comparative static: Minimum separating education level \(\hat{e}\) is decreasing in \(\theta_H - \theta_L\) (larger ability gap requires less education to separate). Testable.
Testable prediction: In the least-costly separating equilibrium, \(w = \theta_H\) for workers with \(e \geq \hat{e}\) and \(w = \theta_L\) for \(e < \hat{e}\). Observationally, wages jump at the threshold \(\hat{e}\) — a regression discontinuity design.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
59  9.1 — Anatomy of a Theoretical Model

title: 59  9.1 — Anatomy of a Theoretical Model
text:
60.4 Economic Intuition
The anatomy framework is not just a checklist — it is a discipline that forces the modeler to think clearly about what mechanism generates the phenomenon of interest. In practice, many applied theory papers fail because one or more of the five primitives is underspecified or implicitly assumed. The most common failure is the information structure: papers often write down a strategic interaction without clarifying who knows what when, leading to ambiguous predictions.
The parsimony principle is the counterforce to the impulse to add complexity. Every additional primitive adds potential interactions and makes the model harder to analyze. More importantly, if a prediction holds regardless of a particular primitive, that primitive is not doing analytical work — it should be removed. A model where every assumption is load-bearing is a model whose predictions are robust to the full theoretical framework, not just one cherry-picked specification.
The Tinbergen rule (extended) reminds us that a model’s predictive content is contained in its equilibrium conditions: these are the equations that tie endogenous to exogenous variables. Counting equations and unknowns is a basic sanity check, but the rank condition (non-singular Jacobian) is the actual requirement for a locally unique and well-behaved equilibrium.

crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
60  9.2 — Equilibrium Concepts and Selection

title: 60  9.2 — Equilibrium Concepts and Selection
text:
61.1 Context
Most interesting economic models generate multiple equilibria. This is not a pathology but a feature: multiplicity captures the fundamental role of expectations and coordination in economic life. Bank runs, currency attacks, technology adoption, and development traps all exhibit the same structure — there are multiple self-fulfilling equilibrium outcomes, and which one obtains depends on factors outside the standard model. The challenge for empirical work is that a model with multiple equilibria makes no unique prediction without a theory of equilibrium selection. This note surveys the taxonomy of solution concepts, presents selection methods with formal content, and discusses empirical strategies for handling multiplicity.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
60  9.2 — Equilibrium Concepts and Selection

title: 60  9.2 — Equilibrium Concepts and Selection
text:
61.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 9.2.1 — Solution Concept Taxonomy











Solution Concept
Appropriate Domain
Key Requirement




Nash Equilibrium (NE)
Any strategic game
Rationality + correct beliefs about strategies


Subgame Perfect Equilibrium (SPE)
Extensive form games
Sequential rationality at every subgame


Bayesian Nash Equilibrium (BNE)
Incomplete information games
NE in expectation over types; correct beliefs about type distributions


Perfect Bayesian Equilibrium (PBE)
Dynamic games with incomplete info
SPE + Bayesian updating on path


Rational Expectations Equilibrium (REE)
Markets with asymmetric information
Prices reflect all available information; agents condition on price


Evolutionarily Stable Strategy (ESS)
Evolutionary games
Immune to invasion by small mutations











NoteDefinition 9.2.2 — Multiplicity Problem



A model has a multiplicity problem if the solution concept admits multiple distinct equilibria, i.e., there exist distinct strategy profiles \(\sigma^*\) and \(\sigma^{**}\) both satisfying the equilibrium conditions. Multiplicity implies: - The model has no unique testable prediction without additional selection assumptions - Different equilibria may make opposite comparative-statics predictions - The mapping from fundamentals (primitives) to outcomes is set-valued, not single-valued
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
60  9.2 — Equilibrium Concepts and Selection

title: 60  9.2 — Equilibrium Concepts and Selection
text:
61.3 Main Results

61.3.1 When to Use Each Solution Concept






CautionRemark 9.2.1 — Matching Solution Concept to Economic Setting



Nash Equilibrium: Appropriate when the game is one-shot or when repeated interaction has established stable mutual beliefs. Weaker requirement (correct beliefs in equilibrium but no off-path consistency) makes it appropriate when the history of play is observable but the game is not dynamic.
SPE: Required when the game has a non-trivial extensive form — when timing of moves matters, when commitment is at issue, when threats must be credible. The Coase theorem, hold-up problems, and bargaining theory all require SPE.
BNE: Required whenever agents have private information that affects their optimal actions. Auctions, signaling games, Bayesian games in oligopoly. The BNE requirement that strategies be optimal in expectation is weaker than dominant strategy optimality (as in VCG).
PBE: Required in dynamic games with incomplete information — signaling games (Spence), career concerns, reputational models, cheap talk (Crawford-Sobel). The on-path consistency requirement (Bayesian updating) is the minimal rationality constraint for these games.
REE: Required when prices serve as signals — asset pricing with asymmetric information, prediction markets, mechanism design with many agents. The REE requirement that agents condition on prices (as signals) means prices are endogenously informative, unlike BNE where prices are exogenous.




61.3.2 Equilibrium Selection Methods

Refinements






NoteDefinition 9.2.3 — Trembling-Hand Perfect Equilibrium (Selten 1975)



A Nash equilibrium \(\sigma^*\) is trembling-hand perfect if there exists a sequence of completely mixed strategies \(\{\sigma^k\} \to \sigma^*\) such that \(\sigma_i^*\) is a best response to every \(\sigma^k\) for all \(k\) sufficiently large. Trembling-hand perfection eliminates equilibria that rely on incredible threats — Nash equilibria that are not best responses to any slightly perturbed strategy of the opponent.








CautionRemark 9.2.2 — Relationship Between Refinements



The refinement hierarchy (strongest to weakest) is roughly: \[\text{Proper NE} \subset \text{THP NE} \subset \text{Sequential Equilibrium} \subset \text{PBE} \subset \text{NE}\] Each refinement eliminates equilibria by restricting off-path beliefs or requiring robustness to small perturbations. The Intuitive Criterion (see 7.4 Signaling) lies between PBE and sequential equilibrium in restrictiveness.




Global Games






ImportantTheorem 9.2.1 — Carlsson-Van Damme Global Games Equilibrium Selection



Consider a symmetric \(2\times 2\) coordination game with payoff matrix: \[\begin{pmatrix} a(\theta) & 0 \\ 0 & b \end{pmatrix}\] where \(\theta \in \mathbb{R}\) is the state of the world (e.g., fundamentals), \(a(\theta)\) is increasing in \(\theta\), and \(b > 0\) is constant. When \(a(\theta) > 0 > b\): action 1 is dominant (invest). When \(a(\theta) < 0 < b\): action 0 is dominant (don’t invest). In the intermediate region \(a(\theta) \in [0, b]\): both \((1,1)\) and \((0,0)\) are Nash equilibria (coordination game).
In the global game (Carlsson-Van Damme 1993), each player \(i\) observes a noisy signal \(x_i = \theta + \varepsilon_i\) where \(\varepsilon_i \sim U[-\varepsilon, \varepsilon]\). There exists a unique equilibrium threshold \(\theta^*\) such that: - Player \(i\) plays action 1 iff \(x_i > x^*\) where \(x^* = \theta^*\) - As \(\varepsilon \to 0\), \(\theta^* \to\) the risk-dominant equilibrium threshold: \(a(\theta^*) = b/2\)








TipProof





Step 1: Threshold strategies. In the noisy game, each player’s strategy is a threshold \(\hat{x}\): play 1 iff \(x_i > \hat{x}\). Given player \(j\) plays threshold \(\hat{x}\), player \(i\) with signal \(x_i\) believes \(\theta \approx x_i\) and estimates the probability that \(j\) plays 1 as: \[p_j = \Pr(x_j > \hat{x} | x_i = x_i) = \Pr(\theta + \varepsilon_j > \hat{x} | \theta \approx x_i)\] For small \(\varepsilon\), \(\theta \approx x_i\), so \(p_j \approx \Pr(\varepsilon_j > \hat{x} - x_i) = \frac{\hat{x} - x_i + \varepsilon}{2\varepsilon}\) (for \(x_i\) near \(\hat{x}\)).
Step 2: Indifference condition. At the threshold \(\hat{x}\), the player is indifferent: \[a(\hat{x}) \cdot p_j = b \cdot (1 - p_j)\] i.e., \(a(\hat{x}) \cdot \frac{1}{2} = b \cdot \frac{1}{2}\) (since at the threshold, \(p_j = 1/2\) by the uniform distribution).
Step 3: Unique threshold. As \(\varepsilon \to 0\), the indifference condition becomes \(a(\theta^*) = b\), so \(\theta^*\) is the unique solution to \(a(\theta^*) = b\). This is the risk-dominant threshold when \(a\) and \(b\) are symmetric.
More precisely, with heterogeneous payoffs (asymmetric game), the selected equilibrium threshold is \(a(\theta^*) = b\), which corresponds to the risk-dominant equilibrium. The noise uniquely selects a single equilibrium even though the noise vanishes in the limit.
Uniqueness: The threshold equilibrium is unique because higher-order uncertainty pins down beliefs: even though \(\varepsilon \to 0\), players remain uncertain about whether others know the fundamentals are such that action 1 dominates. This uncertainty about beliefs-about-beliefs-about-… (iterated reasoning) converges to the unique threshold strategy.









CautionRemark 9.2.3 — Applications of Global Games



Global games have been applied to: - Currency attacks (Morris-Shin 1998): speculators attack a peg when they believe enough others will attack; unique threshold on fundamentals - Bank runs (Rochet-Vives 2004): depositors run when they fear others will run; global games select a unique run threshold - Technology adoption (Chamley 1999): agents adopt a technology when they believe critical mass will be reached; global games predict adoption thresholds - Civil conflict (Chassang-Padró i Miquel 2010): fighting breaks out when individuals expect enough others to fight; threshold on state capacity or economic shocks




Evolutionary Stability






NoteDefinition 9.2.4 — Evolutionarily Stable Strategy (ESS)



A strategy \(\sigma^*\) is an ESS if for every mutant strategy \(\sigma \neq \sigma^*\), there exists \(\bar\varepsilon > 0\) such that for all \(\varepsilon \in (0, \bar\varepsilon)\): \[u(\sigma^*, \varepsilon \sigma + (1-\varepsilon)\sigma^*) > u(\sigma, \varepsilon \sigma + (1-\varepsilon)\sigma^*)\] i.e., \(\sigma^*\) earns higher payoff against a population that is mostly \(\sigma^*\) with a small fraction of mutants than the mutant strategy does. ESS is a refinement of NE; every ESS is a NE, but not vice versa.




Focal Points






CautionRemark 9.2.4 — Schelling’s Focal Points



Schelling (1960) observed that when multiple equilibria exist, real players often coordinate on focal points — equilibria that are salient by virtue of cultural convention, historical precedent, simplicity, or context. Examples: meeting at “Grand Central Station” when no meeting point was specified; agreeing on “50-50” as the default split; using round numbers as price points.
Focal points are outside-the-model selection devices: they reflect shared cultural knowledge that is not formalized in the payoff structure. For empirical work, this matters because: (1) the equilibrium played may depend on non-economic features of the environment (natural salience); (2) the relevant selection device may vary across contexts, populations, or time periods; (3) policy interventions that change the focal point (e.g., by making one equilibrium legally default) can shift behavior without changing payoffs.





61.3.3 Empirical Strategy Under Multiplicity






CautionRemark 9.2.5 — Three Empirical Approaches to Multiplicity



Approach 1: Test across equilibria using additional moments. If the model predicts that equilibrium A has observable feature \(X\) while equilibrium B does not, include \(X\) as a test. If data exhibit \(X\), infer equilibrium A was played. This requires the model to generate observable features that differ across equilibria — not just levels but potentially moments (variances, covariances, persistence).
Approach 2: Partial identification. The model predicts a set of outcomes consistent with rationality, without selecting among them. The econometrician estimates the identified set — the set of parameters consistent with the data and the model’s assumptions, but without imposing additional selection. Manski (1990), Tamer (2003). This approach is honest about what the model can and cannot say.
Approach 3: Model the selection equation. Treat equilibrium selection as a primitive of the empirical model. The selection mechanism — which equilibrium is played as a function of observables — is estimated as a separate equation. This requires additional variation (e.g., instruments that affect equilibrium selection without changing payoffs) and is formally equivalent to estimating a switching regression with endogenous regime.




61.3.4 Application: Diamond-Dybvig Bank Run Model






NoteExample 9.2.1 — Diamond-Dybvig (1983) Bank Run



Two depositors each deposit 1 unit. The bank invests in a long-term project yielding \(R > 1\) per unit at \(t=2\), or the bank liquidates early yielding \(r < 1\) per unit at \(t=1\).
Each depositor is independently a “patient” type (prefers \(t=2\) consumption) with probability \(1-\lambda\), or an “impatient” type (must consume at \(t=1\)) with probability \(\lambda\). Types are private information.
The bank offers a demand deposit contract: depositor can withdraw at \(t=1\) (get \(c_1 > 1 > r\), financed by bank liquidation) or at \(t=2\) (get \(c_2 > c_1\) from long-term returns).
Multiple equilibria: - Good equilibrium (“no run”): Only impatient types withdraw at \(t=1\); patient types wait; bank’s investments are not liquidated early; \(c_2 > 1\); optimal for patient types to wait given belief that others wait - Bad equilibrium (“run”): All depositors withdraw at \(t=1\), fearing the bank will fail; bank liquidates early; payoffs are \(\leq r < 1\); optimal to run given belief that others run
Global games selection: Introducing a signal about the bank’s return \(R\) pins down a unique threshold: the run occurs iff \(R\) is below a threshold \(R^*\) (Morris-Shin 1998). This generates an empirical prediction: bank runs should cluster in bad fundamental states, and the threshold should shift with liquidity coverage and deposit insurance.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
60  9.2 — Equilibrium Concepts and Selection

title: 60  9.2 — Equilibrium Concepts and Selection
text:
61.4 Economic Intuition
Multiplicity is not a failure of economic theory; it is a prediction. The real world contains genuine coordination problems: which equilibrium is played depends on history, expectations, and the coordination technology. The theoretical contribution is to characterize the set of equilibria and their properties, not to insist on unique predictions.
For empirical work, the key message is that multiplicity must be handled explicitly. An empirical specification that implicitly assumes a unique equilibrium (e.g., a reduced-form regression of conflict on economic fundamentals) is identifying some average or marginal effect across all the equilibria that are played, which is a valid reduced-form estimate but has no clean structural interpretation. For structural analysis, one of the three approaches to multiplicity must be adopted.
Global games are the most elegant theoretical resolution: a small amount of noise in private information uniquely pins down the equilibrium through iterated deletion of strictly dominated strategies. The selected equilibrium corresponds to the risk-dominant equilibrium as noise vanishes. This provides a theoretically grounded, testable selection mechanism that connects equilibrium play to fundamentals through a threshold rule.

title: 61  9.3 — Comparative Statics Methodology
text: 62 9.3 — Comparative Statics Methodology
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics

title: 61  9.3 — Comparative Statics Methodology
text:
62.1 Context
Comparative statics is the workhorse of applied theory: it predicts how equilibrium outcomes change when model parameters change. These predictions are the link between the formal model and empirical data. The classical approach uses the Implicit Function Theorem (IFT) to differentiate equilibrium conditions around a smooth interior optimum. But IFT requires differentiability, applies only locally, and fails at corners or kinks. The modern approach — monotone comparative statics using supermodularity and Topkis’s theorem — is more general: it delivers global comparative statics under weaker conditions, without differentiability, and even for discrete choice problems. This note develops both approaches with full proofs.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics

title: 61  9.3 — Comparative Statics Methodology
text:
62.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 9.3.1 — Comparative Statics



Let \(x^*(\alpha) = \arg\max_{x \in X} f(x, \alpha)\) be the equilibrium outcome as a function of parameter \(\alpha\). Comparative statics is the characterization of how \(x^*\) depends on \(\alpha\): specifically, the sign and magnitude of \(\frac{dx^*}{d\alpha}\) (or the direction of the change in \(x^*\) in the lattice sense when the choice set is not an ordered field).








NoteDefinition 9.3.2 — Supermodularity



A function \(f: X \times T \to \mathbb{R}\) (where \(X\) and \(T\) are partially ordered sets) is supermodular in \((x, t)\) if for all \(x' \geq x\) and \(t' \geq t\): \[f(x', t') + f(x, t) \geq f(x', t) + f(x, t')\] Equivalently, when \(X, T \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and \(f\) is \(C^2\): \(f\) is supermodular iff \(\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x \partial t} \geq 0\) (complementarity between \(x\) and \(t\)).








NoteDefinition 9.3.3 — Strong Set Order



For two sets \(A, B \subseteq X\) (a lattice), \(A \geq_s B\) (strong set order) if for all \(a \in A\) and \(b \in B\): \(a \vee b \in A\) and \(a \wedge b \in B\), where \(\vee\) and \(\wedge\) denote meet and join in the lattice. For single-valued choices, \(x^*(t') \geq x^*(t)\) means the optimal choice is weakly higher under \(t'\) than \(t\).
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics

title: 61  9.3 — Comparative Statics Methodology
text:
62.3 Main Results

62.3.1 The Implicit Function Theorem Method






ImportantTheorem 9.3.1 — Implicit Function Theorem (Comparative Statics Version)



Suppose \(x^*(\alpha)\) is characterized by the FOC \(F(x^*, \alpha) = 0\) where \(F: \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) is \(C^1\), and \(\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\big|_{(x^*, \alpha)} \neq 0\). Then \(x^*(\alpha)\) is a \(C^1\) function of \(\alpha\) in a neighborhood of \((x^*, \alpha)\), with: \[\frac{dx^*}{d\alpha} = -\frac{\partial F / \partial \alpha}{\partial F / \partial x}\bigg|_{(x^*, \alpha)}\]








TipProof





By the Implicit Function Theorem (Rudin, Theorem 9.28): if \(F(x^*, \alpha) = 0\) and \(\partial F/\partial x \neq 0\) at \((x^*, \alpha)\), then there exist open sets \(U \ni \alpha\) and \(V \ni x^*\) and a unique \(C^1\) function \(g: U \to V\) such that \(F(g(\alpha), \alpha) = 0\) for all \(\alpha \in U\). The derivative formula follows from total differentiation: \[\frac{\partial F}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{dx^*}{d\alpha} + \frac{\partial F}{\partial \alpha} = 0 \implies \frac{dx^*}{d\alpha} = -\frac{\partial F/\partial \alpha}{\partial F/\partial x}\]









NoteExample 9.3.1 — Cournot Output and Marginal Cost



In the Cournot duopoly with firms \(i \in \{1,2\}\), linear demand \(P = a - b(q_1 + q_2)\), and marginal costs \(c_1, c_2\), firm 1’s FOC is: \[F(q_1, q_2, c_1) = a - b q_2 - 2b q_1 - c_1 = 0\] Solving the system of FOCs gives: \[q_1^* = \frac{a - 2c_1 + c_2}{3b}\] Direct IFT application: \(\frac{\partial q_1^*}{\partial c_1} = -2/(3b) < 0\) (own cost increase reduces output) and \(\frac{\partial q_1^*}{\partial c_2} = 1/(3b) > 0\) (rival’s cost increase raises own output).








NoteExample 9.3.2 — Walrasian Equilibrium Price and Endowment



Consider a two-good, two-consumer exchange economy with agent \(i = 1,2\) having utility \(u_i(x_i, y_i) = \alpha_i \ln x_i + (1-\alpha_i) \ln y_i\). At Walrasian equilibrium, market clearing in good \(x\) gives: \[\sum_i \frac{\alpha_i I_i}{p} = \omega_x\] where \(I_i = p \omega_{xi} + \omega_{yi}\) is agent \(i\)’s income, \(\omega_x = \sum_i \omega_{xi}\).
Total differentiating with respect to \(\omega_{x1}\) (endowment of good \(x\) for agent 1): \[\frac{dp}{d\omega_{x1}} = -\frac{\partial F/\partial \omega_{x1}}{\partial F/\partial p}\] where \(F\) is the excess demand for \(x\). Computing: \(\frac{\partial F}{\partial \omega_{x1}} = -\alpha_1 < 0\); \(\frac{\partial F}{\partial p} = -\sum_i \alpha_i \omega_{yi}/p^2 < 0\). Therefore \(\frac{dp}{d\omega_{x1}} = \frac{\alpha_1}{\sum_i \alpha_i \omega_{yi}/p^2}\). The sign depends on the Walras Law structure; generically, an increase in supply of \(x\) lowers its equilibrium price.




62.3.2 Limitations of the IFT






CautionRemark 9.3.1 — When IFT Fails



The IFT requires: (1) differentiability of \(F\); (2) non-zero Jacobian at equilibrium; (3) interior solution (so FOC is valid). It fails when: - Corners/kinks: Optimal choice is at a boundary; FOC does not hold as equality. Example: labor supply with binding minimum wage. - Discrete choice: \(x \in \{0,1\}\); no FOC exists. Example: investment decision under uncertainty. - Multiple equilibria: Jacobian is singular; IFT is not applicable. Example: models with strategic complementarities at the bifurcation point. - Non-differentiable payoffs: Payoffs with kinks (e.g., max functions, absolute value). Example: loss-averse agents.
In all these cases, monotone comparative statics (Milgrom-Shannon, Topkis) provides an alternative.




62.3.3 Topkis’s Theorem






ImportantTheorem 9.3.2 — Topkis’s Theorem (Monotone Comparative Statics)



Let \(X\) be a compact sublattice of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) and let \(f: X \times T \to \mathbb{R}\) where \(T\) is a partially ordered set. If \(f\) is supermodular in \((x, t)\) (i.e., \(f\) has increasing differences in \((x, t)\): \(f(x', t') - f(x', t) \geq f(x, t') - f(x, t)\) for \(x' \geq x\), \(t' \geq t\)), then: 1. \(x^*(t) = \arg\max_{x \in X} f(x, t)\) is non-decreasing in \(t\) in the strong set order: if \(t' \geq t\), then for every \(x' \in x^*(t')\) and \(x \in x^*(t)\), \(x' \geq x\) (or \(x^*(t')\) is higher than \(x^*(t)\) in the strong set order) 2. The conclusion holds without requiring differentiability or compactness of \(T\)








TipProof





We prove the single-valued case (\(x^*\) is unique); the set-valued case follows similarly.
Suppose, for contradiction, that \(t' \geq t\) but \(x^*(t') < x^*(t)\). Denote \(x_H = x^*(t)\) and \(x_L = x^*(t')\) with \(x_H > x_L\).
Since \(x_H = x^*(t)\): \(f(x_H, t) \geq f(x_L, t)\). Since \(x_L = x^*(t')\): \(f(x_L, t') \geq f(x_H, t')\).
Summing these two inequalities: \[f(x_H, t) + f(x_L, t') \geq f(x_L, t) + f(x_H, t')\] Rearranging: \[f(x_H, t') - f(x_H, t) \leq f(x_L, t') - f(x_L, t)\]
But this contradicts supermodularity: for \(x_H > x_L\) and \(t' > t\), supermodularity (increasing differences) requires: \[f(x_H, t') - f(x_H, t) \geq f(x_L, t') - f(x_L, t)\]
Contradiction. Therefore \(x^*(t') \geq x^*(t)\) whenever \(t' \geq t\).









NoteExample 9.3.3 — Supermodularity in a Coordination Game



In a 2-player coordination game, player \(i\) chooses action \(a_i \in [0,1]\); payoff is \(f(a_i, a_j, \theta) = a_i(\theta + \rho a_j) - a_i^2/2\) where \(\rho > 0\) captures strategic complementarity and \(\theta\) is a fundamental.
\(f\) is supermodular in \((a_i, \theta)\): \(\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial a_i \partial \theta} = 1 > 0\). \(f\) is supermodular in \((a_i, a_j)\): \(\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial a_i \partial a_j} = \rho > 0\).
By Topkis: \(a_i^*(\theta, a_j)\) is increasing in both \(\theta\) (own fundamentals) and \(a_j\) (strategic complementarity). In symmetric Nash equilibrium: \[a^*(\theta) = \frac{\theta}{1 - \rho}\] (from \(\theta + \rho a^* = a^*\), so \(a^* = \theta/(1-\rho)\) for \(\rho < 1\)).
Monotone comparative statics: \(da^*/d\theta = 1/(1-\rho) > 0\) (stronger fundamentals \(\to\) higher equilibrium action). This connects to the global games result: the threshold \(\theta^*\) below which the low equilibrium is selected is decreasing in \(\rho\) (stronger complementarity makes coordination easier).




62.3.4 Milgrom-Shannon Single Crossing Property






NoteDefinition 9.3.4 — Single Crossing Property (Milgrom-Shannon)



A function \(f: X \times T \to \mathbb{R}\) satisfies the single crossing property (SCP) in \((x; t)\) if for all \(x' > x\) and \(t' > t\): \[f(x', t) \geq f(x, t) \implies f(x', t') \geq f(x, t')\] and \[f(x', t) > f(x, t) \implies f(x', t') > f(x, t')\] SCP is weaker than supermodularity (it does not require \(f\) to be differentiable or the differences to be increasing, only that the sign of differences is preserved).








ImportantTheorem 9.3.3 — Milgrom-Shannon Monotone Comparative Statics



If \(f(x, t)\) satisfies SCP in \((x; t)\) and \(x^*(t) = \arg\max_{x \in X(t)} f(x, t)\) where \(X(t)\) is a sublattice that is non-decreasing in \(t\) (in the strong set order), then \(x^*(t)\) is non-decreasing in \(t\).








TipProof





The proof follows the same contradiction argument as Topkis’s Theorem but replaces the supermodularity condition with SCP. Suppose \(t' > t\) and \(x^*(t') < x^*(t)\). Then \(f(x^*(t), t) \geq f(x^*(t'), t)\) (optimality at \(t\)) implies \(f(x^*(t), t') \geq f(x^*(t'), t')\) (by SCP applied with \(x' = x^*(t) > x^*(t')\)). But this contradicts \(x^*(t') = \arg\max f(\cdot, t')\).
SCP is strictly weaker than supermodularity: it allows non-monotone individual payoffs and non-differentiable functions while still delivering monotone comparative statics.





62.3.5 The Envelope Theorem as Comparative Statics






ImportantTheorem 9.3.4 — Envelope Theorem



Let \(V(\alpha) = \max_{x \in X} f(x, \alpha)\) be the value function, with \(x^*(\alpha)\) the unique maximizer. If \(f\) is \(C^1\) in \(\alpha\) and \(x^*(\alpha)\) is in the interior of \(X\), then \(V\) is differentiable in \(\alpha\) with: \[V'(\alpha) = \frac{\partial f(x^*(\alpha), \alpha)}{\partial \alpha}\] When there is a binding constraint \(g(x, \alpha) = 0\) with Lagrange multiplier \(\lambda^*\), the Lagrangian envelope theorem gives: \[V'(\alpha) = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \alpha}\bigg|_{x^*(\alpha), \lambda^*(\alpha)} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \alpha} + \lambda^* \frac{\partial g}{\partial \alpha}\]








TipProof





Let \(x^* = x^*(\alpha)\). Then \(V(\alpha') = f(x^*(\alpha'), \alpha')\) and \(V(\alpha) = f(x^*(\alpha), \alpha) \geq f(x^*(\alpha'), \alpha)\). Therefore: \[V(\alpha') - V(\alpha) \leq f(x^*(\alpha'), \alpha') - f(x^*(\alpha'), \alpha)\] Similarly: \(V(\alpha) - V(\alpha') \leq f(x^*(\alpha), \alpha) - f(x^*(\alpha), \alpha')\). Therefore: \[f(x^*(\alpha'), \alpha') - f(x^*(\alpha'), \alpha) \geq V(\alpha') - V(\alpha) \geq f(x^*(\alpha), \alpha') - f(x^*(\alpha), \alpha)\] Dividing by \((\alpha' - \alpha)\) and taking \(\alpha' \to \alpha\): both bounds converge to \(\frac{\partial f(x^*(\alpha), \alpha)}{\partial \alpha}\) (since \(x^*(\alpha') \to x^*(\alpha)\) by continuity). Therefore \(V'(\alpha) = \frac{\partial f(x^*(\alpha), \alpha)}{\partial \alpha}\).









CautionRemark 9.3.2 — Interpreting the Lagrange Multiplier



The envelope theorem gives the Lagrange multiplier \(\lambda^*\) a precise economic interpretation: \(\lambda^* = \frac{\partial V}{\partial b}\) where \(b\) is the right-hand side of the binding constraint. It is the shadow price of the constraint — the marginal value of relaxing the constraint by one unit. In the consumer problem, \(\lambda^*\) is the marginal utility of income. In the firm problem, \(\lambda^*\) is the shadow price of the budget constraint. In the principal-agent model, \(\lambda^*\) is the marginal value of relaxing the participation constraint — i.e., the marginal cost of the agent’s reservation utility.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics

title: 61  9.3 — Comparative Statics Methodology
text:
62.4 Economic Intuition
Comparative statics is the mode of inquiry that connects a formal model to empirical data. The IFT gives the derivative — the local linear approximation — of the equilibrium outcome function, which is exactly what a regression coefficient estimates. The sign and magnitude of \(dx^*/d\alpha\) from IFT corresponds directly to the coefficient estimate from a regression of the outcome on the parameter.
Topkis’s theorem is the non-parametric version: it tells us the direction of the effect (non-decreasing) without pinning down the magnitude, and it does so under much weaker conditions. For empirical work, this matters because: (1) the sign of a comparative static is often sufficient to test a model; (2) Topkis applies when the payoff structure is known only qualitatively (supermodular) rather than parametrically; (3) it handles discrete choice and non-differentiable objectives.
The envelope theorem connects the value function to welfare analysis: \(\lambda^*\) is the shadow price of a constraint, and \(V'(\alpha)\) is the welfare effect of changing a parameter. This is the foundation for all welfare calculations in structural economics.

title: 62  9.4 — Deriving Testable Predictions
text: 63 9.4 — Deriving Testable Predictions
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics

title: 62  9.4 — Deriving Testable Predictions
text:
63.1 Context
A theoretical model has value beyond pure intellectual interest only if it generates predictions that can, in principle, be confronted with data. A prediction is empirically useful if it restricts the joint distribution of observables in a way that would be violated if the model were wrong. This note develops the methodology of moving from a solved model to empirically testable restrictions: sign restrictions on comparative statics, cross-equation restrictions, moment conditions for GMM estimation, shape restrictions on demand functions, and the sharp testable restrictions generated by classical consumer and producer theory.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics

title: 62  9.4 — Deriving Testable Predictions
text:
63.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 9.4.1 — Testable Prediction



A testable prediction of model \(\mathcal{M}\) is a restriction on the joint distribution of observable variables \((y_1, \ldots, y_k)\) — i.e., a set \(\mathcal{R}\) such that: (i) if \(\mathcal{M}\) is true, the data distribution satisfies \(\mathcal{R}\); (ii) if \(\mathcal{M}\) is false (or the relevant assumption of \(\mathcal{M}\) fails), the data distribution may violate \(\mathcal{R}\).
A model with no testable predictions is unfalsifiable: no data can distinguish it from its complement. An unfalsifiable model is not science but tautology.








NoteDefinition 9.4.2 — Moment Condition



A moment condition is a population restriction of the form \(\mathbb{E}[m(y, x, \theta_0)] = 0\) where \(y\) are outcome variables, \(x\) are regressors/instruments, \(\theta_0\) are structural parameters, and \(m(\cdot)\) is a known function derived from the model. The GMM estimator solves: \[\hat\theta = \arg\min_\theta \left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i m(y_i, x_i, \theta)\right)^T W \left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i m(y_i, x_i, \theta)\right)\] for a weighting matrix \(W\).
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics

title: 62  9.4 — Deriving Testable Predictions
text:
63.3 Main Results

63.3.1 Five Types of Empirical Implications






CautionRemark 9.4.1 — Taxonomy of Testable Predictions



Type 1: Sign restrictions on comparative statics. The simplest form. If the model predicts \(\frac{\partial x^*}{\partial \alpha} > 0\), the empirical test is: estimate \(\frac{\partial E[x|z]}{\partial z}\) for an instrument \(z\) that shifts \(\alpha\), and test whether the coefficient is positive. This is essentially a test of the sign of a reduced-form slope.
Example: The contest model (see APP Conflict and Resource Models) predicts that higher resource value \(R\) increases conflict effort. Testable via: does a positive commodity price shock increase conflict incidence? If \(\frac{\partial \text{conflict}}{\partial R} > 0\), and commodity prices instrument for \(R\), a positive IV coefficient confirms the sign restriction.
Type 2: Cross-equation restrictions. Structural models link parameters across multiple equations. Testing these links (overidentifying restrictions) is a test of the model’s internal consistency.
Example: In the permanent income hypothesis, the Euler equation \(\Delta c_{t+1} = r + \varepsilon_{t+1}\) (where \(\varepsilon\) is forecast error) links consumption growth across time periods and requires that consumption growth is unpredictable given lagged information. Cross-equation restriction: the coefficient on lagged income in a regression of consumption growth should be zero.
Type 3: Moment conditions. Structural models often imply that a function of observables and structural parameters has zero expectation at the true parameter value. These are GMM moment conditions.
Type 4: Shape restrictions. Monotonicity, concavity, homogeneity of degree zero — these are non-parametric restrictions on functional forms derived from optimization. Slutsky symmetry is the canonical example.
Type 5: Exclusion restrictions. Variable \(z\) affects outcome \(y\) only through mechanism \(x\) modeled explicitly. The exclusion restriction is the theoretical claim that \(z\) does not enter the structural equation for \(y\) directly — a claim justified by the model’s structure.




63.3.2 FOC-Based Moment Conditions






ImportantProposition 9.4.1 — First-Order Conditions as Moment Conditions



Suppose the agent solves \(\max_{x} u(x, \theta, p)\) subject to \(g(x) \leq 0\) where \(p\) is observed by the econometrician and \(\theta\) is a structural parameter to be estimated. At an interior optimum, the FOC: \[\frac{\partial u(x^*, \theta, p)}{\partial x} = 0\] evaluated at observed \((x^*, p)\) gives the moment condition: \[m(x^*, p, \theta) \equiv \frac{\partial u(x^*, \theta, p)}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x = x^*} = 0\] Under rational expectations, \(\mathbb{E}[m(x^*, p, \theta_0) | p] = 0\) at the true parameter \(\theta_0\).








NoteExample 9.4.1 — Consumer Euler Equation as Moment Condition



Consider the intertemporal consumption problem: \(\max \sum_{t=0}^T \beta^t u(c_t)\) subject to the budget constraint. The Euler equation at an interior solution is: \[u'(c_t) = \beta (1+r_t) u'(c_{t+1})\] Rearranging and taking conditional expectations: \[\mathbb{E}\left[\beta(1+r_t) \frac{u'(c_{t+1})}{u'(c_t)} - 1 \bigg| \mathcal{F}_t\right] = 0\] For CRRA utility \(u(c) = c^{1-\rho}/(1-\rho)\): \[\mathbb{E}\left[\beta(1+r_t) \left(\frac{c_{t+1}}{c_t}\right)^{-\rho} - 1 \bigg| \mathcal{F}_t\right] = 0\] This is a moment condition for \((\beta, \rho)\): for any instrument \(z_t \in \mathcal{F}_t\), \(\mathbb{E}[m_t \cdot z_t] = 0\) where \(m_t = \beta(1+r_t)(c_{t+1}/c_t)^{-\rho} - 1\). GMM uses a vector of such moments to estimate \((\beta, \rho)\).








NoteExample 9.4.2 — Household Agricultural Production Model



A household chooses fertilizer input \(x\) to maximize \(\pi = p \cdot f(x, A, \theta) - w x\) where \(p\) is the output price, \(A\) is land area, \(\theta\) is productivity (potentially unobserved), and \(w\) is the input price. The FOC (at an interior optimum): \[p \cdot f_x(x^*, A, \theta) = w\] Under a Cobb-Douglas production function \(f(x, A, \theta) = \theta A^\alpha x^\gamma\): \[p \cdot \theta \gamma A^\alpha x^{*\gamma-1} = w\] Taking logarithms: \[\ln x^* = \frac{1}{1-\gamma}\left[\ln(p\theta\gamma) + \alpha \ln A - \ln w\right]\] This can be written as a regression equation with structural parameters \((\gamma, \alpha)\) appearing as coefficients on \((\ln p + \ln\theta, \ln A, \ln w)\). The moment condition is: \[\mathbb{E}[\ln x^* - \frac{1}{1-\gamma}(\ln p\theta\gamma + \alpha \ln A - \ln w)] = 0\] The challenge: \(\theta\) is unobserved. An exclusion restriction (input prices \(w\) determined by markets external to the household, i.e., \(w \perp \theta\)) provides the identifying variation. See 9.6 Identification in Structural Models.




63.3.3 Overidentification Tests






NoteDefinition 9.4.3 — Overidentification and the Sargan-Hansen Test



An overidentified model has more moment conditions than parameters: \(\dim(m) > \dim(\theta)\). The \(J\)-test (Sargan-Hansen) tests the joint validity of the moment conditions: \[J = n \cdot \hat{m}(\hat\theta)^T \hat{W} \hat{m}(\hat\theta) \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2(\dim(m) - \dim(\theta))\] under the null that all moment conditions hold. Rejection implies either: (a) the model’s structural assumptions are wrong; or (b) the instruments are invalid (correlated with structural errors). The test cannot distinguish between these two interpretations without additional information.








CautionRemark 9.4.2 — Overidentification Test as Model Test



When additional moment conditions come from the model’s structural restrictions (not from additional instruments), the \(J\)-test is a direct test of the model. Failure of the test indicates the model’s structural assumptions are rejected by the data. This is the empirical counterpart of the theoretical question: “Is the model wrong?” Examples: - Testing the Euler equation with different instruments: if the Euler equation is correctly specified, all instruments should be valid - Testing symmetry of demand cross-effects: the Slutsky matrix should be symmetric if consumer theory holds - Testing orthogonality of supply and demand errors: if both equations are correctly specified, the error from one should not predict the residual from the other




63.3.4 Sharp Testable Restrictions from Classical Theory






ImportantProposition 9.4.2 — Slutsky Symmetry as Testable Restriction



From the Slutsky equation, the compensated cross-price effects must be symmetric: \[\frac{\partial x_i^*(p, u)}{\partial p_j} = \frac{\partial x_j^*(p, u)}{\partial p_i}\] Equivalently, the Slutsky matrix \(S(p, u)\) with \(S_{ij} = \partial x_i^h / \partial p_j\) is symmetric and negative semi-definite (NSD). These are testable restrictions on the demand system: run a demand system regression; test whether the cross-price coefficients satisfy the symmetry restriction; test whether the substitution matrix is NSD.








ImportantProposition 9.4.3 — GARP as Testable Restriction (Revealed Preference)



The Generalized Axiom of Revealed Preference (GARP) states: if consumption bundle \(x\) is revealed preferred to \(y\) (directly or indirectly, through a chain of budget-feasible choices), then \(y\) is not strictly preferred to \(x\). Formally: if \(x^t\) is chosen at prices \(p^t\) and \(p^t \cdot x^s \leq p^t \cdot x^t\) (bundle \(s\) is affordable when \(t\) is chosen), then \(x^t\) is revealed preferred to \(x^s\); GARP requires \(p^s \cdot x^t \leq p^s \cdot x^s\) cannot hold (otherwise \(x^t\) should have been chosen at period \(s\), but \(x^s\) is also affordable — contradiction). GARP is testable from panel data on consumption and prices using the Afriat efficiency index.








ImportantProposition 9.4.4 — Factor Demand Symmetry from Producer Theory



For a competitive firm with profit function \(\pi(p, w_1, w_2)\) (output price \(p\), input prices \(w_1, w_2\)): \[\frac{\partial y^*}{\partial p} = \frac{\partial^2 \pi}{\partial p^2} \geq 0 \quad \text{(supply is non-decreasing in price)}\] \[\frac{\partial x_i^*}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial^2 \pi}{\partial w_i \partial w_j} = \frac{\partial^2 \pi}{\partial w_j \partial w_i} = \frac{\partial x_j^*}{\partial w_i} \quad \text{(symmetry)}\] \[\frac{\partial x_i^*}{\partial w_i} = \frac{\partial^2 \pi}{\partial w_i^2} \leq 0 \quad \text{(own-price demand non-increasing)}\] All three are testable restrictions on estimated factor demand equations.




63.3.5 The Identification-Prediction Duality






CautionRemark 9.4.3 — Identifying Assumptions Embed Theoretical Claims



A key insight from the model-building perspective: every identifying assumption in an empirical specification embeds a theoretical claim. For example:

The exclusion restriction “commodity prices affect household income but not household utility directly” is equivalent to the theoretical claim that households are price-takers in output markets (no market power or subsistence production).
The instrument validity condition “conflict shocks are uncorrelated with confounders of the outcome equation” is equivalent to the theoretical claim that conflict is driven by opportunity costs, not by the outcome variable itself.

Making identifying assumptions explicit is therefore a form of model-building: it forces the researcher to state what they are assuming about the economic structure. The best empirical papers present the identification argument as a theoretical claim that can itself be tested (through overidentification tests, pre-trend tests, or heterogeneity predictions).
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics

title: 62  9.4 — Deriving Testable Predictions
text:
63.4 Economic Intuition
Testable predictions are the output of the model-building pipeline: they are what the model is for, empirically speaking. The process of deriving testable predictions forces clarity about what the model implies and what it does not. Many models generate a multitude of comparative statics, but not all comparative statics are testable with available data. The art is to identify which predictions are: (a) implied by the core mechanism of interest (not just a feature of a specific functional form); (b) falsifiable with available or feasible-to-collect data; (c) distinct from what competing models predict.
The third criterion is crucial for discriminating between theories. If the model predicts \(\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} > 0\) and so does every competing model, the test cannot discriminate. Powerful tests come from predictions that hold uniquely in the proposed model — often from the interaction effects, the heterogeneity of treatment effects, or the cross-equation restrictions that rival models do not share.

title: 63  9.5 — Structural vs. Reduced Form
