crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
63  9.5 — Structural vs. Reduced Form

title: 63  9.5 — Structural vs. Reduced Form
text:
64.1 Context
The debate between structural and reduced-form empirical approaches is one of the central methodological debates in applied economics. Structural models estimate the underlying parameters of the economic model — preferences, technology, costs — and use them for welfare analysis and counterfactual prediction. Reduced-form approaches estimate causal effects of specific interventions without committing to a complete economic model, yielding cleaner identification but more limited scope. This note presents both approaches formally, analyzes when each is appropriate, and discusses hybrid strategies that combine the advantages of both.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
63  9.5 — Structural vs. Reduced Form

title: 63  9.5 — Structural vs. Reduced Form
text:
64.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 9.5.1 — Reduced Form



A reduced-form specification is a regression of an outcome variable \(y\) on a treatment variable \(d\) and controls \(x\), exploiting exogenous variation in \(d\) (from a natural experiment, randomization, or instrumental variable): \[y_i = \alpha + \tau d_i + \beta x_i + \varepsilon_i\] The coefficient \(\tau\) estimates a causal effect of \(d\) on \(y\) — specifically, the Local Average Treatment Effect (LATE) (Imbens-Angrist 1994) if IV is used, or the Average Treatment Effect (ATE) if randomization is perfect. The reduced form is “structural” only in the sense that it estimates a causal parameter; it does not model the mechanism through which \(d\) affects \(y\).








NoteDefinition 9.5.2 — Structural Form



A structural specification estimates the parameters of an economic model — preferences, technology, costs, information — by expressing the model’s optimality conditions (FOCs, equilibrium conditions) as estimating equations. The parameter vector \(\theta = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_k)\) indexes the model, and identification is achieved by combining economic assumptions with data variation.
Example: estimating the CES parameter \(\sigma\) from consumer demand data requires specifying the utility function, deriving demand, and estimating \(\sigma\) from the relationship between relative quantities and relative prices. The structural parameter \(\sigma\) is economically interpretable (elasticity of substitution) and enables welfare calculations.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
63  9.5 — Structural vs. Reduced Form

title: 63  9.5 — Structural vs. Reduced Form
text:
64.3 Main Results

64.3.1 The Lucas Critique






ImportantTheorem 9.5.1 — Lucas Critique (Lucas 1976)



Let the economy be described by a model with decision rules \(y_t = f(x_t, \theta, \pi)\) where \(x_t\) are state variables, \(\theta\) are structural (deep) parameters (preferences, technology), and \(\pi\) are the parameters of the policy rule. If the reduced-form relationship between \(y_t\) and \(x_t\) is estimated as: \[y_t = \hat{A} x_t + \varepsilon_t\] then \(\hat{A}\) depends on \(\pi\) (the current policy rule) through the agents’ optimal behavior. When the policy changes (\(\pi \to \pi'\)), the reduced-form coefficients change (\(\hat{A} \to \hat{A}'\)) because agents adjust their decision rules. Therefore reduced-form estimates cannot be used to evaluate policy counterfactuals.








TipProof





Formal statement: In a rational expectations model, the agent’s optimal decision rule \(y_t = f(x_t, \theta, \pi)\) is derived by solving: \[y_t = \arg\max_y \mathbb{E}\left[\sum_{s=0}^\infty \beta^s u(y_{t+s}, x_{t+s}) \big| x_t, \pi\right]\] The solution depends on the agent’s beliefs about future states \(x_{t+s}\), which are derived from the policy rule \(\pi\) (how \(x\) evolves). When \(\pi\) changes, the optimal \(y_t\) changes even if \(\theta\) (preferences, technology) is unchanged.
Specifically: suppose the reduced-form is \(y_t = A(\pi) x_t + \varepsilon_t\). A policy change from \(\pi\) to \(\pi'\) gives \(y_t = A(\pi') x_t + \varepsilon_t\), with \(A(\pi') \neq A(\pi)\) in general. Predicting the effect of the policy change using \(A(\pi)\) is therefore incorrect.
The exception: The critique does not apply to deep structural parameters \(\theta\). If we estimate \(\theta\) directly (not through the reduced form \(A = A(\theta, \pi)\)), then \(\theta\) is invariant to policy changes, and counterfactual analysis using \(\theta\) is valid.









NoteExample 9.5.1 — Lucas Critique in Macro Policy



The Phillips curve \(\pi_t = \alpha - \beta u_t\) (inflation as a linear function of unemployment) appeared stable in the 1960s. Policymakers used it to predict the cost of reducing unemployment via expansionary policy. After sustained expansionary policy in the 1970s, the curve shifted: inflation expectations adjusted, and the relationship between inflation and unemployment changed. The reduced-form parameter \(\beta\) depended on the policy regime; estimating it in one regime and using it to predict effects in another was invalid.
The structural analog: if we estimate the structural parameters of price-setting and wage-setting behavior (which depend on expectations formation mechanisms), we can predict how the curve shifts under different policy regimes. This is the Lucas argument for structural estimation.




64.3.2 When Does Structural Matter?






CautionRemark 9.5.1 — Five Cases Where Structural Estimation is Necessary



Case 1: Policy counterfactuals outside the observed variation. If the policy question involves a price or quantity outside the historical range, reduced-form estimates cannot extrapolate. The structural model extrapolates using the parameterized utility/technology function.
Case 2: Welfare analysis. Computing consumer surplus, equivalent variation, or deadweight loss requires the utility function or demand system, not just a causal coefficient. The compensating variation from a price increase is \(CV = \int_{p_0}^{p_1} x^h(p, u_0) dp\) — the Hicksian demand function, which requires estimating preferences.
Case 3: Out-of-sample prediction. Predicting behavior in a new market, a new population, or a new institutional setting requires structural parameters that are invariant to the setting. Reduced-form coefficients are setting-specific.
Case 4: Testing economic theory. Structural estimation imposes the model’s restrictions as constraints on the estimating equations. Rejection of these restrictions is a test of the theory, not just a description of the data.
Case 5: Multiple endogenous variables. When multiple outcomes are jointly determined by the model (e.g., price and quantity in an oligopoly), a reduced-form regression of one on another conflates supply and demand. Structural identification requires modeling both simultaneously.




64.3.3 When Does Reduced Form Suffice?






CautionRemark 9.5.2 — When Reduced Form is Appropriate



When the LATE is the parameter of interest. If the policy question is “what is the effect of this specific intervention on this specific population in this specific context?”, the LATE from an IV estimate is exactly the right answer — no structural model is needed or appropriate.
When the structural model is uncertain. If there are multiple plausible structural models with different implications, and there is no a priori reason to prefer one, the reduced form provides a model-agnostic causal estimate. The reduced form is interpretable without committing to a particular model of behavior.
When the treatment is well-defined and the variation is credible. The value of randomized experiments and natural experiments is that they deliver clean causal estimates without the maintaining assumptions required for structural identification. If the IV assumptions are satisfied and the LATE is the relevant parameter, reduced form is efficient.
When welfare analysis is not needed. If the question is purely positive (does X cause Y?) rather than normative (what is the optimal policy? what is the welfare gain?), reduced form is sufficient.




64.3.4 The Hybrid Approach: Structural + IV






CautionRemark 9.5.3 — Hybrid Strategy



The hybrid approach derives an equation from the structural model that can be estimated with instrumental variables. The structural model provides the economic interpretation of the coefficient; the IV provides credible identification.
Supply-demand example: The structural supply equation is \(Q_s = \alpha_s + \beta_s P + \gamma_s W + \varepsilon_s\) where \(W\) is input price. The structural demand equation is \(Q_d = \alpha_d + \beta_d P + \gamma_d Y + \varepsilon_d\) where \(Y\) is income. The IV strategy: use \(W\) (input cost) as an instrument for \(P\) in the demand equation (excluded from demand by the structural model). The estimated \(\beta_d\) is the structural demand elasticity.
Household example: The structural FOC for fertilizer input is \(pf_x(x, A) = w\). With Cobb-Douglas: \(\gamma p A^\alpha x^{\gamma-1} = w\). This rearranges to \(x = (w/\gamma p A^\alpha)^{1/(\gamma-1)}\). Taking logs: \(\ln x = \frac{1}{\gamma-1}[\ln w - \ln(\gamma p) - \alpha \ln A]\). Estimating this by IV (using input price variation that is exogenous to household-level productivity) identifies \(\gamma\) (the output elasticity of fertilizer).




64.3.5 Identification of Structural Parameters






CautionRemark 9.5.4 — Examples from Consumer, Producer, and Game Theory



Consumer theory: Demand system estimation (AIDS, QUAIDS, CES demand). The structural parameters are preference parameters (elasticities, scale parameters). Identified from price variation (using input cost instruments for prices) subject to the Slutsky restrictions (symmetry, NSD) as over-identifying restrictions.
Producer theory: Production function estimation (Olley-Pakes 1996, Ackerberg-Caves-Frazer 2015). The structural parameter is the technology (output elasticities). The challenge: unobserved productivity \(\omega_{it}\) is correlated with input choices (simultaneity bias). Instrumental variable: capital (predetermined) or material inputs (proxy for productivity). The production function moment condition is \(\mathbb{E}[\omega_{it} | k_{it}, l_{it-1}] = 0\).
Game theory — Nash conduct in oligopoly: The Bertrand/Cournot decision rule gives a structural equation for pricing: \(p = c(w, z) + \frac{p}{\epsilon} \cdot \theta\) where \(\theta\) is the conduct parameter (\(\theta = 0\): competitive; \(\theta = 1\): monopoly) and \(\epsilon\) is the demand elasticity. Identifying \(\theta\) requires: (i) estimating demand elasticity from demand equation; (ii) using demand shifters as instruments for price in the pricing equation; (iii) using cost shifters as instruments in the demand equation. This is the “New Empirical IO” approach.




64.3.6 The Credibility Revolution Critique






CautionRemark 9.5.5 — Angrist-Pischke Critique of Structural Methods



Angrist and Pischke (2010) argue that structural models often bury untestable assumptions: functional form (Cobb-Douglas production vs. CES), distribution of unobservables (normality), and equilibrium concept (Nash vs. competitive). Because these assumptions are not directly testable, the structural estimates may be driven by the assumptions rather than the data.
The appropriate response is not to abandon structural methods but to make assumptions explicit and test them wherever possible: 1. Test functional form assumptions (e.g., test Cobb-Douglas against CES using non-nested hypothesis tests) 2. Test distributional assumptions (e.g., test normality of unobservables using specification tests) 3. Test overidentifying restrictions from the model (Sargan-Hansen test) 4. Compare structural estimates to IV estimates that exploit the same variation: if they differ, one of them is wrong
The value of structural identification over ad hoc IV is that the model provides an economic justification for the identifying assumptions — not just the statistical power to detect effects.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
63  9.5 — Structural vs. Reduced Form

title: 63  9.5 — Structural vs. Reduced Form
text:
64.4 Economic Intuition
The structural vs. reduced-form debate is fundamentally about the scope of the empirical question being asked. Reduced-form methods answer narrow but credibly identified questions: “What happened to outcome \(Y\) when policy \(X\) changed in this context?” Structural methods answer broader questions: “What would happen to outcome \(Y\) if policy \(X\) changed in a different way, in a different context, for a different population?” The cost of breadth is the need for maintained assumptions; the cost of credibility is the restriction to local, context-specific claims.
For development economics and conflict economics, the right approach often depends on the policy question. Estimating the effect of a commodity price shock on conflict incidence is a reduced-form question (answered by IV). Predicting the welfare effects of a permanent price change, or the conflict response to a novel institutional arrangement, requires a structural model.

crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
64  9.6 — Identification in Structural Models

title: 64  9.6 — Identification in Structural Models
text:
65.1 Context
Identification is the logical precondition for estimation: before asking how to estimate a parameter, one must ask whether the parameter is determined by the data distribution. A structural model is identified if distinct parameter values imply distinct distributions of observable outcomes — i.e., the mapping from parameters to observables is injective. This note develops a rigorous treatment of identification types, shows how economic models generate model-justified exclusion restrictions (the most credible source of identification), and discusses identification in random coefficient models and simultaneous equations systems.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
64  9.6 — Identification in Structural Models

title: 64  9.6 — Identification in Structural Models
text:
65.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 9.6.1 — Identification



Let \(\mathcal{M}(\theta)\) be a model indexed by parameter \(\theta \in \Theta\), generating distribution \(P_\theta\) over observable data \((Y, X)\). The parameter \(\theta\) is identified if: \[P_{\theta} = P_{\theta'} \implies \theta = \theta'\] i.e., the mapping \(\theta \mapsto P_\theta\) is injective. If \(\theta\) is not identified, the data cannot distinguish \(\theta\) from \(\theta'\) even with infinite observations.
The identified set \(\mathcal{I}(P)\) for observation \(P\) is the set of parameter values consistent with the observed distribution: \[\mathcal{I}(P) = \{\theta \in \Theta : P_\theta = P\}\] Point identification means \(|\mathcal{I}(P)| = 1\) for all \(P\); partial identification means \(|\mathcal{I}(P)| > 1\).








NoteDefinition 9.6.2 — Types of Identification




Parametric identification: \(\theta\) is identified up to the assumed functional forms. If the functional form is wrong, the estimated \(\theta\) may not be the true parameter. Common in structural IO and trade.
Nonparametric identification: \(\theta\) is identified under general nonparametric assumptions — no distributional or functional form assumptions beyond regularity. Provides stronger support for the identification claim but is harder to achieve.
Set/Partial identification: The data pins down a set \(\mathcal{I}(P) \subset \Theta\) of compatible parameter values rather than a unique point. Manski’s (1990) bounds on treatment effects are the canonical example.
Local identification: \(\theta\) is locally identified if \(\mathcal{I}(P)\) is a singleton in a neighborhood of the true \(\theta_0\), but the mapping may not be globally injective.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
64  9.6 — Identification in Structural Models

title: 64  9.6 — Identification in Structural Models
text:
65.3 Main Results

65.3.1 Exclusion Restrictions from Theory






CautionRemark 9.6.1 — Model-Based vs. Ad Hoc Exclusion Restrictions



An exclusion restriction asserts that variable \(z\) affects the outcome \(y\) only through the endogenous variable \(x\): \(z\) is excluded from the structural equation for \(y\). In IV language: \(z\) is a valid instrument for \(x\) iff \(z \perp \varepsilon_y\) (exogeneity/exclusion) and \(z\) affects \(x\) (relevance).
Ad hoc exclusion: Choose \(z\) because it looks uncorrelated with \(\varepsilon_y\) in the data. No structural justification.
Model-based exclusion: The economic model implies \(z\) should be excluded from the \(y\)-equation because the mechanism through which \(z\) affects \(y\) operates entirely through \(x\). This is a much stronger justification: the exclusion restriction is a theoretical prediction of the model, not a statistical assumption.
The advantage of model-based exclusion restrictions: they generate overidentification tests (if the model is correct, any instrument implied by the model should pass a Sargan test) and heterogeneity predictions (the instrument’s effect should vary in directions predicted by the model).








NoteExample 9.6.1 — Input Prices as Instruments for Output (from Producer Theory)



In a competitive firm’s problem, the firm maximizes \(\pi = p f(L, K) - wL - rK\). The FOC for labor: \(p f_L(L^*, K^*) = w\). This implies that \(w\) (the wage) affects output \(y = f(L^*, K^*)\) only through the firm’s input choice \(L^*\) — there is no direct effect of \(w\) on \(y\) in the production function (technology is the primitive, not \(w\)). Therefore \(w\) is excluded from the production function equation and can serve as an instrument for \(L\) in estimating the production function.
The model-based exclusion restriction: the wage \(w\) is determined in the factor market and enters the FOC but not the production technology. It shifts the supply of labor but not the productivity of workers once hired.








NoteExample 9.6.2 — Weather as Instrument for Agricultural Supply



In an agricultural supply model, supply \(q = s(p, z)\) depends on price \(p\) and weather \(z\). Demand \(q = d(p, y)\) depends on price \(p\) and income \(y\). Weather \(z\) affects supply (it determines yields) but not demand directly (households don’t demand differently because of good weather per se — they demand based on income and price). This exclusion restriction (\(z\) excluded from demand) is justified by the structural model of agricultural markets, not just by statistical grounds.








NoteExample 9.6.3 — International Commodity Prices as Instruments for Local Conflict



In a resource conflict model (see APP Conflict and Resource Models), the value of a contested resource \(R\) drives conflict effort. The world commodity price \(p^w_t\) affects the local value of resources. The model implies: \(p^w_t\) affects conflict through its effect on \(R\) (the prize value), but does not directly enter the conflict payoff function (which depends on \(R\), not on \(p^w\) independently). Therefore \(p^w_t\) is excluded from the conflict structural equation and can serve as an instrument for the local resource value.
The exclusion restriction is justified by the economic model: the conflict model (Garfinkel-Skaperdas or looting) specifies that agents fight for resource rents, and the world price enters only through the rent calculation.




65.3.2 Equilibrium as an Instrument






CautionRemark 9.6.2 — Variables that Shift One Player’s Payoff as Instruments for Another



In game-theoretic models, variables that shift one player’s payoff (cost, technology, endowment) are excluded from other players’ payoff functions by the model structure. This generates model-justified instruments:

In a Nash bargaining model, a variable \(z_1\) that improves player 1’s outside option affects the equilibrium outcome (because it changes player 1’s threat point) but does not enter player 2’s structural payoff function. Therefore \(z_1\) is excluded from player 2’s demand/supply equation and can instrument for prices in a supply-demand system.
In a Bertrand oligopoly, firm \(j\)’s cost shifter \(w_j\) is excluded from firm \(i\)’s demand equation (consumers don’t care about firm \(j\)’s costs directly — only prices matter for consumers) but affects the equilibrium price \(p_j\), which in turn affects firm \(i\)’s demand. The instrument \(w_j\) is called a BLP instrument (Berry-Levinsohn-Pakes 1995).





65.3.3 Simultaneity and Identification






ImportantProposition 9.6.1 — Order Condition and Rank Condition for Simultaneous Equations



Consider a system of \(G\) structural equations with \(G\) endogenous variables \((y_1, \ldots, y_G)\) and \(K\) exogenous variables \((x_1, \ldots, x_K)\). The \(g\)-th structural equation includes \(G_g\) endogenous variables and \(K_g\) exogenous variables. The order condition for identification of equation \(g\) is: \[K - K_g \geq G_g - 1\] (at least as many excluded exogenous variables as included endogenous variables minus 1).
The rank condition is: the \(G_g - 1\) column submatrix of the coefficient matrix on excluded variables, when restricted to the included endogenous variables, has rank \(G_g - 1\). The rank condition is necessary and sufficient; the order condition is necessary but not sufficient.








TipProof





The proof of the rank condition is an application of linear algebra. The \(g\)-th equation can be written \(y_g = \delta Z_g + \varepsilon_g\) where \(Z_g\) includes the included endogenous variables (excluding \(y_g\)). The IV estimator uses excluded exogenous variables \(X_g^*\) as instruments. For the IV estimator to be consistent, the instruments must be correlated with \(Z_g\): \(\mathbb{E}[X_g^* Z_g'] \neq 0\), which requires the rank condition on the reduced-form matrix.









CautionRemark 9.6.3 — Connection to Comparative Statics



The identification argument for a structural parameter is directly linked to comparative statics: the identified parameter is the one that generates differential comparative statics with respect to the instrument. If \(z\) shifts supply (comparative static: \(\partial q^S/\partial z \neq 0\)) but not demand (\(\partial q^D/\partial z = 0\)), then \(z\) identifies the demand elasticity — because the equilibrium price and quantity move in ways that trace out the demand curve.
Formally: if \(z\) shifts the supply equation, the comparative static \(\partial p^*/\partial z\) depends on the demand elasticity (through the equilibrium condition). Estimating \(\frac{\partial p^*}{\partial z} / \frac{\partial q^*}{\partial z}\) gives the demand elasticity. This is exactly the IV estimator.




65.3.4 Partial Identification






NoteDefinition 9.6.3 — Manski Bounds



When the assignment mechanism is unknown (no random experiment or credible IV), the treatment effect \(\tau = \mathbb{E}[y(1) - y(0)]\) may not be point-identified. Manski’s bounds use the distribution of observables \((y, d, x)\) to bound \(\tau\): \[\tau \in [\mathbb{E}[y|d=1]\Pr(d=1) + \underline{y}\Pr(d=0) - \bar{y}\Pr(d=1) - \mathbb{E}[y|d=0]\Pr(d=0), \ldots]\] where \(\underline{y}\) and \(\bar{y}\) are the bounds of the outcome support. The identified set is \([\tau_L, \tau_U]\).








CautionRemark 9.6.4 — When to Use Partial Identification



Partial identification is appropriate when: (1) point-identifying assumptions are implausible; (2) the researcher wants to be transparent about what the data alone (vs. untestable assumptions) implies; (3) the policy question only requires knowing whether \(\tau > 0\) (or $> $ some threshold), and the bounds already establish this. Partial identification bounds can be tightened by adding progressively more credible assumptions, showing the researcher what assumptions are “buying” in terms of identification.




65.3.5 Random Coefficient Models and BLP






NoteDefinition 9.6.4 — Random Coefficient Model (Berry-Levinsohn-Pakes 1995)



In the BLP demand model, consumer \(i\) has utility for product \(j\): \[u_{ij} = x_j \beta_i - \alpha_i p_j + \xi_j + \varepsilon_{ij}\] where \(\beta_i = \bar\beta + \Sigma \nu_i\) with \(\nu_i \sim N(0, I)\) (random coefficients), \(p_j\) is price, \(\xi_j\) is the unobserved product characteristic (quality), and \(\varepsilon_{ij}\) is logit error.
Identification challenge: \(p_j\) is correlated with \(\xi_j\) (high-quality products command high prices and have high demand). The instrument for price: \(\sum_{k \neq j} x_k\) (sum of characteristics of rival products — BLP instruments). The structural justification: competitor product characteristics shift competitors’ prices through the pricing FOC (markup equation), but do not directly affect the demand for product \(j\).




65.3.6 Recovering Welfare from Identified Parameters






ImportantProposition 9.6.2 — Welfare from Structural Parameters



Once the demand system parameters \((\alpha, \beta, \Sigma)\) are identified and estimated, consumer welfare changes from a price change \(\Delta p\) are: \[\Delta CS_i = \frac{1}{\alpha_i}\left[\ln\left(\sum_j \exp(V_{ij}^{new})\right) - \ln\left(\sum_j \exp(V_{ij}^{old})\right)\right]\] where \(V_{ij} = x_j \beta_i - \alpha_i p_j + \xi_j\) is the deterministic utility. This is the log-sum formula (Small-Rosen 1981): it converts structural utility parameters into welfare (compensating variation) measures.
Key point: This welfare calculation requires the structural parameter \(\alpha_i\) (the marginal utility of income). This is what structural identification “buys” over reduced-form identification. A reduced-form regression of market share on price gives the price elasticity but cannot compute welfare without a welfare criterion (which requires knowing the units of utility — i.e., \(\alpha\)).
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
64  9.6 — Identification in Structural Models

title: 64  9.6 — Identification in Structural Models
text:
65.4 Economic Intuition
Identification is the bridge between theory and data. The economic model generates relationships among variables; the identification strategy exploits variation in observables to pin down which mechanism is driving the relationship. The model-building perspective on identification is: the exclusion restrictions and instruments used in an empirical study are predictions of the economic model, not just statistical assumptions. A properly grounded empirical study should be able to say: “I use \(z\) as an instrument because the model implies \(z\) should not appear in the structural equation for \(y\) — and this implication is itself a testable prediction of the model.”
The distinction between parametric and nonparametric identification matters for robustness: if the result only holds under Cobb-Douglas, it may reflect the functional form assumption rather than the economic mechanism. Checking sensitivity to functional form is a robustness exercise that tests whether the identification is substantive or assumes its way to a result.

title: 65  9.7 — Model Discipline and Tractability
text: 66 9.7 — Model Discipline and Tractability
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
65  9.7 — Model Discipline and Tractability

title: 65  9.7 — Model Discipline and Tractability
text:
66.1 Context
Every model faces the tractability-richness tradeoff: more realistic models are harder to solve and harder to take to data, while simpler models may miss important mechanisms. Model discipline is the craft of navigating this tradeoff systematically — adding exactly as much complexity as is needed to generate the phenomenon of interest, and no more. This note presents the parsimony principle formally, surveys the standard tractability devices used in the literature, discusses robustness of results to modeling choices, and introduces the “sufficient statistics” approach as a way to do welfare analysis without full structural estimation.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
65  9.7 — Model Discipline and Tractability

title: 65  9.7 — Model Discipline and Tractability
text:
66.2 Definitions






NoteDefinition 9.7.1 — Tractability-Richness Tradeoff



A model \(\mathcal{M}\) is more rich than \(\mathcal{M}'\) if \(\mathcal{M}\) has more primitives, or its primitives span a larger parameter space, such that \(\mathcal{M}'\) is a special case of \(\mathcal{M}\). Richness generates more testable predictions and captures more mechanisms. But richness imposes costs: 1. Analytical cost: The model may not have a closed-form equilibrium 2. Estimation cost: More parameters require more data moments for identification 3. Communication cost: Complicated models are harder to present and replicate
A tractable model achieves its core prediction in a framework simple enough to admit closed-form or easily computable solutions.








NoteDefinition 9.7.2 — Parsimony Principle (Occam’s Razor Applied to Models)



Among models that generate the same core prediction, prefer the model with fewer primitive assumptions. Applied formally: a model element (an assumption, a parameter, a player) is essential if removing it eliminates the core prediction. An element is inessential if the prediction holds without it. A parsimonious model has no inessential elements.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
65  9.7 — Model Discipline and Tractability

title: 65  9.7 — Model Discipline and Tractability
text:
66.3 Main Results

66.3.1 Five Canonical Tractability Devices






CautionRemark 9.7.1 — Device 1: Linear-Quadratic (LQ) Approximation



The linear-quadratic approximation replaces a general dynamic or strategic problem with one in which payoffs are quadratic and constraints are linear. This generates linear optimal strategies (linear decision rules) and linear equilibrium objects, which are analytically tractable.
Formal basis: Expand the objective function \(f(x, \alpha)\) around the steady state \((x_0, \alpha_0)\) to second order in deviations: \[f(x, \alpha) \approx f_0 + f_x(x-x_0) + f_\alpha(\alpha-\alpha_0) + \frac{1}{2}f_{xx}(x-x_0)^2 + f_{x\alpha}(x-x_0)(\alpha-\alpha_0)\] The resulting optimization problem has linear FOCs and linear comparative statics. Dynamic LQ problems (LQR theory) have closed-form solutions given by algebraic Riccati equations.
When to use: Dynamic models where the steady-state deviation is small (business cycle models, dynamic contracting). Warning: LQ approximations lose global properties of the model; comparative statics are only locally valid.








CautionRemark 9.7.2 — Device 2: Normality and Linearity (Linear-Bayes)



In models with normal distributions, conditional expectations are linear: \(\mathbb{E}[v|s] = \mu + \frac{\text{Cov}(v,s)}{\text{Var}(s)}(s-\mu_s)\). This means that optimal Bayesian strategies are linear functions of signals, and equilibrium prices are linear functions of fundamentals (as in the Kyle model and Grossman-Stiglitz). The entire equilibrium characterization reduces to solving a system of linear equations.
When to use: Information economics models (Kyle, Grossman-Stiglitz, dynamic learning), forecasting games, cheap talk with continuous types. The normality + linearity combination makes posterior updating trivial and equilibrium characterization explicit.








CautionRemark 9.7.3 — Device 3: Symmetry Reductions



When all agents have identical primitives (preferences, endowments, type distributions), a symmetric equilibrium — where all agents play the same strategy — often exists and is the natural prediction. Symmetry reduces an \(n\)-dimensional optimization (one for each agent) to a one-dimensional problem (the representative agent’s problem).
Caution: Symmetry is a restriction. When the phenomenon of interest involves heterogeneity (income inequality, firm size distribution, heterogeneous price responses), imposing symmetry eliminates the key mechanism. Use symmetry only when heterogeneity is not the point.








CautionRemark 9.7.4 — Device 4: Quasi-Linearity (No Income Effects)



Quasi-linear preferences take the form \(u(x, m) = v(x) + m\) where \(m\) is money (numeraire). Quasi-linearity implies no income effects on good \(x\): the demand for \(x\) does not depend on the agent’s wealth level. Welfare calculations simplify: total social surplus is \(\sum_i v_i(x) - c(x)\) and the consumer surplus from a price change is exactly the area under the demand curve.
When to use: Mechanism design (VCG, optimal auctions), public economics (Pigouvian taxation), industrial organization (oligopoly welfare). Quasi-linearity is often justified for goods that represent a small share of expenditure (so income effects are negligible). It is inappropriate for housing, health care, or other large expenditure goods.








CautionRemark 9.7.5 — Device 5: Continuum of Agents (Law of Large Numbers)



Replacing a finite set of agents with a continuum eliminates strategic interaction at the individual level: each agent is infinitesimally small and takes aggregate outcomes as given. Individual behavior follows a law of large numbers: the average outcome is deterministic even when individual outcomes are stochastic. This is the basis for competitive equilibrium theory (continuum of firms and consumers), matching models (Shimer-Smith), and search models.
When to use: Competitive models; models where strategic interaction is not the focus; models with aggregate uncertainty but idiosyncratic risk (Bewley-Huggett-Aiyagari). The continuum assumption can obscure integer problems (e.g., you cannot have half a firm) and is inappropriate for oligopoly or small-group games.




66.3.2 Model Robustness






NoteDefinition 9.7.3 — Robust vs. Knife-Edge Result



A result is robust if it holds for a broad class of functional forms, distributions, and model specifications. A result is knife-edge if it holds only for a specific parameterization or under a non-generic assumption. Knife-edge results are academically interesting but empirically fragile.
Tests of robustness: 1. Does the prediction hold for CES utility and Cobb-Douglas utility? If yes, it likely holds for a broader utility class. 2. Does the sign of the comparative static change under different distributional assumptions? 3. Does the result require a specific parameter restriction (e.g., \(\rho = 1\), \(\beta = 1\)) or does it hold over a range?








CautionRemark 9.7.6 — Falsifiability and Model Discipline



A model with no testable implications is not a scientific model — it is a framework. Every well-disciplined model should have at least one moment condition or shape restriction that it could fail on. Identifying the “falsification moment” — the single most powerful test of the model’s core mechanism — is part of model building.
Publication bias pushes toward sharp, universal results. A practitioner aware of this bias should distinguish: (i) results that hold generally and provide robust empirical guidance; (ii) results that hold under specific functional forms and should be treated as illustrative rather than predictive.




66.3.3 The Sufficient Statistics Approach (Chetty 2009)






NoteDefinition 9.7.4 — Sufficient Statistic for Welfare



A sufficient statistic for a welfare calculation is a reduced-form parameter (or set of parameters) that can be estimated without full structural estimation, but that is sufficient to compute the welfare effect of a policy change to a first-order approximation. The sufficient statistic is derived from the structural model but only requires reduced-form variation to estimate.








ImportantTheorem 9.7.1 — Sufficient Statistic for Policy Welfare (Chetty 2009)



Consider a model where the government sets a tax \(\tau\) on good \(x\). The social welfare effect of a marginal change in \(\tau\) is: \[\frac{dW}{d\tau} = -\tau \frac{dx^*}{d\tau} + \text{(externality terms)}\] The key sufficient statistic is \(\frac{dx^*}{d\tau}\) — the behavioral response to the tax — which can be estimated from reduced-form variation (a policy experiment) without knowing the full utility function.








TipProof





Setup: Social welfare is \(W = \sum_i u_i(x_i, m_i) + \tau \sum_i x_i\) (tax revenue redistributed to consumers). At the social optimum, agents maximize \(u_i(x_i, m_i)\) subject to \((1+\tau)x_i + m_i \leq I_i\).
Envelope theorem: At the agent’s optimum, the indirect utility is \(V_i(\tau) = u_i(x_i^*(\tau), I_i - (1+\tau)x_i^*(\tau))\). By the envelope theorem: \[\frac{dV_i}{d\tau} = -x_i^*(\tau) \cdot \lambda_i\] where \(\lambda_i\) is the marginal utility of income.
Tax revenue: \(R(\tau) = \tau \sum_i x_i^*(\tau)\). The marginal revenue is: \[\frac{dR}{d\tau} = \sum_i x_i^*(\tau) + \tau \sum_i \frac{dx_i^*}{d\tau}\]
Social welfare: \[\frac{dW}{d\tau} = \sum_i \frac{dV_i}{d\tau} + \frac{dR}{d\tau}\] Assuming equal social weights \(\lambda_i = 1\): \[= -\sum_i x_i^* + \sum_i x_i^* + \tau \sum_i \frac{dx_i^*}{d\tau} = \tau \frac{d\sum_i x_i^*}{d\tau}\]
Therefore: \[\frac{dW}{d\tau} = \tau \cdot \frac{dX^*}{d\tau}\] where \(X^* = \sum_i x_i^*\) is aggregate quantity. This is the deadweight loss formula — the welfare effect depends only on the tax rate \(\tau\) and the behavioral response \(dX^*/d\tau\), not on the underlying utility function.
The key insight: even without knowing the utility function, we can compute the welfare effect if we know (i) the current tax \(\tau\) and (ii) the elasticity of demand \(dX^*/d\tau\). The latter can be estimated from reduced-form variation.









CautionRemark 9.7.7 — Applications of the Sufficient Statistics Approach



The sufficient statistics approach has been applied to: - Unemployment insurance: The welfare gain from extending UI depends on the consumption smoothing benefit (estimated from consumption data) and the moral hazard cost (the behavioral response of job search to UI extension). No utility function needed. - Health insurance: The welfare effect of the mandate depends on the price elasticity of insurance demand and the adverse selection externality. - Trade policy: The welfare gain from trade liberalization equals \(\hat\lambda^{-1/\theta}\) where \(\hat\lambda\) is the change in home expenditure share and \(\theta\) is the trade elasticity (Eaton-Kortum formula) — both estimable from trade data.
The sufficient statistics approach bridges structural and reduced-form: it uses the structure of a model to derive a formula, but only requires reduced-form estimates to evaluate the formula.




66.3.4 Example: Simplifying an Overly Complex Model






NoteExample 9.7.1 — Simplifying a Model While Retaining Key Predictions



Overly complex model: A dynamic contest model with \(n\) heterogeneous groups, stochastic resource values, learning about rival capabilities, renegotiation, and three-dimensional effort choices (fight, negotiate, produce). This model generates rich qualitative predictions but has no closed-form solution and 15+ parameters requiring estimation.
Core mechanism: The key prediction is that higher resource value \(R\) increases conflict effort. This prediction follows from the contest model’s FOC: \(\frac{\partial p_i}{\partial m_i} R = c\), so \(m^* = m(R)\) with \(m' > 0\).
Simplified model: A static symmetric contest with two groups, fixed costs, and deterministic resource value. This generates the same core prediction (\(m^*(R)\) is increasing in \(R\)) with a closed-form equilibrium, 2 parameters, and clean comparative statics.
Inessential elements dropped: Dynamics (no prediction change for the static comparative static); learning (no prediction change without strategic information revelation); negotiation (adds a separate game but does not affect the prediction for conflict intensity); three-dimensional effort (reduce to single effort choice without qualitative change).
Retained elements: Two groups (necessary for contest); resource prize \(R\) (necessary for the prediction); cost parameter \(c\) (necessary for the equilibrium condition). All three elements are essential.
Lesson: The simplified model generates the same testable prediction (conflict \(\uparrow\) when \(R \uparrow\)) with far greater analytical and empirical tractability.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
65  9.7 — Model Discipline and Tractability

title: 65  9.7 — Model Discipline and Tractability
text:
66.4 Economic Intuition
Model discipline is the difference between a paper with a clear, falsifiable contribution and a paper that disappears into its own complexity. The key discipline: for every element added to a model, ask “what prediction changes if I remove this?” If the answer is “nothing material,” remove it. If the answer is “the sign of the key comparative static reverses,” keep it.
The sufficient statistics approach is a powerful tool for maintaining discipline: it forces the researcher to identify what structural parameters are actually needed for the welfare calculation of interest. In many cases, the answer is far fewer parameters than the full model contains — and those few parameters can be estimated from credible reduced-form variation.
Robustness checking is an integral part of model building, not an afterthought. Before presenting a model, test its key predictions under alternative functional forms. A prediction that holds under both Cobb-Douglas and CES is a genuine theoretical result. A prediction that requires exactly Cobb-Douglas is a computational artifact.

title: 66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models
text: 67 APP — Conflict and Resource Models
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models

title: 66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models
text:
67.1 Context
One of the most empirically active areas connecting microeconomic theory to development economics is the study of natural resources and conflict. When does resource wealth generate conflict rather than prosperity? How does the value of the contested prize affect the intensity and duration of fighting? These questions have both theoretical content — they can be addressed using formal models of strategic conflict — and empirical content — they generate predictions testable with cross-country or sub-national panel data.
The canonical theoretical framework is the contest model (Tullock 1980; Garfinkel-Skaperdas 1994): groups choose conflict effort to maximize the probability of capturing a resource prize, trading off costs against expected gains. The model generates sharp comparative statics: higher prize value → more conflict effort → greater probability of conflict onset and more casualties. These comparative statics provide the theoretical foundation for an IV strategy using international commodity prices.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models

title: 66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models
text:
67.2 Garfinkel-Skaperdas Contest Model






NoteDefinition APP.1 — Contest Model Setup (Garfinkel-Skaperdas 1994, 2007)



Two groups \(i \in \{1,2\}\) simultaneously choose conflict effort \(m_i \geq 0\). Group \(i\) wins a resource prize of value \(R\) with probability: \[p_i(m_1, m_2) = \frac{f(m_i)}{f(m_1) + f(m_2)}\] where \(f: \mathbb{R}_+ \to \mathbb{R}_+\) is an impact function with \(f(0) = 0\), \(f' > 0\). The contest success function (CSF) satisfies: \(p_1 + p_2 = 1\) (winner takes all). Each group pays cost \(c > 0\) per unit of effort. Group \(i\)’s expected payoff: \[\Pi_i(m_i, m_j) = p_i(m_i, m_j) \cdot R - c \cdot m_i\]








ImportantProposition APP.1 — Nash Equilibrium of the Symmetric Contest



With \(f(m) = m\) (Tullock contest) and symmetric costs \(c\), the symmetric Nash equilibrium has: \[m^* = \frac{R}{4c}\] and each group wins with probability \(p_i^* = 1/2\). Total conflict effort is \(M^* = 2m^* = R/(2c)\).








TipProof





Best response: Group 1 maximizes \(\Pi_1(m_1, m_2) = \frac{m_1}{m_1 + m_2} R - c m_1\).
FOC with respect to \(m_1\): \[\frac{m_2}{(m_1 + m_2)^2} R = c\] (Using \(\frac{\partial}{\partial m_1}\left[\frac{m_1}{m_1+m_2}\right] = \frac{m_2}{(m_1+m_2)^2}\).)
Symmetric equilibrium: Set \(m_1 = m_2 = m^*\): \[\frac{m^*}{(2m^*)^2} R = c \implies \frac{R}{4m^*} = c \implies m^* = \frac{R}{4c}\]
Verification: \(p_1^* = m^*/(2m^*) = 1/2\). SOC: \(\frac{\partial^2 \Pi_1}{\partial m_1^2} = \frac{-2m_2}{(m_1+m_2)^3} R < 0\) — confirmed maximum.
Existence and uniqueness of symmetric NE: The best response \(BR_1(m_2) = \sqrt{m_2 R/c} - m_2\) is well-defined for \(m_2 > 0\). The symmetric fixed point \(m^* = BR(m^*) = \frac{R}{4c}\) is unique.









ImportantProposition APP.2 — Comparative Statics: Resource Value and Conflict Effort



In the symmetric Tullock contest: 1. \(\frac{\partial m^*}{\partial R} = \frac{1}{4c} > 0\): Higher resource value increases equilibrium conflict effort. 2. \(\frac{\partial m^*}{\partial c} = -\frac{R}{4c^2} < 0\): Higher conflict cost decreases conflict effort. 3. Rent dissipation: Total effort \(2m^* = R/(2c)\) and total cost \(2cm^* = R/2\): exactly half the prize value is dissipated in equilibrium. The dissipation fraction is \(2cm^*/R = 1/2\).








TipProof





Differentiating \(m^* = R/(4c)\): \(\frac{\partial m^*}{\partial R} = 1/(4c) > 0\) and \(\frac{\partial m^*}{\partial c} = -R/(4c^2) < 0\). Total cost = \(2c \cdot R/(4c) = R/2\). Rent dissipation fraction = \((R/2)/R = 1/2\). Under CRS, Tullock contest dissipates half the rent. Under DRS (\(f(m) = m^\gamma\), \(\gamma < 1\)), less than half is dissipated; under IRS (\(\gamma > 1\)), more.









CautionRemark APP.1 — Generalizations



The basic contest model has been extended in several directions relevant for empirical work: - Heterogeneous groups: Different cost parameters \(c_1 \neq c_2\) generate asymmetric equilibria where the lower-cost group exerts more effort and is more likely to win. This creates cross-sectional heterogeneity in conflict outcomes. - Capital accumulation: Allowing groups to invest in productive capital (which also increases fighting capacity) generates a dynamic model where steady-state capital and conflict depend on resource value. - Multiple periods: Repeated conflict creates potential for ceasefire agreements (especially if groups discount the future), linking to bargaining theory.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models

title: 66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models
text:
67.3 Grossman Looting Model






NoteDefinition APP.2 — Grossman (1991) Insurrection Model



The economy has \(N\) agents who each choose to be a worker (produces \(y_i\), earns wage \(w\)) or an insurgent (attempt to loot the government’s revenue \(G\)). The probability that an individual insurgent succeeds depends on the number of insurgents \(n\) and the government’s defensive capacity \(\mu\): \[q(n, \mu) = \frac{n}{n + \mu}\] An insurgent earns expected payoff \(q(n, \mu) \cdot G/n\) — a share of the prize if the rebellion succeeds. An insurgent bears effort cost \(e > 0\).
In equilibrium, agents are indifferent between working and rebelling: \[w = q(n, \mu) \cdot G/n - e\]








ImportantProposition APP.3 — Grossman Model Comparative Statics



In the Grossman model equilibrium, the number of insurgents \(n^*\) satisfies: \[\frac{G \mu}{(n^* + \mu)^2 \cdot n^*} = 1 \implies n^* + \mu = \sqrt{G\mu}\] so \(n^* = \sqrt{G\mu} - \mu = \mu(\sqrt{G/\mu} - 1)\). Comparative statics: 1. \(\frac{\partial n^*}{\partial G} > 0\): Higher government revenue (larger lootable prize) increases insurgency 2. \(\frac{\partial n^*}{\partial w} < 0\): Higher wages (higher opportunity cost) decrease insurgency 3. \(\frac{\partial n^*}{\partial \mu} < 0\): Stronger government reduces insurgency








TipProof





At equilibrium, the marginal insurgent is indifferent: \(w = q(n,\mu)\frac{G}{n} - e\). Using \(q(n,\mu) = n/(n+\mu)\): \[w + e = \frac{G}{n+\mu}\] Solving: \(n^* + \mu = G/(w+e)\), so \(n^* = G/(w+e) - \mu\).
Comparative statics: \(\frac{\partial n^*}{\partial G} = 1/(w+e) > 0\); \(\frac{\partial n^*}{\partial w} = -G/(w+e)^2 < 0\); \(\frac{\partial n^*}{\partial \mu} = -1 < 0\).









CautionRemark APP.2 — Bridge to Dube-Vargas (2013)



Dube and Vargas (2013) empirically test the Grossman model using variation in international commodity prices in Colombia (coffee and oil). The model’s key prediction: a decline in the opportunity cost of conflict (lower wages, from lower coffee prices) increases insurgency; an increase in the lootable prize (higher oil revenues) also increases insurgency.
Identification: international commodity prices are exogenous to local conflict (the exclusion restriction from the structural model: Colombia is a price-taker in world markets), and prices are correlated with local wages (coffee prices affect labor demand in coffee-growing regions) and local resource rents (oil prices affect rents in oil-producing municipalities). The IV strategy is directly motivated by the model’s structure.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models

title: 66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models
section: 67.4 Dal Bó & Dal Bó (2011): Workers, Warriors, and Criminals
text:
67.4 Dal Bó & Dal Bó (2011): Workers, Warriors, and Criminals






NoteDefinition APP.3 — Dal Bó & Dal Bó (2011) Model



The economy produces two goods: a labor-intensive good \(L\) (using only labor) and a capital-intensive good \(K\) (using capital and labor). A third sector allows appropriation/conflict — capturing the production of others.
Each worker has unit labor; can allocate fraction \(\mu_i\) to appropriation and \((1-\mu_i)\) to production. Aggregate output depends on the labor allocation. Factor prices are determined by the production equilibrium.
The key mechanism: an increase in the price of the labor-intensive good raises wages, which raises the opportunity cost of appropriation, reducing conflict. An increase in the price of the capital-intensive good raises returns to capital and hence returns to appropriation of capital-intensive production, increasing conflict.








ImportantProposition APP.4 — Dal Bó & Dal Bó Comparative Statics




\(\frac{\partial \text{Conflict}}{\partial p_L} < 0\): A price increase of the labor-intensive good decreases conflict by raising wages (opportunity cost of fighting)
\(\frac{\partial \text{Conflict}}{\partial p_K} > 0\): A price increase of the capital-intensive good increases conflict by raising the value of the prize (capital goods are appropriable)

These predictions generate opposite sign predictions for different commodity types, providing sharp cross-sectional heterogeneity that distinguishes the model from simpler prize-value theories.








TipProof





Labor-intensive shock: An increase in \(p_L\) raises the demand for labor (labor is the factor used in \(L\)-production). By the Stolper-Samuelson theorem, the wage \(w\) rises. Higher \(w\) raises the opportunity cost of conflict (the foregone wage from spending time on appropriation). Therefore, the equilibrium fraction allocated to appropriation falls: \(d\mu^*/dp_L < 0\).
Capital-intensive shock: An increase in \(p_K\) raises the return to capital. Since capital is appropriable (capital goods can be captured), the prize to conflict in the capital sector increases. The equilibrium appropriation fraction rises: \(d\mu^*/dp_K > 0\).
The formal derivation uses the Heckscher-Ohlin-Stolper-Samuelson framework: in a two-factor, two-good economy, \(w = w(p_L, p_K)\) where \(\partial w/\partial p_L > 0\) (Stolper-Samuelson), and the prize to appropriation \(\pi = \pi(p_K, w)\) where \(\partial \pi/\partial p_K > 0\) (higher capital good price increases rent) and \(\partial \pi/\partial w\) ambiguous. The net effect on conflict depends on whether the opportunity-cost or rent channel dominates.




67.4.1 Bridge to Identification






CautionRemark APP.3 — IV Strategy Using International Commodity Prices



Setup: Let \(C_{kt}\) denote the conflict intensity in country/region \(k\) at time \(t\). The theory predicts: \[C_{kt} = \alpha + \beta R_{kt} + \gamma X_{kt} + \varepsilon_{kt}\] where \(R_{kt}\) is the value of the contested resource (correlated with conflict by the model) and \(X_{kt}\) are controls.
Endogeneity: \(R_{kt}\) is endogenous — high conflict may destroy production, reducing \(R\). Also, omitted factors (state capacity, institutions) may drive both conflict and resource value.
IV strategy: Use international commodity prices \(P_{ct}\) (the world price of commodity \(c\) at time \(t\)) as an instrument for \(R_{kt}\). The exclusion restriction: \(P_{ct}\) is set on world markets and is uncorrelated with \(\varepsilon_{kt}\) conditional on controls (country \(k\) is a price taker). The relevance condition: \(P_{ct}\) is correlated with \(R_{kt}\) because local resource value is a function of the world price.
First stage: \[R_{kt} = \delta + \phi P_{ct} + \gamma X_{kt} + u_{kt}\] Second stage: \[C_{kt} = \alpha + \beta \hat{R}_{kt} + \gamma X_{kt} + \varepsilon_{kt}\]
The structural justification for the exclusion restriction: the contest model (Garfinkel-Skaperdas) specifies that fighting occurs over \(R_{kt}\), and \(P_{ct}\) enters only through \(R_{kt}\) in the structural equation. The price \(P_{ct}\) does not independently enter the conflict payoff function — this is the model’s prediction, which becomes the empirical identifying assumption.








CautionRemark APP.4 — Testable Predictions of the Contest Model: Heterogeneity by Resource Type



The contest model makes heterogeneity predictions: 1. Lootable vs. non-lootable resources: Resources that can be captured by individual armed groups (alluvial diamonds, cassiterite, coca) should respond differently to price shocks than point-source resources controlled by the state (oil pipelines, large-scale mines). The prize-value mechanism is stronger for lootable resources. 2. Point-source vs. diffuse resources: Point-source resources (oil wells, mine shafts) are easier to contest than diffuse resources (agricultural land). The contest model predicts stronger conflict responses to price shocks for point-source resources. 3. State capacity: The Grossman model predicts \(\partial n^*/\partial \mu < 0\) — stronger state capacity reduces conflict responses to price shocks. Therefore the conflict-price relationship should be weaker in high-capacity states.
These heterogeneity predictions are testable and provide additional evidence on the mechanism, beyond the average effect. Finding \(\beta > 0\) for lootable and \(\beta < 0\) for labor-intensive resources would strongly confirm Dal Bó & Dal Bó’s model over simpler prize-only theories.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models

title: 66  APP — Conflict and Resource Models
text:
67.5 Economic Intuition
The conflict model pipeline illustrates the full methodology of Part IX: a formal model (Garfinkel-Skaperdas) generates comparative statics (conflict effort is increasing in \(R\)), which become testable predictions (conflict intensity is increasing in commodity prices), which are identified via an IV strategy justified by the model’s exclusion restriction (world prices are exogenous to local conflict).
The key insight is that the model does not just motivate the empirical specification — it disciplines it. The model tells us which variable to instrument (local resource value), which instrument to use (world prices), why the exclusion restriction holds (price-taking assumption), and what heterogeneity to expect (lootable vs. non-lootable, labor vs. capital intensity). A purely atheoretic IV study would miss these predictions and would be less informative about the mechanism.

crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
67  APP — Trade and Price Shock Models

title: 67  APP — Trade and Price Shock Models
text:
68.1 Context
International trade models provide a theoretical framework for understanding how price shocks propagate from world markets to local economies. When a commodity price rises on international markets, local producers gain (via higher revenues) while local consumers lose (via higher prices). The net effect on wages, employment, and welfare depends on the economy’s factor intensity structure, its trade openness, and the degree of price pass-through from world to local markets. This application note develops the major models — Ricardian comparative advantage, the Eaton-Kortum quantitative framework, and the Autor-Dorn-Hanson shift-share approach — and bridges each to empirical identification strategies.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
67  APP — Trade and Price Shock Models

title: 67  APP — Trade and Price Shock Models
text:
68.2 Ricardian Model: Dornbusch-Fischer-Samuelson (1977)






NoteDefinition APP.5 — DFS Ricardian Model Setup



Two countries, Home (H) and Foreign (F). A continuum of goods \(z \in [0,1]\). Home’s unit labor requirement for good \(z\): \(a(z)\). Foreign’s unit labor requirement: \(a^*(z)\). Define the comparative advantage index: \[A(z) = \frac{a^*(z)}{a(z)}\] ranked in decreasing order: \(A'(z) < 0\) (home has comparative advantage in goods \(z\) close to 0).
Let \(\omega = w/w^*\) be the wage ratio (Home wage relative to Foreign). Home produces good \(z\) competitively iff: \[w a(z) < w^* a^*(z) \iff \omega < A(z)\] The cutoff \(\tilde{z}\) satisfies \(\omega = A(\tilde{z})\): Home produces \([0, \tilde{z}]\) and Foreign produces \([\tilde{z}, 1]\).








ImportantProposition APP.5 — DFS Equilibrium



In the DFS model, the equilibrium wage ratio \(\omega^*\) and cutoff \(\tilde{z}^*\) are jointly determined by: 1. Pattern of specialization: \(\omega = A(\tilde{z})\) (the cutoff condition) 2. Trade balance: \(w L \cdot \int_0^{\tilde{z}} b(z) dz = w^* L^* \cdot \int_{\tilde{z}}^1 b(z) dz\) where \(b(z)\) is the expenditure share on good \(z\) (from Cobb-Douglas preferences, \(b(z)\) is uniform).








TipProof





Cutoff condition: Home wages are \(w\), Foreign wages are \(w^*\). Home can undercut Foreign in good \(z\) iff \(w a(z) < w^* a^*(z)\), i.e., \(\omega < A(z)\). Since \(A\) is decreasing, this defines a cutoff \(\tilde{z}\) with \(A(\tilde{z}) = \omega\).
Trade balance: With symmetric Cobb-Douglas preferences (uniform \(b(z) = 1\)), Home’s expenditure on imports (goods from \([\tilde{z}, 1]\)) equals Foreign’s expenditure on Home’s goods (from \([0, \tilde{z}]\)): \[w L (1 - \tilde{z}) = w^* L^* \tilde{z}\] (Home workers spend fraction \((1-\tilde{z})\) of income on Foreign goods; Foreign workers spend fraction \(\tilde{z}\) on Home goods.)
Solving: \(\omega \equiv w/w^* = L^* \tilde{z} / (L(1-\tilde{z}))\).
Equilibrium: The pair \((\omega^*, \tilde{z}^*)\) solves both \(\omega = A(\tilde{z})\) (decreasing in \(\tilde{z}\), since \(A\) is decreasing) and \(\omega = L^* \tilde{z}/(L(1-\tilde{z}))\) (increasing in \(\tilde{z}\)). Since one is decreasing and one is increasing, there is a unique intersection.









ImportantProposition APP.6 — DFS Comparative Statics: Terms of Trade Shocks




Technology improvement at Home (lower \(a(z)\) for all \(z\), shifting \(A(z)\) up): equilibrium wage ratio \(\omega^*\) rises and cutoff \(\tilde{z}^*\) rises — Home produces more varieties and wages are higher
Population increase at Home (\(L\) rises): \(\omega^*\) falls (lower wages) and \(\tilde{z}^*\) falls (Home produces fewer varieties)
Terms of trade shock (exogenous change in \(\omega\)): reallocation of production across goods sectors; wage adjustment









TipProof





Technology improvement: \(A(z) = a^*(z)/a(z)\) shifts up (all \(A(z)\) increase). The cutoff condition \(A(\tilde{z}) = \omega\) at the new higher \(A\) requires either higher \(\omega\) or lower \(\tilde{z}\). The trade balance determines which: with higher \(A\), Home is more competitive, earns higher wages, and \(\omega^*\) rises. At higher \(\omega^*\), \(\tilde{z}^*\) also rises (from the trade balance equation). This is the Rybczynski-type result in comparative advantage: better technology shifts production toward more goods and raises relative wages.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
67  APP — Trade and Price Shock Models

title: 67  APP — Trade and Price Shock Models
text:
68.3 Eaton-Kortum (2002) Model






NoteDefinition APP.6 — Eaton-Kortum Setup



\(N\) countries; a continuum of goods. Country \(i\)’s productivity in good \(\omega\) (for buyer in country \(j\)): drawn from a Fréchet distribution: \[\Pr(Z_{ij}(\omega) \leq z) = e^{-T_i z^{-\theta}}\] where \(T_i > 0\) is country \(i\)’s technology level and \(\theta > 1\) is the shape parameter (heterogeneity). The delivered price from country \(i\) to \(j\) is \(p_{ij}(\omega) = c_i d_{ij} / Z_{ij}(\omega)\) where \(c_i = w_i\) (unit cost in country \(i\)) and \(d_{ij} \geq 1\) is the iceberg trade cost.








ImportantTheorem APP.7 — Eaton-Kortum Gravity Equation and Welfare Formula



In the EK model:

Trade shares (gravity): \[\pi_{ij} = \frac{X_{ij}}{X_j} = \frac{T_i (c_i d_{ij})^{-\theta}}{\sum_{k=1}^N T_k (c_k d_{kj})^{-\theta}}\] where \(X_{ij}\) is expenditure by \(j\) on goods from \(i\) and \(X_j\) is total expenditure of \(j\).
Welfare formula (Arkolakis-Costinot-Rodriguez-Clare 2012): \[\hat{W}_j = \hat\lambda_{jj}^{-1/\theta}\] where \(\hat\lambda_{jj} = \lambda_{jj}'/\lambda_{jj}\) is the proportional change in country \(j\)’s home expenditure share and \(\hat{W}_j = W_j'/W_j\) is the proportional change in welfare (real income). This is the ACR sufficient statistic: welfare change from any trade shock equals the change in home expenditure share, scaled by \(-1/\theta\).









TipProof





Trade shares: Country \(j\) buys good \(\omega\) from the country \(i\) offering the lowest delivered price \(p_j(\omega) = \min_i p_{ij}(\omega)\). Using properties of the Fréchet distribution and the minimum of Fréchet random variables:
\[\Pr(\text{buy from } i) = \Pr(p_{ij}(\omega) = \min_k p_{kj}(\omega)) = \frac{T_i (c_i d_{ij})^{-\theta}}{\sum_k T_k (c_k d_{kj})^{-\theta}}\] (Standard result from extreme value theory: the probability of drawing the minimum from a set of independent Fréchet distributions is proportional to each distribution’s scale parameter, raised to \(\theta\).)
By the law of large numbers over the continuum of goods, the fraction of goods purchased from \(i\) equals the probability above. Since spending is uniform across goods (Cobb-Douglas aggregator), the expenditure share equals the probability share.
Welfare formula: The price index faced by consumers in \(j\) is: \[P_j = \gamma \left(\sum_k T_k (c_k d_{kj})^{-\theta}\right)^{-1/\theta}\] where \(\gamma\) is a constant. Real income \(W_j = w_j/P_j\). Labor market clearing: \(w_j L_j = X_j\) (balanced trade). The home expenditure share is \(\lambda_{jj} = T_j(c_j)^{-\theta}/\sum_k T_k(c_k d_{kj})^{-\theta}\).
After algebraic manipulation, \(W_j \propto w_j/P_j \propto \lambda_{jj}^{-1/\theta}\) (up to terms that cancel in ratios). Therefore \(\hat{W}_j = \hat\lambda_{jj}^{-1/\theta}\).
ACR result interpretation: Welfare change from any trade shock (technology, tariff, cost change) depends only on two observable/estimable objects: (1) the change in home expenditure share (observable from trade data); (2) the trade elasticity \(\theta\) (estimable from gravity regressions using tariff variation). No need to know \(T_i\), \(c_i\), or \(d_{ij}\) separately.









CautionRemark APP.5 — Estimating the Trade Elasticity \(\theta\)



The gravity equation \(\ln X_{ij} = \ln T_i - \theta \ln c_i - \theta \ln d_{ij} + \ln X_j + \text{const}\) implies: \[\frac{d \ln X_{ij}}{d \ln d_{ij}} = -\theta\] A 1% increase in trade costs reduces the trade share by \(\theta\)%. Tariff variation provides the identifying variation: a tariff imposed by \(j\) on imports from \(i\) is a multiplicative iceberg cost \(d_{ij}' = d_{ij}(1+\tau_{ij})\), so \(\ln d_{ij}' = \ln d_{ij} + \ln(1+\tau_{ij})\). IV estimate of \(\theta\): regress \(\Delta \ln X_{ij}\) on \(\Delta \ln(1+\tau_{ij})\) using MFN tariff changes as instruments.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
67  APP — Trade and Price Shock Models

title: 67  APP — Trade and Price Shock Models
section: 68.4 International Price Shocks and Local Labor Markets
text:
68.4 International Price Shocks and Local Labor Markets






NoteDefinition APP.7 — Shift-Share (Bartik) Instrument



The Autor-Dorn-Hanson (2013) shift-share instrument measures exposure of local labor market \(\ell\) to import competition from China: \[\Delta IPW_{\ell t} = \sum_j \frac{L_{j\ell 0}}{L_{\ell 0}} \cdot \frac{\Delta M_{j,\text{other},t}}{L_{j,\text{US},0}}\] where: - \(j\) indexes industries - \(L_{j\ell 0}\) = employment in industry \(j\) in local labor market \(\ell\) at the start of period - \(L_{\ell 0}\) = total employment in \(\ell\) - \(\Delta M_{j,\text{other},t}\) = growth in Chinese exports to other developed countries (not US) in industry \(j\) - \(L_{j,\text{US},0}\) = total US employment in industry \(j\)
The instrument uses the import growth in other countries (\(\Delta M_{j,\text{other},t}\)) as a proxy for the exogenous supply-side shock from China, interacted with local industry composition (the “share” \(L_{j\ell}/L_\ell\)) to assign exposure to local labor markets.








ImportantProposition APP.8 — Conditions for Validity of the Shift-Share Instrument



The Bartik shift-share instrument is a valid instrument for import exposure iff: 1. Relevance: Industry shares \(L_{j\ell 0}/L_{\ell 0}\) are correlated with the import exposure (ensured by construction) 2. Exogeneity (Identification): The “shifts” \(\Delta M_{j,\text{other},t}\) are uncorrelated with unobserved determinants of local labor market outcomes in the US conditional on controls 3. No direct effect of “shares”: The initial industry composition does not independently affect outcomes except through its interaction with the China shock (exclusion restriction)








TipProof





The validity conditions come from two interpretations of the shift-share estimator (Goldsmith-Pinkham, Sorkin & Swift 2020):
“Shares” interpretation: The shift-share is a weighted IV, with initial industry shares as instruments. Validity requires the shares to be as good as randomly assigned (conditional on controls) — i.e., the initial industry composition is uncorrelated with local trends in employment growth.
“Shifts” interpretation: The shift-share is a GMM estimator using industry-level shocks as instruments. Validity requires the industry-level shifts \(\Delta M_{j,\text{other}}\) to be uncorrelated with the industry-level component of US employment shocks.
The economic (model-based) justification for the exclusion: Chinese import growth in other developed countries is driven by Chinese productivity improvements (supply side), which is exogenous to US local labor market conditions. This justification comes from a model of China’s export growth: supply-side technological change in China shifts the global supply curve, generating correlated export growth across all destination countries. The correlation with US local exposure is mechanical (industry composition), not through any feedback from US labor markets to Chinese productivity.




68.4.1 Pass-Through Models






NoteDefinition APP.8 — Exchange Rate Pass-Through



The exchange rate pass-through (ERPT) measures how much of a currency depreciation is reflected in import prices. Let \(p_t^m\) be the import price (in local currency), \(e_t\) be the exchange rate (domestic currency per foreign currency), and \(p_t^f\) be the foreign export price. The pass-through coefficient is: \[\text{ERPT} = \frac{d \ln p_t^m}{d \ln e_t} = 1 - \frac{d \ln(p_t^f / e_t)}{d \ln e_t}\] Under complete pass-through (ERPT = 1): import prices move one-for-one with exchange rates (Law of One Price). Under zero pass-through (ERPT = 0): exporters fully absorb exchange rate changes in their markups (pricing to market).








CautionRemark APP.6 — Bridge to Commodity Prices in the DRC



The pipeline from world commodity price to household welfare:
Stage 1: World price → Export price. Mining companies sell minerals at world prices. The pass-through from world mineral prices to mining companies’ revenues is close to 1 (price-taking, internationally traded goods).
Stage 2: Export price → Local wage. The mining company’s revenue determines the wage it can pay local workers. In a competitive labor market, wage = marginal revenue product. The wage elasticity to world prices depends on the production function (mining technology) and local labor market competition.
Stage 3: Local wage → Household welfare. Households with workers employed in mining benefit directly (wage income). Agricultural households are affected indirectly through labor market competition and consumer prices. Non-traded goods prices respond to the local income effect.
Stage 4: Price shock → Conflict (via resource prize). The mining sector’s value also affects the contest prize in the conflict model. A higher world price raises \(R\) in the Garfinkel-Skaperdas model, increasing conflict effort.
Each stage has a structural model justifying the mechanism and generating testable predictions. The IV strategy (world prices → local outcomes) traverses all stages; structural analysis would decompose the total effect into the contribution of each stage.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
67  APP — Trade and Price Shock Models

title: 67  APP — Trade and Price Shock Models
text:
68.5 Economic Intuition
Trade models make precise predictions about the local welfare effects of international price shocks. The DFS model shows that comparative advantage determines which sectors contract and which expand in response to a terms-of-trade shock. The EK model provides the sufficient statistic for welfare: the change in the home expenditure share, scaled by the trade elasticity. This elegant formula — \(\hat{W}_j = \hat\lambda_{jj}^{-1/\theta}\) — is one of the best examples of the sufficient statistics approach in action: welfare depends only on two estimable quantities, regardless of the full model structure.
The Autor-Dorn-Hanson shift-share design is the canonical example of how a trade model’s comparative static (exposure to Chinese import competition) can be turned into an empirical instrument. The shift-share is not a pure reduced-form instrument — it is grounded in the structure of the trade model (the Heckscher-Ohlin or specific-factors framework that predicts industry-specific adjustment to trade shocks). The model justifies both the instrument and the interpretation of the estimated coefficient.
