crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals

title: 68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals
text:
69.1 Context
Financial markets aggregate information from many traders with dispersed private signals. Prediction markets are designed to do this deliberately: they create Arrow-Debreu securities whose prices represent market-implied probabilities of future events. The question “how efficiently do markets aggregate information?” has both positive (theoretical) and empirical dimensions. The positive theory — Grossman-Stiglitz, Kyle — characterizes the equilibrium degree of information revelation as a function of information acquisition costs and strategic behavior. The empirical methodology — event studies — tests whether prices respond correctly and completely to informative events. This application note bridges the theoretical models to empirical methodology, with specific focus on implications for studying how conflict events and commodity discoveries are priced in financial markets.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals

title: 68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals
text:
69.2 Efficient Markets Hypothesis






NoteDefinition APP.9 — Efficient Markets Hypothesis (Fama 1970)



The Efficient Markets Hypothesis (EMH) states that asset prices fully reflect all available information. Three forms: - Weak form: Prices reflect all past prices and trading volume; technical analysis cannot generate excess returns - Semi-strong form: Prices reflect all publicly available information; fundamental analysis cannot generate excess returns - Strong form: Prices reflect all information including private; insider trading cannot generate excess returns
Formal statement (risk-neutral, semi-strong): Asset price \(p_t\) is a martingale with respect to the public information set \(\mathcal{F}_t\): \[\mathbb{E}[p_{t+1} | \mathcal{F}_t] = p_t\] Equivalently, abnormal returns \(AR_t = R_t - \mathbb{E}[R_t | \mathcal{F}_{t-1}]\) are serially uncorrelated (a white noise process) with respect to public information.








CautionRemark APP.7 — Limitations of the EMH



The EMH is a joint hypothesis: it assumes both (1) rational pricing and (2) a specific asset pricing model for expected returns. Rejection of \(\mathbb{E}[AR_t | \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = 0\) could mean either that markets are inefficient or that the pricing model for \(\mathbb{E}[R_t]\) is wrong. This makes EMH tests inherently joint tests and limits their power to distinguish efficiency from model misspecification.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals

title: 68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals
text:
69.3 Grossman-Stiglitz Revisited






CautionRemark APP.8 — Grossman-Stiglitz for Prediction Market Design



The Grossman-Stiglitz paradox (see 7.6 Prediction Markets and Information Aggregation) has direct implications for prediction market design:

Full information efficiency is a knife-edge: A perfectly informationally efficient prediction market would drive the return to information acquisition to zero, deterring acquisition. Any viable prediction market must be imperfectly efficient.
Noise traders as a necessity: The noisy REE shows that noise traders (or equivalently, liquidity-motivated traders) are necessary for the existence of prediction markets. Without noise, there is no trading, and hence no price.
Optimal market design: The liquidity parameter \(b\) in the LMSR (Logarithmic Market Scoring Rule) controls the tradeoff between responsiveness (how quickly prices update to new information) and stability (how resistant they are to noise). Higher \(b\) → more responsive → more trading but also more noise sensitivity.
Testable implication (Grossman-Stiglitz): More volatile assets should have greater private information acquisition, larger spreads (bid-ask spread as a proxy for adverse selection), and higher analyst coverage. This can be tested by regressing measures of information environment (analyst coverage, bid-ask spread, price impact) on asset return volatility.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals

title: 68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals
text:
69.4 Prediction Market Design: Formal Treatment






NoteDefinition APP.10 — Market Scoring Rule (LMSR)



A Logarithmic Market Scoring Rule (LMSR) is an automated market maker for an event \(E\). Let \(q_1\) and \(q_2\) be the total quantities sold (shares in \(E\) vs. \(\neg E\)) and \(b > 0\) be the liquidity parameter. The cost function is: \[C(q_1, q_2) = b \ln(e^{q_1/b} + e^{q_2/b})\] The price for a share in \(E\) is the marginal cost: \[p_E = \frac{\partial C}{\partial q_1} = \frac{e^{q_1/b}}{e^{q_1/b} + e^{q_2/b}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(q_2 - q_1)/b}}\] This is a softmax function: prices are bounded in \((0,1)\) and sum to 1.








ImportantProposition APP.9 — LMSR Properties



The LMSR satisfies: 1. Bounded loss: The market maker’s maximum loss is \(b \ln(2)\) regardless of the outcome 2. Properness: A trader who believes \(\Pr(E) = p\) maximizes expected profit by purchasing shares until the price equals \(p\); truth-telling is optimal 3. Path independence: The market maker’s final liability depends only on the terminal \((q_1, q_2)\), not the order of trades








TipProof





Properness: Trader with belief \(p\) buys \(\delta\) additional shares in \(E\) when the current price is \(p_E\). The expected profit from a marginal purchase is \(p - p_E\) (expected payoff minus cost). This is positive iff \(p > p_E\) (if trader believes higher probability than market price). The trader buys until \(p_E = p\). Therefore, each trade pushes the price toward the trader’s belief — trading is truth-inducing.
Bounded loss: The market maker pays \(q_1\) if \(E\) occurs and \(q_2\) if \(\neg E\) occurs. Revenue collected is \(C(q_1, q_2) - C(0,0) = b\ln(e^{q_1/b} + e^{q_2/b}) - b\ln(2)\). Maximum loss = maximum of \(q_1 - C\), \(q_2 - C\). Using \(C = b\ln(e^{q_1/b} + e^{q_2/b})\): loss if \(E\) occurs \(= q_1 - b\ln(e^{q_1/b} + e^{q_2/b}) + b\ln(2) = b\ln(2) - b\ln(1 + e^{(q_2-q_1)/b}) \leq b\ln(2)\). Similarly for \(\neg E\).
Path independence: \(C(q_1, q_2)\) depends only on the final quantities \((q_1, q_2)\), not the history of trades. Therefore the market maker’s liability is the same regardless of the order of purchases.









CautionRemark APP.9 — LMSR and Bayesian Updating



When traders update beliefs by Bayes’ rule and trade truthfully (as properness implies), the sequence of LMSR prices \(p_E^{(1)}, p_E^{(2)}, \ldots\) follows the sequence of posterior probabilities. Starting from a uniform prior, after \(n\) traders report their private signals through trades, the market price equals the optimal Bayesian posterior given all signals. The LMSR is therefore a mechanism for distributed Bayesian inference.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals

title: 68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals
text:
69.5 Event Studies: Formal Methodology






NoteDefinition APP.11 — Event Study Methodology (MacKinlay 1997)



An event study measures the price impact of a discrete event at time \(\tau\) on the value of a traded security.
Steps: 1. Identify the event: Define the event and its announcement date \(\tau\) 2. Estimation window: Estimate the normal return model over a pre-event window \([\tau_1, \tau_2]\) (typically \([-250, -10]\) trading days) 3. Normal return model: \(R_{it} = \hat\alpha_i + \hat\beta_i R_{mt}\) (market model) or Fama-French three-factor model 4. Abnormal return: \(AR_{it} = R_{it} - (\hat\alpha_i + \hat\beta_i R_{mt})\) for \(t\) in the event window \([\tau - k, \tau + k]\) 5. Cumulative abnormal return (CAR): \[CAR_i(\tau_1, \tau_2) = \sum_{t=\tau_1}^{\tau_2} AR_{it}\] 6. Statistical test: \(CAAR(\tau_1, \tau_2) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i CAR_i(\tau_1, \tau_2) \sim N(0, \sigma_{CAR}^2/N)\) under the null of no event effect








ImportantProposition APP.10 — Theoretical Interpretation of Event Studies Under EMH



Under the semi-strong EMH and risk neutrality: 1. At announcement (\(t = \tau\)): All information contained in the event is incorporated immediately: \(AR_\tau = \Delta p_\tau = \mathbb{E}[v | \text{event announced}] - \mathbb{E}[v | \text{pre-event beliefs}]\) 2. Post-announcement (\(t > \tau\)): No predictable price movements: \(\mathbb{E}[AR_t | \mathcal{F}_\tau] = 0\) for all \(t > \tau\) 3. Pre-announcement (\(t < \tau\)): No predictable drift unless there is information leakage: \(\mathbb{E}[AR_t | \mathcal{F}_{t-1}] = 0\) for \(t < \tau\)








TipProof





At announcement: The event announcement changes the information set from \(\mathcal{F}_{\tau^-}\) (pre-announcement) to \(\mathcal{F}_\tau = \mathcal{F}_{\tau^-} \cup \{\text{event}\}\). By the martingale property of asset prices: \[\mathbb{E}[p_\tau | \mathcal{F}_\tau] = \mathbb{E}[v | \mathcal{F}_\tau]\] \[p_\tau - p_{\tau-1} = \mathbb{E}[v | \mathcal{F}_\tau] - \mathbb{E}[v | \mathcal{F}_{\tau-1}]\] The abnormal return \(AR_\tau = p_\tau - p_{\tau-1} - \hat{R}_{\tau}\) measures the value-relevant information in the event, stripped of the normal return.
Post-announcement: By the law of iterated expectations, \(\mathbb{E}[p_{t+1} - p_t | \mathcal{F}_t] = 0\) for all \(t \geq \tau\) (martingale property). Therefore expected abnormal returns are zero post-event.
Pre-announcement drift: If no information is available before \(\tau\), \(p_t = \mathbb{E}[v | \mathcal{F}_{t-1}]\) and no systematic drift is predicted. If informed traders acquire information before \(\tau\) and trade on it, the Kyle model predicts price impact from order flow, generating a pre-announcement drift of magnitude \(\lambda \mathbb{E}[|x_t|]\) per period.




69.5.1 Pre-Event Drift and the Kyle Model






CautionRemark APP.10 — Kyle Model Predicts Pre-Event Drift



In the multi-period Kyle model, the informed trader privately knows the event outcome (e.g., whether a commodity discovery is positive or negative) before the public announcement at \(\tau\). The informed trader spreads their trades across periods \(t < \tau\) to minimize price impact. The equilibrium generates a gradual drift toward the true value:
\[\mathbb{E}[p_t | v] = \mu_0 + \frac{t}{T}(v - \mu_0) \quad \text{for } t = 0, 1, \ldots, T = \tau\]
This linear drift is the Kyle model’s prediction for the cumulative abnormal return prior to an announcement: \[\mathbb{E}[CAR(-k, -1)] = \frac{k}{T}(v - \mu_0) > 0 \quad \text{if } v > \mu_0 \text{ (positive event)}\]
Empirical implication: A significant positive \(CAR(-k, -1)\) before an announcement suggests informed trading. The magnitude of the pre-announcement CAR relative to the announcement-day CAR estimates the fraction of information revealed before the public announcement. In markets for mineral commodities, this could capture insider knowledge of mining discoveries or production capacity changes.




69.5.2 Application: Conflict Events and Commodity Prices






CautionRemark APP.11 — Event Studies for Conflict-Commodity Price Research



The theoretical setup: mining operations in conflict zones (such as the DRC) face the risk of disruption, occupation, or shutdown due to armed conflict. This risk should be priced into: - Mineral commodity futures: If a major conflict disrupts supply from a key producing region, futures prices should jump at the announcement of the conflict event - Mining company equity: Companies with operations in the affected region should exhibit negative abnormal returns - Country sovereign bonds: Countries dependent on mining exports may see sovereign spreads widen
Research design: 1. Construct a database of conflict events in major mining regions (ACLED, UCDP data for the DRC, Congo, Central African Republic) 2. Match each event to the relevant commodity (cassiterite, coltan, gold, cobalt) and futures contract 3. Compute \(AR_t\) for futures contracts around each conflict event 4. Test \(H_0: CAAR(\tau-k, \tau+k) = 0\) vs. \(H_1: CAAR \neq 0\)
Theoretical predictions from the conflict model: - Conflict disrupts supply → futures prices rise (positive CAR for mineral futures at conflict onset) - Pre-event drift suggests insider knowledge of impending conflict — captured by positive CAR before the first public ACLED report - Heterogeneity by lootability: Events targeting lootable resources (coltan, cassiterite — hand-mined, easily transported) should generate larger price responses than events at large industrial mines (which can continue operating with security) - Anticipation effects: In regions with persistent conflict, market participants learn to anticipate certain types of conflict escalation, dampening the immediate price response
Identification challenge: The event study is identified by the assumption that the announcement of a conflict event is the relevant information shock — that no other price-relevant information is released simultaneously. This is standard EMH identification but can be threatened by: (a) confounding macro events; (b) conflict events that are anticipated vs. unexpected; (c) thin markets with few traders.








CautionRemark APP.12 — Event Studies as Tests of Information Aggregation



A portfolio of conflict events can be used to test the Kyle model’s prediction: the ratio \(CAR(-k, -1)/CAR(-k, \tau)\) (pre-event drift relative to total price movement) estimates the share of information revealed before public announcement. Under the Kyle model, this ratio should equal \(k/T\) — the fraction of the informed trader’s horizon elapsed.
If the pre-event drift is larger for events in markets with lower liquidity (higher \(\lambda\) in the Kyle model), this confirms the model’s prediction: high price impact markets reveal information more slowly (informed traders hold back larger positions to limit price impact). Testing this cross-sectional prediction requires: (a) estimating Kyle’s \(\lambda\) for each commodity market; (b) computing the pre-event drift ratio for each; (c) regressing the drift ratio on \(\lambda\).
This exercise would connect the structural microstructure model (Kyle) to the empirical methodology (event studies) in a way that uses both as complements: the Kyle model motivates what to expect; the event study tests whether the expected pattern appears in data.

68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals

title: 68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals
section: 69.6 Empirical Evidence on Prediction Market Accuracy
text:
69.6 Empirical Evidence on Prediction Market Accuracy






CautionRemark APP.13 — Summary of Empirical Evidence



A substantial literature documents the accuracy of prediction markets:

Iowa Electronic Markets (IEM): Markets for US presidential elections; prices outperform opinion polls in 3/4 of elections, measured by mean absolute error. Particularly accurate in the final weeks before election day.
Betfair (now Smarkets): UK and European sports betting; betting markets are well-calibrated probability forecasters; show minimal pre-announcement drift.
Polymarket: Decentralized prediction markets; liquidity varies substantially by contract; calibration degrades for illiquid contracts.
Internal corporate prediction markets (HP, Google, Intel): Outperform official forecasts for product demand, project completion times.

Theoretical conditions for accuracy (from the model): (1) Sufficient diversity of trader beliefs; (2) Absence of common value manipulation; (3) Liquid enough to make uninformed trading costly; (4) No systematic correlation between trader beliefs and trader wealth (which would bias the price toward wealthy traders’ beliefs rather than the average belief).
Conditions for failure: (1) Thin markets (few traders → price dominated by noise); (2) Correlated biases (e.g., all informed traders have similar systematic errors); (3) Market manipulation by large players with common-value information and market power.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals

title: 68  APP — Prediction Markets and Signals
text:
69.7 Economic Intuition
Prediction markets are a mechanism design solution to the information aggregation problem: by creating financial incentives for truthful belief revelation (through the LMSR’s properness), they elicit and aggregate dispersed private information into a single price. The price is the sufficient statistic for the market’s collective belief.
Event studies test whether the price mechanism achieves its information aggregation function efficiently. The null hypothesis (EMH) says it does: all information is immediately and correctly reflected in prices. Rejections of EMH can reflect either genuine inefficiency (slow updating, biased learning) or model misspecification (wrong normal return model). Distinguishing these requires additional theoretical structure.
For research connecting commodity prices to conflict, the event study methodology offers a complementary approach to the IV strategy: instead of using price variation to identify conflict effects on local economies, it uses conflict events to identify how markets price supply disruption risk. Both approaches rest on the same theoretical model — but they exploit the causal arrow in different directions.

title: 69  Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics
text:
70 Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics
Instructions: These problems are integrative: they require combining theoretical analysis (formal model solution) with empirical methodology (identification strategy design). For each problem, provide (a) the complete formal model with all definitions; (b) full derivations; and (c) an empirical strategy with explicit identification assumptions and testable predictions. Write all mathematics in LaTeX. Problems are designed at the level of a second-year PhD qualifying examination or an applied theory paper.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
69  Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics

title: 69  Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics
section: 70.1 Problem 1 — Extended Spence Signaling Model with Three Types
text:
70.1 Problem 1 — Extended Spence Signaling Model with Three Types
Background: The two-type Spence model has a unique surviving PBE under the Intuitive Criterion: the least-costly separating equilibrium. Extending to three types creates richer equilibrium structure.
Setup: Three types \(\theta \in \{1, 2, 3\}\) with \(\theta_1 < \theta_2 < \theta_3\). Education cost: \(c(e, \theta) = e^2/(2\theta)\). Wages: \(w = \mathbb{E}[\theta | e]\). Prior: \(\Pr(\theta_i) = \lambda_i\) with \(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 1\).
(a) Full separation: Characterize conditions on \((\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3, \lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3)\) under which there exists a fully separating PBE where \(e_1 < e_2 < e_3\). Derive the conditions on education levels: find the IC constraints for each type (9 constraints), and argue which are binding and which are slack at the optimal separating equilibrium.
(b) Partial pooling: Show that there may also exist equilibria in which \(\theta_2\) and \(\theta_3\) pool at a common education level \(\bar{e}\) while \(\theta_1\) separates at \(e_1 = 0\). Derive the wage for the pooling bundle: \(w(\bar{e}) = (\lambda_2 \theta_2 + \lambda_3 \theta_3)/(\lambda_2 + \lambda_3)\). Write out the IC constraints supporting this equilibrium.
(c) Intuitive Criterion in the three-type model: Apply the Intuitive Criterion to the fully separating equilibrium. Does the least-costly separating equilibrium (where each type’s education is the minimum that deters lower types from mimicking) survive? Show that with three types, the Intuitive Criterion may not fully pin down a unique equilibrium — there may be a set of surviving equilibria. Characterize this set.
(d) D1 criterion: Apply the D1 criterion to the three-type model. Show that D1 achieves a unique selection. State the unique surviving equilibrium.
(e) Testable prediction and identification: State one clear comparative static of the model: how does the minimum separating education level for \(\theta_3\) change when \(\lambda_2\) increases (more middle-type workers)? Design an empirical strategy to test whether the observed education-wage relationship in a labor market reflects signaling. Describe: (i) the ideal natural experiment; (ii) the regression specification; (iii) how you would use variation in cohort composition (\(\lambda\)) to test the signaling model vs. human capital accumulation.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
69  Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics

title: 69  Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics
section: 70.2 Problem 2 — Household Agricultural Input Choice Under Price Uncertainty
text:
70.2 Problem 2 — Household Agricultural Input Choice Under Price Uncertainty
Background: This problem develops a structural model of household fertilizer demand under output price uncertainty and derives an Euler equation-style moment condition.
Setup: A smallholder household maximizes expected utility over consumption in two periods. In period 1, the household purchases fertilizer \(x\) at price \(w\) and plants a crop. In period 2, the crop yields \(q = f(x, z) \cdot \varepsilon\) where \(f\) is the production function, \(z\) is land quality (exogenous), and \(\varepsilon \sim F\) is an output shock (lognormal with \(\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 1\)). Output is sold at stochastic price \(p \sim G\), independent of \(\varepsilon\). Period 2 consumption: \(c_2 = p \cdot q - d\), where \(d\) is a debt repayment from period 1 borrowing to purchase \(x\).
(a) Formal model: Write the household’s optimization problem formally. Specify the period 1 budget constraint (borrowing to finance \(x\) at interest rate \(r\)), the period 2 budget constraint, and the objective function \(\max_x \mathbb{E}[u(c_2)]\) where \(u\) is CRRA with parameter \(\rho\).
(b) First-order condition: Derive the FOC for the optimal fertilizer input \(x^*\). Show that it can be written as: \[\mathbb{E}[u'(c_2(x^*)) \cdot p \cdot f_x(x^*, z) \cdot \varepsilon] = (1+r) w \cdot \mathbb{E}[u'(c_2(x^*))]\] Interpret this condition: the expected marginal utility-weighted marginal revenue product of fertilizer equals the cost of capital times the expected marginal utility.
(c) Log-linearization: Under Cobb-Douglas \(f(x,z) = A z^\alpha x^\gamma\) and CRRA \(u\), log-linearize the FOC around the non-stochastic steady state to obtain an expression of the form: \[\ln x^* = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \ln w + \beta_2 \ln p_0 + \beta_3 \ln z + \beta_4 \rho + \text{error}\] Identify each \(\beta_i\) as a function of structural parameters \((\gamma, \alpha, r, \rho)\).
(d) Moment conditions: Write the FOC as a GMM moment condition: \(\mathbb{E}[m(c_2, x^*, p, w, z; \gamma, \alpha, \rho)] = 0\). Specify the function \(m(\cdot)\) explicitly.
(e) Identification: The challenge: output price \(p\) is realized only after the planting decision, but \(p_0\) (the expected price) may be correlated with the household’s productivity \(A\) (good farmers plant in productive areas and also have better information about prices). Propose an instrument \(v\) for \(p_0\) that: (i) is correlated with \(p_0\); (ii) is excluded from the structural equation by the model’s assumptions; (iii) allows identification of the risk aversion parameter \(\rho\). Explain why (ii) holds using the model’s structure.
(f) Welfare calculation: Using the identified structural parameters, compute the certainty equivalent loss from output price risk relative to the risk-free world: \(CE\_loss = \mathbb{E}[c_2(p)] - CE(c_2(p))\) where \(CE\) is the certainty equivalent under CRRA utility. Show how this is a function of structural parameters estimable from the moment conditions.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
69  Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics

title: 69  Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics
section: 70.3 Problem 3 — Contest Model with Heterogeneous Groups: Theory and IV
text:
70.3 Problem 3 — Contest Model with Heterogeneous Groups: Theory and IV
Background: This problem extends the symmetric Garfinkel-Skaperdas contest to heterogeneous groups, derives comparative statics, and designs an IV strategy.
Setup: Two groups \(i \in \{1,2\}\) with heterogeneous fighting costs: group 1 has unit cost \(c_1\) and group 2 has unit cost \(c_2 \neq c_1\). The Tullock contest success function is \(p_i = m_i/(m_1 + m_2)\). The prize is \(R > 0\). Each group maximizes \(\Pi_i = p_i R - c_i m_i\).
(a) Asymmetric Nash equilibrium: Derive the Nash equilibrium \((m_1^*, m_2^*)\) with asymmetric costs. Show that: \[m_i^* = \frac{c_j R}{(c_1 + c_2)^2}\] Verify: \(\sum_i m_i^* = R c_1 c_2 (1/c_1 + 1/c_2)/(c_1+c_2)^2\) … (compute and simplify). At what cost ratio \(c_1/c_2\) is total conflict effort maximized?
(b) Comparative statics: Compute \(\partial m_1^*/\partial R\), \(\partial m_2^*/\partial R\), \(\partial m_1^*/\partial c_1\), and \(\partial m_1^*/\partial c_2\). Interpret each. Show that an increase in \(R\) increases both groups’ efforts. Show that an increase in group 1’s cost decreases group 1’s effort and also changes group 2’s effort — derive the direction of the spillover.
(c) Observable conflict intensity: Let \(I\) denote observable conflict intensity (casualties, incidents) which proxies total effort: \(I = \kappa (m_1^* + m_2^*) + \eta\) where \(\kappa > 0\) and \(\eta\) is measurement error. Derive \(\partial I/\partial R\) in terms of structural parameters. Write this as a regression equation: \[I = \alpha + \beta R + \varepsilon\] where \(\beta\) is a structural parameter. Express \(\beta\) in terms of \((c_1, c_2, \kappa)\).
(d) IV strategy: Suppose \(R = \phi P^w\) where \(P^w\) is the international commodity price (exogenous) and \(\phi\) is a local technology parameter (time-invariant, potentially correlated with unobservables). The regression of interest is: \(I_{kt} = \alpha + \beta R_{kt} + \gamma X_{kt} + \varepsilon_{kt}\). Propose an IV strategy: - State the first stage: \(R_{kt} = \delta_0 + \delta_1 P^w_t \cdot S_k + \delta_2 X_{kt} + u_{kt}\) where \(S_k\) is the resource endowment of region \(k\). Identify \(S_k\) and \(P^w_t\) precisely. - State the exclusion restriction: \(P^w_t\) should be uncorrelated with \(\varepsilon_{kt}\) conditional on controls. State this as a model assumption derived from the Garfinkel-Skaperdas payoff structure. - Discuss threats to the exclusion restriction: what economic channels could cause \(P^w_t\) to directly affect conflict without passing through \(R_{kt}\)?
(e) Heterogeneity predictions: The model predicts that the conflict-resource value relationship is stronger when groups are more equal in fighting capacity (\(c_1 \approx c_2\)). Show this formally: compute \(\frac{\partial^2 (m_1^* + m_2^*)}{\partial R \, \partial \Delta c}\) where \(\Delta c = c_1 - c_2\), and show the sign. Propose an empirical test using cross-sectional variation in group capability.
(f) Dynamic extension: Extend the model to two periods. In period 2, the losing group from period 1 has degraded capacity (a conflict trap). Write down the two-period model and derive whether this creates a complementarity between period 1 and period 2 conflict, generating strategic reasons for early termination or escalation.
crumbs: Part IX — Model Building for Empirics
69  Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics

title: 69  Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics
section: 70.4 Problem 4 — Technology Adoption with Coordination and Global Games
text:
70.4 Problem 4 — Technology Adoption with Coordination and Global Games
Background: Technology adoption exhibits strategic complementarities: a technology is more valuable when more others adopt it (network externalities). This creates multiple equilibria. Global games provide a unique selection.
Setup: A continuum of firms, each deciding whether to adopt a new technology (\(a = 1\)) or stick with the old one (\(a = 0\)). The payoff from adoption depends on the fraction \(\phi\) of others who adopt and on a state parameter \(\theta\) (the “technology shock,” e.g., a subsidy or productivity improvement): \[\pi(1, \phi, \theta) = \theta + \rho \phi - k\] \[\pi(0, \phi, \theta) = 0\] where \(k > 0\) is the adoption cost and \(\rho > 0\) is the network externality.
(a) Full information game: Show that with full information about \(\theta\), there are multiple equilibria for intermediate values of \(\theta\): - For \(\theta < k\) (even if all adopt): “No adoption” (\(\phi = 0\)) is the unique equilibrium - For \(\theta > k - \rho\) (even if no one adopts): “Full adoption” (\(\phi = 1\)) is the unique equilibrium - For \(\theta \in [k - \rho, k]\): Both \(\phi = 0\) and \(\phi = 1\) are Nash equilibria Find the conditions precisely and verify.
(b) Global games equilibrium: Now each firm observes a noisy private signal \(x_i = \theta + \varepsilon_i\) where \(\varepsilon_i \sim U[-\epsilon, \epsilon]\). Apply the Carlsson-Van Damme argument to derive the unique equilibrium threshold \(\theta^*\) as \(\epsilon \to 0\). Show that \(\theta^* = k - \rho/2\) — the “risk-dominant” threshold at which the firm is indifferent between adopting and not adopting when they believe half of others adopt.
(c) Policy implications: Suppose the government provides a subsidy \(s\), reducing \(k\) to \(k - s\). Compute the new threshold \(\theta^*(s) = k - s - \rho/2\). Show that the probability of the adoption equilibrium (when \(\theta \sim U[\underline\theta, \bar\theta]\)) is: \[\Pr(\text{adoption}) = \Pr(\theta > \theta^*(s)) = \frac{\bar\theta - k + s + \rho/2}{\bar\theta - \underline\theta}\] Compute the marginal effect of the subsidy: \(d\Pr(\text{adoption})/ds\). Interpret as the policy multiplier.
(d) Testable prediction: The global games model generates a regression discontinuity prediction: adoption rates should jump discontinuously at the threshold \(\theta^*\). Propose an empirical design that: - Defines a running variable \(X\) that proxies for \(\theta\) (e.g., a productivity index, a subsidy amount, a demand shock) - Defines the outcome \(Y\) (technology adoption rate in a region or sector) - Identifies the RD bandwidth \(h\) around the threshold - States what a discontinuous jump in \(Y\) at \(X = \theta^*\) would imply about the global games equilibrium selection - Discusses the assumptions needed for the RD to be valid (no manipulation, no other discontinuities)
(e) Multiplicity vs. coordination failure: Distinguish between two interpretations of low adoption rates in the data: (i) \(\theta < \theta^*\) (fundamentals are bad; the unique global games equilibrium is no adoption); (ii) \(\theta > \theta^*\) but coordination failure has selected the bad equilibrium (multiplicity). Describe an empirical test that distinguishes these interpretations. What does the test require theoretically and empirically?

title: 69  Problem Set — Part IX: Model Building for Empirics
text:
70.5 Problem 5 — Original Model-Building Exercise
Instructions: This problem asks you to build an original formal model, following the methodology of Part IX. Choose a stylized empirical phenomenon from the list below (or propose your own), and complete the full pipeline.
Choose one phenomenon: (A) Firms in conflict zones pay a “war tax” to armed groups in exchange for protection; explain when this arrangement is stable. (B) Mining companies invest more in CSR (community development) in regions with stronger community organizations; explain the mechanism. (C) Humanitarian aid in conflict zones may be captured by armed groups; explain when and how much aid is captured in equilibrium. (D) Journalists self-censor in environments with uncertain reprisals; model when truthful reporting is an equilibrium.
Complete each step:
(a) Define the economic environment \(\mathcal{E}\). Specify: - The agents (who are they? what are their objectives?) - The action space for each agent - The timing (who moves first? what do they observe when moving?) - The information structure (what is private? what is common knowledge?) - The technology (how do actions map to outcomes?)
(b) Derive the equilibrium. Specify the solution concept explicitly. Derive the Nash equilibrium (or PBE, or SPE — as appropriate). Check existence and uniqueness. If there are multiple equilibria, acknowledge this and select using a refinement.
(c) Derive comparative statics. Find the most important comparative static of your model: how does the key outcome change when the key parameter changes? Use the IFT or Topkis as appropriate. Verify the sign.
(d) Derive a testable prediction. Translate the comparative static into a testable empirical prediction. Specify: - The observable outcome variable \(Y\) - The observable predictor \(X\) (the empirical proxy for the key parameter) - The direction of the predicted relationship: \(\mathbb{E}[Y | X = x+1] - \mathbb{E}[Y | X = x] \gtrless 0\)
(e) Design an identification strategy. Propose a credible empirical strategy for testing the prediction: - Specify the regression equation (or matching/RD/DID design) - Specify the instrument \(Z\) if an IV strategy is needed - Justify the exclusion restriction \(Z \perp \varepsilon_Y | X\) using the structure of your model - Describe what data would be needed and where it could be obtained - Identify the main threat to identification and propose a robustness test
(f) Discuss the model’s limitations. List two assumptions of your model that are likely violated in practice. For each, describe: (i) how the violation would change the model’s predictions; (ii) whether the violation would bias the empirical estimate upward or downward.
